document: Relation extraction (RE) seeks to identify semantic relations between entities mentioned in text. By granularity, RE is commonly grouped into sentence-level (Doddington et al., 2004; Xu et al., 2016) and document-level (Gupta et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019) settings. Early work primarily targeted sentence-level relations with feature engineering and pattern rules. Pantel and Pennacchiotti (2006) propose a rule-based approach, and Mintz et al. (2009) design features for classifying relations. In the past decade, neural networks have become dominant for sentence-level RE (Zeng et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015; Song et al., 2019). Document-level relation extraction (DocRE) considers interactions across entity mentions scattered over multiple sentences. Compared with sentence-level RE, DocRE must model long-range dependencies and aggregate inter-sentence evidence efficiently. As shown by Yao et al., large-scale DocRE benchmarks expose diverse reasoning types and annotation challenges. Practical systems must therefore align entity coreference, discourse cues, and implicit links to infer document-level relations. Recent efforts propose both sequence-based and graph-based architectures to address these challenges. Sequence-based DocRE models encode a document as a word or sentence sequence and typically rely on pretrained Transformers (Devlin et al., 2019). Zhou et al. (2021) demonstrate that attention can capture cross-sentence context and introduce adaptive thresholds for entity pairs. Zhang et al. (2021) cast DocRE as semantic segmentation and predict an entity-level relation matrix to integrate local and global cues. Other works enrich sequences with discourse structure or hierarchical encoders to better pool evidence across sentences (Tang et al., 2020; Nan et al., 2020). Graph-based DocRE models abstract mentions, entities, and sentences as nodes and connect them via edges derived from co-occurrence and syntax. Graph neural networks then propagate and compose information to support multi-hop reasoning (Quirk and Poon, 2017; Christopoulou et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2020). Zeng et al. (2020) explore dual graphs that couple mention-document structures with entity-level paths. Xu et al. (2021a) further incorporate logical, commonsense, and coreference reasoning on top of hybrid sequence–graph features. Despite progress, over-smoothing, noise from spurious edges, and weak supervision remain open issues (Sahu et al., 2019; Marcheggiani and Titov, 2016). A complementary line of work emphasizes identifying evidence sentences that support predicted relations to improve robustness and explainability. Evidence selection reduces error propagation by concentrating gradient updates on informative spans rather than noisy context (Jiang et al., 2021). However, naively optimizing for accuracy can entangle evidence discovery with label prediction, leading to unstable training under distribution shift. We propose SIEF, a simple yet general framework that can be combined with diverse sequence-based and graph-based DocRE models. SIEF introduces a sentence importance score to highlight relation-bearing sentences and a sentence focusing loss to regularize training. These components encourage models to prioritize salient evidence while maintaining coverage of supporting context. The framework is model-agnostic and complements both attention-based and message-passing architectures. In experiments, SIEF improves robustness and overall DocRE performance across strong baselines on public benchmarks (Devlin et al., 2019; Vaswani et al., 2017. We further analyze error types and show consistent gains for long-range and multi-sentence relations. Ablations isolate the contribution of importance scoring and focusing loss, and sensitivity studies verify stability under hyperparameter changes.

document: Detection of adversarial examples is a well-explored field in the image domain. Early approaches include input transformations, statistical tests, and auxiliary binary detectors (Xu et al., 2018; Grosse et al., 2017; Metzen et al., 2017). However, Carlini and Wagner (2017) showed that adaptive attackers can circumvent many heuristics. In parallel, novelty detection literature suggests that generative modeling can flag anomalous inputs (Bishop, 1994). This intuition was later extended by using deep features for maximum-likelihood scoring of distributional fit. In NLP, research on detecting word-level adversarial attacks remains comparatively limited. Zhou et al. (2019) proposed DISP, which trains a detector on adversarially perturbed corpora for joint detection and defense. Building on lexical rarity cues, FGWS improves detection performance by scoring frequency-based statistics (Mozes et al., 2021). Yet such methods can be brittle when attacks preserve frequency profiles or mimic natural errors (Nguyen et al., 2020; Smith and Lee, 2019. Le et al. (2021) targeted trigger-based attacks such as UniTrigger (Wallace et al., 2019) with a tailored recognition pipeline. While effective, their specialization raises concerns about generalization across attack families and domains. Orthographic corruption defenses focus on spelling noise but may overlook semantic-preserving paraphrases (Pruthi et al., 2019). Recent trends favor representation-space monitoring that tracks distributional shifts in hidden states across layers. Complementary efforts explore uncertainty estimation and calibration to separate benign variation from malicious shifts (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016; Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Our work synthesizes these threads by proposing a unified evaluation protocol and a detector that leverages token saliency, sentence semantics, and uncertainty signals.

document: Various NLP data augmentation techniques have been proposed and they are mainly divided into two categories: one is to modify raw input directly, and the other interferes with the embedding (Miyato et al., 2016. The most commonly used method to modify the raw input is token replacement, which selects tokens in a sentence and substitutes them with semantically similar tokens to synthesize a new sentence. Wei and Zou (2019) directly use the synonym table WordNet (Miller, 1998) for replacement. Kobayashi (2018) proposes contextual augmentation that samples substitutes using forward and backward language models. Later work incorporates BERT's masked language modeling to capture bidirectional context during substitution (Devlin et al., 2018). Gao et al. (2019) introduce soft augmentation by replacing a one-hot token vector with a learned distribution over the vocabulary. Unlike Gao et al. (2019), we use an MLM to generate smoothed representations for entire sentences. This strategy better leverages deep contextual cues that integrate local selection with sentence-level semantics. In addition, our approach offers strong parallelism because all token positions can be processed in a single forward pass. We further control augmentation intensity via interpolation in the embedding space. Such control permits task-aware perturbations that preserve label consistency in classification settings. Prior studies also explore adversarial perturbations on embeddings to regularize encoders (Miyato et al., 2017). However, noise-based approaches may reduce fluency or induce unintended label flips when the perturbation budget is large. In contrast, lexical replacement maintains surface plausibility but can drift semantically without additional constraints. Recent surveys categorize augmentation along axes of granularity, supervision, and model awareness (Feng et al., 2021). Within this taxonomy, our method falls under model-informed perturbations that exploit pretrained language models. We adopt calibration-oriented objectives to balance fidelity and diversity during augmentation. Empirically, we observe that smoothed examples improve expected calibration error under distribution shift. They also increase minority-class recall where purely token-level heuristics underperform. For reproducibility, we detail sampling temperatures, mixing coefficients, and filtering thresholds. Our experiments compare against strong baselines across sentiment, topic categorization, and natural language inference benchmarks. Baseline configurations include synonym substitution, contextual augmentation, and soft replacement with distributional smoothing. These baselines have been widely adopted in text classification pipelines (cf. Wei and Zou, 2019; Kobayashi, 2018; Gao et al., 2019, but their sampling policies seldom enforce label-aware priors. Consequently, models trained on such data can be overconfident on spurious lexical cues. Our method introduces an explicit regularizer that nudges replacements toward class-compatible semantics. We also incorporate uncertainty estimates to avoid injecting overly confident yet misleading substitutes. Ablation studies confirm that interpolation strength is the primary driver of robustness gains. The next section formalizes our approach and outlines the training objective.

document: This section introduces the problem of few-shot slot tagging and situates it within the broader context of low-resource semantic parsing. We begin by defining the task and motivating its practical importance in conversational interfaces. In production dialog systems, new domains emerge faster than annotated data can be collected, creating a persistent data bottleneck. Few-shot learning offers a way to generalize to unseen intents and slots with only a handful of labeled examples. However, slot definitions are often ambiguous, domain dependent, and sparsely represented at training time. These characteristics make classical sequence labeling approaches brittle when domain shift is severe. Traditional methods rely on token-level classifiers trained with conditional random fields or BiLSTMs to model label dependencies. Such models achieve strong performance in fully supervised regimes but degrade sharply as supervision wanes. Pretrained language models have partially alleviated this problem by providing transferable representations that encode syntax and semantics. Prompt-based adaptation further narrows the gap by reformulating labeling as conditional text completion guided by task-specific templates. In this paradigm, discrete or soft prompts steer a frozen or lightly tuned encoder toward slot-aware predictions. A parallel line of work explores verbalizers that map label semantics into natural language tokens to anchor predictions. Cross-domain generalization has also been improved by leveraging schema descriptions and example-driven calibration. Despite these advances, the community lacks a unified understanding of when prompts outperform classical taggers under realistic constraints. We therefore organize prior art along three axes, namely supervision type, conditioning signal, and inference objective. Under full supervision, CRF-enhanced transformers remain competitive for within-domain tagging. Under few-shot supervision, prompt-tuning and pattern-exploiting training often surpass fine-tuning baselines on new domains. Hybrid methods inject label semantics via definitions, exemplars, or ontologies to mitigate mismatches between source and target distributions. Retriever-augmented prompting supplements the input with in-context demonstrations selected from a support set. Calibration techniques adjust verbalizer probabilities to counter label frequency imbalances and domain priors. From a data perspective, benchmarks spanning multiple domains with standardized schemas enable controlled evaluation. From a modeling perspective, stability under different prompt initializations and demonstration orders is a key concern. Recent studies also examine the interplay between prompt length, template naturalness, and the capacity of the underlying encoder. In addition, parameter-efficient tuning methods such as adapters and low-rank updates provide competitive accuracy under strict memory budgets. Evaluation protocols benefit from reporting confidence-aware metrics and error typologies that disentangle boundary, type, and span errors. To contextualize our approach, we compare against strong baselines drawn from both classical labeling and prompt-based families. We adopt conventional citation style throughout and reference representative advances using parenthetical markers when appropriate. Where relevant, we discuss foundational models for sequence-to-sequence prompting Raffel et al., 2020) as they inform our prompt design.

document: Recent progress in interpretable machine learning has emphasized methods that shed light on individual predictions and global model behavior. Post-hoc explanations such as feature attributions and rationales have been widely studied in vision and language domains (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2016; Lundberg and Lee, 2017). However, explanations that merely describe why a prediction occurred do not directly assist users in achieving preferred outcomes (Lipton, 2018; Miller, 2019). Counterfactual explanations reframe interpretability as the problem of finding minimal, feasible changes to inputs that flip the model decision (Wachter et al., 2017; Ustun et al., 2019). In NLP, counterfactual data augmentation and contrastive editing have demonstrated benefits for robustness and fairness (Zmigrod et al., 2019; Kaushik et al., 2020). Yet, generating actionable counterfactuals for discrete, structured text remains challenging due to grammaticality, semantics, and domain constraints (Madaan et al., 2021; Ross et al., 2021). Prior work on natural language rationales offers complementary supervision but often lacks explicit guidance on how to change an input (Hendricks et al., 2018; DeYoung et al., 2020). Interactive learning for semantic parsing has shown that users can iteratively correct systems, improving accuracy over time (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). Our perspective connects these strands by formalizing user-desired objectives and constraints within a principled optimization framework (Karimi et al., 2020; Russell, 2019). We also draw on causal reasoning to separate interventions from observations in model behavior (Pearl, 2009; Kilbertus et al., 2017). In particular, feasibility requires modeling actionable features and permissible edits for language inputs (Kusner et al., 2017; Barocas et al., 2020). Complementary lines of research compare local surrogate models with example-based and counterfactual approaches for end-user decision support (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Lipton, 2016; Miller, 2019. Beyond feasibility, actionable counterfactuals must be sparse and faithful so that a minimal set of edits suffices to flip the decision (Mothilal et al., 2020; Keane and Smyth, 2020). Reliability further depends on verifying that proposed edits generalize under small perturbations rather than exploiting spurious lexical cues (Gardner et al., 2020; Feder et al., 2021). To this end, we integrate constraints on fluency, semantic similarity, and label monotonicity into a unified search objective (Pruthi et al., 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2020). Our method contrasts with gradient-based attribution, which may be unstable and does not prescribe concrete actions (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Kindermans et al., 2019). It also differs from example-based case-based reasoning that retrieves neighbors without guaranteeing causal sufficiency (Keane and Kenny, 2019; Looveren and Klaise, 2021). From a usability perspective, counterfactuals can double as natural language interfaces that communicate trade-offs between accuracy, cost, and risk (Bhatt et al., 2020; Dodge et al., 2019). In structured prediction, work on edit-based debugging for semantic parsers suggests the value of minimal textual interventions (Iyer et al., 2017; Lawrence and Riezler, 2018). For classification, constraints derived from lexical resources and paraphrase models help maintain meaning while exploring alternative decisions (Gao et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020). We evaluate on tasks where discrete changes reflect realistic user actions, including slot-value flips, lexical substitutions, and insertion or deletion of modifiers (Feng et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018). Our experiments compare counterfactual policies across multiple datasets and models, reporting both fidelity metrics and human judgments (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Prabhakaran et al., 2022). The results indicate that carefully constrained counterfactuals improve transparency without sacrificing predictive performance in common NLP settings (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020; Bansal et al., 2020). We conclude by discussing limitations and opportunities for integrating counterfactual explanations with interactive annotation and data governance (Arrieta et al., 2020; Suresh and Guttag, 2019).

document: Transfer learning has become central to modern NLP, offering strategies that reuse knowledge across tasks. Two prominent paradigms are multi-task learning, which trains on several tasks simultaneously, and STILTs, which adds supervised intermediate tasks before fine-tuning. Despite their popularity, practitioners still lack clear guidance on when to prefer one approach over the other. Prior studies have examined conditions under which task transfer is positive or negative (Bingel and Søgaard, 2017;Vu et al., 2020;Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b). Others have emphasized phenomena such as catastrophic forgetting and representation drift during sequential adaptation (Phang et al., 2018;Wang et al., 2018a). However, the question of method selection remains underexplored relative to the abundant work on performance prediction. Our goal in this paper is to systematize the decision process between MTL and STILTs for typical NLP scenarios. We focus on measurable task attributes, data regimes, and model capacity as primary determinants of the choice. We also study practical constraints such as computational budget and hyperparameter sensitivity. The motivation stems from empirical irregularities reported across benchmarks, where nominally similar settings yield divergent outcomes (Ruder, 2017;Wang et al., 2019). Multi-task learning has been repeatedly shown to help when tasks share structure and inductive biases (Caruana, 1997;Ruder et al., 2019). Yet, negative transfer arises when auxiliary tasks introduce conflicting gradients or misaligned label spaces (Standley et al., 2020;Kiperwasser and Ballesteros, 2018). STILTs, by contrast, leverages targeted supervision to shape intermediate representations before final adaptation (Phang et al., 2018). While simple to implement, its benefits vary with the compatibility between intermediate and target tasks (Pruksachatkun et al., 2020b). Selection heuristics have been proposed, including similarity measures over task embeddings and gradient agreement metrics (Achille et al., 2019;Sharir et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these heuristics are not yet standardized and can be costly to compute at scale. Our analysis integrates these strands with a view toward a prescriptive framework for method choice. We revisit claims that universal intermediate tasks improve all targets, a position challenged by both small and large scale studies (Chang and Lu, 2021. In particular, we distinguish between representation priming effects and optimization benefits, which are often conflated in the literature. We argue that the decision to use MTL or STILTs should be conditioned on observed transfer signals in a pilot phase. To support this claim, we derive a set of diagnostic metrics that can be estimated with limited training. We then test their predictive value across a battery of tasks spanning sequence labeling, classification, and generation. Our experiments employ standardized evaluation protocols to reduce confounds such as training instability and data leakage (Dodge et al., 2019;Gururangan et al., 2020). We complement aggregate scores with variance analyses to probe robustness under seed changes and hyperparameter sweeps (Bouthillier et al., 2021). Related work on curriculum and continual learning provides additional context for staged training designs (Soviany et al., 2022;Ke et al., 2021). Similarly, research on adapter-based transfer informs resource-aware choices that can accompany either paradigm (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Prior meta-learning approaches also target sample efficiency but introduce new complexities in optimization and tuning (Finn et al., 2017;Hospedales et al., 2022). We do not aim to replace these directions but to clarify when simpler baselines suffice. Our contributions are threefold and center on actionable guidance for practitioners. First, we provide a taxonomy that links task attributes to recommended training strategies, with illustrative case studies. Second, we present a lightweight selection procedure that compares short-horizon signals under controlled budgets. Third, we release a suite of scripts and configurations that make the procedure reproducible across common toolchains. The resulting recommendations are deliberately conservative so that they degrade gracefully under dataset shift. We discuss theoretical implications of our findings and outline hypotheses to be tested in follow-up work. Among these are conjectures about the role of sparsity, gradient coherence, and invariances in shaping transfer dynamics (Ganin et al., 2016;Fort et al., 2020). We conclude by situating our guidance within broader efforts to standardize evaluation and reporting practices in transfer learning (Liu et al., 2021;Raffel et al., 2020).

document: Machine translation has increasingly moved toward neural architectures that model the full conditional distribution over target sequences. Sequence‑to‑sequence models with attention offer strong baselines for both translation and summarization tasks. Nevertheless, data sparsity, domain shift, and stylistic variability still limit robustness in real-world deployments. To address these gaps, researchers have explored latent-variable models that capture hidden factors of variation. Conditional variational autoencoders provide a principled way to introduce stochasticity conditioned on source inputs. In the basic formulation, a prior network predicts a latent distribution given the source, while a recognition network approximates the posterior during training. The Kullback–Leibler regularizer encourages the prior to align with the posterior, balancing exploration and reconstruction. This approach has been adopted in neural machine translation to better model lexical choice and syntactic alternations, following Sohn and Lee 2015). It has also been studied in monolingual abstractive summarization where compression and salience interact in complex ways. Cross-lingual summarization further complicates the picture by demanding both translation and content selection under constrained budgets. Prior work shows that modeling latent discourse structure can improve factual consistency in compressed outputs. At the same time, controllable generation frameworks enable users to steer length, style, or terminology. Combining controllability with latent variables raises questions about disentanglement and training stability. A common solution is to anneal the KL term to prevent posterior collapse in powerful decoders. Curriculum schedules and auxiliary mutual-information losses have been proposed to maintain informative latents. For multilingual applications, parameter sharing across languages reduces redundancy while preserving language-specific signals. Recent pretraining on massive corpora has made encoder representations more transferable across tasks and domains. However, fine-tuning with small supervised datasets can still overfit spurious correlations. Semi-supervised objectives that mix labeled and unlabeled data offer a compelling alternative in low-resource settings. In machine translation, back-translation remains a dominant technique for leveraging monolingual text. In summarization, pseudo-parallel construction via round-trip translation has been explored to augment scarce pairs. For cross-lingual summarization, dual learning and cycle consistency provide additional constraints that couple the two directions. Variational models naturally integrate with these paradigms by treating synthetic examples as samples from approximate posteriors. They also facilitate minimum-risk decoding by sampling diverse candidates from the learned prior. Inference efficiency can be retained through amortization and low-dimensional latent spaces. Yet, evaluating the contribution of the latent variable remains challenging due to metric sensitivity and beam search confounds. Rigorous ablation studies that isolate the effect of the KL term are therefore essential for credible claims. When comparing to deterministic baselines, it is crucial to match parameter counts, training steps, and data regimes. Well-designed validation protocols mitigate early-stopping biases that would otherwise favor stronger language models. Our work synthesizes these insights into a unified framework for MT, monolingual summarization, and cross-lingual summarization. We adopt standard datasets and report results under consistent tokenization, evaluation metrics, and decoding settings. Building on seminal advances in conditional variational modeling, we structure the prior and posterior networks to reflect source-conditioned uncertainty. We also situate our approach alongside earlier applications in MT and CLS that emphasize robustness and controllability.

document: Vision-and-Language Navigation has emerged as a core task aligning visual observations with natural-language instructions. The R2R benchmark combines real indoor imagery and English navigation commands (Chang et al., 2017). Subsequent methods blend imitation learning with reinforcement learning to stabilize training and explore effectively (Wang et al., 2019). Data scarcity has motivated augmentation techniques that synthesize trajectories or paraphrase instructions (Fried et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2021a). Auxiliary objectives exploit semantic cues to provide richer training signals (Majumdar et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020). Pretraining across large image–text corpora has further improved cross-modal representations for navigation (Hao et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2021). Nevertheless, limited environment diversity during pretraining can hinder generalization to novel scenes. Among model architectures, VLN-BERT transfers knowledge from abundant out-of-domain image–text pairs to strengthen instruction–path alignment (Majumdar et al., 2020). Building on this line, we introduce an in-domain corpus via trajectory sampling with templated instruction generation and pair it with prompt-based pretraining to bridge domain gaps. In parallel, vision-and-language pretraining has progressed through one-stream and two-stream Transformers pretrained with masked modeling and alignment objectives (Li et al., 2019; Su et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a; Zhou et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020b; Alberti et al., 2019). For efficient adaptation, we employ soft-prompt strategies such as prefix-style conditioning and continuous templates while freezing backbone parameters (Li and Liang, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner et al.; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021c; Han et al., 2021).

document: Visual storytelling (VIST) is the task of generating a coherent multi-sentence narrative from a sequence of images. The problem was formalized with the release of the Sequential Image Narrative Dataset, which paired five-image sequences with human-authored stories (Huang et al., 2016). Each image sequence in the dataset is accompanied by multiple references to capture narrative diversity and stylistic variation (Huang et al., 2016). Unlike captioning, storytelling requires modeling event progression, character continuity, and causal coherence across images (Park and Kim, 2015). Early approaches adapted image captioning pipelines with sequence decoders, but they often produced list-like descriptions lacking narrative flow (Jain et al., 2017). Subsequent work incorporated hierarchical decoders to represent paragraph structure and story beats more explicitly (Fan et al., 2019). To enrich content selection, some models integrate external knowledge graphs that supply entities, relations, and commonsense cues relevant to the visual context (Hsu et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021a). Graph-enhanced systems have been shown to increase lexical diversity and reduce repetitiveness in generated stories (Chen and Zhu, 2020). Reinforcement learning has also been explored to optimize non-differentiable objectives tied to human preferences and discourse quality (Hu et al., 2020a. However, reinforcement signals depend critically on evaluation metrics, and weak proxies can lead to gaming behaviors or degraded fluency (Paulus et al., 2018). Consequently, evaluation remains a central bottleneck in VIST research. Reference-based metrics such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE measure n-gram overlap but often misalign with narrative quality aspects like plot coherence and character consistency (Papineni et al., 2002; Banerjee and Lavie, 2005; Lin, 2004). Studies in dialog and story generation report low correlations between such metrics and human judgments, especially when multiple valid phrasings exist (Wang et al., 2018b; Hsu et al., 2019; Modi and Parde, 2019). Embedding-based metrics attempt to address surface mismatch by comparing contextual representations derived from pretrained language models (Zhang et al., 2019). BLEURT extends this idea by fine-tuning on human ratings to better approximate perceived quality in downstream tasks (Sellam et al., 2020). Domain-specific metrics like UNION further tailor scoring functions to narrative structure and content selection biases in storytelling (Guan and Huang, 2020). Despite these advances, automatic metrics still struggle to reflect fine-grained preferences among competing narrative candidates. Human evaluation therefore remains essential for benchmarking progress in VIST. Common protocols ask annotators to rate fluency, relevance, and overall quality on Likert scales, though operational definitions of aspects vary across papers (Hu et al., 2020b; Wang et al., 2020b). Pairwise ranking has gained traction because it mitigates scale calibration issues and yields more reliable comparative judgments (Hsu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020b; Hsu et al., 2021a). Nevertheless, ranking can be sensitive to annotator bias and presentation order, complicating statistical analysis of results (Gehrmann et al., 2023). A promising direction combines human preference labels with learned reward models to guide generation toward globally coherent narratives (Stiennon et al., 2020). Beyond metrics, modeling choices also affect narrative coherence through discourse planning and entity tracking. Planning-based methods first predict high-level story outlines or semantic frames before surface realization, improving long-range consistency (Yao et al., 2019). Memory-augmented architectures maintain representations of entities and events across sentences to preserve character states and temporal continuity (Clark et al., 2018). Multimodal fusion techniques align visual regions with textual spans, enabling models to ground mentions and avoid hallucination (Anderson et al., 2018). Recent work explores instruction-tuned vision–language models for few-shot storytelling, though alignment with human narrative styles remains challenging (OpenAI, 2023). Cross-domain generalization is limited because style, cultural references, and photographer intent vary widely across collections (Mitchell et al., 2020). Data augmentation via paraphrasing and retrieval of related stories has been proposed to expand training diversity without additional annotation cost (Bhardwaj et al., 2020). However, augmentation can introduce label noise and off-distribution artifacts that degrade factual consistency (Feng et al., 2021). In this work, we revisit evaluation by constructing a suite of controlled story pair comparisons to probe metric sensitivity to discourse structure. We analyze whether metrics correctly prefer stories with improved plot progression, entity consistency, and causal linkages over superficially similar baselines. To avoid confounds, we ensure that compared stories share comparable lexical statistics while differing in specific narrative properties. We further examine how metric-guided reinforcement learning shapes generator behavior and whether gains transfer to human preferences. Our findings suggest that embedding-based and learned metrics capture fluency but underweight causal connectivity and cross-sentence grounding. We conclude that progress in visual storytelling requires joint advances in discourse-aware modeling and the development of evaluation schemes closely aligned with human narrative judgments.

document: Human values are of concern to most if not to all social sciences (Rokeach, 1973) and have also been integrated into computational frameworks of argumentation (Bench-Capon, 2003). In NLP, values have been analyzed for personality profiling (Maheshwari et al., 2017), but not yet for argument mining, as considered here. Understanding how values surface in argumentative texts is important for modeling persuasion, policy discourse, and ethical reasoning. Prior work in argument mining has focused on structure and relations among claims, premises, and attacks (Mochales and Moens, 2011; Stab and Gurevych, 2014). However, the value-laden content of arguments remains underexplored despite its centrality in deliberative settings (Habernal and Gurevych, 2017. The social psychology literature provides robust taxonomies for values, most notably Rokeach’s terminal and instrumental values and Schwartz’s basic human values (Schwartz, 1992; Schwartz, 2012). These taxonomies have been adopted in computational social science to study political ideology, moral framing, and social attitudes (Graham et al., 2013; Johnson and Goldwasser, 2018). At the same time, advances in pretrained language models enable more nuanced semantic analysis of argumentative texts (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Bridging these strands requires operationalizing value constructs in a way that is both theoretically grounded and computationally tractable. Several recent studies annotate moral or value dimensions at the sentence or document level to train classifiers and sequence labelers (Hoover et al., 2020; Kiesel et al., 2022). Yet, most datasets target general stance or morality rather than the specific value categories theorized in social psychology (Strapparava et al., 2017; Bennett et al., 2023). For argument mining, a central challenge is distinguishing value signals from rhetorical devices, topical cues, and speaker identity. We hypothesize that value indicators can be captured through a combination of lexicon priors, contextual embeddings, and discourse features. Previous attempts to represent values often rely on lexicon matching that struggles with polysemy and context sensitivity (Haidt and Graham, 2007; Gardenfors, 2014). In contrast, neural models can learn abstract value semantics when guided by interpretable supervision such as exemplar phrases and argument roles (Alshomary et al., 2021; Peldszus and Stede, 2015). Cross-domain generalization is particularly difficult because value expressions vary across topics and registers (Toulmin, 1958; Addawood et al., 2017). To address this gap, we review literature on value theory, moral psychology, and argumentative discourse to derive a mapping from theoretical categories to linguistic realizations. We then propose a corpus construction protocol that couples expert-guided guidelines with iterative model-in-the-loop annotation (Settles, 2011; Zhou et al., 2020). Our approach complements prior work on moral foundations by emphasizing instrumental and terminal goals that motivate argumentative moves (Frimer et al., 2017; Roy et al., 2021). We also situate value detection within the pipeline of argument mining, connecting value cues to claim types and inference schemes (Walton et al., 2008; Stede and Schneider, 2018). From a methodological standpoint, we compare feature-based baselines, transformer encoders, and prompt-based models under low-resource constraints (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Lester et al., 2021). We further evaluate cross-topic transfer to test whether value representations are more stable than topic indicators (Guerra et al., 2014; Bamman et al., 2015). For interpretability, we adapt post-hoc concept activation techniques to quantify the contribution of value prototypes to model predictions (Kim et al., 2018; Koh and Liang, 2017). We position our work relative to broader ethical NLP discussions that advocate modeling social constructs with transparent assumptions (Bender and Friedman, 2018; Hovy and Spruit, 2016). Unlike stance detection, value identification requires disentangling reasons from goals, which may co-occur within the same argumentative unit (Sobhani et al., 2015; Bar-Haim et al., 2017). Our review reveals consistent gaps in multilingual resources for value-centric argument mining despite growing interest in cross-lingual argument analysis (Cabrio and Villata, 2018; Eger et al., 2018). Accordingly, we outline an agenda for multilingual value annotation and knowledge transfer using aligned taxonomies and shared semantic spaces (Conneau et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019). Altogether, this introduction clarifies the conceptual landscape and motivates a principled approach to modeling values in argumentative text.

document: Several data augmentation methods have been introduced for compositional semantic parsing. Early work on recombination explored grammar-guided transformations over utterance–logical form pairs. Jia and Liang (2016) recombined data by SCFG to produce adversarial yet meaning-preserving examples. Andreas (2019) used simple rules to exchange tokens with shared contexts and to inject lexical diversity. However, these approaches relied on superficial grammars or rules with limited capacity to capture deep structure. Akyürek et al. (2020) learned to recombine and resample data with a prototype-based generative model in lieu of hand-written rules. Substructure substitution has also been studied for augmentation in other NLP tasks (Shi et al., 2021). Dependency tree cropping and rotation were used for low-resource POS tagging (Şahin and Steedman, 2019) and for dependency parsing (Vania et al., 2019). Tree swapping and controlled perturbations have been explored for low-resource dependency parsing (Dehouck and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020). Despite this progress, subtree substitution with fine-grained meaning functions remains understudied in semantic parsing. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to explore tree manipulation tailored to compositional meaning. Beyond augmentation, advances in neural architectures have reshaped semantic parsing methodologies. Sequence-to-sequence models with attention established strong baselines for mapping language to programs (Dong and Lapata, 2016). Subsequent work investigated copying and pointer mechanisms to better align tokens with symbols (Gu et al., 2016; See et al., 2017). Pretrained language models substantially improved data efficiency across semantic parsing benchmarks (Devlin et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Encoder–decoder pretraining further enabled low-shot generalization to novel compositional combinations (Xia et al., 2021). In parallel, constrained decoding and grammar-aware networks were introduced to ensure well-typed outputs (Yin and Neubig, 2017; Krishnamurthy et al., 2017). Program synthesis with neural-guided search offered complementary benefits under weak supervision (Akkalyoncu Yilmaz et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). Data augmentation remains crucial because labeled logical forms are expensive to obtain at scale. Paraphrase generation using back-translation produced lexical variety without altering denotations (Sennrich et al., 2016; Iyer et al., 2018). Graph-based rewrites attempted to manipulate compositional templates while preserving executability (Herzig and Berant, 2021). Contrastive learning over meaning-preserving pairs improved encoder invariance to surface form (He et al., 2020; Giorgi et al., 2021). Nevertheless, uncontrolled perturbations can drift semantics and silently corrupt program executability. This motivates augmentation schemes that explicitly track and preserve meaning functions at the subtree level. We build on structured operations that respect type signatures and argument orderings common in executable formalisms. Our approach is compatible with modern pretrained encoders and decoders widely used in semantic parsing. Recent encoder–decoder models such as T5 Raffel et al., 2020) and BART (Lewis et al., 2020) have demonstrated robust transfer to compositional tasks. We therefore study how principled tree manipulations interact with pretrained initialization and decoding constraints. In particular, we control the locus of change to isolate operator, argument, and scope alterations. This control allows us to generate examples that probe both systematicity and generalization beyond seen compositions. We evaluate on standard semantic parsing datasets that require compositional generalization (Lake and Baroni, 2018; Finegan-Dollak et al., 2018). Our experiments compare rule-based, learned, and hybrid augmentation strategies under identical training budgets. We report accuracy, denotation correctness, and calibration across grids of data sizes and perturbation strengths. Ablation studies examine the contribution of type-aware selection, rewrite depth, and structural diversity. Error analyses reveal common failure modes, including scope misattachment and spurious lexical cues. Finally, we discuss broader implications for augmentation in structured prediction and outline directions for scalable curation.

document: Large-scale pre-training models such as BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) have drawn substantial attention for their generalization capacity over massive corpora. These frameworks unify representation learning across tasks and enable efficient adaptation with lightweight fine-tuning (Devlin et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Nevertheless, most advances still emphasize single-modal objectives that either read text or process speech, but rarely both within a single training recipe. As a result, cross-modal generation and understanding remain fragmented across model families and evaluation suites. Early attempts in speech-language pre-training improved spoken language understanding via encoders augmented with task-specific heads (Chung et al., 2021b; Kim et al., 2021). However, these pipelines seldom pre-train decoders for generative targets such as speech synthesis or abstractive summarization. The proposed SpeechT5 method is closely related to T5 as a sequence-to-sequence architecture that casts tasks into a text-to-text format (Raffel et al., 2019). SpeechT5 extends this idea by generalizing the interface to accept and emit both speech and text through modality-specific pre and post nets. Unlike single-purpose encoders, the approach seeks a shared encoder-decoder backbone that can be fine-tuned on diverse spoken language tasks with minimal architectural changes. Related lines of work include Speech Chain, which forms a closed learning loop between ASR and TTS for leveraging unlabeled data (Tjandra et al., 2020). Another example is SpeechNet, which designs a modular system for multi-task speech processing under a unified training objective (Chen et al., 2021b). In contrast, SpeechT5 pursues a unified multimodal backbone that is jointly pre-trained on large-scale speech and text to facilitate transfer across generation and recognition settings. Benchmarking initiatives such as SUPERB standardize evaluation for frozen encoders with small task heads, revealing broad capabilities and gaps (Yang et al., 2021). While SUPERB highlights the value of shared representations, it deliberately constrains decoders to lightweight predictors, limiting generative assessment. For research on universal encoder-decoder frameworks, richer pretraining signals and task mixtures are therefore essential. SpeechT5 addresses this need by aligning acoustic and textual tokens within a single modeling space to encourage transfer. The strategy benefits downstream tasks like ASR, TTS, and spoken translation, where cross-modal supervision is abundant in semi-parallel corpora. It also improves data efficiency in low-resource scenarios by reusing abstractions learned from complementary modalities. Prior multimodal learning explored audio-text contrastive objectives to connect speech segments with textual captions (Radford et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2021). However, contrastive schemes often learn shallow correspondences and may underperform on sequence-level generation compared to encoder-decoder pretraining. Multi-task transducers and hybrid CTC-attention models have also been used to share encoders across ASR and auxiliary tasks (Graves et al., 2006; Kim et al., 2017). Despite these successes, pretraining that unifies both modalities within a generative backbone remains comparatively underexplored. Survey articles have noted increasing interest in joint speech-text modeling but point to open questions on data curation and scaling laws (Zhang et al., 2022). For a comprehensive discussion, see (see Wang et al., 2021 on cross-modal transfer. Compared to these directions, our perspective is to integrate speech and text under a shared vocabulary and training objective. Concretely, we hypothesize that aligning discrete speech units with subword tokens can simplify multi-task supervision across modalities. We also posit that the decoder should be pre-trained to autoregress over both acoustic and textual tokenizations to unify generation interfaces. This design allows reusing infrastructure for datasets, schedulers, and evaluation across ASR, TTS, and spoken dialogue systems. It further enables parameter-efficient adapters that selectively route computation for robustness under domain shift (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Nevertheless, careful accounting is required to avoid label leakage between tasks when constructing mixed-modality batches. We evaluate in settings that mirror SUPERB but add generative tracks for TTS and summarization to stress-test the decoder. The results indicate consistent gains over strong baselines across languages, speaker conditions, and noise regimes. Error analyses show that improvements correlate with tasks that benefit from cross-modal priors rather than pure scaling of acoustic data (Park et al. 1). Ablations also suggest that curriculum over input modalities stabilizes optimization and prevents collapse in early training. We additionally compare against targeted data augmentation methods and find complementary effects when mixing pseudo-parallel pairs. Finally, we highlight ethical considerations in collecting speech-text corpora, including consent, demographic balance, and potential misuse.

document: In this section, we first go through some of the most related works for BERT compression in the literature and then review the few works that have used Kronecker decomposition for compression of CNNs and RNNs. Model compression for transformers has progressed along four main axes: pruning, distillation, quantization, and low-rank factorization. Pruning methods remove redundant parameters or attention heads while preserving downstream accuracy (Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019). Unstructured magnitude pruning and movement pruning have also demonstrated competitive sparsity–accuracy trade-offs for BERT-like encoders (Gordon et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation compresses large teachers into smaller students via soft targets or intermediate-layer matching (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019). Quantization further reduces memory and latency by lowering precision with minimal calibration (Jacob et al., 2018; Zafrir et al., 2019). Low-rank and tensor factorizations approximate weight matrices to cut compute, building on classical SVD approaches and modern tensor decompositions (Denton et al., 2014; Novikov et al., 2015; Tai et al., 2016). Within this family, Kronecker-structured approximations have emerged as particularly effective for convolutional kernels and recurrent transitions (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019 showing promising speedups. Early CNN work factorized 4D convolutional tensors into sums of Kronecker products to reduce both parameters and FLOPs (Luo et al., 2017; Wang and Zhang, 2018). For RNNs, Kronecker-factored recurrent matrices have been used to accelerate training while maintaining expressive power (Martens and Grosse, 2015; Thomas et al., 2018). These structural constraints complement channel pruning and group sparsity techniques that operate at higher architectural granularity (He et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017). In transformer settings, low-rank or block-structured parameterizations of attention and feed-forward layers have yielded notable gains without retraining from scratch (Lan et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021). However, transferring Kronecker ideas to transformers is less straightforward because parameter tying and multi-head patterns break simple separability assumptions (Kovaleva et al., 2019). Recent studies therefore combine Kronecker factors with layer-wise distillation and quantization-aware fine-tuning to mitigate loss spikes after factor replacement (Zhang et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2020). Beyond raw compression ratios, evaluation increasingly emphasizes on-device latency, energy, and fairness impacts in multilingual and specialized domains (Ganesh et al., 2020; Hooker, 2021). Our review synthesizes these strands to motivate a unified view of Kronecker-structured compression for sequence models and to identify open questions in scaling, robustness, and task transfer.

document: Knowledge Distillation Recently, many attempts have been made to accelerate large neural networks. Knowledge distillation is a prominent method for training compact networks to achieve comparable performance to a deep network. Hinton et al. (2015) first introduced the idea of knowledge distillation to exploit the "dark knowledge" (i.e., soft label distribution) from a large teacher model as additional supervision for training a smaller student model. Since its introduction, several works (Romero et al., 2015; Zagoruyko & Komodakis, 2017; Tung & Mori, 2019; Park et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019) have investigated methods that align different latent representations between the student and teacher models for better knowledge transfer. In the context of knowledge distillation, MetaDistil shares some common ideas with the line of work that utilizes a sequence of intermediate teacher models to make the teacher network better adapt to the capacity of the student model throughout the training process, including teacher assistant knowledge distillation (TAKD) (Mirzadeh et al., 2020) and route constraint optimization (RCO). However, the intermediate teachers are heuristically selected independently of the training process and the evolution of the teacher network is discrete. In contrast, MetaDistil employs meta learning to make the teacher model adapt to the current state of the student model and provide a continuously evolving meta-teacher that can better teach the student. Concurrently, Park et al. (2021) and Shi et al. (2021) propose to update the teacher model jointly with the student model with task specific objectives (e.g., cross-entropy loss) during the KD process and add constraints to keep student and teacher similar to each other. Their approaches make the teacher model aware of the student model by constraining the teacher model's capacity. However, the teacher models in their methods are still not optimized for knowledge transfer. In addition, Zhang et al. (2018) introduced deep mutual learning where multiple models learn collaboratively and teach each other throughout the training process. While it is focused on a different setting where different models have approximately the same capacity and are learned from scratch, it also encourages the teacher model to behave similarly to the student model. Different from all aforementioned methods, MetaDistil employs meta learning to explicitly optimize the teacher model for better knowledge transfer ability, and leads to improved performance of the resulting student model. Beyond feature alignment, several studies explore logit matching, temperature scaling, and data augmentation to stabilize teacher signals in low-capacity regimes (Gou et al., 2021 and report consistent improvements across benchmarks (Yuan et al., 2020). Prior work on online knowledge distillation further suggests co-training strategies where a dynamic teacher is formed as an ensemble of students to mitigate confirmation bias (Zhang et al., 2018; Anil et al., 2018). Recent efforts also address domain shift by transferring from a teacher trained in a source domain to a student deployed in a target domain using curriculum schedules and regularization (Mensi et al., 2022; Pham et al., 2021). To this end, meta learning has been proposed as a way to adapt teacher behavior to the evolving student, but existing implementations still rely on fixed schedules or manually chosen checkpoints (Rusu et al., 2019). A related line models the teacher update as a bilevel optimization problem and derives gradients through the student update to directly improve transfer efficiency in the next step (Liu et al., 2018). Practical applications in vision and NLP report gains when the teacher is made aware of the student’s architecture and capacity constraints via auxiliary losses and architecture-conditioned distillation (Touvron et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2019). Nevertheless, many pipelines keep the teacher static and emphasize student-side regularization such as label smoothing, confidence calibration, and mixup training (Müller et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021), following Hinton et al. In our formulation, the meta-teacher is optimized to anticipate the student’s next improvement, thereby turning the outer loop into an explicit objective for transferability rather than task accuracy alone (Finn et al., 2017). For fair comparison, we also consider strong baselines that jointly update teacher and student under task losses with similarity constraints, as advocated by prior concurrent work (Park et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2021). We note that early expositions of the distillation principle continue to influence modern practice, including the role of temperature, softened targets, and the balance between hard and soft losses, as originally discussed by Hinton et al. (2015). Finally, we position MetaDistil as a general framework that unifies assistant teachers, online ensembling, and bilevel updates while preserving simplicity for large-scale training in real systems.

document: Adversarial evaluation has become essential for stress-testing NLP models beyond standard benchmarks. Adversarial GLUE (Wang et al., 2021) introduced expert-designed templates to synthesize challenging inputs for multiple tasks. Despite its breadth, the suite does not include named entity recognition (NER), leaving open questions about token-level robustness. Prior work on adversarial NER has explored perturbing entity spans and contexts to elicit label flips. For example, Gui et al. (2021) concatenate sentences, inject character-level noise, and swap entities across categories to study failure modes. Zeng et al. (2020) propose constrained swaps that preserve the original entity type to maintain grammaticality. In contrast, our approach targets hard cases by altering mentions while expanding supportive context to induce controlled relabeling. Similar template-driven resources have informed adversarial test design in (Wang et al., 2021, but their protocols emphasize sentence-level phenomena over token-level supervision. Beyond adversarial inputs, augmentation strategies that modify the training distribution can improve generalization for sequence labeling. Mixup, introduced by Zhang et al. (2018), interpolates examples and labels to construct a vicinal distribution around the data manifold. In classification, mixup reduces overconfidence under shift and smooths decision boundaries (Thulasidasan et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2019). Recent studies adapt mixup for token classification by applying interpolation at representation, logit, or span levels (Liang et al., 2018; Chen and Yu, 2021). However, the interaction between adversarially augmented corpora and mixup has been underexplored for NER. We hypothesize that mixing adversarial and natural instances can regularize boundary decisions around ambiguous contexts. To situate our study, we compare augmentation families spanning character noise, paraphrasing, entity replacement, and context-controlled relabeling. Comparative surveys highlight these themes in (Smith et al., 2019; Jones and Lee, 2020; Kim, 2021) while noting gaps for token-level robustness benchmarks. Building on these observations, we design perturbations that preserve syntactic plausibility yet challenge entity typing decisions. We then couple these perturbations with mixup during training to calibrate confidence under distribution shift. Our contributions include a taxonomy of NER-specific adversarial manipulations, an evaluation protocol, and evidence that mixup amplifies robustness without degrading in-domain accuracy.

document: Semantic parsing has been widely studied across instruction following, code generation, and knowledge graph question answering (Chen and Mooney, 2011; Artzi and Zettlemoyer, 2013; Oda et al., 2015; Iyer et al., 2018; Berant et al., 2013; Yih et al., 2015). We focus on table-based semantic parsing that translates natural language questions paired with tables into executable SQL programs, commonly referred to as text-to-SQL (Androutsopoulos et al., 1995; Minock et al., 2008). Although we concentrate on English, similar ideas extend to multilingual settings (Jie and Lu, 2014; Sherborne et al., 2020). Formally, given a question q and a table T, the task is to produce a program π whose execution over T yields the answer α. We adopt a fully supervised training regime in which gold executable programs supervise the parser. Standard generalization assumes i.i.d. splits, yet practical deployments demand robustness to novel compositions and domains (Gu et al., 2021), (cf. Daumé III and Marcu, 2006, which situates generalization beyond matching train–test distributions. Work on compositional generalization often relies on synthetic data or repartitioned benchmarks and shows persistent difficulty (Keysers et al., 2020; Lake and Baroni, 2018; Finegan-Dollak et al., 2018; Shaw et al., 2021). We study domain generalization, where entire schemas may be unseen or rarely seen during training (Motiian et al., 2017). Despite advances in classification, machine translation, and question answering, domain generalization for semantic parsing remains comparatively underexplored (Daumé III and Marcu, 2006; Daumé III and Jagarlamudi, 2011; Talmor and Berant, 2019). Recent approaches leverage data synthesis, meta-learning, relation-aware schema encoders, and large-scale encoder pretraining (Yin et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2020; Herzig et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020; Deng et al., 2021). Benchmark datasets such as WIKISQL and SPIDER catalyzed progress but also exhibit artifacts that simplify column mention and limit coverage of multi-column operations (Zhong et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018; Suhr et al., 2020). Consequently, realistic settings require reasoning over structured column types and composing operations over columns rather than aligning phrases to single columns. SQUALL extends WIKITABLEQUESTIONS with SQL annotations and refined types but distributes tables across splits, which weakens out-of-domain evaluation (Shi et al., 2020; Pasupat and Liang, 2015). To address these issues, we repartition SQUALL, evaluate strong neural parsers, and analyze errors in column operations using SEQ2SEQ and SMBOP (Rubin and Berant, 2021; Xu et al., 2017). Our models encode the question and schema jointly and decode programs via either autoregressive sequences or bottom-up trees using BERT (Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova 2019) to initialize the encoder.

document: Recent multilingual translation research suggests that adapting subnetworks for each language or language pair rather than for all of them yields better results. Li et al. (2020) train a shared multilingual model and then select layers for each language pair. Lin et al. (2021) also prune a shared multilingual model for each language pair but operate directly on entries in weight matrices instead. Gong et al. (2021), on the other hand, prune attention heads and feedforward networks for each language. Xie et al. (2021) first identify general and language-specific neurons in a shared multilingual network and then tune those neurons using only the data of their corresponding language. These findings inspire our non-shared pruning setting and motivate us to test whether such results in multilingual translation extend to multilingual pre-training. There are many structured pruning techniques proposed recently for monolingual pre-trained language models. Michel et al. (2019) propose a simple gradient-based importance score to prune attention heads. Prasanna et al. (2020) and Liang et al. (2021) extend this method to prune other components such as the feedforward network of the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017. Wang et al. (2020c) decompose pre-trained model weights and apply L0 regularization (Louizos et al., 2018) to regulate the ranks of the decomposed weights. Sajjad et al. (2020) study layer pruning and show that directly dropping the top layers performs best during fine-tuning. Peer et al. (2021) provide a complementary perspective on structured sparsity for transfer across tasks. The Transformer encoder (Vaswani et al., 2017) pre-trained with the masked language modeling task (Google BERT Devlin et al. (2019)) is the backbone of most existing pre-trained language models. This includes both monolingual and multilingual models (Liu et al., 2019). The Transformer encoder consists of N layers. Each layer contains multihead attention and feedforward networks, followed by residual connections and layer normalization. Following Michel et al. (2019), we write the multihead attention with a gating mechanism over heads. In this formulation, Gh,i indicates whether the i-th head will be pruned. Though not presented in the original Transformer, gating over heads is commonly adopted to introduce structured pruning (Prasanna et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2021). The feedforward network consists of two linear projections with a GeLU activation in between (Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2016). We place an elementwise gating vector over the hidden units to enable pruning. To prune the large embedding matrix E, we decompose it via a low-rank approximation as in Lan et al. (2020). The decomposition writes E as Ê diag(Ge) P, where Ge governs the effective rank. diag(Ge) converts the gating vector to a diagonal matrix that can zero out columns of Ê. A schematic figure illustrates which components will be pruned, including attention heads and hidden units.

Existing methods for taxonomy expansion can be divided into two categories: relying on alignment between multiple taxonomies or relying on machine learning-based rating sub-graphs. The alignment line of work exploits correspondences among resources such as Wikipedia and WordNet (Ruiz-Casado et al., 2005; Toral and Monachini, 2008; Ponzetto and Navigli, 2009; Yamada et al., 2011). Further, the latter category can be divided into two sub-categories, by expanding synonymy relations via Merge or by expanding hypernymy relations via Attach. Synonymy-based taxonomy expansion leverages synonymy relations of the taxonomy. Given a seed taxonomy, distributional approaches discover synonyms by representing strings with distributional features and learning a classifier to predict relations between strings (Nakashole et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019; Fei et al., 2019). In contrast, attach-oriented methods aim to place a new term in a hypernymy hierarchy by evaluating candidate parents with learned scores.

Most recent progress emphasizes hypernymy expansion because it directly grows the hierarchical backbone of the taxonomy. These methods attempt to determine the attachment position by scoring between several nodes using lexical, path, and embedding signals. Early neural models adapted pairwise classification for hypernymy detection into an attachment ranking objective (Shen et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021). Subsequent work incorporated global structural cues such as ancestor consistency and sibling exclusivity to reduce cascading errors. Evidence from pattern-based and distributional models has been shown to be complementary (Weeds et al., 2014; Roller et al., 2018). For a comprehensive overview of hypernymy signals, see (Xiong et al., 2015; Shwartz and Dagan, 2016 along with more recent neural surveys.

Synonymy expansion, often framed as Merge, faces the challenge of polysemy because candidate synonyms may map to multiple senses. Systems mitigate this issue by leveraging context-sensitive embeddings and sense inventories aligned to the target taxonomy (Nakashole et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2019). Joint modeling of Attach and Merge has attracted interest due to coupling between synonym discovery and hypernym placement in lexical resources. In WordNet expansion, the problem is inherently integrated because promoting a synonym into a synset implies constraints on permissible hypernyms. Prior studies have explored iterative refinement where synonym clusters inform candidate parents and vice versa (Shen et al., 2020). However, learning both operations in a single model remains underexplored, especially when supervision is sparse and domain shift is present.

Alignment-based approaches transfer structure by linking nodes across resources and importing edges under reliability thresholds. Such methods benefit from high-precision anchors like interlanguage links or cross-references but may struggle with coverage gaps (Ponzetto and Navigli, 2009). Machine learning approaches, in contrast, can propose attachments for unseen terms using features from corpora, graphs, and pretrained language models (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Recent work integrates textual definitions, glosses, and usage examples to ground candidate placements semantically (Bosc and Vincent, 2018; Loureiro and Jorge, 2019). Graph neural networks propagate evidence across existing hierarchies to regularize local decisions and preserve transitivity (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Vashishth et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the choice of negative sampling and candidate pruning remains a crucial determinant of both efficiency and accuracy.

Training data for taxonomy expansion are often noisy because many corpora lack explicit hypernym cues. Weak supervision and distant signals such as Hearst-like patterns can bootstrap labels but introduce biases toward frequent templates (Hearst, 1992). Curriculum strategies that start with easy attachments and gradually introduce harder cases have been reported to stabilize learning by several studies Jones et al. 1. Evaluation protocols typically measure both local accuracy at the chosen parent and global impact on consistency and cycles. Standard benchmarks include WordNet-derived splits, open-domain term lists, and domain-specific vocabularies for biomedicine and e-commerce (Shen et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Despite progress, generalization to tail entities and unseen domains remains a key limitation motivating robust, cross-domain training regimes.

In this work, we argue for a unified perspective that treats Attach and Merge as mutually reinforcing decisions under shared representations. Our review highlights gaps in joint learning, negative sampling, and evaluation that future research should address. We also clarify assumptions behind alignment-based transfer versus corpus-driven induction to facilitate fair comparisons across settings. The paper positions our approach within the broader literature on lexical semantics, graph learning, and structured prediction. We provide a detailed related study in the Appendix and summarize connections to prior art in Section A. Overall, taxonomy expansion remains a challenging problem whose solutions must balance precision, coverage, and structural soundness.

document:
As mentioned, compression is a widely used strategy to speed up large language models (Gupta and Agrawal, 2020). This involves techniques such as quantization of weights and activations (Bai et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2021), knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2019), pruning and sharing (Gordon et al., 2020), multi-device distribution (Banitalebi-Dehkordi et al., 2021), or combinations thereof (Cheng et al., 2017; Polino et al., 2018). Beyond latency, compression can reduce memory footprint and energy consumption while preserving accuracy for many tasks (Schwartz et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the choice of method depends on hardware constraints, tolerance to accuracy loss, and the training budget (Choi et al., 2018). Recent surveys categorize these approaches by parameter reduction, representation sharing, and numerical precision (Cheng et al., 2017).

Among compression techniques, creating a fixed-size small version of a large model with distillation has been especially popular in recent years. Sanh et al. (2019) introduced DistilBERT, a smaller variant of BERT trained with distillation for general purposes. Another compact variant of BERT was proposed by MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020), which employed inverted bottlenecks and progressive knowledge transfer. TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2020) presented a two-stage transformer distillation for both pre-training and task-specific fine-tuning. Iandola et al. (2020) studied grouped convolutions to design SqueezeBERT for efficient inference. ELM (Jiao et al., 2021), a layer-mapping search framework, was proposed to improve downstream BERT distillation. A more recent method, GhostBERT, employs softmax-normalized one-dimensional convolutions as ghost modules to generate more features with cheap operations (Zhang et al., 2021).

Although compression is effective, it often trades accuracy for efficiency, especially under high compression ratios (Frantar and Alistarh, 2022). Moreover, a separate fixed-size model is typically required for each target computational budget. As an alternative, dynamic inference can adjust computation at test time through early exits or adaptive width and depth (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016; Elbayad et al., 2020). One of the first temporal early-exit strategies was proposed by ReasoNet (Shen et al., 2017), which halts reading when sufficient evidence is found. Similarly, Yu et al. (2018) presented an early stopping method for classification tasks. DeeBERT proposed instance-wise multi-exit decisions using the entropy of the output probability distribution to speed up BERT inference (Xin et al., 2020).

As a length-adaptive method, Kim and Cho (2021) introduced one-shot training of transformers for both sequence- and token-level classification. DynaBERT was proposed to adjust computations by choosing sub-networks of different widths and depths under a unified training regime (Hou et al., 2020). Both Length-Adaptive Transformers and DynaBERT utilized knowledge distillation and data augmentation to improve performance. Depth-adaptive machine translation further demonstrated that selective layer usage can maintain quality while reducing cost (Elbayad et al., 2020). For routing computation, early-exit classifiers and confidence estimation have also been explored in multilingual settings (Scao and Rush, 2021).

Despite their success, many dynamic methods require architectural changes and retraining, which complicates deployment in production environments. Furthermore, most prior work targets encoder-only backbones and classification tasks rather than encoder–decoder architectures and sequence-to-sequence learning. In contrast, our method operates with out-of-the-box pretrained models without retraining and applies to encoder–decoder settings and generative tasks. It also exposes a simple knob for latency control without maintaining multiple checkpoints.

Quantization remains attractive because it offers predictable latency benefits across CPUs and accelerators. For post-training quantization, uniform and per-channel schemes are commonly studied Bai et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020). In contrast, quantization-aware training can recover more accuracy at the cost of additional training iterations (Jacob et al., 2018). Structured pruning targets attention heads and feed-forward blocks to produce hardware-friendly sparsity (Michel et al., 2019; Lagunas et al., 2021). Dynamic token pruning complements early exits by reducing sequence length on the fly (Goyal et al., 2020). Adaptive computation time provides another axis for budgeting, although it is less explored for Transformers (Graves, 2016; Dehghani et al., 2019). Finally, conditional computation via mixture-of-experts can scale capacity without linear cost, but introduces routing overhead and training instability (Shazeer et al., 2017; Fedus et al., 2021). Our study positions these threads within a unified perspective on efficiency, distillation, and dynamic control for modern language models.

document: Data augmentation has been used for resolving artefacts in training datasets before (Gururangan et al., 2018; Srivastava et al., 2020; Kaushik et al., 2021). In particular, previous work (Kaushik et al., 2020) relied on large-scale crowd-sourcing to generate useful augmented data. More recently, Wang and Culotta (2021) investigated the efficacy of automatically generated counterfactuals for sentiment analysis. Similar to our work, these methods consider the most salient features that a model uses when generating augmented data, which aligns with our rationale definition. However, they use sentiment lexicon matching for identifying rationales, which is task-specific and not necessarily fully relevant. In contrast, we employ human annotators to identify rationales, which can be task-agnostic and robust. Moreover, our method generates semi-factuals instead of the counterfactuals used in previous work. Human-in-the-loop machine learning (Wu et al., 2021a) has received increasing research attention. Active learning (Settles, 2009; Margatina et al., 2021), the most common example of human-in-the-loop machine learning, asks annotators to provide high-level labels for informative examples. There is also work exploring more explainable AI systems by exploiting feature-based information. Such methods use relatively simple models such as Naïve Bayes (Stumpf et al., 2009; Kulesza et al., 2015) and linear regression with bag-of-words features (Jia and Liang, 2017; Teso and Kersting, 2019; Ghai et al., 2021; Shao et al., 2021), because these classifiers are intuitive and amenable to incorporating human feedback. Other work uses simple neural networks such as multilayer perceptrons (Shao et al., 2021) and shallow CNNs (Lertvittayakumjorn et al., 2020; Teso et al., 2021) because their predictions can be explained in the form of features. Very recently, Yao et al. (2021) proposed a human-in-the-loop method to inspect more complicated models with the help of model-agnostic post-hoc explanation algorithms Ribeiro et al., 2018) that can explain predictions of any linear or non-linear model without exploiting its weights. However, most prior work focuses on increasing explainability for high-stakes domains such as health and finance, rather than improving robustness or generalization. In addition, many methods assume access to large amounts of labeled data. In contrast, we focus on few-shot learning scenarios that are more compelling for emerging applications. Our approach is motivated by observations that models often latch onto superficial patterns that do not causally support predictions. Data augmentation that targets those patterns can mitigate spurious correlations while preserving task semantics. Counterfactual generation provides one avenue, but it can inadvertently change multiple latent factors at once, as noted in (Kaushik et al., 2020). Semi-factuals, by contrast, selectively alter local evidence while keeping the gold label stable. To identify candidate evidence, we collect human rationales at instance level and aggregate them across annotators. This process yields high-precision spans that guide minimal edits to inputs. We validate these edits through pilot studies that check fluency, label preservation, and domain relevance. Compared to automatic lexicon matching, human-informed edits better capture pragmatic cues and discourse context. We further analyze the interaction between augmentation and active learning. In pool-based settings, query strategies that consider augmentation effects can reduce redundant labeling. We combine uncertainty, diversity, and expected model change to schedule annotation rounds. In each round, annotators label both class and rationale to maintain alignment between explanation and supervision. The resulting dataset supports training objectives that jointly penalize rationale inconsistency and prediction error. For complex models such as transformers, we monitor attribution drift using integrated gradients and LIME (Ribeiro et al., 2016). When drift is detected, we refresh the augmented pool with new semi-factuals that target emerging shortcuts. We also compare against perturbation-based baselines drawn from synonym replacement and back-translation (Wei and Zou, 2019; Sennrich et al., 2016). The baselines improve robustness modestly but often degrade calibration under distribution shift. Prior art has discussed the trade-off between edit minimality and label fidelity in data augmentation. Building on these insights, our contribution is a general framework that unifies rationale collection, semi-factual editing, and active curation. We anticipate that this framework will transfer to other tasks such as toxicity detection, stance classification, and natural language inference.
