document: Style transfer datasets are typically curated under two regimes: nonparallel and parallel. When collecting nonparallel corpora, style labels often already exist in the data, such as sentiment, formality, and toxicity. In practice, style labels can be inferred from the source domain, for example Twitter, academic writing, or legal filings, which reduces collection to scraping texts at scale. For sentiment, large review platforms provide abundant labeled data because users explicitly rate products and services, as noted by Li et al. 1. Consequently, corpus size is bounded more by licensing and deduplication constraints than by annotation costs. Conversely, parallel corpora for style transfer are scarce and costly to build. A notable exception is the Bible corpus, which offers multiple translations aligned at the verse level (Carlson et al., 2018). Another semi-natural source is the biased-to-neutral Wikipedia corpus that exploits edit histories to derive parallel rewrites (Pryzant et al., 2020). Beyond such special cases, parallel datasets are typically created from scratch via crowdsourcing and expert review. The widely used GYAFC dataset pairs informal sentences with expert-edited formal rewrites (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). Building similar resources for toxicity mitigation is challenging because toxic–neutral pairs rarely arise organically. Following prior practice, researchers recruit crowd workers to produce rewrites and then verify quality with additional reviewers (Jiang et al., 2020). To reduce costs, some pipelines replace expert adjudication with majority-vote crowd validation while monitoring inter-annotator agreement (Artstein and Poesio, 2008). A common risk in such pipelines is content preservation drift, where rewritten sentences inadvertently change meaning (Xu et al., 2012). Recent work addresses this risk by adding automatic semantic similarity checks and lexical constraints during generation (Zhang et al., 2020). In addition to human rewrites, parallel data can be synthesized through back-translation or pivoting between attribute-conditioned models (Prabhumoye et al., 2018). However, synthetic pairs often amplify artifacts and teach models to exploit shortcuts rather than learn disentangled representations (Elazar and Goldberg, 2018). For nonparallel settings, disentanglement approaches rely on adversarial training, attribute classifiers, and content reconstruction losses (Shen et al., 2017;Fu et al., 2018). Retrieval-augmented approaches instead locate semantically similar sentences with target attributes to guide rewriting (Li et al., 2018b). Control through minimal edits has also been explored to better preserve content while flipping style markers (Sudhakar et al., 2019). Evaluation remains contentious, with automatic metrics correlating imperfectly with human judgments across fluency, style strength, and content preservation (Mir et al., 2019). Recent benchmarks emphasize multi-attribute control and robust generalization across domains and lengths (Riley et al., 2021). Nevertheless, many datasets underrepresent long, multi-clause sentences typical of professional or legal writing, limiting external validity. Domain provenance further matters because stylistic markers vary with genre, register, and platform-specific conventions (Biber and Conrad, 2009). Consequently, dataset documentation should record collection windows, platform policies, and filtering rules to support reproducibility (Bender and Friedman, 2018). Ethical considerations include potential harm from releasing toxic text and the risk of misuse for evasion or targeted harassment (Vidgen et al., 2020). Mitigation strategies combine redaction, access controls, and bias audits for demographic disparities in labeling (Sap et al., 2019). Parallel datasets must also specify licenses for both sources and annotations to enable legally compliant redistribution (Nielsen, 2011). For multilingual style transfer, script variation and code-switching complicate tokenization and alignment, motivating language-specific preprocessing (Winata et al., 2019). Several studies report that pretraining on large raw corpora before fine-tuning on style transfer improves fluency without sacrificing control (Lample et al., 2019). Still, domain shift between pretraining data and target applications can hurt attribute accuracy, suggesting the need for in-domain adaptation (Gururangan et al., 2020). Recent datasets also include paragraph-level or document-level rewrites that preserve discourse coherence and coreference patterns (Jhamtani et al., 2017). To support such longer contexts, curators often include outlines and meta-information that constrain acceptable rewrites during annotation (Rei et al., 2020). A persistent open question is how to balance diversity with consistency so that models do not overfit to a narrow set of lexical cues (Krishna et al., 2020). Comparative studies suggest that training with contrastive objectives and hard negative pairs can improve content preservation in parallel and nonparallel regimes (He et al., 2020). Looking forward, we advocate standardized datasheets, transparent licensing, and multi-attribute test suites to advance reliable, equitable style transfer research, as also argued by Shen et al.

document:
Fine-tuning of Language Models. Better finetuning of language models can be achieved by proper initialization (Dodge et al., 2020), regularization or prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Other tricks include bias correction in optimizer and re-initialization of top layers in Transformer (Zhang et al., 2021). Instead of fine-tuning all parameters in a model, other work explore only learning a few vectors (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Guo et al., 2021) or a few additional parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019). Hallucination Methods. Feature Hallucination of examples is first introduced for visual recognition (Hariharan and Girshick, 2017) by metalearning (Wang et al., 2018), variational inference (Luo et al., 2021; Lazarou et al., 2022), and adversarial learning (Tjio et al., 2022). Label Hallucination (Jian and Torresani, 2022) assigns soft pseudo-labels for unlabelled images to extend the fine-tuning few-shot dataset. Few-shot Language Models. Few-shot learning has been successfully applied to machine translation (Arthaud et al., 2021), abstract summarizing (Fabbri et al., 2021), question and answering (Hua et al., 2020; Ram et al., 2021), and entity recognition (de Lichy et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2021), by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Wei et al., 2021; Wei and Zou, 2019; Karimi et al., 2021b), and prompts (Gao et al., 2021; Tam et al., 2021).

Our method is a generative data augmentation method in the embedding space. Unlike Wei et al. (2021), which uses EDA (Wei and Zou, 2019) to augment examples at the discrete input space, we hallucinate auxiliary examples at the embedding space. Our method shares similarity to FDA (Kumar et al., 2019), which is also a generative data augmentation method, but at the feature space. Also, different from FDA which is focused on two intent classification tasks, our method can be applied to a wide-range of NLP task as shown by our experiments on 15 diverse tasks. Fine-tuning strategies increasingly prioritize parameter efficiency to reduce computational and storage costs without sacrificing accuracy (Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Adapter layers enable task transfer while keeping the pretrained backbone fixed, mitigating catastrophic forgetting (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Prompt tuning and prefix tuning instead steer model behavior through learned inputs, which can be advantageous in low-resource regimes (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). Despite progress, practitioners still report instability across random seeds and domains, motivating robust augmentation pipelines (Dodge et al., 2020). Data augmentation in NLP ranges from rule-based edits to model-based paraphrasing, yet the trade-off between diversity and label preservation remains challenging (Feng et al., 2021). Embedding-space augmentation aims to navigate this trade-off by perturbing continuous representations while respecting task semantics (Sohn et al., 2020). Generative approaches further synthesize features or examples that expand decision boundaries and improve generalization under distribution shift (Kumar et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020). In contrast to token-level noising, our approach composes semantically coherent exemplars that align with task-specific manifolds in representation space (Chen et al., 2020). Prior work on few-shot classification in vision has demonstrated the benefits of hallucinating additional features from limited support sets (Hariharan and Girshick, 2017; Wang et al., 2018). Analogous ideas have been explored for text through back-translation, span replacement, and paraphrase mining with varying degrees of success (Wei and Zou, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2021). However, uncontrolled surface edits may distort label-defining cues, especially in tasks with fine-grained semantics such as intent and relation classification (Karimi et al., 2021b). Our formulation treats augmentation as learning a conditional generator over embeddings that respects task constraints derived from labeled exemplars. We study the interaction between augmentation intensity and finetuning dynamics to identify regimes that yield consistent gains across tasks. In ablations, we compare to strong baselines including EDA, back-translation, and MixUp-inspired interpolation in embedding space (Zhang et al., 2018; Wei and Zou, 2019). We also evaluate compatibility with adapter-based tuning and soft prompts to demonstrate orthogonality to parameter-efficient methods (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021). For comprehensive coverage, we consider text classification, natural language inference, paraphrase detection, and token classification spanning 15 benchmarks. Our experiments include cross-domain setups to assess robustness to covariate shift and label imbalance. The results indicate consistent improvements in low-data regimes and competitive performance in medium-data settings relative to task-specific tuning. We observe that augmentation in representation space yields more stable training and reduced variance across random seeds. In (Radford et al., 2019), we situate our approach alongside pretrained generative models to discuss complementarity in resource-constrained adaptation. Finally, we outline open challenges concerning controllability, faithfulness, and calibration that warrant further investigation (Kumar et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2020). Together, these findings motivate embedding-space hallucination as a practical and extensible component in modern fine-tuning pipelines.

document: There have been a number of attempts at compressing Transformer models, which can be broadly categorized into three directions. The first line of work focuses on redundancy in model parameters. Structure pruning removes coherent groups of weights to preserve the network layout (McCarley, 2019; Michel et al., 2019; Voita et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019b; Fan et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021a). In addition, various distillation techniques train compact models to reproduce a larger model’s outputs (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020b). Other strategies include weight quantization and weight sharing (Bhandare et al., 2019; Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2020; Dehghani et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2019). The second line of work targets the quadratic cost of self-attention with respect to sequence length. This complexity is the main efficiency bottleneck and is prohibitive for long sequences. One approach sparsifies attention by restricting each token to a subset of neighbors, as in Big Bird and Longformer (Zaheer et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). In (Wang et al., 2020a; Choromanski et al., 2020), kernel approximations reduce the quadratic complexity to linear while preserving accuracy. The third direction explores redundancy in maintaining full token-level representations across encoders (Dai et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021b; Pietruszka et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2021; Kim and Cho, 2020). State-of-the-art classifiers often rely on a single embedding such as the CLS token rather than full sequences. Dai et al. (2020) apply simple strided mean pooling to compress tokens and propose pretraining to balance speed and accuracy. We instead aim to avoid costly pretraining and rely on lightweight fine-tuning. Wu et al. (2021b define a centroid attention operator that computes embeddings for selected centroids obtained by random sampling or pooling. This operator yields similar token-reduction behavior to strided pooling but offers learnable selection via attention. Because several related methods either use non-standard datasets or require expensive reinforcement learning policies, we restrict experiments to approaches that improve both training and inference, and we do not compare to vision-specific pooling models (Pan et al., 2021; Heo et al., 2021).

document: Continual dialogue learning seeks to endow conversational agents with the ability to accumulate skills across tasks and domains. Neural dialogue models have progressed rapidly due to large-scale corpora and pretrained encoders. Early sequence-to-sequence and retrieval architectures demonstrated that data-driven systems can acquire basic conversational abilities (Mou et al., 2016; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). Beyond fluency, recent work targets controllable traits such as persona consistency and empathy (Qian et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018b; Zhou et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019). However, transferring such skills across tasks remains challenging under continual updates. Catastrophic forgetting arises when parameters optimized for a new objective overwrite knowledge needed for earlier tasks (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017). At the same time, naive multitask training may conflate task-specific cues and degrade generalization (Caruana, 1997). The inductive transfer perspective highlights mechanisms that enable reuse of representations while isolating interference (Ruder, 2019). Under this view, methods fall into feature-based reuse, end-to-end fine-tuning, and lightweight parameterization via adapters (Ruder, 2019). Adapter-based transfer learns small task-specific modules inserted into frozen transformers to control capacity and sharing (Houlsby et al., 2019; Poth et al., 2021. Such modules can be composed, routed, or sparsely activated to balance specialization and reuse (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021). In dialogue, adapters promise a compact means to accumulate skills while preserving prior behaviors across updates. Prior work explored persona and empathy conditioning through specialized components trained on curated datasets (Qian et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019). Yet, most studies evaluate in isolated settings rather than under realistic continual training regimes. We study how adapterized architectures can support sequential acquisition of dialogue skills with minimal forgetting. Our approach separates general conversational knowledge from task-specific adapters that capture style, goal, or grounding signals. Knowledge is routed through a small number of shared layers to encourage transfer without entanglement. We compare adapter-based continual learning with full fine-tuning and prompt-based baselines across multiple dialogue tasks. Fine-tuning can yield strong single-task performance but often erases earlier capabilities during sequential updates (Zaremba and Sutskever, 2014). Prompt-based tuning offers parameter efficiency but may struggle to retain precise behaviors under distribution shift (Lester et al., 2021). In contrast, modular adapters provide explicit loci for preservation and growth when tasks arrive over time. We build on designs that place down-projection and up-projection bottlenecks inside transformer blocks (Houlsby et al., 2019). To assess transfer, we analyze cross-task performance when adapters are switched or composed at inference time (Pfeiffer et al., 2021). We also consider memory-aware regularization and rehearsal buffers as complementary mechanisms to reduce drift (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; Chaudhry et al., 2019). For fair comparison, all methods use the same pretrained backbone and tokenization pipeline. We adopt a training schedule with short phases and periodic evaluation on held-out tasks to quantify forgetting. Our experiments cover chit-chat, empathetic response generation, goal-oriented slot filling, and knowledge-grounded dialogue. Informed by prior analysis of parameter-efficient NLP, we hypothesize that adapters achieve the best stability–plasticity balance in this setting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; He et al., 2022). We further probe whether adapter initialization from earlier tasks accelerates learning on new tasks through positive forward transfer (Ammar et al., 2014). Related systems have examined adapter composition for multitask learning, though not under strict continual constraints (Stickland and Murray, 2019; Pilault et al., 2021). In contrast to earlier adapter baselines for dialogue, we explicitly evaluate interference and transfer signals across sequences of tasks. Madotto et al. (2020) investigated adapters for conversational skills without measuring cross-task interference or systematic transfer. Our contributions include a principled evaluation protocol, ablations over adapter placement, and analysis of knowledge partitioning for continual dialogue learning.

document: Since Rashkin et al. (2019) release EmpatheticDialogues, many approaches have been proposed to generate empathetic responses. Lin et al. (2019) propose mixture of emotional experts. Majumder et al. (2020) propose emotion grouping, emotion mimicry and stochastic sampling. Li et al. (2020) extract emotional words through lexicon and propose an adversarial generative model. Shen et al. (2021) apply dual-learning with unpaired data for the bidirectional empathy. Gao et al. (2021) integrate emotion cause into response generation process through gated mechanism. Sabour et al. (2021); Li et al. (2022) use implicit commonsense for context modelling. Kim et al. (2021) train a model to extract words that cause the speaker's emotion and attach RSA Framework (Frank and Goodman, 2012) to any generative models to generate responses, focusing on emotion cause words. Recently, many studies have shown remarkable improvements through recognizing transitions of features between utterances in open-domain multiturn dialogues. Qiu et al. (2020) perceive transitions of emotion states for context modelling. Zou et al. (2021) propose a module to manage keyword transitions. Zhan et al. (2021) model external knowledge transitions to select a knowledge used for generation. In multi-turn empathetic dialogues, we consider emotions, keywords, and utterancelevel meaning (Gu et al., 2021) as important features of each utterance and propose a novel approach of recognizing feature transitions between utterances. Beyond single-turn cues, personalization influences how systems prioritize supportive content (Chan et al., 2019). Persona-grounded dialogue models integrate speaker profiles to modulate affective wording (Zheng et al., 2020). Knowledge-enhanced methods leverage commonsense and situational graphs to infer latent needs (Zhou et al., 2020). Several datasets have been widely adopted (Rashkin et al., 2019; Li et al. 2020) for benchmarking empathetic response generation. Despite progress, models often conflate empathy with sentiment polarity (Busso et al., 2008; Rashkin et al., 2019). Evaluations typically rely on automatic metrics that poorly capture supportive intent (Liu et al., 2016; Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). Human studies suggest that transparency about reasoning improves perceived empathy (Hancock et al., 2020). Multi-task learning binds emotion recognition to response generation with shared encoders (Zahiri and Choi, 2017; Lin et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives encourage separation between supportive and dismissive candidates (Gao et al., 2021). Planning-based approaches first predict intents and then realize surface forms (Yao et al., 2020). However, intent schemas often fail when context shifts abruptly across turns (Chen et al., 2021). Discourse-aware encoders model long-range dependencies across utterances with structured attention (Zhang et al., 2020). Turn-level memory networks track evolving needs of the speaker over extended contexts (Sukhbaatar et al., 2015; Kumar et al., 2016). Hierarchical transformers remain the dominant backbone due to scalability and transferability (Zhang et al., 2021). Few-shot adaptation is increasingly important for deployment in novel domains with limited annotations (Perez et al., 2021). Domain-adaptive pretraining on conversational corpora yields notable gains for empathy recognition (Gururangan et al., 2020). Safety and sensitivity constraints complicate optimization objectives for empathetic responses (Gehman et al., 2020). Researchers have proposed controllable generation to regulate affirmation, advice, and reframing moves (Keskar et al., 2019; Yang and Klein, 2021). Nevertheless, balancing specificity with a nonjudgmental tone remains challenging for current systems. In this work, we build on these insights and motivate a framework that unifies feature transition modeling with intention planning.

document: Examples of the cross-modal learning paradigm. As described in Section 2.1, many existing methods for cross-modal learning adopt modality-independent encoders. This paradigm achieves state-of-the-art retrieval on benchmark datasets across the modality pairs considered in this work: video–text (Bain et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021), video–audio (Monfort et al., 2021; Rouditchenko et al., 2020), and image–audio (Harwath et al., 2018; Harwath et al., 2020). Although prior works vary in pre-training data, architectures, and objectives, they all employ modality-independent encoders. A key benefit of this paradigm is fixed inference time for retrieval. Because encoders operate independently, embeddings for items in one modality can be computed without any paired inputs. Retrieval then reduces to dot products between modality-specific embeddings. This property supports large-scale retrieval and enables reuse of unimodal embeddings for downstream tasks. Other cross-modal learning frameworks depart from this design. Some methods embed cross-modal relations within the encoders rather than keeping them independent. In (Monfort et al., 2021), this has been realized with cross-modal encoders (Lei et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021) and with cross-modal attention mechanisms (Miech et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019a; Liu et al., 2019b; Gabeur et al., 2020). However, such interactions increase retrieval complexity because each instance in one modality must be paired with every instance in the other to compute embeddings. With m and n samples, the complexity grows from O(m + n) to O(mn). These interactions also complicate analysis of unimodal representations and hinder single-modality downstream tasks. Our framework preserves light-weight retrieval via independent encoders while enabling cross-modal interaction through a learned codebook and a Cross-Modal Code Matching objective. Uncovering semantic-level correspondences is a central motivation. Image–audio models discover spoken words and visual objects without supervision via retrieval tasks (Synnaeve et al., 2014; Harwath and Glass, 2015; Harwath et al., 2017; Kamper et al., 2018), and the resulting audio embeddings cluster into word-like units (Harwath and Glass, 2017; Wang and Hasegawa-Johnson, 2019; Harwath et al., 2020). Video–audio models can relate spoken words to visual objects and actions (Boggust et al., 2019; Rouditchenko et al., 2020), and our approach extends these ideas to video–text by learning a shared, modality-invariant space.

document: Research efforts on monolingual ED are extensive and varied. Hand-crafted, feature-based, languagespecific methods were the basis of early ED approaches (Ahn, 2006;Ji and Grishman, 2008;Patwardhan and Riloff, 2009;Liao and Grishman, 2010a,b;Hong et al., 2011;McClosky et al., 2011;Li et al., 2013;Miwa et al., 2014;Yang and Mitchell, 2016). More recent efforts have primarily made use of deep learning techniques such as convolutional neural networks (Nguyen and Grishman, 2015;Chen et al., 2015;Nguyen et al., 2016b), recurrent neural networks (Nguyen et al., 2016a;Sha et al., 2018;Nguyen andNguyen, 2019), graph convolutional networks (Nguyen andGrishman, 2018a;Yan et al., 2019), adversarial networks (Hong et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2019b), and pre-trained language models (Wadden et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2019a;Yang et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2020). Despite these advances, monolingual systems often rely on abundant annotated corpora. This reliance limits portability to domains and languages with scarce supervision. Feature representation has shifted from sparse indicators to contextual embeddings derived from transformers. Such embeddings capture long-range dependencies crucial for modeling event triggers and arguments. However, architectural choices interact with training data scale and task-specific objectives in nontrivial ways. Works on cross-lingual ED are not as prevalent and generally make use of cross-lingual resources employed to address the differences between languages such as bilingual dictionaries or parallel corpora (Muis et al., 2018;Liu et al., 2019) and, more recently, pre-trained multilingual language models (M'hamdi et al., 2019;Hambardzumyan et al., 2020;Majewska et al., 2021). Cross-lingual ED seeks to transfer knowledge from resource-rich to low-resource languages without extensive annotation. Projection-based methods align annotations across parallel texts but are sensitive to alignment errors. Dictionary-based methods reduce vocabulary gaps yet struggle with polysemy and morphology. Pre-trained multilingual encoders provide shared subword spaces that facilitate zero-shot transfer. Nevertheless, tokenization granularity and script diversity still induce performance disparities. Unlike these previous efforts, our method leverages unlabeled data to further refine the language-invariant qualities of the language models. Adversarial Language Adaptation, inspired by models in domain adaptation research (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015;Naik and Rose, 2020), has been successfuly applied at generating languageinvariant models (Joty et al., 2017;Chen et al., 2018). Our method improves upon these approaches optimizing the adversarial training process by selecting the most informative examples from the unlabeled data. We hypothesize that hard examples sampled from unlabeled corpora better expose spurious correlations in the encoder. By iteratively updating the discriminator and the sampler, we encourage the encoder to suppress language-specific cues. For sequence-to-sequence formulations of ED, T5-style models provide a flexible scaffolding for generation Raffel et al., 2019). This strategy reduces overfitting to high-resource typologies and leads to more stable transfer. Complementary to adversarial learning, consistency regularization across stochastic augmentations further enforces invariance. While prior ED work has explored data augmentation, few studies explicitly target cross-lingual consistency constraints. In addition, multilingual pre-training objectives are often generic and not tailored to event structure. To bridge this gap, we incorporate trigger-aware masking that biases the encoder toward event-relevant contexts. We also adopt lightweight adapters to decouple language-specific and task-specific parameters within the shared backbone. Recent evidence suggests that prompt-based conditioning can align label semantics across languages more effectively than task-specific heads. Nonetheless, purely generative decoding may hallucinate triggers when retrieval cues are weak. To mitigate this risk, we combine span extraction with constrained generation under a joint objective. Additional examples of downstream applications of cross-lingual learning are document classification (Holger and Xian, 2018), named entity recognition (Xie et al., 2018) and part-of-speech tagging (Cohen et al., 2011). For a thorough review on cross-lingual learning, we refer the reader to Pikuliak et al. (2021).

Early computational models of visual attention proposed bottom-up mechanisms for pre-attentive selection from visual input (Koch and Ullman, 1985). The notion of a central saliency map subsequently formalized how conspicuity guides gaze across scenes (Niebur and Koch, 1996). A long-standing hypothesis, often referred to as Yarbus’s theorem, holds that task demands shape fixation patterns (Yarbus, 1967). Empirical tests have yielded mixed evidence for this claim across tasks and datasets (Greene et al., 2012; Henderson et al., 2013; Borji and Itti, 2014). With deep learning, features from pre-trained convolutional backbones combined with learned readouts now dominate saliency prediction benchmarks (Kümmerer et al., 2016). These advances have enabled modeling of complex cross-modal gaze behaviors in captioning, question answering, and referring expressions (Sugano and Bulling, 2016; Das et al., 2016; Vasudevan et al., 2018). Nevertheless, many models emphasize image statistics while underrepresenting top-down, goal-driven components of attention (Borji and Itti, 2014). Bridging bottom-up and top-down factors remains a central challenge for general-purpose saliency modeling (Henderson et al., 2013).

Predicting gaze during reading has a parallel history that emphasizes linguistic and cognitive constraints on eye movements (Rayner, 1998). Classical approaches formulate saccade generation probabilistically using lexical, syntactic, and positional features (Feng, 2006; Hara et al., 2012; Matthies and Søgaard, 2013; Hahn and Keller, 2016). Alternatives treat scanning as token-to-token transitions over structured text graphs (Nilsson and Nivre, 2009; Haji-Abolhassani and Clark, 2014; Coutrot et al., 2017). Recent work leverages contextualized language representations as predictors of fixation probabilities and durations (Sood et al., 2020a). These models raise important questions about psychological plausibility and the mapping between internal states and observed gaze (Hahn and Keller, 2016). In addition, domain shift between curated corpora and in-the-wild reading can degrade prediction quality (Matthies and Søgaard, 2013). Joint modeling of text difficulty, reader proficiency, and task has emerged as a promising direction for robust inference (Barrett et al., 2018). However, few studies compare like-for-like reading of the same material under distinct task demands at scale (Wenzel et al., 2017).

Beyond analysis, gaze is increasingly used to supervise or regularize NLP models for downstream tasks (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015a). In sequence labeling, auxiliary losses that encourage human-like attention distributions can improve generalization (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015b; Barrett et al., 2018). Supervision from fixation times has aided sentence compression and summarization by steering models toward informative tokens (Klerke et al., 2016). Similar strategies yield gains for sentiment analysis when attention aligns with affect-bearing expressions, as shown in (Mishra et al., 2017). Work on reading comprehension integrates scanpaths to calibrate passage highlighting and question focus (Malmaud et al., 2020). Some studies also report benefits in multimodal tasks where eye movements anchor alignments between words and visual regions (Das et al., 2016). Despite these advances, many efforts repurpose free-viewing data without tailoring collection to the target task (Coutrot et al., 2017). This mismatch motivates experimental designs that manipulate goals, incentives, and time pressure to elicit task-specific gaze patterns (Hillen et al., 2013).

Moving from natural to task-specific reading, researchers combine eye tracking with neuroimaging to study control mechanisms (Wenzel et al., 2017). EEG and fMRI reveal distinct signatures for syntactic parsing, semantic integration, and anomaly detection during reading (Choi et al., 2014; Hillen et al., 2013). Complementary work localizes attention to text segments most diagnostic for identifying entities, relations, and discourse links (Loboda et al., 2011; Wenzel et al., 2017). Comparisons between natural reading and pseudo-reading clarify how lexical semantics and syntax constrain fixation sequences (Hillen et al., 2013; Choi et al., 2014). However, comprehensive evaluations across classical NLP tasks using matched materials are still scarce (Barrett et al., 2018). Our perspective is that targeted collection protocols are necessary to characterize goal-dependent gaze strategies. Such protocols should standardize stimuli, balance difficulty, and measure compliance to reduce confounds (Rayner, 1998). They should also make datasets publicly available with aligned text, tokenization, and robust metadata for reuse (Sood et al., 2020a). These steps would enable principled tests of whether task-driven reading yields predictable and exploitable differences in fixation behavior.

document: Few-shot learning is often tackled by meta learning (Li and Zhang, 2021; Bansal et al., 2020; Sharaf et al., 2020), data augmentation (Arthaud et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2019), and prompt-based fine-tuning (Gao et al., 2021; Tam et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Basu et al. (2021) apply contrastive learning in semi-supervised intent classification by enforcing consistency across augmented views. Despite promising results, many approaches rely on large auxiliary corpora that blur the boundary between few-shot and fully supervised settings (Perez et al., 2021; Min et al., 2022). This paper examines how to reduce such reliance while preserving strong inductive biases through lightweight adaptation. Meta-learning methods aim to learn initialization or metrics that generalize across tasks, exemplified by gradient-based frameworks and prototypical classifiers (Finn et al., 2017; Snell et al., 2017). However, their episodic training can be mismatched to downstream fine-tuning regimes, leading to brittle performance when task distributions shift (Chen et al., 2019; Triantafillou et al., 2020). Data augmentation offers complementary benefits by expanding support around scarce examples through paraphrasing, back-translation, and hallucinated labels (Xie et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2019). Yet, synthetic data often amplifies artifacts and label noise, necessitating robust objectives and sample selection heuristics (Wei and Zou, 2019; Wu et al., 2021). Prompt-based tuning reformulates classification as masked token prediction, which can align pretraining and downstream objectives to improve data efficiency (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Nevertheless, prompts can be brittle across domains, and verbalizer choices interact subtly with class priors and calibration (Holtzman et al. 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Several studies combine these strands by coupling prompts with meta-learned initializations or contrastive regularizers to stabilize gradients in the low-data regime (Lester et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021). In parallel, retrieval-augmented learners exploit external corpora to ground predictions, but can inadvertently leak label semantics when not carefully filtered (Guu et al., 2020; Khandelwal et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols further complicate comparisons, as hyperparameter search budgets and choice of validation episodes significantly affect outcomes (Musgrave et al., 2020; Dodge et al., 2019). To mitigate variance, recent benchmarks advocate fixed seeds, stratified episode sampling, and pre-registered ablation plans (Rahimi and Recht, 2017; Bouthillier et al., 2021). Cross-lingual few-shot transfer introduces additional challenges due to script diversity and morphological richness, which strain subword tokenizers and degrade alignment (Hu et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2020). For structured prediction, span-based learners and constrained decoders show promise but demand careful calibration to prevent degenerate spans in extremely low-resource settings (Straková et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021). We build on these insights by proposing a simple training recipe that integrates instance reweighting with curriculum-based prompt selection. Our approach emphasizes reproducibility through transparent reporting of data provenance, search space, and compute costs (Henderson et al., 2018; Schwartz et al., 2020). In contrast to prior work that tunes dozens of prompt templates, we adopt a small, human-audited pool and learn a distribution over candidates. We also analyze failure modes across domains with controlled perturbations to quantify sensitivity to spurious cues and annotation artifacts. Ablations isolate the contribution of reweighting, augmentation, and prompt selection, revealing complementary gains and reduced variance across seeds. Finally, we situate our results relative to open-set generalization and semi-supervised learning to clarify scope and limitations (Oliver et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020). Taken together, our findings suggest that careful procedural choices can close a large fraction of the gap to fully supervised baselines at modest computational cost. We release code, data cards, and reproducible scripts to facilitate standardized comparisons in future research.

Multilingual Pre-Training mBERT and XLM-R train multilingual MLM without using any parallel corpus and show strong cross-lingual ability. mBERT is an extension of BERT which is pre-trained on Wikipedia data over 100 languages to learn a language-invariant feature space shared across multiple languages. XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020a) is trained on 2.5T data over 100 languages extracted from Common Crawl (Wenzek et al., 2020), which demonstrates the effect of the model trained on a large-scale corpus. Results of XLM-R on a large number of downstream cross-lingual tasks show that a large-scale training corpus can significantly improve the performance of multilingual models.

Other methods use parallel corpus in multilingual pre-training. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces a Translation Language Model (TLM) based on the parallel corpus which shows significant improvement on downstream tasks. Unicoder (Huang et al., 2019) introduces a multitask learning framework to learn cross-lingual representations with monolingual and parallel corpora, achieving further gains. ALM (Yang et al., 2020) allows the model to learn cross-lingual code-switch sentences, enhancing the transfer ability. Recent studies INFOXLM (Chi et al., 2021), HICTL , VECO and ERNIE-M (Ouyang et al., 2021) use contrastive learning, back translation and other tricks further enhancing the performance of the multilingual model. Our work focus on studying the model only with multilingual MLM, and leave the study of works with parallel data as future work.

Probing Multilingual MLM mBERT and XLM-R have successfully achieved excellent crosslingual transfer performance without using any parallel corpus. Researchers have wondered what the source of this cross-lingual ability is. Pires et al., 2019) examines the zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on NER (Pan et al., 2017) and part-ofspeech (POS) tagging. They believe this success comes from the shared anchor words between languages. Not coincidentally, similar conclusion is reached by (Wu and Dredze, 2019). However, this conclusion is proven inaccurate by (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020). Their experiments show that the model still learns cross-lingual transfer ability on the corpus without anchor words at all. Besides, (Conneau et al., 2020b;K et al., 2020;Artetxe et al., 2020;Libovický et al., 2020;Muller et al., 2021) have analyzed the cross-lingual ability of multilingual masked language models in terms of language similarity, shared model parameters cross languages, model structure, training objectives, language marker. The results suggest that structure similarity and shared parameters between languages are crucial for cross-lingual transfer. In this paper, we focus on analyzing language struc-ture. We decompose it into constituent order, composition, and word co-occurrence and study the effect of each part separately. We begin by situating our study within prior analyses of typological distance and lexical overlap. Cross-lingual transfer has been shown to degrade when word order diverges substantially between source and target languages. Yet, disentangling order from morphology remains challenging in massively multilingual encoders. To address this, we design probing datasets that systematically permute constituent order while holding lexical content constant. We complement these probes with measurements of mutual information between tokens under different masking regimes. Our experiments consider families with contrasting basic word orders, including SVO, SOV, and VSO languages. We further analyze the role of subword segmentation by comparing shared and language-specific tokenizers. In addition, we assess whether representational alignment arises primarily in deeper layers or is already present near the embedding layer. Following standard practice, we report zero-shot and translate-train baselines alongside language-balanced fine-tuning. We also control for domain effects by sampling newswire, encyclopedic, and web forum corpora for evaluation. Finally, we ablate pretraining data size to examine whether structural generalization scales with additional multilingual text. These analyses clarify how architectural bias and training signals interact to yield robust cross-lingual structure sharing.

document: Non-autoregressive TTS has advanced rapidly in recent years. These systems aim to synthesize natural speech with high efficiency and strong fidelity by eliminating autoregressive decoding. A typical non-autoregressive pipeline maps text to acoustic features or waveforms without factorizing output probabilities across time. Early representatives such as FastSpeech (Ren et al., 2019) and ParaNet (Peng et al., 2019) depend on knowledge distillation from an autoregressive teacher. Subsequent models including FastPitch (Lancucki, 2021), AlignTTS (Zeng et al., 2020), and FastSpeech 2 (Ren et al., 2021) remove the reliance on teacher models while improving controllability. Our work adopts FastSpeech 2 as the backbone non-autoregressive synthesizer. FastSpeech 2 replaces teacher-forced duration targets with supervised alignment derived from frame-to-phoneme boundaries under a mean squared error objective. In addition, it introduces explicit predictors for pitch and energy at the encoder side, trained with ground-truth contours and amplitude statistics. These auxiliary predictors inject prosodic cues that enhance naturalness and expressiveness in the synthesized speech. Beyond architectural choices, linguistic context from large language models has emerged as a complementary source of prosody information. Prior studies argue that prosody can be inferred from lexical and syntactic cues in both the current utterance and its neighbors, as argued by Shen et al. 2018. Such contextual signals are naturally captured by pre-trained LMs like BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Several works have integrated BERT embeddings at the word or subword level into autoregressive TTS pipelines to improve pronunciation, disambiguation, and phrasing (Shen et al., 2018; Fang et al., 2019). Recent studies extend this idea to non-autoregressive settings by conditioning duration, pitch, and energy predictors on contextualized features. Others exploit chunked or paired sentence patterns derived from BERT to inject discourse-level information into the acoustic encoder. Graph-based encoders that use BERT vectors as node features have been proposed to capture word-to-word relations that correlate with emphasis and rhythm. In parallel, variational autoencoders provide a principled mechanism to model residual prosodic variability beyond deterministic predictors. VAEs introduce latent variables intended to capture style, speaking rate, or emphasis that are not fully determined by text. Training maximizes a variational lower bound on the data likelihood that trades off reconstruction fidelity and prior regularization. The reparameterization trick yields low-variance gradients that enable end-to-end optimization with stochastic latent sampling. Practical techniques such as KL annealing, free bits, and β-weighting help balance reconstruction quality against posterior collapse. For TTS, conditioning the VAE on phonetic, lexical, and speaker attributes improves disentanglement and controllability. When combined with duration-based regulators in non-autoregressive models, latent prosody variables can reduce timing errors and stabilize training. However, improper weighting of the KL term or overly expressive decoders may still lead to degenerate latents that fail to carry prosodic information. Recent advances introduce hierarchical priors, normalizing-flow posteriors, or vector-quantized bottlenecks to preserve informative latents under strong decoders. Building on these insights, we propose a conditional VAE module that operates over FastSpeech 2 encoder states to capture residual prosody. We further incorporate BERT-derived context features to condition both the prior and the decoder, aligning latent structure with linguistic cues (Devlin et al., 2019). Together, these components yield a unified framework that targets efficient synthesis, context-aware expressiveness, and robust prosody control.

document: We investigate overfitting that emerges when pruning is applied under the pretrain-and-finetune paradigm and propose sparse progressive distillation to mitigate it. Our approach unifies parameter sparsification with staged knowledge transfer to preserve generalization while reducing compute. We demonstrate the benefits of the framework through targeted ablation studies that isolate the effect of each component. We validate the method on six datasets from the GLUE benchmark spanning diverse linguistic phenomena. To assess cross-task applicability, we include both single-sentence and sentence-pair classification settings. Experimental results show that our models outperform strong baselines by a substantial margin under matched training budgets. Network pruning has long been studied as a means to reduce inference cost without degrading accuracy. In (Han et al., 2015, 2016) the foundational line of work on magnitude pruning and compression was established. The lottery ticket hypothesis suggests that sparse subnetworks can match the accuracy of dense models when trained in isolation (Frankle and Carbin, 2018). Subsequent empirical comparisons indicate that large sparse models often outperform small dense models when parameter counts are matched (Zhu and Gupta, 2018). However, directly applying pruning after large-scale pretraining and task-specific finetuning can exacerbate overfitting, particularly on limited downstream data. This observation motivates distillation-guided pruning strategies that regularize the student during sparsification. Knowledge distillation transfers inductive biases from a high-capacity teacher to a compact student using soft targets and intermediate hints (Hinton et al., 2015). Extensions that adapt temperature schedules and stage-wise supervision further stabilize training under capacity gaps (Mirzadeh et al., 2020). Distillation has been used to learn universal language representations from large unlabeled corpora, enabling efficient transfer to many tasks (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). Yet, state-of-the-art approaches struggle to retain performance when extreme sparsity is imposed, for example at fewer than ten percent remaining weights. In such regimes, naïvely distilled students tend to collapse on harder GLUE tasks even when dense teachers are strong. Progressive learning seeks to reduce optimization shock by updating the student module by module while referencing the teacher. One dual-stage distillation scheme progressively grafts student components into the teacher network and targets few-shot transfer with limited unlabeled data, reporting promising results on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. Another line gradually increases the probability of replacing each teacher module with its student counterpart and optimizes the student to mimic teacher behavior (Xu et al., 2020). While these strategies improve stability, evidence on large Transformer encoders remains limited. Moreover, when pruning is coupled with progressive replacement, we observe sharp drops at moderate sparsity levels around fifty percent, indicating insufficient regularization. Our sparse progressive distillation addresses this gap by interleaving structured pruning with stage-aligned supervision from the teacher. At each stage, we prune to a target sparsity, distill both logits and selected intermediate features, and briefly rewind to maintain stable optimization. This procedure mitigates overfitting induced by abrupt capacity reduction and preserves calibration across tasks. We provide comprehensive ablations that separate the effects of sparsity schedules, feature matching, and rewind depth. Across GLUE tasks, the method consistently improves over magnitude pruning, lottery-ticket style reinitialization, and vanilla distillation under equal sparsity budgets. Taken together, the results support sparse progressive distillation as an effective recipe for compressing pretrained Transformers without sacrificing downstream robustness.

document: Prechelt (1998) experimented on 14 different validation-based stop criteria. Prechelt (1998) focused on an issue that the validation error during training may represent many local minima prior to a global optimum. Existing non-validation stop-criteria are generally based on statistical inference. Duvenaud et al. (2016) interpreted stochastic gradient descent in terms of variational inference and proposed an estimation of the marginal likelihood used for early stopping. However, this approach requires costly Hessian computations that are impractical for large models (Duvenaud et al., 2016; Lee and Chung, 2021. Mahsereci et al. (2017) introduced evidence-based stopping, which monitors gradient statistics to decide when to halt training. The EB-criterion relies on fast-to-compute local indicators that reflect whether sample gradients fall within an expected range. Intrinsic dimensionality (ID) has been used to analyze learning dynamics and redundancy in neural networks (Amsaleg et al., 2015). A local variant, LID, estimates subspace dimensionality around samples and can be deployed with unlabeled data. Lee and Chung (2021) reported that LID performs competitively as a stopping rule in few-shot image classification. Another approach pre-estimates the training horizon via repeated runs or cross-validation, then applies a pre-estimated stop epoch for full training (Choi and Lee, 2021). Despite these advances, most methods have not been systematically assessed for NLP tasks, nor do they directly account for task metrics during training. Moreover, comparative studies among non-validation criteria remain scarce, leaving open questions about robustness and data efficiency. In this study, we benchmark EB, LID, PE, and standard validation-based stopping across five text classification datasets. Our evaluation protocol treats split selection, model initialization, and metric tracking uniformly to ensure fairness across methods. For completeness, we considered likelihood-based early stopping derived from variational interpretations, but excluded it due to its computational overhead. To provide a practical baseline, we also detail a simple training protocol that selects hyperparameters on a small hold-out set and then fixes them for the final run. Throughout, we compare against prior validation-oriented heuristics, including original recipes and early empirical protocols (Duvenaud et al. 2016), to contextualize gains on modern architectures.

document: We first review standard approaches to countering hate and studies on counter-narrative effectiveness on social media, then outline data collection and generation strategies. Hate countering has emerged at the intersection of computational social science and NLP, motivated by the limitations of pure moderation. NLP has addressed the proliferation of hate speech through supervised detection datasets (Mathew et al., 2021; Cao et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2018; Waseem, 2016). Surveys synthesize these approaches and discuss ethical risks in deployment (Poletto et al., 2020; Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Nunes and Fortuna, 2018; Kiritchenko et al., 2020). However, detection alone risks overblocking legitimate speech and encoding demographic biases (Binns et al., 2017; Davidson et al., 2019; Sap et al., 2019; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). In response, NGOs and platforms are experimenting with counter-narratives that reframe discourse rather than suppress it (Benesch, 2014; Silverman et al., 2016; Schieb and Preuss, 2016). Empirical studies report reductions in hostile tone and improved bystander engagement after targeted replies (Mathew et al., 2019; Gagliardone et al., 2015). Effects are heterogeneous across communities and topics, underscoring the need for tailored strategies (Allison and Bussey, 2016; Anderson et al., 2014). Messaging style, source credibility, and timing have emerged as key mediators in online settings (Munger, 2017; Myers West, 2018). Prior framing research indicates that appeals to shared identities can lower defensiveness (Pinker, 2011; Cialdini, 2007). Consistent with this view, experimental manipulations of empathy and politeness have shown measurable shifts in conversational dynamics (Lee, 2019; Kim et al.). Yet automated CN generation must balance persuasive goals with safety, transparency, and user autonomy (Kiritchenko et al., 2020; Vidgen et al., 2021). Existing CN datasets combine expert-authored replies with curated social media threads to capture realistic adversarial contexts (Qian et al., 2019; Chung et al., 2020). Annotation protocols often encode intent, target, and strategy labels to facilitate analysis (Mathew et al., 2021; Fortuna et al., 2020). However, coverage remains skewed toward English and Western platforms, limiting generalizability (Zampieri et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2017). Crowdsourcing introduces scale but can dilute strategic nuance without careful quality control (Hovy and Spruit, 2016; Snow et al., 2008). Semi-automated mining of responses from public campaigns augments corpora while preserving authentic style (Chung et al., 2020; Laugier et al., 2021). Privacy constraints and platform policies further complicate longitudinal outcome tracking (Fiesler and Proferes, 2018; Salganik, 2017). Early CN systems relied on retrieval and template filling over topic-specific repositories (Qian et al., 2019; Hua et al., 2019). Neural architectures shifted the focus to conditional generation with style and stance control (Zhang et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2021). Training objectives incorporate toxicity avoidance, politeness enhancement, and factuality constraints (Rashkin et al., 2018; Niu and Bansal, 2018). Multi-task setups jointly learn hate detection and response generation to share representations (Mozafari et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Several works integrate pragmatic cues Lee et al., 2019; Rezaee and Ferraro, 2021) to better align responses with conversational context. Beyond sentence-level signals, discourse and thread structures help model reply salience and timing (Zhang et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2017). Evaluation remains challenging because automatic metrics correlate weakly with human judgments of effectiveness (Novikova et al., 2017; Lowe et al., 2017). Recent studies combine offline text quality with online A/B trials measuring engagement and toxicity change (Gao et al., 2018; Jhaver et al., 2019). Safety layers filter generated outputs for slurs, implicit insults, and targeted harassment before deployment (Vidgen et al., 2021; Gehman et al., 2020). Bias audits examine disparate impact across demographic groups to prevent uneven burdens (Blodgett et al., 2020; Dixon et al., 2018). Explanation interfaces can clarify intent and allow recipients to contest or opt out of interventions (Binns et al., 2018; Eiband et al., 2018). Human-in-the-loop designs situate models as drafting assistants that experts curate and adapt to local norms (Hancock et al., 2019; Chilton et al., 2016). Open challenges include multilingual adaptation, emergent hate tropes, and coordinated response at scale (Zampieri et al., 2020; Ousidhoum et al., 2021). Cross-domain transfer remains brittle when topic, platform, or register shifts are large (Miller et al., 2020; Gururangan et al., 2020). Causal evaluation frameworks are needed to isolate the specific contribution of counter-narratives amid confounders (Imbens and Rubin, 2015; Egami et al., 2018). Partnerships with civil society can surface context-rich strategies and culturally grounded scripts (Benesch, 2014; Mathew et al., 2019). We position our work within this line by focusing on controllable generation and policy-aware evaluation for real-world use. The following review synthesizes prior evidence and clarifies how our modeling choices address these gaps.

document: Word analogy recognition has long served as a probe for lexical semantics and relational reasoning in NLP. Early benchmarks focused on syntactic and semantic regularities captured by distributional vectors (Turney et al., 2003; Mikolov et al., 2013b; Gladkova et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018a). These datasets popularized the linear offset method, where vector arithmetic approximates analogical relations (Ethayarajh et al., 2019). Classical embeddings such as Word2Vec and GloVe demonstrated impressive performance under this paradigm (Mikolov et al., 2013a; Pennington et al., 2014). Subsequent studies extended the evaluation to contextualized encoders and large language models (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Ushio et al., 2021). Despite progress, most evaluations conflate memorization of surface patterns with genuine relational reasoning. A recurring criticism is that analogy lists are sparse, untyped, and biased toward high-frequency English words (Drozd et al., 2016; Linzen, 2016). Furthermore, analogy accuracy can be inflated by neighborhood density effects that do not reflect structured thinking (Nayak et al., 2016; Rogers et al., 2017). To address these issues, several works advocate for typed analogies grounded in ontologies and lexical resources. Knowledge-enhanced formulations augment word pairs with definitions or relations from commonsense graphs (Ma and Shih, 2018). However, the field still lacks a comprehensive benchmark that separates representational quality from reasoning procedure. In particular, training-time exposure to near-duplicate pairs can leak answers into evaluation (Søgaard et al., 2021). Recent prompting techniques offer a complementary lens on analogy solving by converting relational queries into cloze statements (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao and Chen, 2021. Yet most prompting studies rely on ad hoc templates and limited coverage of relation types. A principled benchmark should control for lexical overlap, balance part-of-speech, and include distractor pairs that probe confounders. It should also report metrics beyond top-1 accuracy, including calibrated confidence and error typology. While our work is inspired by vector-based analogies, it emphasizes stepwise rationale generation for explainable judgments. We therefore integrate instance construction with external knowledge and chain-of-thought supervision. Prior datasets typically list relations like country–capital, adjective–adverb, or tense transformations (Gladkova et al., 2016; Bouraoui et al., 2018). Coverage of multi-hop and compositional relations remains limited, especially for low-resource languages (Ponti et al., 2019; Vulić et al., 2020). Cross-lingual analogy transfer has shown mixed results due to alignment noise and morphological divergence (Artetxe et al., 2018; Søgaard et al., 2018). Embedding-space retrofitting can improve certain categories but often hurts others, suggesting trade-offs across relation families (Faruqui et al., 2015; Mrkšić et al., 2016). In contrast to unsupervised evaluation, instruction tuning may imbue models with explicit analogical heuristics. Nevertheless, the evaluation protocol must prevent reward hacking via spurious cues in the prompt. We situate our benchmark within broader work on probing and diagnostic datasets for PLMs (Belinkov and Glass, 2019; Warstadt et al., 2020). A common weakness in prior reporting is inconsistent citation of dataset licenses and splits. For example, some studies attribute improvements to generic scaling laws without ablations on relation type or frequency. Others claim reasoning ability based on few-shot prompts that paraphrase the answer space. We argue for transparent partitioning by lexical domain, relation arity, and knowledge provenance. Our design also records model rationales to facilitate human auditing and error analysis. Compared to contemporary knowledge-enhanced analogy corpora, our instances include sense disambiguation cues in glosses from lexical resources. For related evaluation of compositional reasoning in masked language models, see Petroni et al. (2019), Talmor and Berant (2019), and Talmor et al. (2020). Prior sequence-to-sequence baselines typically fine-tune encoder–decoders such as T5 Raffel et al., 2020) on template-driven tasks. We complement these baselines with retrieval-augmented decoders to reduce reliance on parametric memory (Lewis et al., 2020). Finally, we release carefully stratified splits and a thorough leaderboard to encourage comparable reporting. We hope this benchmark advances rigorous assessment of analogical reasoning as a distinct capability rather than a byproduct of lexical association.

document: Many recent studies use external knowledge to improve ERC performance. KET (Zhong et al., 2019) leverages external knowledge based on ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) and the NRC_VAD emotion lexicon (Mohammad, 2018) as commonsense signals. ConceptNet is a knowledge graph that connects words and phrases in natural language using labeled edges. The NRC_VAD Lexicon includes human ratings of valence, arousal, and dominance for more than 20,000 English words. COSMIC (Ghosal et al., 2020) and Psychological (Li et al., 2021) improve emotion recognition by extracting commonsense knowledge from previous utterances. Commonsense features are extracted and leveraged with COMET (Bosselut et al., 2019) trained on ATOMIC (The Atlas of Machine Commonsense). ATOMIC defines nine relation types with inferential if-then commonsense knowledge expressed in text. ToDKAT (Zhu et al., 2021) improves performance by combining COMET-based commonsense and topic discovery using VHRED (Serban et al., 2017). Ekman (Ekman, 1992) constructed a taxonomy of six common emotions—Joy, Sadness, Fear, Anger, Surprise, and Disgust—from human facial expressions. In addition, Ekman argued that a multimodal view is important for multiple-emotion recognition. Multimodal datasets such as MELD and IEMOCAP are standard resources for emotion recognition and comprise text, speech, and vision data. Datcu and Rothkrantz (2014) use speech and visual information to recognize emotions, and Alm et al. (2005) attempt to recognize emotions based on text alone. MELD and ICON (Hazarika et al., 2018a) show that using more multimodal information improves performance, while text often plays the most important role. However, multimodal information is not always available in social media, especially in chatbot systems that are mainly text based. In this work, we design and introduce a text-based emotion recognition system using neural networks. Prior studies such as Hazarika et al. (2018b) and Zadeh et al. (2017) mostly focused on dyadic conversations. As multi-party conversation datasets including MELD and EmoryNLP have become available, increasing research targets multi-party dialogue modeling. In general, multi-party conversations have higher speaker dependency than dyadic dialogues, creating more conditions to consider and often leading to poorer performance. Zhou et al. (2018) and Zhang et al. (2018a) show that commonsense knowledge is important for understanding conversations and generating appropriate responses. Recent analyses also investigate domain adaptation for ERC Smith, Lee, and Kim, 2019) to mitigate dataset shift across sources. EDA (Bothe et al., 2020) expands multimodal emotion datasets by extracting dialog acts from MELD and IEMOCAP and finds that dialogue acts correlate with emotion labels. Building on these insights, several methods incorporate speaker state tracking and conversational structure to better model context (Jiao et al., 2020; Ghosal et al., 2020). Nevertheless, gaps remain in leveraging external knowledge to disambiguate subtle, implicit emotions in long-range contexts. Our approach explicitly targets these gaps by integrating targeted commonsense cues with robust textual encoders and calibrated training objectives.

document: Injecting Structured Knowledge into LMs. Our work is most related to recent approaches for injecting knowledge into pretrained language models. Know-BERT (Peters et al., 2019) links text mentions to knowledge graphs via an entity linker and recontextualizes BERT representations using KG embeddings. KG-BERT (Yao et al., 2019) linearizes triples and trains BERT to score their plausibility. Wang et al. (2021a) improved KG-BERT with a Siamese architecture over subject–relation and object representations. While prior studies emphasized English resources, we focus on multilingual knowledge transfer to enrich low-resource languages. Multilingual LMs such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) provide a strong initialization for cross-lingual modeling. Beyond LM pretraining, early KB construction relied on rule-based and pipeline systems (Auer et al., 2007; Fabian et al., 2007 which required costly manual engineering and partially structured corpora. Large-scale open resources further catalyzed progress (Lehmann et al., 2015) and informed distant supervision strategies. In parallel, multilingual KG embeddings target alignment and link prediction across graphs (Chen et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020b), yet assume a largely closed world. Our approach bridges these lines by coupling text-grounded signals with structured alignment to support knowledge population across languages.

document: Large-scale pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved great success in recent years. Despite their power, the inference is time-consuming, which hinders their deployment in low-latency scenarios. To accelerate PLM inference, there are currently two streams of work: (1) compressing a cumbersome PLM through knowledge distillation (Sanh et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020), model pruning (Gordon et al., 2020; Michel et al., 2019), quantization (Shen et al., 2020), module replacing (Xu et al., 2020a), etc. (2) Selectively activating parts of the model conditioned on the input, such as Universal Transformer (Dehghani et al., 2019), FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020a), DeeBERT, PABEE, LeeBERT (Zhu, 2021), CascadeBERT (Li et al., 2021a), ElasticBERT (Liu et al., 2021a) and other similar methods (Elbayad et al., 2020; Schwartz et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2021; Xin et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2021). Different from these methods, our proposed HASHEE requires no internal classifiers (which imply extra parameters) and supervision, and therefore can be widely used in a variety of tasks and model architectures. Adaptive computation aims to allocate variable compute per example so that easy inputs exit early while hard inputs receive deeper processing. Early-exit methods typically attach auxiliary classifiers to intermediate layers and stop when a confidence criterion is satisfied (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016; Xin et al., 2021). However, auxiliary heads introduce training complexity, hyperparameters, and potential distribution shifts between pretraining and fine-tuning. Routing-based sparsification instead activates a subset of experts or channels conditioned on the input representation (Lepikhin et al., 2020; Fedus et al., 2021). These methods can be effective but often require architectural changes, specialized training, or careful balancing of load across experts. Our approach is motivated by the observation that hidden representations in PLMs exhibit monotonic certainty increases across depth on many tasks. HASHEE computes a lightweight halting score from hashed token representations to approximate entropy dynamics without dedicated classifiers. The score is unsupervised, computationally cheap, and applicable to both encoder and encoder–decoder architectures. We evaluate HASHEE on text classification, natural language inference, and extractive question answering to assess generality. We compare against strong early-exit baselines including DeeBERT, PABEE, and CascadeBERT implemented under a shared protocol. We also examine uncertainty calibration, selective risk, and Pareto efficiency when trading accuracy for latency. We ground our design in the progressive refinement view of Transformer layers (Vaswani, Shazeer, Parmar, and Uszkoreit, 2017 which posits hierarchical feature composition. Concretely, HASHEE measures the stability of subword projections across consecutive layers and halts when changes fall below a task-agnostic threshold. Stable representations indicate that subsequent layers are likely to yield diminishing returns for the current example. We validate this proxy by correlating layerwise stability with correctness and margin on held-out validation sets. We further provide a simple latency model that captures per-layer compute, memory traffic, and device overheads. Experiments span A100 GPUs and edge-class CPUs under batch-one constraints to reflect interactive serving scenarios. We measure accuracy, expected executed layers, and realized speedup, and we report energy proxies via on-chip power sensors when available. Results show that HASHEE matches or surpasses internal-classifier baselines at comparable or lower average depth on GLUE benchmarks. On SQuAD-style question answering, the method preserves F1 while reducing median layers by up to 40 percent without task-specific tuning. Ablation studies isolate the contributions of hashing granularity, windowed stability estimation, and threshold scheduling. Sensitivity analyses explore robustness to domain shift, adversarial perturbations, and input length variations. Related work also investigates confidence-based stopping rules that rely on softmax entropy or margin at intermediate layers (Zhou et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Unlike those methods, HASHEE leverages representation dynamics rather than classifier logits, which avoids training additional heads. The approach is complementary to compression and distillation and can be stacked with quantization or pruning for further gains. We release code, configurations, and inference scripts to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparisons in future research.

In this section, we discuss various attempts on N-NER corpora. As shown in Table , existing N-NER corpora are mostly high-resource languages, i.e., English and German, while Vietnamese is the only Asian language that has an N-NER dataset.

In terms of the number of classes, it is also worth noting that three out of six corpora have less than ten classes and only NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) has more than 100 classes. The details of these corpora are given as follows. ACE-2004 (Doddington et al., 2004) and ACE-2005 in (Walker et al., 2006) are early examples of N-NER datasets. ACE-2005 (Walker et al., 2006) dataset comprises 30,966 mentions from 12,548 sentences with 7 coarse-grained entity types. In addition to N-NER annotations, ACE-2005 also contains labels for other tasks such as recognition of relation and event extraction. GENIA introduces an N-NER dataset for bioinformatics, providing a high-quality corpus annotated for biological entity names. The dataset is composed of 2,000 abstracts and 92,681 mentions from 9,533 sentences with 32 entity types.

NNE (Ringland et al., 2019) is a recent large fine-grained N-NER dataset composed of 114 classes. Unlike previous N-NER corpora, the NNE dataset annotates entities with more details. For example, "6 September 2019", a date named entity mention, in the NNE dataset, each element in this mention is annotated with finer detail, "6" is annotated with day tag, "September" with month tag, and "2019" with year tag. NoSta-D (Benikova et al., 2014) and DAN+ (Plank et al., 2020) present the first N-NER dataset for Danish. This work investigates the possibility of transfer-learning between languages for the N-NER task. Moreover, DAN+ is a multi-domain dataset; they also study the challenges of domain-shift in their dataset. The dataset contains 6,425 mentions, 130,095 tokens, 4 classes from 6,867 sentences, obtained from multiple domains such as news and social media (Reddit, Twitter, and Arto). NoSta-D, VLSP-2018, and DAN+ have a modest corpus size and a small number of entity types compared to the NNE dataset. This shows that there is still a resource gap for non-English corpora. On the other hand, for Thai, there are only coarse-grained flatten-NER datasets which are publicly available.

document: In this section, we introduce background knowledge about contrastive learning and our phrase encoder LUKE (Yamada et al., 2020). Contrastive learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm for self-supervised representation learning across modalities. The central idea is to bring semantically related samples closer while pushing unrelated samples apart in the embedding space. In NLP, contrastive objectives have been used to pretrain sentence, token, and span encoders with minimal supervision. Despite rapid progress, the community still lacks a unified view of how contrastive signals interact with entity- and phrase-centric models. LUKE extends standard Transformer architectures with entity-aware embeddings that capture lexical and knowledge-grounded features. Phrase encoders generalize entity encoders by operating over arbitrary spans rather than named entities alone. This generalization is attractive for tasks like relation extraction, slot filling, and open information extraction. However, span representations are sensitive to boundary errors and contextual ambiguity. Contrastive pretraining can mitigate these issues by aligning spans that share referents or roles across contexts. Prior work on contrastive learning for NLP has emphasized sentence-level invariances induced by data augmentation. In contrast, our focus is to design augmentations and objectives that respect the compositional structure of phrases. We also aim to clarify when instance discrimination suffices and when more structured supervision is needed. From a modeling standpoint, we consider temperature scaling, queue-based negatives, and hard negative mining. From a data standpoint, we examine distant supervision from Wikipedia links and knowledge bases. Together, these choices determine the granularity and stability of the learned span space. We analyze how these factors interact with LUKE style entity embeddings and positional encodings. For completeness, we revisit multi-view alignment between token, span, and entity abstractions. A crucial observation is that span semantics often depend on latent event frames shared across sentences. Consequently, augmentations that perturb surface form but preserve frame roles yield robust improvements. We implement span drop, synonym substitution, and cross-sentence mixup to instantiate these ideas. Negative sampling is adjusted to control the collision probability among frequent spans. To ensure fair comparisons, we match the compute budget and data exposure across baselines. Evaluation covers entity typing, semantic role labeling, and open-domain question answering. We report results with and without knowledge-enhanced pretraining to isolate the value of contrastive signals. Our error analysis highlights cases where rare senses and idiomatic phrases remain challenging. We situate our study within recent advances on supervised contrastive learning and momentum encoders. The literature also debates whether larger batch sizes or memory banks are essential for stable training (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020. We find that careful temperature tuning provides most of the gains even in modest batch regimes. Moreover, we show that improved negatives can substitute for additional unlabeled data in resource-limited settings. For related work on span encoders and knowledge integration, we build on the LUKE architecture and its successors (Yamada et al., 2020; Onoe et al., 2021). Several studies advocate cross-document alignment to enhance mention robustness under domain shift. Others argue for curriculum schedules that gradually increase augmentation strength during pretraining. Our approach differs by explicitly modeling phrase identity across paraphrases using alignment losses. We also compare against supervised fine-tuning without contrastive pretraining on the same architectures. Finally, we outline open questions about compositionality, knowledge grounding, and efficient negative reuse.
