document: Document-level machine translation (DocNMT) seeks to leverage inter-sentential context to improve adequacy, coherence, and pronoun resolution across entire documents. Integrating document-level information meaningfully into NMT is challenging and has driven research on context-aware architectures, including simple concatenation models (Tiedemann and Scherrer, 2017; Junczys-Dowmunt, 2019; Lopes et al., 2020). Additional directions include multi-source encoders (Jean et al., 2017; Bawden et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018), hierarchical context modeling (Miculicich et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2020), and multi-pass or deliberation-style decoding (Voita et al., 2019; Mansimov et al., 2020). Dynamic context selection has also been explored to reduce noise and computational cost (Kang et al., 2020). At the same time, recent work has revisited evaluation protocols for context use to better capture discourse phenomena (Freitag et al., 2021). Despite steady progress, many methods tacitly assume abundant document-parallel resources, while low-resource settings remain underexplored. In this study we adopt the simple concatenation baseline as an experimental control and leave richer modeling choices to future work. Given the evolving landscape of contextual MT evaluation, we report complementary automatic and human metrics to provide a more complete picture (Kocmi et al., 2021). Multilingual modeling tends to cluster semantically similar sentences from different languages in a shared space (Kudugunta et al., 2019). Such shared representations enable zero-shot transfer in numerous cross-lingual tasks, with especially strong gains from pretrained multilingual Transformers (Eriguchi et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2021; Ruder et al., 2021; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau and Lample, 2019; Xue et al., 2021). In translation, multilingual sentence-level NMT achieves zero-shot directions by transferring generation competence to unseen language pairs (Firat et al., 2016; Johnson et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019a; Aharoni et al., 2019; Arivazhagan et al., 2019b). We extend this idea from SenNMT to DocNMT by training on languages with document-level supervision and testing zero-shot document effects on languages with only sentence-level data. Our approach examines whether discourse-aware behavior can emerge via cross-lingual transfer without in-domain document annotations. To contextualize our design choices, we reference Devlin et al. (2019 for pretraining paradigms while focusing on translation-specific adaptations. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate document-level zero-shot transfer across languages for machine translation.

document:
Knowledge Probing Benchmarks for PLMs.

LAMA established this research direction by compiling single-token relational probes from sources like Wikidata and ConceptNet (Petroni et al., 2019; Speer et al., 2017). Subsequent analyses identified leakage from surface forms and proposed stricter filtering of overlapping tokens between head and tail entities (Poerner et al., 2019). Cross-lingual extensions such as X-FACTR and mLAMA explored multilingual probing and the challenges of multi-token answers (Jiang et al., 2020a). These resources motivated decoding methods that aggregate subword outputs to form coherent multi-token predictions. However, naive decoding strategies often underperform in specialized domains like biomedicine, where terminology is long and compositional. BioLAMA introduced a concurrent benchmark targeting biomedical relations and terminology coverage (Sung et al., 2021). Comparisons across LAMA, MedLAMA, and BioLAMA typically report the number of relations, queries, and answer lengths to contextualize difficulty. Beyond dataset design, the choice of prompt exerts a strong influence on measured knowledge. Early studies mined paraphrastic prompts to stabilize results and reduce prompt sensitivity during evaluation (Jiang et al., 2020b). Later work parameterized prompts and optimized them on task-specific data, blurring the line between probing and training (Shin et al., 2020b; Qin and Eisner, 2021). While trainable prompts can boost scores, they may exploit distributional shortcuts unrelated to stored factual knowledge (Fichtel et al., 2021). Consequently, many evaluations now distinguish zero-training probes from methods that adapt prompts with supervision. In biomedical settings, supervised prompt tuning has demonstrated gains when sufficient labeled tuples are available (Zhong et al., 2021). Yet strict knowledge probing aspires to minimize exposure to target answers during setup to avoid biasing the measurement. Prompt sensitivity has been documented across languages and relations (Jiang et al., 2020a; (Jiang et al., 2020b; Winata et al., 2021). To mitigate variance, some studies ensemble multiple templates and average predictions over prompt families. Others propose calibration techniques that normalize likelihoods by control prompts to reduce spurious preferences. A complementary line adapts the model with small corpora of raw sentences to refresh surface statistics without revealing gold answers. This light adaptation can help models recall domain terms while preserving the spirit of knowledge elicitation. In biomedicine, domain-adaptive pretraining has been shown to improve retrieval of long entity names and rare relations (Nadkarni et al., 2021). Nevertheless, careful partitioning is essential to prevent contamination between pretraining corpora and evaluation triples. Benchmark protocols increasingly require de-duplication, leakage checks, and transparent reporting of preprocessing steps. Another open issue is the definition of answer equivalence when synonyms, abbreviations, and morphological variants exist. Recent works therefore incorporate canonicalization rules and human verification to judge correctness more robustly. The robustness of scoring also depends on whether partial matches or alias expansions are counted as correct. Studies have examined how negative sampling and distractor answers affect calibration and error analysis. Moreover, multilingual probes must disentangle translation artifacts from genuine knowledge transfer in the model. Researchers have proposed language-specific templates and cross-lingual alignment checks to control for these factors. Overall, the field is moving toward standardized protocols that balance realism, reproducibility, and minimal supervision. Our work follows this trajectory by combining careful dataset design with conservative adaptation and transparent reporting.

document: Our contributions add to a large body of work on grounded representation learning. Much of this work augments language modeling objectives with images (Silberer and Lapata, 2012;Lazaridou et al., 2015;Kiela et al., 2017) and videos (Sun et al., 2019). In this work, we focus on representations that encode verb semantics. Prior work on verb learning has been conducted in the computer vision community, typically described as "humanobject interactions" (Regneri et al., 2013;Chao et al., 2018;Ji et al., 2019). In contrast, we focus on learning representations from trajectory data. We are not aware of any prior work that attempts to learn verb semantics from trajectory data. However, prior work in grounded language learning focuses on reasoning about objects through space and time in text (Aroca-Ouellette et al., 2021) and grounding to 3D objects with varying rotation (Thomas, 2019). Outside of NLP, models have been trained on trajectory data for applications like human motion path forecasting (Giuliari et al., 2021) and human activity recognition (Wang et al., 2018). Our work lies at the intersection of grounded language learning and spatiotemporal machine learning, using representations of trajectory data to study verb semantics. The motivation for studying verbs is that actions structure events and provide relational glue between entities in narratives. Early distributional semantics treated verbs as context-averaged word vectors without explicit grounding (Mikolov et al., 2013;Pennington et al., 2014). Subsequent multimodal models injected visual grounding to refine lexical meaning, often via joint embedding spaces learned from paired text and images (Kiros et al., 2014;Frome et al., 2013). These methods have shown gains on word similarity and caption retrieval, yet they seldom capture the kinematics inherent to actions. Motion-centric approaches in vision treat actions as spatiotemporal patterns over pixels or skeletons (Simonyan and Zisserman, 2014;Tran et al., 2015;Du et al., 2015). While effective for recognition, such models generally lack an explicit linguistic interface for compositional verb semantics. A complementary line of work grounds language in control and robotics, mapping instructions to trajectories or policies (Tellex et al., 2011;Andreas and Klein, 2015;Fried et al., 2018). In these settings, trajectories act as supervision for verbs like move, place, or rotate, but the learned representations are task specific and hard to transfer. Recent sequence models over point clouds and skeletons further emphasize temporal dynamics as key to action understanding (Yan et al., 2018;Shi et al., 2019). Our premise is that abstracted trajectories, rather than raw pixels, better disentangle verb meaning from object appearance. To that end, we build on advances in self-supervised learning that align modalities through contrastive or masked modeling objectives (He et al., 2020) Radford et al., 2021) (Baevski et al., 2020). Prior NLP studies on event semantics have modeled roles and frames but rarely leverage continuous motion signals (Baker et al., 1998;Schuler, 2005). Work on affordances relates actions and objects but often infers dynamics indirectly from co-occurrence statistics (Gibson, 1979;Chao et al., 2015). By representing trajectories as sequences in a metric space, we can encode velocity, curvature, and periodicity that correlate with verb classes. Our approach contrasts trajectories across textual descriptions to learn cross-modal invariances that generalize beyond training environments. We situate our method within literature on aligning language and action by weak supervision from narrations or captions (Rohrbach et al., 2016;Miech et al., 2020). Datasets for egocentric or third-person action understanding provide a natural testbed but require adaptation when only sparse trajectories are available (Grauman et al., 2022;Sigurdsson et al., 2016). In evaluation, we follow protocols used in verb sense induction and lexical substitution to measure semantic fidelity of learned embeddings (Erk and McCarthy, 2009;Cotterell and Schütze, 2015). We also compare against baselines that fuse textual embeddings with handcrafted kinematic features derived from trajectories (Roethe et al., 2021). For pretraining, we adopt architectures that can ingest variable-length sequences and support bi-directional context aggregation (Vaswani et al., 2017;Devlin et al., 2019). A separate line of research explores using language models to predict action scripts or preconditions directly from text, without continuous signals (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008;Sap et al., 2019). Finally, we discuss how grounded verb representations may benefit downstream tasks such as instruction following and temporal commonsense reasoning (Bisk et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2021).

document: Abstractive Opinion Summarization aims to generate a fluent summary that reflects salient opinions in input reviews. Due to the lack of sufficient amount of reference summaries, the most common solution is the unsupervised approach (Chu and Liu, 2019;Bražinskas et al., 2020b;Amplayo et al., 2021;Elsahar et al., 2021;Im et al., 2021;Wang and Wan, 2021;Isonuma et al., 2021, inter alia). Recent opinion summarization models use the few-shot learning approach that fine-tunes a pretrained Transformer model with a limited amount of pairs of input reviews and reference summaries. Bražinskas et al. 2020a) and Oved and Levy (2021) show that the few-shot learning approach substantially outperforms unsupervised learning models. All the existing methods listed above are designed for general opinion summarization and, thus, are not necessarily suitable for comparative opinion summarization, as shown in the experiments. Comparative summarization has a line of work on extracting comparative information from single or multiple documents. Lerman and McDonald (2009) defined the contrastive summarization problem and presented early work on the problem. Their method selects sentences so that two sets of summaries can highlight differences. Wang et al. (2013) developed an extractive summarization method for a problem of Comparative Document Summarization, which is to select the most discriminative sentences from a given set of documents. Bista et al. (2019) tackled a similar problem by selecting documents that represent in-cluster documents while they are useful to distinguish from other clusters. Other studies (Kim and Zhai, 2009;Huang et al., 2011;Sipos and Joachims, 2013;Ren et al., 2017) tackled similar tasks by extracting sentences and phrases from given sets of documents for comparative document analysis. Topic models have been also used to capture comparative topics for better understanding text corpora, but they do not generate textual summaries (Ren and de Rijke, 2015;He et al., 2016;Ibeke et al., 2017). Our work differs from the existing work in two points. First, none of them focuses on generating common summaries. Second, all of the previous studies for contrastive summary generation use the extractive approach. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to develop an opinion summarization model and a benchmark for the abstractive contrastive and common summary generation tasks. We evaluate our approach on multiple product review domains using both automatic and human assessments. Following standard practice, we report ROUGE and BERTScore alongside faithfulness and coverage judgments from expert annotators. These results underline the advantages of modeling contrastive and common aspects jointly within a unified abstractive framework.

document:
The LMs typically use a softmax layer to predict P S (x|c t ), the probability of the next word x given the context at the tth position c t.

In this formulation, P S denotes the model’s categorical distribution over the vocabulary conditioned on the context c t.

Specifically, h ct is the tth hidden state in the context c, and w x is the output word embedding for the word x (i.e., the linear weights that project the hidden state to the logit of the word x).

Yang et al. (2018) point out that the log probability distribution over all the words in the vocabulary V is log P S (x|c t).

The distribution is a linear projection from the hidden state h ct with dimension D, so the degree of freedom in the distribution is only D (i.e., there cannot be more than D linearly independent log distributions).

We call this restriction the softmax bottleneck theory.

During training, the ideal output word embedding w x should be close to the hidden states of the contexts h ct that co-occur with the word x while far away from the other hidden states.

This objective is similar to the objective function of Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) except that the context embeddings are contextualized (Kong et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020).

If a context c t has a higher chance to co-occur with queen compared to king, the context also has a higher chance to co-occur with woman compared to man to a similar degree.

This is the main reason that makes queen−king ≈ woman−man in the Word2Vec space (Ethayarajh et al., 2019).

Therefore, the same linear relations tend to hold in the output word embedding space of GPT-2 as well (Wang et al., 2019).

Beyond geometric analogies, several studies have examined whether low-rank constraints induced by the softmax layer limit expressivity in practice (Melis et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019).

Proposed remedies include mixtures of softmaxes and adaptive embeddings, which aim to increase the effective rank without sacrificing training stability (Shazeer et al., 2017; Grave et al., 2017).

Empirically, these modifications improve perplexity on large corpora but do not fully close the gap to theoretically optimal factorization under realistic compute budgets (Merity et al., 2018).

Another strand of work connects distributional structure to bias in analogy completion and gender associations, prompting evaluation protocols that separate semantic from stylistic regularities (Bolukbasi et al., 2016; Garg et al., 2018; Levy et al., 20).

In contrast to prior analyses that focus on static embeddings, we emphasize that contextualization preserves many linear trends while modulating them by syntax and discourse signals (Tenney et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019).

As a result, probing methods that rely solely on global linear classifiers may underestimate the capacity of deep decoders to reweight contextual cues at inference time (Voita and Titov, 2020).

To maintain comparability with established baselines, we adopt standard tokenization and optimization settings and report results on commonly used benchmarks (Marcus et al., 1993; Zaremba et al., 2014).

Finally, we situate our analysis within recent calls for interpretable evaluation of language models across architectures and datasets (Rogers et al., 2020; Linzen et al., 2021).

document: A key finding in previous studies in discourse analysis is that most sentences have an independent well-formed sub-tree in the full document-level discourse tree (Joty et al., 2012). Researchers have taken advantage of this finding to build parsers that utilize different granularity levels of the document to achieve the state-of-the-art results (Kobayashi et al., 2020). This design has been empirically verified to be a generally advantageous framework, improving not only works using traditional feature engineering (Joty et al., 2013;Wang et al., 2017), but also deep learning models (Jia et al., 2018b;Kobayashi et al., 2020). We, therefore, introduce this design to our dependency parsing framework. Specifically, sub-trees for each sentence in a discourse are first built separately, then assembled to form a complete discourse tree. However, our model differs from prior works in that we make a clear distinction to derive better contextualized representations of EDUs from fine-tuning BERT separately for intra-and intersentential levels to dynamically capture different information needed for discourse analysis at different levels. We are also the first to design stacked sequence labeling models for discourse relation identification so that its hierarchical structure can explicitly capture both intra-sentential and intersentential writing patterns. In the case of implicit relations between EDUs without clear connectives, it is crucial to introduce sequential information from the context to resolve ambiguity. Feng and Hirst (2014) rely on linearchain CRF with traditional feature engineering to make use of the sequential characteristics of the context for discourse constituent parsing. However, they greedily build up the discourse structure and relations from bottom up. At each timestep, they apply the CRF to obtain the locally optimized structure and relation. In this way, the model assigns relation gradually along with the construction of the parsing tree from bottom up, but only limited contextual information from the top level of the partially constructed tree can be used to predict relations. Besides, at each time-step, they sequentially assign relations to top nodes of the partial tree, without being aware that those nodes might represent different levels of discourse units (e.g. EDUs, sentences, or even paragraphs). In contrast, we explicitly train our sequence labeling models on both intra-and inter-sentential levels after a complete discourse tree is constructed so that we can infer from the whole context with a clear intention of capturing different writing patterns occurring at intra-and inter-sentential levels. Our framework first segments the document into EDUs using a robust pointer network and then composes sentence-level structures with a lightweight chart parser. We deliberately avoid decoding decisions that interleave structure building with relation assignment, because this coupling often propagates early errors. Instead, we freeze tree topology after a beam-searched global optimization and defer relation labeling to the stacked sequence taggers. This separation enables the intra-sentential tagger to focus on syntagmatic cues such as lexical triggers and clause boundaries. Conversely, the inter-sentential tagger leverages discourse cues that span multiple sentences, including topic shifts and entity continuity Kobayashi et al., 2020). To calibrate cross-sentence context, we introduce a gated memory that aggregates signals from neighboring sentences while suppressing noise from unrelated spans. Contextualized encoders are fine-tuned with distinct objectives so that gradients do not collapse toward a single notion of coherence. To prevent overfitting to positional biases, we augment training with section shuffling and adversarial boundary perturbations. Ablations show that stacking yields consistent improvements over single-pass classifiers across diverse domains. We also observe that the benefits are largest for rare relation types, where hierarchical cues compensate for limited surface statistics. Taken together, these design choices align with the intuition that discourse operates at multiple levels and that models should reflect this stratification.

document: Knowledge-enhanced methods have become central to improving PTLMs. Recently, huge efforts on integrating knowledge into PTLMs have been made. One typical form of knowledge is a knowledge graph. There have been efforts of using knowledge graph to inject entity and relation representations, which are pre-computed from external source, into PTLMs (Zhang et al., 2019; Peters et al., 2019. Some other works try to retrieve or generate the sub-graph from the graph to solve the problem (Lin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). Another existing form of knowledge is extra large-scale corpus. Works that use such corpus present knowledge-related pre-training objectives such as concept order recovering (Zhou et al., 2021), entity category prediction (Yu et al., 2020) and source of knowledge prediction (Wang et al., 2021). These approaches primarily aim to encode factual semantics that text corpora express explicitly. However, they are mostly focused on injecting world knowledge presented in text, rather than physical and visual commonsense knowledge that can be found in images. Bridging symbolic graphs and continuous representations remains a challenge for generalization across domains. Early fusion methods concatenate graph embeddings with token representations at multiple layers, while late fusion applies graph-informed re-ranking of model outputs. Retrieval-augmented models complement pretraining by fetching context from external stores at inference time (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Despite these advances, selecting what knowledge to inject and where in the architecture to place it is still an open question. Prior analyses have shown that naïvely appended knowledge can even degrade performance when it conflicts with context, as demonstrated by (Lin et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020), especially under distribution shift. In this work, we revisit these trade-offs and outline a taxonomy of integration strategies that accounts for source reliability, retrieval latency, and modality alignment.

document: Cross-lingual machine reading comprehension has emerged as a key benchmark for evaluating multilingual language models. Early progress was driven by pre-trained transformers such as multilingual BERT, which demonstrated surprising zero-shot transfer across distant languages. Hsu et al. (2019) reported that mBERT could answer questions in Chinese after training only on English data. Subsequent work explored data augmentation and translation-based pipelines to reduce the transfer gap for low-resource targets. For example, Cui et al. (2019) combined back-translation with multilingual fine-tuning to improve cross-lingual generalization. However, translation-based methods may entangle lexical and structural properties in ways that obscure what knowledge actually transfers. Recent studies therefore emphasize modeling choices that separate semantic content from language-specific syntax to enhance portability. In this view, disentangled representations offer a principled strategy for isolating meaning while allowing surface realization to vary by language. Prior NLP research on disentanglement has introduced variational architectures that allocate distinct latent variables to semantics and syntax (Zhang et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018). Among these, the vMF-Gaussian VAE proposed by Chen et al. (2019) is notable for coupling a semantic hyperspherical prior with a syntax-oriented Gaussian component. Although originally developed for monolingual paraphrase modeling, the framework can be adapted to multilingual settings with appropriate cross-lingual signals. Early evidence on zero-shot cross-lingual MRC with mBERT suggests substantial transfer without parallel data (Hsu et al., 2019. Building on this observation, several authors pursue architectures that minimize sensitivity to word order while preserving predicate-argument structure (Artetxe et al., 2020; Conneau et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the extent to which syntactic divergence alone impedes transfer remains contested in controlled evaluations. Our perspective is that cross-lingual MRC benefits from explicitly decoupling semantics from morphosyntactic features during training. To that end, we incorporate auxiliary supervision from universal POS taggers and dependency parsers to stabilize the syntax channel. We also introduce a cross-lingual reconstruction objective that uses parallel question–context pairs to align the semantic latent space. This objective complements a discriminative paraphrase loss that regularizes instance-level meaning invariance across languages. Unlike standard translation-based augmentation, our approach avoids directly generating target-language text during training, thereby reducing exposure bias. In practice, we fine-tune multilingual PLMs jointly with the disentangling modules using a two-stage curriculum. The first stage emphasizes stable syntax extraction with restricted dropout to prevent leakage into the semantic variable. The second stage emphasizes task-specific supervision for answer span prediction, with the semantic variable attending over shared context encodings. We evaluate on widely used cross-lingual MRC benchmarks and report improvements in zero-shot and few-shot settings over competitive baselines. Ablation studies confirm that both POS- and tree-informed losses contribute additively to transfer robustness. Finally, we discuss limitations related to annotation quality for syntactic resources and outline directions for broader typological coverage in future work.

document: Multiple efforts have sought to define, measure, and mitigate biases in NLP models, spanning word embeddings, sequence models, and large language models (Sun et al., 2019; Mehrabi et al., 2019a; Sheng et al., 2021). Early formalizations of fairness in machine learning outlined group and individual notions that later inspired NLP-specific metrics (Dwork et al., 2012; Barocas et al., 2019). In parallel, the community has curated datasets and benchmarks to expose social stereotypes and disparate outcomes in language technologies (Nangia et al., 2020; Sap et al., 2020). Researchers have proposed automatic bias scores using sentiment, toxicity, and regard classifiers to operationalize these evaluations (Sheng et al., 2019; Dixon et al., 2018). Benchmark construction has highlighted annotation artifacts and domain coverage gaps, as seen in datasets like WinoBias and CrowS-Pairs (Zhao et al., 2018; (Nangia et al., 2020; Nadeem et al., 2021). Despite progress, most metrics remain task-specific and are difficult to transfer across domains or modalities without extensive recalibration (Blodgett et al., 2020; Hutchinson et al., 2021). A further challenge is that many bias estimates rely on gold demographic labels or controlled counterfactuals that are rarely available in production systems (De-Arteaga et al., 2019b; Garg et al., 2018). Moreover, correlations between automatic bias metrics and human judgments are inconsistent, raising questions about construct validity and practical utility (Ethayarajh, 2020; Sap et al., 2019). Some studies use inequality indices to present unified treatments of group and individual fairness, although extensions to free-form text are nontrivial (Speicher et al., 2018; Heidari et al., 2019). Others propose robust statistical bounds to represent uncertainty in measured disparities and mitigate spurious effects of sampling noise (Ethayarajh, 2020; Williamson and Menon, 2019). For generative models, bias assessment often mixes intrinsic tests on prompts with extrinsic end-task evaluations, yet a common yardstick is still lacking (Gehman et al., 2020; Lucy and Bamman, 2021). Recent audits also emphasize intersectional harms that emerge when multiple protected attributes interact, compounding measurement complexity (Kearns et al., 2018; Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). At the same time, deployment contexts impose latency and privacy constraints that preclude heavy calibration or access to sensitive attributes at inference time (Veale and Binns, 2017; Corbett-Davies and Goel, 2018). These realities motivate model-agnostic metrics that capture directional sensitivity of predictions to attribute cues without requiring ground truth labels for every group (Black et al., 2022; Kennedy et al., 2020). In text, such cues can be subtle or culturally specific, as in references to occupations, activities, or honorifics that function as proxies for gender, race, or religion (Caliskan et al., 2017; Manzini et al., 2019). Crucially, sensitivity-based measures should separate semantic content from protected-attribute signaling to avoid penalizing legitimate topic differences (Tatman, 2017; Park et al., 2018). We build on these insights to define an accumulated prediction sensitivity metric that aggregates local gradients over minimal text edits targeting attribute markers (Prabhakaran et al., 2019; Ravfogel et al., 2020). We also examine alignment between automatic scores and human ratings using carefully sampled sentence pairs from multiple domains Blodgett et al. 2021, demonstrating when the metric tracks perceived harms. Our evaluation spans both classification and generation settings, covering toxicity, sentiment, and open-ended completion tasks with diverse demographic axes (Dixon et al., 2018; Sheng et al., 2019). To test robustness, we analyze performance under domain shift and adversarial paraphrases that remove surface cues while preserving meaning (Madaan et al., 2022; Morris et al., 2020). We compare against widely used baselines, including TPR gaps, equalized odds variants, and embedding-space association tests, re-implementing each with consistent preprocessing (Hardt et al., 2016; Caliskan et al., 2017). Across datasets, our metric shows stronger correlation with human judgments than alternatives while maintaining low variance under resampling, especially in sparse-data regimes (Kumar et al., 2020; Ethayarajh, 2020). Nevertheless, we surface limitations when attribute markers are deeply entangled with semantics, as in code-switching or dialectal text, where causal disentanglement remains an open problem (Blodgett et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2021). We release code, evaluation templates, and domain-balanced samples to facilitate standardized comparisons and future meta-analyses (Mitchell et al., 2019; Raji et al., 2020). Taken together, our study clarifies how to quantify directional harms in language technologies while acknowledging open gaps in normative framing, data coverage, and cross-cultural generalizability (Selbst et al., 2019; Crawford, 2017).

document: Several approaches to zero-shot cross-lingual transfer can be grouped into data-based transfer, which transforms training data, and model-based transfer, which modifies the learning routine. In data-based transfer, translate-train pipelines project or align entity labels across translated utterances to enable supervised learning on pseudo-labeled target data. One widely used component is fastalign (Dyer et al., 2013), an unsupervised word aligner trained on parallel corpora. Label projection can also rely on word-by-word translation and source label copying (Yi and Cheng, 2021). Additional entity alignment steps have been proposed to refine projected spans (Li et al., 2021a). Machine translation combined with entity matching and distributional statistics can further improve performance, albeit at higher cost (Jain et al., 2019). Word substitution strategies such as code-switching or dictionary-enhanced pretraining have also yielded gains (Qin et al., 2020;Kuwanto et al., 2021). Prior work formalized label projection as alignment over translated utterances (Dou and Neubig, 2021 and reported consistent improvements across multiple languages. Prior to multilingual transformers such as XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), task-oriented XNLU commonly relied on BiLSTM encoders with multilingual embeddings (Schuster et al., 2019). More recent methods keep inference largely unchanged while adding training-time objectives to better align source and target representations. Xu et al. (2020) fuse source and target embeddings with attention and jointly align and predict entity labels. Qi and Du (2020) introduce an adversarial language discriminator to encourage language-agnostic sentence representations. Translation-pair contrastive objectives have proven effective for cross-lingual alignment during pretraining (Pan et al., 2020; Chi et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these techniques often require sizable annotated corpora and expensive pretraining (Chi et al., 2020). We compare most closely with XeroAlign, which minimizes cross-lingual CLS-based sentence embedding distances and achieves strong intent classification results on our evaluation suites.

document: This section surveys datasets for question answering with a focus on narratives and educational applications. We concentrate on resources that assess comprehension of stories, books, and long-form media, as well as classroom-oriented benchmarks. Our review contrasts dataset design choices including source material, annotation protocols, and answer formats. We also discuss how these choices influence model performance, evaluation reliability, and downstream use. Narrative question answering emerged to test discourse understanding beyond factoid retrieval. Early resources emphasized short passages with span extraction and minimal reasoning. Subsequent corpora introduced multi-hop inference across scenes, chapters, or episodes. Educational datasets add pedagogical constraints such as curriculum alignment and formative feedback. These settings often require grading rubrics alongside the reference answers. A further dimension concerns openness of domain, licensing, and redistribution policies. Openly licensed corpora facilitate reproducibility, student projects, and classroom deployment. Privacy-preserving collections restrict content and may require paraphrasing or substitution. Data collection for narratives commonly uses public-domain literature, scripts, or plot summaries. Annotations can be crowd-sourced or expert-authored, each with distinct trade-offs. Crowd labels scale rapidly but show higher variance and occasional annotation artifacts. Expert labels cost more yet better encode instructional intent and domain norms. Answer types range from extractive spans to multiple-choice, abstractive responses, and rationales. Some datasets explicitly require explanations that accompany the final answers. Such rubric-aware tasks encourage models to justify predictions rather than guess. Evaluation metrics vary from token overlap to entailment-based and rubric-based scoring. Reference diversity is crucial for fair grading of abstractive responses and paraphrases. Several benchmarks include adversarial or counterfactual items to reduce annotation shortcuts. Long-context datasets stress memory, retrieval, and hierarchical encoding components. Narrative structure introduces dependencies that span chapters and non-linear timelines. Educational QA further imposes alignment with learning objectives and standards frameworks. Automatic difficulty estimation helps instructors curate assignments and personalize practice. Metadata such as reading level, genre, and source provenance supports fair comparisons. Benchmarks differ in their balance between multiple-choice and open-ended prompts. Multiple-choice simplifies grading but may cue shallow lexical heuristics. Open-ended prompts reveal reasoning processes but complicate automatic scoring. Pragmatic concerns include licensing clarity, accessibility of raw texts, and community adoption. We summarize dataset attributes and typical pitfalls observed in prior audits Smith et al. 1; Jones et al. 2. Common pitfalls include leakage between splits and ambiguous question wording. Reliance on weakly supervised labels can inflate headline scores without real gains. Reproducibility improves when splits are standardized and transparent baselines are reported. We therefore advocate clearer documentation, error analyses, and comprehensive ablations in future releases.

document:
2.1 Pre-trained language model (PLM)

Pre-trained language models (PLMs) have become the default backbone for a wide range of natural language processing tasks. Most contemporary systems are built on the transformer architecture Vaswani et al., 2017). The transformer employs self-attention and position-wise feed-forward networks to model long-range dependencies efficiently (Vaswani et al., 2017). PLMs typically use the encoder stack for bidirectional context modeling via masked language modeling objectives (Devlin et al., 2019). BERT introduced a simple yet powerful recipe combining masked language modeling with next sentence prediction and demonstrated strong transfer across tasks (Devlin et al., 2019). Subsequent variants refined data, objectives, and optimization to push performance without architectural changes, as exemplified by RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019). ALBERT reduced parameter counts through factorized embeddings and cross-layer parameter sharing while retaining accuracy (Lan et al., 2019). ELECTRA replaced masked token prediction with a more sample-efficient discriminator–generator pretraining scheme (Clark et al., 2020). DeBERTa further improved context encoding by disentangling position and content representations plus enhanced attention mechanisms (He et al., 2021). To enable deployment under resource constraints, DistilBERT offered a compact student trained by knowledge distillation from a larger teacher (Sanh et al., 2019). For long documents, sparse and blockwise attention mechanisms enabled kilometer-length contexts as in Longformer and Big Bird (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). Encoder–decoder PLMs broadened the paradigm by framing diverse tasks as text-to-text problems, notably with T5 and BART (Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Autoregressive generative models scaled to hundreds of billions of parameters and excelled at few-shot learning through in-context prompting (Brown et al., 2020). Tokenization choices such as byte-pair encoding and unigram language models influence vocabulary granularity and downstream robustness (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kudo, 2018). Large-scale pretraining data mixtures balance web sources, books, code, and curated corpora to improve coverage and reduce domain shift (Raffel et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020). Task adaptation has traditionally relied on supervised fine-tuning atop frozen or partially frozen encoders (Peters et al., 2018; Howard and Ruder, 2018). Parameter-efficient techniques such as adapters, prefix-tuning, and LoRA reduce memory footprint while retaining competitive quality (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Hu et al., 2022). Instruction tuning and alignment with human feedback further steer general-purpose PLMs toward helpfulness and safety (Ouyang et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Multilingual models extend pretraining across scripts and languages, with strong cross-lingual transfer in mBERT and XLM-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Domain-adapted PLMs such as SciBERT and BioBERT show gains in technical and biomedical text understanding through continued pretraining on in-domain corpora (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Retrieval-augmented methods couple neural encoders with external indexes to ground generation and reasoning in evidence (Lewis et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020). Knowledge-intensive tasks also benefit from parameterized factual memory, though they remain sensitive to pretraining coverage and temporal drift (Petroni et al., 2019; Lazaridou et al., 2021). Training efficiency has improved through mixed precision, gradient checkpointing, and distributed optimization strategies (Micikevicius et al., 2018; Sergeev and Del Balso, 2018). Robustness analyses reveal brittleness to adversarial perturbations, spurious correlations, and out-of-distribution inputs (Jia and Liang, 2017; Hendrycks and Dietterich, 2019). Bias and fairness concerns persist due to demographic skews and harmful associations present in large pretraining corpora (Bender et al., 2021; Blodgett et al., 2020). Data contamination and evaluation leakage complicate benchmarking and can inflate reported gains if not carefully controlled (Dodge et al., 2021; Magar and Schwartz, 2022). Despite impressive zero-shot abilities, transfer degrades when task formats diverge from pretraining signals, necessitating better task specification and calibration (Perez et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Continual pretraining enables adaptation to new domains and time periods while mitigating catastrophic forgetting through replay and regularization (Gururangan et al., 2020; Ke et al., 2021). Lightweight on-device deployment benefits from pruning, quantization, and distillation that trade minimal accuracy for substantial latency and memory reductions (Han et al., 2016; Dettmers et al., 2022). Open-source ecosystems accelerate progress by releasing checkpoints, data pipelines, and reproducible baselines under permissive licenses (Wolf et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). In applied settings, careful problem formulation, data curation, and evaluation design remain as important as model scale (Lin et al., 2021; Dehghani et al., 2023). In the remainder of this section, we summarize notation and conventions used for encoder-only PLMs Devlin et al., 2019), fine-tuning procedures, and reporting standards. Overall, the literature indicates that architectural simplicity coupled with high-quality data and efficient adaptation continues to deliver the strongest and most reliable gains.

document: Explainable classification emphasizes models that justify predictions to users. Unlike post-hoc analysis approaches for explainability (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Sundararajan et al., 2017), prototype classification networks (Li et al., 2018; Hase et al., 2019) are white-box models with explanations built in via case-based reasoning (Kolodner, 1992) rather than extractive rationales (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019; Jain et al., 2020; Glockner et al., 2020). They are the neural variant of prototype classifiers (Bien and Tibshirani, 2011; Kim et al., 2014), predicting based on similar known instances. Contemporary work (Rajagopal et al., 2021) also stresses the importance of global explainability through training examples. Yet in that approach, the similar training examples are not directly integrated into the decision itself. In contrast, we rely on learned prototypes to provide more transparency. Our work builds on Li et al. (2018), which we lay out in Section 3.1. Later work (Hase et al., 2019 enables prototype learning of partial images. In NLP, Guu et al. (2018) retrieved prototype examples from training data for edit-based natural language generation. Hase and Bansal (2020) used a variant of Li's work to examine several approaches to explanation. Unlike our work, they used feature activation to obtain explanations similar to post-hoc methods. They also did not handle the absence of relevant content in inputs. Evaluating explainability is a multi-faceted problem. HCI concerns include for whom we design explanations, what goals users pursue, how to convey information without excessive cognitive load, and whether explainable systems foster more effective human+AI partnerships (Amershi et al., 2019; Wickramasinghe et al., 2020; Liao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Bansal et al., 2021). On the other hand, algorithmic concerns include generating faithful and trustworthy explanations (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), balancing local versus global criteria, and weighing post-hoc against self-explanations (Danilevsky et al., 2020). Explainability evaluation methods (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017) include measuring faithfulness (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020), enabling model simulatability (Hase et al., 2019), behavioral testing (Ribeiro et al., 2020), and evaluating intelligent user interactions (Nguyen et al., 2018). Task-grounded evaluations in misinformation and fact-checking further highlight the need for actionable, user-aligned rationales. Human+AI fake news detection benefits from explainable fact-checking (Kotonya and Toni, 2020b) that supports human-in-the-loop verification (Nakov et al., 2021; Demartini et al., 2020). However, studies rarely assess a human+AI team in combination (Nguyen et al., 2018). In fact, human+AI teams often underperform the human or the AI working alone (Bansal et al., 2021), emphasizing the need to carefully baseline performance. Propaganda detection occupies the broader space of detecting disinformation Da San Martino et al., 2019). An explainable propaganda system could provide decision support to both content moderators and end users. Because propaganda detection is a hard task for non-expert users and state-of-the-art models are not yet accurate enough for practical use, explainability may improve adoption of computational propaganda detection systems (Martino et al., 2020). We adopt a white-box model with human evaluation to better support an effective human+AI partnership. This design aims to bring automated models closer to practical adoption in high-stakes settings. Our formulation emphasizes transparency without sacrificing competitive accuracy on standard benchmarks. We also discuss open challenges in evaluating the causal faithfulness of prototype attributions. Finally, we outline how our approach relates to broader trends in interpretable machine learning and human-centered AI.

document: There has been the argument revolving deep learning models that they are black-box and hard to offer explainable and interpretable predictions, which has led to a considerable amount of research work regarding their interpretability (Linardatos et al., 2021). The pre-trained BERT language model has been extensively analyzed since its launch, and a growing body of work now seeks to characterize what it learns and where it fails (Rogers et al., 2020). In particular, feature-based probes have been proposed to show how a particular layer, head, or neuron of BERT works on a downstream NLP task. Usually with a small set of additional parameters, a probe is trained in a supervised manner using feature representations from the pre-trained BERT, e.g., contextualized embeddings, to solve a particular task (Wu et al., 2020). Attention and structural probes have been invented to investigate different aspects of BERT and linguistics (Lin et al., 2019; Jawahar et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2019; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Manning et al., 2020; Tenney et al., 2019a, 2019b; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020). Latent ontology of contextual embeddings has also been investigated via cluster analysis (Michael et al., 2020). For works that emphasize probing contextualized representations for lexico-semantic relations, previous studies have investigated BERT for lexical relation classification via a neural network probe on type-level embeddings (Vulić et al., 2020). Beyond English, researchers have examined crosslingual transfer of lexical relations using multilingual encoders (Conneau et al., 2020; Pires et al., 2019). A complementary line of research analyzes subword tokenization effects on semantic generalization (Bostrom and Durrett, 2020; Rust et al., 2021). Diagnostic datasets targeting hypernymy detection and entailment have been widely used to probe transitivity and monotonicity (Levy et al., 2015; Glockner et al., 2018). Traditional distributional approaches to hypernymy rely on lexical entailment signals such as Hearst patterns and distributional inclusion (Hearst, 1992; Santus et al., 2016). Neural methods augment these signals with contextual encoders to improve robustness under ambiguity (Shwartz et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2020). Within this landscape, the question of whether BERT encodes IS-A transitivity remains open despite encouraging results on standard benchmarks (Rimell, 2014; Roller et al., 2018). Prior work has highlighted that high benchmark scores can arise from annotation artifacts and lexical memorization rather than genuine reasoning (Gururangan et al., 2018; Niven and Kao, 2019). Following Peters et al. 2018, we consider sense-sensitive representations that disambiguate polysemy during probing. Sense inventories and contextual alignment can be operationalized using lexical resources such as WordNet and BabelNet coupled with context encoders (Miller, 1995; Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012). Recent studies propose clustering-based sense induction that leverages contrastive objectives and geometry-aware regularization (Reif et al., 2019; Aharoni and Goldberg, 2020). However, these methods often ignore the algebraic structure of hyponymy, particularly the transitive closure and partial order constraints central to taxonomy learning (Nickel and Kiela, 2017; Ganea et al., 2018). Inference-time consistency checks have been proposed to enforce logical constraints on neural predictions (Minervini et al., 2018; Rocktäschel and Riedel, 2017). Another thread evaluates whether attention patterns correlate with dependency structure and semantic roles, though correlation does not imply causation (Clark et al., 2019; Kovaleva et al., 2019). Model interpretability surveys synthesize these perspectives and argue for stronger evaluation protocols Rogers et al., 2020). Building on these insights, we focus on the robustness of IS-A reasoning under controlled perturbations of context and predicate phrasing. We formulate evaluation protocols that distinguish between pattern matching and structural generalization by composing multi-hop entailment chains. Our analysis compares layerwise probing against parameter-efficient fine-tuning to separate representational capacity from task adaptation (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021). We further investigate how lexical frequency, definition exposure, and pretraining corpora bias affect generalization to rare concepts (Kassner and Schütze, 2020; Petroni et al., 2019). To mitigate confounds, we balance positive and hard negative instances sampled from curated taxonomies and apply calibration to reduce threshold sensitivity (Camacho-Collados et al., 2018; Desai and Durrett, 2020). We also report ablations on sense mapping fidelity, which quantify the trade-off between granularity and stability across domains. Overall, our study positions transitivity as a stringent test of structured semantic knowledge in contextual encoders rather than a byproduct of surface regularities. By clarifying how and when BERT supports logically consistent IS-A chains, we aim to guide the design of future models and benchmarks that capture abstract relational structure.

document: Open-domain dialogue systems have increasingly relied on large pretrained language models trained on social media conversations. This paradigm promises broad coverage, stylistic diversity, and scalability without costly annotation. However, it also raises concerns about safety, factuality, and controllability in interactive settings. Early neural conversational agents were trained on message–response corpora with hierarchical encoders and decoders (Serban et al., 2016). The advent of transformer-based pretraining shifted the field toward massive web-scale data and few-shot adaptation (Zhang et al., 2020; Adiwardana et al., 2020). Subsequent work explored recipes that combine large-scale retrieval, supervised fine-tuning, and human feedback to enhance engagingness (Roller et al., 2020). Large dialogue-optimized models further emphasized safety and groundedness through curated datasets and prompt engineering (Thoppilan et al., 2022). While instruction tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback have improved helpfulness in general-purpose assistants (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022), their transfer to open-domain chatting remains underexplored. A central challenge is that pretraining data inherits the biases and toxicity of online platforms (Gehman et al., 2020). Filtering pipelines attempt to redact unsafe spans, profanity, and personal data, yet inevitably trade off coverage and style diversity (Dinan et al., 2019). Researchers have investigated decoding-time controls such as attribute models and toxicity-aware reranking to mitigate harmful outputs (Dathathri et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021). However, these controls can reduce linguistic richness and lead to generic responses if applied aggressively. Complementary strategies encourage social grounding via persona and empathy objectives to elicit more contextualized replies (Zhang et al., 2018; Rashkin et al., 2019). Knowledge grounding with retrieval or web browsing also reduces hallucination and keeps conversations topical (Shuster et al., 2021; Roller et al., 2021). Despite these advances, evaluation remains difficult because automatic metrics correlate weakly with human judgments in open-ended dialogue. Human evaluations are costly, noisy, and sensitive to prompt design and rater priors. Recent benchmarks thus emphasize adversarial probing and longitudinal user studies over one-off turn-level scoring (Kiela et al., 2021). In production, safety budgets and latency constraints further complicate the adoption of complex control mechanisms. Consequently, many practitioners prefer lightweight filtering, robust decoding, and targeted red-teaming during deployment. We argue that a principled approach should jointly consider data curation, objective design, and interaction-time safeguards. On the pretraining side, data selection can shape style while preserving diversity through mixture-of-sources sampling and topic balancing. Weak supervision from community moderation signals and toxicity classifiers can prioritize safer threads without discarding entire domains. During fine-tuning, multi-objective losses can align helpfulness, honesty, and harmlessness with minimal degradation in engagement. Preference learning with conversation-level comparisons is particularly promising but remains data hungry. At inference, calibrated uncertainty estimates and refusal policies can bound risk under distribution shift. Retrieval augmentation further anchors responses in verifiable evidence when sensitive claims arise. To situate our contributions, we review representative datasets, modeling techniques, and safety interventions from the literature. Reddit-based corpora, pushshift archives, and mixed-domain dialog sets have become standard pretraining sources (Zhang et al., 2020. Dialogue-optimized transformers such as Meena, Blender, and DialoGPT exemplify the benefits and pitfalls of this path (Adiwardana et al., 2020; Roller et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Safety-focused datasets introduce adversarial prompts and annotated refusals to stress-test toxic or biased generations (Dinan et al., 2019; Gehman et al., 2020). Methods like DExperts and PPLM showcase controllable generation but complicate deployment due to added compute (Liu et al., 2021; Dathathri et al., 2019). More recent work integrates retrieval and browsing to support grounded, up-to-date answers in multi-turn settings (Shuster et al., 2021). Instruction-tuned assistants demonstrate improved steerability yet often over-refuse or under-specify follow-up questions in chit-chat (Ouyang et al., 2022). Open challenges include robust detoxification without loss of personality, scalable human oversight, and rigorous longitudinal evaluation. Addressing these gaps, our study proposes a data-centric pipeline that preserves stylistic variety while meeting safety targets. We show that careful filtering, balanced fine-tuning, and efficient decoding can yield engaging and responsible open-domain dialogue systems.

Quality Estimation Current SOTA models in sentence-level QE, which is typically framed as a regression task, mainly use multilingual representations from pre-trained transformers (Devlin et al., 2018), notably XLM-R Conneau et al., 2019). The input to a sentence-level QE model is a concatenation of the source and translated sentences, separated by the [SEP] token. The sequence is encoded by the pre-trained Transformer model, and the [CLS] token is passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) layer to obtain a sentence-level score. During fine-tuning both the parameters of the pre-trained model and the parameters corresponding to the MLP layer are updated.

Word-level QE is typically addressed as a binary classification task, where the QE model needs to predict a binary label indicating whether a word is correct or wrong for each word in the MT output (Lee, 2020). As illustrated in Figure (left), some supervised approaches use both sentence-level and word-level objectives in a multi-task setting, which results in superior performance (Kim et al., 2017;Lee, 2020). Methods that do not require word-level training data either need access to the MT model (Rikters and Fishel, 2017;Fomicheva et al., 2020b), or still treat the problem as a supervised task but use synthetically generated data for supervision (Tuan et al., 2021).

Rationale Extraction for NLP SOTA NLP models based on deep neural networks achieve high performance in a variety of tasks, often at the cost of interpretability (Lipton, 2016). Recent work aims to address this issue by focusing on two different goals. On the one hand, the aim is to produce justifications for model predictions that are plausible to the users, in order to increase users' trust (Ribeiro et al., 2016). On the other hand, the aim is to reveal the inner workings of the model and faithfully explain its predictions, so the explanation can be useful to model developers (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020).

Typically, explainability methods operate by selecting a portion of the input that justifies model prediction for a single data point. This can be done either by modifying the model architecture, or by trying to explain the predictions of a given model. The first type of approaches (a.k.a. rationalization by construction) involves imposing restrictions on the generated rationales to satisfy certain constraints, e.g. compactness (Yu et al., 2019; Chalkidis et al., 2021. Note that such restrictions often result in lower performance and indeed are not guaranteed to explain the behaviour of an unconstrained model (Jain et al., 2020). The second type of approaches (the so called post hoc) usually rely on feature attribution methods, which assign an importance value to each input feature of a network (Sundararajan et al., 2017;Schulz et al., 2020). These methods do not allow for introducing useful biases during training, but focus on faithfully explaining model behaviour.

Feature attribution has a long tradition in image recognition tasks (Simonyan et al., 2013) and only recently have been applied to some NLP tasks, most commonly text classification (DeYoung et al., 2019). QE is fundamentally different from text classification where clues are typically separate words or phrases (Zaidan et al., 2007) which often can be considered independently of the rest of the text. This independence assumption does not hold for the task of evaluating translation quality where a word cannot be identified as a clue (e.g. translation error) without considering the surrounding context. Furthermore, SOTA NLP models based on contextualized representations for input words make rationale extraction especially challenging, as the representation for a given word can encode not only the word identity but also its interactions with other words in the text. Recent work has revealed various interesting properties that characterize the information flow through hidden layers in deep transformer models (Voita et al., 2019;De Cao et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2021). We provide additional insights on this topic in Section 5.2. We summarize implications for downstream evaluation in Section 6.

document: Synthetic translations have become a central tool for improving neural machine translation (NMT) by leveraging abundant monolingual data. Prior work distinguishes forward translation, which translates source monolingual text into the target to create pseudo-parallel pairs, from back-translation, which translates target monolingual text back into the source to augment training data (Zhang and Zong, 2016;Sennrich et al., 2016a). These strategies have repeatedly improved generalization in low-resource and domain-mismatched scenarios (Edunov et al., 2018;Caswell et al., 2019). Beyond simple augmentation, several studies repurpose synthetic translations to reshape the training distribution and stabilize learning. For example, researchers generate multiple candidate translations for each side of a sentence and combine them with authentic bitext to train robust ensembles (Nguyen et al., 2020;Jiao et al., 2020). Another influential line frames synthetic data as a vehicle for sequence-level knowledge distillation, where a strong teacher produces translations that a smaller student model can imitate (Kim and Rush, 2016). Such distillation has proven particularly helpful for non-autoregressive and semi-autoregressive architectures that benefit from smoother targets (Gu et al., 2017;Ghazvininejad et al., 2019). A commonly hypothesized explanation is that distilled outputs reduce sequence-level entropy and prune spurious modes, making optimization easier (Zhou et al., 2019a;Xu et al., 2021). Nevertheless, synthetic data are not a panacea, and their efficacy depends on data selection, model capacity, and noise control. Quality filtering of mined or generated sentences remains crucial to prevent domain drift and exposure bias amplification (Koehn et al., 2018;Wenzek et al., 2020). In domain adaptation, synthetic translations offer a practical bridge between scarce in-domain bitext and plentiful in-domain monolingual corpora (Chu and Wang, 2018). Beyond supervised settings, synthetic supervision also underpins unsupervised and weakly supervised MT by creating cross-lingual signals through iterative self-training (Lample et al., 2018b;Artetxe et al., 2018). The interplay between synthetic data and pretraining has further reshaped the NMT landscape, as large pretrained encoders and decoders can be fine-tuned more effectively when guided by curated pseudo-parallel examples (Conneau and Lample, 2019;Liu et al., 2020). At the same time, over-reliance on synthetic pairs may entrench translationese artifacts that harm downstream performance if not carefully mitigated (Edunov et al., 2020). Recent research thus emphasizes data diversification, curriculum design, and dynamic sampling to balance authenticity and syntheticity throughout training (Wang et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2019). In parallel, advances in tokenization, denoising objectives, and multilingual transfer have interacted synergistically with synthetic pipelines. For instance, subword regularization and noising during decoding can broaden coverage while maintaining fluency in pseudo-targets (Kudo, 2018;He et al., 2020). Moreover, multilingual pretraining helps propagate improvements from high-resource directions to low-resource ones when combined with targeted back-translation (Aharoni et al., 2019;Tang et al., 2020). Despite these successes, open questions persist regarding how much synthetic data to generate, how to schedule its introduction, and how to evaluate its marginal utility across domains. Empirical studies suggest diminishing returns beyond certain ratios of synthetic to authentic pairs, yet the break-even point varies with task difficulty and architecture (Ott et al., 2018;Nguyen et al., 2020). There is also debate about whether iterative back-translation saturates too quickly or continues to yield steady improvements under stronger teachers (Hoang et al., 2018;Popel et al., 2020). On the evaluation side, reliance on BLEU alone may obscure gains in adequacy or terminology that synthetic methods can deliver, motivating broader metrics and human studies (Post, 2018;Mathur et al., 2020). From a systems perspective, generating, filtering, and retraining on synthetic data introduces additional compute and engineering complexity that must be justified by consistent gains. Practical pipelines therefore integrate lightweight data selection, caching, and asynchronous generation to amortize costs over multiple training runs (Junczys-Dowmunt, 2018;Schwenk et al., 2021). In our study, we revisit synthetic translation strategies through the lens of model capacity, data source heterogeneity, and feedback-enabled generation. We compare conventional single-pass back-translation with iterative, quality-aware loops that exploit uncertainty estimates and constrained decoding (Edunov et al., 2018;Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). We also examine how the presence of pretrained sequence encoders changes the optimal balance between authentic and synthetic supervision. To that end, we assess teacher choice and student receptivity when initializing with large-scale contextual representations like BERT (Devlin et al. 2019) and mBART (Liu et al., 2020b). Crucially, we interrogate whether distilled targets overly simplify long-range reordering phenomena, and whether selective reintroduction of original bitext can remedy this effect (Zhou et al., 2019a;Sun et al., 2020). Our experiments span high-resource and mid-resource directions with varied morphology and typology to test generality. We report automatic and human evaluations and analyze error typologies relevant to synthetic data, including literalness, omission, and hallucination (Raunak et al., 2021;Lee et al., 2019). Finally, we situate our findings within the broader shift toward data-centric NMT development and articulate open challenges for scalable, trustworthy augmentation. Together, these results refine practical guidance for using synthetic translations across architectures, data regimes, and domains.

Conversational question answering. In recent years, several conversational question answering datasets have emerged, such as QuAC (Choi et al., 2018), CoQA (Reddy et al., 2019), andDoQA (Campos et al., 2020). Different from singleturn QA datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), CQA requires the model to understand the question in the context of conversational history. There have been many methods proposed to improve CQA performance (Ohsugi et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2020;Qu et al., 2019;Kim et al., 2021) and significant improvement has been made on CQA benchmarks.

Besides text-based CQA tasks, there also exist CQA benchmarks that require other forms of modeling ability, such as combining textual evidence with background knowledge (Saeidi et al., 2018), utilizing structured knowledge base (Saha et al., 2018;Guo et al., 2018), as well as CQA in other modalities (Das et al., 2017).

Evaluation with predicted history. Only recently has it been noticed that the current method of evaluating CQA models is flawed. Mandya et al. (2020); Siblini et al. (2021) point out that using gold answers in history is not consistent with the realworld scenario and propose to use predicted history for evaluation. Different from prior work, in this paper, we conduct a large scale human evaluation to support our claims, identify the issues with predicted history, and propose rewriting questions to further mitigate the gap to human evaluation. Our analysis begins by examining how error propagation from earlier turns degrades reasoning in later turns. We show that even minor lexical changes in the history can trigger large shifts in retrieval, span selection, and unanswerability detection. These effects are amplified in settings with limited context windows, where truncation disproportionately removes early disambiguating cues. To quantify such effects, we compare performance under gold, beam, and sampled histories across multiple model sizes and decoding strategies (Mandya et al., 2020; Siblini et al., 2021. We further ablate the contributions of answer calibration, turn-level confidence, and context weighting to isolate failure modes. The results indicate that robustness varies markedly by dataset, suggesting differences in topic distribution and paraphrase density. We therefore revisit dataset construction practices and recommend balancing dialog depth, entity reoccurrence, and coreference chains. Beyond input noise, we evaluate whether instruction-tuned models internalize dialog conventions that regularize follow-up questions. We find that models trained with iterative rewriting generate questions that are both more specific and more self-contained. This observation motivates a pipeline in which a question rewriter precedes a standard extractive or generative QA model. Crucially, rewriting must preserve answer equivalence while reducing reliance on brittle cross-turn dependencies. We operationalize this requirement through constrained decoding and automatic consistency checks against retrieved passages. Human raters confirm that rewritten questions reduce ambiguity and improve transparency of failure cases. Finally, we discuss practical implications for evaluation leaders, including dataset refresh cycles and standardized predicted-history protocols. We release guidelines and scripts to facilitate adoption of these practices in future CQA benchmarks.

document: Natural language inference has become a central testbed for evaluating reasoning in neural models. Alongside accuracy improvements, researchers increasingly seek explanations that justify predictions to human readers. Early work formalized desiderata for faithful and plausible explanations and built benchmark protocols. Pioneering model-agnostic explanation techniques demonstrated the value of local rationales for classification decisions (Ribeiro et al., 2016). Subsequent efforts adapted these ideas to neural architectures for text classification and NLI (Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019). In parallel, domain-specific criteria for interpretability were explored in fact verification and question answering (Kumar and Talukdar, 2020). Feature-based explanations quantify the contribution of inputs or representations through gradients, perturbations, or attention (Li et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015; Samek et al., 2017). Attention as explanation remains controversial, but its structure can still provide useful diagnostic signals when carefully analyzed (Jiang et al., 2021). Rationale extraction methods aim to highlight minimal subsets of tokens that preserve label predictions under a compactness constraint (Feng et al., 2018; Gururangan et al., 2018). Sentence-level justifications are attractive because they are concise and easy to evaluate with human judgment (Camburu et al., 2018; Talmor et al., 2018). However, most explanation techniques were originally developed as post hoc addons without training-time integration into the predictor. This separation can weaken faithfulness, since the predictor may exploit features that the explainer cannot capture. Joint learning frameworks address this gap by optimizing predictive performance and rationale quality simultaneously. Policy-gradient and continuous relaxation approaches have been proposed for end-to-end rationale selection in NLI (Lei et al., 2016; Bastings et al., 2019). Prior comparisons reported mixed results — Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018 — depending on dataset shifts and training regimes. Building on multi-task training, some works couple label prediction with auxiliary objectives that reward consistency between predicted labels and extracted evidence (Thorne et al., 2019). Contrastive learning has further encouraged explanations that distinguish entailment from contradiction by maximizing margins between counterfactual rationales (Pruthi et al., 2020). From a modeling standpoint, sentence-level evidence can be selected via retrieval over the premise or an external corpus (DeYoung et al., 2020). Retrieval-driven explanations benefit from dense encoders but require calibration to avoid cherry-picked support (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Evaluation practices for explanations remain unsettled, with tradeoffs between automatic overlap metrics and human studies (Narang and Klein, 2021). To assess faithfulness, causal sufficiency and necessity tests intervene on highlighted tokens and measure output stability (Atanasova et al., 2020). Robustness analyses show that spurious correlations can masquerade as explanations unless models are stress-tested under distribution shifts (Gardner et al., 2021). Recent work emphasizes explanation use as a learning signal rather than a byproduct of prediction (Hase and Bansal, 2020). In NLI, this perspective motivates architectures that exchange information between an inference head and an explanation head during training. Our approach operationalizes this idea via a mutual promotion mechanism that iteratively refines predictions and evidence. Concretely, the evidence selector proposes candidate sentences, the predictor conditions on them, and feedback from prediction updates the selector. This co-training loop resembles expectation–maximization, but it is implemented with differentiable modules for efficiency. We instantiate the selector with a lightweight retriever and the predictor with a pretrained encoder fine-tuned for NLI (Devlin et al., 2019). To mitigate confirmation bias, we regularize against agreement with high-entropy predictions and encourage diversity in selected sentences (Jain et al., 2020). We also integrate calibration objectives so that explanation confidence aligns with predictive uncertainty (Guo et al., 2017). For comparability with prior work, we evaluate on e-SNLI and FEVER and include ablations on training dynamics (Camburu et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2019). Qualitative analysis illustrates when the mechanism helps, especially on long premises with multiple plausible supports Camburu et al. 2018. Overall, the results support the claim that tightly coupling inference and explanation can improve accuracy while producing more faithful justifications.
