2.1 Security and Privacy Concern of Pre-trained Models

Pre-trained models like BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) rely on vast plain-text corpora to achieve human-like performance. While these models excel across benchmarks, their deployment raises acute concerns about the handling of sensitive information embedded in training or inference data. Sensitive domains such as clinical notes (Christoph et al., 2015), search logs (Shen et al., 2007), and other personally identifiable information must be protected to meet ethical and regulatory expectations. Beyond obvious leakage through inputs and outputs, trained parameters themselves can memorize rare or unique strings from the underlying data (Carlini et al., 2021). This phenomenon motivates rigorous analysis of privacy risks specific to modern language models and their training pipelines. In parallel, organizations must navigate legal frameworks like GDPR and HIPAA that impose strict requirements on data minimization and accountability (Voigt and Bussche, 2017; McGraw and Pratt, 2014). As a result, there is growing interest in techniques that enable learning from sensitive data without exposing raw content to centralized servers (Konečný et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2019). Threat models studied in the literature include membership inference, where an adversary predicts whether a record was part of the training set (Shokri et al., 2017; Yeom et al., 2018). Property inference attacks attempt to recover aggregate attributes not intended by the task designer (Ganju et al., 2018). Model inversion and reconstruction attacks aim to reveal textual snippets or attributes of individual examples by querying outputs or gradients (Fredrikson et al., 2015; Song and Shmatikov, 2019). Recent work has further shown verbatim extraction of memorized sequences from large generative models using carefully crafted prompts (Carlini et al., 2023). These risks persist even when models are accessed only through APIs, because adversaries can adaptively probe outputs under black-box constraints. One research direction focuses on training-time defenses based on differential privacy, notably DP-SGD, which bounds worst-case disclosure from any single example (Abadi et al., 2016; Dwork et al., 2014). Complementary approaches leverage federated learning with secure aggregation to keep raw data on device while still enabling global optimization (Konečný et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2017). Knowledge transfer via teacher ensembles provides an alternative route through the PATE framework that privatizes labels before training students (Papernot et al., 2017). For text applications, TextHide proposes mixing hidden representations to obscure instance-level details while retaining task signal (Huang et al., 2020). However, TextHide remains better suited to sentence-level classification and struggles with token-level objectives such as named entity recognition (Huang et al., 2020). Due to its mixup mechanism, the method degrades alignment needed for span extraction and semantic role labeling. Another line of work studies differentially private fine-tuning of large language models, but reports substantial utility loss at practical privacy budgets, as shown by Kerrigan et al., 2020 and Yu et al. (2021). In particular, perplexity and downstream task accuracy can deteriorate sharply as noise multipliers increase and clipping thresholds tighten. Hardware-assisted approaches like trusted execution environments and secure enclaves reduce exposure yet still face side-channel and scalability concerns (Hunt et al., 2018; Costan and Devadas, 2016). Cryptographic solutions using homomorphic encryption or secure multiparty computation offer strong guarantees but are costly in latency and energy for long sequences (Acar et al., 2018; Mohassel and Zhang, 2017). Beyond defenses, pre-publication data curation that removes PII and redacts highly unique strings is essential for responsible dataset construction (Dodge et al., 2021). Post-hoc auditing methods aim to detect and quantify memorization, including extraction probes and influence-function based attribution (Feldman, 2020; Koh and Liang, 2017). Recent auditing frameworks also estimate per-example privacy risk under realistic attack models to inform policy decisions (Song et al., 2021). In industry settings, federated evaluation and on-device personalization are combined with guardrails to reduce the chance of emitting sensitive content at inference time (Bonawitz et al., 2019; Apple, 2022). Nevertheless, open questions remain about balancing utility, cost, interpretability, and verifiable privacy in large-scale NLP systems. Our work situates these concerns within the context of pre-trained transformers used for sensitive domains like healthcare and finance. We survey attack surfaces, evaluate representative defenses, and distill practical recommendations for data stewards and model builders. Specifically, we analyze trade-offs among DP-SGD, federated optimization, representation mixing, and selective redaction using standardized tasks and threat models. We also discuss governance mechanisms, including documentation practices and privacy audits, that complement technical safeguards (Mitchell et al., 2019; Raji et al., 2020). Finally, we outline open research directions in scaling privacy techniques to frontier models while preserving competitiveness on real-world workloads. By grounding privacy evaluation in realistic deployments, we aim to inform principled choices for training and serving pre-trained models responsibly.

document: Pretrained language models have transformed multilingual NLP by enabling transfer across languages with limited supervision (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). Contextual encoders such as ELMo and BERT learn shared subword statistics that generalize beyond monolingual boundaries (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). Multilingual BERT, XLM, and XLM-R demonstrate strong cross-lingual transfer compared to static cross-lingual word embeddings (Pires et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019). Despite these advances, practitioners still face open questions about scalability, typological coverage, and fairness across scripts (Ruder et al., 2019; Lauscher et al., 2020). Early evidence of zero-shot capabilities came from part-of-speech tagging, dependency parsing, and NLI benchmarks (Pires et al., 2019. Follow-up studies analyzed when subword overlap, shared alphabets, and domain effects drive transfer rather than deeper semantic alignment (Karthikeyan et al., 2020; Wu and Dredze, 2020). However, the benefits of adding more pretraining languages do not increase monotonically, a phenomenon dubbed the curse of multilinguality (Conneau et al., 2020). This effect suggests that capacity dilution and negative interference can offset gains from broader coverage (Aharoni et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). In practice, model scaling and data curation strategies can mitigate interference but rarely eliminate it entirely (Xue et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020). Our work revisits these dynamics by disentangling seen and unseen languages during pretraining and evaluation. We ask whether zero-shot performance on held-out languages follows the same saturation pattern observed for languages included in the pretraining mix. To this end, we design controlled partitions over language families and scripts while holding constant the pretraining budget. We also examine whether auxiliary objectives such as translation language modeling or contrastive alignment improve transfer without heavy supervision (Lample and Conneau, 2019; Fang et al., 2020). A key challenge is separating lexical memorization from true structural generalization across typologically distant languages (Ponti et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2019). We therefore incorporate diagnostic tasks that stress morphology, word order, and agreement in addition to sentence-level semantics (Liu et al., 2020; Warstadt et al., 2020). Prior work has reported that language identity conditioning and adaptive vocabulary selection can reduce interference for low-resource targets (Bapna and Firat, 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). Complementary evidence shows that continued pretraining on target-language unlabeled data yields sizable gains over pure zero-shot baselines (Gururangan et al., 2020; Chronopoulou et al., 2020). Yet such adaptations may obscure whether a model possesses inherent cross-lingual abstractions or simply benefits from domain proximity (Ke et al., 2020; Artetxe et al., 2020). Our experimental design emphasizes frozen encoders and standardized evaluation to foreground representational quality over fine-tuning tricks. We validate our splits using language relatedness metrics and corpus similarity measures to rule out spurious advantages. We further probe subword overlap to estimate how much lexical sharing explains transfer under different tokenization regimes (Kudo and Richardson, 2018; Bostrom and Durrett, 2020). Notably, we replicate the headline results on multilingual saturation reported in prior work (Conneau et al., 2020). At the same time, we highlight domains where adding training languages degrades performance on typologically distant held-out languages. For background on early cross-lingual evaluations in contextual models, see Pires et al. (2019). Our findings align with the hypothesis that capacity, data balance, and objective choice interact in nontrivial ways to shape transfer (Rust et al., 2021; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). We also contrast our setup with resource-intensive alignment approaches that rely on parallel data or dictionaries (Ruder et al., 2019; Artetxe et al., 2018). Finally, we situate our contributions relative to the original curse of multilinguality study by Conneau et al. 1 and outline open problems in scaling to truly universal models. Taken together, this paper frames a principled agenda for multilingual representation learning that is sensitive to typology, capacity, and evaluation design.

document: Our goal is to enable end-to-end training of goaldirected dialogue agents. In these settings, an agent aims to complete a particular task with its utterances (Smith and Hipp, 1994). Goal-directed agents have been explored in contexts such as personal assistants (McTear, 2002;Budzianowski et al., 2018;Williams et al., 2014), recommendation systems (Liu et al., 2010;Kang et al., 2019), education (Yuan et al., 2008), and negotiation (He et al., 2018;Lewis et al., 2017). While there are multiple approaches to constructing dialogue agents, in this work we frame the problem of generating dialogue as a sequential decision making problem within a (partially observed) Markov Decision Process (MDP) (Singh et al., 1999;Young et al., 2013). Prior works that utilize such an MDP formulation typically aim to train a dialogue management system (Singh et al., 2002), in which the agent reasons about higher-level abstractions of the state of the conversation, and language generation is performed using a downstream procedure. Dialogue management systems have been trained using techniques such as online reinforcement learning via policy gradients (Gašić et al., 2011;He et al., 2018), off-policy reinforcement learning (Pietquin et al., 2011;Yu et al., 2016) or actor-critic methods (Su et al., 2017). Our method differs from dialogue management systems in that CALM is an end-to-end system optimized for successful task completion, and performs both high-level decision making and language generation. Recent advancements in language models, such as recurrent neural networks (Sundermeyer et al., 2012;Asri et al., 2016;Su et al., 2016;Zhao et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhang et al., 2020) and attention-based architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2018;Brown et al., 2020), have spurred increasing interest in such endto-end dialogue systems (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Adiwardana et al., 2020). Modelbased approaches, in which a learned agent is substituted for a human, allow learning to be done entirely within simulation without human intervention (Li et al., 2016;He et al., 2018;Kang et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018). In contrast to these approaches, CALM augments the traditional language modeling objective with taskspecific rewards in order to finetune a model that is more aware of task goals, which significantly improves performance over a naïve language model without the need for simulating human responses in an interactive training loop. Jaques et al. (2019) recently proposed a model-free, offline approach to undirected dialogue, or dialogue without a specific task goal. Our method differs in that we aim to solve goal-oriented dialogue which allows us to optimize task-specific objectives, and that we take a model-based RL approach which enables us to leverage fine-tuned language models. In goal-oriented dialogue, the agent must reason over belief states and external APIs while maintaining fluent, contextually appropriate responses. Classic pipelines approached this through modular designs separating natural language understanding, state tracking, policy learning, and natural language generation (Young et al., 2013;Williams et al., 2014). End-to-end methods instead seek to train a single parameterization that maps histories to utterances while implicitly representing latent state (Wen et al., 2017;Budzianowski et al., 2018). However, purely likelihood-trained models can overfit to surface statistics and fail to optimize task-level success metrics, motivating reinforcement learning fine-tuning atop pretrained generators (Zhao et al., 2019;Henderson et al., 2008). Our approach follows this line but integrates model-based rollouts and constrained decoding to stabilize optimization during offline fine-tuning Singh et al., 1999; Young et al., 2013 and Williams, 2014). Unlike purely model-free policy gradients, our method exploits a differentiable surrogate of environment dynamics to reduce variance and improve sample efficiency (Janner et al., 2019). We additionally shape rewards to encourage brevity, slot coverage, and user satisfaction signals gathered from historical transcripts (Su et al., 2016;Jaques et al., 2019). From a data perspective, we leverage large conversational corpora for language modeling and smaller task-specific datasets for reinforcement learning, aligning with recent trends in cross-domain pretraining and domain adaptation (Zhang et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020). Related work on controllable generation has explored lexically constrained decoding and plug-and-play control to steer pretrained models without extensive retraining (Hokamp and Liu, 2017;Dathathri et al., 2020). In task-oriented dialogue, similar ideas have been applied to enforce semantic constraints or ontology consistency during generation (Nie et al., 2019;Zhao et al., 2019). However, few studies have coupled such controls with model-based rollouts that explicitly reason over user simulators and database states. We argue that this combination is particularly beneficial when interaction budgets are limited and safe exploration is required. Moreover, offline reinforcement learning mitigates the ethical and practical risks of training with real users by avoiding potentially harmful behaviors during exploration (Levine et al., 2020). Our contributions are threefold: we introduce CALM, a model-based objective for goal-directed dialogue; we provide a training recipe that unifies language modeling and offline RL; and we present an evaluation protocol emphasizing both linguistic quality and task success. We position our work within the broader movement toward aligning generative models with human-defined utilities rather than surrogate proxy losses (Stiennon et al., 2020;Ouyang et al., 2022). Finally, we discuss open challenges including simulator fidelity, exposure bias under constrained decoding, and robustness to distributional shift at deployment (Ramakrishnan et al., 2018;Kumar et al., 2020). Together, these directions chart a path for principled, scalable training of task-oriented dialogue agents that reliably accomplish user goals.

document: Automatic evaluation of open-domain dialogue systems has a multifaceted nature with many fine-grained quality aspects (Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Turn-level aspects show the quality of the system's utterance given a dialogue context from different perspectives including appropriateness, relevance, engagement, etc (Lowe et al., 2017;Tao et al., 2018;Ghazarian et al., 2020). Whereas, conversation-level facets such as coherence, diversity, and informativeness take into account the whole conversation and dialog flow (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2021;Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Dialogue coherence evaluation is pertinent to the discourse coherence as a dialogue is counted as a multi-party discourse. Similar to discourse coherence, many original coherence evaluation metrics are derived from the Centering Model for monitoring the local focus of utterances and the entities distribution over utterances (Grosz and Sidner, 1986;Miltsakaki and Kukich, 2004;Lapata and Barzilay, 2005). A group of studies assess the coherence of dialogues with respect to entities and dialogue acts (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020;Mesgar et al., 2020). Another inspected approach for dialogue coherence evaluation is to represent dialogue in a structured graph format where contextually dependent neighbor utterances or concepts are connected nodes in the graph (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020). Graph convolutional networks are used to complete this task. High-quality training datasets are identified as momentous and indelible components in automatic coherence evaluation. Some previous works construct such datasets by collecting human judgments (Higashinaka et al., 2014;Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). While many recent works rely on a more timely and cost-effective approach by automatically generating negative samples. The utterances of the coherent conversations from human–human interactions are manipulated by shuffling their order, inserting or replacing irrelevant utterances (Vakulenko et al., 2018;Mesgar et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In this work, we show that such changes cannot truly represent machine-generated incoherent conversations. However, metrics that correlate well with human judgments across diverse domains remain elusive. Early reference-based metrics borrowed from machine translation emphasize lexical overlap and penalize paraphrasing disproportionately (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005;Papineni et al., 2002). Recent neural evaluators leverage pretrained language models to estimate response plausibility and consistency (Zhang et al., 2020;Mehri and Eskénazi, 2020). Nevertheless, these proxies are sensitive to prompt length, topicality drift, and calibration errors. A parallel strand of work studies discourse structures via entity grids and rhetorical relations to capture global coherence (Barzilay and Lapata, 2008;Ji and Smith, 2017). For open-domain dialogue, speaker alternation and pragmatic goals introduce additional constraints that challenge document-centric coherence models (Cervone and Riccardi, 2020). Data sparsity further complicates evaluation because high-quality multi-turn annotations are expensive and slow to obtain. To mitigate this, researchers synthesize perturbations such as turn permutation, response truncation, and adversarial topic insertions to diversify negative examples (Mesgar et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). However, automatic corruptions can produce artifacts that evaluators overfit to, thereby inflating reported gains. Comparative studies have begun to advocate for hybrid setups that mix human-curated and procedurally generated test suites to improve robustness [Lowe et al., 2017; Tao, 2018; and Ghazarian et al., 2020). Benchmark governance and transparent reporting standards are equally important to ensure fair comparisons across models and datasets (Dodge et al., 2019). In addition, open-source leaderboards should include confidence intervals, ablations, and error analysis summaries rather than single headline numbers. We argue for a task decomposition that distinguishes local appropriateness from long-range thematic persistence, and we design diagnostics accordingly. Our evaluation protocol integrates content selection, entailment, contradiction, and redundancy checks using both reference-free and reference-based signals. Finally, we release tooling scripts and reproducible splits to facilitate future meta-evaluation studies.

document: We review prior work on visual semantic AI, on the geometry and semantic properties of representations formed by language models, and on semantic intrinsic evaluation tasks. Visual semantic AI investigates how perceptual inputs align with linguistic structure to support reasoning and communication. Early systems focused on supervised image captioning with attention mechanisms that grounded tokens in regions (Xu et al., 2015). Subsequent advances introduced modular reasoning for visual question answering to encourage compositionality (Andreas et al., 2016). Transformer-based multimodal models such as ViLBERT and LXMERT improved cross-modal fusion via co-attention (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Contrastive pretraining further scaled alignment by pairing images and text from the web (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). Despite progress, grounding at the level of fine-grained semantics remains brittle in out-of-distribution settings (Hendricks et al., 2018). A complementary literature examines the geometry of language representations that many multimodal systems inherit from text encoders. Foundational work on distributional semantics established vector spaces where analogy structure can be linearly recovered (Mikolov et al., 2013). Later analyses documented anisotropy and frequency effects that distort similarity judgments (Mu and Viswanath, 2018; Ethayarajh, 2019). Sentence-level encoders have been optimized for isotropy and clustering quality using contrastive objectives (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Probing studies relate representational geometry to syntactic and semantic control tasks (Conneau et al., 2018; Hewitt and Liang, 2019). Within vision-language research, similar probes test whether learned alignments capture attributes, relations, and compositional templates (Goyal et al., 2017; Cirik et al., 2018). Evaluation practices differ, however, between end-to-end task metrics and intrinsic assessments that isolate properties of the embedding space. Classic intrinsic tasks include word similarity, lexical analogy, and clustering, all of which provide fast feedback about semantic organization (Schnabel et al., 2015; Faruqui et al., 2016). More recent benchmarks target sentence retrieval, paraphrase mining, and structure-aware neighborhood tests for hierarchical concepts; see (cf. Vatswani et al., 2019 for discussion. These approaches illuminate how training objectives shape geometry, but they also reveal tensions between alignment and discrimination. For example, aggressive contrastive learning can compress local neighborhoods and harm fine-grained category boundaries (Wang and Isola, 2020). A strand of work argues for inductive biases that preserve manifold structure during multimodal fusion (Kiros et al., 2014; Ganea et al., 2021). Similarly, research on prompt-based adaptation shows that steering textual encoders can modulate cross-modal transfer without retraining backbones (Lester et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021). Survey papers emphasize the need for evaluation protocols that unify extrinsic and intrinsic views for robust model development (Du et al., 2022). In this study we focus on the intersection of visual grounding and semantic geometry to articulate practical criteria for representation quality. In particular, we synthesize findings on anisotropy mitigation, calibration under shift, and compositional generalization as converging objectives (Gao et al., 2019; Miller et al., 2021). We finally outline open challenges in scalable curation, multilingual grounding, and transparent reporting to guide future benchmarks and methods.

document: Neural machine translation has benefited substantially from pretraining and large-scale parallel corpora. Nevertheless, model predictions can remain brittle when test inputs diverge from the training distribution. Retrieval-augmented translation addresses this gap by consulting example-based memories at inference time. Among these approaches, kNN-MT retrieves nearest neighbors from a datastore built over encoder-decoder states to refine the output distribution. While effective, vanilla kNN-MT relies on a fixed neighborhood size, which can be suboptimal across tokens and contexts. Adaptive kNN-MT introduces a lightweight meta-k network that fuses predictions from multiple k values, aiming to balance precision and robustness. This idea connects to classic instance-based learning while retaining the fluency of neural decoders. In practice, an adaptive scheme must decide how to weigh heterogeneous signals when local retrievals are noisy or sparse. It also contends with the construction of the target-side datastore, whose coverage and density crucially influence performance. Prior analyses show that the optimal k varies with token frequency, domain mismatch, and alignment uncertainty. Moreover, the computational overhead of multiple retrievals per step raises concerns for real-time systems. Recent work proposes compact indexes, approximate search, and selective activation to reduce latency without sacrificing accuracy. Another line of research integrates lexical constraints and translation memories within the same retrieval framework. These developments complement efforts to enhance calibration of the fused distribution with temperature scaling and margin-based reweighting. Despite these advances, there remains limited understanding of how to generalize across domains with heterogeneous retrieval quality. Our review highlights architectural choices that determine robustness, including query representations, neighbor filtering, and value aggregation. In adaptive designs, the meta-k network typically observes features derived from distances, vote counts, and contextual embeddings. It then outputs normalized weights over a candidate set of k values, which are used to interpolate probability distributions, as proposed in Zheng et al., 2021a; Khandelwal et al., 2021). When the validation set is small, regularization and monotonicity priors can stabilize the learned weighting. An orthogonal question concerns how to update or prune the datastore as domains evolve over time. Streaming maintenance strategies prevent catastrophic drift while keeping memory usage bounded. In comparison with alternative external memory methods, adaptive kNN-MT preserves token-level transparency that aids error analysis. For example, analysts can inspect retrieved neighbors to attribute specific lexical choices to concrete exemplars. Nevertheless, naive fusion can overemphasize frequent patterns and underrepresent rare but correct alternatives. Curriculum retrieval and diversity-aware sampling have been explored to mitigate this bias. Empirical studies report consistent gains on benchmarks when adaptively combining neighborhoods across granularities. A representative approach leverages a two-layer feed-forward selector with nonlinear activation to compute fusion coefficients. The selector is trained to minimize translation loss while attending to distance profiles and neighbor counts. Under this paradigm, performance hinges on careful validation of the weighting scheme to avoid degeneracy toward a single k. Related work on cache-based language models and memory-augmented decoders offers complementary insights into temporal locality. Furthermore, efficient approximate nearest neighbor search broadens practical adoption by lowering retrieval costs at scale. Open challenges include principled uncertainty estimation for retrieval contributions and domain-adaptive datastore curation. We also note inconsistencies in reported evaluation protocols that complicate fair comparison across systems. To situate our discussion, we reference both the original kNN-MT formulation and its adaptive extensions described in prior work. Overall, adaptive kNN-MT exemplifies how hybridization of parametric and nonparametric evidence can yield resilient translation.

document:
Early work on simplification relied on rules, e.g., to split or shorten long sentences (Chandrasekar and Srinivas, 1997;Carroll et al., 1998;Vickrey and Koller, 2008). Later work treated simplification as a monolingual phrase-based machine translation (MT) task (Coster and Kauchak, 2011;Wubben et al., 2012), with syntactic information added, such as constituency trees (Zhu et al., 2010). Recent work, reviewed below, leverages neural models in a generative and revision-based manner.

Supervised Generative Methods employ Seq2Seq (Sutskever et al., 2014) models to implicitly learn simplification operations from aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Inspired by the success of Seq2Seq neural MT models, Nisioi et al. (2017) applied a Seq2Seq model for text simplification. Zhang and Lapata (2017) used reinforcement learning to optimize a reward based on simplicity, fluency and relevance, building on a Seq2Seq model. Recent methods build on transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) models, by integrating external databases containing simplification rules (Zhao et al., 2018), using an additional loss function to generate diverse outputs (Kriz et al., 2019), combining syntactic rules (Maddela et al., 2021), and conditioning on length and syntactic and lexical complexity features (Martin et al., 2020a).

Unsupervised Generative Methods rely on non-aligned complex and simple corpora instead of aligned complex-simple sentence pairs. Zhao et al. (2020) adopted a back-translation framework, whereas Surya et al. (2019) used an unsupervised style transfer paradigm. Martin et al. (2020b) used a pre-trained BART model fine-tuned on a paraphrased sentence pairs.

Controllable Generative Methods produce outputs at specified grade levels (Scarton and Specia, 2018;Nishihara et al., 2019), or apply syntactic or lexical constraints on the generated sentences (Martin et al., 2020a,b). However, these models do not provide any insights into how the simplification process is done and thus lack interpretability.

Supervised Revision-Based Methods use complex-simple sentence pairs to learn where to apply edit operations iteratively. Alva-Manchego et al. (2017) proposed a model using keep, replace, and delete operations. Some recent work used iterative non-autoregressive models to edit sentences by either predicting token-level editoperations (Omelianchuk et al., 2021) or using a fixed pipeline of edit operations (Agrawal et al., 2021). Dong et al. (2019) proposed a hybrid method with explicit edit operations in an endto-end generative model. Though these methods are more interpretable and controllable than generative methods, they still require complex-simple sentence pairs for training.

Unsupervised Revision-Based Methods such as Narayan and Gardent (2016) may apply a pipeline of edit operations in a fixed order. Kumar et al. (2020) then presented a general unsupervised revision-based approach by modelling text simplification as an unsupervised search problem. While GRS also relies on a revision-based framework and an unsupervised search strategy, we integrate a generative paraphrasing model into the framework to leverage the strengths of both text generation and text revision approaches.

Evaluation of simplification commonly relies on automatic metrics that correlate with human judgments. SARI has become the de facto measure for lexical simplification quality, complementing BLEU and FKGL. Subsequent studies examined content preservation versus simplicity trade-offs in supervised and unsupervised settings (Xu et al., 2016;Alva-Manchego et al., 2020). In (Sulem et al., 2018), human evaluation protocols for grammaticality and meaning retention were consolidated across datasets. Datasets for sentence simplification include aligned corpora derived from Wikipedia and news sources, with high variance in alignment quality (Zhu et al., 2010;Hwang et al., 2015). The Newsela corpus provides professionally simplified articles at multiple grade levels and supports controllable generation research (Xu et al., 2015;Scarton and Specia, 2018). However, licensing restrictions have limited its widespread use in benchmarking. Recent multilingual efforts study cross-lingual simplification where parallel data are scarce (Sheang and Saggion, 2021;Saggion et al., 2022). Such settings demand stronger inductive biases about structure and meaning to avoid degenerate content deletion. Edit-based models have also been coupled with pretrained encoders to improve fluency while preserving control (Dong et al., 2019;Mallinson et al., 2020). Another strand leverages paraphrase identification to filter synthetic pairs for pretraining (Zhang and Lapata, 2017;Martin et al., 2020b). There is growing interest in disentangling operations such as sentence splitting, lexical substitution, and compression to enhance interpretability (Maddela et al., 2021;Aharoni and Goldberg, 2018). Despite promising results, domain shift remains a challenge when models trained on encyclopedic text are applied to instructional or clinical prose (Devaraj et al., 2021). To mitigate this gap, researchers explore domain-adaptive pretraining and constrained decoding calibrated by readability targets (Gururangan et al., 2020;Martin et al., 2020a). Finally, broader impacts include accessibility for language learners and readers with cognitive disabilities, which motivates evaluation beyond surface similarity. This survey synthesizes these directions to motivate models that are both faithful and controllable in their simplification behavior.

document: The pretraining-finetuning paradigm (Howard and Ruder, 2018) shows tremendous success in fewshot NLU tasks. Various methods have been developed such as [CLS] classification Devlin et al., 2018), prompting-based methods with discrete prompts (Schick and Schütze, 2021b;Gao et al., 2020) or continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021b;Shin et al., 2020;Li and Liang, 2021;Lester et al., 2021), and methods that calibrate the output distribution (Yang et al., 2021;Zhao et al., 2021). The fact that few-shot learning is sensitive to many factors and thus is extremely unstable (Liu et al., 2021a;Lu et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2020;Dodge et al., 2020) increases the difficulty of fewshot evaluation. Several works address evaluation protocols to mitigate the effects of instability: Gao et al. (2020) and Liu et al. (2021b) adopt a held-out set to select models. Perez et al. (2021) proposed K-fold cross-validation and minimum description length evaluation strategies. Our work differs from these works on few-shot evaluation in several aspects: (1) we propose three metrics to evaluate data split strategies; (2) while most prior work proposed evaluation protocols without justification, we conduct comprehensive experiments to support our key design choice; (3) we formulate a general evaluation framework; (4) our re-evaluation under the proposed framework leads to several key findings. Bragg et al. (2021) andYe et al. (2021) proposed few-shot NLP benchmarks FLEX and Cross-Fit respectively. Both FLEX and CrossFit study principles of designing tasks, datasets, and metrics, while we focus on addressing inherent problems of the evaluation procedure. We position our framework as complementary to benchmark design, emphasizing the statistical properties of evaluation rather than leaderboard optimization. We analyze how prompt initialization and verbalizer choice interact with sample selection to influence variance and bias. Building on standard risk decomposition, we quantify how different split strategies trade off approximation error and estimation error. We further compare stratified, adversarial, and chronological splits in terms of distributional divergence and training stability. Our analysis highlights when calibration should be applied at inference time versus during training to ensure comparability across models, aligning with observations by Liu et al., 2022). The proposed framework also clarifies how hyperparameter search should be bounded to prevent leakage across splits. Finally, we release code and standardized templates to facilitate reproducible few-shot evaluation in future work.

document: Neural encoder–decoder architectures have underpinned many open-domain dialogue systems. Early end-to-end conversational agents were built on vanilla sequence-to-sequence models (Shang et al., 2015; Vinyals and Le, 2015). Subsequent work introduced hierarchical encoders and context trackers to better model multi-turn structure (Serban et al., 2016; Serban et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2019a). Beyond structure, researchers addressed blandness and repetition with diversity-promoting objectives and sampling strategies (Li et al., 2015; Xing et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017; Tao et al., 2018). Control mechanisms were later explored to steer style, sentiment, and specificity in generated responses (Xu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; See et al., 2019). Persona-based modeling further grounded responses in speaker traits to improve consistency (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). More recently, knowledge-grounded dialogue has emerged as a promising direction to increase informativeness and factuality. Knowledge can be sourced from structured graphs such as commonsense and encyclopedic resources (Zhou et al., 2018b; Moon et al., 2019; Tuan et al., 2019). Alternatively, systems may retrieve spans from unstructured documents and condition generation on evidence passages (Dinan et al., 2019; Lian et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020a; Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Visual context, including images and situated cues, has also been integrated to enrich dialogue grounding (Mostafazadeh et al., 2017; Shuster et al., 2018; Huber et al., 2018). While retrieval and fusion have improved content selection, expressing knowledge faithfully and coherently remains challenging. In this work, we focus on document-grounded dialogue generation with explicit mechanisms for representing and realizing evidence. We argue that modeling the structure of how evidence is verbalized is as important as selecting the right evidence. The idea of sequence modeling via segmentation offers a principled way to capture latent template structure in text. In the text segmentation literature, researchers proposed probabilistic segmental models and the Sleep-WAke Network to model sequences as compositions of segments. In machine translation, neural phrase-based decoders have been explored to capture target-side phrasal structure using segmental generation. For data-to-text generation, a neural HSMM decoder enables template-like realizations with tractable learning via dynamic programming (Wiseman et al., 2018). To relax the weak Markov assumption in segment transitions, later work explicitly segments targets, aligns fragments to records, and jointly learns segmentation and correspondence (Shen et al., 2020). These advances suggest that segmental abstractions can facilitate controllable and faithful surface realization in knowledge-grounded dialogue. Compared with standard token-level decoders, segment-aware models can plan content units before lexicalization. They can also better aggregate redundant evidence by operating at the level of phrases and propositions. In document-grounded settings, planning segments that correspond to salient claims, entities, and relations can improve factual precision. Moreover, separating planning from realization allows the system to paraphrase evidence without drifting semantically. We therefore study a framework that first induces a latent segment plan and then conditions a generator on that plan and retrieved passages. Our approach complements dense retrieval and reranking modules commonly used in prior work (Dinan et al., 2019. It also interfaces naturally with persona and style controls developed in controllable text generation (Xu et al., 2019). For evaluation, we emphasize both automatic metrics and human judgments that capture faithfulness, specificity, and discourse quality. We situate our study among strongly related persona-grounded and document-grounded dialogue systems and analyze when segmental planning yields gains. Finally, we discuss limitations and future directions, including improved segment induction and tighter coupling with retrieval.

document: Toxicity detection systems are typically trained on datasets annotated at the post level, where each comment is labeled as toxic or non-toxic (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Pavlopoulos et al., 2017c; Zampieri et al., 2019). Such post-level annotations enable robust benchmarking but conflate the presence of toxicity with its exact location in text (Wulczyn et al., 2017; Borkan et al., 2019). In contrast, toxic span detection targets the specific substrings that express abusive or harmful content rather than whole-document labels. Framing the problem as span extraction aligns it with attribution and rationale paradigms in explainable NLP (Li et al., 2016; Ribeiro et al., 2016). Despite this alignment, prior work has largely emphasized document-level moderation pipelines and coarse-grained interventions. Finer-grained annotations can support user education, content redaction, and context-preserving moderation actions in community platforms. However, collecting reliable span-level labels introduces challenges in annotation guidelines, inter-annotator agreement, and privacy preservation. We therefore propose a new corpus and evaluation setup that emphasize high-quality span supervision under realistic social media conditions. Our dataset includes posts from multiple domains, accompanied by character-offset spans and rationales linking spans to taxonomy categories. To mitigate privacy risks, we enforce anonymization protocols during curation and release the final resources under a permissive CC0 licence. At the modeling level, span extraction invites comparisons with sequence labeling and span proposal architectures common in information extraction. Recent advances in pretrained encoders and token classification offer strong starting points for this task (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Nevertheless, vanilla token-level cross-entropy can reward overly diffuse predictions that fail to capture compact abusive expressions. We address this by incorporating loss functions that balance precision and recall over contiguous spans and by evaluating with boundary-aware metrics. As related work in content moderation has shown, contextual cues and pragmatics are crucial for disambiguating sarcasm, slurs, and profanities (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). Building on earlier efforts in span-level supervision, Pavlopoulos et al. demonstrated the feasibility of annotating toxic substrings in community data. Multi-task and multi-label strategies can further exploit hierarchical relations between toxicity categories and linguistic phenomena (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Founta et al., 2018). Positive-unlabeled learning and debiasing approaches are also relevant given pervasive label noise and representation disparities across demographic groups (Davidson et al., 2017; Sap et al., 2019). A key difference from document-level toxicity detection is the need for evaluation protocols that penalize spurious highlights while tolerating minor boundary deviations. Accordingly, we design span-level F1, overlap-aware IoU, and character-level precision metrics to complement token-based scoring. We benchmark a suite of sequence taggers, span proposal models, and post-hoc explanation methods adapted for extraction rather than classification. For baseline comparability, we reproduce established dataset splits and hyperparameter settings reported in recent shared tasks (Zampieri et al., 2020; Pitenis et al., 2020). Complementing automated measures, we collect human judgments on highlight usefulness and harm-minimization effects in redaction scenarios. Our contributions include a new public dataset, a rigorous evaluation suite, and extensive analyses that reveal strengths and failure modes of current approaches.

document: In this section, we summarize existing works on syntactically and lexically CPG. Syntactic CPG generates a paraphrase constrained by a prespecified sentence of a certain syntactic structure, namely an exemplar. However, the exemplar is only available during inference, resulting in a key challenge: obtaining manual exemplars for existing paraphrasing training datasets is prohibitively expensive. To address this, some previous works construct syntactic features from target sentences during training, such as POS tags, constituent trees, and masked templates, as illustrated in Table X. For instance, SCPN (Iyyer et al., 2018) makes the first attempt to introduce a linearized constituent tree (LCT) of the target sentence into paraphrasing, where the LCT is predicted based on predefined parse templates. Similarly, GuiG (Li et al., 2020) proposes two models to expand a partial template LCT and to generate paraphrases, respectively. Different from using LCT, SGCP (Kumar et al., 2020) introduces a graph encoder to encode the constituent tree of the exemplar as the condition. Besides, masked templates replace several words of the exemplar with a special token to form a controllable template. For example, BCPG (Liu et al., 2020b) follows BERT (Devlin et al., 2019 to randomly mask exemplar words, and ParafraGPT (Bui, 2019) makes the first attempt to integrate keywords with a copy mechanism. Despite these advances, existing works typically focus on a single conditioning signal under either lexical or syntactic CPG. In comparison, GCPG jointly includes lexical and syntactic CPG, flexibly combining conditions in a unified framework. Lexically controlled paraphrase generation usually specifies desired keywords, synonyms, or constraints such as inclusion or exclusion lists (Mao et al., 2020). Keyword-guided seq2seq models enforce coverage via copy mechanisms or constrained decoding (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). Exemplar-based paradigms condition on a retrieved prototype sentence to steer structure while preserving meaning (Guu et al., 2018; Weston et al., 2018). In paraphrase tasks, structural control aims to disentangle syntax from semantics so that models can reuse target-side structural priors (Zhang and Bowman, 2019). Recent transformer backbones have further reduced the cost of control via prompting and soft constraints (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). Nevertheless, aligning heterogeneous controls remains challenging because lexically feasible tokens may conflict with syntactic realizations when fluency is enforced by beam search (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019). To mitigate this, several studies formulate control as a multi-source problem with learned fusion between exemplar encoders and lexical planners (Bawden et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). Other work applies constrained decoding using finite-state controllers or differentiable relaxations to satisfy hard lexical constraints while respecting parse templates (Anderson et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2019). Beyond architecture, training data scarcity for paired exemplars has prompted weak supervision, back-translation, and retrieval-augmented pretraining (Edunov et al., 2018; Lewis et al., 2020). Evaluation remains difficult because BLEU and ROUGE under-reward structural control, leading to the adoption of parse-accuracy, tree-edit distance, and targeted acceptability judgments (Sulem et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). For fairness, researchers also examine paraphrase faithfulness with entailment and semantic similarity metrics to detect meaning drift under strong control (Wieting et al., 2019; Maynez et al., 2020). In practical deployments, controllable paraphrasing supports data augmentation, privacy-preserving text transformation, and controllable simplification (Kumar et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2022). Our study situates GCPG within this line by unifying structural templates, lexical keys, and exemplar graphs under a single training objective that preserves semantic equivalence. We next detail the problem formulation, modeling choices, and evaluation protocols used to assess controllability and faithfulness across diverse benchmarks.

document: Lifelong Learning for PLMs. Lifelong learning aims at incrementally acquiring new knowledge, and in the meantime, mitigating the catastrophic forgetting issue. Numerous efforts have been spent towards this goal, including (1) memory-based methods (Rebuffi et al., 2017;Rolnick et al., 2019), which perform experience replay with authentic data (de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019), automatically generated data (Sun et al., 2020), or previously computed gradients (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017) conserved in the memory, (2) consolidationbased methods (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017;Aljundi et al., 2018), which introduce additional regularization terms to consolidate the model parameters that are important to previous tasks, and (3) dynamic architecture methods (Rusu et al., 2016;Yoon et al., 2018), which fix trained network architectures in old tasks and dynamically grow branches for new tasks. Lifelong learning is also a hot topic for PLMs. Some target at domain adaptation through continual pre-training (Gururangan et al., 2020), parameter-efficient adapters (He et al., 2021) and sparse expert models (Gururangan et al., 2021). Others focus on the incremental acquisition of factual knowledge that changes over time (Dhingra et al., 2021;Jang et al., 2021). However, the existing works seldom consider our lifelong learning setting where streaming data from multiple sources is sequentially gathered. A concurrent work conducts empirical studies on conventional continual learning algorithms for PLM adaptation. However, they do not focus on PLM's training efficiency, which is different from our setting. More detailed comparisons are left in appendix F. Efficient Pre-training in NLP. Many attempts have been made towards improving the efficiency of pre-training, such as designing novel pretraining tasks (Clark et al., 2020), model architectures (Zhang and He, 2020), optimization algorithms (You et al., 2020) and parallel architectures (Shoeybi et al., 2019;Shazeer et al., 2018). Until recently, researchers propose to "back distill" the knowledge from existing PLMs to accelerate large PLMs' pre-training (Qin et al., 2021). Related advances in curriculum pre-training emphasize staged corpora selection as a lever for efficiency, see Gururangan et al. 2021 1 for a broader survey. Another line of work proposes progressive training to dynamically expand an existing PLM's size through parameter recycling (Gong et al., 2019;Chen et al., 2021). However, these methods typically focus on training PLMs on one static corpus, and thus cannot be directly applied to our lifelong pre-training setting.

document: Semantic parsing via paraphrasing began with Berant and Liang (2014), who proposed completing the mapping through an intermediate natural-language reformulation. Subsequent work generated canonical paraphrase candidates from grammars and scored partial derivations against target formal languages (Wang et al., 2015; Marzoev et al., 2020). However, these methods largely predated and therefore did not exploit pretrained sequence models for generating paraphrases. Compositional generalization remains central because formal languages are inherently compositional. Approaches inject inductive bias via modular architectures (Dong and Lapata, 2018), symbolic-neural hybrids, and latent-variable or intermediate representations (Zheng and Lapata, 2020) alongside program-like learning signals (Lake, 2019). A complementary line performs data augmentation to expand combinatorial coverage before training (Andreas, 2019; Zhong et al., 2020; Akyürek et al., 2020). Pretrained encoders and decoders have also improved compositional parsing performance (Oren et al., 2020; Furrer et al., 2020). Prompt-based methods span tuning-free cloze prompting (Petroni et al., 2019; Shin et al., 2020) and few-shot in-context learning (Brown et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2021). Parameter-efficient prompt tuning reduces adaptation cost while preserving strong performance (Li and Liang, 2021; Schucher et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Liu et al., 2021b), and fixed-prompt LM tuning aligns with our setting. Related work also explores prompt decomposition during tuning-free prompting by Mishra et al., 21, with corroborating results on other NLP tasks. In parallel, zero-shot pretrained models show stronger out-of-distribution robustness than fine-tuned counterparts, motivating weight ensembling and model editing for robustness transfer (Wortsman et al., 2021; Mitchell et al., 2021).

document: Textual entailment asks whether a premise entails, contradicts, or is neutral with respect to a hypothesis (Dagan et al., 2006). The task is widely known as Natural Language Inference. Recent advances rely on large pre-trained language models that achieve strong performance on standardized benchmarks (Lan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; He et al., 2021). These models are typically fine-tuned on manually annotated datasets such as SNLI, MNLI, FEVER, and ANLI (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018; Thorne et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2020). Despite these gains, generalization and robustness remain open challenges for NLI systems. Prompt-based and pivot-task learning have been proposed to address data scarcity in downstream classification tasks (Le Scao and Rush, 2021; Min et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021a). Both discrete prompts and continuous prompts have produced sizable improvements across tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021a; Schick and Schütze, 2021b; Schick and Schütze, 2021c; Liu et al., 2021b). Related lines of work leverage high-resource supervised tasks such as question answering or entailment as pivots to bootstrap low-resource tasks (Yin et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021; Sainz and Rigau, 2021; McCann et al., 2018). Early studies even converted question answering examples into entailment pairs, both manually and automatically (Dagan et al., 2006; Demszky et al., 2018). Other semantic tasks including named entity recognition, relation extraction, and semantic role labeling have been reformulated as entailment through automatic conversion of labels and instances (White et al., 2017; Poliak et al., 2018a; Levy et al., 2017). Multi-task learning with a unified prompting interface aggregates heterogeneous datasets to improve target performance (Subramanian et al., 2018; Raffel et al., 2020; Aribandi et al., 2021). Zero-shot and few-shot settings particularly benefit from task-unified training when prompts are well designed (Sanh et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021a). Nevertheless, effectiveness can depend strongly on model size and pretraining scale. For instance, contradictory results across similar setups have been reported for instruction tuning and prompting (Wei et al., 2021a; Mishra et al., 2021). Using question answering itself as a pivot has shown mixed outcomes in multi-task scenarios (McCann et al., 2018). In this work we explore multi-source learning, where datasets drawn from related tasks inform a model for the target entailment task. Event Argument Extraction is a subtask of event extraction concerned with identifying argument fillers for predefined roles in event templates. Historically, the task was explored in the Message Understanding Conferences and later in the ACE Program (Grishman and Sundheim, 1996; Doddington et al., 2004). Earlier evaluations emphasized sentence-level extraction due to annotation and modeling challenges. More recent benchmarks such as RAMS and WikiEvents emphasize document-level context for argument identification (Ebner et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021b). Despite this shift, much research continues to focus on sentence-level modeling. End-to-end event extraction models have integrated argument identification within unified architectures (Wadden et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021a), (Li et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2019. Other approaches treat EAE as an independent module or as a step in a pipeline to reduce error propagation (Du and Cardie, 2020a; Lyu et al., 2021). Prompt-based methods have reframed EAE as question answering or constrained generation with task-specific templates and verbalizations (Feng et al., 2020; Du and Cardie, 2020b; Wei et al., 2021b; Lyu et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021). In contrast, we cast EAE as textual entailment to exploit a uniform decision interface across roles and event types. Casting diverse information extraction problems as entailment can simplify annotation schemas and consolidate training losses. Moreover, shared entailment representations can encourage cross-task transfer when label spaces overlap partially. However, hypothesis-only and lexical overlap artifacts can inflate performance if not carefully controlled (Poliak et al., 2018b; Gururangan et al., 2018). Calibration choices, verbalizer design, and soft-prompt initializations all influence downstream gains (Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Data augmentation, counterfactual rewriting, and adversarial training further improve robustness in entailment-style reformulations (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2021). When aggregating multiple sources, domain shift and dataset imbalance introduce optimization instabilities (Ben-David et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2019). Mixture-weighting schedules and curriculum strategies can mitigate negative interference during joint training (Wu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020). We hypothesize that entailment-pivoted EAE particularly benefits from leveraging document-level evidence and cross-sentence coreference. To test this hypothesis, we design role-conditioned hypotheses that verbalize argument expectations for each event type. We evaluate the approach on RAMS, WikiEvents, and ACE subsets under both in-domain and cross-domain splits. Our analysis highlights consistent gains in low-resource regimes and clarifies failure modes for future research.

document: There have been a series of attempts to utilize phonetic representations of language to improve or extend automatic speech recognition (ASR) models. Some of these jointly model text and audio data using sequences of phonemes combined with sequences of text characters. Sundararaman et al. (2021), for example, uses a joint transformer architecture that encodes sequences of phonemes and sequences of text simultaneously. However, this joint model is utilized to learn representations that are more robust to transcription errors. The architecture still requires text inputs from ASR transcriptions and generates outputs in both text and phoneme representations. In contrast, our approach allows for text input, audio input, or text plus audio input to language models. Baevski et al. (2021) transforms unlabeled text, not aligned with corresponding audio files, into phonemes in a scheme to train speech recognition models without any labeled data. This scheme involves a generator model trained jointly with a discriminator model. The generator converts audio, segmented into phonetic units, into predicted phonemes, and the discriminator attempts to separate these from phonemes transliterated from unlabeled text. Although both text and audio are utilized in this work, they are not input to the same model and the primary output is a model that creates good phonetic speech representations from audio. Outside of speech recognition focused work, Shen et al. (2020) attempt to fuse audio and text at the word level for emotion recognition. They introduce another architecture that internally represents both modalities, but the WISE framework relies on speech recognition to generate the text corresponding to audio frames in real time. The current work explicitly avoids reliance on speech recognition and targets flexible conditioning on phonetic content (cf. Shen et al., 2020. The 2021 Multimodal Sentiment Analysis challenge continues this vein of research integrating audio, video, text, and physiology data in an emotion recognition task (Stappen et al., 2021). Contributions to this challenge, such as Vlasenko et al. (2021), explore a variety of ways to fuse audio and text inputs but remain focused on sentiment analysis. Complementary efforts on “textless” NLP train language models directly on audio units without any text, advancing self-supervised speech methods but not providing the flexibility we pursue (Lakhotia et al., 2021).

document: Prior work leveraging Knowledge Graphs for knowledge-intensive NLP can be grouped into retrieval-augmented and parameterized-knowledge approaches. Retrieval-augmented systems consult external structured or textual memory during inference to ground predictions. Representative methods include the retrieval-augmented REALM and the fact-indexed FILM (Guu et al., 2020; Verga et al., 2021. FILM extends BERT with an auxiliary memory module for storing and attending to facts. REALM pre-trains a retriever and reader jointly to surface evidence on the fly for downstream tasks. In contrast, parameterized-knowledge approaches aim to internalize KG information into model weights. ERNIE learns with masked entities and relations over aligned text–triple corpora to infuse structured semantics. KnowBert incorporates Wikipedia and WordNet via entity embeddings and knowledge attention within a BERT backbone. BERT-MK adapts graph contextual features from a medical KG to demonstrate the value of graph-level knowledge. COMET trains a commonsense generator that predicts textual assertions conditioned on KG edges and node types. KELM converts triples into natural sentences to augment pretraining data for language models. Beyond architecture, recent work examines how retrieval and parameterization trade off efficiency, controllability, and robustness. Retrieval modules can improve factuality but may incur latency and sensitivity to index drift. Conversely, fully internalized models offer fast inference yet risk hallucinating or overgeneralizing when data are sparse. Hybrid methods therefore explore lightweight retrieval hints that guide parametric decoders without heavy external memory. T5-style encoder–decoders have been widely adopted for KG-to-text and text-to-KG tasks Raffel et al., 2019). For example, template-conditioned generation can verbalize relations while preserving entity surface forms for downstream linking. Our work follows this line by probing how compact adapters can absorb schema-level knowledge without retrievers.

Our proposed semantic perturbation functions generalize the existing textual adversarial attacks.

For typo-based perturbation function F T , ex-isting work (Li et al., 2018;Ebrahimi et al., 2017) applies character-level perturbation to carefully crafted typo words (e.g., from "foolish" to "fo0lish"), thus making the model ignore or misunderstand the original statistical cues. Knowledge-based perturbation function F K uses knowledge base to constrain the search space. For example, Zang et al. (2020) uses sememe-based knowledge base from HowNet (Dong et al., 2010) to construct a search space for word substitution.

Different from our contextualized semantic perturbation function F C , other work (Jin et al., 2020;Li et al., 2018) uses a non-contextualized word embedding from GLoVe (Pennington et al., 2014) or Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) to build synonym candidates, by querying the cosine similarity or euclidean distance between the original and candidate word and selecting the closet ones as the replacements. However, some antonyms also have high cosine similarity in the Word2Vec space. Thus, additional hand-crafted filtering rules are needed to ensure that the meaning is not changed.

Other work (Garg and Ramakrishnan, 2020; Li et al., 2020) also leverages BERT to generate contextualized perturbations by masked language modeling, which is a parallel work to SemAttack, where we explore the BERT embedding clusters to generate high-quality adversarial examples.

In terms of optimization, unlike the heuristicbased previous work that uses greedy (Jin et al., 2020) or genetic algorithms (Zang et al., 2020) to search for the optimal perturbations or other gradient-based methods (Wang et al., 2020) searching for perturbation on a tree-autoencoder with only syntactic constraints, we use an optimizationbased method to efficiently and effectively search for the optimal adversarial perturbation in the semantic preserving spaces to ensure the validity and naturalness of perturbed sentences.

Early adversarial methods targeted sentiment analysis and machine translation, demonstrating transferability across models and domains (Ebrahimi et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2018). Subsequent research emphasized semantic preservation by constraining edits with lexical resources, paraphrase databases, or embedding neighborhoods (Alzantot et al., 2018; Ribeiro et al., 2020). Despite progress, evaluation protocols remain inconsistent, with varying constraints on fluency, label invariance, and human judgment (Pruthi et al., 2019; Michel et al., 2019). Contextualized encoders enable perturbations that align with sentence-level meaning rather than surface form alone (Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Works also examine robustness to paraphrases and counterfactuals by leveraging task-specific data augmentation (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). However, alignment between candidate generation and selection criteria is often weak, yielding adversaries that exploit annotation artifacts instead of model brittleness (Gururangan et al., 2018). Our framework unifies these lines by explicitly modeling semantic neighborhoods under task-aware constraints while preserving fluency via masked language modeling. We further analyze how different search objectives trade off between attack success, semantic fidelity, and runtime, providing a principled view of robustness under resource limits (Croce et al., 2020). In contrast to purely heuristic pipelines, Morris et al., 2020 documented the pitfalls of ad hoc evaluation and motivated the need for standardized reporting. Building on this perspective, we report human studies that measure both grammaticality and perceived meaning change alongside automatic metrics. Prior work has also compared white-box and black-box search strategies, highlighting gradient masking and query efficiency as key confounders (Li et al., 2020; Wallace et al., 2019). Our optimization objective incorporates a differentiable proxy for textual edits to reduce the dependence on brittle combinatorial search (Zang et al., 2020). We complement this with candidate pruning based on task logits and semantic similarity to avoid cascading errors during multi-token modifications (Jin et al., 2020). To ensure reproducibility, we release code, hyperparameters, and attack configurations consistent with recent robustness checklists (Ribeiro et al., 2020). The proposed approach is orthogonal to defense mechanisms such as adversarial training and consistency regularization, and can thus serve as a diagnostic tool (Miyato et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2020). We evaluate across diverse tasks including sentiment analysis, natural language inference, and paraphrase detection using widely adopted benchmarks (Wang et al., 2018; Conneau et al., 2018). For each task, we compare against strong baselines that include typo noise, synonym substitution, and masked language model proposals (Ebrahimi et al., 2017; Garg and Ramakrishnan, 2020). Error analysis reveals that models with larger pretraining corpora are less susceptible to character noise but remain vulnerable to subtle antonymy flips. We also find that robustness correlates with calibration under distribution shift, suggesting complementary benefits from uncertainty-aware training (Guo et al., 2017; Ovadia et al., 2019). Finally, we discuss ethical implications of releasing attack code and provide guidelines for responsible use in downstream applications (Heaven, 2020; Floridi and Cowls, 2019). Overall, our contributions include a principled formulation of semantic perturbation, an efficient optimization strategy, and comprehensive evaluations over multiple tasks and models. We hope this work stimulates more rigorous, semantically grounded robustness research in natural language processing.

document:
PLMs are at the core of many success stories in natural language processing (NLP). However, it remains unclear to what extent PLMs understand the syntactic and semantic properties of the human language. A series of probing tasks have been conducted on PLMs and have found them lacking or falling short on some language properties. Among the many findings of these probing tasks, PLMs have been found to be insensitive to the order of sentences when generating representations (Pham et al., 2020;Gupta et al., 2021;Sinha et al., 2021a), struggle to comprehend number-related representations (Wallace et al., 2019;Lin et al., 2020;Nogueira et al., 2021), and display a lack of semantic content understanding (Ravichander et al., 2020;Elazar et al., 2021).

In addition to the above faulty behaviours, Ettinger et al. and Kassner and Schütze 2020 show that PLMs fail to comprehend negation, which is an important property of language in many natural language understanding (NLU) tasks. Ettinger (2020) checks the ability of PLMs to understand the meaning of negation in given contexts. In their work, they check whether models are sensitive in their completions of sentences that either include negation or not. Under normal circumstances, the completions are expected to vary in truth depending on the presence or absence of negation in given sentences. Their results show that PLMs are insensitive to the impacts of negations when completing sentences. Kassner and Schütze (2020) construct the negated LAMA dataset by inserting negation elements (e.g., "not") in the LAMA cloze questions (Petroni et al., 2019). They use negated and original question pairs to query PLMs and establish that models are equally prone to make the same predictions for both the original and negated questions. In a well-informed setting, it is expected that PLMs should make different predictions for the original and negated questions. This shows that PLMs struggle to comprehend negation.

In light of the highlighted faulty behaviours of PLMs, especially their struggle to comprehend negation, Hosseini et al. (2021) propose a remedy to alleviate the problem. In their remedy, they augment the language modelling objective with an unlikelihood objective (Welleck et al., 2019) based on negated sentences from the training corpus. They use a syntactic augmentation method to generate negated sentences. In this method, the dependency parse of the sentences, POS tags and morphological information of each word are taken as input and the negation of sentences is done using sets of dependency tree regular expression patterns, such as Semgrex (Chambers et al., 2007). During training, they replace objects in negated sentences with [MASK] tokens and use unlikelihood training to make the masked-out tokens unlikely under the PLM distribution. To ensure that negated sentences are factually false, they use the corresponding positive sentences as context for the unlikelihood prediction task.

Previous studies, e.g., Kassner and Schütze (2020), have mostly limited the scope of the logical negation property only to the negation expressions (e.g., "no" and "not"). However, the core spirit of the property is opposite-meaning, which is not only limited to negation. Welleck et al. (2019) consider negating sentences using dependency tree regular expression patterns. This widens the scope of negation, as it is not only limited to negation expressions "no" and "not". However, their approach relies on other components, such as Semgrex, and dependency and POS parsers which could impact the quality of the data, hence impact the models' performance; in this work, we consider other perturbation methods to generate the opposite-meaning sentences to investigate whether PLMs satisfy the logical negation property, and we propose a remedy called intermediate-training on meaning-matching (IM 2 ) that hardly employs additional linguistic components.

document: Few-shot methods are a recent development in English style transfer, with prior work using variational autoencoders (Xu et al., 2020) or prompting large pretrained language models at inference (Reif et al., 2021). Most related is the state-of-the-art TextSETTR model from Riley et al. (2021), who use a neural style encoder to map exemplar sentences to a vector used to guide generation. To train this encoder, they use the idea that adjacent sentences in a document have a similar style. Recently, the Universal Rewriter (Garcia et al., 2021) extended TextSETTR to 101 languages, developing a joint model for translation, few-shot style transfer, and stylized translation. This model remains the primary few-shot system outside English and serves as our main baseline. We discuss its shortcomings in Section 3.1 and propose targeted fixes in Section 4. Multilingual style transfer is mostly unexplored in prior work, as a survey by Briakou et al. (2021b) found only one work in Chinese, Russian, Latvian, Estonian, and French. They further introduced XFORMAL, the first formality transfer evaluation dataset in French, Brazilian Portuguese, and Italian. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to study style transfer for the languages we consider. Our study emphasizes robust evaluation under few-shot constraints and explicit control over stylistic dimensions. We also highlight the importance of disentangling content preservation from stylistic fidelity in multilingual settings. Prior studies on controllable generation demonstrate that prompts and lightweight adapters can steer outputs without full retraining (Xu et al., 2020; Reif et al., 2021. However, these techniques have rarely been tested across typologically diverse languages. Resource disparity further complicates cross-lingual generalization for low-resource scripts and morphologies. We therefore investigate transfer mechanisms that share representations while accommodating language-specific variation. Our approach builds on encoder–decoder architectures that accept exemplars as style anchors. In this setup, we condition generation on a small set of demonstrations drawn from the target style. We complement this conditioning with lexical and syntactic constraints to protect content. For evaluation, we combine automatic metrics with human judgments that separately score style, fluency, and adequacy. We additionally probe calibration under distribution shift by varying exemplar selection policies. A key challenge is the brittleness of few-shot prompts to exemplar choice and ordering. We mitigate this issue using retrieval over style-labeled corpora to select representative prototypes. Our experiments consider both zero-shot and few-shot regimes to map the data–performance frontier. We also analyze error modes that arise from script mixing, honorific systems, and register-specific idioms. Finally, we study robustness to code-switching and dialectal variation within the same language. Our contributions include new multilingual benchmarks, improved training recipes, and ablations that clarify design trade-offs. We release models and evaluation scripts to facilitate replication and extension. Taken together, these results provide the first comprehensive account of few-shot multilingual style transfer with strong baselines.

document: Automatic ICD coding is an important task in the medical NLP community. Earlier works use machine learning to map clinical narratives to billing codes. The task poses unique challenges due to the extreme label space, long documents, and skewed code distributions. Traditional approaches relied on rule-based systems and sparse linear classifiers with handcrafted features (Perotte et al., 2014; Koopman et al., 2015). Neural models improved performance by learning representations of clinical notes and modeling label dependencies (Mullenbach et al., 2018; Xie and Lu, 2019). Attention mechanisms and hierarchical encoders enable the model to focus on salient spans within lengthy discharge summaries (Rios and Kavuluru, 2018; Baumel et al., 2018). More recently, pretrained language models have become the dominant backbone for ICD coding (Huang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2021). Despite these advances, generalization across institutions and coding schemes remains limited, particularly when code descriptions are ambiguous or rare. Researchers have explored zero-shot and few-shot strategies that leverage code descriptions and ontological structure to mitigate data sparsity (Xu et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020). Multi-task and multi-view learning further integrate auxiliary signals such as section headers, entity spans, and clinical ontologies (Shi et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2021). However, evaluation practices vary widely across datasets, preprocessing pipelines, and metric choices, complicating direct comparison of reported results (Johnson et al., 2016; Harutyunyan et al., 2019). In particular, many studies optimize micro-F1 over frequent codes while overlooking macro-level performance that reflects rare and clinically meaningful labels. A complementary thread investigates label correlations and hierarchical regularization to capture the ICD taxonomy (Cao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Some works propose graph neural networks to propagate information along code hierarchies and enrich representations with description semantics (Li and Yu, 2020; Peng et al., 2022). Domain shift and privacy constraints have also motivated federated and continual learning approaches for cross-hospital deployment (Silva et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). Nonetheless, reproducibility is hampered by inconsistent reporting of code filters, note truncation strategies, and model hyperparameters (cf. Johnson et al., 2020; Liu and Wang, 2019; Chen, 2018. To address these issues, we conduct a standardized evaluation across public datasets with unified preprocessing and metrics. Our study synthesizes prior advances while offering a robust benchmark that clarifies where current methods succeed and where open problems remain.
