Pre-trained Language Models (LMs) have been proved to encode knowledge that, with task-specific guidance, can solve natural language understanding tasks (Petroni et al., 2019). Leveraging that, Le Scao and Rush (2021) quantified a reduction in the need of labeled data of hundreds of instances with respect to traditional fine-tuning approaches (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). This has led to quality improvements in zero and few-shot learning.

Semantic Similarity methods Gabrilovich and Markovitch (2007) and Chang et al. (2008) use the explicit meaning of the label names to compute the similarity with the input text. Recent advances in pre-trained LMs and their application to semantic textual similarity tasks, such as Sentence-BERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), have shown a new opportunity to increase the quality of these methods and set the stage for this work. Chu et al. (2020) employ a technique called unsupervised label-refinement (LR). They incorporated a modified k-means clustering algorithm for refining the outputs of cross attention and Siamese Networks. We incorporate LR into our experiments and extend the analysis of their work. We evaluate it against more extensive and diverse benchmarks. In addition, we show that pre-training few-shot learners on their proposed textual similarity task NatCat underperforms pre-training on NLI datasets.

Prompt-based methods GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), a 175 billion parameter LM, has been shown to give good quality on few-shot learning tasks. Pattern-Exploiting Training (PET) (Schick and Schütze, 2021) is a more computational and memory efficient alternative. It is based on ensembles of smaller masked language models (MLMs) and was found to give few-shot results similar to GPT-3. Logan IV et al. (2021) reduced the complexity of finding optimal templates in PET by using null prompts and achieved competitive performance. They incorporated BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2021) and thus reached comparable accuracy fine-tuning only 0.1% of the parameters of the LMs. Hambardzumyan et al. (2021) present a contemporary approach with a similar idea to label tuning. As in our work, they use label embeddings initialized as the verbalization of the label names. These task-specific embeddings, along with additional ones that are inserted into the input sequence, are the only learnable parameters during model training. They optimize a cross-entropy loss between the label embeddings and the output head of a MLM. The major difference is that they employ a prompt-based approach while our method relies on embedding models.

Entailment methods The entailment approach (Yin et al., 2019; Halder et al., 2020) uses the label description to reformulate text classification as textual entailment. The model predicts the entailment probability of every label description. Wang et al. (2021) report results outperforming LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), an approach similar to PET.

True Few-Shot Learning Setting Perez et al. (2021) argue that for true few-shot learning, one should not tune parameters on large validation sets or use parameters or prompts that might have been tuned by others. We follow their recommendation and rely on default parameters and some hyperparameters and prompts recommended by Wang et al. (2021), which according to the authors, were not tuned on the few-shot datasets. For label tuning, we follow Logan IV et al. (2021) and tune parameters with cross-validation on the few-shot training set.

We focus on classification tasks where label semantics are informative and where short prompts or label embeddings can plausibly steer model behavior. This includes intent detection, topic categorization, and natural language inference in low-resource regimes. Early work on large-scale pretraining was popularized by (Radford et al., 2018 in the context of generative GPT-style models. Subsequent studies demonstrated that careful calibration and verbalizer selection mitigate prediction bias in prompt-based pipelines (Zhao et al., 2021). Concurrently, contrastive representation learning has improved sentence embeddings, complementing label-tuning methods (Gao et al., 2021b). Our study integrates these insights by comparing inference-time prompting with trainable label vectors under identical compute budgets. We further analyze robustness under domain shift and class imbalance to clarify when structure from label text yields consistent benefits. To ensure comparability, we control for template length, verbalizer frequency, and label prior calibration across all settings. Overall, the reviewed literature motivates a unified evaluation of prompt engineering, entailment reformulation, and semantic similarity baselines under a true few-shot protocol.

document: Event Detection (ED) is a core subtask of event extraction that locates event triggers and types in text. It underpins downstream applications such as timeline construction, information retrieval, and situational awareness. Early approaches relied on feature-engineered classifiers with lexical, syntactic, and semantic cues (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008; Liao and Grishman, 2010). Subsequent work explored structured models, joint inference, and cross-sentence context to mitigate sparsity (Hong et al., 2011; Li et al., 2013). With the rise of deep learning, neural architectures based on CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers became dominant (Chen et al., 2015; Nguyen and Grishman, 2015; Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019). These models integrate syntactic information, document-level signals, and external supervision to boost performance (Liu et al., 2018; Wadden et al., 2019; Du and Cardie, 2020; Lai et al., 2021; Pouran Ben Veyseh et al., 2021). Nevertheless, a single unified detector often exhibits uneven accuracy across event types, especially for context-dependent triggers. Prior studies have discussed such disparities and proposed better context-exploration strategies Lu et al. 2019. However, these strategies address symptoms rather than explaining the underlying representational biases. We argue that clarifying type-specific patterns and their salience for the model is essential for robust ED. Feature attribution offers a principled lens to quantify which input tokens and structures drive predictions (Simonyan et al., 2014; Sundararajan et al., 2017). Given a trained classifier, attribution methods assign importance scores to input features with respect to an output of interest (Springenberg et al., 2015; Ribeiro et al., 2016). Methods are broadly categorized into gradient-based approaches that backpropagate sensitivities and reference-based approaches that compare to counterfactual baselines (Sundararajan et al., 2017). Among them, Integrated Gradients is attractive because it satisfies axiomatic properties and is computationally efficient for sequence models (Sundararajan et al., 2017). It has been applied successfully in image classification, translation, and text classification to interpret model behavior (Ding et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018; Bastings and Filippova, 2020). Despite progress in interpretability, few works have leveraged attribution to systematically characterize event-type patterns and dataset artifacts in ED. Our study brings FA tools to ED, using Integrated Gradients to expose which contextual cues, syntactic signals, and lexical triggers contribute to each type. We then link these saliency profiles to error modes, revealing when models over-rely on spurious correlations. In addition, we examine document-level aggregation to separate local trigger evidence from broader topical context (Wadden et al., 2019). This analysis guides a simple training recipe that emphasizes informative contexts while regularizing against type-specific bias. Compared with prior neural ED systems, our approach remains architecture-agnostic and can plug into Transformer backbones without task-specific feature engineering (Nguyen and Nguyen, 2019). We evaluate on standard benchmarks and compare with strong baselines that incorporate syntax and external supervision (Liu et al., 2018; Tong et al., 2020; Lai et al., 2021). The results demonstrate consistent gains on low-frequency and context-dependent types, alongside improved calibration. An ablation study confirms that attribution-guided regularization, not model size, accounts for the improvements. Our findings highlight the value of interpretability-driven training for future ED research and for safer deployment in dynamic domains.

document: We focus primarily on syntactically controlled paraphrase generation, a task that seeks to rewrite content while following a specified structural constraint. Interest in this problem has accelerated with advances in sequence modeling and controllable text generation (Iyyer et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Prior work typically contrasts exemplar driven methods with template or constraint driven methods. Exemplar based approaches transfer the surface form of a retrieved sentence to the target content while attempting to preserve meaning (Iyyer et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019). Template oriented approaches instead guide decoding with parse structures, POS patterns, or dependency arcs (Dong et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2020). Despite promising results, achieving high fidelity to both semantics and syntax remains challenging. A central difficulty is aligning lexical choices with discrete syntactic decisions in a manner that avoids error propagation. Neural decoders often overfit to frequent patterns and ignore rare but important constructions, especially under hard structural constraints (Liu et al., 2020). To mitigate these issues, researchers have explored specialized architectures that explicitly model tree structure. Tree LSTMs and graph encoders propagate information along parent and sibling edges to maintain hierarchical consistency (Tai et al., 2015; Song et al., 2018). Transformer variants extend this idea with attention biases or structure aware positional encodings to capture long distance syntax (Shaw et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). However, generic structural encoders are insufficient when the output must strictly conform to a supplied parse. Accordingly, several studies propose decoders that are conditioned on a target tree and generate words consistent with node level constraints (Zhang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). In parallel, retrieval augmented systems identify syntactic exemplars that are compatible with the source content and use them as weak guidance during generation (Li et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2020). These methods benefit from realistic patterns but struggle when suitable exemplars are unavailable or noisy. Our work introduces a tree transformer that jointly models parent child and sibling relations to better capture the target parse. The model incorporates attention regularization to align word generation with responsible syntax nodes during decoding. We also design a syntactic template retriever that searches a large corpus for candidate structures conditioned on semantic compatibility. The retriever serves as a fallback when gold parses are missing by providing plausible structural targets. Beyond architecture, training objectives greatly influence controllability and meaning preservation. Margin based losses enforce separation between correct and incorrect node alignments but may underutilize soft cues available in attention maps (Wieting et al., 2017). We therefore introduce a consistency objective that penalizes divergence between alignment distributions under stochastic augmentations of the input. This objective complements maximum likelihood training and improves robustness to parser noise. Evaluation of syntactic control requires metrics that assess both form and content. Prior work reports BLEU and ROUGE for content fidelity alongside tree similarity scores such as unlabeled F1 over constituents (Zhang and Shasha, 1989; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Recent studies further adopt parse constrained decoding to reduce search errors and improve validity rates (Shen et al., 2019; Duan et al., 2020). For fair comparison, we follow standard data splits used in paraphrase corpora and report exact match on structural targets when applicable (Iyyer et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020). Related research on controllable generation suggests that soft prompts or control tokens can steer outputs without explicit trees (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020). While effective for style and sentiment, such controls are often too weak to guarantee fine grained syntactic adherence (Harke et al., 2021. In contrast, explicit structural control remains preferable when precise syntactic manipulation is required in downstream tasks such as data augmentation and interpretable rewriting (Reif et al., 2019; Haviv et al., 2021). Finally, we note that exemplar and template approaches are complementary, and hybrid systems that retrieve structures and refine them with constrained decoding achieve the best trade offs seen in recent work (Nguyen et al., 2021).

document: In this section, we highlight the information bottleneck principle. We also outline potential pitfalls when applying it to OOV entity recognition. Furthermore, we review related techniques that motivate our framework. The Information Bottleneck principle, rooted in information theory, provides a rigorous lens on deep neural representations. It casts representation learning as an information trade-off between compression and predictive sufficiency Tishby et al., 2000; Wang et al., 2019). Given an input–label pair (X,Y), the goal is to learn an internal representation Z that optimally balances these terms. Here I denotes mutual information, and the trade-off is controlled by a Lagrange multiplier β. A low objective implies Z discards superfluous details from X while still supporting accurate prediction of Y. Prior analyses suggest that directly applying IB to NER yields limited gains, and the learned features may not generalize well. Moreover, because classifiers are trained to maximize accuracy, they may exploit any accessible signal, a phenomenon linked to shortcut learning (Geirhos et al., 2020). With small training sets, models may memorize entity strings instead of leveraging contextual cues (Agarwal et al., 2021).

document: This paper discusses the evolving arms race between model scale and dataset design in NLP. Prior critiques emphasize downsides of ever larger pretrained models, including environmental and ethical costs (Schwartz et al., 2020; Bender et al., 2021). Others question whether scale alone leads to deeper abstraction and understanding (Bender and Koller, 2020). Complementing this line, we focus on dataset curation intended to reduce spurious correlations and shortcut learning. Dataset balancing is commonly framed as a mechanism to elicit more robust generalization. Yet balancing often changes the data distribution in ways that can create new artifacts. Benchmarks are typically released with the promise of catalyzing modeling advances. However, performance rankings can remain stable across synthetic and natural datasets, limiting diagnostic value (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Recent work argues that benchmarks capture narrow skills rather than broad capabilities (Raji et al., 2021). Our study articulates related concerns regarding hidden cues in training sets. We analyze how balancing policies interact with model inductive biases. We also examine how small changes in annotation guidelines propagate through evaluation. Spurious correlations arise from annotation artifacts, lexical priors, and dataset collection interfaces (Gururangan et al., 2018). In natural language inference, hypothesis-only baselines revealed systemic cues (Poliak et al., 2018). Later studies showed that models exploit parse heuristics rather than syntactic reasoning (McCoy et al., 2019). Adversarial datasets attempted to break these shortcuts by flipping labels under controlled edits (Kaushik et al., 2020). While instructive, such datasets can encourage overfitting to adversarial patterns. Further, retraining on balanced sets sometimes yields negligible improvements in out-of-domain generalization (Recht et al., 2019). Calibration and uncertainty estimation have been proposed to mitigate overconfidence under distribution shift (Ovadia et al., 2019). Nevertheless, reported gains often evaporate when evaluated on truly novel perturbations. Pretraining objectives and tokenization interact with balancing in subtle ways. For example, frequency reweighting can reduce surface cues but distort semantic coverage. The vision literature highlights analogous trade-offs between diversity and bias. Texture bias versus shape bias illustrates how models pick easy signals over causal ones (Geirhos et al., 2019). Analogous shortcuts are abundant in language tasks Geirhos et al., 2020). Consequently, we argue that benchmark design should foreground construct validity and measurement theory. Construct validity requires that items measure the intended skills, not artifacts. Following this principle, we recommend layered evaluation with controlled counterfactuals (Gardner et al., 2020). Layering discourages single-number leaderboards and instead promotes capability profiles (Wang et al., 2018). Complementary qualitative error analysis can reveal failure modes that aggregate metrics obscure (Ribeiro et al., 2020). We situate our analysis in reading comprehension because the field offers mature datasets and clear metrics (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Yet even here, minor prompt changes can cause large performance swings. We therefore benchmark models under matched-control splits that isolate artifact sensitivity. Our results show that apparent robustness on one balanced set often fails to transfer to another. These findings challenge the assumption that balancing alone ensures progress. We conclude by advocating for benchmark governance that couples transparent data processes with theory-driven evaluation.

document: Fully supervised event extraction has been studied for over a decade (Ahn, 2006; Ji and Grishman, 2008). Most traditional approaches follow a fully supervised setting and rely on classification models in pipeline-style frameworks (Nguyen et al., 2016; Sha et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Wadden et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). To better leverage shared knowledge between triggers and arguments, several works incorporate global features to jointly decide both (Li et al., 2013; Yang and Mitchell, 2016; Lin et al., 2020). Recently, generation-based formulations for event extraction have been proposed alongside these classification pipelines. TANL (Paolini et al., 2021) treats event extraction as translation between augmented natural languages. Its target-augmented notation embeds labels into the input via brackets and special symbols, which can hinder the model from fully leveraging label semantics. BART-Gen focuses on document-level event argument extraction but still adopts a pipeline that limits cross-task knowledge sharing. While these fully supervised methods achieve strong performance with abundant annotations, they are not tailored to low-resource scenarios. In the low-resource setting, one line of work frames event extraction as machine reading comprehension to reduce reliance on task-specific labels. Text2Event Lu et al., 2021) introduces a sequence-to-structure paradigm that linearizes events and trains a generative model to recover the structured outputs. However, its unnatural output format can limit the use of pretrained knowledge, and meta-learning approaches address only event detection rather than end-to-end extraction (Shen et al., 2021; Cong et al., 2021; Deng et al., 2020).

document: Traditional methods treat NER as a sequence labeling task, where each word or character in the sentence is assigned to a special label. As a representative, Huang et al. (2015) utilized a BiLSTM encoder to learn contextual representations, and then exploited a Conditional Random Field (CRF) decoder to label tokens. The BiLSTM-CRF architecture achieved superior performance on various datasets, hence many following works adopted similar designs (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016). Subsequent advances leveraged contextualized pretraining to improve both feature quality and transferability, notably ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). In Chinese NER, researchers proposed lexicon-based models that incorporate external dictionary information and reported notable gains. Zhang and Yang (2018) investigated Lattice-LSTM to inject word lexicons into a character-based backbone. However, the lattice structure complicates batching and hinders parallel computation on modern accelerators. To address this issue, Gui et al. (2019) introduced a lexicon-based graph neural network and cast Chinese NER as node classification. Other studies incorporated all matched lexicon words into character representations to further enrich local context (Ma et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Despite their success, lexicon-centric methods depend on curated resources, which are costly to maintain and brittle under domain shift. A complementary line of research explores internal regularities of text spans, seeking to capture compositional and boundary-aware cues without external lists. Span-based formulations model entities as contiguous segments and perform scoring over candidate spans rather than per-token label tagging (Lee et al., 2017; Sohrab and Miwa, 2018). These trends are echoed in prior work (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al.; Lample et al., 2016 underscoring the shift toward representation learning for NER. Beyond architecture, pretraining objectives and data scale substantially influence downstream robustness and sample efficiency (Liu et al., 2019; Gururangan et al., 2020). For Chinese NER, character granularity alleviates segmentation errors, but it blurs multiword entity boundaries and weakens type disambiguation. Span-oriented encoders mitigate these issues by attending over start and end positions, pooling internal tokens, and enforcing boundary-aware constraints (Yan et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2019). Another challenge is nested and overlapping entities, which strain BIO tagging but fit naturally into span selection or region-based decoding (Ju et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). Recent studies also explore generative formulations that serialize entity sets, allowing flexible decoding while leveraging strong language models (Yan et al., 2021; Paolini et al., 2021). However, generative approaches can hallucinate, require careful constrained decoding, and often trade precision for recall in dense entity settings. In parallel, prompt-based adaptation of pretrained encoders has emerged as an alternative to architecture-heavy designs, especially in low-resource regimes (Liu et al., 2023). Prompt templates cast NER as masked token prediction or span extraction with verbalizers, but designing robust prompts remains nontrivial. Data augmentation techniques such as synonym replacement, entity swapping, and back-translation offer additional regularization but risk distribution shift if applied indiscriminately (Wei and Zou, 2019; Fadaee et al., 2017). Knowledge-enhanced encoders inject gazetteers or knowledge graphs to improve recall, yet these benefits degrade when the external sources lag behind evolving domains (Zhang et al., 2021). Multi-task and joint models couple NER with part-of-speech tagging, segmentation, or relation extraction to share inductive biases and improve span boundary detection (Sanh et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2020). Nevertheless, joint objectives complicate optimization and may overfit to auxiliary signals if not balanced carefully. Benchmarks such as OntoNotes, MSRA, and Weibo provide standardized comparisons, but they differ in segmentation conventions and annotation granularity, complicating cross-corpus conclusions (Pradhan et al., 2013; Peng and Dredze, 2015). For fair evaluation, researchers increasingly report performance under domain transfer, cross-lingual adaptation, and few-shot settings alongside in-domain metrics (Keung et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2022). Beyond metrics, calibration and uncertainty estimation help characterize model confidence and guide human-in-the-loop triage in practical deployments (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). From a systems perspective, inference latency and memory footprint matter for production NER, motivating parallelizable span scorers and efficient pruning strategies. In this work, we study the internal regularity of spans by leveraging character composition, affix patterns, and positional cues that recur across entity mentions. Our approach avoids reliance on external lexicons, thereby reducing maintenance costs and improving portability to new domains and languages. Concretely, we design a lightweight span candidate generator, a context-sensitive span scorer, and a boundary consistency module trained end-to-end. The design accommodates nested entities without bespoke heuristics and scales to long documents via batched span pruning. Through extensive experiments, we compare against strong tagging, lattice, graph, and generative baselines while controlling for pretraining and decoding confounders. We conclude with an error analysis and ablation studies that illuminate the contributions of each component and outline directions for future research.

Balancing short-range statistical constraints with long-range discourse structure has long been a core challenge in language generation. Before neural LMs, n-gram models and early neural architectures used back-off and interpolation to combine predictors of different context lengths (Chen and Goodman, 1996; Bengio et al., 2003). The advent of gated recurrent units enabled more stable propagation of information across distant timesteps (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997; Cho et al., 2014). Subsequent work reintroduced alignment variables into generation via attention, greatly improving conditional modeling (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Vaswani et al., 2017). Attention now anchors Transformer language models, including large-scale autoregressive systems.

As corpora and contexts have grown, researchers have extended models to process longer sequences efficiently (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021; Press et al., 2021). Nevertheless, empirical studies show that LMs can remain under-sensitive to distant content and syntax (Khandelwal et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2021). They are also vulnerable to recency bias in few-shot prompts and multi-turn dialogues (Zhao et al., 2021; Sankar et al., 2019).

Beyond architecture, inference-time control has emerged as a complementary axis for improving coherence. Tempered sampling, top-k, and nucleus truncation modify local distributions to reduce entropy and avoid low-probability tails (Fan et al., 2018; Holtzman et al., 2019). Holtzman et al. (2019) and Meister and Cotterell (2021) report gains on long-form generation from these local adjustments. We adopt and extend their evaluation methodology to study how global signals can complement local filtering.

One global strategy is to rescore or mix distributions conditioned on different contexts to counteract myopic tendencies. In dialog, mutual-information decoders explicitly optimize dependence on the prompt and reduce dull responses (Li et al., 2016). In multiple-choice settings, selecting completions purely by conditional likelihood can overfavor answers with high unconditional probabilities. Here we note that Brown, Vinyals, and Le 2020 documented this effect and proposed normalizing by unconditional likelihood when appropriate.

Normalization by a background model is closely connected to pointwise mutual information and to surface-form rebalancing. Follow-up analyses attribute some errors to surface form competition among near-synonymous completions (Holtzman et al., 2021). However, others argue that surface-form competition alone cannot explain failures when the choice space is the full vocabulary or a single-token decision (Zhao et al., 2021).

Our perspective synthesizes these threads by viewing decoding as a constrained optimization that trades off local fluency and global consistency. We formalize coherence boosting as a mixture of distributions computed from complementary contexts. This perspective clarifies why mixing can increase the rank of the semantically correct token even when both components err locally.

Prior work has evaluated related strategies on open-domain dialogue, story continuation, and factual cloze tasks. We focus on setups that stress long-range dependence, such as multi-paragraph prompts and cross-sentence coreference chains. To ensure comparability, we reuse standard corpora and follow established preprocessing and decoding budgets.

Our contributions complement architectural advances in efficient attention and memory mechanisms rather than replacing them (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). They also provide a bridge between classic information-theoretic rescaling and modern neural decoding heuristics. By consolidating methodology across local and global controls, we aim to provide a clearer picture of when each tool is most effective. The remainder of this paper details the mixture formulation, connects it to prior scoring rules, and presents empirical evidence across diverse tasks.

document: Active Learning. Active learning has been widely applied to various NLP tasks (Yuan et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Shelmanov et al., 2021;Karamcheti et al., 2021). So far, AL methods can be categorized into uncertainty-based methods (Gal et al., 2017;Margatina et al., 2021a,b), diversitybased methods (Ru et al., 2020;Sener and Savarese, 2018) and hybrid methods (Yuan et al., 2020;Ash et al., 2020;Kirsch et al., 2019). Ein-Dor et al. (2020) offer an empirical study of active learning with PLMs. In our study, we leverage the power of unlabeled instances via self-training to further promote the performance of AL (Vaswani et al. 2017). Semi-supervised Active Learning (SSAL). Gao et al. (2020); Song et al. (2019); Guo et al. (2021) design query strategies for specific semi-supervised methods, Tomanek and Hahn (2009); Rottmann et al. (2018); Siméoni et al. (2020) exploit the mostcertain samples from the unlabeled with pseudolabeling to augment the training set. So far, most of the SSAL approaches are designed for CV domain and it remains unknown how this paradigm performs with PLMs on NLP tasks. In contrast, we propose ACTUNE to effectively leverage unlabeled data during finetuing PLMs for NLP tasks. Self-training. Self-training first generates pseudo labels for high-confidence samples, then fits a new model on pseudo labeled data to improve the generalization ability (Rosenberg et al., 2005;Lee, 2013). However, it is known to be vulnerable to error propagation (Arazo et al., 2020;Rizve et al., 2021). To alleviate this, we adopt a simple momentumbased method to select high confidence samples, effectively reducing the pseudo labels noise for active learning. Note that although Mukherjee and Awadallah (2020); Rizve et al. (2021) also leverage uncertainty information for self-training, their focus is on developing better self-training methods, while we aim to jointly query high-uncertainty samples and generate pseudo-labels for low-uncertainty samples. The experiments in Sec. 3 show that with appropriate querying methods, ACTUNE can further improve the performance of self-training. Batch-mode selection has been shown to benefit from combining uncertainty with diversity to reduce redundancy in the acquired set (Ash et al., 2020;Sener and Savarese, 2018). With PLMs, representation drift across AL rounds can degrade the usefulness of simple margin sampling, motivating round-aware scoring and warm-start training (Ein-Dor et al., 2020;Margatina et al., 2021b). Consistency regularization methods, such as virtual adversarial training, have also been integrated into AL to stabilize learning from small labeled sets (Miyato et al., 2019;Clark et al., 2018). From a systems perspective, amortizing inference cost through cached embeddings is crucial when repeatedly evaluating large pools under multiple perturbations (Schroeder and Weng, 2020) (Sohn et al., 2020. Recent work further explores label-efficient pretraining objectives that complement AL by improving data utilization in the low-label regime (Gururangan et al., 2020;Zoph et al., 2020). Calibration is another key factor, since overconfident models can misguide both querying and pseudo-label assignment (Guo et al., 2017). We therefore pair ACTUNE with temperature scaling and simple ensembling to temper confidence estimates before selection and self-training (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017;Guo et al., 2017). Our approach differs from prior SSAL pipelines by interleaving acquisition and pseudo-label refresh, rather than running them in separate phases (Chen et al., 2020). This design aims to reduce confirmation bias by mixing supervision sources within each AL round (Arazo et al., 2020). Finally, we discuss limitations related to domain shift, annotation noise, and compute overhead to situate ACTUNE among realistic deployment constraints (Northcutt et al., 2021).

document: Distillation was first introduced in the context of computer vision (Hinton et al., 2015) and has since been widely explored for language models (Sun et al., 2019; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2019). For example, Sanh et al. (2019) propose to extract information not only from output probabilities of the last layer in the teacher model, but also from intermediate layers in the fine-tuning stage. Recently, Rotman et al. (2021) adapt causal analysis methods to estimate the effects of inputs on predictions to compress models for better domain adaptation. In contrast, we focus on imbuing the student with the causal structure of the teacher. Classic teacher–student formulations align the logits or hidden states of a larger model with those of a compact student, often under temperature scaling (Hinton et al., 2015). Subsequent work exploits attention transfer and relational knowledge to capture structural properties that conventional losses overlook (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017; Park et al., 2019). Interventions on neural networks were originally used as a structural analysis method aimed at illuminating neural representations and their role in network behavior (Pryzant et al., 2021; Vig et al., 2020; Elazar et al., 2020; Giulianelli et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2020; Geiger et al., 2021a). Geiger et al. (2021b) extend these methods to network optimization. We contribute to this existing research by adapting intervention-based optimization to the task of language model distillation. Our approach draws on insights from representation control to identify mediating components that most directly transmit task-relevant information (Feder et al., 2021). Transformers have become the de facto backbone for NLP, starting with Vaswani et al., 2017 and subsequent scaling has amplified their capacity. Modern language models rely on deep Transformer encoders and decoders that exhibit layered semantic progression (Rogers et al., 2020). The standard pretraining objectives, including masked language modeling and next sentence prediction, induce contextual abstractions that are amenable to compression (Devlin et al., 2019). However, aligning only surface outputs risks neglecting causal pathways that determine how inputs shape predictions in context. We therefore study whether targeted interventions can guide the student to internalize causally salient features while preserving generalization. Our experiments consider both in-domain and out-of-domain scenarios, evaluating performance under capacity constraints and varying amounts of supervision (Clark et al., 2019). Compared to baselines that match logits or attention patterns, our method yields consistent gains on question answering, natural language inference, and sequence labeling benchmarks (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Bowman et al., 2015; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003). Finally, we analyze sensitivity to the choice of intervened layers and show that effect alignment complements traditional knowledge distillation losses.

document: Large neural language models pretrained on self-supervised tasks (Lewis et al., 2020;Liu et al., 2019;Devlin et al., 2019) have recently gained traction in data-to-text (D2T) generation research (Ferreira et al., 2020). Following Chen et al. (2020c), subsequent works adopt PLMs for few-shot D2T generation (Chang et al., 2021b;Su et al., 2021a). Linearized representations can outperform graph encoders on graph-to-text datasets, as shown in (Kale and Rastogi, 2020b) and corroborated by Ribeiro et al. (2020), though graph-based models soon regained the lead (Ke et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2020a). Simple handcrafted templates for individual keys or predicates efficiently inject domain knowledge while preventing text-to-text models from overfitting to a specific data format (Heidari et al., 2021;Kale and Rastogi, 2020a). Transforming triples into text is also used by Laha et al. (2020), whose pipeline for zero-shot D2T relies on rules for surface realisation. However, these rule-based systems do not address content planning beyond local decisions. Content planning—ordering input facts and aggregating them into sentences—is a long-standing component of the D2T pipeline (Ferreira et al., 2019;Gatt and Krahmer, 2018;Reiter and Dale, 1997). Several studies have shown that explicit plans improve the quality of neural outputs (Moryossef et al., 2019a,b;Puduppully et al., 2019;Trisedya et al., 2020). Unlike prior models that plan directly over predicates or keys, our planner is trained to order natural-language sentences. Sentence ordering seeks to arrange a set of sentences to maximize global coherence (Barzilay et al., 2001;Lapata, 2003). Neural approaches capture pairwise and global signals, combining local interactions (Li and Jurafsky, 2017) with document-level attention (Gong et al., 2016;Wang and Wan, 2019). We base our ordering module (§5.1) on Calizzano et al. (2021), using a pointer network (Wang and Wan, 2019;Vinyals et al., 2015) on top of a PLM. The compact nature of target descriptions often requires aggregating multiple facts into a single sentence, for which prior work treats sentence segments as latent variables (Wiseman et al., 2018;Shao et al., 2019;Shen et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2021). We instead assume an ordered sequence of facts and insert delimiters to mark sentence boundaries before a final compression stage. As the last step, paragraph compression performs sentence fusion, rephrasing, and coreference resolution, unlike text summarization or simplification (Jiang et al., 2020), and differs from sentence fusion and compression (Geva et al., 2019;Barzilay and McKeown, because we operate over multi-sentence paragraphs.

document: Multi-hop reading comprehension (MHRC) requires models to integrate evidence across multiple passages to answer complex questions. Early neural approaches emphasized attention mechanisms that iteratively refine query and context representations. Memory-augmented readers demonstrated gains by layering attentions over document states and question states (Dhingra et al., 2018). Similar architectures explored bidirectional attention and span selection for compositional reasoning (Clark and Gardner, 2018). Subsequent work investigated multi-step aggregation with graph-style propagation over sentences and paragraphs (Zhuang and Wang, 2019). Parallel to these trends, cross-document signal integration improved entity tracking and coreference handling for multi-hop inference (Jiang and Bansal, 2019). To render discourse structure explicit, researchers built entity graphs that connect mentions across passages and then ran message passing on these graphs (De Cao et al., 2019). Graph neural networks over entity and sentence nodes have been shown to facilitate long-range information flow in MHRC (Ding et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2019). Further refinements combined edge types, relation extraction signals, and hierarchical pooling to enhance path discovery (Tang et al., 2020; Song et al., 2018; Tu et al., 2019). Complementary strategies framed reasoning as searching paths that connect question entities to answers under neural scoring functions (Kundu et al., 2019). Pointer-network based path modeling offered a supervised way to enumerate and prioritize latent reasoning chains. Despite these advances, many methods emphasize local coherence over global consistency and often depend on external NER tools for entity grounding. Retrieval oriented pipelines converted raw text into pseudo knowledge bases to enable symbolic style lookups (Dhingra et al., 2020). Another line of work decomposed questions into subgoals using neural module networks and executed learned operators over evidence (Min et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). While modularization improves interpretability, the learned programs still struggle to expose the full latent logic behind answer selection. Neuro-symbolic learning seeks to combine the statistical robustness of deep networks with the compositional guarantees of logic. In one paradigm, neural architectures parameterize truth values of predicates and soft logical operators under differentiable objectives (França et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2016). Subsequent frameworks injected rules as regularizers or constraints over latent representations to bias models toward valid derivations (Manhaeve et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018; Li and Srikumar, 2019; Wu et al., 2020). A harder agenda is differentiable inductive logic programming, where rule templates and parameters are jointly learned from data (Evans and Grefenstette, 2018; Dong et al., 2019; Yang and Song, 2020). These methods have seen traction in knowledge base completion by embedding symbols, relations, and constraints into continuous spaces (Guo et al., 2016). Alternative approaches used tensor factorization to capture multi-relational patterns in structured graphs (Cohen, 2016). Adversarial and variational training regimes further improved robustness and calibration of logical predictions (Minervini et al., 2017; Qu and Tang, 2019). Differentiable theorem proving introduced soft unification and gradient-based backward chaining for end-to-end reasoning Campero et al., 2018), extending neural proof search with continuous relaxations (Minervini et al., 2020). However, unlike MHRC, most KB-oriented systems operate over pre-structured triples and avoid the ambiguity inherent in natural language inputs. Bringing these ideas to open text requires entity linking, relation normalization, and rule grounding under noisy supervision. Recent efforts thus explore hybrid pipelines that first extract candidate facts and then apply neural reasoning to reconcile conflicts across sources. In multi-hop QA specifically, symbolic constraints have been used to restrict answer types and enforce path consistency across supporting sentences. Others leverage latent variable models to marginalize over reasoning chains while still providing partial rationales to users. Despite improvements, current readers often fail to generalize beyond seen compositions and are brittle to surface perturbations. Moreover, reliance on heavy supervision for graph construction or rule templates limits portability across domains. We argue that integrating differentiable objectives with lightweight structural priors can reduce supervision while maintaining interpretability. Our approach therefore targets three desiderata for MHRC models, namely data efficiency, transparent aggregation, and controllable reasoning depth. Concretely, we couple a retrieval module with a neural proof system that scores soft rule applications over entity and sentence graphs. The retriever selects minimal yet sufficient evidence to support multi-step derivations without overfitting to dataset artifacts. The proof system composes operations such as conjunction, transitive closure, and type checking under smooth relaxations suitable for gradient descent. We situate our contribution within prior art while emphasizing gains in robustness, sample efficiency, and explanation quality demonstrated in controlled studies.

document: Language-informed video understanding is a complex task that has been extensively addressed in the multimodal (natural language and computer vision) machine learning research, by proposing diverse tasks and benchmarks. It encompasses tasks such as video question answering, dense and sparse captioning, temporal action localization, and multimodal retrieval. Progress in these areas is driven by the synergy between advances in visual encoders, sequence modeling, and pretrained language representations. Early works leveraged 3D convolutional networks combined with recurrent decoders to align appearance and motion with textual outputs (Yao et al., 2015; Donahue et al., 2015). Video question answering has evolved from short clip multiple-choice formats to open-ended, long-form reasoning over minutes-long narratives (Tapaswi et al., 2016; Lei et al., 2020). Captioning benchmarks expanded from single-sentence descriptions to dense, temporally grounded captions with hierarchical structure (Krishna et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2018). Temporal moment localization introduces language-guided search over untrimmed videos, emphasizing grounding and compositionality (Gao et al., 2017; Hendricks et al., 2018). Cross-modal retrieval exploits joint embedding spaces to support zero-shot transfer across domains and tasks (Miech et al., 2019; Bain et al., 2021). More recent multi-task suites integrate these objectives to facilitate holistic evaluation under unified protocols (Zellers et al., 2021; Nagrani et al., 2022). Architectures have shifted from CNN-RNN stacks toward Transformer backbones with spatiotemporal self-attention and cross-modal fusion (Girdhar et al., 2019; Arnab et al., 2021). Language priors are injected via pretrained text encoders and token-level alignments that modulate visual token processing (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Fusion strategies range from early token concatenation to late interaction with co-attention blocks and gated modulation (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Contrastive pretraining on web-scale video–text pairs has emerged as a robust foundation for downstream generalization (Radford et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Weakly supervised objectives that align subtitles or ASR transcripts with frames further reduce labeling costs while preserving semantic grounding (Miech et al., 2020; Rouditchenko et al., 2020). Dedicated video-language transformers extend image-text models with temporal factorization, sliding windows, and memory tokens (Lei et al., 2021; Zellers et al., 2021). Instruction tuning with multimodal prompts shows promise in aligning models with user intent across varied video tasks (Liu et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Nevertheless, long-range reasoning remains challenging due to quadratic attention costs and information dilution over hours-long videos (Wu et al., 2022; Rao et al., 2021). Precise language grounding requires consistent handling of coreference, temporal anaphora, and event schemas that span multiple scenes (Hendricks et al., 2017; Rohrbach et al., 2016). Learning under weak supervision is impeded by temporal misalignment and pervasive label noise inherent in web corpora, as noted by Wang et al. 2020 (Ghadiyaram et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). Models also inherit dataset biases, amplifying spurious correlations between words and frequent visual contexts (Agrawal et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2021). Robustness under distribution shift remains limited, with sharp performance drops on out-of-domain scenes, accents, and actions (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Miller et al., 2021). Evaluation practices increasingly combine automatic metrics with human judgments to assess temporal grounding fidelity and answer faithfulness (Papineni et al., 2002; Gehrmann et al., 2022). A unified benchmark should standardize protocol, report compute budgets, and encourage ablations that disentangle architectural from data effects (Raji et al., 2021; Hooker, 2020). In this work, we synthesize prior art to chart the landscape of language-informed video understanding and identify open problems. We categorize methods into retrieval-augmented models, temporally aware fusion transformers, and memory-augmented reasoning architectures (Bain et al., 2021; Girdhar et al., 2019). We further compare scaling laws across model size, clip sampling density, and pretraining dataset mixtures (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). Objective functions are grouped into contrastive, matching, masked modeling, and instruction-following losses with task-specific adapters (He et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022). We also review zero-shot and few-shot adaptation via prompting, retrieval, and lightweight parameter-efficient tuning (Hu et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022). Ethical and societal impacts are examined with respect to privacy, consent in video data collection, and representational harms (Birhane et al., 2021; Shankar et al., 2017). Related lines of research include video grounding in robotics and embodied instruction following that demand real-time multimodal reasoning (Shridhar et al., 2020; Brohan et al., 2023). On the dataset side, instructional and narrated video corpora provide rich language alignment signals beyond sparse captions (Miech et al., 2019; Zellers et al., 2021). Methodologically, hybrid pipelines pair frozen encoders with learned adapters and late-fusion decoders to balance efficiency and accuracy (Feichtenhofer et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Comparative studies report that pretraining on instructional videos yields superior grounding for imperatives and procedural language (Miech et al., 2020; Alayrac et al., 2022). However, scaling data alone is insufficient without improved temporal modeling and better alignment objectives (Feichtenhofer et al., 2019; Zeng et al., 2022). We therefore advocate standardized ablations, strong baselines, and transparent reporting to accelerate reliable progress in the field. The remainder of this paper details our taxonomy, benchmarking protocol, and recommendations for future research.

Early work on writing assistants largely focuses on grammar error correction; for a survey see Wang et al. (2020). Neural models have broadened the scope of assistance to style transfer, paraphrasing, and local rewriting with increasing fluency and control (Sun et al., 2021). Sequence-to-sequence architectures such as BART and T5 have been adapted to editing tasks by conditioning on prompts or diff-style targets (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). Beyond surface fluency, recent systems aim to maintain discourse structure and topical coherence across multiple sentences (Xu et al., 2022). Nevertheless, most assistance remains unguided by external evidence and thus risks hallucination or outdated content (Ji et al., 2023). Our study addresses this limitation by framing assistance as grounded updating in which edits are derived from explicit citations and retrieved sources.

The problem of updating encyclopedia articles relates to document-level generation with verifiable support. Prior work on Wikipedia generation follows data-to-text pipelines that convert infoboxes or tables into prose (Lebret et al., 2016; Bao et al., 2018). A complementary line uses multi-document summarization to synthesize sections from clusters of references (Banerjee and Mitra, 2016; Liu et al., 2018). More recently, controlled insertion of post-modifiers has been explored to localize small factual augmentations (Kang et al., 2019). Datasets pairing article passages with hyperlinks and reference snippets enable evidence-aware modeling at scale (Parikh et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2021). Our approach leverages hyperlink graphs to propose candidate evidence while preserving the narrative flow of target paragraphs. In contrast to unconditional rewriting, we require that every added claim be attributable to at least one cited source. This design links content selection, citation placement, and surface realization within a single generation framework.

Grounded updating intersects with fact verification, which studies whether claims are supported or refuted by a corpus (Thorne et al., 2018. Work on claim detection and evidence retrieval provides strong priors for identifying where updates are necessary (Hanselowski et al., 2019; Malon, 2018). At the sentence level, datasets of factual replacements capture micro-edits but do not address section-scale organization (Schuster et al., 2021). In encyclopedic domains, contradiction detection must also account for temporal drift and changing entity attributes (Dhingra et al., 2022). Building on these insights, our pipeline flags stale or conflicting statements before proposing edits based on corroborated passages. Prior evaluations often reduce verification to binary labels, which obscures the quality of textual realizations and citation fidelity (Falke et al., 2019). We therefore pair automated support checks with human judgments of factuality, relevance, and stylistic conformity.

Retrieval-augmented generation provides a natural substrate for grounded editing by conditioning models on evidence snippets (Lewis et al., 2020). Dense retrievers can surface semantically similar passages, whereas hyperlink traversal exploits the curated structure of Wikipedia (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2021). To mitigate citation drift, we enforce copy-anchoring of named entities and dates during decoding (Cao et al., 2021). We additionally explore pointer-augmented architectures that splice quoted fragments while paraphrasing surrounding context (Chen et al., 2020). A reranker selects candidate edits using features that approximate verifiability, novelty, and coverage. This setup helps balance conservatism with the introduction of genuinely new, source-backed content. Finally, constrained decoding rules ensure that citations align with the precise spans they support.

Annotation protocols adapt practices from editorial workflows by requiring annotators to read both the evidence and the target article. We define error taxonomies that distinguish unsupported insertions, misattributed citations, and discourse-incoherent rewrites (Pagnoni et al., 2021). For automatic metrics, we report lexical overlap, entailment-based consistency, and citation precision and recall (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Durmus et al., 2020). We also include longitudinal evaluation to test robustness under new evidence and periodic content drift. Ablations assess the effect of retrieval breadth, citation density, and constrained decoding on overall quality.

Our contributions are a dataset of evidence-aligned updates, a modeling framework that unifies retrieval and editing, and an evaluation protocol centered on verifiability. Together, these components advance writing assistance toward accountable, update-oriented generation rather than stylistic paraphrase alone. We release code and data to facilitate reproducibility and future extensions.

We introduce the Moral Foundations Theory (MFT) and the Moral Foundations Twitter Corpus (MFTC), which underpin our experiments.

MFT is a well-established framework in social and cultural psychology that posits a finite set of innate moral foundations guiding judgment and behavior (Graham et al., 2013).

These foundations are commonly organized as five vice–virtue pairs—Care/Harm, Fairness/Cheating, Loyalty/Betrayal, Authority/Subversion, and Sanctity/Degradation—providing a compact ontology for moral language.

In our study, we leverage MFTC, a collection of tweets annotated for the 10 moral values plus a nonmoral class, to evaluate moral category recognition at scale (Hoover et al., 2020).

The corpus is divided into topical subsets such as All Lives Matter, Baltimore protests, Black Lives Matter, hate speech and offensive language, the 2016 U.S. presidential election, the MeToo movement, and Hurricane Sandy, enabling cross-domain analysis.

Each tweet was independently labeled by multiple annotators with any applicable moral values, reflecting that vice and virtue expressions need not co-occur in the same utterance.

To determine gold labels, annotators’ votes are aggregated with majority voting, and items without a majority are assigned to the nonmoral category.

The theory has been extensively applied in cross-cultural studies Graham et al., 2013) and provides a principled scaffold for interpreting moral discourse in noisy social media settings.

Prior work on MFTC documents the annotation process, inter-annotator agreement, and quality controls that support its reliability for computational research (Hoover et al., 2020).

We report illustrative examples and overall label distributions to characterize dataset difficulty and topical variation.

Following common practice in multilabel imbalance analysis, we use MeanIR to quantify skew, where IR for a label is the ratio between the count of the majority class and the count of that label (Charte et al., 2015).

We observe substantial heterogeneity in MeanIR across topics, which mirrors real-world differences in moral salience among domains.

This heterogeneity motivates evaluation protocols that test transfer across domains rather than relying solely on in-domain performance.

In particular, we treat each topical subset as a distinct domain and measure generalization when training on some domains and testing on others.

Together, MFT and MFTC offer a theoretically grounded and practically challenging testbed for modeling moral language in short, informal texts.

Multimodal sentiment analysis has gained increasing attention from the community recently and some process has been made. In general, there are three findings presented by previous work.

Performing the cross-modal alignment is helpful for multimodal feature fusion. considered that the holistic features mainly contain global information, which may fail to capture local information. Therefore, they applied the force-alignment to align the visual and acoustic features with the words and further obtained the word-level features. To effectively fuse them, they proposed the GME-LSTM(A) model, which consists of two modules, the gated multimodal embedding and the LSTM with the temporal attention. However, obtaining the word-level features needs to perform the force-alignment, which is time-consuming. To address it, proposed the MulT model, which uses the crossmodal attention to align different modal features implicitly. Instead of performing the alignment in the time dimension, some works focusing on semantic alignment. Hazarika et al. (2020) considered that the semantic gaps between heterogeneous data could hurt the model performance and proposed the MISA model, which maps the different modal data into a shared space before multimodal feature fusion. first utilized the cross-modal prediction task to distinguish the shared and private semantics of non-textual modalities compared to the textual modality and then fuse them. The above works show that performing the cross-modal alignment is helpful for multimodal feature fusion.

Training the MSA models in an end-to-end manner is more effective. Most of the previous studies adopt a two-phase pipeline, first extracting unimodal features and then fusing them. Dai et al. (2021 considered that it may lead to suboptimal performance since the extracted unimodal features are fixed and cannot be further improved benefiting from the downstream supervisory signals. Therefore, they proposed the multimodal endto-end sparse model, which can optimize the unimodal feature extraction and multimodal feature fusion jointly. The experimental results on the multimodal emotion detection task show that training the models in an end-to-end manner can obtain better results than the pipeline models.

Leveraging the unimodal sentiment labels to learn more informative unimodal representations is useful for multimodal feature fusion. Yu et al. (2020) considered that introducing the unimodal sentiment labels can help the model capture the unimodal sentiment information and model the difference between modalities. Motivated by it, they built the CH-SIMS dataset, which contains not only the multimodal sentiment labels but also unimodal sentiment labels. And based on it, they proposed a multi-task learning framework to leverage two types of sentiment labels simultaneously. However, this method needs unimodal labels, which is absent for most of the existing datasets. To address it, Yu et al. (2021) proposed the Self-MM model, which first generates the unimodal labels by utilizing the relationship between the unimodal and multimodal labels and then uses the multi-task learning to train the model. These two works both address the usefulness of introducing unimodal labels.

However, even though lots of models are proposed and obtain promising results on the benchmark datasets, there are few works considering the noisy inputs when the MSA models are deployed in the real world. presented the Gated Multimodal Embedding to filter out the noises from the acoustic and visual data. Pham et al. (2019) considered that visual and acoustic data may be absent and proposed the MCTN model to handle it. and Mittal et al. (2020) also mainly focused on dealing with the noises introduced by the visual and acoustic data, and their models are based on the word-level features, which are obtained by aligning the audios with the gold texts. There is only one work (Dumpala et al., 2018) considering that the texts are output by the ASR models, which may be erroneous. But this work does not study how do the ASR errors affect the MSA models and does not evaluate the SOTA MSA models on the datasets. Besides, the proposed model needs the gold texts when training, which is time-consuming and labor-consuming.

Comparing to the above works, we evaluate the SOTA MSA models on the real-world datasets and observe that the performance of models decreases sharply because of the erroneous ASR texts. Through in-depth analysis of the ASR outputs, we find the sentiment word substitution error in the ASR texts could hurt the MSA models directly. To address it, we propose the sentiment word aware multimodal refinement model, which only uses the ASR texts in the training and testing phrases. We further discuss ethical considerations around annotation noise and deployment in sensitive applications. Overall, our analysis highlights the need for robustness-aware architectures and evaluation protocols in multimodal sentiment research.

document: There exist a number of large-scale Twitter datasets concentrated on topics such as COVID-19 (Banda et al., 2021) or Climate Change (Littman and Wrubel, 2019). However, it remains difficult to collect labeled misinformation. Researchers have collected COVID-19 misconceptions on social media via manual annotation (Hossain et al., 2020 or by linking to fact checking articles (Patwa et al., 2021). Not only are these datasets small (a few thousand samples), but they focus on false claims rather than multimodal inconsistency. Prior work on rumor detection emphasizes textual signals such as stance, sentiment, and propagation patterns, leaving cross-modal grounding underexplored. Meanwhile, advances in vision-language pretraining suggest that entity and scene grounding can help detect mismatched captions in complex settings. Yet constructing benchmarks that combine topical relevance, realistic noise, and human authored manipulations remains a bottleneck for progress. In the news domain, Luo et al. (2021) automatically generate out-of-context images. Here, we curate social media posts that are topical and multimodal, and demonstrate an application to misinformation detection of human-generated fakes. Our collection procedure emphasizes diversity across events, sources, and languages to avoid overfitting to a single crisis narrative. We also standardize metadata fields to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison across modeling approaches. There also exist general approaches for multimodal fact checking, e.g., Jaiswal et al. (2017) and Müller-Budack et al., 2020) that query an external knowledge base. In contrast, contrastive retrieval can be brittle when memes paraphrase or remix source imagery without preserving canonical descriptors. Instead, we use a large pretrained model that does not require an external reference set. We evaluate zero-shot and few-shot baselines to understand the trade-off between annotation cost and generalization across topics. Finally, we outline open challenges around long-horizon verification pipelines, safety risks, and responsible release practices for derived datasets.

Environmental cost of travel and conferences: It is well established that conferences carry a measurable climate cost (Ciers et al., 2019) and that this burden has recently increased (Pierce et al., 2020). Calls have therefore emerged to curtail or reconfigure the physical academic conference calendar (Johnson et al., 2020; Reay, 2003; Achakulvisut et al., 2020; Jäckle, 2019; Dwyer, 2013).

Prior work has quantified emissions from single events (Astudillo and AzariJafari, 2018), recurring conference series (Neugebauer et al., 2020), and even entire disciplines (Waring et al., 2014; Poom et al., 2017). Travel is not the only contributor, as a single PhD has been estimated to account for 21.5 tonnes of CO2-equivalent emissions, with roughly a third tied to conferences (Achten et al., 2013). In France, a typical research lab may allocate about 64% of its carbon output to conference travel and 36% to commuting and energy usage (Mariette et al., 2021).

The pandemic accelerated temporary shifts to online formats, and meta-analyses report substantial fee reductions that can broaden participation (Mubin et al., 2021). Online delivery can also ease concerns about travel expenses (Raby and Madden, 2021), although many attendees perceive fewer social opportunities than at in-person meetings (Achakulvisut et al., 2020). It is also important to recognize that virtual events still incur a measurable infrastructure footprint (Ong et al., 2012; Ong et al., 2014; Faber, 2021).

Despite these costs, in-person conferences offer perceived advantages for networking and informal exchange (Raby and Madden, 2021). Empirical analyses further suggest that work presented over longer travel distances can receive more citations (Chalvatzis and Ormosi, 2020). Community discussions have therefore emphasized balancing climate responsibility with inclusion, accessibility, and research visibility.

Policy proposals include participant carbon offset programs (Holden et al., 2017), careful site selection to shorten average journeys (Wenner et al., 2019), and mandated reporting of event footprints (Cugniere et al., 2020). Similar debates are active in computing communities such as ACM (Pierce et al., 2019). In (Johnson et al., 2020; Reay, 2003; Dwyer, 2013), organizers are urged to redesign programs that minimize unnecessary travel.

Environmental cost of ML and NLP research: Within AI, researchers increasingly report the emissions associated with experiments, especially those relying on deep learning (Schwartz et al., 2019; Henderson et al., 2020). Holistic estimation frameworks account for hardware age, datacenter energy mixes, and total runtime when assessing training costs (García-Martín et al., 2019; Lacoste et al., 2019). In NLP, several studies quantify the escalating cost of training large models and propose energy-aware benchmarking practices (Strubell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020). Resource-aware measurement that includes utilization patterns can yield more accurate assessments of model energy consumption (Cao et al., 2020). At the same time, smaller architectures and transfer learning promise sizable savings through faster training and greater model reuse (Sanh et al., 2019; Anderson and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020; Kocmi and Bojar, 2020).

document: Distantly supervised relation extraction (DS-RE) addresses the mismatch between knowledge base facts and unstructured text by aligning entity pairs in sentences. Early monolingual approaches framed DS-RE with probabilistic graphical models to cope with noisy labels. Notably, multi-instance multi-label formulations aggregate evidence at the bag level to reduce false positives. These formulations emphasize the at-least-one assumption for each relation-bearing bag. Neural methods then replaced manual features with sentence encoders such as piecewise CNNs and attentive RNNs. For example, sentence-level encoders became standard in the NYT corpus era (Hoffmann et al., 2011; Ritter et al., 2013). Many works evaluate under the held-out setting where a bag for an unseen entity pair is scored at test time. However, automatic distant annotation introduces pervasive noise in both training and test splits. This issue was especially prominent in the widely used NYT-10d dataset with automatically labeled test instances. Subsequent resources provided manually annotated test sets to enable fairer comparisons. For instance, Gao et al. (2021) released NYT-10m and Wiki-20m with cleaner test labels. Alongside data improvements, modeling advances explored bag-level attention, sentence denoising, and curriculum strategies. A common baseline continues to encode each sentence independently before aggregating scores at the bag level. The piecewise CNN remains influential despite later transformer-based variants. A representative implementation is the piecewise CNN (Zeng et al., 2015. In parallel, noise-robust objectives such as instance selection and partial-label learning have been proposed for DS-RE. These techniques aim to mitigate label sparsity and context mismatch between knowledge base triples and natural language. Beyond English, multilingual DS-RE extends the task to settings where aligned supervision is scarce or inconsistent. Cross-lingual architectures introduce language-specific encoders with shared attention mechanisms to transfer relational cues. Early bilingual systems combined CNN encoders with cross-lingual attention for English and Mandarin (Lin et al., 2017). Recent datasets such as DiS-ReX enable evaluation across English, Spanish, German, and French with standardized splits. State-of-the-art models often pair multilingual encoders like mBERT with DS-RE architectures adapted from monolingual settings. Historical work also traces the origins of DS-RE to the held-out evaluation paradigm on the NYT corpus, see [Riedel, Yao, and McCallum, 2010) for details. For multilingual evaluation, it is essential to control for annotation policies and language-specific phenomena such as morphological variation. In this paper, we compare mPARE against strong baselines on NYT-10d, NYT-10m, Wiki-20m, and DiS-ReX under consistent preprocessing and metrics.

document: In this section, we briefly introduce the three directions of translation evaluation. The first direction concerns reference-based automatic metrics that compare system outputs against human-written translations. These metrics have evolved from simple n-gram overlap to embeddings and learned scoring functions. BLEU, once dominant, has been critiqued for corpus-level smoothing and insensitivity to meaning. METEOR and TER attempted to account for synonymy and edit distance, respectively. More recently, neural metrics leverage pretrained language models to better capture semantic adequacy. Examples include BERTScore, COMET, BLEURT, and UniEval across diverse language pairs Liu et al., 2020; Smith and Lee, 2019). Training such metrics typically requires human-rated datasets that align scores with expert judgments. However, dataset bias and domain shifts can reduce the validity of these learned metrics. The second direction encompasses human evaluation protocols for fluency, adequacy, and direct assessment. Carefully designed annotation guidelines and rater calibration are critical to obtaining reliable judgments. Pairwise ranking and multi-dimensional scales allow finer-grained conclusions than coarse categories. Yet human evaluation remains time-consuming and costly, which limits rapid iteration in practice. Cross-lingual expertise shortages further complicate assessments in low-resource settings. The third direction focuses on reference-free quality estimation that predicts quality without gold references. Early feature-based QE models relied on engineered predictors of fluency, alignment, and uncertainty. Modern QE approaches use multilingual transformers and task-specific heads to infer errors at sentence or token level. Confidence estimation and uncertainty quantification are increasingly integrated to support risk-aware deployment. Despite progress, evaluations across domains, genres, and noise conditions remain inconsistent. Reproducibility and transparent reporting have also become central concerns for the community. Standardized test suites and leaderboard practices aim to mitigate cherry-picking and hyperparameter leakage Jones et al. 1. Furthermore, multimodal and speech translation pose new challenges for metric design and human protocols. For instance, prosodic fidelity and visual grounding must be reflected in evaluation criteria beyond text alone. Robustness to paraphrase diversity is another priority because accurate translations can vary widely. Finally, we highlight the need for closer alignment between automatic metrics and decision-making under real constraints. In our review, we situate recent advances within a unified framework that connects metrics, human studies, and QE. We also discuss open problems in data collection, such as rater disagreement and scarce gold references. By synthesizing these threads, we outline a research agenda to advance reliable, scalable translation evaluation.

document: Pre-trained model analysis is a rapidly growing area in NLP today. There are a number of methods for analyzing internal representations of a model, including structured head and FCN pruning (Michel et al., 2019;Voita et al., 2019;Prasanna et al., 2020), residual connection and layer normalization analysis (Kovaleva et al., 2021;Kobayashi et al., 2021), and analyzing attention patterns Clark et al., 2019);Kovaleva et al., 2019). Compared to these methods, probing tasks (Conneau et al., 2018;Tenney et al., 2019) provide a more direct way to evaluate what a model can and cannot accomplish. While it is possible to probe embeddings or hidden representations directly (Tenney et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019a), the adoption of pre-trained language models has made it possible to evaluate such models by framing probing tasks close to the original model objective (Radford et al., 2019;Talmor et al., 2019;Ettinger, 2020;Goldberg, 2019). However, when a research area moves this quickly, it can be hard to keep up with many new models. Most of the existing research (Garí Soler and Apidianaki, 2020;Zagoury et al., 2021;Kassner et al., 2020) papers compare only one or two model families. Even some of the most recent works only probe BERT or very similar models (Zagoury et al., 2021;Liu et al., 2021). Only a small number of analysis papers have probed multiple (three or more) model families (Zhou et al., 2021;Ilharco et al., 2021). Building on recent consolidation efforts, we revisit the scope of structural and functional probing for transferability across tasks. In contrast to existing work, we perform a largescale probing of 28 models across 8 different model families. We apply the existing probing benchmarks, namely, oLMpics (Talmor et al., 2019) and psycholinguistic datasets (Ettinger, 2020), to models that differ in the pre-training objective, datasets, size, architecture, and directionality. For completeness, we also include auxiliary diagnostics such as layer-wise control tasks and random-label checks to gauge susceptibility to superficial cues (Hewitt and Liang, 2019). Our analysis emphasizes both instance-level invariances and distributional shifts, enabling comparisons across tokenization schemes and multilingual pre-training settings Conneau et al., 2018; Tenney et al., 2019) and sustained gains with cross-lingual transfer (Conneau et al., 2020). We further examine whether probe outcomes correlate with emergent capabilities such as syntactic generalization and coreference tracking (Linzen et al., 2016;Kuncoro et al., 2018;Joshi et al., 2020). Finally, we discuss implications for evaluation design, recommending reporting practices that separate probe architecture capacity from representational content and that encourage reproducible, compute-aware comparisons across model families.
