document: Encoder–decoder models are widely used in open-domain dialogue response generation, yet they often default to bland and non-committal replies. To mitigate this issue, prior work explores diversity-promoting objectives (Li et al., 2016a), alternative decoding strategies (Li et al., 2016b), incorporation of auxiliary content (Xu et al., 2019), and the integration of structured knowledge (Liu et al., 2018 to guide content selection. However, these techniques can trade specificity and factuality for surface-level diversity. Latent-variable approaches address the one-to-many nature of dialogue by modeling global discourse factors. Classical conditional VAEs improve variety while maintaining coherence when trained with mutual information or bag-of-words auxiliaries (Serban et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017a; Zhao et al., 2018). Extensions combine latent variables with hierarchical encoders to capture multi-turn dependencies (Park et al., 2018). Orthogonal efforts steer generation with controllable attributes such as sentiment, politeness, or topic (Keskar et al., 2019; See et al., 2019). Retrieval-augmented generation increases specificity by grounding responses in retrieved passages (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021; Shuster et al., 2021). Knowledge-grounded dialogue leverages external knowledge bases and memory modules to support informative responses (Dinan et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018a). Large conversational models fine-tuned on diverse corpora provide strong baselines but still struggle with long-horizon coherence (Roller et al., 2021; Thoppilan et al., 2022). Automatic evaluation remains problematic because lexical overlap correlates weakly with human judgments (Liu et al., 2016; Lowe et al., 2017). Embedding-based and learned reference metrics attempt to address this gap with improved semantic sensitivity (Zhang et al., 2020; Sellam et al., 2020; Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). Safety, bias, and calibration issues further complicate deployment in real-world settings (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2021). Data augmentation and counterfactual rewriting can regularize models against spurious correlations and shortcut learning (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Multi-turn coherence has also been enhanced through planning, discourse representations, and entity tracking (Yao et al., 2019; Rashkin et al., 2021). Controllable generation methods such as PPLM and prefix tuning modulate outputs without retraining the base model (Dathathri et al., 2020; Li and Liang, 2021). Despite these advances, the interaction between latent variables and knowledge grounding remains underexplored. Prior work shows that VAEs can increase diversity but sometimes reduce relevance, Serban et al. 2017; Zhao et al., 2018, motivating mechanisms that preserve on-topic content. Conversely, retrieval-augmented systems are specific yet can become extractive and stylistically rigid (Shuster et al., 2021). We hypothesize that coupling a discourse-level latent with retrieval can achieve both diversity and faithfulness. To test this, we pretrain a hierarchical latent-variable encoder–decoder with a contrastive objective over large conversational corpora (Hjelm et al., 2019). At fine-tuning time, we integrate a lightweight retriever and a selector that conditions the latent prior on retrieved evidence (Lewis et al., 2020). Experiments on DailyDialog, ConvAI2, and Wizard of Wikipedia show consistent gains in human-rated specificity and diversity over strong baselines (Li et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018; Dinan et al., 2019). Ablation studies and error analyses indicate that each component contributes to improvements while controlling for degeneration and off-topic drift.

document: Document understanding is a critical and challenging task in information processing. There have been many related research works in this direction. Prior work spans keyword detection and topic modeling to produce compact representations of content (Liu et al., 2009; Tixier et al., 2016; Blei et al., 2003). Typical pipelines first harvest candidate terms with heuristics and then rank them using statistical or graph-based criteria (Hulth, 2003; Shang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2005). Headline generation and summarization provide alternative compressions that target readability and salience (David and Zajic, 2003; Lopyrev, 2015; Rush et al., 2015; See et al., 2017). Outline generation has been proposed as a hierarchical prediction problem by Zhang et al. (2019 to jointly predict section boundaries and headings. Aspect detection is closely related, with early rule-based and lexicon-driven methods later supplanted by supervised sequence labeling (Qiu et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2016; Li et al., 2010; Mitchell et al., 2013; Yang and Cardie, 2012). Neural approaches based on RNNs and pre-trained encoders further improve extraction quality given sufficient labeled data (Wang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2015; Devlin et al., 2019). Unsupervised topic models and their deep variants offer complementary signals for discovering coherent aspects without annotations (García-Pablos et al., 2018; Srivastava and Sutton, 2017; Luo et al., 2019; He et al., 2017). However, these methods are rarely evaluated on long financial or legal documents where section length and terminology pose additional challenges. We address this gap by integrating TF-IDF weighting with aspect discovery to better handle lengthy domain-specific filings. Complementarily, we study question generation to enrich navigation with high-level guiding questions (Shukla et al., 2019; Saeidi et al., 2018). Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence models such as BART and PEGASUS provide strong baselines for this setting (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Building on these models, reinforcement learning with task-specific rewards can bias outputs toward fluency, relevance, and answerability (Kumar et al., 2019; Zhang and Bansal, 2019; Xie et al., 2020). Our system, DYNAMICTOC, combines aspect cues and question generation to enable persona-based, non-linear navigation in long documents. We evaluate the pipeline with automatic metrics and a human study on SEC 10-K filings and community QA data (Fan et al., 2019).

document: Proposing a dependency parsing approach to the full task of SSA, Barnes et al. (2021) show that it leads to strong improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. Peng et al. (2021) propose a sparse fuzzy attention mechanism to deal with the sparseness of dependency arcs in the models from Barnes et al. (2021) and show further improvements. However, in order to apply the parsing algorithm of Dozat and Manning (2018), both of these approaches have to rely on a lossy conversion to bi-lexical dependencies with ad-hoc internal head choices for the nodes of the abstract sentiment graph. This lossy behaviour is caused by nested text spans in the sentiment graphs, as illustrated by Figure 1, which are ambiguous in their bi-lexical dependency encoding (see Section A in the Appendix). More generally, decoding structured graph information from text has sparked a lot of interest in recent years, especially for parsing meaning representation graphs (Oepen et al., 2020). There has been tremendous progress in developing complex transition-based and graph-based parsers (Hershcovich et al., 2017; McDonald and Pereira, 2006; Dozat and Manning, 2018). In this paper, we adopt PERIN (Samuel and Straka, 2020), a state-of-the-art graph-based parser capable of modeling a superset of graph features needed for our task. Structured sentiment analysis aims to recover opinion holders, targets, expressions, and their relational structure from text. Unlike flat sequence labeling, the output here is a directed, labeled graph with potential nesting and reentrancies. Prior work has often simplified the problem by projecting graphs into trees, which removes cross-edges and distorts scope. While such projections enable the use of off-the-shelf dependency parsers, they systematically discard latent structure that may be crucial for downstream applications. Alternative approaches explore hypergraph representations to capture multi-argument predicates without lossy transformations. Yet these models typically require specialized inference that is hard to scale and tune for domain-specific corpora. We argue that a general graph parser with flexible edge typing provides a better match to the phenomena observed in sentiment graphs. PERIN fits this requirement by supporting arbitrary target factorization and global decoding with cycle control. It further allows span-level nodes to be predicted jointly with token-level anchors via a unified tagging and edge module. This joint treatment reduces exposure bias between node creation and relation prediction under realistic annotation noise. For dependency-based models, biaffine scoring remains a strong baseline Dozat and Manning 2018), which many later systems adapt. However, biaffine heads assume single-head structures and thus struggle with reentrant event frames common to sentiment. Recent semantic parsers tackle reentrancy with latent alignment variables and constrained decoding to preserve graph well-formedness. In sentiment tasks, alignment ambiguity is exacerbated by idiomatic expressions whose surface forms underdetermine role assignments. PERIN mitigates these issues by considering candidate spans and edges jointly, scoring them with shared contextual encoders. The choice of encoder also matters, as domain-specific pretraining has been shown to improve robustness to figurative language. In our setting, we consider both multilingual and monolingual encoders to test portability across review and news domains. We follow common practice in evaluating with labeled F1 over nodes and edges, micro-averaged across documents. To contextualize our results, we also report conversion-based baselines that emulate prior dependency-only formulations. This allows a controlled comparison between information-preserving parsing and lossy projection strategies. For datasets with long sentences, we adopt gradient accumulation and subword regularization to stabilize training. Hyperparameters are tuned on development sets using a small grid over learning rates and dropout coefficients. We ablate span pooling, biaffine relation scoring, and label smoothing to identify the contributions of each component. Our error analysis highlights that most remaining errors involve overlapping spans and long-distance polarity propagation. These cases often require document-level context that is beyond the scope of sentence-bound encoders. We therefore experiment with shallow cross-sentence attention to test whether limited context windows can help. Preliminary results indicate modest gains on documents with cohesive opinion threads but little change otherwise. Finally, we release code and converted baselines to facilitate reproducibility and standardized comparisons. By framing SSA as general graph parsing, we provide a principled alternative to projection-based pipelines and close the gap to task-specific systems.

document: We now introduce the task of entity set expansion (ESE), existing paradigms and evaluation methods. Entity set expansion aims to grow a small seed set of exemplars into a more complete list of entities belonging to the same semantic class. Classical approaches leverage distributional similarity in large corpora, assuming that co-occurrence contexts encode category membership (Hearst, 1992; Pantel and Pennacchiotti, 2006). Pattern-based bootstrapping expands seeds by harvesting lexico-syntactic patterns and iteratively extracting candidates (Etzioni et al., 2005; Kozareva et al., 2008). More recent neural methods learn embedding spaces or encoders that map entities and contexts into comparable representations for retrieval (Shi et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2020). Weak supervision and distant supervision have been explored to reduce annotation costs, especially when constructing seed sets from heterogeneous sources (Ratner et al., 2017; Safranchik et al., 2020). Bootstrapping reliability is often improved by enforcing diversity and precision constraints during iteration (Nguyen et al., 2020 while calibrating confidence scores to prevent semantic drift. In parallel, search-enhanced expansion leverages query logs and web tables to rank candidates with click-through and co-table signals (Dalvi et al., 2012; Lehmberg et al., 2016). Interactive ESE systems integrate human-in-the-loop feedback, balancing exploration of novel candidates with exploitation of high-confidence expansions (Yang et al., 2017; Strohmann and Schütze, 2019). Evaluation protocols typically report precision at k, mean average precision, and area under the precision–recall curve on held-out gold lists assembled from knowledge bases or expert annotations (Jin et al., 2013; Rong et al., 2016). However, metrics alone can be misleading when seed sets are biased or when categories have long tails that favor recall-heavy methods. To address this, several works propose stratified test splits and stress tests that vary seed quality, domain shift, and candidate universe size (Gao et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2022). Recent pre-trained language models have further improved expansion quality by contextualizing entity mentions and enabling zero-shot generalization across domains (Petroni et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, these models require careful negative sampling and calibration to avoid overfitting to surface forms and spurious correlations. In what follows, we situate our approach within these paradigms, specify a robust evaluation suite, and highlight open challenges for reproducible ESE research.

document: Paraphrase generation aims to produce meaning-preserving rephrasings for downstream tasks such as question answering and style transfer. Recent research has focused on achieving high-quality paraphrases while balancing fidelity and diversity. Broadly, methods can be grouped into supervised and unsupervised approaches. In supervised settings, neural sequence models are trained with parallel paraphrase corpora to maximize likelihood under structural constraints. One line of work seeks diversity through decoding heuristics that penalize repetition or encourage coverage of distinct n-grams during beam search (Vijayakumar et al., 2018). Another line perturbs latent representations to sample multiple outputs that diverge lexically yet remain semantically consistent (Gupta et al., 2018; Park et al., 2019). Complementary strategies train distinct generators or mixture-of-experts to induce output variance aligned with stylistic factors (Qian et al., 2019). However, these methods often lack interpretable control over specific attributes of the generated text. Controlled paraphrase generation addresses this gap by exposing knobs for lexical, syntactic, or semantic variation. Some studies target lexical control by conditioning on salient keywords or attribute tags, as in Zeng et al. and Thompson and Post (2020). Others pursue syntactic control by aligning the output to exemplars or parse templates derived from external sources (Iyyer et al., 2018; Li and Choi, 2020). A related approach leverages explicit modeling of permissible reordering patterns to expand the set of candidate structures (Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Exemplar-based methods guide surface form by matching the target to a reference sentence with desired syntax, as in Bao et al. Despite these advances, fine-grained control across multiple dimensions remains challenging in practice. In particular, jointly coordinating lexical choice, syntactic shape, and semantic adequacy can create conflicting objectives at decoding time. Recent work explores multi-aspect control by combining attribute-specific losses with calibrated search procedures. Yet comprehensive evaluations often reveal trade-offs between meaning preservation and novel expression that depend on domain and training data. On the unsupervised side, paraphrases are induced without parallel data by learning from denoising objectives or pseudo-parallel retrieval. Techniques that restrict copying during inference have been shown to increase diversity while maintaining adequacy (Niu et al., 2020). Search-based formulations recast paraphrasing as optimizing a weighted objective over semantic similarity, fluency, and distance from the source (Liu et al., 2020). Reinforcement learning further integrates textual entailment and stylistic penalties to reward high-quality alternatives (Garg et al., 2021; Siddique et al., 2020). These unsupervised approaches can complement supervised training by providing candidates and priors for control signals. Evaluation remains a central issue because automatic metrics may conflate diversity with degradation in meaning. Recent studies therefore combine semantic similarity, lexical novelty, and human judgments to obtain a balanced view of system performance. Given this landscape, our study aims to jointly control lexical, syntactic, and semantic dimensions in a simple training and decoding pipeline. We hypothesize that mild regularization on attribute predictors and light-weight constraints at inference can yield robust diversity gains. To test this, we situate our method among prior supervised baselines and unsupervised oracles under common datasets and protocols. We also analyze failure cases to understand when control settings interact adversely with dataset characteristics and model calibration.

Temporal Commonsense. Work on temporal reasoning ranges from extracting and normalizing temporal expressions (Strötgen and Gertz, 2010;Angeli et al., 2012;Vashishtha et al., 2019), to inferring possibly explicit temporal attributes of events, including their order (Ning et al., 2018;Vashishtha et al., 2019), duration (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008;Vashishtha et al., 2019), and typical times or frequencies (Zhou et al., 2019).

Various benchmarks were proposed to measure models' temporal reasoning abilities. The bAbI suite contains a task that requires reasoning about the order of time expressions (Weston et al., 2015). MC-TACO is a reading comprehension task pertaining to ordering, duration, stationarity, frequency, and typical times of events (Zhou et al., 2019). TI-MEDIAL (Qin et al., 2021 is a dialogue QA task focusing on temporal commonsense. Zhou et al. (2021) and Thukral et al. (2021) both cast the temporal ordering task as an NLI task. In another line of work, tracking state changes in procedural text is also related to temporal ordering (Dalvi et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2020). Despite the success of pre-trained LMs on language understanding tasks, their performance on these benchmarks is limited, maybe due to the fact that many temporal relations are not explicitly stated in text (Davis and Marcus, 2015). A promising direction is to train LMs explicitly on temporal knowledge (Zhou et al., 2020). Recent pretraining objectives encode temporal relations implicitly, yet dedicated temporal corpora remain scarce. This motivates constructing resources that capture both commonsense and culturally specific temporal norms.

Cultural Commonsense. There is little focus on cultural differences in NLP in general (Hovy and Yang, 2021) and in research about commonsense reasoning in particular. Recently, Acharya et al. (2021) made a first step in addressing this gap. They surveyed crowdsourcing workers in the US and India regarding rituals that are commonly found across cultures such as birth, marriage, and funerals. In particular, they asked questions pertaining to temporal aspects such as typical time and duration of each event. The paper mentions anecdotal differences such that a wedding lasts a few hours in the US but a few days in India.

Although there is no direct mapping between culture and language, one can often teach about the other. For example, in ConceptNet, a multilingual commonsense knowledge base, the English entry for breakfast specifies pancakes as breakfast food, while the Chinese entry mentions noodles (Speer et al., 2017).

Language Grounding and World Knowledge. Our work is related to language grounding (Roy and Reiter, 2005) and to extracting world knowledge from text corpora (Carlson et al., 2010;Tandon et al., 2014). In the intersection of these two lines of work, Forbes and Choi (2017) extracted from a corpus physical commonsense knowledge about actions and objects along five dimensions (size, weight, strength, rigidness, and speed), while Elazar et al. (2019) induced distributions of typical values of various quantitative attributes such as time, duration, length, and speed. Elazar et al. (2019) mention cultural differences that arose when crowdsourcing workers were asked to estimate whether an item's price was expensive or not: annotators from India judged prices differently from annotators in the US.

document: QG has progressed rapidly due to new datasets and model improvements. Many different QG models have been proposed, starting from simple vanilla sequence-to-sequence neural network models (seq2seq) Yuan et al., 2017) to more recent transformer-based models (Dong et al., 2019; Chan and Fan, 2019; Varanasi et al., 2020; Narayan et al., 2020; Bao et al., 2020). Early seq2seq approaches framed question generation as conditional language modeling with attention and copying mechanisms (Du et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2017). Subsequent work incorporated syntactic and semantic features to better capture answer focus and discourse structure (Harrison and Walker, 2018; Khullar et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019a; Dhole and Manning, 2020). Parallel advances in pretraining yielded strong encoder–decoder backbones that improved fluency and controllability (Dong et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2020). Researchers also explored content selection strategies to identify question-worthy spans before decoding (Li et al., 2019; Scialom et al., 2019). Another line of work controlled interrogative form, answer type, or style to steer generation toward task needs (Duan et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2018; Kang et al., 2019). These advances collectively shifted the field from pipeline heuristics to end-to-end learning with explicit constraints and priors (Chen et al., 2021). Beyond monolingual settings, multilingual QG has examined transfer and adaptation across languages with limited supervision (Chan and Fan, 2019; Varanasi et al., 2020). Domain-specific QG has addressed educational, clinical, and conversational scenarios where factuality and safety are crucial (Liyanage and Ranathunga, 2019; Jia et al., 2021). In conversational dialogue, models aim to ask coherent follow-up questions grounded in dialogue state and external knowledge (Gu et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Visual question generation builds questions from images or videos by aligning visual features with linguistic templates or decoders (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Shin et al., 2018; Shukla et al., 2019). Recent studies investigate controllability to produce questions with specified difficulty, style, or answer type via prompts or control codes (Zhang and Bansal, 2019; Pasunuru and Bansal, 2018). Data augmentation has been another key driver, with synthetic pairs expanding coverage and reducing annotation costs (Alberti et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2019). However, quality filtering and de-duplication are essential to avoid overfitting to artifact-laden examples (Sulem et al., 2020). Evaluation remains challenging because common metrics correlate imperfectly with answerability and pedagogical utility (Callison-Burch et al., 2006; Chaganty et al., 2018). To mitigate this, extrinsic evaluation through QA performance and human studies is increasingly adopted (Scialom et al., 2019; Narayan et al., 2020). At the modeling level, copy and coverage mechanisms reduce repetition and support faithful extraction of key entities (See et al., 2017; Tu et al., 2016). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models like BART and T5 provide strong initialization and generalization (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). Nevertheless, purely generative approaches can hallucinate or ask unanswerable questions when context modeling is weak (Maynez et al., 2020). Hybrid retrieval–generation architectures address this by grounding decoding on retrieved passages or structured knowledge (Lewis et al., 2020; Das et al., 2018). Content selection modules based on span ranking or graph propagation further enhance the precision of target selection (Li et al., 2019; Song et al., 2018). Recent prompt-based methods reframe QG as constrained infilling to better align with masked or prefix objectives in pretrained models (Qin and Eisner, 2021; Scao and Rush, 2021). In educational applications, QG supports formative assessment and intelligent tutoring by generating diverse yet valid questions from curricular materials (Heilman and Smith, 2010; Kurdi et al., 2020). For clinical narratives, systems must maintain terminological accuracy and patient privacy while avoiding speculative prompts (Liu et al., 2019a; Yue et al., 2020). Curiosity-driven QG explores open-ended information-seeking behaviors that promote exploration in interactive systems (Mostafazadeh et al., 2016; Rothe et al., 2017). Robustness to domain shift remains an open problem, particularly for specialized jargon and low-resource settings (Yu et al., 2021). Few-shot and continual learning paradigms attempt to address data scarcity while retaining previously learned skills (Brown et al., 2020; de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019). Ethical considerations highlight the need to prevent biased, leading, or unsafe questions in sensitive contexts (Bender et al., 2021; Weidinger et al., 2022). Bringing these strands together, current research emphasizes controllability, calibration, and attribution to ensure faithful and useful question generation (Kang et al., 2019; Dhole and Manning, 2020). This paper builds on these insights to study scalable QG that balances fluency, faithfulness, and content selection across diverse domains.

document: Late-interaction models. The idea of running several transformer layers for the document and the query independently, and then combining them in the last transformer layers, was developed concurrently by multiple teams: PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020), EARL (Gao et al., 2020a), DC-BERT (Nie et al., 2020), DiPair , and the Deformer (Cao et al., 2020). These works show that only a few layers where the query and document interact are sufficient to achieve results close to the performance of a full BERT ranker at a fraction of the runtime cost. For each document, the contextual token vectors are stored in a cache and retrieved during the document ranking phase. This impacts both storage cost as well as latency cost of fetching these vectors during the ranking phase. MORES (Gao et al., 2020b), extends lateinteraction models, where in the last interaction layers only the query attends to the document (and not vice-versa). As document are typically much longer, this results in additional performance improvements with similar storage requirements. Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) is another variant that runs all transformer layers independently for the query and the document, and the interaction between the final vectors is done through a sumof-max operator. A similar work, the Transformer-Kernel (TK) (Hofstätter et al., 2020b), has an interaction block based on a low-complexity kernel operation. Both ColBERT and TK result in models with lower runtime latency at the expense of a drop in ranking quality. However, the storage requirements for both approaches are still significant. Some of the works above acknowledge the issue of storing the precomputed document representations and proposed partial solutions. In Col-BERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), the authors proposed to reduce the dimension of the final token embedding using a linear layer. However, even moderate compression ratios caused a large drop in ranking quality. In the PreTTR model (MacAvaney et al., 2020), it was proposed to address the storage cost by using a standard auto-encoder architecture and the float16 format instead of float32. Again, the ranking quality drops even with moderate compression ratios (they measured up to 12x). Several other works (Guu et al., 2020;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Qu et al., 2020;Lu et al., 2020) proposed representing the queries and documents as vectors (as opposed to a vector per token), and using dot product as the interaction block. While this ranker architecture approach is simple (and can also be used for the retrieval step via an approximate nearest neighbor search such as FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017) or ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020)), the overall ranking quality is generally lower compared to methods that employ a query-document cross-attention interaction. Compressed embeddings. Our work reduces storage requirements by reducing the number of bits per floating-point value. Quantization gained attention and success in reducing the size of neural network parameters (Gupta et al., 2015;Essam et al., 2017;Wang et al., 2018;Wu et al., 2018) and distributed learning communication costs (Suresh et al., 2017;Alistarh et al., 2017;Konečnỳ and Richtárik, 2018;Vargaftik et al., 2021b). Specifically, compressing word embeddings has been studied as an independent goal. May et al. (2019) studied the effect of quantized word embeddings on downstream applications and proposed a metric for quantifying this effect with simple linear models that operate on the word embeddings directly. As our work is concerned with compressing contextual embeddings, these methods do not apply since the set of possible embeddings values is not bounded by the vocabulary size. Nevertheless, as in (May et al., 2019), we also observe that simple quantization schemes are quite effective. Our work uses recent advances in this area to further reduce storage requirements for document representation, which, to the best of our knowledge, were not previously attempted in this context. Finally, we discuss practical considerations for serving compressed representations at scale. Related toolkits are widely available Johnson et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2020).

document: Patient-Specific Literature Retrieval. Since 2014, the Text REtrieval Conference (TREC) has organized a series of challenges to advance research in this area. The TREC Clinical Decision Support (CDS) tracks focused on evaluating systems on the task of retrieving biomedical articles relevant for answering generic clinical questions about patient medical records (Simpson et al., 2014; Roberts et al., 2015; Roberts et al., 2016). TREC CDS 2014 and 2015 used short case reports as idealized representations of medical records due to the lack of available de-identified records. TREC 2016 shifted to using real-world medical records from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care database (Johnson et al., 2016). Building on this line of work, we examine how retrieval conditioned on patient notes can support downstream clinical prediction. In our work, our focus is on predicting clinical outcomes using ICU admission notes and patient-specific retrieved literature. Prior work explored contextualized representations for more structured retrieval tasks not involving clinical notes (Ueda et al., 2021; Voorhees et al., 2021). However, there remains an open question about the behavior of large pretrained language models when applied to long and noisy EHR text. We address this gap by experimenting with language models for retrieval based on EHR narratives. Clinical Outcome Prediction. The idea of using automated outcome prediction for assisting clinical triage, workflow optimization, and hospital resource management has received much interest recently, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. Predictive models based on structured information, such as laboratory results, and unstructured information, such as nursing notes, have been built for key clinical outcomes including mortality (Jain et al., 2019; Feng et al., 2020), length of hospital stay (van Aken et al., 2021), readmission (Jain et al., 2019), and sepsis (Feng et al., 2020). Additional tasks have covered prolonged mechanical ventilation and diagnostic coding (Jain et al., 2019; van Aken et al., 2021). Most recently, large pretrained language models have been used for outcome prediction in intensive care van Aken et al., 2021), demonstrating competitive performance at scale. At the same time, there is growing interest in augmenting clinical predictors with information retrieved from the biomedical literature. Early evidence suggests that linking notes to relevant abstracts can provide complementary signals beyond structured fields (Boag et al., 2018; Jin et al., 2018). Our approach generalizes this idea by integrating retrieval and prediction in a modular pipeline. We call the resulting system BEEP, which consists of a literature retrieval stage followed by an outcome prediction stage. Figure provides a high-level illustration of BEEP and unpacks it with more detail in subsequent sections. For a given patient ICU admission note, the retrieval module first retrieves relevant biomedical abstracts from a clinical outcome specific index and then reranks a top ranked subset. The prediction module aggregates information from these reranked abstracts and fuses it with the admission note to make the final classification. Concretely, given a patient note Q and an outcome of interest y, we identify a set of abstracts Docs(Q) = {D1 through Dn} from PubMed that may inform the decision. We then augment the input to an EHR based predictor with these retrieved abstracts, namely Q ∪ Docs(Q), and predict the final outcome. Our design choice separates retrieval from prediction to support interpretability and flexible index updates. We also briefly experiment with a joint formulation that trains both stages end to end. To situate our contribution, we note that work on case based retrieval for clinical decision support has typically targeted question answering rather than prediction (Voorhees et al., 2021). By contrast, our study focuses on patient-specific retrieval to directly improve supervised outcome prediction (Boag et al., 2018.

document: Long-context modeling has become central to modern NLP and multimodal systems. The canonical Transformer scales quadratically in sequence length, which limits practical context windows (Vaswani et al., 2017). A large body of work proposes sparse attention patterns to reduce this cost (Child et al., 2019;Beltagy et al., 2020;Zaheer et al., 2020). Kernel and low-rank approximations offer an orthogonal route to subquadratic complexity (Wang et al., 2020;Katharopoulos et al., 2020;Choromanski et al., 2021). Memory-augmented recurrence extends effective horizons without processing every token pair (Dai et al., 2019;Rae et al., 2020). Positional representations are another crucial design axis for long sequences (Shaw et al., 2018;Press et al., 2021). Hierarchical encoders attempt to summarize local spans before global integration (Liu and Lapata, 2019). Retrieval-augmented models amortize long-range knowledge through external memories (Borgeaud et al., 2022). Efficient architectures are now evaluated on standardized long-range suites such as the Long Range Arena (Tay et al., 2021). Domain-specific benchmarks for scientific and legal documents further stress scaling behavior (Chen et al., 2021;Chalkidis et al., 2022). Beyond language, long-sequence models support video understanding and speech processing (Sun et al., 2019;Gulati et al., 2020). Summarization of book-length material remains a demanding testbed for these methods (Kryściński et al., 2021;Huang et al., 2021). Long-form question answering integrates document retrieval with extended reasoning over evidence chains (Fan et al., 2019;Pang et al., 2021). Machine translation with document context benefits from models that carry discourse across sentences (Liu and Zhang, 2020;Voita et al., 2018). Recent surveys have synthesized design choices and empirical trade-offs in this area (Tay et al., 2020;Narayan et al., 2022). Despite progress, throughput, stability, and quality often pull in different directions during training (Shazeer, 2019;Chen et al., 2020). Implementation advances such as fused kernels and attention tiling mitigate some bottlenecks (Dao et al., 2022;Rabe and Staats, 2021). Comparative studies increasingly emphasize cross-domain robustness and fair resource accounting Shaham et al. 1; Tay et al. 2021. Our work situates itself in this landscape by targeting efficient attention with strong inductive bias. We focus on sparse global tokens that adapt to content while preserving per-layer efficiency (Zaheer et al., 2020;Beltagy et al., 2020). We adopt relative position encodings to stabilize extrapolation beyond the training window (Shaw et al., 2018). To avoid information starvation, we combine dilation patterns with learned global selectors (Child et al., 2019;Roy et al., 2021). We further incorporate chunked feed-forward layers to match attention bandwidth at long lengths (Dao et al., 2022). Our design choices are informed by negative results that caution against brittle sparsity masks (Tay et al., 2021). We also heed evidence that retrieval and parametric memory interact nontrivially at scale (Borgeaud et al., 2022). In evaluation, we adopt benchmarks spanning algorithmic, textual, and multimodal tasks for balance (Tay et al., 2021;Shaham et al., 2022). For textual workloads, we include document-level summarization and cross-document QA (Kryściński et al., 2021;Pang et al., 2021). For code and math, we sample long chain-of-thought completions with controlled prompts (Cobbe et al., 2021;Lewkowycz et al., 2022). For speech and vision, we track sequence truncation effects and compute budgets (Gulati et al., 2020;Bain et al., 2021). We report efficiency metrics alongside accuracy to reflect real deployment constraints (Huang et al., 2021;Dao et al., 2022). Our ablations test the sensitivity to window size, sparsity density, and positional schemes (Press et al., 2021;Shaw et al., 2018). We compare against strong baselines including BigBird, Longformer, Performer, and Linformer (Zaheer et al., 2020;Beltagy et al., 2020;Choromanski et al., 2021;Wang et al., 2020). The results suggest that modest global capacity, placed judiciously, yields favorable Pareto fronts. Errors concentrate in examples requiring precise cross-paragraph coreference or arithmetic carryover. We conclude that efficient attention remains a productive frontier for scaling long-context reasoning. We release code and preprocessed datasets to facilitate reproducible comparisons (Tay et al., 2021).

document: Vision-language few-shot learning. Recently, several few-shot learners for vision-language tasks were proposed, including GPT (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020), Frozen (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021), PICa, and SimVLM. Frozen (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021) is a large language model based on GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) that is transformed into a multimodal few-shot learner by extending soft prompting to incorporate a set of images and text. Their approach demonstrates few-shot capability on visual question answering and image classification tasks. Similarly, PICa uses GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) to solve VQA tasks in a few-shot manner by providing a small number of in-context examples. It converts images into textual descriptions so that GPT-3 can condition on language alone. SimVLM is trained with prefix language modeling on weakly supervised datasets. It demonstrates strong zero-shot performance on image captioning benchmarks. While these models achieve notable gains on few-shot tasks, they are impractical to deploy in many real-world applications due to their extreme model sizes. Language model prompting. Providing prompts or task descriptions plays a vital role in improving pre-trained language models across many tasks (Radford et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b; Brown et al., 2020). Among them, GPT-style models (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020) achieved strong results when guided by task demonstrations and carefully designed prompts. Prompt-based approaches further improve small pre-trained models in few-shot text classification tasks Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b). CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) also explores prompt templates for image classification, which substantially affect zero-shot performance. Building on these ideas, we aim to improve zero-shot and few-shot performance using prompts in vision-language tasks. We pretrain FEWVLM with masked language modeling and prefix language modeling to align visual cues with linguistic prompts. Our design emphasizes parameter efficiency while retaining competitive transfer performance under limited supervision. We anticipate that principled prompting will reduce the dependence on expensive annotation and facilitate broader applicability in multimodal settings.

document: Sequence Ordering is a key challenge for modeling procedural narratives in multimodal settings. Story sequencing tests have long examined children’s abilities in sequential reasoning and procedural understanding (Tomkins, 1952; Baron-Cohen et al., 1986; Loucks et al., 2017). In NLP, the sequencing task is often operationalized as sorting a series of unordered sentences drawn from abstracts or short paragraphs (Cui et al., 2018; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Oh et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Calizzano et al., 2021). While some studies extend this setup to multimodality, datasets like Visual StoryTelling contain album images that were not designed to encode stepwise procedures or to supply unstated details to complement the text. In computer vision, researchers leverage shuffled frame prediction to learn video representations (Lee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Cycle-consistency constraints are also used to capture temporal dynamics in untrimmed footage (Epstein et al., 2021). Work on learning temporal commonsense from scripted videos introduces pairwise frame reordering objectives to acquire event-level priors (Zellers et al., 2021). However, as demonstrated by Zellers et al. 2021, most downstream tasks in that line emphasize visual reasoning and frame–text matching rather than end to end procedural sequencing. Our work takes a different perspective by tackling comprehensive multimodal sequencing with a focus on procedural task solving and the helpfulness of complementary modalities. WikiHow offers a rich resource for studying task knowledge at scale due to its stepwise instructions and crowdsourced illustrations. In NLP, WikiHow text has been used for abstractive summarization (Koupaee and Wang, 2018), procedural understanding (Tandon et al., 2020), and intent estimation (Zhang et al., 2020a). Prior work studies temporal ordering of events in WikiHow but focuses primarily on pairwise relations rather than full sequence recovery (Zhang et al., 2020b). A concurrent line infers visual goals from instructional content to bridge language and perception (Yang et al., 2021). Our curation aims to advance comprehensive multimodal procedural understanding by aligning steps, states, and outcomes across modalities. Recent advances in multimodal grounding provide general purpose backbones for joint vision–language modeling (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020b; Huang et al., 2020; Wen et al., 2021). We utilize VisualBERT and CLIP-ViL because they are simple to adapt to our task and align well with our proposed pretraining strategies. Nevertheless, the framework can incorporate any of the aforementioned models without architectural changes. Pretraining objectives that emphasize temporal coherence are particularly relevant for sequence recovery from weakly aligned modalities. Shuffled prediction losses encourage models to discover order-sensitive cues in both text and imagery (Lee et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019). Complementarily, consistency constraints over cycles expose violations that arise from incorrect step transitions (Epstein et al., 2021). Pairwise reordering can serve as a proxy for learning temporal common sense in video when combined with structured supervision. Still, translating such pairwise signals into faithful multi sentence sequencing remains underexplored in multimodal settings. Our study evaluates whether textual procedural cues and visual affordances contribute additively or redundantly to ordering accuracy. We further analyze the robustness of learned orderings under missing modalities and noisy step descriptions. Beyond accuracy, we consider calibration and uncertainty to assess when models should abstain from confident but incorrect orderings (Guo et al., 2017). The evaluation protocol includes synthetic perturbations that scramble local neighborhoods and stress global consistency. We benchmark against strong unimodal and multimodal baselines trained with identical supervision budgets. Taken together, these investigations situate multimodal sequencing within a broader effort to model procedural knowledge that generalizes across tasks and domains.

document: Temporal drift has been studied in several NLP tasks, including document classification (Paul, 2018, 2019), sentiment analysis (Lukes and Søgaard, 2018), named entity recognition (Rijhwani and Preotiuc-Pietro, 2020), neural machine translation (Levenberg et al., 2010), and language modelling (Lazaridou et al., 2021). However, prior work largely ignores the interplay between class imbalance and temporal concept drift. Most studies have been diagnostic, offering analyses rather than deployable mitigation strategies. In multi-label document classification, class imbalance has primarily been approached via architectural innovations. To improve performance for underrepresented classes, (Snell et al., 2017) introduced Prototypical Networks that aggregate instances into prototype label vectors. In a similar vein, Mullenbach et al. (2018) proposed the Label-Wise Attention Network, which learns label-wise representations by attending to informative tokens. Subsequent work by Rios and Kavuluru (2018) incorporated label descriptions and hierarchical relations through graph convolution. Alternative encoders show modest few-shot gains but can degrade overall accuracy (Chalkidis et al., 2020a). Fairness-aware learning provides complementary tools to address disparities that arise from data and objectives. Comprehensive surveys outline algorithmic definitions and techniques for fairness (Mehrabi et al., 2021). Recent studies connect class imbalance to group-level biases, suggesting that label-wise improvements can cascade to demographic parity gains (Blakeney et al., 2021; Subramanian et al., 2021). Building on this literature, we examine optimization strategies rather than only model architectures. Specifically, we adapt group-robust objectives originally designed for protected-attribute disparities to reduce performance gaps across labels. In (Paul et al., 2018, 2019) the notion of temporal stratification motivates our dataset splits into train, development, and test periods. Concretely, we use a training set of 20k documents from 1975–2002, a development set of 8.5k from 2002–2008, and a test set of 8.5k from 2008–2018.

document: Online toxic discourse detection has become a central problem in content moderation for social platforms. Early systems relied on lexicons and simple heuristics to flag prototypical slurs and profanities. While such methods offered high precision on explicit abuse, they performed poorly on implicit or context-dependent toxicity. The release of annotated datasets catalyzed machine learning approaches that moved beyond dictionary lookups. Pioneering corpora labeled abusive language in Twitter and other forums, enabling supervised modeling (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018). Traditional classifiers with sparse features established strong baselines using n-grams, character cues, and metadata (Badjatiya et al., 2017; Gao and Huang, 2017). Subsequent research adopted deep neural architectures to leverage distributed representations and contextual cues (Pitsilis et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Zampieri et al., 2019). The advent of large pre-trained language models further improved transferability and robustness across domains (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). Despite performance gains, accuracy alone is insufficient given the high societal stakes of moderation. Studies have documented systematic disparities in false positive and false negative rates across demographic groups (Hovy and Spruit, 2016; Park et al., 2018; Sap et al., 2019). These findings motivated work on debiasing, reweighting, and controlled data augmentation to mitigate spurious correlations (Borkan et al., 2019; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). Parallel efforts improved evaluation by introducing more nuanced labels and context-aware test suites (Caselli et al., 2020; Vidgen et al., 2021). Another active thread examines domain shift, as toxicity manifests differently across communities and platforms. Researchers proposed adversarial training and contrastive objectives to increase robustness to paraphrases and obfuscations (Hosseini et al., 2017; Pavlopoulos et al., 2020). Benchmark competitions standardized tasks and spurred reproducible comparisons across modeling choices (Zampieri et al., 2019). At the same time, interpretability methods have been used to diagnose model reliance on topic and identity terms (Schmidt and Wiegand, 2017; Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Data curation remains a bottleneck due to annotation subjectivity, annotator demographics, and shifting community norms. Sampling strategies that combine keyword seeding with active learning have been explored to reduce label imbalance and capture long-tail phenomena (Founta et al., 2018; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). In particular, multilabel schemes recognizing insults, threats, harassment, and toxicity gradients aim to reflect real moderation needs (Caselli et al., 2020). Within this landscape, contextual encoders such as BERT and RoBERTa serve as strong backbones for classification heads (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Auxiliary tasks like sentiment analysis, stance detection, and paraphrase identification have been leveraged for multi-task learning to share representations across related phenomena (Ruder, 2017; Clark et al., 2019). Cross-lingual extensions adapt models to multilingual communities using translation, alignment, or shared subword vocabularies (Conneau et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, limitations persist in handling sarcasm, reclaimed slurs, and evolving euphemisms that evade static filters (Sap et al., 2019; Vidgen et al., 2021). Early supervised datasets such as Waseem and Hovy 2016 spurred benchmark-driven progress. More recently, research has emphasized safety-aware deployment practices, including calibrated thresholds and human-in-the-loop review (Borkan et al., 2019; Vidgen and Derczynski, 2020). Our work situates itself in this trajectory by focusing on compositional context and pragmatic cues that determine when otherwise benign words are harmful. We synthesize prior advances in representation learning with principled evaluation protocols to assess generalization under distribution shift. Finally, we outline open challenges and argue for community-shared testbeds that track concept drift and linguistic innovation over time.

document: Fair machine learning research has accelerated as concerns about bias and accountability in AI have become mainstream. The literature on inducing approximately fair models from biased data is rapidly growing. Several surveys synthesize this progress, including a recent overview by Mehrabi et al. (2021). We rely on this literature to define fairness, to select baselines, and to motivate our experimental design. Following common practice, we conceptualize group fairness through parity in performance or error across protected groups. In particular, we operationalize performance parity as minimizing worst-group loss following Hashimoto et al. (2018). We also consider equal risk objectives inspired by distributionally robust optimization and formalized by Donini et al. (2018). Beyond definitions, algorithmic strategies range from reweighting and constraints to representation learning and robust training. Among robust training methods, Group Distributionally Robust Optimization has emerged as a strong baseline (Sagawa et al., 2020). Invariant Risk Minimization aims to learn predictors stable across environments, though its practical benefits are debated (Arjovsky et al., 2020). We evaluate these approaches alongside standard empirical risk minimization to contextualize their trade-offs. Prior work has examined their fairness effects in text classification, including toxicity detection Koh et al., 2021). Foundational critiques highlight that fairness metrics can be mutually incompatible under differing base rates (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2017). This impossibility motivates careful metric selection aligned with application goals and stakeholder values (Corbett-Davies et al., 2017). In criminal justice, algorithmic risk assessments have become a focal point for fairness debates. A landmark investigation by Angwin et al. (2016) analyzed the COMPAS system used for pretrial or parole decisions in the United States. The system relied on extensive questionnaire responses and criminal history features to generate recidivism scores. Angwin et al. (2016) reported that Black defendants were nearly twice as likely as white defendants to be mislabeled as high risk. Follow-up studies compared human judgments and algorithmic scores, finding comparable accuracy but divergent error profiles (Dressel and Farid, 2018). In face recognition, differential error rates have been documented across gender and skin tone, underscoring dataset and modeling biases (Buolamwini and Gebru, 2018). Methodologically, fairness interventions can be introduced pre-, in-, or post-processing depending on data access and deployment constraints (Dwork et al., 2012; Hardt et al., 2016). Constraint-based training incorporates fairness as a soft or hard requirement, often via convex surrogates for demographic parity or equalized odds (Zafar et al., 2017; Agarwal et al., 2018). Adversarial representation learning pursues invariance to protected attributes while preserving task performance (Edwards and Storkey, 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Causal perspectives seek counterfactual definitions that better align with fairness intuitions in settings with confounding (Kusner et al., 2017; Kilbertus et al., 2017). Yet, causal approaches require strong assumptions that may be violated in observational data common to many applications. Recent work integrates uncertainty estimation with fairness objectives to reduce worst-group risk under domain shift (Oren et al., 2019). Our study contributes empirical evidence on when robust training improves group parity relative to simpler baselines in realistic label-noise regimes. We also release code and standardized evaluation splits to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparisons across future studies.

document: Recent advances in transfer learning have reshaped approaches to sentence-level and pairwise tasks in NLP. Supplementary training strategies often exploit intermediate objectives to bridge pretraining and task-specific fine-tuning. Prior work found that initializing RTE, STS, and MRPC from an MNLI-tuned checkpoint consistently improves accuracy over starting from a vanilla encoder. This observation supports the intuition that entailment-aware representations provide generalizable inductive bias for semantic evaluation (Phang et al.; Liu et al.). Complementary to this line, sentence embedding methods explore intermediate contrastive or NLI-based objectives to enhance semantic textual similarity. Representative systems such as SentenceBERT, BERT-flow, and SimCSE report strong STS gains when pre-fine-tuned on large NLI datasets (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Li et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Moreover, task transformation via entailment framing has been shown to help low-resource single-sentence classification by reusing rich sentence-pair corpora (Wang et al., 2021). Beyond full fine-tuning, adapter tuning offers a parameter-efficient alternative that inserts small bottleneck layers within frozen transformers (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020b). On GLUE, adapters typically recover near full fine-tuning performance while updating only a small fraction of parameters, enabling scalable multi-task deployment. Structural compression techniques such as Compactor further reduce adapter size with minimal loss by exploiting Kroneckerized parameterization (Mahabadi et al., 2021). In parallel, prompt-based learning reframes objectives by conditioning large language models with natural or soft prompts. Early work demonstrated that carefully written textual prompts can steer generation and classification in GPT-3 with little or no gradient updates (Brown et al., 2020). Subsequent studies introduced automated prompt search to improve few-shot robustness under constrained label budgets (Gao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). To overcome the brittleness of discrete tokens, continuous prompting methods optimize trainable embeddings prepended to the model input or activations. Prefix tuning and P-tuning v2 attach learned vectors across transformer layers to elicit task-relevant behavior without touching backbone weights (Li and Liang, 2021; Liu et al., 2021a). Related approaches such as prompt tuning and P-tuning interleave trainable parameters in the input embedding layer rather than at every depth (Lester et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021b). Collectively, these techniques highlight a spectrum from full fine-tuning to lightweight adaptation that trades compute for modularity and reuse. Despite progress, most prompting studies optimize a single prompt per task and overlook instance-level variability that affects decision boundaries. Dynamic prompt generators attempt to address this gap by conditioning prompts on example features to capture local semantics. Our method, IDPG, extends this idea by learning to synthesize prompts conditioned on both task descriptors and instance encodings. The resulting generator cooperates with a frozen encoder to balance stability from pretraining and flexibility from prompt adaptation. In experiments, we compare against strong baselines across RTE, STS, and MRPC using standard splits and evaluation protocols. We also analyze transfer from MNLI-style intermediate training to zero- or few-shot regimes on related classification tasks. For fairness, we adopt consistent hyperparameters for adapter, prefix, and prompt tuning methods while reporting variance across seeds. Finally, we situate our contributions within the broader literature by contrasting dynamic prompting with sentence embedding pre-fine-tuning and adapter compression.

document: Sequence-to-sequence models have long been central to data-driven dialogue generation across open-domain and task-oriented settings (Sutskever et al., 2014; Vinyals and Le, 2015). Building on encoder–decoder architectures first popularized for neural machine translation, researchers have explored mechanisms that better control content selection and style (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016a). Early neural conversational agents struggled with blandness and inconsistency, motivating persona modeling and topic conditioning (Li et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2018). Subsequent work introduced multi-turn encoders and hierarchical attention to capture discourse-level dependencies (Serban et al., 2016; Xing et al., 2017). As pre-trained language models became prevalent, fine-tuning them for dialogue yielded sizable gains in fluency and knowledge recall (Wolf et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, reliance on surface patterns often leads to hallucinated facts and brittle context usage in real conversations (Shuster et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2023). To address these limitations, knowledge-grounded dialogue generation leverages external documents or structured triples during response planning (Dinan et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2018a). A key challenge is selecting salient evidence spans while avoiding distraction from irrelevant snippets in long documents (Chen et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020c). Retrieval-augmented models fuse latent LM priors with explicit evidence through gating, cross-attention, or late fusion (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020). Recent efforts also investigate treating the language model itself as a soft knowledge base to be queried and calibrated (Petroni et al., 2019; Kassner and Schütze, 2020). However, implicit knowledge alone is insufficient for verifiable grounding in information-seeking dialogues (Roller et al., 2021; Shuster et al., 2021). Document-grounded systems thus combine retrieval, knowledge selection, and response generation in a unified pipeline (Zhao et al., 2020a; Zheng et al., 2021). Multi-source models further incorporate commonsense graphs to complement factual passages with inferential cues (Zhang et al., 2019; Bosselut et al., 2019). Effective training objectives align the chosen evidence with both the interlocutor’s intent and the final utterance via contrastive or margin-based losses (Humeau et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020). In the low-resource regime, data augmentation and weak supervision have been used to reduce annotation cost and improve robustness (Zhao et al., 2020b; Parthasarathi and Pineau, 2018). End-to-end memory networks and differentiable memory controllers remain attractive for tracking entities and dialogue state across turns (Sukhbaatar et al., 2015; Madotto et al., 2018). Beyond textual evidence, integrating knowledge graphs enables multi-hop reasoning over entities and relations in a controllable manner (Zhou et al., 2018b; Moon et al., 2019). Attention mechanisms over graph neighborhoods can be layered with evidence sentence selectors to guide generation toward verifiable statements (Koncel-Kedziorski et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). Despite these advances, models remain sensitive to retrieval noise and often overfit to shortcut correlations in benchmarks (Goyal et al., 2017; Akula et al., 2020. Improved negative sampling and debiased evaluation protocols are therefore critical for meaningful progress in grounded dialogue (Sap et al., 2019; Dinan et al., 2019). Recent studies compare extract-then-generate pipelines with jointly trained architectures under controlled ablations to quantify evidence utilization (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Beyond offline metrics, human evaluations target factuality, specificity, and engagement to capture conversational quality more holistically (See et al., 2019; Deriu et al., 2021). Robustness to paraphrases and document updates is another frontier, motivating continual retrieval and editable memory components (De Cao et al., 2021; Lazaridou et al., 2022). Finally, safety considerations require grounding not only for informativeness but also for preventing harmful or misleading content (Bender et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2022). In this work, we introduce CNTF, a multi-source knowledge-grounded model that unifies named entity–aware commonsense with domain-specific factual graphs to drive response planning. CNTF employs hierarchical retrieval, adaptive evidence reweighting, and multi-hop attention to maintain consistency across evolving topics and entities in long dialogues. Experiments on Wizard of Wikipedia and CMU_DoG demonstrate consistent gains in grounding precision and response factuality over strong retrieval-augmented baselines (Dinan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Ablations reveal that entity linking, graph-conditioned cross-attention, and targeted negative sampling contribute most to the observed improvements (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020).

document: Generalized quantifiers (GQs) are developed upon first-order predicate logic, denoting relations between sets (Mostowski, 1957). Given a universe E, a quantifier Q is treated as a mapping Q_E from the Cartesian product of powersets P(E) × P(E) to the set {false, true}, or as a binary relation on subsets of E (Dvořák and Holčapek, 2015). GQs are generalizations of the ∀ and ∃ quantifiers from first-order predicate logic (Mostowski, 1957; Lindström, 1966; Montague, 1973; Bach et al., 1995; 2012; Keenan and Paperno, 2012). A generalized quantifier is, abstractly, a relation between sets. Generalized quantifier theory, while developed by logicians, is used by formal linguists to analyze the meaning of quantifier words in combination with referential expressions (Barwise and Cooper, 1981; Higginbotham and May, 1981). Most human languages contain ways of expressing generalized quantifiers, and their semantics exhibit striking similarities across languages (Matthewson, 2004; von Fintel and Matthewson, 2008; Steinert-Threlkeld, 2019). At the same time, generalized quantifiers can be instantiated very differently across languages due to pragmatic considerations (Grice, 1989) or cognitive economy and cost-benefit optimisation in the exchange of information (Sperber and Wilson, 1986; Levinson et al., 2000). Quantifier words also exhibit syntactic differences, with some languages having specialized determiners to express quantity, while others rely on metaphorical uses of common nouns (Katsos et al., 2012). In English, most is a determiner, but Spanish and French express the same concept through common nouns such as la mayoría and la majorité. The relative stability of the core semantics of quantifiers enables cross-linguistic comparison, yet the syntactic and pragmatic variation associated with their expression poses challenges for multilingual NLU. We consult quantifier taxonomy studies (Keenan and Westerståhl, 1997; Peters and Westerståhl, 2006; Szymanik and Thorne, 2015; Szymanik, 2016) and derive a categorization set for quantifier analysis in NLU benchmarks. In Table 1, we list an 11-way quantifier categorization and provide logical denotations based on set theory. Prior work in NLP has examined other formal semantic phenomena, including coreference (Ogrodniczuk et al., 2019; Ogrodniczuk et al., 2020), negation (Hossain et al., 2020; Hartmann et al., 2021), and logical consistency (Li et al., 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2019; Asai and Hajishirzi, 2020). By contrast, there has been relatively little work on generalized quantifiers as a systematic source of error in NLU, especially in multilingual settings. It remains an open problem whether language models represent the semantics of quantifier words adequately or provide a basis for resolving scopal ambiguities. Building on formal semantics, we target properties such as monotonicity, conservativity, and proportionality that have direct behavioral signatures in inference tasks (Peters and Westerståhl, 2006; Szymanik, 2016). Empirical studies in psycholinguistics suggest that quantifiers differ in processing difficulty and bias, which can inform computational modeling (Katsos et al., 2012; Szymanik and Thorne, 2015). In computational semantics, early work modeled generalized quantifiers in semantic parsing for database querying and question answering (Zettlemoyer and Collins, 2005; Liang et al., 2011). More recently, neural NLI systems have been probed for sensitivity to monotonicity and quantifier scope using controlled evaluation sets (Yanaka et al., 2018; Geiger et al., 2020). However, these evaluations often isolate English and do not consistently align semantics with language-specific morphosyntax (Cotterell et al., 2018; Ponti et al., 2019). Within typology, quantifier expressions vary in whether they are realized as determiners, pronouns, or partitive constructions, which complicates cross-lingual transfer (Haspelmath, 2010; Dryer and Haspelmath, 2013). Following Keenan and Westerståhl (1997; Peters and Westerståhl, 2006), we adopt a taxonomy that distinguishes conservative determiners from non-conservative and numerical quantifiers for downstream probing. We instantiate this taxonomy in a multilingual resource that maps surface forms to abstract denotations and scope behavior. To enable controlled testing, we build minimal pairs that vary only the quantifier while keeping predicates and plausibility constant. We evaluate encoder-only and encoder-decoder language models on entailment and masked language modeling probes designed around quantifier reasoning. Automatic metrics are complemented with targeted diagnostics that separate lexical confounds from structural generalization. Our results indicate substantial performance drops when monotone inferences require downward-entailing contexts, even for strong pretrained models (Raffel et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2019). Cross-linguistic analyses reveal that models trained primarily on English corpora struggle with partitive and proportional quantifiers in Romance languages. Error analysis further shows that heuristics based on lexical overlap often override the truth-conditional contribution of quantifiers in NLI. These findings have practical implications for safety-critical applications where quantifier misinterpretation can flip entailment judgments or risk assessments. We conclude by outlining limitations of our study and proposing future work on data augmentation, multilingual alignment, and neuro-symbolic modeling of scope.

document: Model uncertainty estimation and posterior calibration continue to attract interest across machine learning, statistics, and applied domains such as meteorology (Bröcker, 2009), fairness (Liu et al., 2019), healthcare (Jiang et al., 2012), and economics (Gneiting et al., 2007). The central goal is to learn posteriors p(y|x) that assign low confidence to samples poorly explained by the training data. A common post-hoc approach treats multiclass prediction as K one-vs-all binaries and calibrates each marginal. In this setting, uncertainty is summarized from f(x) via classwise scores and normalized likelihoods. Binary calibration methods include parametric scaling such as Platt, temperature, and vector scaling (Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017) and nonparametric techniques like isotonic regression or Bayesian binning (Zadrozny and Elkan, 2001; Naeini et al., 2015; Zadrozny and Elkan, 2002). An alternative reduces calibration to estimating accuracy conditional on confidence, p(y=ŷ | max_k f_k(x)). True multiclass calibration instead aims to estimate p(y|f(x)), but its sample complexity grows exponentially with the number of classes. Regularized estimators such as matrix and Dirichlet scaling provide linear mappings for p(y=k|f(x)) (Guo et al., 2017; Kull et al., 2019), while MLP-based mappings preserve order constraints (Rahimi et al., 2020a,b). A complementary line models uncertainty with Bayesian neural networks, which exhibit higher predictive uncertainty in data-sparse regions (MacKay, 1992). Event-based and feature-based approximations restrict attention to informative subsets of outputs Kuleshov and Liang, 2015) and have been adapted to neural sequence models (Jagannatha and Yu, 2020). Because exact weight marginalization is intractable, variational objectives and stochastic approximations are commonly used (Graves, 2011; Blundell et al., 2015; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). These methods can curb overconfidence but often require training-time changes that limit adoption in modern NLP (Kristiadi et al., 2020). Structured prediction poses additional challenges because the label space is combinatorial and exact calibration of sequence-level posteriors is infeasible (Nguyen and O'Connor, 2015; Jung et al., 2020). Practical work therefore calibrates proxies tied to downstream decision criteria, for example using BLEU-linked estimators for sequence tasks (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). This paper synthesizes these directions and emphasizes scalable, post-hoc calibration procedures that preserve task performance while enabling reliable uncertainty quantification.

document: We begin by providing background on two span selection tasks considered in our work: coreference resolution and SRL. We describe some previous works on these tasks and contrast them to our work. Span selection underpins both linking mentions that refer to the same entity and identifying argument spans with respect to predicates. Coreference resolution seeks clusters of mentions that co-refer within and across sentences, historically approached with mention-pair and entity-mention models (Ng and Cardie, 2002;Soon et al., 2001). Neural end-to-end architectures treat all potential spans as candidates and learn scoring and antecedent selection jointly (Lee et al., 2017;Lee et al., 2018). Span-focused pretraining has further improved mention representations and long-range reasoning (Joshi et al., 2019;Xu and Choi, 2020). Despite progress, error analyses highlight over-merging of nominal mentions and brittleness under domain shift (Moosavi and Strube, 2016;Kummerfeld and Klein, 2013). Recent audits emphasize annotation artifacts and evaluation blind spots in coreference corpora (Kummerfeld et al., 2019, but systematic remedies remain limited. Span encoders with deep contextualization enable efficient scoring of tuples while mitigating feature engineering (Devlin et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2018). In semantic role labeling, the objective is to identify who did what to whom, where, and when, often defined over PropBank frames (Gildea and Jurafsky, 2002;Kingsbury and Palmer, 2002). Early neural models combined BiLSTMs with conditional random fields to capture local and structural constraints (He et al., 2017;Marcheggiani and Titov, 2017). Subsequent work incorporated syntax or attention to better model long spans and discontinuous arguments (Strubell et al., 2018;Ouchi et al., 2018). Pretrained transformers drastically improved SRL transfer and low-resource performance (Shi and Lin, 2019;Kondratyuk and Straka, 2019). Span-based SRL contrasts with dependency-based formulations, though both can be expressed with span scoring primitives (Hajič et al., 2009;Surdeanu et al., 2008). Recent multilingual evaluations show that span models benefit from subword sharing and data augmentation (Cai et al., 2018;Heinzerling and Strube, 2019). Across both tasks, the design of span representations, pruning strategies, and inference procedures is central to accuracy and efficiency (Lee et al., 2018;Kitaev et al., 2019). Data scarcity and annotation costs continue to motivate semi-supervised and weakly supervised approaches (Settles, 2009;Ratner et al., 2017). For coreference, silver labels derived from distant supervision and rules can bootstrap mention detection and clustering (Clark and Manning, 2016;Uryupina et al., 2019). For SRL, augmentation via paraphrasing and cross-task distillation has shown promising gains (Yasunaga et al., 2018;Zhang et al., 2021). Joint modeling of coreference and SRL can exploit shared span boundaries and discourse cues to improve both tasks (Peng et al., 2017;Fei et al., 2020). In setting our baselines, we follow the predicate-argument conventions and BIOUL labeling strategies popularized by Punyakanok et al. and adapt them to span-centric inference. We additionally compare to end-to-end coreference models that use coarse-to-fine pruning and antecedent scoring with bilinear attention (Lee et al., 2018;Xia et al., 2020). Our training objective balances cross-entropy over span boundaries with margin-based antecedent ranking to harmonize precision and recall (Peters et al., 2018;Wu et al., 2020). We evaluate on OntoNotes for both tasks and include in-domain and out-of-domain splits to assess robustness (Weischedel et al., 2013). Metrics include CoNLL F1 for coreference and span F1 for SRL, with calibration analyses for threshold-sensitive decisions (Pradhan et al., 2014;Reimers and Gurevych, 2017). We report ablations on pruning depth, negative sampling ratios, and encoder width to isolate contributions of span scoring components. Finally, we discuss computational trade-offs and memory footprints relevant to large-batch span processing on modern accelerators (Shaw et al., 2018;Kitaev et al., 2020). Our contributions are to unify span selection across coreference and SRL, to provide a rigorous empirical comparison, and to release reproducible code and scripts. The remainder of this paper details our modeling choices, datasets, and results before concluding with limitations and future directions.
