document:
Personalized Language Modeling. King and Cook (2020) examined methods for creating personalized language models. They compared interpolation, finetuning, and priming, but unlike our work, they did not leverage similarity across users to select auxiliary data. Inspired by Lynn et al. (2017), they also analyzed adaptation for users with shared demographic attributes and found that fully personalized models tend to outperform demographic adaptation. Shao et al. (2020) further explored interpolation for personalization with an emphasis on handling out-of-vocabulary tokens. Wu et al. (2020) learned user embeddings from Reddit posts by building on sentence embeddings produced by a BERT model. These embeddings supported downstream prediction of gender, depression, and MBTI type, suggesting that they encode intrinsic user attributes. Our approach instead learns user embeddings for similarity estimation and uses that similarity to select data for improved language modeling.

Learning from Limited Data. Antonello et al. (2020) trained a model to predict which examples would be most informative for fine-tuning under budget constraints. In our setting, similarity metrics guide the selection of indivisible author-level data to fine-tune personalized models.

The cold-start problem is well known in recommender systems. Prior work recommends items to new users with little or no history, often via matrix factorization (Zhou et al., 2011). Huang et al. (2016) framed language modeling as a cold-start problem in which no writing from a user is available but a social graph enables interpolation from connected users.

Language Models. We adopt a widely used LSTM language model that integrates strong regularization and optimization techniques such as averaged SGD, embedding dropout, and recurrent dropout (Merity et al., 2018b). Later variants can further reduce perplexity but require substantially more training time, which is impractical for our user-specific experiments (Gong et al., 2018). Extremely large transformer models have also shown impressive results (Radford et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019. Using such models as a basis could be fruitful, but fine-tuning in low-data regimes is difficult and highly variable (Dodge et al., 2020). Controlled text generation with transformers has been demonstrated by conditioning on metadata such as domain, date, authors, and headline (Zellers et al., 2019). While an ablation isolating author conditioning would be informative, we prioritize models that are faster and cheaper to train, since large systems can require weeks of computation and substantial cost. In parallel, contextualized embedding models typically need considerable data to train effectively, though exploring this comparison is an interesting direction. Variants of LSTMs continue to achieve strong results without massive compute budgets, motivating our choice of architecture (Merity et al., 2018a; Melis et al., 2019; Merity, 2019; Li et al., 2020).

Authorship attribution serves as one of our similarity tasks, with early methods based on lexical features and n-grams and recent work using neural models to compare users (Koppel et al., 2009; Stamatatos, 2009; Ge et al., 2016).

document: Our approach is closely related to prior work on cross-lingual NER, knowledge distillation, and siamese networks. Cross-lingual NER targets entity extraction in a language without labels while relying on supervision from a different source language. Methods are commonly grouped into shared feature space models, translation-based models, and knowledge-distillation frameworks. Shared feature space models learn language-agnostic encoders using multilingual corpora and have been strengthened by mBERT (Tsai et al., 2016; Keung et al., 2019, Devlin et 2019. Additional transfer modules on top of multilingual encoders further improve adaptation to target languages (Keung et al., 2019). Despite these advances, performance remains limited by sparse target-language annotations. Translation-based approaches synthesize pseudo-labeled target data by projecting annotations across languages (Wu et al., 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021). Our method avoids translation and instead exploits entity similarity directly in target-language text. Knowledge distillation trains a student to mimic a source-trained teacher on unlabeled target data, capturing useful cross-lingual cues (Wu et al., 2020c). Beyond imitation, we integrate a siamese objective to learn token-level similarity signals inspired by metric learning (Koch et al., 2015; Neculoiu et al., 2016). The overall design aligns with earlier cross-lingual NER studies (Tsai et al., 2016) while addressing token-level pairing challenges.

document: Efficient Pre-training for NLP. Pre-trained language models have advanced a wide range of NLP tasks through scale, data diversity, and prolonged optimization. Empirical studies show that larger models and longer training generally yield better performance (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). Such gains, however, come with substantial computational, energy, and monetary costs (Patterson et al., 2021). To mitigate these costs, researchers have explored mixed-precision arithmetic to reduce memory footprint and accelerate throughput (Shoeybi et al., 2019). Parallel and distributed training further improve scalability across devices and interconnects (Shoeybi et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019). Optimization techniques such as large-batch training and adaptive schedulers stabilize and speed up convergence (You et al., 2020). Architectural innovations, including efficient attention and sparsity, complement these systems advances (Lan et al., 2020; Beltagy et al., 2020). Another line of methods proposes progressive expansion strategies that grow networks during training (Gong et al., 2019). These approaches start from a compact model and gradually increase depth or width while reusing parameters, a process often termed parameter recycling. Parameter recycling offers appealing efficiency in early phases of training and can reduce the peak resource budget for exploration (Gong et al., 2019). Nonetheless, many recycling schemes impose architectural coupling between the initial and final models, limiting their applicability to heterogeneous settings. In scenarios requiring flexibility across backbones, knowledge integration becomes preferable to strict structural growth. We therefore consider knowledge distillation as a vehicle for knowledge integration without architecture constraints. Unlike pure recycling, distillation can aggregate signals from multiple sources, including models trained on different domains or modalities. It also naturally supports domain adaptation by aligning teacher and student representations on target corpora. We provide a detailed comparison between knowledge integration and parameter recycling in Appendix F. Beyond structural considerations, recent literature underscores the role of sequence-to-sequence pre-training in unifying tasks Raffel et al., 2019), but efficiency remains a first-order concern. Knowledge Distillation for PLMs. Knowledge distillation compresses the behavior of a strong teacher into a smaller or otherwise constrained student (Hinton et al., 2015). The paradigm has been adapted to the pre-training stage, where students learn from large teachers on unlabeled corpora (Sanh et al., 2019). Complementary work distills during task-specific fine-tuning to obtain efficient inference without major accuracy loss (Sun et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020). Some studies jointly apply distillation at both stages to maximize transfer and reduce catastrophic forgetting (Jiao et al., 2020). Beyond logits, researchers distill intermediate states such as hidden representations and attention maps to better align inductive biases (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). Conventional narratives assume teachers define an upper bound that students can only approximate, especially when the student has fewer parameters. However, this assumption weakens when the student is equal to or larger than the teacher, as in knowledge integration settings. In such cases, the objective shifts from compression to guided pre-training that shapes optimization trajectories. Outside NLP, evidence suggests that even weak or noisy teachers can confer useful inductive biases under proper regularization (Yuan et al., 2020). This observation motivates reversed distillation, where a stronger or larger student leverages a smaller teacher to accelerate learning dynamics. Reversed distillation can be interpreted as curriculum design over model outputs, exposing the student to smoothed targets early in training. It also offers a mechanism to incorporate specialized domain expertise captured by smaller, focused models. When combined with large-scale pre-training, such strategies may reduce compute while preserving downstream generalization. In this work, we systematically study reversed distillation for pre-training with modern PLMs under diverse data and capacity regimes. We evaluate configurations that vary teacher quality, student size, and alignment of pre-training objectives. Our experiments investigate when reversed distillation accelerates optimization, improves sample efficiency, or enhances transfer to downstream tasks. The findings illuminate design choices for efficient pre-training and articulate open questions for future research.

document: While research in Natural Language Processing initially prioritized narrow technical interventions to address the social impact of language technologies, a shift has emerged toward a more comprehensive understanding of bias (Shah et al., 2020; Blodgett et al., 2020). Along this line, attention has turned to analyzing bias in model internals and outputs (Vig et al., 2020; Costajussà et al., 2020b). Researchers also examine the validity of bias measurement practices (Blodgett et al., 2021; Antoniak and Mimno, 2021; Goldfarb-Tarrant et al., 2021). A complementary strand argues that, rather than seeking broad generalizations, gender bias detection should incorporate contextual and linguistic specificity (González et al., 2020; Ciora et al., 2021; Matthews et al., 2021; Malik et al., 2021; Kurpicz-Briki and Leoni, 2021). This perspective has received limited attention due to the heavy focus on English NLP. In languages with grammatical gender, agreement operates across morphology and syntax, with gender marked on multiple parts of speech and features that must align across related tokens. General methodological prescriptions risk overreach and can impede robust conclusions, as seen in monolingual studies on grammatical gender languages (Zhou et al., 2019; Gonen et al., 2019) and in machine translation scenarios (Vanmassenhove et al., 2018; Moryossef et al., 2019). In (Stanovsky et al., 2019) WinoMT remains a standard benchmark for English-to-gendered-language evaluation. It has been extended to cover additional features and adapted for speech translation (Costa-jussà et al., 2020a). Yet, because it concatenates corpora built for English monolingual tasks—WinoGender and WinoBias—it underrepresents language-specific phenomena (Zhao et al., 2018; Rudinger 2018). Subsequent work augments WinoBias with strongly gender-stereotyped verbs and adjectives to increase variability (Troles and Schmid, 2021). However, these evaluations typically focus on translating profession terms while overlooking other parts of speech that bear gender inflection. Natural bilingual and multilingual benchmarks instead curate phenomena tailored to each language to better probe feminine under-representation (Alhafni et al., 2021). Because variability is not mapped to fine-grained linguistic features, such corpora make it difficult to isolate which instances drive bias. Prior attempts to link agreement and bias show that systems may mishandle gender triggers or produce inconsistent inflections across unrelated words in the same sentence (Renduchintala and Williams, 2021).

document: We work with eight corpora covering six tasks, summarized below and exemplified in Table 1. We select two corpora for question answering: CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019) and COPA (Roemmele et al., 2011). CommonsenseQA consists of multiple-choice questions with five candidate answers that require varying degrees of commonsense. COPA presents a premise and a question, and the system must choose between two plausible alternatives. For textual similarity and paraphrasing, we select QQP and STS-B (Cer et al., 2017). QQP consists of pairs of questions and the task is to determine whether they are paraphrases. STS-B consists of pairs of texts and the task is to determine how semantically similar they are on a scale from 0 to 5. For inference, we work with QNLI (Rajpurkar et al., 2016), which consists in determining whether a text is a valid answer to a question. We use WiC (Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados, 2019) for word sense disambiguation, where the goal is to decide whether a target word has the same meaning in two sentences. For coreference resolution, we choose WSC (Levesque et al., 2012), which tests whether a pronoun and a noun phrase are co-referential. Finally, we work with SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013) for sentiment analysis, where the task is to determine whether a sentence has positive or negative sentiment. For convenience, we rely on the formatted versions released with GLUE and SuperGLUE; see Wang, Singh, and Michael (2018 for details. Related work has shown that widely used NLI datasets such as SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015) and MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) contain annotation artifacts that models can exploit (Gururangan et al., 2018). Simple adversarial manipulations involving negation cues also degrade performance (Naik et al., 2018; Wallace et al., 2019), and transformer models show limitations with negation in language modeling and inference (Ettinger, 2020; Geiger et al., 2020; Yanaka et al., 2019). Most related to our work, Hossain et al. (2020) analyze the role of negation in RTE (Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampiccolo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009), SNLI, and MNLI, and we extend this analysis across six tasks.

document: Large pre-trained Seq2Seq Transformer models have transformed neural text generation across summarization, translation, and data-to-text. They are typically trained with denoising or reconstruction objectives that scale well with data. T5 treats every NLP problem as text-to-text and leverages span denoising to learn robust representations (Raffel et al., 2020). BART combines bidirectional and autoregressive training through denoising autoencoding to yield strong generative performance (Lewis et al., 2020). PEGASUS optimizes a gap-sentence objective tailored to extractive-like summarization signals (Zhang et al., 2020). Despite their accuracy, these models are computationally intensive at training and inference time. Model compression methods aim to mitigate these costs without severely degrading quality. Knowledge distillation transfers behavior from a large teacher to a compact student using soft targets or intermediate representations (Hinton et al., 2015;Romero et al., 2014). Early work showed that students trained on teacher logits can match or surpass baselines in classification tasks (Ba and Caruana, 2014). Later studies extended distillation to attention maps, hidden states, and gradients to better shape student learning (Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017;Czarnecki et al., 2017). Distillation for pre-trained Transformers has produced efficient encoders for natural language understanding, including DistilBERT, TinyBERT, MobileBERT, and MiniLM Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020) and subsequent variants (Sun et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020). However, compression of Seq2Seq generators presents unique challenges because sequence-level behavior matters. A key idea is sequence-level distillation, which replaces gold outputs with teacher-generated hypotheses to align the student with the teacher's decoding distribution (Kim and Rush, 2016). This approach consistently improves training stability and decoding efficiency in neural machine translation (Gu et al., 2017;Denkowski and Neubig, 2017;Kasai et al., 2020). Recent work on summarization adopted similar pseudo-labeling strategies and compared them to direct KD on token distributions (Shleifer and Rush, 2020). Nonetheless, open questions remain about how to combine token-level and sequence-level signals effectively. Shrink-and-fine-tune methods prune layers and then re-optimize the teacher to recover lost capacity (Shleifer and Rush, 2020). Other lines of work study progressive module replacement to gradually morph a teacher into a student while preserving outputs (Xu et al., 2020). Self-training and self-distillation explore using the model's own predictions to improve generalization without changing capacity (Furlanello et al., 2018;Xie et al., 2020). For generative models, these techniques must address exposure bias and search errors that propagate into pseudo labels. Beam-search generated targets can be biased toward brevity and high-probability modes, which may underrepresent diversity. Temperature scaling and sequence reweighting have been proposed to smooth targets and emphasize informative examples. Another promising direction is to distill from multiple teachers trained with heterogeneous objectives to improve robustness. Research on task-specific compression indicates that students benefit from aligning with evaluation losses, such as ROUGE-aware objectives for summarization. While much prior work targets encoders, there is a growing need to design distillation protocols that respect autoregressive dependencies. Cross-attention supervision can guide students to attend to salient source tokens that teachers rely on. Consistency regularization across augmentations offers a complementary signal in low-resource regimes Sanh et al., 2019). Beyond accuracy, latency and memory footprint are critical metrics for deployment on edge devices. Quantization and pruning can be combined with distillation to yield additional speedups without drastic quality loss. In multilingual settings, distilled students can inherit cross-lingual generalization from a shared teacher. Nevertheless, rigorous evaluation across domains and input lengths is necessary to avoid overstating gains. Our study investigates how to structure pseudo-labeling to better preserve teacher coverage while reducing search cost. We compare against strong baselines and analyze errors by source length, extractiveness, and novelty. Results demonstrate that a carefully tuned sequence-level distillation pipeline achieves favorable trade-offs in accuracy and efficiency. We release code and scripts to facilitate reproducible research on generative model compression.

document: Word Sense Disambiguation was first formulated as a computational task by Weaver (1949) in the context of Machine Translation. The two fields then followed parallel paths, with periodic attempts to reconnect them (Carpuat and Wu, 2005; Vickrey et al., 2005; Carpuat and Wu, 2007). Carpuat and Wu (2005) reported negative results when integrating a supervised WSD predictor into a Statistical Machine Translation model. Two years later the same authors improved a phrase-based SMT system by leveraging a new phrase-based WSD model (Carpuat and Wu, 2007). More recently, Pu et al. (2018) and Nguyen et al. (2018) showed that sense information can benefit NMT, but they required pre-disambiguation of ambiguous words, which limits practicality. Contextualized embeddings have also been used to mine sense-specific target sentences for back-translation, improving coverage of infrequent senses (Hangya et al., 2021). Despite these advances, lexical ambiguity remains challenging, with models often mistranslating rare senses and relying on spurious word correlations (Emelin et al., 2020; Raganato et al., 2019; Gonzales et al., 2017). ContraWSD introduced an adversarial evaluation where sentences with an ambiguous source word are paired with correct and incorrect translations (Gonzales et al., 2017). The metric checks whether the system assigns higher probability to the correct translation than to distractors, but early sets covered only two language pairs and are now too easy Emelin et al., 2019). MuCoW advanced the idea by using BabelNet and sense embeddings to build larger, harder test suites across five language pairs, though full automation introduced noise (Raganato et al., 2019; Navigli and Ponzetto, 2012; Camacho-Collados et al., 2016; Mancini et al., 2017). Emelin et al. (2020) further proposed two English→German challenge sets targeting frequency effects and adversarial sense flips with manually refined clusters. Nevertheless, only a handful of mitigation approaches have been explored, and few are effective with modern Transformer-based architectures (Gonzales et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018). Parallel corpora have been exploited for sense annotation, but prior efforts used outdated disambiguation methods that recent neural architectures surpass (Bonansinga and Bond, 2016; Delli Bovi et al., 2017). Recent WSD research now reaches inter-annotator upper bounds with strong supervised models and sense embeddings (Conia and Navigli, 2021; Blevins and Zettlemoyer, 2020; Loureiro and Jorge, 2019; Scarlini et al., 2020a,b; Wang et al., 2020). Building on this progress, we create high-quality sense-annotated parallel data and use it to regularize NMT, reducing disambiguation bias and improving translation quality.

document: The automatic processing of referring expressions has been studied for a long time (Winograd, 1972). Early accounts adopted logical forms in which expressions were either intrinsic or relational, corresponding to unary and binary predicates (Dale and Haddock, 1991. Subsequent corpus studies showed that human speakers frequently rely on relational descriptions, even in simple scenes (Viethen and Dale, 2006). Referring expressions are linguistically rich and vary with task demands and context. Overspecification can aid identification when redundant attributes are easy to recognize (Paraboni et al., 2017). Vagueness and gradable adjectives further make appropriateness strongly context dependent (Quirk et al., 1980). Syntactic ambiguities in naturally produced REs have also been documented (Chantree et al., 2005; Khan et al., 2008). Psycholinguistic work has emphasized the collaborative nature of reference and the role of common ground (Clark and Wilkes-Gibbs, 1986; Clark, 1996). Classic theories model how interlocutors introduce, locate, and confirm referents across shared domains (Hawkins, 1978). In visually grounded settings, such collaborative mechanisms underlie many dialog behaviors (Dale and Reiter, 1995). As shown in (Cirik et al., 2018), recent REC research stresses the importance of dataset probing and careful ablations to reveal spurious shortcuts. Building on these insights, we perform disaggregated evaluation and detailed error analysis rather than relying solely on aggregate metrics. The introduction of the transformer architecture enabled new ways to fuse vision and language (Vaswani et al., 2017; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Lu et al., 2019). Pretraining objectives range from masked token modeling and image–text matching to alignment with regions and scene graphs (Sun et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020a; Li et al., 2020b; Guo et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Although large-scale pretraining benefits grounding, it often prioritizes local or global matching and overlooks compositionality and indirection (Kamath et al., 2021; Li and Sigal, 2021). We therefore explore transformer-based models that directly regress bounding boxes for referred objects (Deng et al., 2021; Du et al., 2021), starting from a design akin to Deng et al. (2021).

document: Dense retriever pre-training has emerged as a key strategy for improving open-domain question answering. Prior work explores weakly supervised objectives that approximate relevance at scale. Early studies in web search exploited logs and heuristic signals to distill ranking cues. For example, Borisov et al. (2016) trained on click logs to capture user preference structures. Likewise, Dehghani et al. (2017) leveraged BM25-induced labels to guide representation learning. These approaches foreshadowed modern dense retrieval by emphasizing implicit supervision. Beyond traditional rankers, unsupervised cloze-style pre-training proved influential. Lee et al. (2019) introduced the inverse cloze task, which masks a sentence and uses its context as a pseudo query. ICT encourages learning query-document alignment without manual annotations. Subsequent work adapted ICT to scale with large corpora and more expressive encoders. Parallel efforts proposed pre-training via structural links in encyclopedic text. Chang et al. (2020) formulated wiki link prediction to pair anchor contexts with destination pages. They also proposed body-first selection to prioritize salient segments during contrastive training. These tasks harvest supervision from Wikipedia without requiring click data. However, they may emphasize document-level relatedness rather than fine-grained passage relevance. As illustrated by common anchor patterns, anchors can be topical yet loosely tied to local evidence. This gap motivates objectives that capture both local and global contextual signals. Work on retrieval-augmented language modeling further strengthened the connection between retrieval and generation. Guu et al. (2020) demonstrated that pretraining retrieval within a generative framework benefits downstream QA. Their approach integrates candidate passage selection with language modeling signals. In (Guu et al., 2020) we also see the use of hyperlinks as distant supervision for selecting semantically compatible passages. Nevertheless, hyperlink matching alone does not guarantee answer-bearing granularity. More recent methods enrich supervision with paragraph boundaries, sentence markers, and discourse cues. These design choices aim to sharpen the retriever’s sensitivity to evidence spans. In the context of OpenQA, dense retrievers must trade off recall and precision under strict latency budgets. Effective pre-training should thus shape embeddings that separate distractors from minimally sufficient evidence. Contrastive losses with hard negatives are a common tool to enforce this separation. Mining strategies range from BM25 negatives to online in-batch adversaries. Pre-training curricula often interleave unsupervised and supervised signals to stabilize optimization. Multi-stage schedules can mitigate distribution shift between generic corpora and QA-specific data. Our framework builds on these insights by unifying hyperlink-induced and cloze-inspired objectives. We pair anchor contexts with target passages while injecting local sentence-level perturbations. The retriever is trained to prioritize passages that jointly satisfy topical and evidential constraints. This setup produces embeddings that transfer well to zero-shot and few-shot retrieval benchmarks. In the following sections, we detail the training pipeline and discuss empirical findings across diverse datasets.

Fine-grained document representations: Another similar line of work is modeling fine-grained document-document similarity at the level of words or latent topics. Examples include early work El-Arini and Guestrin (2011) presenting paper recommendation methods with unigram-level similarity between papers using authorship and citation links or using latent document topics (Gong et al., 2018; Yurochkin et al., 2019; Dieng et al., 2020).

Our approach represents documents via sentences, a common and intuitive structure for reason-ing about scientific document facets (Chan et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2020). Ginzburg et al. (2021) present a self-supervised model for contextual sentence representations in long documents similar to our ICT baseline (Lee et al., 2019).

Ad-hoc Search: A range of recent work in information retrieval presents multi-vector models intended to capture different aspects of candidate documents with score aggregation relying on summations, max, or attention functions (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Luan et al., 2021; Humeau et al., 2020), these however focusing on short-text queries seen in search or question answering (QA). Mitra et al. (2017) explore an approach to model termlevel fine-grained similarities with neural networks, while subsequent work models fine-grained matches for QA at the phrase level (Liu et al., 2018 with improved pooling mechanisms. Importantly, these methods rely on supervision from knowledge bases or QA datasets, limiting applicability to specific span definitions and areas with these resources, often not present in the scientific domain (Hope et al., 2021a).

Beyond retrieval, sentence-level representations have been leveraged for summarization and discourse analysis, suggesting that hierarchical encoders can capture salient facets of long documents. However, extending these ideas to end-to-end scientific recommendation requires representations that are robust to sparse citations and evolving terminology. A range of modeling approaches in the context of other tasks resemble elements of our approach. We describe these in Appendix J.

document: To-do Management: Intelligent systems can assist users with task management in many ways (Gil et al., 2012). To-do tasks can be inferred automatically from emails (Mukherjee et al., 2020). Systems can detect types of to-do items and suggest relevant applications or resources to users (Landes and Di Eugenio, 2018;Gil et al., 2012;Shah and White, 2021). Once to-do tasks have been created, a system can help users manage the completion progress, e.g., by sending reminders (Graus et al., 2016). Complex tasks can be decomposed automatically into more manageable sub-steps (Nouri et al., 2020;Zhang et al., 2021). In all these use cases, a common step is to represent the input language as computational vector representations, but none of the existing studies has produced general-purpose representations of to-do tasks. Short-text Representations: Multiple NLP areas involve very short texts with some unique characteristics. Several methods have been developed for tweets (e.g., (Nguyen et al., 2020)). Tweets pose the added challenge of containing many non-standard colloquial expressions and contain non-language text like URLs. Still, Wang et al. (2020) present a similar finding to ours: massively pre-trained encoders do not always perform well. Search queries are also short, with an average of three terms (Taghavi et al., 2012). Unlike to-dos, information such as click logs (Zhang et al., 2019) can be used as an indicator of user intent. Another key difference is that search queries are written with the goal of having a machine interpret them. Multi-task Learning: Multi-task learning improves the performance of pre-trained language models in various NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019a;Shuster et al., 2020;Aghajanyan et al., 2021). The common perception in the research community is that auxiliary training tasks are effective when they are similar to the target domain/task Shui et al., 2019). However, there are few relevant tasks and datasets for the to-do domain. Our study is the first work to propose a time-and cost-efficient way to harvest weak-supervision for MTL in that domain. Prior work on intelligent assistants has emphasized intent detection, slot filling, and dialogue management. To-do creation intersects with these areas but differs because tasks are often stand-alone utterances with limited context. The lack of context makes entity linking and temporal grounding particularly challenging. Several studies suggest that task understanding benefits from modeling user routines and temporal patterns. However, privacy constraints frequently limit the availability of longitudinal behavioral data. Consequently, methods that generalize from minimal context are especially desirable. Representation learning for to-do items has often borrowed techniques from sentence encoders and intent classifiers. Yet many encoders are tuned on news or web text and fail to capture imperative mood and telegraphic style common to to-dos. Domain adaptation methods, such as continued pretraining on in-domain corpora, can help but may require large proprietary datasets. Few-shot approaches offer an appealing compromise by leveraging task descriptions and label semantics. In our setting, we consider labels that reflect actionable categories rather than topical themes. This distinction encourages representations that emphasize verbs, temporal cues, and resource requirements. We further hypothesize that compositional meaning is crucial for mapping short directives to downstream tools. From a systems perspective, robust representations enable cross-application routing, deduplication, and deadline prediction. Evaluating such representations requires both intrinsic metrics and task-driven outcomes. Intrinsic evaluations can include clustering purity and semantic similarity on curated pairs. Task-driven evaluations can measure improvements in reminder scheduling, application handoff, and user satisfaction. Because annotated data are scarce, we explore weak supervision derived from templates, paraphrases, and heuristic labeling rules. Multi-task objectives then integrate these weak labels with small amounts of gold annotations to stabilize training.

document: Privacy has emerged as a central concern in machine learning as models increasingly learn from sensitive data. In this section, we provide an overview of conceptualizations of privacy, training algorithms with formal guarantees, and implications for large language models. We first outline the foundational definitions that motivate formal privacy accounting in learning systems. Differential privacy offers a mathematically rigorous framework that bounds the influence of any single example on the algorithm’s output (Dwork et al., 2006). In practice, most modern private training uses randomized mechanisms to inject calibrated noise while tracking composition of privacy loss (Abadi et al., 2016). Extensions to account for tighter composition and group effects have improved the practical utility of private models without violating formal guarantees (Mironov, 2017; Balle et al., 2020). Beyond formalism, practitioners must reason about threat models, attack surfaces, and the interplay between privacy and utility in deployment contexts. Empirical studies have documented membership inference and extraction risks in overparameterized models trained on sensitive corpora (Shokri et al., 2017; Carlini et al., 2021). These risks motivate algorithmic defenses that reduce memorization and increase robustness to adaptive adversaries. Private stochastic gradient descent remains the dominant approach for deep networks because it scales and integrates with standard optimizers (Abadi et al., 2016). Advances in privacy accounting, such as Rényi DP and privacy profiles, enable tighter bounds for complex training schedules (Mironov, 2017; Dong et al., 2019). Mechanism design for clipping, noise allocation, and per-layer budgeting has further narrowed the accuracy gap to non-private baselines (Bu et al., 2020; De et al., 2022). Complementary to algorithmic techniques, data curation strategies like filtering, deduplication, and synthetic augmentation can substantially mitigate leakage (Lee et al., 2022; Kandpal et al., 2022). Large language models introduce new challenges due to scale, long-tailed data distributions, and emergent capabilities that exacerbate memorization. Researchers have proposed private pretraining, private finetuning, and hybrid approaches that confine privacy budgets to high-risk stages of the pipeline (Yu et al., 2021; De et al., 2022). However, utility trade-offs remain significant for generative tasks that rely on rare linguistic patterns and long-range dependencies. Auditing techniques, including canary insertion and probabilistic extraction tests, provide partial visibility into memorization behaviors of generative models (Carlini et al., 2019; Meehan et al., 2022). Policy and governance frameworks emphasize transparency in data provenance, consent management, and accountable reporting of privacy parameters alongside performance metrics (NIST, 2023; Veale et al., 2018). Building on Vatswani et al., 2019, we structure our review around three axes: definitions, algorithms, and evaluations. Along the definitions axis, we discuss connections between stability, generalization, and privacy guarantees for empirical risk minimization (Bassily et al., 2014; Dwork et al., 2015). Along the algorithmic axis, we survey optimization advances, adaptive clipping heuristics, and privacy amplification by subsampling (Wang et al., 2019; Thakkar et al., 2022). Along the evaluation axis, we examine black-box versus white-box audits, heterogeneous user harms, and domain-specific constraints in health, finance, and education (Kaissis et al., 2020; Jagielski et al., 2023). We conclude that rigorous privacy requires end-to-end design choices spanning data collection, modeling, and evaluation rather than a single post hoc mechanism. The remainder of the paper details practical guidelines for selecting privacy budgets, auditing deployments, and communicating risks to stakeholders.

document: Prompt-based Language Models. Prompt-based learning leverages PLMs by prepending task-specific instructions to inputs for conditioning behavior. Early work mainly utilized hand-crafted prompts to adapt to various generation tasks (Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020; Zou et al., 2021). However, manually designed prompts are inflexible and hard to generalize across tasks. Consequently, recent studies have focused on automating the discovery of discrete prompts (Shin et al., 2020; Gao et al., 20). Yet optimization in discrete space can be brittle and sub-optimal. To address this, many works optimize continuous prompts (Liu et al., 2021c; Li and Liang, 2021), which better adapt to heterogeneous objectives. Among these, prefix-tuning prepends trainable vectors to the input for generation tasks (Li and Liang, 2021). By contrast, we study soft-prompt transfer to show that related generation tasks can help each other via prompt reuse. Transferability of Natural Language Processing. Prior studies show that cross-task transfer mitigates data scarcity and supports compositional reasoning while improving representations (Jeong et al., 2020; Wiese et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019).

document:
Semantic dependency parsing is the task of producing a dependency graph for a sentence, each dependency generally representing a predicate-argument relation. If one views each dependency as a decision to make, both plain dependency parsing (DP) and semantic dependency parsing (SDP) are known to exhibit high interdependence of decisions. For instance in DP, when parsing a question answering machine, choosing machine as root is linguistically coherent with the machine → answering → question analysis only, whereas (wrongly) choosing question as root is syntactically coherent with the question → answering → machine analysis.

Yet, in DP the interdependence between arc decisions is partially solved by the tree constraint: choosing one head for a given token amounts to ruling out all other heads. This structural interdependence is absent in SDP. Complex structural, lexical and semantic factors control whether a given dependent should be attached to zero, one or several heads.

Several approaches exist in the literature to capture interdependence of arcs in SDP, which often derive from proposals made for DP. One is to use a higher-order graph-based parser. Wang et al. (2019) achieve state-of-the-art results (without pretrained LM) by using second-order factors to score the graphs, yet at the cost of an O(n 3 ) complexity.

Another main approach is to use sequential decisions, and hence take advantage of previous decisions by encoding the partial output structure. This is the case in the transition-based parser of Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez (2020), or in the system of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), which iteratively selects heads for all words, using reinforcement learning to order the selection of heads. Both models have an O(n 2 ) complexity (when used without cycle detection), and in both cases, sequential decisions benefit from previous decision encoding, yet at the cost of error propagation. For that reason, Bernard (2021) proposes a system close to that of Kurita and Søgaard (2019), yet allowing the system to overwrite previous decisions and hopefully correct itself.

On the contrary, the biaffine system of Dozat and Manning (2018) performs a simultaneous scoring of all candidate arcs, and the decision for each candidate arc is made independently of the other ones. This results in a highly parallelizable O(n 2 ) inference, with surprisingly high performance albeit below second-order parsing.

As for most NLP tasks, SDP performance increases when integrating transformer-based contextual representations when encoding input tokens. On the English dataset from the SemEval 2015 Task 18 (Oepen et al., 2015), (Fernández-González and Gómez-Rodríguez, 2020) report a +0.7 and +2.0 increase for ID and OOD respectively. Using the biaffine architecture of (Dozat and Manning, 2018), He and Choi (2020) report a +2 and +3 point increase in ID and OOD.

In this work, we retain the simple O(n 2 ) biaffine architecture of Dozat and Manning (2018) (hereafter DM18), and we investigate how simple auxiliary tasks can introduce some interdependence between arc decisions, in a multi-task learning setting (Caruana, 1997). We show modest but systematic improvements on the widely used Semeval2015-task18 English data (Oepen et al., 2015; Dozat). We also test another appealing property of the biaffine architecture, which is the absence of formal constraints on the output graphs. Experiments on French deep syntactic graphs, which are highly cyclic, also demonstrate the effectiveness of our auxiliary tasks for SDP.

We compare our auxiliary-task variants with recent structured decoders and show that gains persist across both ID and OOD splits. Ablations indicate that learning to predict local edge neighborhoods promotes more consistent head selection without harming labeled F1. We further analyze calibration of arc probabilities and observe reduced overconfidence relative to the baseline DM18 model. Finally, we discuss computational trade-offs, noting that our multitask objectives add negligible parameters and do not change the O(n 2 ) inference profile. In related work, semi-supervised objectives such as consistency regularization have been explored for SDP with limited annotations (Sun et al., 2021), but they target data scarcity rather than structural interdependence. Our approach is complementary to recent prompting strategies that wrap token classification inside generative models while retaining graph decoding for evaluation (Wei et al., 2022). Taken together, these observations suggest that injecting light forms of global context into local scorers is a practical path toward stronger and faster semantic parsers.

document: While much of this paper discusses the state of the NLP literature, we emphasize several starting points for further reading. Early critiques connect performance claims to socio-technical risk and accountability. Bender and Koller (2020) argue that ungrounded system goals invite misleading abstractions. Raji et al. (2021) document audit failures arising from opaque development pipelines. Together these works motivate a careful treatment of claims and evidence. Jin et al. (2021) survey safety concerns and propose pragmatic mitigation practices. Rogers (2021) synthesizes best practices for evidence-driven NLP scholarship. Both emphasize community norms that align research incentives with public benefit. A complementary thread examines overclaiming in robustness studies. Morris et al. (2020) caution that synonym-based attacks can exaggerate fragility. Hauser et al. (2021) show that benchmarked perturbations sometimes misrepresent human judgments. These findings complicate the interpretation of small accuracy deltas. Looking further ahead, foundation models raise systemic questions. Bommasani et al. (2021, §4.9) connect alignment debates to language-centric capabilities. They call for granular taxonomies of risk and evaluation targets. Work on evaluation design stresses validity, reliability, and construct coverage. Welty et al. (2019) review pitfalls in test construction for knowledge-centric tasks. Linzen (2020, 2021) discusses competence-oriented psycholinguistic probes. These studies converge on the need for transparent measurement models. Meanwhile, architectural advances have amplified both opportunity and risk. The arrival of the Transformer reshaped data, compute, and evaluation practices as first demonstrated by Vaswani 2017. As a canonical reference, Vaswani et al. (2017) formalize attention-centric sequence modeling. Subsequent scaling blurred the line between pretraining and task learning. Evaluation norms, however, lag behind model capabilities. Prior work advocates reporting uncertainty, effect sizes, and ablation discipline. Replication initiatives report inconsistent gains across random seeds and datasets. Dataset curation research emphasizes documentation and sampling transparency. Governance frameworks link documentation to external accountability regimes. Yet stylistic and citation conventions remain uneven across subcommunities. Divergent practices complicate meta-analysis and literature mapping. To anchor our contribution, we prioritize falsifiable questions and measurable claims. We adopt reporting checklists and preregistration where feasible. We integrate human-in-the-loop evaluation for consequential error types. We highlight known failure modes and dataset artifacts in our analysis. Finally, we position our results alongside established baselines and prior critiques. This framing aims to support cumulative progress while tempering hype.

document: The average treatment effect (ATE) is defined as τ = E[Y_i(1) − Y_i(0)], where Y_i(t) denotes the potential outcome for unit i under treatment t. In randomized experiments, identification is straightforward because treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes (Rubin, 1974). In observational studies, however, treatment may be confounded by covariates X_i that jointly influence exposure and outcomes. Under the assumption of conditional exchangeability and positivity, one can identify the ATE using propensity score methods (Rosenbaum and Rubin, 1983). A common estimator is inverse propensity weighting, which reweights observations by the inverse of their estimated treatment probability π(X_i) (Robins et al., 1994). Practical implementations often combine weighting with outcome regression to achieve double robustness (Bang and Robins, 2005). This strand of work emphasizes the bias–variance tradeoff induced by extreme weights and limited overlap (Kang and Schafer, 2007). To mitigate instability, researchers recommend stabilized weights, trimming, or covariate balancing scores (Robins et al., 2000; Imai and Ratkovic, 2014). Matching provides an alternative route that prioritizes design over modeling and can reduce extrapolation beyond common support (Stuart, 2010). Modern machine learning extends these ideas by using flexible nuisance estimators while preserving valid causal targets through orthogonalization (Chernozhukov et al., 2018). For heterogeneous effects, tree-based methods such as causal forests estimate conditional ATEs with asymptotic guarantees under honesty constraints (Wager and Athey, 2018). Comprehensive overviews synthesize identification assumptions, estimators, and practical diagnostics for applied researchers (Hernán and Robins, 2020; Imbens and Rubin 2015. IPW estimators are sensitive to model misspecification of the propensity score, motivating diagnostics such as standardized mean differences and overlap plots (Austin, 2011). Targeted maximum likelihood estimation unifies flexible learning with semiparametric efficiency through targeted updates of an initial outcome model (van der Laan and Rubin, 2006). Recent work develops covariate balancing propensity scores that directly optimize balance rather than likelihood (Imai and Ratkovic, 2014; Zubizarreta, 2015). Entropy balancing reframes weighting as moment matching with convex optimization to guarantee exact balance on specified moments (Hainmueller, 2012). When positivity violations are severe, researchers may redefine the target estimand to the overlap population to improve stability (Li et al., 2018). Sensitivity analysis remains essential because unmeasured confounding can vitiate identifying assumptions even when covariate balance is achieved (Rosenbaum, 2002). Instrumental variables offer an alternative route to identification under exclusion and monotonicity assumptions, but they target local effects rather than the ATE (Angrist et al., 1996). Mediation analysis extends the potential outcomes framework to decompose effects through intermediate variables under additional assumptions (Imai et al., 2010). Emerging practices integrate causal inference with domain adaptation to address dataset shift between training and deployment environments (Magliacane et al., 2018). In high-dimensional settings, sparsity-inducing methods and sample splitting help control overfitting in nuisance estimation while retaining valid inference (Belloni et al., 2014). Throughout, transparent reporting of identification, estimation, and diagnostics improves the credibility and reproducibility of empirical claims (Gorinova et al., 2020). This paper builds on these insights to compare weighting and doubly robust estimators under limited overlap and to provide practical guidance for applied users.

document:
Few studies have addressed bias in language models in French.
Irvine et al. (2013) have investigated semantic bias induced by domain in the context of domain adaptation for machine translation.
They present experiments for the French/English language pairs for a statistical phrase-based translation system trained on parliament transcripts and applied to other domains such as science and medicine.
Prior analyses have primarily focused on English benchmarks and communities, leaving significant gaps for Romance languages.
Consequently, model developers often extrapolate findings across languages without evidence of comparable sociolinguistic dynamics.
In a blog post, Daumé III (2016) describes the "black sheep" problem, evidencing that language use does not necessarily reflect reality and that the same notion may come across differently in different languages.
Beyond anecdotal observations, systematic studies have warned about dataset documentation and representativeness as sources of downstream bias (Bender and Friedman, 2018; Gebru et al., 2021).
In NLP, such concerns materialize in evaluations where lexical artifacts or demographic skews confound conclusions about fairness (Nangia et al., 2020; Sheng et al., 2021).
Kurpicz-Briki (2020) presents a study of cultural differences in origin and gender bias in pre-trained English, German and French Word Embeddings.
The author adapts the WEAT method (Caliskan et al., 2017; Zhao) that contains material for measuring bias in English language word embeddings to (Swiss) French and German and shows that the bias identified differ between the three languages studied.
This is probably the effort that is closest to the present study.
However, the WEAT method relies on word sets rather than full sentences as in CrowS-pairs and only two types of bias are considered in the French and German adaptations.
Other work has explored sentence-level probes that better approximate model behavior in context, including minimal pairs and controlled templates (Nangia et al., 2020; Maudslay et al., 2019).
However, sentence-level probes require careful curation to avoid leading cues and to maintain naturalness across languages.
More importantly, Goldfarb-Tarrant et al. (2021) show that the WEAT metrics, which was created to measure the biases in the embeddings themselves, does not correlate with results obtained using extrinsic evaluation of biases, using downstream applications.
This is a good motivation to develop evaluation corpora in as many languages as possible.
In the same paper, the authors also point out the need for cultural adaptation in addition to translation, because many elements of language, including people's names, have different implications in different languages.
For example, they report that the name Amy, which is arguably common in American English, has an association with upper class in Spanish therefore a translation keeping the name verbatim in Spanish would convey a nuance unintended in the original sentence.
We agree with this analysis and one of our goals was to address it in the translation of the CrowS-pairs dataset as illustrated in some of the examples in Table.
Zhao et al. (2020) study gender bias in a multilingual context.
They analyze multilingual embeddings and the impact of multilingual representations on transfer learning for NLP applications.
A word dataset in four languages (English, French, German, Spanish) is created for bias analysis.
Cross-lingual transfer further complicates evaluation because translation shifts can alter pragmatic implicatures even when lexical content is preserved (Artetxe et al., 2020).
These challenges are amplified for morphologically rich varieties of French where agreement and register interact with perceived social categories.
Blodgett et al. (2021) present a study of four benchmark datasets for evaluating bias, including CrowS-pairs.
The authors report a number of issues with the datasets that translate in limitations to assess language models for stereotyping.
Our work validated the limitations identified for CrowS-pairs and proposes revisions to the original and translated corpus in order to address them.
Their analysis emphasizes that aggregate scores can mask divergent subgroup behaviors, calling for stratified reporting.
We align with this perspective and adopt annotation and review protocols designed to mitigate ambiguous stimuli.

document: Event factuality prediction (EFP) aims to determine whether an event mentioned in text happened, is uncertain, or is counterfactual. Accurate factuality judgments support downstream tasks such as information extraction, question answering, and temporal reasoning. Early research approached EFP with handwritten rules grounded in modality and negation cues. For example, polarity shifters, modal verbs, and embeddings in factuality lexicons were composed deterministically to assign veridicality values (Nairn et al., 2006; Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2012). Subsequent work explored feature-rich classifiers that incorporate syntactic dependencies, lexical cues, and discourse markers (Lee et al., 2015; Stanovsky et al., 2017). With the rise of neural models, sequence labeling and sentence-level classification architectures achieved strong gains over feature-based baselines (Qian et al., 2018; Rudinger et al., 2018). However, many sentence-level systems ignore document context, which often carries decisive evidence about source belief and commitment. Recent studies therefore broadened the scope from sentences to documents to capture cross-sentence attribution and evidentiality (Kivlichan et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). A parallel line of research frames EFP as structured prediction over modal dependencies. In this view, events are linked to conceivers or sources through edges that encode commitment, polarity, and uncertainty (Yao et al., 2021). Modal dependency parsing unifies attribution and factuality inference by predicting a graph rather than isolated labels. Pipeline implementations typically first detect events and possible sources, and then select parent nodes by ranking candidates (Lee et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2021). Despite reasonable performance, such pipelines can propagate early errors and require carefully engineered candidate sets. End-to-end formulations attempt to alleviate these issues by collapsing detection and linking into a single modeling objective (Zhang and Ji, 2021). Orthogonally, prompt-based learning has emerged as an effective strategy for steering large language models toward low-resource tasks. Templated prompts transform factuality decisions into cloze-style completions or constrained generation (Liu et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Several works show that prompts can encode task instructions while leveraging pretraining priors for calibration (Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Prior art on modal dependency parsing often relies on candidate ranking and heuristic pruning (Yao et al., 2021. We depart from ranking by directly prompting a pretrained encoder-decoder to generate the conceiver for a target event. The model composes an input representation from the event mention, its local syntax, and a lightweight instruction describing the prediction target. This formulation reduces pipeline complexity and avoids brittle candidate generation. Moreover, it allows us to inject document-level cues through retrieved context windows and coreference chains (Nguyen et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Our approach is related to prompt-based extraction methods for events, relations, and coreference but differs in the structured graph objective (Liu et al., 2020; Paolini et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022). Unlike generation-only baselines, we couple prompting with constrained decoding to enforce type and role consistency. We also introduce a calibration term that balances prior likelihoods across positive, uncertain, and counterfactual outcomes (Holtzman et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). In addition, we exploit weak supervision from distant labels derived from hedge cues and reporting verbs to warm-start training (Kotonya and Toni, 2020; Karttunen, 2010). To evaluate document-level EFP, we consider corpora that annotate fine-grained commitment and source attributions. We adopt standard splits and metrics to ensure comparability with prior work (Saurí and Pustejovsky, 2012; Stanovsky et al., 2017). We further analyze model behavior under interventions that mask cues for negation, modality, and evidentiality. The ablation study reveals complementary gains from prompts, constraints, and contextual retrieval. Error analysis highlights frequent confusions between reported beliefs and narrator assertions in news narratives. We observe that cross-sentence attribution edges are especially sensitive to pronoun resolution and quotation detection. These findings suggest that improvements in discourse parsing and coreference resolution could further enhance factuality modeling (Prasad et al., 2008; Yu et al., 2020). Our study contributes a simple yet competitive alternative to ranking-based modal dependency parsers. By reframing EFP as prompt-guided structured generation, we bridge fine-grained source attribution with scalable pretrained models. We conclude by outlining directions for multilingual extensions and for integrating temporal reasoning into factuality parsing.

document: Knowledge-grounded conversation aims to produce responses supported by explicit external knowledge. Prior studies have explored integrating structured and unstructured sources to improve informativeness and factuality. Representative sources include knowledge graphs, which encode entity relations at scale (Zhou et al., 2018a). Unstructured documents supply topical passages that can be retrieved on demand (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2018b). Visual context has also been used to ground dialogue in shared scenes (Das et al., 2017). Together, these modalities have established knowledge grounding as a crucial ingredient of intelligent conversational systems. A common pipeline decomposes the problem into knowledge selection and utterance generation (Kim et al., 2020). Knowledge selection identifies a small set of candidate facts or passages from a large repository. The generation module then conditions on the selected items to produce fluent and relevant replies. Errors in selection often cascade to response generation, motivating targeted research on retrieval quality. Early neural systems used memory networks to store and query candidate snippets (Ghazvininejad et al., 2018). Latent variable models capture uncertainty over multiple plausible knowledge choices (Kim et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2020). Reinforcement learning has been applied to align selection with long-term conversational rewards (Zhao et al., 2020b). Dense retrieval with dual-encoders further improves recall over sparse term matching baselines (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Graph neural networks reason over paths in knowledge graphs to surface multi-hop evidence (Xiong et al., 2017). Cross-encoder rerankers refine initial candidates using fine-grained interaction features (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Despite these advances, personalization in knowledge selection remains underexplored. Users differ in background, preferences, and goals, which should shape what knowledge is considered relevant. Personal memory and long-term user profiles provide additional signals for tailored retrieval (Zhang et al., 2018). However, collecting labeled data that couples user attributes with gold knowledge choices is expensive. Dual learning offers a promising semi-supervised route for leveraging unlabeled interactions. Originally introduced for machine translation by He et al. (2016), it pairs forward and reverse tasks with mutual feedback. It was later extended to back-translation He et al., 2017) to exploit large monolingual corpora. The framework encourages consistency between dual objectives by optimizing cyclic reconstruction risks. Recent variants incorporate reinforcement signals and differentiable reward estimators (Hoang et al., 2018). In conversational settings, dual learning can connect knowledge selection with response generation through closed-loop training (Meng et al., 2020). Our approach adapts this principle to couple personal memory inference with individualized knowledge selection. We hypothesize that enforcing dual consistency yields gains in both personalization and overall task performance.

document: Text attribute transfer has a vast literature with deep learning methods becoming popular (Jin et al., 2020a). The problem typically involves rewriting a sentence to alter a salient attribute while preserving the underlying content. Approaches are commonly divided into supervised and unsupervised families. Supervised methods repurpose sequence to sequence models used in machine translation to achieve the goals (Rao and Tetreault, 2018). However, collecting parallel corpora for every attribute pair is costly and often impractical. As a result, unsupervised methods that rely on weak or pseudo parallel signals have become prominent. Disentanglement based architectures were among the first to dominate unsupervised attribute transfer. In these models, content and attributes are separated in a latent representation to enable targeted manipulation. A popular tactic is to train an encoder while adversarially discouraging attribute information in the shared content space (Fu et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018a; John et al., 2019). Yet the literature has paid comparatively little attention to defining and preserving content beyond surface heuristics. Cycle consistency encourages that a transformed sentence, when mapped back, reconstructs the source, thereby serving as a weak content proxy (Prabhumoye et al., 2018; Logeswaran et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2020; Yi et al., 2020). Despite its utility, cycle enforcement can be computationally heavy or require reinforcement learning to handle discrete text. Moreover, over reliance on reconstruction may inadvertently penalize legitimate paraphrases that improve fluency or coherence. Recent work has thus explored alternatives that directly model content similarity with learned objectives. Transformer based models, pretrained at scale, have accelerated this shift Dai et al., 2019; Krishna et al., 2020. However, many transformer systems still lack explicit mechanisms to preserve semantic relations that must remain invariant under style edits. For example, named entities, quantities, and event roles should persist unless the target attribute logically demands change. Several studies measure this persistence using content word overlap or entailment scores, but these proxies are imperfect (Logeswaran et al., 2018; Yi et al., 2020). Contrastive learning provides a principled alternative by pulling matched content pairs together while pushing mismatched pairs apart in representation space. When combined with attribute conditioning, such objectives can reduce leakage between style and content factors (Huang et al., 2020). Our framework formalizes content preservation as a set of constraints over entities, sentiment independent facts, and syntactic scaffolds. We then implement lightweight, differentiable losses that approximate these constraints without requiring full cycle reconstruction. Unlike prior disentanglement pipelines that depend on fragile adversarial games, our approach integrates smoothly with pretrained encoders (Subramanian et al., 2018). We also analyze failure modes where attribute control conflicts with content fidelity and propose calibration strategies for decoding. Empirically, we evaluate on sentiment, formality, and toxicity transfer using standardized splits and automatic metrics. Human evaluations further assess meaning preservation, grammaticality, and attribute strength to complement automatic scores. The results indicate consistent gains in content faithfulness with competitive style control across diverse domains. We conclude by outlining open questions on controllability, evaluation, and fairness that future work should address.

Interpretability There has been a lot of work on better interpreting models' decision process, e.g., understanding BERT (Clark et al., 2019b; Kovaleva et al., 2019) and attention in transformers (Hao et al., 2020), or through text generation models (Narang et al., 2020). In this paper we utilize the attention scores as a generic way to understand what features a model relies on for making its predictions. Other common model interpretation techniques (Sundararajan et al., 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2016), or more recent work on hierarchical attentions (Chen et al., 2020) and contrastive explanations (Jacovi et al., 2021), can be used as well. In Pruthi et al. (2020), the authors found that attention scores can be manipulated to deceive human decision makers. The reliability of existing interpretation methods is a research topic by itself, and extra care needs to be taken when using attention for auditing models on fairness and accountability (Aïvodji et al., 2019). Beyond attention, gradient- and perturbation-based techniques provide complementary lenses on feature reliance and can be triangulated for robustness. We therefore adopt a multi-pronged view of interpretability that treats explanation methods as measurement tools subject to calibration and bias.

Robustness and Bias An increasing body of work has been conducted on understanding robustness in deep neural networks, particularly, how models sometimes pay attention to spurious correlations (Tu et al., 2020; Sagawa et al., 2020) and take shortcuts (Geirhos et al., 2020), leading to vulnerability in generalization to out-of-distribution data or adversarial examples in various NLP tasks: NLI (McCoy et al., 2019), Question-Answering (Jia and Liang, 2017), and Neural Machine Translation (Niu et al., 2020). Different from most existing work that defines types of spurious correlations or shortcut patterns beforehand (Ribeiro et al., 2020; McCoy et al., 2019; Jia and Liang, 2017), which is often limited and requires expert knowledge, in this work we focus on automatically identifying models' unrobust regions at scale. Another line of work aims at identifying shortcuts in models (Wang and Culotta, 2020a) by training classifiers to better distinguish "spurious" correlations from "genuine" ones from human annotated examples. In contrast, we propose a cross-dataset approach and a knowledge-aware perturbation approach to automate this identification process with less human intervention in-between. Prior studies have emphasized the role of dataset artifacts in inflating headline results (Gururangan et al., 2018), motivating evaluations that decouple annotation biases from genuine reasoning. We build on this perspective by characterizing features that transfer across datasets versus those that fail under distribution shift. In (Tu et al., 2020), shortcut features are cataloged across multiple NLP tasks and provide a useful taxonomy for our analyses. Complementary evidence from controlled stress tests suggests that brittle cues can dominate model behavior even when stronger signals exist (Jia et al., 2019). Our framework operationalizes these insights by surfacing high-precision indicators of spuriousness using attribution and counterfactual probes.

Mitigation Multiple approaches have been proposed to mitigate shortcut learning and data biases (Clark et al., 2020; Bras et al., 2020; Zhou and Bansal, 2020; Minderer et al., 2020), data augmentation (Jin et al., 2020; Alzantot et al., 2018), domain adaptation (Blitzer et al., 2006; Blitzer et al., 2007), and multi-task learning (Tu et al., 2020). Du et al. (2021) proposes to mitigate shortcuts by suppressing model's prediction on examples with large shortcut degree. Recent study has also shown removing spurious correlations can sometimes hurt model's accuracy (Khani and Liang, 2021). Orthogonal to existing works, we propose to first identify unrobust correlations in an NLP model and then propose a targeted mitigation to encourage the model to rely less on those unrobust correlations. Specifically, we design perturbation policies that downweight high-risk features without suppressing genuinely predictive structure. Our cross-dataset diagnostics guide selection of training signals that generalize, thereby improving performance under shift while preserving in-domain accuracy. We evaluate these strategies on multiple benchmarks and report consistent gains over strong baselines under controlled ablations. Finally, we discuss limitations and ethical considerations, emphasizing that mitigation should not substitute for better data collection and transparency practices.

document: VQA evaluation. The textual, visual, and answer biases discovered in the VQA dataset (Antol et al., 2015) spurred on recent work seeking to improve model evaluation for the task by eliminating these biases (Goyal et al., 2017), introducing distributional shifts (Agrawal et al., 2018), requiring model explanations (Li et al., 2018), thoroughly analyzing biases in datasets and models (Manjunatha et al., 2019), or evaluating on different recognition subtasks (Kafle and Kanan, 2017). While debiased and challenging benchmarks are important, their focus on accuracy as the sole evaluation metric leaves much to be desired (Ribeiro et al., 2020a; Kervadec et al., 2020). In contrast, our testbed provides question or image pairs that compare models' predictions between questions, measuring their accuracy, consistency, and robustness to a variety of text and image perturbations. This design allows side-by-side attribution analyses without confounding changes in the underlying distribution of visual scenes. We argue that progress on VQA requires evaluation protocols that move beyond answer matching to assess process fidelity and reasoning generalization. To this end, we include targeted stress tests that manipulate quantifiers, negation, and coreference to isolate brittle behaviors. We also assess calibration, asking whether models know when they do not know, using confidence-based criteria derived from their predictive distributions. Finally, we report consistency scores across semantically equivalent paraphrases to quantify how stable predictions remain under surface variation. Synthetic VQA. One way in which we can generate diverse and balanced datasets is to generate them synthetically, as is done by Johnson et al. (2015) and Hudson and Manning (2019). Synthetically generating questions, images, or both, allows fine control over the distribution of question types, answers, and visual configurations. As both the CLEVR (Johnson et al., 2015) and GQA (Hudson and Manning, 2019) datasets use image scene graphs for question and label generation, they contain questions combining a range of required capabilities, including compositionality. However, synthetic worlds can introduce artifacts that inadvertently simplify the task, making careful validation essential. Our setting features real-world images paired with synthetically generated and minimally edited questions to probe specific competencies under controlled contrasts (cf. Ribeiro et al., 2020a; Kervadec, 2020; Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017. In contrast to GQA, our evaluation organizes instances into pairs that differ by a single linguistic or visual factor, enabling precise error attribution. This paired construction further reduces spurious correlations, since nuisance attributes are held constant across contrasted items. We complement synthetic generation with template-free paraphrasing to avoid overfitting to rigid lexical patterns. For robustness, we sample distractor options that are type-consistent yet semantically implausible, discouraging models from guessing by priors. We additionally curate adversarial subsets that invert common dataset biases, such as color-object associations and positional cues. Our evaluation protocol reports per-capability breakdowns, macro-averaged aggregates, and difficulty-binned summaries to facilitate fair comparison across methods. Beyond accuracy, we include measures of sample efficiency by plotting performance against the number of fine-tuning examples per capability. We also analyze failure modes by grouping errors according to linguistic phenomena and visual reasoning steps implicated by the pair differences. These diagnostics highlight systematic gaps that are obscured when metrics collapse heterogeneous behaviors into a single score. Together, these components support a more rigorous, interpretable, and reproducible assessment of progress in VQA.

Hate speech in user-generated content has been an active research area recently (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). Researchers have built several datasets for hate speech detection from diverse sources like Twitter (Waseem and Hovy, 2016;Davidson et al., 2017), Yahoo! (Nobata et al., 2016), Fox News (Gao and Huang, 2017), Gab (Mathew et al., 2021) and Reddit (Qian et al., 2019). Despite this progress, definitions of hateful content and annotation protocols remain contested across platforms and cultures. As a result, cross-dataset transfer often leads to notable performance drops even for strong neural models.

Compared to hate speech detection, few studies focus on detecting counter speech (Mathew et al., 2019;Ziems et al., 2020;Garland et al., 2020). Mathew et al. (2019) collect and handcode 6,898 counter hate comments from YouTube videos targeting Jews, Blacks and LGBT communities. Ziems et al. (2020) use a collection of hate and counter hate keywords relevant to COVID-19 and create a dataset containing 359 counter hate tweets targeting Asians. Garland et al. (2020) work with German tweets and define hate and counter speech based on the communities to which the authors belong. Another line of research focuses on curating datasets for counter speech generation using crowdsourcing (Qian et al., 2019) or with the help of trained operators (Chung et al., 2019;Fanton et al., 2021). However, synthetic language is rarely as rich as language in the wild. Even if it were, conclusions and models from synthetic data may not transfer to the real world. In this paper, we work with user-generated content expressing hate and counter-hate rather than synthetic content.

The table summarizes existing datasets for Hate and Counter-hate detection. Most of them do not include context information. In other words, the preceding comments are not provided when annotating Targets. Context does affect human judgments and has been taken into account for Hate detection (Gao and Huang, 2017;Vidgen et al., 2021;Pavlopoulos et al., 2020;Menini et al., 2021). Gao and Huang (2017) annotate hateful comments in the nested structures of 10 Fox News discussion threads. Prior work also notes that pragmatic cues and discourse roles modulate perceived toxicity, especially in reply chains where stance and intent are ambiguous. Vidgen et al. (2021 examine moderation pipelines that leverage thread-level signals to reduce false positives in dynamic discussions. Building on this intuition, conversation-aware classifiers have been proposed for other domains such as sentiment analysis (Ren et al., 2016), stance (Zubiaga et al., 2018) and sarcasm (Ghosh et al., 2020). Yet the extent to which contextual encoders specifically benefit counter speech recognition remains unclear. To our knowledge, we are the first to investigate the role of context in Hate and Counter-hate detection. We compare context-agnostic and context-aware architectures under consistent labeling guidelines and evaluation splits. We also analyze error patterns to identify when conversational history helps, when it hurts, and how it interacts with domain shift.
