document: Named Entity Recognition The mainstream NER systems are designed to recognize flat entities and based on a sequence tagging framework. Collobert et al. (2011) introduced the linear-chain conditional random field (CRF) into neural networkbased sequence tagging models, which can explicitly encode the transition likelihoods between adjacent tags. Many researchers followed this work, and employed LSTM as the encoder. In addition, character-level representations are typically used for English tasks (Huang et al., 2015;Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016;Chiu and Nichols, 2016), whereas lexicon information is helpful for Chinese NER (Zhang and Yang, 2018;Ma et al., 2020;Li et al., 2020a). Nested NER allows a token to belong to multiple entities, which conflicts with the plain sequence tagging framework. Ju et al. (2018) proposed to use stacked LSTM-CRFs to predict from inner to outer entities. Straková et al. (2019) concatenated the BILOU tags for each token inside the nested entities, which allows the LSTM-CRF to work as for flat entities. Li et al. (2020b) reformulated nested NER as a machine reading comprehension task. Shen et al. (2021) proposed to recognize nested entities by the two-stage object detection method widely used in computer vision. Recent years, a body of literature emerged on span-based models, which were compatible with both flat and nested entities, and achieved SOTA performance (Eberts and Ulges, 2020;Yu et al., 2020;Li et al., 2021). These models typically enumerate all possible candidate text spans and then classify each span into entity types. In this work, the biaffine model (Yu et al., 2020 is chosen and re-implemented with slight modifications as our baseline, because of its high performance and compatibility with boundary smoothing. In addition, pretrained language models, also known as contextualized embeddings, were also widely introduced to NER models, and significantly boosted the model performance (Peters et al., 2018;Devlin et al., 2019). They are used in our baseline by default. Label Smoothing Szegedy et al. (2016) proposed the label smoothing as a regularization technique to improve the accuracy of the Inception networks on ImageNet. By explicitly assigning a small probability to non-ground-truth labels, label smoothing can prevent the models from becoming too confident about the predictions, and thus improve generalization. It turned out to be a useful alternative to the standard cross entropy loss, and has been widely adopted to fight against the over-confidence (Zoph et al., 2018;Chorowski and Jaitly, 2017;Vaswani et al., 2017), improve the model calibration (Müller et al., 2019), and denoise incorrect labels (Lukasik et al., 2020). Our proposed boundary smoothing applies the smoothing technique to entity boundaries, rather than labels. This is driven by the observation that entity boundaries are more ambiguous and inconsistent to annotate in NER engineering. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first that focuses on the effect of smoothing regularization on NER models. Prior research on boundary uncertainty in NER has examined disagreement patterns among annotators and their impact on model bias (Gadde et al., 2020). However, most studies operationalize boundary noise through label-level perturbations rather than explicitly regularizing the boundary region. Our approach differs by redistributing probability mass over neighboring offsets, which can be interpreted as a structured analogue of label smoothing. This design is compatible with span classification and sequence tagging, and it preserves the learning signal for exact matches. From an optimization perspective, boundary smoothing reduces the gradient variance near ambiguous spans and stabilizes early training. We hypothesize that this regularization is particularly beneficial when the encoder is highly expressive, such as with large pretrained Transformers. In contrast to data augmentation methods that sample spans with heuristic jitter, our method is parameter-free and incurs negligible computational overhead. The following sections detail the algorithmic formulation, training schedule, and evaluation protocol used to validate these claims.

There have been several studies addressing technical solutions for aspect-based sentiment analysis. The emotional elements involved in ABSA mainly include aspect term, opinion term, aspect category, and sentiment polarity. In order to extract these emotional elements, the main research direction of ABSA is to extract aspect terms (Liu et al., 2015;Yin et al., 2016;Li et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019) and categorize the sentiment of a given aspect (Wang et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2019;Zhang and Qian, 2020), and to jointly predict multiple elements simultaneously at the same time (Li et al., 2019a;Wan et al., 2020;Peng et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020).

The early ABSA problems were mostly expressed as sequence labeling or multi-classification problems (Li et al., 2019a), which were predicted by designing task-specific classification networks and using the class index as labels for training (Huang and Carley, 2019;Wan et al., 2020). However, this approach requires the design of different classification models and ignores the label semantics. Recent works achieve good performance by converting the ABSA problems as a text generation problem (Yan et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2021a,b).

In addition, the generative framework has been proven effective for many other natural language processing problems including dialogue state tracking (Feng et al., 2020), entity linking (De Cao et al., 2020), event extraction, information extraction (Sui et al., 2020), named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021;Tan et al., 2021;Raffel et al., 2019;Athiwaratkun et al., 2020). In particular, Paolini et al. (2021) solved various NLP tasks in a unified generative framework. The transition from pipeline designs to joint modeling aims to mitigate error propagation between extraction and classification stages. Joint decoders explicitly capture interdependencies among elements, e.g., between aspect and opinion terms, improving robustness under domain shift (Li et al., 2019a;Wan et al., 2020). Span-based formulations further reduce label sparsity by enumerating and scoring candidate spans with structured constraints (Ma et al., 2019;Zhang and Qian, 2020). Nevertheless, span enumeration can be computationally expensive without pruning strategies derived from syntactic signals (Huang and Carley, 2019). Dependency-aware methods leverage heads and modifiers to connect aspects with their opinion expressions through shortest paths (Wang et al., 2016;Chen et al., 2017). Graph neural approaches propagate sentiment cues over heterogeneous graphs of tokens, spans, and categories to support multi-element reasoning (Peng et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020). Pretrained language models offer strong priors for such architectures, yet most ABSA work adopts BERT in a task-agnostic manner Devlin 2019. Encoder-decoder generative paradigms reformulate ABSA as conditional text-to-text mapping, enabling unified training across subtasks (Raffel et al., 2019;Paolini et al., 2021). Under this view, outputs can be serialized using special markers that delineate aspect positions, categories, and polarities (Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Sequence-to-sequence learning also facilitates multitask pretraining where auxiliary objectives encourage better extraction of opinion expressions (Yan et al., 2021;Tan et al., 2021). However, label verbalization choices and decoding constraints strongly influence faithfulness to the target schema (Athiwaratkun et al., 2020). Moreover, exposure bias in autoregressive decoding may hurt boundary fidelity for long aspects unless augmented with constrained beam search. Beyond modeling, domain adaptation is central because vocabularies and opinion targets vary across review genres and languages (Jiang et al., 2019;Zhang and Qian, 2020). Unsupervised adaptation exploits pseudo-labeling with confidence filtering and contrastive alignment between source and target domains (Wan et al., 2020). Data augmentation via back-translation and opinion span swapping can further regularize models against spurious lexical cues (Li et al., 2018). Recent studies also integrate external knowledge such as sentiment lexicons and attribute schemas to guide decoding under low-resource conditions (Liu et al., 2015;Yin et al., 2016). Multilingual ABSA extends these trends by coupling translation-based transfer with language-agnostic subword representations (Raffel et al., 2019). Evaluation practices remain inconsistent, with varied protocols for matching spans, handling implicit aspects, and aggregating category-level metrics. To enable fair comparison, several benchmarks now recommend explicit reporting of extraction, classification, and joint scores under identical preprocessing (Zhang et al., 2021a,b). Nevertheless, many works still underreport error analyses that reveal cross-component dependencies and typical failure cases. This paper situates our approach within the generative line of research while emphasizing structure-aware decoding and principled evaluation to advance ABSA practice.

document: Robustness of MRC Models. The robustness of MRC models is usually evaluated against test-time perturbations and out-of-domain data. Research on test-time perturbation proposes attacks at different levels, such as lexical, syntactic, and discourse manipulations. Typical lexical methods include word replacement with nearest neighbors in embedding space (Rychalska et al., 2018. Other strategies paraphrase questions or passages to alter surface form while preserving semantics (Gan and Ng, 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2018). Sentence-level distractors are injected to confuse span selection and calibration (Jia and Liang, 2017; Zhou et al., 2020). A complementary line tests models on datasets with shifted passage or question distributions (Fisch et al., 2019; Sen and Saffari, 2020). These evaluations reveal brittle behavior under small edits and limited transfer across domains. Our work mainly falls under test-time perturbation with a focus on entity-centric edits. We study how replacing or obfuscating named entities affects answer prediction and evidence selection. The motivation is that real-world entities often have aliases, transliterations, and evolving names across time and sources. Robust models should therefore ground answers to entity roles and contexts rather than surface strings. Model Robustness to Entity Substitution. Handling the large and dynamic space of entities is non-trivial for neural NLP models. Early audits replaced entities with plausible alternates to probe reliance on memorization versus reasoning (Agarwal et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2021). Similar ideas have appeared in Natural Language Inference and relation extraction to check sensitivity to proper nouns (Mitra et al., 2020; Alt et al., 2020). However, systematic methodologies for entity substitution in MRC remain underexplored. Existing perturbations often ignore coreference structure and knowledge base constraints that govern entity compatibility. We propose a taxonomy that differentiates identity-preserving substitutions from role-swapping and type-consistent insertion. Identity-preserving edits maintain answer truth while altering surface forms like aliases and abbreviations. Role-swapping exchanges entity mentions to test whether models conflate co-occurring names with answer spans. Type-consistent insertion introduces unseen but schema-valid entities to assess generalization. To operationalize this taxonomy, we couple named entity linking with typed span masking and constrained generation. Our framework allows precise control over perturbation strength and locality within questions and passages. We evaluate across extractive and generative MRC architectures, including span predictors and sequence-to-sequence decoders. Results show that entity-focused perturbations degrade calibration more than accuracy, revealing silent failures in confidence estimates. Additional analysis indicates that retrieval-augmented systems are less sensitive when evidence explicitly mentions canonical identifiers. Nevertheless, models trained with naive augmentation sometimes overfit to surface formats and underperform on alias-rich cases. We contrast our approach with adversarial training that optimizes perturbations against model gradients (Si et al., 2021). While gradient-based attacks can be strong, they may yield unnatural entity strings without semantic plausibility checks. Our substitutions prioritize linguistic fidelity and factual consistency to better approximate real deployments. We further relate to cross-domain robustness that manipulates topical distributions rather than entity realizations, e.g., Hendrycks et al. 2020. Finally, we discuss evaluation metrics that separate answer correctness, evidence faithfulness, and calibration under perturbations. This perspective underscores the need for standardized protocols spanning perturbation design, masking policies, and reporting practices. In summary, we aim to move beyond token-level noise toward entity-aware robustness grounded in structured semantics. The proposed setup complements prior test suites and invites reproducible comparisons across models and datasets.

There is vast prior research on textual entity linking. Multiple datasets have been proposed over the years, including manually annotated high‑quality resources like AIDA (Hoffart et al., 2011), automatically annotated large‑scale corpora like CWEB (Guo and Barbosa, 2018), and zero‑shot benchmarks like Zeshel (Logeswaran et al., 2019). In common practice, models are trained on AIDA‑train and evaluated on AIDA‑test, MSNBC (Cucerzan, 2007), and AQUAINT (Milne and Witten, 2008), among others. However, as noted in Cao et al., 2021), many methods have converged to similarly high scores on these suites. This saturation suggests that we may be approaching the ceiling of what can be concluded from these datasets, making incremental advances harder to measure.

In recent years, the growing trend toward multimodality has motivated extensions of EL from text‑only inputs to joint vision–language settings. Early multimodal EL benchmarks include datasets derived from Weibo, Wikipedia, and Richpedia that align mentions with CN-DBpedia, Wikidata, and Richpedia, respectively, as described by Moon et al. (2018) and Peng (2021). Nevertheless, using Wikipedia as a target source can introduce leakage because many language models are pre‑trained on its contents. Consequently, evaluating on web or social media images with less overlap to pre‑training data is increasingly recommended.

Multimodal EL systems typically combine visual encoders with text encoders to produce entity‑aware representations that are compared against knowledge base entries. Recent approaches leverage contrastive alignment to fuse image regions with mention spans, while retrieval‑augmented modules supply candidate entities from large KBs. However, cross‑modal ambiguity, sparse supervision, and long‑tail entities remain major obstacles to reliable grounding.

Our MEL dataset is also related to entity‑aware image captioning resources (Biten et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2020) and to multimodal NER corpora (Lu et al., 2018). Unlike EL, these resources typically annotate mentions but do not link them to a unified KB, which limits downstream reasoning and disambiguation. By providing grounded links alongside multimodal context, MEL datasets can support both recognition and resolution of entities under realistic conditions.

Building on these observations, we argue for benchmarks that minimize pre‑training leakage and emphasize cross‑domain robustness. We also highlight the need for stronger negative sampling and harder distractors to better probe fine‑grained disambiguation. Complementary analyses of calibration, out‑of‑distribution generalization, and annotation quality are essential for meaningful comparisons. Together, these directions aim to reinvigorate progress on EL by shifting evaluation toward settings that better reflect real‑world multimodal understanding.

document: Open-domain response generation aims to produce fluent and contextually appropriate replies without constraints on topic. Early research framed the task as a form of machine translation from messages to responses (Ritter et al., 2011). Sequence-to-sequence architectures with attention quickly became the dominant approach (Sutskever et al., 2014; Bahdanau et al., 2015). Subsequent work introduced hierarchical encoders to model multi-turn context effectively (Sordoni et al., 2015; Serban et al., 2016). The rise of transformer-based decoders further improved fluency and long-range coherence (Vaswani et al., 2017; Wolf et al., 2019). Personalization through explicit persona profiles showed consistent gains in engagingness (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018). Control signals have also targeted discourse coherence, topicality, and sentiment (Xu et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2018). Other studies explored response-relatedness and mutual information to reduce dull replies (See et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016). Despite these advances, most methods are optimized and evaluated on single-domain corpora. In practical deployment, systems must reconcile stylistic, lexical, and pragmatic variation across multiple heterogeneous datasets. Cross-corpus generalization remains challenging due to domain shift, annotation artifacts, and differing conversational norms. Embedding-based control provides a compact interface to steer generation by domain, speaker attributes, or dialogue acts. Prior multi-domain work often treats the domain label as a simple discrete token rather than a structured control signal (Gurumurthy et al., 2019). We argue for continuous control vectors that encode corpus-level properties learned from metadata and content statistics. This perspective aligns with advances in controllable text generation that map attributes to low-dimensional embeddings (Dathathri et al., 2020). Retrieval-augmented response models complement control by injecting corpus-specific evidence into the decoder (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020). However, naively mixing retrieved passages from disparate sources can destabilize stylistic control and factual consistency. Multi-task training partially mitigates this issue but often suffers from negative transfer when domains are weakly related (Zamir et al., 2020). Recent evaluations also highlight that BLEU and ROUGE undercapture conversational quality, necessitating human and learned metrics (See et al., 2019; Mehri and Eskenazi, 2020). Safety, bias, and toxicity concerns further complicate cross-domain deployment and must be explicitly modeled (Gehman et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2021). Data curation practices, including filtering, deduplication, and speaker normalization, have measurable effects on control fidelity (Roller et al., 2021). Continual and lifelong learning strategies aim to incorporate new corpora without catastrophic forgetting (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017; de Masson d'Autume et al., 2019). Adapter modules and prefix tuning offer parameter-efficient mechanisms to attach domain expertise to a shared backbone (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021). Contrastive objectives can encourage separation between domain embeddings while preserving shared conversational skills (Gao et al., 2021). Our study revisits the classical view initiated by Ritter et al. (2011) and examines control across corpora. We operationalize control as a learned embedding conditioned on dataset identity, register, and conversational function. Unlike discrete tags, our embeddings capture graded similarity between corpora and support interpolation during decoding. We benchmark on three public datasets spanning casual chat, knowledge-grounded dialogue, and empathetic conversation (Zhang et al., 2020; Dinan et al., 2019; Rashkin et al., 2019). For historical context, several baselines follow machine translation style training (Ritter et al., 2011). We reference the foundational dataset (Ritter et al., 2011 to ground our comparisons. Training uses a shared transformer with domain-conditioned adapters and a small set of frozen backbone parameters. Decoding employs constrained nucleus sampling with a classifier-guided penalty for attribute drift (Holtzman et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2021). Our contributions include a unified formulation of multi-corpus control, an extensive empirical study, and open-source resources. We show consistent gains in human judgments of appropriateness, specificity, and style match across all domains. We further analyze embedding geometry to reveal clusters that correspond to conversational norms and register. Finally, we discuss limitations and future directions, including harder domain shifts, multilingual settings, and safety-aware control.

document: Information extraction aims to identify structured relations from unstructured text to support downstream reasoning and search. Open information extraction (OpenIE) relaxes schema constraints by extracting surface triples without predefined relation types. Given a sentence s = w1 w2 ... wn where wi is the ith token in the sentence, an OpenIE system outputs triples of the form (subject; predicate; object). Early OpenIE systems emphasized scalability and redundancy to harvest high-precision assertions from web text (cf. Banko et al., 2007. Subsequent lines of work incorporated linguistic constraints such as clause splitting, dependency patterns, and light semantic typing (Fader et al., 2011; Mausam, 2016). Neural methods have since reframed extraction as sequence labeling, generation, or span classification with contextual encoders (Stanovsky et al., 2018; Cui et al., 2018; Kolluru et al., 2020). Despite these advances, fidelity to predicate-argument structure and boundary calibration remain open challenges, particularly in low-resource domains (Liu et al., 2020). Our work examines how structural priors and constrained decoding improve triple completeness while controlling hallucination rates. We begin by reviewing canonical formulations and datasets that have shaped evaluation practice in OpenIE. Classic benchmarks such as OIE2016 and CaRB standardized matching protocols and precision–recall reporting (Stanovsky and Dagan, 2016; Bhardwaj et al., 2019). However, dataset artifacts and partial credit strategies can mask boundary errors and predicate underspecification, complicating fair comparison (Gashteovski et al., 2021). To address these issues, several authors advocate for entailment-based or QA-based evaluation that ties extractions to verifiable paraphrases (Gabriel et al., 2020; Levy et al., 2017). In parallel, multilingual and domain-specific corpora expose brittleness in models trained solely on English newswire (Rivière et al., 2022; Saha et al., 2018). We align our problem definition with prior OpenIE conventions while integrating modern constraints from structured prediction. The literature offers complementary perspectives on balancing precision with coverage in open-domain relation extraction (Mausam, 2016; Niklaus et al., 2018). A frequent theme is the trade-off between aggressive clause simplification and the risk of losing essential semantic roles (Saha and Mausam, 2018). For instance, nominal predicates and light-verb constructions often lead to fragmented or duplicated triples when heuristics are overapplied (Pal and Mausam, 2016). Recent generative approaches mitigate sparsity with pretrained language models yet require careful calibration to avoid overgeneration (Kolluru et al., 2020; Zhan and Zhao, 2020). Another line explores calibration via confidence estimation and selective prediction to reduce downstream propagation of noisy facts (Kamath et al., 2020; Geifman and El-Yaniv, 2019). We also consider evaluation settings that require entity canonicalization and temporal scoping beyond the surface form of a triple (Hosseini et al., 2018). Finally, we situate our contributions relative to foundational OpenIE systems, including ReVerb, OLLIE, ClausIE, and modern neural baselines (Riedel et al., 2013; Angeli et al., 1. According to Etzioni’s program of open extraction, coverage should scale with corpus size, though error accumulation necessitates robust filtering (Banko et al., 2007). In this context, we reassess how training objectives align with inference-time constraints on argument contiguity and relation granularity (Zhang and Ji, 2021). We conclude that principled decoding, calibration, and evaluation together yield measurable gains across diverse corpora and relation types.

Given a set of entities E and a set of relations R, a knowledge graph K ⊆ E × R × E is a collection of subject-predicate-object (s, p, o) triples. Link prediction is the task of predicting missing triples in K by answering queries of the form of (s, p, ?) and (?, p, o). This is typically accomplished using knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models.

Conventional KGEs assign an embedding vector for each entity and relation in the KG. They model the plausibility of (s, p, o) triples via model specific scoring functions f (e s , e p , e o ) using the subject (e s ), predicate (e p ) and object (e o ) specific embeddings. Once trained, these embeddings are used for downstream tasks such as question answering.

Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) is the task of answering a natural language question using a KG as source of knowledge. The questions can be either simple factual questions that require a single fact retrieval (e.g. What language is spoken in India?), or they can be complex questions that require reasoning over multiple facts in the KG (e.g. What is the predominant religion where the leader is Ovadia Yosef?). KGEs can be utilized to perform KGQA when the background KGs are incomplete.

In the next few sections we will go into more detail about existing work on KGEs and KGQA.

Early translational models represent relations as translations in the embedding space and encourage s + p ≈ o under a chosen norm. Bilinear models instead parameterize relations as matrices and compute interactions via multiplicative forms that capture symmetry and antisymmetry. More recent neural models compose entity and relation features through non-linear transformations that can adapt to diverse relational patterns. Training commonly relies on margin or logistic losses with negative sampling to approximate the softmax over all entities. Regularization strategies such as embedding norm constraints and dropout mitigate overfitting on dense subgraphs. Evaluation typically reports mean reciprocal rank and Hits@k, with filtered metrics excluding triples that are true in the KG but absent from the test set. Rotational models on complex or hypercomplex spaces aim to preserve relational patterns like inversion and composition in a geometrically principled way. Recent surveys provide comprehensive overviews of scoring functions and training losses (Smith, Brown, and Lee 2020), including translational, bilinear, and neural formulations. Beyond static KGs, temporal and multi-modal extensions integrate timestamps and textual descriptions to improve disambiguation. Inductive settings require generalizing to entities unseen at training time by encoding textual or structural features rather than relying solely on identifiers. Rule-guided and neuro-symbolic methods combine differentiable objectives with mined logical rules to enhance generalization and interpretability. Despite progress in link prediction, answering compositional queries remains challenging because supervision rarely covers complex query structures. Logical query embedding approaches represent sets of answers to conjunctive queries as regions or distributions in the embedding space. These methods target multi-hop reasoning patterns while avoiding full grounding of intermediate variables. In the context of KGQA, pipeline architectures first map the question to a formal query and then retrieve answers from the KG. Neural semantic parsers learn to produce graph queries from text by leveraging weak supervision and denotations. Embedding-based KGQA maps questions and candidate answers into a shared space and ranks entities using the underlying KGE scores. Hybrid systems integrate retrieval with reading comprehension over textual corpora to address incompleteness in the KG.

document: Quantization of Pre-trained Language Models. Quantization compresses a model by representing the 32-bit floating-point parameter with a low-bit representation, and has been widely used in various domains as it does not require designing a new model architecture. There have been many attempts to quantize task-specific BERT models (Zafrir et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Zadeh et al., 2020) with only negligible performance drop on natural language understanding tasks. Recent works Bai et al., 2021) even push the weight bit-width down to as low as 1-bit. Despite the success of these approaches for BERT models, attempts to quantize generative PLMs are scarce, and the underlying difficulty remains unclear. Autoregressive generation magnifies quantization errors because each decoding step conditions on previous outputs. Small perturbations in logits or hidden states can propagate and destabilize long-form generation. Several studies report that aggressive weight quantization degrades attention sharpness and context retention in transformers (Dettmers et al., 2022). Activation quantization presents an additional challenge due to outlier features in key and value projections (Nagel et al., 2020). These challenges motivate training-time regularizers that align latent spaces between full-precision and quantized models. Contrastive Learning. Contrastive learning aims at pushing the representations of similar samples together while pulling those of dissimilar ones apart. It is widely used for large-scale self-supervised learning in various domains (Sun et al., 2020a; Baevski et al., 2020; Anonymous, 2022) and for multi-modal learning (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021). SimCLR directly uses other in-batch samples as negatives, and a sufficiently large batch size is required to work well. MoCo maintains a large number of negative samples in a queue and uses a moving average key encoder to improve consistency. Contrastive learning without negative samples is also proposed in BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen and He, 2021). Contrastive representation distillation (Tian et al., 2019) distills knowledge from a teacher network to a student network by maximizing the mutual information between them. Recent advances adapt contrastive objectives to token-level or patch-level supervision to better preserve fine-grained structure (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). In natural language processing, contrastive regularization has been explored to stabilize pretraining and to improve robustness under distribution shifts (Gunel et al., 2021). The closest work to our token-level contrastive distillation is Wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020), which uses in-utterance representations at different positions as negatives in speech learning. Besides the difference in modality and tasks, our method also differs from theirs in model design. We quantize both model parameters and activations, whereas their approach remains in full precision. For each sample, we use the outputs of the full-precision and the quantized networks as two views, while they use the quantized and the contextualized representation. Our contrastive loss is computed over all tokens in an autoregressive manner, whereas theirs focuses on masked tokens non-autoregressively. This design aligns the generative behavior of the quantized model with its full-precision counterpart at each decoding step. As a result, the student is encouraged to preserve token-wise predictive geometry even under low-bit constraints. We hypothesize that such alignment reduces error accumulation during decoding and narrows the performance gap to full precision. The following sections present our quantization-aware training objective, experimental setup, and ablation analyses.

document: Crafting adversarial examples and using them in training was previously shown to be an effective technique in improving NLP and ML models (Nie et al., 2020;Dinan et al., 2019;Kiela et al., 2021). Not only that, but adversarial attacks can reveal important vulnerabilities in our systems (Zhang et al., 2020a). Although previous work has studied adversarial examples in NLP (Li et al., 2017;Zang et al., 2020) most of them focused on accuracy as a metric of interest. Among the ones that studied toxicity and other ethical considerations (Wallace et al., 2019;Sheng et al., 2020) they did not put the focus on either conversational agents or they did not consider attacks being imperceptible. Cheng et al. (2019); Niu and Bansal (2018) studied adversarial attacks on conversational agents; however, their focus was on task oriented dialogue systems and also did not consider toxicity but accuracy as a metric. Xu et al. (2020) also considered conversational domains; however, they relied on human adversaries which can be costly and non-scalable. Early advances in adversarial training in computer vision inspired analogous efforts in NLP, particularly after the formulation of fast gradient methods (Goodfellow et al., 2015). In language tasks, discrete token spaces demanded alternative strategies such as character manipulations and paraphrase-based attacks (Ebrahimi et al., 2018;Jia and Liang, 2017). Subsequent work introduced semantically constrained paraphrases to maintain meaning while misleading classifiers and question answering systems (Iyyer et al., 2018;Ribeiro et al., 2018). Toxicity and safety research has moved in parallel, highlighting annotation artifacts and bias in widely used toxicity datasets (Borkan et al., 2019;Sap et al., 2019). Despite increasing sophistication of detectors, the open-ended nature of conversation introduces context shifts that can exacerbate harmful outputs. This work positions adversarial robustness as a prerequisite for safe conversational deployment rather than a mere post hoc filter. We emphasize imperceptible attacks that preserve fluency, topic, and intent while subtly provoking toxic continuations. Our study contrasts black-box paraphrastic attacks with limited query budgets against white-box surrogate-assisted attacks that approximate gradients. We analyze transferability across models trained on distinct dialogue corpora and with different decoding strategies. Related methods augment training data with adversarially generated paraphrases to increase robustness without sacrificing naturalness (Si et al., 2020;Yoo and Qi, 2021). However, most augmentations target intent classification or natural language inference rather than open-domain dialogue safety. Popular dialogue datasets, including ConvAI2 and Blended Skill Talk, provide useful testbeds but were not designed for adversarial safety evaluation (Zhang et al., 2018;Roller et al., 2021). Safety toolkits combine lexical blocklists, toxicity-aware reranking, and detoxification fine-tuning to mitigate harmful generations (Gehman et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2021). Nevertheless, blocklist-based defenses remain brittle under paraphrase and obfuscation that preserve semantics. Comprehensive overviews of adversarial robustness in NLP and safety summarize these directions (Anderson et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020. Beyond surveys, causal and counterfactual evaluation frameworks encourage stress tests that control for confounds and spurious cues (Keith et al., 2020;Pryzant et al., 2021). Reinforcement learning from human feedback can reduce overt toxicity but may also encourage evasive phrasing that complicates detection (Ziegler et al., 2019;Bai et al., 2022). Complementary approaches use constrained decoding and minimum risk training to balance helpfulness and harmlessness in dialogue generation (Krause et al., 2021;Askell et al., 2021). Textual adversarial generation techniques span genetic algorithms, gradient-guided search, and paraphrastic substitution using large semantic resources (Alzantot et al., 2018;Ren et al., 2019;Morris et al., 2020). For toxicity detection, a key open question is whether near-synonymous paraphrases can mask harmful intent without degrading human-perceived quality. We therefore define imperceptibility through a combination of semantic similarity, fluency, and paraphrase acceptability metrics calibrated on human judgments. Our evaluation reports expected calibration error, balanced accuracy, and utility-aware curves alongside standard precision and recall. We compare training regimes that include vanilla fine-tuning, adversarial fine-tuning, and a mixed curriculum that interleaves clean and adversarial examples. We examine stability under distribution shift by testing across unseen topics and styles drawn from diverse conversational sources. To assess generalization, we study cross-model transfer where attacks crafted on one architecture succeed against others without further optimization. We further evaluate data efficiency by measuring gains under constrained annotation budgets and simulated user feedback. Our ablation studies vary attack budgets, paraphrase beam widths, and classifier thresholds to reveal sensitivity patterns. We release reproducible code, attack scripts, and an evaluation harness to facilitate community benchmarking. The broader impacts of this work include clearer risk assessments for deployment and guidance for mitigation strategies that do not overly censor benign content. Future research should integrate longitudinal user studies and robust monitoring to track safety performance over time.

document: Traditional sentiment analysis often targets sentence-level or document-level granularity (Yang and Cardie, 2014; Severyn and Moschitti, 2015; Dou, 2017; Lyu et al., 2020). In contrast, aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) seeks fine-grained judgments tied to specific aspects or entities within text (Hu and Liu, 2004). Canonical subtasks in ABSA include aspect term extraction, aspect sentiment classification, and opinion term extraction (Wang et al., 2016b; Ma et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2018; Chen and Qian, 2020). Early studies often addressed these subtasks independently, which limited the modeling of interdependencies among aspects and opinions (Li et al., 2018a; Xu et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019). To mitigate this limitation, joint formulations were proposed to co-extract aspects and opinions or to extract aspect–sentiment pairs in a unified manner (Wang et al., 2016a; He et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019b). The co-extraction line of work typically designs sequence tagging or table-filling strategies to detect related spans with minimal error propagation (Miwa and Sasaki, 2014; Gupta et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2020a). Beyond pair extraction, researchers have aimed to predict complete aspect–sentiment–opinion triplets within a single framework (Ma et al., 2018; Dai and Song, 2019; Wang and Pan, 2019). The ASTE formulation crystallized this triplet-centric perspective and spurred new benchmark settings and datasets Peng et al. 2020. Pipeline approaches decompose ASTE into sequential modules for aspect detection, opinion detection, and sentiment polarity classification, often sharing encoders across stages (Chen et al., 2021a). However, pipeline designs may accumulate cascading errors and struggle with long-distance dependencies across the modules (Xu et al., 2021). End-to-end methods alleviate these issues by coupling span detection and sentiment inference within a single optimization objective (Yan et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020a). Grid tagging schemes recast ASTE as a two-dimensional labeling problem that jointly marks token relations and polarities (Wu et al., 2020a). Alternative designs rely on pointer networks or biaffine classifiers to directly connect aspect and opinion mentions under polarity constraints (Ma et al., 2018; He et al., 2019). Meanwhile, generative approaches frame ASTE as conditional text generation and decode structured triplets via constrained decoding (Yan et al., 2021). Recent studies also explore machine reading comprehension formulations to query aspects and opinions with shared pretrained encoders (Mao et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021a). Despite architectural diversity, leveraging syntactic cues and dependency structures consistently improves triplet extraction robustness (Zhang et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020). Incorporating part-of-speech, constituency, and dependency signals has been shown to reduce boundary ambiguity and sentiment drift (Li et al., 2019a; Chen and Qian, 2020). Advances in contextualized representations further enhance generalization across domains and review styles (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Nevertheless, pretrained encoders can overfit surface patterns without explicit relational bias when supervision is sparse (Ruder, 2019). Multi-task learning has therefore been used to couple ASTE with auxiliary objectives such as span detection and opinion role labeling (Zhou et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2021). Data augmentation complements these strategies by generating plausible paraphrases and polarity-preserving substitutions (Wei and Zou, 2019; Fadaee et al., 2017). Cross-domain transfer remains challenging because aspect vocabularies and opinion expressions vary widely across product categories (Barnes et al., 2018; Pontiki et al., 2016). Robust evaluation thus requires fine-grained metrics that separately assess extraction accuracy and polarity fidelity (Schmitt et al., 2018). In addition, reporting span-level F1 alongside triplet-level precision and recall clarifies sources of model improvement (Xu et al., 2021). Beyond metrics, consistent annotation schemas are needed to align boundary definitions and polarity inventory across datasets (Pontiki et al., 2014). Recent benchmarks introduce clearer guidelines for nested or overlapping mentions, improving inter-annotator agreement (Bai et al., 2021). Against this backdrop, we position our model as an end-to-end system that integrates structural bias with pretrained representations to extract reliable triplets. Our approach emphasizes sample efficiency, domain transferability, and calibrated confidence estimates for practical deployment.

document:
We describe related works on multi-document summarization categorized by model architecture.

Flat solutions.
Flat concatenation is a simple yet powerful solution because the multi-document summary generation is treated as a single-document summarization task, thus leveraging state-of-the-art pre-trained summarization models.
Consequently, processing all documents as a flat input requires models capable of handling long sequences.
As previously done by (DeYoung et al., 2021), Xiao et al. (2021) propose to leverage the LongFormer-Encoder-Decoder model pre-trained with a novel multi-document summarization specific task.
They proved that a long-range transformer that encodes all documents is a straightforward yet effective solution, and they achieved new state-of-the-art results in several multi-document summarization datasets.
However, such models may struggle to handle a massive cluster of topic-related documents as they need to truncate them because of architectural limits.
Further, processing all documents in a cluster could be noisy if some of them are not relevant or factual with respect to the final summary.

Hierarchical solutions.
To better preserve crossdocument relations and obtain semantic-rich representations, hierarchical concatenation solutions leverage graph-based techniques to work from word and sentence level (Wan and Yang, 2006; Liao et al., 2018; Nayeem et al., 2018; Antognini and Faltings, 2019) to document level (Amplayo and Lapata, 2021.
Other hierarchical approaches include multi-head pooling and inter-paragraph attention architectures (Liu and Lapata, 2019a), attention models with Maximal Marginal Relevance (Fabbri et al., 2019), and attention across different granularity representations (Jin et al., 2020).
Such models are often dataset-specific because of the custom architecture, so they struggle to adapt to other datasets and effectively leverage pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers.

Our solution.
In this work, we show how the summary-relevant information can be discriminated from a cluster of topic-related documents by a probabilistic neural method trained end-to-end.
In particular, our solution fully leverages pre-trained state-of-the-art transformers and does not apply input truncation that causes performance drop and discards much important content, unacceptable for the medical domain.
We further outline how our training objective encourages content selection that is both salient and non-redundant across documents.

document: Temporal Knowledge Graph Embedding. Knowledge graph embedding learning (Bordes et al., 2013;Yang et al., 2014;Trouillon et al., 2016;Dettmers et al., 2018;Shang et al., 2019;Sun et al., 2019;Ji et al., 2021) has been an active research area with applications directly in knowledge base completion and relation extraction. Recently, several works have extended the static KG embedding models to temporal KGs. Jiang et al. (2016) first attempt to extend TransE (Bordes et al., 2013), and Lacroix et al. (2020) extend ComplEx with time based on the canonical decomposition of order-4 tensors. Temporal QA on Knowledge Graph. Temporal QA has mostly been studied in the context of reading comprehension. ForecastQA (Jin et al., 2021) formulates the forecasting problem as a multiple-choice question answering task, where both the articles and questions include the timestamps. The recently released TORQUE (Ning et al., 2020) is a dataset that explores the temporal ordering relations between events described in a passage of text. Another direction is the temporal question answering over knowledge bases (KB) (Jia et al., 2018b,a), which retrieves time information from the KB. TempQuestions (Jia et al., 2018a) is a KGQA dataset specifically aimed at temporal QA. Based on this dataset, Jia et al. (2018b) design a method that decomposes and rewrites each question into non-temporal sub-question and temporal sub-question. Here the KG used in TempQuestions is based on a subset of FreeBase which is not a temporal KG. Later, Jia et al. (2021) propose a first end-to-end system (EXAQT) for answering complex temporal questions, which takes advantage of the question-relevant compact subgraphs within the KG and relational graph convolutional networks (Schlichtkrull et al., 2018) for predicting the answers. All previous datasets only include a limited number of temporal questions. Recently, a much larger temporal KGQA dataset CRONQUES-TIONS (Saxena et al., 2021) is released, which includes both the temporal questions and the temporal KG with time annotation for all edges. Based on this dataset, the CronKGQA model (Saxena et al., 2021) is presented, which exploits recent advances in temporal KG embeddings and achieves performance superior to all baselines. Despite this progress, modeling temporal validity and recurrence remains challenging. Early temporal embedding methods introduce decay functions to modulate fact plausibility over time (Trivedi et al., 2017;Goel et al., 2020). Subsequent work encodes time with sinusoidal or learned positional features attached to entities and relations (Lacroix et al., 2020;Xu et al., 2020). Interval-based models capture start and end timestamps to handle facts with duration (Dasgupta et al., 2018;Jain et al., 2020). Other approaches parameterize relation dynamics via recurrent architectures or temporal point processes (Huang et al., 2020;Deng et al., 2021). Beyond embeddings, rule-based reasoning has been extended with temporal operators to maintain interpretability (Liu et al., 2021a;Saeed et al., 2021). Benchmarks such as ICEWS, GDELT, and YAGO offer diverse temporal patterns and granularity for evaluation (Boschee et al., 2015;Leetaru and Schrodt, 2013;Mahdisoltani et al., 2015). However, differences in timestamp precision and event density confound cross-dataset comparison protocols Rossi et al., 2021). Recent surveys synthesize these trends and call for unified temporal reasoning objectives (Kazemi et al., 2020;Ma et al., 2021). In contrast to temporal link prediction, temporal KGQA must align linguistic expressions with temporal constraints grounded in the graph (Talmor and Berant, 2018;Saxena et al., 2021). Neural semantic parsers map questions to executable programs over temporal KGs using weak supervision (Guo et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented pipelines decompose complex questions into anchored sub-queries that incorporate time filters (Vakulenko et al., 2020;ElSahar et al., 2019). Pretrained language models have been adapted to encode temporal signals via time-aware prompts and adapters (Zhou et al., 2021;Dhingra et al., 2022). Nevertheless, temporal reasoning often degrades under distribution shifts in time, relation schema, or question form (Mirza et al., 2021). Data augmentation with counterfactual timelines has been proposed to improve temporal robustness (Qian et al., 2021). Another line of work integrates numerical reasoning to handle questions involving ages and durations (Ran et al., 2019;Geva et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize compositional generalization across entities, relations, and temporal operators (Lake and Baroni, 2018;Keysers et al., 2020). Despite these efforts, reporting practices vary widely across datasets and metrics, complicating reproducibility (Pineau et al., 2020). To address these gaps, we adopt standardized splits and include ablations on time granularity, history length, and negative sampling (Shao et al., 2021;Zhu et al., 2021). We also release code and data to facilitate fair comparison and future research (Henderson et al., 2018).

document:
For G2P, converting words into pronunciations does not require as much expertise as needed to prepare correspondences between graphemes and phonemes. Therefore, a method of learning correspondences from a large number of wordpronunciation pairs has been used (van den Bosch and Daelemans, 1993). To solve the problem that graphemes and phonemes often have many-to-many correspondence, a hidden Markov model-based method (Jiampojamarn et al., 2007) and weighted finite-state transducer-based methods (Novak et al., 2012a,b) have been developed.

In addition, there have been some attempts to apply encoder-decoder models to learn end-to-end G2P models. For example, Toshniwal and Livescu (2016) applied a sequence-to-sequence architecture (Sutskever et al., 2014; Luong et al., 2015). Yolchuyeva et al. (2020) applied a transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) to train a single model that can deal with a large number of languages. Early approaches often relied on joint-sequence models that maximized the likelihood of aligned letter and phone sequences under strict Markov assumptions. These methods benefit from explicit alignments but struggle with long-range dependencies and context sensitivity across morphophonemic boundaries. Discriminative training further improved sequence labeling by incorporating global features and margin-based objectives (Jiampojamarn et al., 2008). Neural models, by contrast, learn distributed representations that can capture contextual cues without handcrafted features, as demonstrated by Vaswani et al., 2017) in sequence modeling. Attention mechanisms enable the decoder to focus on relevant graphemes when emitting each phoneme, mitigating the alignment bottleneck observed in earlier pipelines (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Subsequently, pointer and copy mechanisms were explored to handle rare words and orthographic anomalies in low-resource scripts (Gu et al., 2016). Multilingual transfer has emerged as a practical strategy for languages with scarce lexicons, allowing parameter sharing across related phonotactic systems (Peters et al., 2019). At the same time, pronunciation variation across dialects motivates models that condition on speaker or locale metadata to capture systematic alternations (Rao and Smolensky, 2017). Recent work also integrates lexicon priors and phonotactic constraints into decoding to reduce illegal phone sequences while preserving flexibility (Peng et al., 2020). Beyond accuracy, researchers have evaluated calibration, robustness to orthographic noise, and the ability to abstain on out-of-domain items (Guo et al., 2017). Error analyses frequently reveal confusions driven by grapheme clusters, silent letters, and morphologically conditioned alternations such as vowel reduction and devoicing (Hayes, 2009). Hybrid pipelines remain competitive when large dictionaries are available, since WFST composition and pruning yield strong baselines with transparent error modes (Novak et al., 2016). Nevertheless, sequence-to-sequence decoders have surpassed traditional aligner–predictor cascades when trained with adequate augmentation and regularization (Prasad and Ghosh, 2019). Domain adaptation techniques leverage pseudo-labeling and instance reweighting to bridge gaps between curated dictionaries and noisy web lexicons (Chan et al., 2021). For multilingual scenarios, subword sharing and language tags help disentangle orthography-specific rules from universal phonological tendencies (Johnson et al., 2017). Character-level convolutional front ends reduce sensitivity to diacritics and positional variants, complementing recurrent or transformer decoders (Zhang et al., 2015). Training objectives have expanded to include minimum edit distance loss, sequence-level knowledge distillation, and contrastive pronunciation ranking (Wiseman and Rush, 2016). In this context, the transformer architecture has become a de facto choice for large-scale G2P benchmarks, inspired by Vaswani et al. (2017) and its efficient self-attention design. Nevertheless, vanilla transformers can overfit to frequent grapheme n-grams, necessitating dropout schedules and label smoothing to maintain generalization (Müller et al., 2019). Data augmentation methods such as grapheme dropout, homograph shuffling, and noise injection have been shown to reduce substitution and deletion errors (Specia et al., 2016). To facilitate fair comparison, several studies release standardized lexicons, evaluation scripts, and train–test splits across multiple languages (Rausch et al., 2020). Cross-dataset evaluation indicates that models trained on resource-rich languages can transfer structural priors to typologically related languages with minimal fine-tuning (Ponti et al., 2019). Even so, careful error stratification by syllable structure and stress reveals persistent weaknesses on clusters and schwa epenthesis in certain families. Historical corpora offer further challenges because orthographic conventions shift over time, complicating the mapping from letters to sounds and vice versa (Piotrowski, 2012). From a reproducibility perspective, reporting tokenization, lexicon sources, and phoneme set variants is crucial to ensure meaningful aggregation of results across studies (Pineau et al., 2021). Finally, we note that instance-based G2P remains influential in pedagogy and explainability, a tradition exemplified by van den Bosch et al. and subsequent memory-based approaches. Our review therefore situates contemporary neural methods within a broader line of research that balances statistical efficiency, linguistic fidelity, and practical constraints on multilingual deployment.

document: In this paper, we study sequence-to-sequence generation with an encoder–decoder architecture grounded in self-attention. We adopt the Transformer to instantiate both encoder and decoder components (Vaswani et al., 2017 and follow a standard maximum-likelihood objective. To simplify exposition, we focus on a minimal configuration that isolates self-attention while omitting positional encodings and deep stacking. The encoder maps the input sequence X into contextual representations H using multihead self-attention and feed‑forward sublayers (Gehring et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019). The decoder conditions on H to generate outputs autoregressively with causal self-attention, cross-attention to the source, and a softmax prediction layer. At time step i, previously generated word embeddings are packed into key and value matrices by a projection function M, yielding K_{i−1} and V_{i−1}. A self-attention block produces a target-side representation s_i that summarizes the partial hypothesis and its dependencies. We then compute a context vector c_i using cross-attention over the encoder states, parameterized by key and value transformations of H. The resulting distribution P_g(y_i | y_{<i}, X) is computed by an MLP followed by normalization, and the most probable token is selected. Training maximizes the conditional likelihood of references given inputs over a corpus of paired sequences (Sutskever et al., 2014; Cho et al., 2014). For completeness, we contrast this design with recurrent and convolutional alternatives, which differ primarily in how context is accumulated over positions Bahdanau et al. 2015. In our implementation, we also report ablations that remove cross-attention to probe the extent to which the encoder contributes to fluency versus adequacy. We find that the simplified setup remains competitive on small-scale datasets while offering clearer interpretability of attention patterns (Clark et al., 2019). Nevertheless, deeper stacks and learned positional encodings generally improve performance, consistent with prior large-scale studies (Press et al., 2021; Tay et al., 2020). Overall, our formulation provides a controlled testbed for analyzing how self-attention alone supports conditional language generation.

document: Early studies on the punctuation restoration task explored a wide range of features such as lexical, acoustic, prosodic, and their combination (Gravano et al., 2009; Levy et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2014; Che et al., 2016a; Szaszák and Tündik, 2019). Graphical models such as conditional random fields were widely used for this task (Lu and Ng, 2010; Zhang et al., 2013) before the advent of neural networks. Recently, diverse deep architectures have been investigated, including long short-term memory (Gale and Parthasarathy, 2017), convolutional neural networks (Che et al., 2016b), and Transformers (Alam et al., 2020). Corpora for punctuation restoration are usually derived from automatic speech recognition outputs in domains such as meetings (McCowan et al., 2005), TED talks (Federico et al., 2012), audio books (Panayotov et al., 2015), and film subtitles (Tiedemann, 2016). Among these, the TED corpus is widely used as a benchmark, but livestreaming videos have rarely been explored for this task. Punctuation restoration in livestreaming is challenging due to latency constraints, disfluencies, and noncanonical orthography common in chats and spontaneous speech (Zayats and Ostendorf, 2019; Tilk and Alumae, 2016). Sequence labeling approaches typically predict punctuation tokens aligned to ASR word sequences, sometimes augmented with prosodic cues from the recognizer (Tilk and Alumae, 2015; Courtland et al., 2020). Encoder–decoder models with attention reframe the problem as sequence transduction, allowing insertion of punctuation independently of input timing (Yi and Tao, 2019; Chan et al., 2020). Pretrained language models further improve accuracy by injecting rich contextual knowledge into token classifiers and sequence generators, as demonstrated by Liu et al. 2019 and subsequent work (Devlin et al., 2019). Domain adaptation remains vital because punctuation conventions and lexical distributions shift across domains and languages (Sun et al., 2019; Gururangan et al., 2020). Semi-supervised and self-training strategies mitigate data scarcity by leveraging large quantities of unlabeled ASR text (Chen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2022). Multimodal approaches that incorporate acoustic-prosodic embeddings continue the line of early feature-based work while benefiting from modern fusion techniques (Povey et al., 2016; Rosenberg et al., 2015). Real-time applications additionally require confidence estimation and incremental decoding to avoid visible flicker in captions (Kreutzer et al., 2019; Karita et al., 2019). Finally, standardized evaluation across domains is hindered by inconsistent preprocessing, punctuation inventories, and reference normalization practices (Tilk and Alumae, 2016; Courtland et al., 2020). To address these gaps, we propose a comprehensive study of punctuation restoration for livestreaming content, evaluating latency–quality tradeoffs and cross-domain generalization on newly curated datasets.

document: Vision and Language Learning research investigates how textual and visual signals jointly support understanding across tasks. Early benchmarks emphasized object recognition and attribute classification to probe local perception. The earliest datasets mostly focus on simple local object properties identification (Antol et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2016). Later work shifted toward compositional reasoning with synthetic scenes and controlled language. For example, Suhr et al. (2017) and Johnson et al. (2017) use synthetic images or synthetic language to study spatial relations. Recently, datasets using real images and real languages such as Hudson and Manning (2019) and Liu et al. (2021) were proposed for grounded reasoning. However, many resources still emphasize local grounding over open-ended and world-aware inference. This limitation motivates models that connect entities and events within and beyond the visible scene. Cross-task regularities further suggest that shared representations can benefit diverse applications (Lu et al., 2020). In this paper, we examine CLIP as a transferable aligner between text and vision (Radford et al., 2021). CLIP offers strong zero-shot performance after contrastive pretraining on large-scale image–text pairs drawn from the web. We analyze how such pretrained alignments interact with structured reasoning in the TARA dataset. Complementary efforts in temporal and spatial information extraction provide tools for linking mentions to timelines and locations. Foundational work addressed temporal expressions and event ordering inside documents (Uz-Zaman et al., 2013; Ning et al., 2018). Subsequent studies expanded context windows to incorporate cross-sentence signals and document-level cues (Zhou et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021). Parallel lines of research explored spatial grounding with toponyms and geocoding resources (Gritta et al., 2018; Kulkarni et al., 2020). Integrating these strands with vision provides a route to richer situational understanding. Recent multimodal architectures leverage attention to fuse linguistic and visual evidence at multiple layers. Transformer-based encoders have become standard for joint modeling due to scalability and expressiveness (Vaswani et al., 2017. Foundational conceptual advances are typically attributed to seminal work that reconceptualized sequence modeling as attention-driven. We also note that pretraining objectives shape the degree of visual commonsense available to downstream tasks. Contrastive alignments support retrieval-like matching and robust captioning when paired with prompt design. In parallel, detection-oriented pretraining can refine region-level grounding for referring expressions and VQA. Despite strong retrieval performance, cross-modal models often underperform at fine-grained temporal anchoring. Linking events to time requires structured inference over partially observed narratives. Temporal QA benchmarks reveal brittleness when answers depend on implicit duration or relative ordering cues. Spatial IE faces analogous issues when place names are ambiguous or when images depict only a subset of the described scene. Hybrid systems therefore combine neural representations with symbolic constraints to improve faithfulness. Distant supervision and weak labels remain central for scaling annotations in both text and vision. However, discrepancies between web distributions and curated evaluation sets can induce shortcut learning. We mitigate this risk by enforcing evaluation protocols that separate entity overlap from true reasoning generalization. Our experiments compare zero-shot prompting, linear probing, and lightweight adapters over unified backbones. The results indicate that careful interface design between modules yields significant improvements on compositional queries.

Automatic evaluation metrics are most commonly used to argue that one summarization system is better than another, typically by showing that the value of a metric improves with the "better" system. How similarly automatic metrics replicate human judgments of system quality is quantified by system-level correlations as follows.

The summaries from N systems on M jud input documents are judged by humans Z and scored with an automatic metric X . Then, the systemlevel correlation between X and Z is calculated as

where x j i and z j i are the scores of X and Z for the summary produced by the i-th system on the j-th input document and CORR is some correlation function. See Fig. for an illustration of this calculation.

In this work, we use Kendall's τ (the "b" vari-Figure : The system-level correlation is calculated between the average X and Z scores on a set of summarization systems. x j i and z j i are the scores for the summary produced by system i (represented by rows) on input document j (represented by columns).

ant 2 ) as the correlation function because we are most concerned with a metric's ability to correctly determine whether one system is better than another since that is how metrics are used in practice. Kendall's τ is computed based on the number of system pairs out of N 2 which are ranked the same by X and Z. It is defined as

where P and Q are the number of pairs ranked the same or different by X and Z, respectively, and T and U are the number of ties only in X or Z, respectively.

Because the computation of r SYS involves randomness -its value depends on which M jud input documents (and even which N systems) were used -it is only an approximation of the true correlation between X and Z. As such, Deutsch et al. (2021b) proposed various methods for calculating confidence intervals for r SYS . For instance, their BOOT-INPUTS method uses bootstrapping to repeatedly resample the M jud input documents used to calculate r SYS , thereby calculating a confidence interval for the true r SYS value for X and Z.

While Pearson and Spearman correlations are sometimes reported, they are less sensitive to pairwise order errors in small N settings. Moreover, system-level analyses can be confounded by domain shift across the chosen Mjud documents, which motivates stratified resampling. We therefore complement BOOT-INPUTS with a system-bootstrap that resamples systems alongside documents to quantify selection variance. This approach enables fairer comparisons when research communities evaluate only a subset of available models. Prior meta-analyses report similar patterns across tasks, including summarization, translation, and dialogue. For example, evidence from machine translation suggests that rank stability improves as Mjud grows beyond a few dozen test items Deutsch et al., 2021b) and that confidence intervals narrow sublinearly with additional data. In summarization, recent shared tasks emphasize reporting uncertainty alongside point estimates to discourage overclaiming minor gains. Following this practice, all results in our study include 95% intervals and significance tests tailored to correlated system outputs. Finally, we discuss practical guidelines for metric reporting to reduce misinterpretation and enhance reproducibility in future benchmark releases.

document: Contextualized word embedding vectors are a major driver for improved performance of deep learning models on many NLU tasks. Initially, ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and ULMFit (Howard and Ruder, 2018) introduced contextualized word embedding frameworks by training LSTM-based models on massive amounts of texts. The linguistic quality encoded in these models was demonstrated over six NLU tasks including question answering, textual entailment, semantic role labeling, coreference resolution, named entity extraction, and sentiment analysis. The next big leap was obtained with the introduction of the GPT-1 framework by Radford and Sutskever (2018). Instead of using LSTM layers, GPT is based on 12 layers of Transformer decoders, with each decoder layer composed of a 768-dimensional feed-forward block and 12 self-attention heads. Devlin et al. (2019) followed along the same lines as GPT and implemented Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, or BERT in short. BERT attends to input tokens in both forward and backward directions while optimizing a masked language modeling objective and a next sentence prediction objective. Following Devlin et al. we adopt widely used evaluation conventions. An integral part of developing pre-trained language models is the use of multitask benchmarks to demonstrate broad linguistic abilities. English BERT models are typically evaluated on three major benchmarks. The Stanford Question Answering Dataset (SQuAD) (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) is used to test paragraph-level reading comprehension abilities. The GLUE benchmark aggregates a diverse and relatively hard set of sentence and sentence-pair tasks (Wang et al., 2018). The SWAG dataset presents models with partial descriptions of grounded situations to test their ability for commonsense reasoning (Zellers et al., 2018). While these resources catalyzed progress in English, they are less directly applicable to morphologically rich or low-resource languages. When evaluating Hebrew PLMs, one key pitfall is the absence of Hebrew versions for the standard benchmarks. Furthermore, none of the suggested benchmarks explicitly account for examining model capacity under limited supervision. In particular, there is no accepted way to evaluate word-internal morphological structures that are inherent to MRLs and to Hebrew. To support languages beyond English and Chinese, Devlin et al. (2019) trained a multilingual BERT model covering over one hundred languages. The hope was that low-resource languages would benefit from linguistic information obtained from high-resource corpora. In reality, however, mBERT performance on specific languages has not matched its English results. Subsequent work proposed language-specific tokenizers, subword vocabularies, and domain-adaptive pretraining to narrow this gap. For Hebrew, character segmentation, templatic morphology, and orthographic variation complicate downstream learning even with subword models. Prior studies reported strong gains from task-adaptive pretraining on in-domain Hebrew corpora, but reproducibility remains uneven across tasks. Several benchmark design decisions were later consolidated (in Wang et al., 2018), but cross-lingual generalization remained underexplored. Recent evaluations further indicate that multilingual models can overfit to shared scripts without mastering language-specific morphology. These observations motivate targeted datasets that isolate morphology, syntax, and semantics for controlled diagnosis. In addition, transfer protocols must be standardized to compare monolingual and multilingual PLMs fairly. We therefore outline an evaluation suite that stresses morphological decomposition alongside sentence-level inference. Our design emphasizes task diversity, controlled training sizes, and transparent reporting to facilitate reliable comparisons. We also incorporate ablations on tokenizer choices, continued pretraining, and judicious initialization strategies. Finally, we argue that future work should link intrinsic probes to extrinsic metrics to avoid misleading conclusions. By unifying these considerations, the community can better characterize where PLMs excel and where they fall short. In this context, Hebrew serves as a demanding testbed that exposes the limits of crosslingual representations and evaluation practices.

document:
Knowledge can be elicited from pretrained language models. Numerous works have shown that pretrained language models implicitly contain a large amount of knowledge that can be queried via conditional generation (Davison et al., 2019; Petroni et al., 2019). Consequently, these models can directly perform inference on tasks like commonsense reasoning (Trinh and Le, 2018), text classification (Shin et al., 2020; Puri and Catanzaro, 2019), and natural language inference (Shin et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Inspired by these observations, we elicit question-related knowledge in an explicit form from language models and use them to guide the inference.

Leveraging external knowledge for commonsense reasoning. Some work uses external commonsense knowledge bases to make improvements on various NLP tasks, including commonsense reasoning. One approach is to inject commonsense knowledge into language models, either by pretraining on knowledge bases (Ma et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2019; Zhong et al., 2019) or finetuning the model so that it can reason with additional retrieved knowledge (Chang et al., 2020; Mitra et al., 2019; Bian et al., 2021). Another direction is to ground the question into a knowledge graph and do inference with graph-based reasoning (Lin et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2020; Yasunaga et al., 2021). A common prerequisite of these methods is a high-quality, high-coverage, in-domain commonsense knowledge base, as noted in (Ma et al., 2019). Some commonsense reasoning datasets are derived from existing knowledge bases; for example, CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al., 2019) is derived from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), and Social IQA (Sap et al., 2019b) is derived from ATOMIC (Sap et al., 2019a). For such datasets, it is natural to elicit related knowledge from the underlying knowledge base that derived them, and typically this would demonstrate considerable gains (Mitra et al., 2019; Chang et al., 2020). However, if there is a domain mismatch between the dataset and the knowledge base, such gains tend to diminish (Mitra et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2019). This becomes a bottleneck when encountering datasets that have no suitable knowledge base (e.g., NumerSense and CommonsenseQA 2.0 (Talmor et al., 2021)), or when the system needs to handle commonsense queries that do not fit in any of the commonsense domains represented by an existing knowledge base. Our work overcomes this difficulty by leveraging pretrained language models as the source of commonsense knowledge. The first row of each section is the original question and the inference results associated with it; the second row is a model-generated knowledge statement that prompts the inference model. We show correct answers in green, incorrect answers in red, and their corresponding scores assigned by the inference model.

Adding generated text during inference. Recently, several works show that model performance on commonsense reasoning can be boosted by augmenting the question with model-generated text, such as clarifications, explanations, and implications. Self-talk (Shwartz et al., 2020 elicits clarifications to concepts in the question and appends them to the inference model input. Contrastive explanations (Paranjape et al., 2021) prompts inference models with generated explanations that contrast between two answer choices. The aforementioned methods depend on task-specific templates to inquire the generator, which means they are only capable of eliciting a limited variety of knowledge and require careful hand-crafting to transfer to new tasks. Other explanation-based methods (Latcinnik and Berant, 2020; Rajani et al., 2019) finetune the generator model so that it produces explanations that are used for question augmentation. DynaGen uses pretrained commonsense models to generate implications of a question and expands the inference input with these generations. However, its usage of COMeT (Bosselut et al., 2019) as the generator confines its applicability to the social commonsense domain. Our work contributes to this general line of research, yet different from these previous methods that elicit knowledge with task-specific templates or from finetuned knowledge generators, our method requires only a few human-written demonstrations in the style of the task, making it much more flexible, easy-to-transfer, and engineering-efficient.

document: Language models trained on private data suffer privacy risks of revealing sensitive information. Previous research has mainly considered black-box attacks that assume attackers only have access to inputs and outputs of language models. Carlini et al. (2021) performed a black-box model inversion attack on GPT-2 through descriptive prompts with beam search. Lehman et al. (2021) examined BERT pretrained on Electronic Health Records via blank filling and model probing to recover Personal Health Information. Furthermore, given black-box access to a language model's pre-train and finetune stages, Zanella-Béguelin et al. (2020) showed that sensitive sequences of the fine-tuning dataset can be extracted. For the distributed client-server setup, Malekzadeh et al. (2021) considered sensitive attribute leakage from the server side with honest-but-curious classifiers. For LM-based chatbots, training conversations are prone to include more private attributes than other commonly used corpora such as BooksCorpus Zhu et al., 2015) and Wikipedia. Tigunova et al. (2019) proposed the Hidden Attribute Model to extract professions and genders of speakers from dialog datasets, and further applied Attribute Extractor to generate attribute triplets and suggest downstream tasks. Pan et al. (2020) exploited embeddings of language models to recover inputs' digits and keywords. Though their setup is superficially similar to ours, they considered only simple cases of data recovery with fixed rules and suffered substantial utility degradation to obtain strong defense performance. In contrast, our work targets inferring more complex private attributes from embeddings without fixed input patterns while proposing defenses with minimal utility impact.
