Recent years, have seen a wide range of methods have witnessed to expand knowledge transfer of transformer-based (Vaswani et al., 2017) NLU models beyond logits matching. DistillBERT (Sanh et al., 2019a) added a cosine similarity loss between teacher and student embeddings layers. Tiny-BERT (Jiao et al., 2019), MobileBERT (Sun et al., 2020b), and MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b) matched the intermediate layers representations and selfattention distributions of the teacher and the student.

In PKD, Sun et al. (2019) used deterministic mapping strategies to distill a 12-layer BERT teacher to a 6-layer BERT student. PKD-LAST and PKD-SKIP refer to matching layers {1 − 5} of the student with layers {7 − 11} and {2, 4, 6, 8, 10} of the teacher respectively. However, these works ignored the impact of layer selection, as they used a fixed layer-wise mapping. 2 Researchers have found that tuning the layer mapping scheme can significantly improve the performance of ILD techniques (Sun et al., 2019). Nevertheless, finding the optimal mapping can be challenging, which is referred to as the layer skip and search problems by . To address the layer skip problem, CKD (Wu et al., 2020a) is built on top of PKD by partitioning all the intermediate layers of the teacher to the number of student layers. Then, the combined representation of the layers of each partition is distilled into a number of subset corresponding to the number of student layers. However, finding the optimal partitioning scheme requires running exhaustive experiments. Given teacher and student BERT models with n and m layers respectively (where n >> m), it is not trivial to choose the teacher layers that can be incorporated in the distillation process and how we should map them to the student layers (search). ALP-KD overcomes this issue by computing attention weights between each student layer and all the intermediate layers of the teacher. The learned attention weights for each student layer are used to obtain a weighted representation of all teacher layers. Although ALP-KD has shown promising results on 12-layer BERT-based compression, attending to all layers of the teacher adds considerable computational overhead to the training phase. This can become computationally prohibitive when scaling to very large models such as RoBERTa-large or GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019). Alternatively, CODIR (Sun et al., 2020a) exploited contrastive learning (Tian et al., 2019) to perform intermediate layers matching between the teacher and the student models with no deterministic mapping. Similar to ALP-KD, this approach also requires excessive training time due to the contrastive loss calculation and the use of negative samples from a memory bank.

Table summarizes the main characteristics of the existing state-of-the-art intermediate layer distillation techniques (PKD (Sun et al., 2019), CKD (Wu et al., 2020a), and CoDIR) used for pre-trained language models compared with our proposed RAIL-KD. First, PKD and CKD treat the mapping as an extra hyperparameter that requires extensive experiments in order to find the optimal mapping. Second, ALP-KD and CoDIR (Sun et al., 2020a use the attention mechanism and contrastive learning respectively to address the issue, but at the expense of extra computational cost.

Our proposed RAIL-KD method does not add any computational cost to the distillation process, while empirically outperforming previous methods. For instance, RAIL-KD is roughly twice faster than CoDIR in a 24 to 6 layer compression. In addition, it does not require extensive experiments to find the optimal mapping scheme. In this work, we position ourselves to works that tackle the skip and search problem 3 . Otherwise said, we don't compare with works like TinyBERT (Jiao et al., 2019) or MiniLM (Wang et al., 2020b), which use extra losses like self-attention distribution matching. However, we expect that these methods, as well as state-of-the-art (Rashid et al., 2021; He et al., 2021) ones can take full advantage of RAIL-KD, since they use a deterministic layer mapping scheme.

We evaluate RAIL-KD across general language understanding tasks to contextualize gains beyond individual datasets. Prior work typically reports results on GLUE, where improvements from intermediate-layer tricks translate into higher scores with minimal degradation in calibration (Wang et al., 2019). To probe robustness, we also consider out-of-domain setups such as MNLI mismatched and question answering transfer from SQuAD to NewsQA (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Trischler et al., 2017). Compression often harms calibration and uncertainty estimates, a phenomenon mitigated by representation-aware distillation that preserves teacher geometry (Guo et al., 2017; Minderer et al., 2021). Our approach further complements token-level supervision such as soft-label KD and attention transfer without requiring architectural changes (Hinton et al., 2015; Zagoruyko and Komodakis, 2017). In ablations, we find that mapping adaptivity contributes more than additional losses, particularly in deeper teachers like DeBERTa (He et al., 2021). We release code and checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparisons across compression budgets. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations around energy consumption and model deployment, emphasizing that efficiency gains should not come at the expense of transparency or fairness.

Revealing affirmative interpretations from negations is a challenging endeavor. In the literature, researchers primarily seek to identify scope and focus of negation. The creation of the BioScope and ConanDoyle-Neg (Morante and Daelemans, 2012 corpora spearheaded research on scope detection (Morante and Daelemans, 2009), and the PB-FOC (Blanco and Moldovan, 2011) corpus. The main goal of this paper is to find these affirmative counterparts to generate affirmative interpretations.

More related to the work presented here, Sarabi et al. (2019) present a corpus of negations and their underlying affirmative interpretations (they call them positive interpretations). We are inspired by them but bypass several of their limitations. First, they only work with negations from Simple Wikipedia, a site devoted to English learners. As a result, their corpus uses (relatively) unsophisticated vocabulary and grammar. Second, they impose several restrictions on the negations they work with (e.g., negation cue modifies root verb, sentences between 6 and 25 tokens and not including certain tokens (because, until, etc.)). Third, their affirmative interpretations are restricted to a rephrasing of the statement containing negation with only one change: an argument of the negated predicate. In contrast, we barely impose restrictions on the negations we work with (no questions and no auxiliary verbs). More importantly, we introduce a questiondriven approach that allow us to obtain multiple affirmative interpretations with increasing degrees of complexity (see examples in Table 1).

Recently, Jiang et al. (2021) study the problem of identifying commonsense implications of negations and contradictions. More specifically, they work with if-then rules such as If X does not learn new things, then X does not gain new knowledge and If X does not leave the building, then X stays in the building. These rules capture general commonsense knowledge about what happens if an event does not occur. Unlike them, we work with naturally occurring sentences that include negated predicate-argument structures with many arguments (agent, theme, manner, time, etc.). In addition, our affirmative interpretations reveal that predicates that are grammatically negated are actually factual (but with different arguments). Our dataset construction pipeline combines automated retrieval with human validation to ensure both coverage and precision. We further outline evaluation metrics and baseline models to situate our contributions within the broader literature on negation understanding.

document: Summarization systems can be generally categorized into two paradigms: extractive and abstractive. Extractive systems extract certain sentences and clauses from input, for example, based on salient features (Zhou and Rush, 2019) or feature construction (He et al., 2012). Abstraction systems generate new utterances as the summary, e.g., by sequence-to-sequence models trained in a supervised way Zhang et al., 2020). Recently, unsupervised abstractive summarization is attracting increasing attention. For example, Yang et al. (2020) propose to use the Lead baseline (first several sentences) as the pseudo-groundtruth. However, such an approach only works with well-structured articles (such as CNN/DailyMail). Wang and Lee (2018) and Baziotis et al. (2019) use cycle consistency for unsupervised summarization. Zhou and Rush (2019) propose a step-by-step decomposable scoring function and perform beam search to generate summaries. Schumann et al. (2020) propose an edit-based local search approach, which allows a more comprehensive scoring function and outperforms cycle consistency and beam search. Our paper follows Schumann et al. (2020) but trains a machine learning model to improve efficiency and smooth out search noise. Previously, we fine-tuned a GPT-2 model based on search results for unsupervised paraphrasing. We extend previous work in a non-trivial way by designing a non-autoregressive generator and further proposing a length-control decoding algorithm. Non-autoregressive generation was originally proposed for machine translation (Gu et al., 2018). Recently, Jia et al. (2021) apply non-autoregressive models to extractive document-level summarization. Su et al. (2021) stack a non-autoregressive BERT model with a conditional random field (CRF) for abstractive summarization; since the summary is shorter than the input text, their approach puts multiple end-to-sequence (EOS) tokens at the end of the sentence, and thus is unable to utilize the strong input–output correspondence in the summarization task. Yang et al. (2021) apply auxiliary part-of-speech (POS) loss and explore pretraining strategies for encoder–decoder non-autoregressive summarization; their length is given by POS tag/EOS predictions. All these studies concern supervised summarization, and none can explicitly control the output length. By contrast, our paper focuses on unsupervised summarization. We adopt CTC training in our encoder-only architecture, allowing blank tokens to better align input and output words, which is more appropriate for summarization. We further propose a dynamic programming algorithm to control the summary length. Unsupervised methods typically rely on self-training, reconstruction losses, or pseudo-parallel data distilled from large unlabelled corpora. They often struggle to preserve factual consistency without teacher signals. We address this by integrating a denoising objective that penalizes deletions of salient named entities. In addition, we incorporate content selection priors estimated from TF–IDF and topic models. Classic abstractive baselines demonstrate the limitations of pointer and coverage mechanisms (Narayan et al., 2018; See et al., 2017. Controllable summarization further enables users to steer style, focus, and length through simple constraints (Fan et al., 2018; He et al., 2020). Our length-control decoding computes a constrained best path under the CTC alignment lattice. This allows the generator to meet exact token budgets without retraining for each target length. Unlike prior compression-based approaches, our model can insert or paraphrase phrases when beneficial. We evaluate on CNN/DailyMail, XSum, and Reddit-TIFU without using any reference summaries during training. Evaluation uses ROUGE, BERTScore, and human judgments of coherence and faithfulness. The proposed search-aware training halves inference cost compared with iterative local search while improving quality. Ablations show that CTC alignment and length-control both contribute substantially to gains. Error analysis reveals that most failures stem from rare entity drift and unresolved coreference. We release code and model checkpoints to facilitate reproducibility and further research. In summary, our study bridges search-based unsupervised objectives with non-autoregressive generation for practical, controllable summarization.

document: Probing pretrained language models. The unprecedented capability of modern PLMs to provide rich contextualized input representations has taken the NLP community by storm. Alongside the raising wave of successes collected by PLMs in an ever increasing number of areas, researchers started to question and investigate what happens inside these models and what they really capture, probing for knowledge and linguistic properties (Hewitt and Manning, 2019; Chi et al., 2020; Vulić et al., 2020). This body of work quickly attracted increasing attention and grew to become a field of study with a name of its own: BERTology (Rogers et al., 2020). Probing a PLM usually consists in defining a very precise task, e.g. identifying whether two words are linked by a syntactic or a semantic relation, and then in designing and training a simple model, called probe, to solve the task using the contextualized representations provided by the PLM. The idea is to design a probe that is as simple as possible, often a single-layer model: if the probe is able to address the task, then it must be thanks to the contextual information captured by the PLM since the expressiveness of the probe is limited by its simplicity. One could argue that some complex relations may require a non-linear probe (White et al., 2021) which can reveal hidden information as long as it is accompanied by control experiments (Hewitt and Liang, 2019) to verify that such nonlinear probe is still extracting information from the underlying PLM rather than learning to solve the probing task. Over the past few years, these probing techniques have been used to great effect and revealed that PLMs have been "rediscovering" the classical NLP pipeline (Tenney et al., 2019), and that they often encode distances between syntactic constituents (Hewitt and Liang, 2019), lexical relations (Vulić et al., 2020) and morphology (Chi et al., 2020), inter alia. Probing techniques for SRL. As in several other fields of NLP, recent studies tried to shed some light on how, where and to what extent PLMs encode information relevant to SRL. Among others, Tenney et al. (2019) devised an edge probing mechanism with the aim of understanding the capability of BERT to identify which semantic role ties a given predicate to a given argument span, showing that such task is "solved" mainly by the middle layers of BERT. Recent advances in SRL. Thanks to their effectiveness, PLMs are now the de facto input representation method in SRL (Conia and Navigli, 2020; Blloshmi et al., 2021). Recently proposed approaches have achieved impressive results on several gold benchmarks (Hajič et al., 2009; Pradhan et al., 2012), both in span-based and in dependency-based SRL, but also in multilingual and cross-lingual SRL, even though there still seems to be a significant margin of improvement on out-of-domain settings. At the same time, work on task-specific adapters has examined whether parameter-efficient tuning alters the locus of role-related information across transformer layers. Complementary lines of research explore counterfactual data augmentation to disentangle surface cues from true role semantics, with mixed evidence on generalization benefits reported on benchmarks Hajič et al., 2009; Pradhan et al., 2012) across domains. Several studies emphasize the importance of control baselines such as random-label probes and matched-capacity architectures to avoid misattributing gains to the probe itself. Evaluation protocols have also diversified to include out-of-domain diagnostics and challenging compositions, for example roles realized by discontinuous or implicit arguments common in newswire and biomedical text. Despite this progress, understanding how probing signals translate into deployable SRL improvements remains an open question that motivates our investigation.

document: Discourse structure. Our work is closely related to functional structures defined through content types explored in other domains; prior work has affirmed the usefulness of these structures in downstream NLP tasks. In news, Choubey et al. (2020) adopted Van Dijk (2013)'s content schema cataloging events (e.g., main event, anecdotal), which they showed to improve the performance of event coreference resolution. In scientific writing, content types (e.g., background, methodology) are shown to be useful for summarization (Teufel and Moens, 2002;Cohan et al., 2018), information extraction (Mizuta et al., 2006;Liakata et al., 2012), and information retrieval (Kircz, 1991;Liddy, 1991). The discourse structure of argumentative texts (e.g., support, rebuttal) (Peldszus and Stede, 2013;Becker et al., 2016;Stab and Gurevych, 2017) has also been applied on argumentation mining. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has studied the discourse structure of long-form answers. Question Answering. Recent work by Cao 2021 has investigated the ontology of questions, which includes comparison questions, verification questions, judgement questions, etc. We construct the ontology of functional roles of answer sentences. One of the roles in our ontology is summary, yielding an extractive summarization dataset. This shares motivation with a line of work studying query-focused summarization (Xu and Lapata, 2020). Lastly, our work builds up on two datasets containing long form answers (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019;Fan et al., 2019) and extends the analysis of long form answers from earlier studies (Krishna et al., 2021). Prior studies on answer paragraph structure in community forums emphasize coherence and redundancy control (Fabbri et al., 2021). Long-form question answering also requires modeling discourse cues to guide planning and aggregation of evidence (Fan et al., 2019). Conventional extractive QA focuses on sentence or span selection without explicitly modeling functional roles (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). In contrast, our perspective treats an answer as a sequence of role-labeled segments that jointly satisfy the information need. This view aligns with research on rhetorical zoning in scientific articles, where structured sequences correlate with reader comprehension (Teufel and Moens, 2002). Beyond rhetorical zones, recent work integrates discourse markers into neural summarizers to improve salience estimation (Liu and Lapata, 2019). We hypothesize that explicit role supervision can serve as an inductive bias for controllable answer generation. To situate our approach, we review taxonomies of sentence roles proposed for educational and biomedical corpora (Liakata et al., 2012). Argument mining has similarly benefited from role-aware representations for claims, premises, and counterarguments (Stab and Gurevych, 2017). In information seeking dialogues, role modeling improves turn-level relevance and grounding (Zhang et al., 2018). Our work differs by targeting single-turn, retrieval-augmented answering with long outputs. We frame role detection as a supervised labeling task that can be pre-trained and transferred across domains. We then use the predicted roles to regularize content selection and ordering during generation. This pipeline complements retriever improvements that prioritize diverse and aspect-specific evidence (Xiong et al., 2021). It also interfaces with neural content planning methods that outline key points before realization (Hua and Wang, 2020). From a data perspective, the availability of open-domain QA resources provides a testbed for our analysis (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). While SQuAD and similar datasets emphasize short factual answers, they inform retrieval components that we reuse (Rajpurkar et al., 2016;Nguyen et al., 2016). For evaluation, long-form metrics such as coverage, non-redundancy, and discourse completeness complement n-gram overlap (Kryscinski et al., 2019). We additionally analyze human preferences over role structure to assess controllability and answer utility. Prior findings suggest that introductory summaries and concluding takeaways are especially valued by non-expert readers (Liu et al., 2022). To connect with explainability, we consider how role tags expose the rationale for including cited evidence (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). Our approach is orthogonal to advances in parameter-efficient adaptation that reduce fine-tuning cost on QA tasks (Houlsby et al., 2019). Finally, we highlight open challenges in cross-domain generalization of role labels and ambiguity between roles such as background and context (Peldszus and Stede, 2013). These observations motivate a unified framework for modeling discourse roles in long-form answers and evaluating their impact on user-centered quality.

document: Table-based fact verification (TB-FV) seeks to determine whether a textual claim is supported by structured evidence in tables. Unlike passage retrieval, TB-FV must align schema elements, aggregate cells, and execute latent programs to derive evidentiary statements. Early pipelines integrated table parsing with symbolic solvers, but they struggled with coverage and brittleness under noisy claims (Pasupat and Liang, 2015). Neural program induction later improved compositional reasoning by learning soft operators for selection, aggregation, and comparison (Neelakantan et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2017). In (Zhong et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020) models often predict executable operations to justify verdicts over heterogeneous tables. Recent systems combine program sketches with neural execution to improve faithfulness while retaining differentiability (Chen et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2020). This line of work emphasizes numerical and logical reasoning to reduce spurious correlations and shortcut cues (Aly et al., 2021). At the same time, pre-trained language models augmented with table encoders have improved retrieval and entailment when paired with lightweight reasoning modules (Herzig et al., 2020; Eisenschlos et al., 2020). Nevertheless, generalization across domains, schemas, and out-of-distribution numbers remains a central challenge for TB-FV (Oren et al., 2022). To address robustness, some studies adopt mixture-of-experts architectures that route inputs to specialized experts for arithmetic, comparison, or lookup skills (Shazeer et al., 2017; Fedus et al., 2022). Classical formulations encourage competition among experts through gating and auxiliary load-balancing losses to prevent expert collapse (Jacobs et al., 1991; Shazeer et al., 2017). Recent sparse MoE transformers scale parameters while maintaining manageable inference cost via top-k routing and capacity constraints (Lepikhin et al., 2021; Zoph et al., 2022). For TB-FV, expert specialization aligns naturally with decomposed program steps, enabling interpretable routing and modular training (Liu et al., 2022). However, naively combining experts can propagate brittle behaviors unless the router is calibrated on failure modes and numerical edge cases (Wortsman et al., 2022). We therefore investigate a self-adaptive MoE that learns to weight experts based on instance-level signals derived from program traces and verification confidence. Our approach builds on competitive learning while introducing curriculum-aware routing that gradually increases expert diversity during training (Bengio et al., 2009; Zhou et al., 2022). We further relate our design to hybrid neuro-symbolic verifiers that interleave soft execution with discrete constraints for faithful rationales (Das et al., 2019; Arabshahi et al., 2021). Empirically, we show improvements on benchmark TB-FV datasets with stronger compositional generalization and calibrated uncertainty over verdicts (Rebuffi et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2022).

Seed-Guided Topic Discovery. Seed-guided topic models aim to leverage user-provided seeds to discover underlying topics according to users' interests. Early studies take LDA (Blei et al., 2003) as the backbone and incorporate seeds into model learning. For example, Andrzejewski et al. (2009) consider must-link and cannot-link constraints among seeds as priors. SeededLDA (Jagarlamudi et al., 2012) encourages topics to contain more seeds and encourages documents to select topics related to the seeds they contain. Anchored CorEx (Gallagher et al., 2017) extracts maximally informative topics by jointly compressing the corpus and preserving seed relevant informa-tion. Recent studies start to utilize embedding techniques to learn better word semantics. For example, CatE (Meng et al., 2020) explicitly encourages distinction among retrieved topics via categoryname guided embedding learning. However, all these models require the provided seeds to be invocabulary, mainly because they focus on the input corpus only and are not equipped with general knowledge of PLMs.

Embedding-Based Topic Discovery. A number of studies extend LDA to involve word embedding. The common strategy is to adapt distributions in LDA to generate real-valued data (e.g., Gaussian LDA (Das et al., 2015), LFTM (Nguyen et al., 2015), Spherical HDP (Batmanghelich et al., 2016), andCGTM (Xun et al., 2017b)). Some other studies think out of the LDA backbone. For example, TWE (Liu et al., 2015) uses topic structures to jointly learn topic embeddings and improve word embeddings. CLM (Xun et al., 2017a) collaboratively improves topic modeling and word embedding by coordinating global and local contexts. ETM (Dieng et al., 2020) models word-topic correlations via word embeddings to improve the expressiveness of topic models. More recently, Sia et al. (2020) show that directly clustering word embeddings (e.g., word2vec or BERT) also generates good topics; Thompson and Mimno (2020) further find that BERT and GPT-2 discover high quality topics, but RoBERTa does not. These models are unsupervised and hard to be applied to seed-guided settings. In contrast, our SEETOPIC framework jointly leverages PLMs, word embeddings, and seed information.

Despite these advances, bridging seed semantics and corpus evidence remains nontrivial. Seeds can be polysemous, sparse, or noisy, and naive expansion can dilute topical coherence. In (Chen et al., 2018; Wang, 2017), such heuristics were shown to bias topic priors. Recent neural approaches exploit contextual encoders to disambiguate seed meanings across documents. Pre-trained language models can supply background knowledge that compensates for missing in-vocabulary seeds. However, aligning PLM representations with topic interpretability still requires careful objectives and constraints. Our framework adopts lightweight supervision while preserving the transparency valued by classical topic models.

Weak supervision and constraints have long been used to steer unsupervised models toward user intent. Posterior regularization and generalized expectation criteria exemplify principled mechanisms for such control. Constraint-driven methods can encode lexicon, ontology, or structure, thereby improving precision on narrow domains. Yet over-constraining models often yields brittle solutions that fail to generalize beyond the seed lists. For recent assessments of clustering-based topic induction with contextual encoders, see (Sia et al., 2020 for a detailed comparison. Complementary efforts introduce seed scoring schemes that attenuate the influence of ambiguous terms. Others propose interactive refinement loops in which users iteratively adjust seeds and receive model feedback.

We position our contribution within this line of research and target a pragmatic balance between guidance and flexibility. Concretely, we integrate seed priors with representation learning, combine local and global signals, and report robust gains on diverse corpora. The resulting method preserves interpretability while enabling discovery of salient, user-aligned topics at scale.

document: While research into automatic cloze test generation is vast (Mostow et al., 2017; Kurdi et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021), work on open cloze tests for language learning is comparatively scarce. As shown by Pino et al., open cloze questions can be generated using sample sentences from a learners' dictionary guided by linguistic criteria. A later version of their system adds hints for gapped words to support learners' inference (Pino and Eskenazi, 2009). Exercise Maker is a rule-based open source system that emulates exam-style items based on frequently tested words (Malafeev, 2014). Evaluations indicate that many of its suggested gaps are useful and that the resulting exercises are hard to distinguish from authentic tests. For phrasal verbs, news-derived sentences have been used to create gap–answer pairs by identifying particle verbs (Chinkina et al., 2017). Similarly, gaps have been selected by part of speech to practice function words such as articles and prepositions (Soonklang et al., 2017). From a modeling perspective, LSTMs have been used to decide where to insert a single gap within a sentence (Marrese-Taylor et al., 2018. Beyond item generation, several studies aim to control the difficulty of gaps so that they align with target proficiency levels. For example, C-tests have been manipulated by varying the position and length of gaps to modulate difficulty (Grotjahn et al., 2002). A related line of work predicts item difficulty with machine-learning models that correlate with CEFR levels (Settles et al., 2020; McCarthy et al., 2021). In such adaptive settings, tests are dynamically tailored to the examinee during the session to maintain an appropriate challenge level. Complementarily, controlling gap entropy has been shown to support designing tests that target different CEFR bands (Felice and Buttery, 2019). Our work extends this literature by modeling the selection of a full set of paragraph-level gaps that satisfy design and testing principles. To this end, we adapt transformer-based models and evaluate them with both automatic metrics and human judgments to assess test quality.

document: Data augmentation is a central technique for improving robustness in NLP. Early methods emphasized token-level substitutions guided by lexical resources. For instance, synonym replacement showed gains on small models (Zhang et al., 2015). Subsequent work explored retrieval-based swaps such as KNN substitution (Wang and Yang, 2015; Vijayaraghavan et al., 2016). Frequency-aware replacements including TF-IDF and uniform sampling were also studied (Xie et al., 2020a). Neural generators further enabled context-sensitive paraphrases and entity edits (Kobayashi, 2018; Raiman and Miller, 2017; Miao et al., 2020; Yue and Zhou, 2020). EDA combined simple heuristics and proved competitive on small datasets (Wei and Zou, 2019). Back-translation became a staple for sequence tasks and classification alike (Fadaee et al., 2017; Sennrich et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2018). However, these methods often yield diminishing returns when paired with large pretrained encoders (Longpre et al., 2020). Beyond surface edits, augmentation in representation space has gained traction (Zhang et al., 2018a; Guo et al., 2020). Such approaches perturb hidden states or mix examples to synthesize new views (Chen et al., 2020a; Chen et al., 2020b; Kumar et al., 2019). Data generation with conditional language models provides another complementary avenue (Yoo et al., 2019; Ng et al., 2020; Hou et al., 2018). Recent studies also leverage large pretrained generators like GPT-2, BERT, and BART for controllable augmentation (Kumar et al., 2020; Anaby-Tavor et al., 2020; Yoo et al., 2021). Self-training remains a powerful semi-supervised paradigm that iteratively labels unlabeled data with a teacher model (Yarowsky, 1995; Riloff, 1996). Classic formulations date to early machine learning and have been refined for modern deep networks (Hinton et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2013; Reed et al., 2015). Practical recipes emphasize strong augmentation, equal or larger student capacity, and calibrated noise (Zoph et al., 2020; He et al., 2020a; Pham et al., 2020). Label flipping addresses spurious correlations by decoupling lexical cues from labels in training pairs (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). Manual flipping can expose shortcut features, but it is expensive to scale in diverse domains. Automated procedures for flipping aim to generalize the idea with minimal human intervention while preserving semantic plausibility. Our framework FlipDA filters candidate flips with a teacher to reduce noise and emphasizes contrastive signals across label boundaries. This design relates to contrastive learning, which constructs positives via augmentation and negatives from other samples in the batch (He et al., 2020b; Chen et al., 2020a). In limited data regimes, even modest augmentations can substantially improve calibration and sample efficiency. Several studies report complementary gains using representation perturbations and pseudo-labeling, see cf. Zhang et al., 2018; Guo and Chen, 2020a; Li, 2017) for methodological details. Nevertheless, naive augmentation may amplify biases if selection mechanisms ignore error modes specific to the domain. We therefore analyze failure cases and couple flipping with targeted sampling to avoid reinforcing high-frequency artifacts. Our experiments cover sentiment, topic classification, and natural language inference with standardized splits and metrics. We further compare manual versus automatic flips to disentangle the effect of human priors on generalization. The results suggest that combining curated flips with lightweight self-training yields robust few-shot improvements across models and tasks.

document: Hallucination in neural language generation has recently received increasing attention from the NLP community, including machine translation (Raunak et al., 2021; Wang and Sennrich, 2020). Beyond translation, abstractive summarization and data-to-text systems also exhibit content that is unsupported by inputs (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020). Prior work distinguishes between intrinsic hallucinations, which contradict source content, and extrinsic hallucinations, which introduce unverifiable facts (Ji et al., 2023). Evaluating hallucination remains difficult due to annotation costs and ambiguous definitions, prompting both model-based and knowledge-grounded metrics (Laban et al., 2022; Dziri et al., 2022). Studies in controlled decoding show that penalties over unfaithful spans can reduce errors without sacrificing fluency (Zhou et al., 2021; Filippova, 2020). In contrast, post-hoc verification leverages retrieval and entailment to filter or edit outputs before release (Rashkin et al., 2021; Manakul et al., 2023). Our work surveys these developments and identifies open challenges in faithful generation for safety-critical settings such as healthcare and law (Lehman et al., 2023; Bommasani et al., 2021). Methods that align models with external knowledge bases, like Wikipedia or domain ontologies, have shown promise in curbing fabrication (Lazaridou et al., 2022; Shuster et al., 2021). Following Nguyen, Li, and Park et al., we argue that faithfulness requires both architectural and data-centric interventions. Additionally, domain adaptation can exacerbate hallucination when target distributions diverge significantly from pretraining corpora (Gururangan et al., 2020; Yogatama et al., 2019). Instruction tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback may reduce certain hallucination modes, but effects are brittle across prompts and tasks (Ouyang et al., 2022; Bai et al., 2022). For multilingual applications, constrained decoding with lexicons or dictionaries remains a practical guardrail in low-resource settings (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). Insights on distributional shift are relevant here, as discussed by Hendrycks et al. 1, who analyze a suite of robustness benchmarks and failure modes. Nevertheless, measuring factual consistency should account for legitimate paraphrase and world knowledge updates over time (Honovich et al., 2022; Min et al., 2023). We conclude by outlining a taxonomy of evaluation desiderata and recommending standardized protocols for future studies (Fabbri et al., 2021; Pagnoni et al., 2021).

document: There has been much prior literature on improving the efficiency of Transformers. The most common technologies include: Knowledge distillation refers to training a smaller student model using outputs from various intermediate representations of larger pre-trained teacher models. In the BERT model, there are multiple representations that the student can learn from, such as the logits in the final layer, the outputs of the encoder units, and the attention maps. The distillation on output logits is most commonly used to train smaller BERT models (Sanh et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020). The output tensors of encoder units contain meaningful semantic and contextual relationships between input tokens. Some work creates a smaller model by learning from the outputs of the teacher's encoder (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020a). Attention map refers to the softmax distribution output of the self-attention layers and indicates the contextual dependence between the input tokens. A common practice of distillation on attention maps is to directly minimize the difference between the self-attention outputs of the teacher and the student (Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2020). This line of work is orthogonal to our approach and our proposed FCA can be applied to the distillate models to further accelerate their inference speed. Recent approaches also explore progressive module replacement, where smaller adapters mimic full blocks before they substitute them during inference (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Early-exit mechanisms reduce computation by allowing confident examples to stop at shallower layers while maintaining accuracy on harder inputs (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016; Xin et al., 2020). Pruning refers to identifying and removing less important weights or computation units. Pruning methods for BERT broadly fall into two categories. Unstructured pruning methods prune individual weights by comparing their absolute values or gradients with a pre-defined threshold Mao et al.; Gordon et al. The weights lower than the threshold are set to zero. Unlike unstructured pruning, structured pruning aims to prune structured blocks of weights or even complete architectural components in the BERT model. Voita et al. (2019) pruned attention heads using a method based on stochastic gates and a differentiable relaxation of the L0 penalty. Fan et al. (2019) randomly dropped Transformer layers to sample small sub-networks from the larger model during training, which are selected as the inference models. Goyal et al. (2020) progressively reduced sequence length by pruning word-vectors based on the attention values. This work is partly similar to the fine-grained computing units in our proposed FCA. However, they ignored the coarse-grained units that may cause information loss. Layer dropping is complemented by width pruning that removes attention heads or neurons to balance latency and accuracy (Michel et al., 2019; Lagunas et al., 2021). Complementary to pruning, low-rank factorization decomposes weight matrices to reduce multiplications with minimal loss (Sainath et al., 2013; Lan et al., 2020). In addition, there are some engineering techniques to speed up the inference speed, such as Mixed Precision (Micikevicius et al., 2018) and Quantization (Zafrir et al., 2019; Fan et al., 2021). Using half-precision or mixed-precision representations of floating points is popular in deep learning to accelerate training and inference speed. Quantization refers to reducing the number of unique values required to represent the model weights, which in turn allows to represent them using fewer bits. Operator fusion and kernel-level optimizations further amortize memory traffic and improve utilization on modern accelerators (Dao et al., 2022). Caching and dynamic batching strategies can also yield substantial throughput gains in real-world serving systems (Narayanan et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022).

document: Long Document Summarization has been studied across multiple domains, including news, patents, books, scientific publications, and clinical records. Early datasets and models for news summarization established the basic benchmarks (Nallapati et al., 2016). Domain-specific analyses of patent texts highlighted the need for structured representations (Trappey et al., 2009). Scholarly summarization emphasized citation-aware extraction to maintain context (Qazvinian and Radev, 2008). Work on clinical narratives underscored privacy constraints and long-context dependencies (Cohan et al., 2018). Beyond datasets, researchers proposed hierarchical and segment-based architectures to handle long inputs. Gidiotis and Tsoumakas (2020) introduced a divide-and-conquer paradigm that aggregates segment summaries. Grail et al. (2021) designed a hierarchical encoder that embeds block-level structure. While effective, single-pass segmentation often limits adaptability to documents of varying length. In contrast, modular frameworks aim to preserve backbone architectures while scaling to longer inputs. The GovReport dataset pushes summarization systems beyond typical news lengths, often exceeding 9000 words. This scale exposes computational bottlenecks in encoder–decoder attention (Subramanian et al., 2019). To mitigate costs, Huang et al. (2021) proposed head-wise positional strides that thin attention patterns. Reformer replaces full self-attention with locality-sensitive hashing to approximate similarity search (Kitaev et al., 2020). For sparse attention, Beltagy et al. introduced Longformer for long inputs. BigBird extends block sparse attention with theoretical guarantees for sequence mixing (Zaheer et al., 2020). Despite these innovations, sparsification can drift from the inductive biases of pretrained models. Consequently, methods that keep full attention while restructuring input organization remain attractive. Long dialogue summarization presents additional hurdles such as speaker turns and topic shifts. HMNet leverages hierarchical encoders to jointly model utterance and session context (Zhu et al., 2020). HAT-BART augments cross-turn interactions with structured attention over dialogue segments (Rohde et al., 2021). Data augmentation and weak supervision have been explored to improve robustness on lengthy conversations (Fabbri et al., 2021). Pretraining strategies tailored for dialogue coherence further reduce factual drift (Zhong et al., 2021a). Nevertheless, architectural changes often complicate integration with existing toolchains and checkpoints. Recent variants of BigBird target different pretraining regimes (Zaheer et al., 2020a, 2020b but maintain similar sparsity patterns. In practical deployments, input splitting and staged summarization remain popular due to engineering simplicity. Yet naive splitting can harm discourse consistency and cross-section coreference. Systems therefore benefit from modules that reconcile segment outputs through learned planning. Evaluation for long documents requires metrics that capture global coverage and local faithfulness. Emerging protocols combine automatic metrics with hierarchical human assessment for better reliability. Our study positions SUMM N as a structure-preserving framework that retains pretrained attention while scaling. We compare against segment-wise and sparse-attention baselines under uniform computational budgets. The results motivate future work on hybrid strategies that couple full-attention backbones with adaptive routing.

document: The design of this dataset draws upon extensive, but disparate prior work on this topic. Many works, some addressed below, have taken advantage of the availability of review text hosted on OpenReview. Argument-level review labeling has developed label sets that address different phenomena. Hua et al. (2019) introduced the study of discourse structure in peer review by annotating argumentative propositions in the AMPERE dataset with a set of labels tailored to the peer review domain (EVALUATION, REQUEST, FACT, REFERENCE, and QUOTE). Similarly, Fromm et al. (2020)'s AMSR dataset frames the problem as an argumentation process, in which the stance of each argument towards the paper's acceptance or rejection is of paramount importance. Both view peer review as argumentation, using argument mining techniques to highlight spans of interest. While its goal is not to examine discourse structure per se, Yuan et al. (2021 propose a coarse taxonomy of review acts that overlaps with argumentative function labels. Subsequent studies refine these categories to capture nuances such as hedging, politeness, and evidence type in the context of peer review (Li et al., 2022; Weld et al., 2022). Beyond labeling schemes, researchers have explored automatic identification of argumentative units using sequence tagging models trained on review corpora (Gao et al., 2020; Lauscher et al., 2021). Transfer learning from general-domain argument mining has been shown to provide strong initialization for reviewer-style discourse (Opitz et al., 2019). However, domain shift remains a challenge due to differences in tone, structure, and conventions between scholarly reviews and web arguments (Ajjour et al., 2017). To mitigate sparsity, several works leverage weak supervision, distant labels, or self-training with pseudo-annotations derived from review templates (Zhang et al., 2021; Gururangan et al., 2020). Our dataset design follows this trajectory by unifying argument labels with pragmatic signals, including requests, justifications, and references to prior art. We additionally encode section-level context to capture where within a review an argument occurs, which has proven helpful for downstream acceptance prediction (Du et al., 2021). Parallel efforts have examined reviewer calibration and bias, modeling how linguistic markers correlate with score variance and meta-review recommendations (Rennie et al., 2022; Stelmakh et al., 2021). Such findings motivate constructing datasets that link textual arguments to rating justifications rather than treating them as isolated snippets. On the modeling side, pretrained transformers fine-tuned with span-level supervision outperform classical feature-based baselines on argument extraction in reviews (Beltagy et al., 2020; Devlin et al., 2019). Recent architectures incorporate hierarchical encoders to jointly represent sentences, paragraphs, and document structure for long-review processing (Pappas et al., 2019; Dai et al., 2019). Multi-task learning with auxiliary objectives such as sentiment analysis and fact identification further improves robustness under label scarcity (Clark et al., 2019; Zhan et al., 2021). Nevertheless, annotation guidelines vary substantially across datasets, complicating fair comparison and transfer. In response, we harmonize label inventories through a mapping informed by prior ontologies and inter-annotator studies (Hovy et al., 2013; Biber and Conrad, 2009). We also release cross-domain splits to assess generalization across venues and time, aligning with calls for more rigorous evaluation protocols in the review-mining literature (Kang et al., 2020). Finally, we discuss ethical and privacy considerations surrounding the release of reviewer text, including redaction of identifying details and consent procedures (Raji et al., 2020; Mitchell et al., 2019). Taken together, this related work motivates a dataset that interleaves argumentative content with pragmatic and structural cues to support comprehensive analysis of peer reviews.

document: Parameter-Efficient Tuning of PLMs. As it is increasingly infeasible to train and store full copies of large PLMs for diverse downstream tasks, methods that update only a small fraction of weights have become central to deployment. We consider the landscape of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) under two broad families, depending on whether the method freezes the backbone and adds small trainable modules or only trains a carefully chosen subset of existing parameters. The former family includes adapters, low-rank updates, and prompt or prefix mechanisms that leave the original backbone mostly intact while injecting compact capacity (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021). The latter family typically tunes classification heads, biases, or layer normalizations, reducing storage costs while preserving most pretrained knowledge (Zaken et al., 2022; Houlsby et al., 2019). Despite their appeal, these strategies differ in computational footprint, memory traffic, and the degree to which they enable cross-task transfer. In production settings, storing thousands of task-specific adapters or low-rank matrices is far cheaper than duplicating a full model, yet careful design is needed to avoid interference among tasks (Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Moreover, quantization-aware techniques such as QLoRA enable training at low precision without sacrificing accuracy, further shrinking the memory budget (Dettmers et al., 2023). However, the community still lacks a unified understanding of when structural edits outperform selective fine-tuning under distribution shift and strict latency constraints (He et al., 2021). Prior work has predominantly studied English classification and generation benchmarks, with fewer systematic evaluations on multilingual or long-context scenarios (Xue et al., 2021; Tay et al., 2022). Our study situates PEFT within a broader perspective on continual and multi-domain adaptation, emphasizing storage-optimal parameter allocation and safe rollback. We revisit canonical PLMs and instruction-tuned LLMs to highlight how capacity placement interacts with scale and pretraining mixture (Devlin et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Chung et al., 2022). We also synthesize emerging evidence on soft prompts, showing that task- and layer-wise routing can recover strong performance with minimal updates (Hu et al., 2022; Li and Liang, 2021, particularly on low-resource domains. To ensure fair comparisons, we standardize training budgets, activation checkpointing, and optimization hyperparameters across model families (Mosbach et al., 2021). We further analyze storage-amortized cost by accounting for per-task deltas, optimizer states, and optional quantization metadata, which clarifies the practical value of different design points. Finally, we identify open questions around safety, privacy, and data governance for PEFT in regulated domains, motivating shared benchmarks and reporting standards for reproducible, efficient adaptation.

document: Neural machine translation has become the dominant paradigm for sequence-to-sequence learning. Despite strong accuracy, its predicted probabilities are often poorly calibrated. Miscalibration undermines downstream decisions such as risk-aware decoding and active learning. We first review the architecture used by most modern systems. The Transformer uses self-attention and feed-forward layers to encode and decode token sequences. Its decoder produces a distribution over the vocabulary via a softmax layer at each step. Training commonly relies on maximum likelihood with teacher forcing. Label smoothing is frequently applied and is known to distort confidence estimates. Beam search and length normalization further reshape probability mass during inference. Prior studies have explored temperature scaling as a simple post-hoc correction. Others investigated Bayesian approximations that capture parameter uncertainty through dropout. Deep ensembles provide another route by averaging predictions from multiple independently trained models. However, these solutions are computationally expensive or brittle under domain shift. Calibration for structured outputs remains especially challenging because errors are correlated across time steps. Token-level confidence can be aggregated to sentence-level risk, but naive aggregation amplifies bias. Metrics such as expected calibration error and Brier score provide imperfect yet useful diagnostics. We also consider coverage-based evaluation through risk-control curves. Our analysis emphasizes the interplay between modeling choices and calibration behavior. Following Vaswani 2017, we adopt a standard encoder–decoder backbone without architectural modifications. We then examine how training losses, data augmentation, and regularization affect probability estimates. We study both in-domain and out-of-domain settings to reveal robustness gaps. We compare post-hoc and training-time calibration strategies under identical decoding budgets. Finally, we outline open questions that connect calibration with controllable generation and interpretability. This section sets the stage for the methods and experiments that follow.

document: This paper situates our approach at the intersection of search algorithms for generation and methods for promoting diversity in outputs. Early analyses of neural text generation behavior provided descriptive and causal insights into decoding dynamics (Xu and Durrett, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021). Complementary strands have examined beam search and its limitations in conditional language modeling (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019; Meister et al., 2020a). Building on these threads, our work explores design choices that trade accuracy for calibrated diversity in a controlled manner. Best-first beam search formalizes a family of search policies that reorder expansions based on utility estimates (Meister et al., 2020b). A broader set of search variants continues to be investigated for efficiency and robustness (Meister et al., 2021a; Meister et al., 2021b). Prior critiques have argued that beam search can amplify model errors and reduce lexical variety in translation (Huang et al., 2017; Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019). However, these critiques often focus on machine translation and leave open questions in summarization and data-to-text settings (See et al., 2017; Fan et al., 2018). The literature on degenerative decoding has underscored failure modes such as repetition, dullness, and exposure bias (Holtzman et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020). Foundational observations about sampling strategies and nucleus thresholds have shaped subsequent practice in open-ended generation (Radford et al., 2019; Holtzman et al., 2020). In parallel, researchers proposed mechanisms for encouraging diverse candidates without sacrificing faithfulness (Vijayakumar et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016). Studies specific to paraphrasing demonstrated the benefits of structured constraints and exemplar conditioning (Iyyer et al., 2018; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). Broader surveys emphasize how diversity interacts with metrics and human preferences (Urbanek et al., 2019; Howcroft et al., 2020). Within this context, our contributions target coverage of salient content units and lexical choice variation (Gehrmann et al., 2018; Cao and Wang, 2021). Transformer-based architectures provide the modeling backbone for most contemporary decoders (Vaswani et al., 2017). Subsequent pretraining has further improved controllability and fluency in downstream tasks (Lewis et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). Despite these advances, practical decoding remains a key determinant of output quality in constrained generation scenarios (Stahlberg and Byrne, 2019). Our study revisits scoring functions that balance local likelihood with global penalties for redundancy (Paulus et al., 2018; Meister et al., 2020a). We additionally evaluate reranking strategies that incorporate semantic similarity features between candidates (Zhang et al., 2020; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). To situate the approach, we compare against canonical search baselines and sampling regimes under shared resource budgets (Fan et al., 2019; Holtzman et al., 2020). Beyond accuracy, we consider calibration of diversity-promoting penalties to avoid overdispersed outputs (Guo et al., 2017; Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). This emphasis aligns with reports that naive temperature scaling can degrade factual consistency (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2020). We therefore analyze factuality-aware signals in reranking to rein in hallucinations (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020). In addition to automatic metrics, we measure pairwise diversity using distinct-n and self-BLEU while monitoring task success (Papineni et al., 2002; Zhu and Bhat, 2018). Prior work also highlights dataset artifacts that bias decoding choices in summarization and dialogue (See et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2019). To control for such factors, we evaluate across news, conversational, and open-domain settings with standardized preprocessing (Narayan et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). Our modeling framework is agnostic to architecture and can be applied to encoder-decoder or decoder-only models (Lewis et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020). We further compare to constrained decoding schemes such as coverage penalties and lexically guided search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). Related streams incorporate dynamic beam allocation to improve exploration in long-form generation (Meister et al., 2020b), along with follow-up analyses (Xu and Durrett, 2021 that study adaptive heuristics). Our ablations test the sensitivity of results to beam width, softmax temperature, and top-p thresholds under fixed compute budgets (Holtzman et al., 2020; Meister et al., 2020a). Beyond decoding, data selection and span-level constraints have been shown to modulate diversity with minimal loss in adequacy (West et al., 2022; He and Glass, 2020). We complement these ideas by integrating learned utility models trained from offline rollouts (Chen et al., 2021; Pang et al., 2021). Error analysis further reveals that aggressive penalties can suppress rare but essential entities, motivating adaptive schedules (Mielke et al., 2021; He and Glass, 2020). Finally, we discuss limitations, including dependence on metric proxies and potential brittleness under domain shift (Hendrycks and Dietterich, 2019; Ribeiro et al., 2020). We hope these findings encourage principled, testable modifications to search that improve practical generation performance without obscuring model behavior (Belinkov and Glass, 2019; Bender et al., 2021).

document: Our work is related to cognitively inspired NLP and a variety of learning formalisms that involve knowledge transfer across different tasks. We briefly review these topics within the scope of NLP and the constraint of space. Since the pioneering work on semantic feature prediction from fMRI responses (Mitchell et al., 2008), connecting statistical NLP representations with human cognition has attracted sustained attention. Subsequent studies examined word- and sentence-level decoding using distributional vectors and contextual encoders (Anderson et al., 2016; Søgaard, 2016; Bulat et al., 2017). Parallel lines of work analyzed how embeddings align with neural and behavioral signals, using both regression and encoding models (Barrett and Søgaard, 2015; Bingel et al., 2016; Gauthier and Levy, 2019; Hollenstein and Zhang, 2019). Beyond passive alignment, researchers have explored task-driven representations to predict brain activity during language comprehension, drawing on advances in pretrained transformers as well as classical feature-based models Smith 2019. This literature increasingly emphasizes the role of fine-grained linguistic controls, such as syntactic surprisal, semantic relatedness, and discourse structure, in explaining neural variance (Hale et al., 2018; Shain, 2019; Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). Complementary to these efforts, knowledge transfer across tasks has been pursued through multitask learning and meta-learning to improve sample efficiency and robustness (Caruana, 1997; Ruder, 2017; Triantafillou et al., 2020). In NLP, transfer from auxiliary syntactic or semantic tasks often yields better generalization on low-resource benchmarks, with benefits attributed to shared inductive biases and representation smoothing (Hashimoto et al., 2017; Clark et al., 2019). Cross-modal transfer has likewise been used to bridge language and vision or speech, providing richer supervisory signals for grounded semantics and auditory-linguistic integration (Lu et al., 2019; Radford et al., 2021; Zellers et al., 2022). Our approach leverages these insights by combining cognitively meaningful supervision with principled transfer objectives, aiming to build models that are both neurally plausible and practically effective.

document: Zero-shot relation extraction was previously framed as a slot-filling task and solved by reading comprehension methods (Levy et al., 2017). However, their approach requires manual template design for each relation label, which cannot scale well to new relation types. On the other hand, RelationPrompt generates synthetic relation samples for any relation without relying on label-specific templates. Another approach to zero-shot relation extraction is the formulation into an entailment task Obamuyide and Vlachos, 2018), which is not constrained to a fixed relation label space and instead determines if arbitrary pairs of sentences and relation labels are compatible, allowing for zero-shot inference. However, it is designed for sentence-level classification and cannot be easily applied to ZeroRTE. Subsequent studies have examined schema induction and prompt engineering as alternatives to manual templates for broadening coverage. Data augmentation is a popular method for improving model performance in supervised low-resource tasks. Simple heuristics such as token manipulation (Kobayashi, 2018) were initially developed, and advances in language modeling have improved the quality of augmented samples (Xie et al., 2020;Wei and Zou, 2019). Although there are augmentation methods that can be applied to structured tasks such as named entity recognition (Ding et al., 2020) and relation extraction (Papanikolaou and Pierleoni, 2020;Lee et al., 2021), they require existing training samples and cannot be easily adapted to zero-shot tasks. As a result, methods that synthesize training instances from label descriptions have attracted renewed interest. Prompting-based methods have shown promise as a new paradigm for inference in natural language processing. Given language models that have large-scale and general language pretraining, well-designed prompts can elicit answer sequences for diverse tasks in few-shot or even zero-shot settings. Another advantage is the potential to adapt very large language models (Reynolds and McDonell, 2021) to new tasks without relatively expensive fine-tuning. Nevertheless, prompt-only approaches have not yet proven effective for more complex information extraction such as triplet extraction. Knowledge retrieval has emerged as a complementary ingredient for producing faithful and contextually grounded outputs. RelationPrompt leverages the knowledge stored in language models (Roberts et al., 2020) to compose relation samples that are situated in realistic contexts. To ensure that the generated samples are factually accurate, the language model requires strong retrieval capabilities (Petroni et al., 2019). Beyond retrieval, controllable generation mechanisms can further align samples with the semantics of target relations. We position our work within this landscape by focusing on zero-shot composition of relation-centric training instances. Our approach emphasizes compatibility between natural language descriptions of relations and candidate textual contexts. We also analyze failure modes arising from label ambiguity, entity typing errors, and lexical overlap with negative classes. In addition, we discuss evaluation protocols that better isolate generalization to unseen relations rather than memorization of distant supervision artifacts. Finally, we outline open challenges in scaling zero-shot relation extraction to multilingual and domain-specific corpora. These directions motivate future work on robust prompting, retrieval-augmented reasoning, and principled data construction for relation-centric NLP.

document: This paper examines how auxiliary natural language explanations can guide classifiers across novel tasks. Learning from such side information promises data efficiency and interpretability. Prior work has leveraged constraints or comments provided by annotators to regularize training. Early examples include posterior regularization and learning from general constraints (Mann and McCallum, 2010; Ganchev et al., 2010). More recently, language has been used as weak supervision for tasks like text classification and question answering (Wang et al., 2020; Ye et al., 2020). In this study we move beyond supervision for single tasks and ask whether explanation-driven models generalize to unseen tasks defined at test time. We frame explanations as concept definitions that describe decision boundaries rather than item-level justifications. This contrasts with local explanation datasets that provide rationales for individual predictions (Wiegreffe and Marasović, 2021). Seminal datasets collected free-form rationales to justify model outputs on commonsense and textual entailment benchmarks (Rajani et al., 2019; Camburu et al., 2018). Our notion of global explanations aligns with prior attempts to define label semantics and rule-like guidance. Our notion of explanations is shared with Andreas et al. (201 and Srivastava et al. (2017, 2018). However, we scale the setting to dozens of heterogeneous classification problems with structured inputs. We also decouple explanation elicitation from any single domain so that benchmarks remain domain agnostic. In contrast to prompting, our explanations specify why a class holds rather than how to phrase a task. Large pretrained language models provide strong few-shot and zero-shot baselines (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). Such models can be adapted with in-context examples or lightweight parameter updates to perform new tasks (Brown et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021). Pattern-based reformulations further improve few-shot learning for smaller models (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Tam et al., 2021). Multi-task prompt tuning has also been used to evaluate generalization across held-out tasks (Mishra et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2021; Min et al., 2021; Aghajanyan et al., 2021). Despite these advances, we find that explicit explanations offer complementary signal not captured by prompts alone. Explanations can support systematic generalization when they encode compositional properties shared across tasks. To study this, we construct a benchmark of classification problems defined over tabular representations. Each task provides a natural language explanation describing the concept to be learned given schema-aware inputs. We compare against prompt-based and label-only baselines under limited supervision. Prior work explored language as supervision for document and sentence classification (Srivastava et al., 2017; Hancock et al., 2018 but typically evaluated within a single task. In contrast, our evaluation measures generalization to new tasks whose schemas and distributions differ from those seen in training. We also study the interplay between explanation granularity and data efficiency. Our method encodes structured inputs with a table-aware encoder and fuses explanations through cross-attention. The architecture is trained end-to-end with contrastive and supervised objectives to align explanations with examples. We analyze how explanation completeness and specificity affect transfer to out-of-domain tasks. We report performance under zero-shot transfer, few-shot adaptation, and full supervision to establish upper bounds. Our experiments include ablations that remove explanations, perturb their content, or swap them across tasks. The results indicate that explanation-aware models consistently outperform strong prompt-based baselines on unseen tasks. Improvements are largest when class semantics require compositional reasoning over multiple fields in the table. We release code, data, and templates to facilitate reproducibility and further research. We conclude by discussing limitations and ethical considerations for explanation elicitation at scale.

document: In this section, we recap related work in dense retrieval and adversarial domain adaptation. Dense retrieval, different from sparse first-stage retrieval models, leverages Transformer-based encoders (Vaswani et al., 2017) to compute continuous representations for both queries and documents. Such dense methods, exemplified by BERT-style dual encoders, conduct retrieval directly in the embedding space (Lee et al., 2019b; Guu et al., 2020; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Luan et al., 2021). Compared with their sparse counterparts, DR systems often improve efficiency while maintaining comparable or superior effectiveness on in-domain datasets. Zero-shot dense retrieval (ZeroDR) has exposed gaps in cross-domain transfer and robustness (Thakur et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021; Li and Lin, 2021). One practical approach is synthetic query generation (Liang et al., 2020), which first trains a doc2query model in the source domain and then applies an NLG model to target-domain documents to produce queries. The resulting pseudo pairs of documents and queries provide weak supervision for training retrieval models at scale, yet much prior work emphasizes data augmentation rather than improving the invariance of learned representations. As several benchmarks indicate, Thakur et al. 2021 highlight persistent failures when training and deployment domains diverge. Adversarial domain adaptation (UDA) has long been explored in vision, offering complementary strategies for distribution shift. For example, moment-matching minimizes a predefined discrepancy (Long et al., 2013; Tzeng et al., 2014; Sun and Saenko, 2016), while adversarial approaches train feature extractors to confuse a domain discriminator (Ganin and Lempitsky, 2015; Bousmalis et al., 2016; Tzeng et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2017). Building on these insights, MoDIR incorporates momentum-based representation alignment tailored to the unique challenges of ZeroDR.

document:
Edit Intention Identification. Identification of edit intentions is an integral component of iterative text revision workflows. Prior work has examined taxonomies of edit actions to better understand editorial behavior and outcomes (Yang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017; Ito et al., 2019). These studies argue that explicit categorization can reveal common revision patterns that correlate with perceived quality (Zhang et al., 2017). However, they rarely investigate how to operationalize intentions within automated systems that plan and execute edits end to end. Domain focus further complicates generalization, because many taxonomies were derived from Wikipedia edits or student essays (Anthonio et al., 2020; Bhat et al., 2020; Faltings et al., 2021). As a result, models trained on narrowly scoped corpora often fail to transfer to professional or creative writing domains. Our work addresses this gap by disentangling edit intention recognition from surface realization and by evaluating across heterogeneous sources.

Single-Pass Text Revision Models. A prevalent formulation reduces revision to single‑pass sequence generation from a noisy draft to a polished target (Botha et al., 2018; Ito et al., 2019; Faltings et al., 2021). While attractive for its simplicity, this approach struggles with long‑range discourse adjustments and multi‑step argumentative restructuring. For instance, adding discourse markers, migrating evidence across paragraphs, and reconciling global style frequently require staged interventions. Moreover, beam search in single‑pass models tends to conflate micro‑edits with macro‑edits, leading to brittle outputs under distribution shift (Zhang et al., 2017). Iterative frameworks instead alternate between planning and local rewriting, progressively refining the draft over multiple passes (Spangher and May, 2021; Xu et al., 2022). Such designs align with cognitive theories of writing, which posit cyclical planning, translating, and reviewing phases (Flower and Hayes, 1981).

Iterative Text Revision Datasets. Existing datasets capture only slices of the broader revision landscape, often focusing on encyclopedic edits or classroom feedback (Anthonio et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017). Recent collections expand to news and reviews but still exhibit limited annotation of intention labels and revision rationales (Spangher and May, 2021; Faltings et al., 2021) (Nguyen et al., 2019. Cross‑domain assemblies that pair granular edit operations with intention tags remain scarce, hindering comparative analysis and robust benchmarking. We propose a unified annotation schema that harmonizes intention labels across domains while preserving task‑specific phenomena. In addition, we release multi‑domain corpora with aligned drafts, intermediate revisions, and final texts, enabling controlled ablations of planning and execution. To ensure reproducibility, we adopt consistent split protocols and provide strong baselines spanning edit‑based and generate‑and‑edit paradigms (Malmi et al., 2019; Reid and Neubig, 2022).

Methodological Context. Intention identification has connections to discourse parsing, quality estimation, and controllable text generation (Webber et al., 2019; Specia et al., 2021; Moryossef et al., 2021). Prior interactive frameworks leverage user signals to guide localized rewriting, including suggestions, critiques, and preferences (Stiennon et al., 2020; Mireshghallah et al., 2022). Active learning and curriculum scheduling can further prioritize edits that most improve downstream objectives (Settles, 2010; Platanios et al., 2019). We build on these insights by formulating intention‑aware controllers that interleave planning with constrained decoding to produce faithful, minimal edits. Finally, we evaluate transfer across domains, revealing where intention modeling yields gains over structure‑agnostic baselines.

document: Referring Expression Comprehension (REC) aims to localize an object in an image given a natural language expression. Most REC methods learn a joint embedding over visual and linguistic inputs and differ in how the space is formed and exploited. Early metric-learning approaches ranked region proposals against text in a shared space (Hu et al., 2016b; Rohrbach et al., 2016; Plummer et al., 2018). Attention-based models subsequently weighted region proposals to select the referent, as shown by (Deng et al., 2018; and follow-ups. Recent transformer architectures fuse cross-modal context to produce aligned representations (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020). These models typically apply self-attention to align proposal-level and token-level cues within a common semantic space. Our approach instead augments region cues with pixel-level features that preserve fine-grained boundaries important for referring segmentation. In addition, relative to Lu et al. (2019), we explicitly capture both inter-modal and intra-modal interactions without adding heavy task-specific heads. Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) addresses the shape sensitivity that bounding box REC often misses. It was first introduced in (Hu et al., 2016a, where a model concatenated CNN features with tiled LSTM language features to predict the mask. Follow-up work refined masks by injecting multi-scale semantics and contextual reasoning across image regions. Since words contribute unequally to object identification, attention mechanisms have been used to weight linguistic tokens during visual grounding (Shi et al., 2018). Transformer-based decoders further capture long-range dependencies between text and pixels to improve delineation quality (Ye et al., 2019). Despite these advances, cross-modal alignment remains brittle when expressions mention relational attributes or occluded parts. We therefore argue for models that jointly exploit proposal cues and dense features while maintaining robust language grounding and efficient inference.

document: Bias terms and their importance are rarely discussed in the literature. Zhao et al. (2020) describe a masking-based fine-tuning method, and explicitly mention ignoring the bias terms, as handling them "did not observe a positive effect on performance". An exception is the work of Wang et al. (2019), who analyzed bias terms from the perspective of attribution methods. They demonstrate that the last-layer bias values are often responsible for the predicted class, and propose a way to back-propagate their importance. Michel and Neubig (2018) fine-tuned the biases of the output softmax in NMT systems to personalize the output vocabulary. Cai et al. (2020) demonstrate that bias-only fine-tuning similar to ours is effective for adapting pre-trained computer vision models. Despite these findings, the role of biases in transfer learning remains underexplored across domains such as ASR and recommender systems. Recent analyses of parameter-efficient adaptation highlight that small subsets of parameters can carry disproportionate task signal (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021). However, most such methods focus on adapters or prompts rather than explicitly modeling bias vectors. In classification networks, biases shift decision boundaries in feature space and can encode priors induced by dataset imbalance. Several works on logit scaling and temperature tuning indirectly manipulate biases through normalization factors (Platt, 1999; Hinton et al., 2015). Related studies on sparsity show that biases persist after heavy pruning and thus may act as stable carriers of task-specific signals (Frankle and Carbin, 2019; Sanh et al., 2020). Comparable observations were made in multilingual models, where language-specific heads differed primarily in bias parameters (Zhao et al., 2020; Wang. To our knowledge, no prior work systematically isolates bias vectors across layers to quantify their transferable capacity. This paper fills that gap by proposing a controlled protocol to learn, freeze, and evaluate biases across domains, comparing against adapters, LoRA, and full fine-tuning baselines.
