document: While elaborated feedback datasets on language learning (Caines et al., 2020;Pilan et al., 2020;Stasaski et al., 2020) appeared recently, they focus on linguistic mistakes, such as grammatical errors, instead of content. Our extensive literature review did not reveal datasets that included content-focused elaborated feedback on short answer responses. However, SAF's feedback can be viewed as a textual explanation of the assigned score. Therefore, comparable NLP datasets with textual explanations and publicly available ASAG datasets without explanations are discussed in the following sections. Automated short answer grading aims to predict holistic or rubric-aligned scores for brief, content-bearing responses in educational settings (Burrows et al., 2015). Early systems relied on handcrafted features, such as lexical overlap and concept mapping, which offered interpretability but limited generalization (Sultan et al., 2016). With the advent of pretrained language models, encoder architectures like BERT have substantially improved scoring accuracy on public benchmarks (Devlin et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020). Nonetheless, most models provide little actionable feedback, leaving teachers and students without guidance on how to improve responses (Nicol and Macfarlane-Dick, 2006). The literature on explainable AI proposes post hoc rationales, but many rationalization methods risk producing plausible yet unfaithful explanations (Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). In educational NLP, elaborated feedback refers to targeted, content-specific guidance that references missing ideas, misconceptions, or evidence quality rather than surface errors (Shute, 2008). Prior datasets for grammatical error correction organize explicit edits and metalinguistic comments, which do not transfer directly to content diagnosis in short answers (Ng et al., 2014;Bryant et al., 2019). Similarly, dialogue-based tutoring corpora capture rich instructional moves, yet typically presuppose multi-turn interaction and do not align with single-response grading scenarios (Graesser et al., 2005). Work on rationales in classification has introduced span selection or free-text justifications to enhance transparency of predictions (Lei et al., 2016;Camburu et al., 2018). However, free-text rationales often optimize for agreement with labels rather than pedagogical usefulness, which constrains their applicability to formative feedback (DeYoung et al., 2020). Research on explanation-centric QA and NLI shows that models can be trained to produce supportive statements alongside predictions, though faithfulness remains contested (Rajani et al., 2019;Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019). Meta-analytic findings further support the educational impact of elaborated feedback Johnson et al., 2017; Smith), motivating its integration into assessment workflows. In ASAG, prompt-specific reference answers and concept lists have been used to structure supervision for content coverage (Somasundaran et al., 2015;Zhang and VanLehn, 2019). Rubric-aware systems further condition predictions on criteria such as claim accuracy, evidence, and reasoning completeness to improve reliability across prompts (Kumar et al., 2020). Score explanation studies explore extracting evidence sentences or generating textual critiques that point to missing key ideas in student responses (Liu et al., 2021;Nadeem et al., 2022). Despite progress, the field lacks standardized datasets where content-focused feedback is paired with reference scores across diverse topics and age groups. This gap complicates evaluation protocols, hinders reproducibility, and slows the development of feedback generation methods that generalize beyond single prompts. Our study positions SAF within this landscape by distinguishing explanatory content feedback from mere score justification. We treat feedback generation as conditional text production that must be faithful to the scoring rationale while being specific, actionable, and concise (Madsen et al., 2022). To contextualize our contribution, we review adjacent areas, including evidence-based argument grading, scientific peer-review assistance, and programmatic feedback from code analysis (Gretz et al., 2020;Roth et al., 2021;Zheng et al., 2022). Dataset design considerations include the granularity of feedback units, the presence of gold rationales, and the availability of domain ontologies or knowledge graphs (Chen et al., 2020). Annotation protocols must manage subjectivity by providing detailed rubrics, multi-annotator adjudication, and calibration examples to ensure consistency (Snow et al., 2008). Evaluation should combine automatic metrics for lexical overlap with human judgments of usefulness, specificity, and faithfulness to the underlying evidence (Yao et al., 2022). We also note growing interest in controllable text generation that styles feedback according to pedagogical intent, such as hints, prompts, or corrective statements (Keskar et al., 2019;Zhou and Neubig, 2021). In sum, establishing content-focused elaborated feedback datasets for short answers can catalyze research on faithful, useful, and generalizable feedback generation.

document: Earlier work in hate speech detection focused on data collection, curation, and robust annotation protocols to create reliable corpora (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; Davidson et al., 2017; Founta et al., 2018). Recent studies broadened label taxonomies to capture targets, intent, and severity, and they emphasized standardized evaluation through shared tasks and leaderboards alongside new benchmarks (Röttger et al., 2021; Mathew et al., 2021 that compare cross-dataset generalization. Parallel analyses have examined dataset artifacts, sampling bias, and temporal drift to contextualize reported performance (Madukwe et al., 2020; Kim et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2019; Swamy et al., 2019; Davidson et al., 2019a). A complementary thread extends coverage beyond English by curating multilingual corpora and transfer protocols (Ousidhoum et al., 2019; Ranasinghe and Zampieri, 2020; Ross et al., 2017; Nozza, 2021; Zoph et al., 2016; Marivate et al., 2020; Nekoto et al., 2020). XTREMESPEECH contributes to this agenda by providing Brazilian Portuguese, German, Hindi, and Swahili data with harmonized labels and quality control. Another line of work investigates annotation bias and annotator pools to better understand how background and expertise shape labels (Al Kuwatly et al., 2020; Waseem, 2016; Ross et al., 2017; Shmueli et al., 2021; Posch et al., 2018). Studies further document racialized and intersectional biases embedded in widely used datasets and model predictions (Davidson et al., 2019b; Laugier et al., 2021). In response, mitigation methods span data balancing, counterfactual augmentation, adversarial training, and fairness-aware objectives (Sap et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2020; Han et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). The consensus emerging from this literature is that dataset design choices can both reflect and propagate annotator bias if not carefully managed. Theoretical foundations have been articulated through taxonomies, operational definitions, and distinctions among abuse, harassment, and hate (Banko et al., 2020; Wiegand et al., 2021; Waseem et al., 2017). Our perspective is additionally informed by ethnographic and anthropological work on the societal impacts of online hate and extreme speech (Boromisza-Habashi, 2013; Donovan and boyd, 2021; Haynes, 2019; Udupa and Pohjonen, 2019; Hervik, 2019). These traditions motivate context-sensitive annotation guidelines that foreground community norms, speaker intent, and power dynamics. Ethical considerations around data stewardship, model deployment, and accountability have become central to the research agenda (Mittelstadt et al., 2016; Jobin et al., 2019; Bender et al., 2021). Calls have intensified for inclusive annotator recruitment, clearer provenance documentation, and multidisciplinary oversight in NLP projects (Leins et al., 2020). Against this backdrop, we propose a cross-lingual benchmark that emphasizes sociolinguistic coverage and transparent evaluation protocols. Our design prioritizes label definitions that align with legal and platform policies while remaining suitable for empirical modeling. We also examine cross-dataset transfer to assess robustness under topical and demographic shift using multilingual encoders (Conneau et al., 2020). In low-resource settings, we explore semi-supervised learning and prompt-based augmentation to reduce annotation load without sacrificing validity (He et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Following Waseem et al. 2017, we operationalize hate speech with a focus on protected characteristics, dehumanization, and calls for exclusion. We situate our annotation schema within prior comparative frameworks to facilitate synthesis across datasets and languages (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018). To encourage reproducibility, we release detailed codebooks, inter-annotator agreement analyses, and adjudication protocols. We also provide strong baselines with lexicon, linear, and transformer models, reporting both accuracy and fairness metrics (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Our results highlight persistent generalization gaps across domains and dialects, underscoring the need for continual dataset refresh and monitoring. By unifying definitions, multilingual coverage, and rigorous evaluation, this work aims to advance reliable and equitable hate speech detection.

Zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning. Recently, thanks to the rapid development of multilingual pre-trained language models such as multilingual BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), a number of approaches have been proposed for zero-shot cross-lingual transfer learning on various downstream tasks, including semantic parsing (Sherborne and Lapata, 2021), natural language generation (Shen et al., 2018) and understanding (Lauscher et al., 2020). In this work, we claim our method is zeroshot because no non-Chinese CSRL annotations are seen during the CSRL training stage. For decoding, we directly use the cross-lingual CSRL model trained on Chinese CSRL data to analyze conversations in other languages. To our best knowledge, our work is the first step to cross-lingual CSRL.

Conversational semantic role labeling. While ellipsis and anaphora frequently occur in dialogues, Xu et al. (2021) observed that most of the dropped or referred components can be found in dialogue histories. Following this observation, they proposed conversational semantic role labeling (CSRL) which required the model to find predicate–argument structures over the entire conversation instead of a single sentence. In this way, when analyzing a predicate in the latest utterance, a CSRL model needs to consider both the current turn and previous turns to search potential arguments, and thus might recover the omitted components. Furthermore, Xu et al. (2020, 2021) also confirmed the usefulness of CSRL to Chinese dialogue tasks by applying CSRL information into downstream dialogue tasks. However, there are still two main problems to be solved for the CSRL task: (1) the performance of current state-of-the-art CSRL models (Xu et al., 2021) is still far from satisfactory due to the lack of high-level conversational and semantic feature modeling; (2) the usefulness of CSRL to conversational tasks in non-Chinese languages has not been confirmed yet due to the lack of cross-lingual CSRL models. In this work, we primarily focus on the latter problem and propose a simple but effective model to perform cross-lingual CSRL. We would like to distinguish our work from the concurrent work (Wu et al., 2021b) which purely focuses on improving the monolingual CSRL performance where they try to model predicate-aware representations. This solution could benefit monolingual CSRL, but hurt cross-lingual performance because the relative positions of the predicates may differ from language to language.

Cross-lingual transfer typically relies on the assumption that multilingual encoders learn language-agnostic representations aligned in a shared space (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019). Under this assumption, label projections from a source language can be applied to target languages without additional supervision Hu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, dialogue data introduces additional challenges such as speaker role alternation, discourse coherence, and pragmatic inference beyond sentence boundaries (Jurafsky and Martin, 2023). Prior work on semantic role labeling in monologues has explored contextual encoders and syntax-aware models to capture long-range dependencies (He et al., 2018; Strubell et al., 2018). In contrast, CSRL must reason over multi-turn structures, identify cross-utterance arguments, and determine whether a candidate referent is salient across turns (Chen et al., 2022). To address these challenges, we integrate multilingual pretraining with conversation-level retrieval of candidate arguments and a lightweight discourse memory inspired by entity tracking models (Clark and Manning, 2016). Our approach further incorporates predicate canonicalization to reduce superficial divergence across languages while preserving role consistency (Hajic et al., 2009). We evaluate on Chinese as the source language and conduct zero-shot transfer to English, Spanish, and German using standard CSRL metrics (P, R, and F1) and ablation studies. For fair comparison, we replicate training and decoding settings from strong baselines and report results on public benchmarks (Conneau et al., 2018; Ruder et al., 2019). Finally, we analyze error types by discourse phenomenon, highlighting failure cases where long-distance anaphora and idiomatic expressions degrade transfer effectiveness.

document: Visual Question Answering. The current dominant framework for VQA consists of an image encoder, a question encoder, multimodal fusion, and an answer predictor (Agrawal et al., 2015). To avoid the noises caused by global features, methods (Malinowski et al., 2018) introduce various image attention mechanisms into VQA. Instead of directly using visual features from CNN-based feature extractors, to improve the performance of the model, BUTD (Anderson et al., 2018) adopts Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015) to obtain candidate regional features, while Pythia (Jiang et al., 2018) integrates the regional feature with grid-level features. Meanwhile, Lu et al. (2016) and Nam et al. (2017) put more attention on learning better question representations. To merge information from different modalities sufficiently, MFB (Yu et al., 2017) and MUTAN (Ben-Younes et al., 2017) explore higher-order fusion methods. Further, BAN (Kim et al., 2018) and DCN (Nguyen and Okatani, 2018) propose dense co-attention models which directly establish interaction between modalities with word-level and regional features. Moreover, with the proposal of the Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), MCAN and DFAF (Gao et al., 2019) adopt self-attention mechanisms to fully excavate the fine-grained information contained in text and image. Meanwhile, to fully cover the holistic scene in an image, MuREL (Cadène et al., 2019) and ReGAT (Li et al., 2019) explicitly incorporate relations between regions into the interaction process. Selvaraju et al. (2020) also introduce sub-questions into their work; the distinctness between us is that it constructs a Sub-VQA dataset for a subset of reasoning questions in the VQA dataset to evaluate consistency of VQA models, while we adopt sub-questions to achieve task dividing. Beyond architectural advances, large-scale pretraining on image–text pairs has become a de facto prerequisite for strong VQA performance (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Li et al., 2020). Such pretraining encourages models to align visual regions and linguistic phrases, which is later specialized via fine-tuning on VQA benchmarks (Goyal et al., 2017; Antol et al., 2015). However, a growing body of work documents that shortcut reasoning and language priors can still dominate predictions, resulting in inconsistent behavior under distribution shift (Agrawal et al., 2018; Cadene et al., 2019). Recent benchmarks therefore emphasize counterfactual evaluation, compositional generalization, and consistency across related question sets (Hudson and Manning, 2019; Kervadec et al., 2021). In parallel, research has examined the role of region proposals versus dense grids, reporting complementary benefits when both are available to the fusion module (Jiang et al., 2018; Anderson et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2020). Despite these advances, modeling multi-step reasoning over scenes with coreference and temporal cues remains challenging for current architectures (Zellers et al., 2019; Le et al., 2020). Visual Dialogue. Different from VQA, visual dialogue (VD) is a continuous conversation grounded in images and prior turns (Das et al., 2017). Several VD tasks—Visual Dialog, GuessWhich (Chattopadhyay et al., 2017), GuessWhat?! (de Vries et al., 2017), and MMD (Saha et al., 2018)—have been proposed. GuessWhat?!, as a goal-directed dialogue task, requires both players to continuously clarify the reference object through dialogue. The Oracle provides the Questioner with relevant information about the target object by constantly answering yes or no questions, and the Guesser generates the final answer based on the historical dialogue. Following this setting, our Co-VQA framework consists of three components in which the Questioner raises sub-questions, the Oracle answers them one by one, and finally the Answerer obtains the answer to the original question. Prior work in VD has highlighted the importance of leveraging co-reference resolution across turns and grounding follow-up questions in visual context (Kottur et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2019). Transformer-based encoders have also been adapted to multi-turn dialogue to capture long-range dependencies across utterances and image regions (Hori et al., 2019; Shuster et al., 2020). In this paper we discuss how task decomposition with auxiliary sub-questions can reduce reasoning load on the final answering module while improving interpretability of intermediate decisions (Selvaraju et al., 2020). We further relate this decomposition to modular training strategies that align question intents with visual evidence retrieved by attention layers (Cornia et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). Finally, we note that stronger regularization and curriculum design are needed to prevent models from overfitting to annotation artifacts that correlate with answer frequency distributions (Goyal et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2017). Under this perspective, we position our approach at the intersection of compositional reasoning and dialogue-aware grounding, building on recent developments in self-attention architectures in (Vaswani et al., 2017. Concretely, we implement sub-question generation and evidence aggregation within a unified training pipeline inspired by advances in cross-modal transformers.

We describe related studies on Chinese BERT model and probing of BERT, respectively.

The authors of BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) provided the first Chinese BERT model which was trained on Chinese Wikipedia data. On top of that, Cui et al. (2019) trained RoBERTa-wwm-ext with whole word masking on extended corpora. Cui et al. (2020) further trained a Chinese ELECTRA model and MacBERT, both of which did not use explicit [MASK] tokens. ELECTRA was trained with a token-level binary classification task, which determined whether a token was the original one or artificially replaced. In MacBERT, [MASK] tokens were replaced with synonyms and the model was trained with WWM and n-gram masking. ERNIE (Sun et al., 2019) employed entity-level masking, in which all tokens of an entity were masked simultaneously. Language-specific features have been emphasized in more recent work on Chinese pretraining. For example, AMBERT and Lattice-BERT (Lai et al., 2021) both integrate lexicon-level word information. Chinese-BERT (Sun et al., 2021) additionally encodes pinyin and glyph features of characters.

Several studies explored multi-granular tokenization to mitigate segmentation ambiguity in Chinese (Zhang et al., 2021). Others investigated pretraining with mixed-script inputs to handle code-switching phenomena (Liu et al., 2022). Domain-adaptive pretraining has also been shown beneficial for Chinese biomedical and legal corpora (Gu et al., 2021; He et al., 2022). Beyond objectives and tokenization, work has evaluated robustness to homophones and character perturbations (Xie et al., 2020). Knowledge-enhanced variants inject lexical or encyclopedic constraints during pretraining to improve downstream factuality (Wang et al., 2021). Collectively, these methods indicate that structure-aware masking and language resources can meaningfully shape Chinese BERT derivatives.

In parallel, probing aims to examine the language understanding ability of pretrained models when model parameters are frozen. Early probes assessed whether masked language models capture factual triples using cloze-style prompts (Petroni et al., 2019). Template-based queries were designed such as “The wife of Barack Obama is [MASK].” to test factual recall. If a model predicts the correct answer “Michelle Obama,” it suggests that pretrained models store some factual associations. Subsequent work studied how models encode commonsense relations and lexical inference (Davison et al., 2019). Other studies targeted symbolic reasoning, numerical comparison, and compositionality with controlled templates (Talmor et al., 2020). Probing frameworks have also been adapted to Chinese to test morphology-like features, word segmentation, and tone information. Dataset-driven analyses compare layerwise representations to supervised predictors for syntax, semantics, and entity typing. Behavioral tests further evaluate robustness to paraphrase, negation, and context sensitivity under minimal-pair transformations. Recent extensions refine masking objectives (Sun et al., 2019; Cui et al., 2020 and introduce multi-task probes that align with human annotations. Calibration-aware probing evaluates whether confidence scores correlate with accuracy across templates and domains. Meanwhile, Chinese-specific probes examine character-level phonetic cues and disambiguation of homophones in context. Cross-lingual probes leverage parallel corpora to measure transfer between Chinese and alphabetic languages (Artetxe et al., 2020). Complementary diagnostics use interventions in attention patterns to test reliance on positional versus lexical features. Some studies caution that high probe performance can reflect probe capacity rather than encoded knowledge (Hewitt and Liang, 2019). To mitigate this, control tasks and selectivity metrics are employed to disentangle representation content from probe complexity. Building on these insights, our work analyzes Chinese BERT variants under unified probing settings for knowledge and reasoning. We integrate lexical resources, robust prompting, and calibration metrics to provide comprehensive and reproducible evaluations. Through this synthesis, we aim to clarify which pretraining choices most strongly influence Chinese-language understanding.

Data Augmentation. Data augmentation (Sennrich et al., 2016a; Cheng et al., 2016; Hoang et al., 2018; Edunov et al., 2018; Kobayashi, 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2020) has been widely used in neural machine translation to deal with the generalization problem of models trained on limited labeled data.

The most popular one is back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016a), which utilizes a target-to-source model to translate monolingual target sentences back into the source language. Wang et al. 2018 proposed to augment each training instance with multiple literal forms by replacing random words with other words in the vocabulary. Cheng et al. (2020) proposed to replace words with other words that are predicted using language model under the same context by interpolating their embeddings.

Our approach also belongs to the family of data augmentation, but we augment each training instance with an adjacency semantic region that covers adequate variants under the same meaning. Semantic Agreement Modeling. Learning the universal representation (Zhang et al., 2016; Schwenk and Douze, 2017 for multiple semantically-equivalent sentences is also related to our approach. In their early work, variational NMT (Zhang et al., 2016) introduced a continuous vector to model underlying semantics of the parallel sentences in the machine translation task. Additionally, Chi et al. (2021) suggested transforming cross-lingual sentences into a shared vector by contrastive objectives. In this context, our method mainly focused on using the well-learned continuous space to overcome the substantial challenge of making more unseen instances under with limited training data.

document: Non-autoregressive edit-based models cast sequence generation as iterative refinement under a Markov Decision Process. A state y is a token sequence, actions edit the sequence, and transitions reflect applied edits. The reward is often the negative distance between the current hypothesis and the reference. Such formulations enable parallelizable operations compared to left-to-right autoregression. Early approaches demonstrated competitive decoding speedups without sacrificing too much quality (Gu et al., 2019). Subsequent work refined the action space to include insertion, deletion, substitution, and reposition (Stern et al., 2019). Among these, the EDITOR model emphasizes reposition and insertion for robust constrained editing (Xu and Carpuat, 2021). In EDITOR, a reposition policy predicts target indices for all tokens, implicitly modeling deletions. An insertion policy then predicts placeholder counts and fills them with concrete tokens. The two policies alternate until convergence or a preset iteration budget is exhausted (Lee et al., 2018). This iterative loop halts early when two consecutive outputs are identical, preventing unnecessary steps. Training typically follows an imitation learning recipe with carefully designed roll-in and roll-out strategies. Roll-ins mix model outputs with noised gold sequences to expose the learner to realistic errors (Lample et al., 2018). Roll-outs estimate the cost-to-go by comparing actions to oracle edit trajectories under a distance metric (Gu et al., 2019; Stern et al., 2019. The Levenshtein distance serves as a natural oracle for supervising discrete editing decisions. In machine translation, these ideas support editing from nonempty seeds such as lexically constrained prompts. They also enable mid-course correction of noisy user inputs in interactive scenarios. Policy factorization helps decouple placement from lexical choice, improving stability across domains. Despite these strengths, exposure bias and compounding errors remain notable concerns. To mitigate them, curricula interleave expert and learned roll-ins during early training epochs. Noise processes such as word dropout and local shuffling further regularize the search space (Ghazvininejad et al., 2019). Evaluations report favorable trade-offs between latency and accuracy on standard MT benchmarks. Error analyses highlight that reposition mistakes often cascade into insertion oversupply. Recent studies explore integrating confidence estimation to decide when to stop refinement. Others augment the action inventory with copy and span-move operations to reduce fragmentation. Comparisons across datasets suggest that edit-based NAR models benefit from richer initialization. We therefore review canonical systems and distill common design patterns. Our discussion situates these models within broader reinforcement learning and structured prediction trends (Ross and Bagnell, 2010).

Multimodal entity and relation extraction has emerged as a central topic in information extraction as users increasingly communicate with text and images (Zhang et al., 2020). While traditional named entity recognition and relation extraction have relied on purely textual cues, social media posts often require visual context for disambiguation (Moon et al., 2018). For example, a caption mentioning a location can only be resolved with a photo that reveals an event setting or organizational logo. These observations have inspired a new line of work on multimodal NER (MNER) and multimodal RE (MRE) that seeks to fuse linguistic and visual signals (Arshad et al., 2019). However, the community still lacks a systematic understanding of which visual features most benefit extraction under noisy, user-generated content. This section reviews core ideas, datasets, and modeling choices that characterize the current landscape.

Early pipelines for text-only extraction established strong baselines with conditional random fields and neural sequence models, later surpassed by pre-trained language models (Lample et al., 2016; Devlin et al., 2019). Despite their effectiveness, purely textual encoders can misclassify entities when the surface form is ambiguous or the context is underspecified. In contrast, images may supply discriminative attributes such as object categories, scene types, and spatial layouts that correlate with target labels. The challenge is to integrate such cues without overwhelming the textual signal or propagating spurious correlations (Kiela et al., 2019). Recent work leverages cross-modal attention to align mention spans with image regions, thereby enabling selective grounding of candidate entities (Yu et al., 2020). Complementary strategies introduce consistency constraints that preserve linguistic structure while admitting visual evidence.

The first wave of MNER systems encoded sentences with recurrent networks and summarized entire images with convolutional backbones, fusing the two streams through attention or gating mechanisms (Moon et al., 2018). Representative approaches demonstrated measurable gains on social media benchmarks by exploiting coarse global features from images (Arshad et al., 2019). Subsequent studies replaced global pooling with object-level detectors to refine alignments between mentions and regions (Ren et al., 2015; Carion et al., 2020). For instance, region proposals from Faster R-CNN or DETR enable word-to-object matching that improves boundary detection and type classification. A parallel line of research explored multi-task setups that jointly learn auxiliary objectives such as visual question answering to regularize cross-modal fusion (Lu et al., 2019). Nevertheless, naive fusion can still amplify noise when images are only weakly related to the text (Kiela et al., 2019).

To address irrelevance, some models estimate a text–image compatibility score and down-weight the visual stream during decoding when the score is low (Wu et al., 2022). Others incorporate contrastive pretraining to align textual mentions with salient regions, adapting ideas from CLIP to the extraction setting (Nguyen et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020; and Zhang, 2021. Robust training regimes further adopt hard-negative mining and modality dropout to prevent overfitting to superficial cues (Chen et al., 2020). Recent transformer-based architectures integrate region features, positional encodings, and linguistic tokens in a unified encoder for end-to-end learning (Vaswani et al., 2017; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). Several studies also focus on sample efficiency by leveraging pseudo-labeling and curriculum schedules to cope with scarce multimodal annotations (Xie et al., 2020). Domain shift remains a practical concern because event distributions, camera viewpoints, and writing styles vary significantly across datasets (Peng et al., 2022).

Evaluation practices increasingly report both overall accuracy and robustness metrics that quantify performance under image perturbations or missing modalities (Hendrycks and Dietterich, 2019). These metrics reveal that strong average scores can mask brittle behavior when visual content is adversarial or unrelated. Beyond architectures, dataset design has influenced conclusions, since many benchmarks include high proportions of weakly correlated image–text pairs (Cai et al., 2022). Consequently, several works introduce auxiliary relevance predictors to filter or reweight training examples before fusion (Wu et al., 2022). Among them, one study learns a binary classifier to detect mismatched pairs and conditions cross-modal attention on the predicted relevance (Wei et al., 2021). However, this line of work often requires costly instance-level annotations and careful threshold tuning to avoid discarding helpful but atypical samples. A complementary strategy models uncertainty explicitly and propagates calibrated confidence scores from both modalities into the decoding objective (Kendall and Gal, 2017). Other research emphasizes interpretability, visualizing alignments between tokens and regions to audit spurious shortcuts and dataset artifacts (Selvaraju et al., 2017). In addition, ablation studies suggest that spatial relations among detected objects are particularly informative for relation extraction in visually complex scenes (Zareian et al., 2020). Putting these strands together, current evidence supports hybrid designs that prioritize text, admit visual cues conditionally, and train with robustness-aware objectives (Wei et al., 2021). We adopt this perspective and summarize representative approaches to motivate a principled framework for reliable multimodal extraction. Throughout, we reference prior art to contextualize modeling choices and highlight open challenges for future work.

document: Media bias has been studied extensively across social science, economics, and political communication, with diverse methods proposed to quantify ideological leanings of news outlets (Groseclose and Milyo, 2005; Miller and Riechert, 2001; Park et al., 2011; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010; Haselmayer and Jenny, 2017). Within this literature, framing bias refers to selective emphasis or omission that guides audience interpretation of events (Entman, 1993; Entman, 2007; Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2006). In NLP, computational approaches capture bias through lexical, syntactic, and discourse cues indicative of slant in political text (Recasens et al., 2013; Yano et al., 2010; Hamborg et al., 2019b). For example, dictionary and frequency based measures estimate exposure to partisan phraseology at scale (Gentzkow and Shapiro, 2010; Iyyer et al., 2014). However, most existing systems prioritize stylistic markers of “how to cover” rather than the informational dimension of “what to cover” (Park et al., 2011; Fan et al., 2019). This gap is consequential because audiences often form beliefs from which facts are selected, not only how they are worded (Entman, 2007). We therefore consider both detection and prediction, aiming to identify frames and generate neutral summaries that reduce partisan spin. Our problem setting focuses on biased headlines, which are concise yet potent carriers of framing choices (Chong and Druckman, 2007). We argue that neutralization plus summarization is preferable to raw aggregation because it reduces reader burden while preserving key facts (Hamborg et al., 2019a). Prior work on mitigation has leveraged news aggregation platforms to juxtapose coverage from multiple outlets on the same topic (Hamborg et al., 2017; Hamborg et al., 2019a). Complementary strategies provide users with auxiliary context, including viewpoint classification, multinational perspectives, and outlet profiles (Laban and Hearst, 2017; Park et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2019b). Despite these advances, current tools seldom generate cross outlet neutral summaries that explicitly counteract framing. Our approach bridges detection and generation by first estimating the salience of contested facets and then producing a bias attenuated headline. We situate this work alongside studies of agenda setting and framing effects in political communication (McCombs and Shaw, 1972; Scheufele and Tewksbury, 2007). Annotated resources for detecting bias and framing remain limited in size and scope, motivating transfer and weak supervision (Baly et al., 2018; Fan et al., 2019). Comparative analyses have also explored outlet level features such as audience, ownership, and geography to predict partisan leaning (Baum and Groeling, 2008; Hamborg et al., 2018). Methodologically, recent advances in pretrained language models enable robust bias signal extraction even under domain shift (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). Nevertheless, generation components can inadvertently amplify bias unless constrained by content selection objectives grounded in factual overlap (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Maynez et al., 2020). We therefore integrate content planning with calibration objectives that penalize sentiment loaded or source naming tokens during decoding (Pagnoni et al., 2021; Narayan et al., 2018). To evaluate progress on the informational dimension, we measure coverage of key facets across partisan sources and neutral outputs (Fan et al., 2019; Hamborg et al., 2019b). For comparability, we adopt standardized metrics from summarization and argument mining to quantify faithfulness and diversity (See et al., 2017; Fabbri et al., 2021). Evidence from cross outlet disagreement modeling suggests that explicit contrastive cues improve detection of what is emphasized versus omitted Park et al., 2011; Fan et al., 2019). Finally, we discuss practical considerations, including transparency of training data, auditing protocols, and user interface design for newsroom deployment (Mitchell et al., 2020; Diakopoulos, 2019). By situating neutral headline generation within a broader framing analysis pipeline, our work contributes tools for both diagnosing and mitigating media bias.

document: We also compare our method with previous SOTA work, where all referred systems exploit the pre-trained BERT model. As shown, compared to Chen et al. (2020), our method performs better when using very limited training data. Besides, our method can match their performance in a semi-supervised setting that uses an additional 10K unlabeled training instances. Moreover, our final model, without utilizing much external knowledge (e.g., document-level or manually designed features), can achieve very competitive performance on the full training set in comparison to most of the previous systems. Furthermore, it should be noted that achieving the absolute SOTA results of our model on the full training data is not the primary motivation of our work. This section reviews prior approaches to low-resource fine-tuning and semi-supervised learning for language understanding. Early transformer-based baselines leveraged supervised objectives with large labeled corpora, prominently BERT (Devlin et al., 2019). Subsequent research explored knowledge distillation and data augmentation to improve label efficiency (Xie et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). In addition, consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling have proved effective in scenarios with scarce annotations (Sohn et al., 2020; Arazo et al., 2020). Domain adaptation techniques aim to bridge distributional shifts between source and target tasks while retaining generalization (Ben-David et al., 2010; Ganin et al., 2016). Recent parameter-efficient tuning methods reduce compute while maintaining accuracy, which aligns with our goal of practical deployment (Houlsby et al., 2019; Lester et al., 2021). From a retrieval perspective, stronger pre-training of encoders can enhance sample efficiency and robustness (Gao et al., 2021). Several studies report similar findings (Sohn et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020), and we refer readers to (Li et al., 2021 for a comprehensive survey. Beyond classification, sequence labeling under limited data has benefited from span-level contrastive learning (Gunel et al., 2021). Data valuation methods further indicate that careful selection of a small subset can rival training on full datasets (Ghorbani and Zou, 2019; Paul et al., 2021). Our comparison emphasizes fairness by fixing backbones, tokenization, and optimization hyperparameters across methods. We also control for randomness by averaging over multiple seeds and reporting mean and standard deviation. In tasks with extreme imbalance, cost-sensitive losses often outperform naive oversampling (Cui et al., 2019). We thus evaluate under both balanced and imbalanced splits to assess robustness to label skew. Another line of work studies the impact of pre-training objectives such as masked language modeling versus replaced token detection (Devlin et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020). While these objectives yield strong representations, they can still overfit in low-signal regimes without appropriate regularization. Our method differs by explicitly modeling uncertainty at both the instance and feature levels, which reduces confirmation bias during self-labeling. We also compare against stronger semi-supervised baselines that integrate confidence-aware thresholds and temperature scaling (Sohn et al., 2020; Rizve et al., 2021). On full-data settings, performance gaps narrow, suggesting diminishing returns for aggressive regularization (Kornblith et al., 2019). Overall, the empirical evidence supports our claim that careful calibration and parameter-efficient adaptation yield robust gains with minimal external knowledge.

document: Language model pre-training followed by task-specific fine-tuning has proven to be a powerful tool for numerous NLP tasks (Liu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019b; Radford et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019a; Lewis et al., 2020; Joshi et al., 2020). BERT introduced Masked Language Model (MLM), where a model predicts masked tokens given a sequence of text input. Fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model has led to improved performance on various NLP tasks. However, MLM predictions are not made auto-regressively, which limits the capability of the BERT family for generation tasks. Raffel et al. (2019a) introduced the span corruption task in T5 as the pre-training objective, where a model predicts the masked token span using an autoregressive model. This formulation enables natural text generation because pretraining is done in a generative way. BART (Lewis et al., 2020) is similar to T5 but uses a denoising autoencoder objective in which spans are masked from the input while the complete output is predicted. Despite these advances, few works systematically investigate pre-training for very long sequence inputs. Standard transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) exhibit quadratic complexity in input length, which renders naive scaling to long contexts impractical. Consequently, recent research focuses on improving the memory and computation efficiency of transformer models for handling long input. One family of approaches uses sparse or structured attention to restrict the attention field, reducing complexity from O(n^2) to O(n log n) or O(n), including Sinkhorn (Tay et al., 2020a), Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020), ETC (Ainslie et al., 2020), and BigBird (Zaheer et al., 2020a). Another family leverages low-rank or kernel-based approximations of attention, such as Linformer (Wang et al., 2020), Performer (Choromanski et al., 2021, Random Feature Attention (Peng et al., 2021), and LUNA (Ma et al., 2021). Hybrid strategies combine local windows with global tokens and learned patterns to preserve retrieval-like behavior in long documents (Zaheer et al., 2020a; Beltagy et al., 2020). Complementary lines of work explore recurrence and memory compression to amortize attention costs across segments (Dai et al., 2019; Rae et al., 2020). Segment-level recurrence allows limited information flow across long contexts without full pairwise attention (Dai et al., 2019). External memory modules with content-based addressing can further decouple capacity from sequence length (Grave et al., 2017; Rae et al., 2020). In pre-training, span-level corruption and permutation objectives have been adapted to long inputs to encourage robustness under partial observability (Raffel et al., 2019a; Child et al., 2019). At the same time, hierarchical encoders attempt to capture discourse structure by composing sentence and document representations (Pappagari et al., 2019; Caciularu et al., 2021). Evaluation for long-context models increasingly relies on summarization, question answering, and retrieval tasks that demand reasoning over thousands of tokens (Fabbri et al., 2021; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, fair comparisons remain challenging due to differences in pre-training data, sequence lengths, and compute budgets (Dehghani et al., 2023). Moreover, strong lexical and positional priors can mask weaknesses in modeling cross-sentence dependencies, motivating controlled diagnostics (Sinha et al., 2021; Papalampidi et al., 2022). Recent work also investigates retrieval-augmented generation to mitigate context length limits by fetching relevant passages at inference time (Lewis et al., 2020b; Borgeaud et al., 2022). While these techniques offer scalability, careful integration with sequence models is needed to avoid train–test mismatch and exposure bias (Shin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). In this paper, we situate our approach within efficient attention and hierarchical modeling, emphasizing stable training and reproducible evaluation protocols (Tay et al., 2020b; Xiong et al., 2021). We argue that principled long-context pre-training can close the gap between encoder-only and encoder–decoder paradigms for both understanding and generation tasks (Joshi et al., 2020; Narang et al., 2021).

document: Two major approaches are used for evaluating chatbots or dialogue systems. Some evaluate a single turn at a time by comparing the response with a ground truth utterance from a static script of real human dialogue or get a score by combining the response with the context. Others evaluate the interaction between human and bot or between bot and bot by some scoring metrics. We will present some typical systems from each of these approaches below and discuss their pros and cons. Most existing evaluation systems are based on static scripts. Traditional metrics such as BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002b), ROUGE (Lin, 2004) and METEOR (Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) are widely used for evaluating text generation systems. More recently, automatic evaluation methods based on static scripts are gradually moving toward using pre-trained language models. Pang et al. (2020) use GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) and Mehri and Eskenazi (2020) use RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) to automatically evaluate the generation in an unsupervised way with a higher correlation to human evaluation than traditional ones. However, these static evaluation metrics which need fixed contexts are not flexible enough to assess chatbot's ability, in need of adapting to dynamic and changing contexts. We argue that interactive systems like ChatMatch are more promising as they test chatbots in a real conversation mode. Interactive evaluation systems attract increasing attention lately. Ghandeharioun et al. (2019) and Deriu and Cieliebak (2019) use dialogues between a bot and itself, which is called self-talk, to evaluate the bot in a more automatic manner. But it often leads to a lot of repeated chat context. Deriu et al. (2020) designed Spot The Bot, a framework that enables a group of bots to chat with each other and then asks humans to annotate if the bots talk more like a human or more like a bot. Prior to our work, there was still no interactive and automatic evaluation framework that works without any participation of human annotators. Our work fills this gap and moreover, it's very flexible since more complex metrics or algorithms can be plugged in as scoring functions. Our results also show that there is a strong correlation between automatic evaluation results and human judgments. Static metrics also struggle with semantic adequacy, penalizing valid paraphrases and rewarding n-gram overlap artifacts. Researchers have proposed learned metrics that regress to human scores given reference conversations (Lowe et al., 2017). Reference-free metrics estimate quality by comparing system replies with the context only (Tao et al., 2018). Embedding-based similarity methods offer partial relief but can be insensitive to factuality and safety (Zhang et al., 2020). Human evaluation remains the gold standard but is costly, slow, and difficult to reproduce at scale (Novikova et al., 2017). Consequently, interactive evaluation frameworks seek to approximate human trials by simulating users, constraining topics, and aggregating conversational signals across many turns. Some frameworks integrate task success proxies such as goal completion rates or click-through signals collected during live A/B tests (Hashimoto et al., 2019). Others leverage controllable user simulators to create repeatable stress tests that probe robustness to ambiguity, off-topic queries, and adversarial inputs. In practice, mixed protocols that combine self-play, simulated users, and lightweight human spot-checks can produce stable rankings with reasonable cost, as shown in Pang et al., 2020). A critical design question is how to separate the quality of the policy that selects actions from the quality of the language generator that realizes them. Dialogue-level metrics like success rate or average return can be complemented by turn-level diagnostics that target coherence, grounding, and persona consistency. Another challenge is domain shift, since many bots overfit to benchmarked corpora and degrade in unseen settings despite strong offline scores. Recent work emphasizes safety and bias evaluation, arguing that interactivity exposes harmful behaviors that static metrics tend to miss (Dinan et al., 2021). At the same time, reproducibility requires careful control of prompts, user profiles, and sampling policies so that results are comparable across labs and time. We therefore define a protocol that fixes simulator seeds, caps conversation length, and reports uncertainty through paired bootstrap confidence intervals. We also release evaluation scripts and leaderboards to encourage standardized reporting and facilitate fair comparison across future systems.

document: Studying persuasiveness in argumentation is an active field of research. Beyond planning content, structure, and style, researchers also consider audience-based features to model persuasiveness. Among these, Durmus and Cardie (2018) and El Baff et al. (2020) analyze how factors such as religion and political background affect outcomes. Al Khatib et al. (2020) further show that user-based features reflecting beliefs, characteristics, and personality can improve prediction of argument success. Moral foundations offer a conceptual model for understanding everyday judgments (Haidt and Joseph, 2004). Liberals emphasize care and fairness, whereas conservatives weigh all foundations more evenly, with a tilt toward loyalty, authority, and purity (Graham et al., 2009). Empirical studies corroborate the theory across behaviors and decisions (Feinberg and Willer, 2015; Fulgoni et al., 2016; Johnson and Goldwasser, 2018). For computational analysis, Kobbe et al. (2020) annotate moral foundations in arguments and link them to quality. We use their corpus to evaluate a moral classifier and to study belief-aware generation. Argument generation has been explored for multiple tasks, including counter-argument generation (Hua et al., 2019; Alshomary et al., 2021b). Other work opposes a given claim (Bilu et al., 2015; Hidey and McKeown, 2019), produces topic-controlled claims (Schiller et al., 2020), or reconstructs implicit conclusions (Alshomary et al., 2020; Syed et al., 2021). However, few approaches aim to tailor argumentative text to a specific audience. Belief-based claim generation addresses this gap by seeking claims aligned with audience stances Alshomary et al. 2021a. Project Debater provides a large-scale system for mining, retrieving, and generating arguments (Slonim et al., 2021). Its hybrid pipeline combines retrieval, mining, clustering, and rephrasing, yielding competitive quality in manual assessments (Slonim et al., 2021). We build on this system to generate morally framed claims and isolate the effect of moral framing on audiences.

document: Meta-Learning. Meta-Learning aims to improve the learning algorithm itself based on the previously learned experience (Thrun and Pratt, 1998; Hospedales et al., 2021). In general, there are three categories of meta-learning methods: model-based methods (Santoro et al., 2016; Obamuyide et al., 2019) which depend on the particular model design to facilitate fast learning; metric-based methods (Vinyals et al., 2016; Snell et al., 2017; Geng et al., 2019) which encode samples into an embedding space and classify them based on the learned distance metric; and optimization-based methods (Finn et al., 2017; Mi et al., 2019) that learn a well-generalized model initialization which allows for fast adaptation to new tasks. For low-resource scenarios in NLP, optimization-based meta-learning methods achieved promising results on tasks such as personalized dialog generation (Madotto et al., 2019; Song et al., 2020), low-resource machine translation (Gu et al., 2018; Sharaf et al., 2020), and few-shot slot tagging (Wang et al., 2021). Despite this progress, meta-learning pipelines remain sensitive to mismatches between meta-training and downstream tasks. This sensitivity is exacerbated when supervision is scarce and domains shift substantially. Memorization overfitting of Meta-learning. Meta-learning algorithms suffer from memorization overfitting. Yin et al. (2020) build an information bottleneck for the model, but this approach can decrease performance due to passive regularization. Rajendran et al. (2020) inject random noise to the ground truth of both support and query sets, while little extra knowledge is introduced to learn a good initialization. Yao et al. (2021) address overfitting by augmenting meta-training tasks through mixing up support and query sets. However, such augmentation for text assumes that labels and data distributions remain unchanged, which is often not true in practice (Chen et al., 2021). Instead of relying only on regularization or generic augmentation, we leverage information stored in support sets to augment meta-learning. Our premise is that episodic memories encode transferable structure that can guide adaptation beyond the limited supervision. External Memory for Few-shot Learning. Memory mechanisms have proven powerful for few-shot learning (Geng et al., 2019; Santoro et al., 2016; Munkhdalai et al., 2019). Current methods either refine representations stored in memory (Ramalho and Garnelo, 2018) or refine parameters using memory (Munkhdalai and Yu, 2017; Cai et al., 2018). In the NLP domain, several approaches store encoded contextual information into an external memory (Holla et al., 2020). Geng et al. (2019) propose a memory induction module with a dynamic routing algorithm for few-shot text classification. Munkhdalai et al. (2019) augment a base model with a differentiable memory that supports rapid reads and writes. Wang et al. (2021) reuse learned features stored in memory for few-shot slot tagging. More recent work explores memory as an interface for retrieval-augmented adaptation in classification and generation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018. Such designs often combine prototype formation with episodic caches to mitigate overfitting during inner-loop updates. Nevertheless, selecting what to store and how to retrieve it remains an open challenge for generalization. A complementary line of research studies meta-regularization that explicitly penalizes memorization. Techniques based on data reweighting and task-level diversity have been proposed to counteract shortcut learning in episodic training. In sequence modeling, curriculum-based sampling interacts with meta-learning dynamics to influence adaptation efficiency. We hypothesize that combining retrieval from a structured memory with robust inner-loop objectives can alleviate such sensitivity. Our approach aggregates support exemplars into a compact cache and performs uncertainty-aware retrieval at adaptation time. This design encourages reuse of abstractions learned across tasks while preventing memorization of idiosyncratic details. In the following, we position our method relative to prior work and highlight where it advances the state of the art.

document: Meta-evaluation studies increasingly interrogate how automatic metrics align with human judgments in summarization. Early work emphasized single-reference overlap metrics like ROUGE, which remain popular due to simplicity and speed (Lin, 2004). More recent approaches incorporate semantic similarity through contextual embeddings, exemplified by BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020). Despite adoption, many metrics were validated on news summarization rather than conversational data, limiting external validity. Dialogue summarization exhibits different discourse structures, speaker turns, and pragmatic cues that challenge n-gram overlap. As a result, human-anchored meta-evaluations are crucial for establishing whether metrics capture salient conversational content. Pyramids provide one rigorous framework by aggregating content units across multiple reference summaries (Nenkova and Passonneau, 2004). Building on this, Bhandari et al. curated relevance annotations to reassess metric correlations on standard corpora (Bhandari et al., 2020). Complementary work expanded the scope of human criteria beyond relevance to include faithfulness and readability (Fabbri et al., 2021b). Typical datasets in these studies include CNN or DailyMail news paired with outputs from strong abstractive systems (Hermann et al., 2015). However, these corpora underrepresent phenomena central to dialogues such as deixis, ellipsis, and speaker-specific entailments. SAMSum was proposed to address this gap by offering multi-turn chats and naturally occurring conversational implicatures (Gliwa et al., 2019). Subsequent meta-evaluations on SAMSum emphasized factual consistency but often covered only a subset of available metrics (Gabriel et al., 2021). In contrast, broader audits should examine sensitivity to paraphrase, hallucination, and truncation errors characteristic of dialogues. Another line of work analyzes model errors qualitatively to guide the design of specialized metrics for conversations (Tang et al., 2021). Related efforts investigate grounding and persona tracking as necessary ingredients for reliable automatic scoring (Chen and Yang, 2020). While these analyses are informative, inconsistent human protocols hinder aggregate comparisons across studies. For example, annotator training, unit of evaluation, and sampling strategies vary widely even within the same dataset, as in (Bhandari et al., 2020). Calibration exercises and adjudication can mitigate variance but are rarely reported with sufficient detail for replication. Cross-metric comparisons also depend on consistent preprocessing, including de-tokenization and handling of speaker tags. Reference scarcity is another obstacle because many conversational datasets provide only one gold summary per dialogue. Multi-reference augmentation via paraphrasing or expert rewriting may improve correlation estimates with human scores. Evaluation artifacts further arise when system outputs are cherry-picked or filtered, inflating measured metric performance. Robust audits therefore require stratified sampling across difficulty, length, and topical diversity to avoid selection bias. Moreover, meta-evaluations should report uncertainty with confidence intervals and significance tests for correlation gaps. Crowd-sourced judgments can scale, but their reliability in dialogue settings depends on well-crafted rubrics and context windows. In the literature, narrative citations are often preferred when discussing methodological innovations introduced by a study. Deviations from that norm can obscure attributions and complicate historical tracing of methodological advances. Our review highlights the need for standardized protocols, diversified corpora, and transparent reporting to strengthen conclusions.

document: Despite being an important research topic with real-life applications, domain adaptation for lowresource/few-shot concept-to-text NLG has not been extensively researched. Wen et al. (2016) leveraged the scarcity of target in-domain data by augmenting it with synthetic data, Tran and Nguyen (2018) used variational autoencoders in conjunction with text similarity and domain critics to better guide the fine-tuning process, while Mi et al. (2019) tackled the problem by defining domain adaptation as an optimisation meta-learning task. Most recently, Peng et al. (2020) and Kale and Rastogi (2020) have proposed the use of pretrained language models to tackle few-shot and zero-shot learning in concept-to-text NLG, achieving significant gains over strong non-pretrained baselines. Specifically, Peng et al. (2020) proposed SC-GPT, a semantically conditioned GPT-2 model, wherein, prior to few-shot learning, the GPT-2 model is further fine-tuned on a number of task-oriented dialogue datasets in order to mitigate the problem of representation bias. On the other hand, in Kale and Rastogi (2020), a set of human-authored templates are used to generate high-quality sentences corresponding to each unit of information in an MR. These are then concatenated and given as input to a T5 model (T2G2) to form a coherent sentence. In this paper's evaluation, we opt to compare our approach against the naive T5 baseline introduced by Kale and Rastogi (2020), as it is shown to clearly outperform SC-GPT by effectively replacing the underlying GPT-2 model with T5, and SC-GPT outperformed all previous non-pretrained baselines. We do not compare against T2G2, as access to human-authored templates or other such manually annotated resources, which are by nature very domain-specific and costly to create, are not necessarily guaranteed in low-resource settings. We note that T2G2 is equivalent to the naive T5 when templates are not employed. In our proposed system, the hierarchy emerges from modelling the lexicalisation and aggregation sub-tasks on separate layers. Previous attempts in exploring hierarchical structures for text generation tasks instead focused on modelling different aspects of the input or output (Fan et al., 2018. In concept-to-text NLG for task-oriented dialogues, Su et al. (2018) proposed a multi-layered decoding process where each layer was responsible for generating words associated with specific part-of-speech tags. Chen et al. (2019) and Tseng et al. (2019) took advantage of the intrinsically hierarchical structure of dialogue acts to create better input representations and ease domain adaptation. Our approach is also related to coarse-to-fine approaches, which have been explored in story (Fan et al., 2018), review (Li et al., 2019) and keyphrase (Chen et al., 2020) generation tasks. However, in these tasks, the output is not necessarily restricted to be an exact realisation of the input, and can be initially loosely prompted or drafted, and subsequently expanded. We therefore position our method as a modular alternative that benefits low-resource domains without relying on task-specific templates or extensive manual curation.

document: Previous work in the image domain has analyzed internal representations to identify adversarial examples and characterize adversarial subspaces. Feinman et al. (2017) proposed that adversarial regions exhibit lower probability density and introduced a Kernel Density (KD) detector. Ma et al. (2018) argued that Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID) better captures the geometry of complex subspaces than KD. Complementarily, Carrara et al. (2019b) modeled trajectories of hidden-layer features with an LSTM to discriminate between authentic and adversarial inputs. Building on distributional structure, Lee et al. (2018) introduced a Mahalanobis Distance Analysis (MDA) detector that compares test samples to class-conditional Gaussians in logit space. Li and Li, 2016) further analyzed convolutional-layer statistics, showing that variance in least principal directions separates original from adversarial inputs but remains vulnerable to tail-suppression attacks. Mao et al. (2019) unified feature learning and detection by jointly learning deep representations tailored for adversary identification. Beyond embedding-space heuristics, Cohen et al. (2019) leveraged influence functions with nearest-neighbor distances, matching or surpassing prior detectors. A parallel line examines uncertainty: Smith and Gal (2018) argue that adversarial inputs are out-of-distribution, implying high epistemic uncertainty, yet exact Bayesian methods are often impractical and approximations are widely used (Leibig et al., 2017; Gal, 2016; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). In NLP, detectors exploit surface anomalies at the character level (Sakaguchi et al., 2017) and spelling errors (Mays et al., 1991; Islam and Inkpen, 2009), but practical threats are increasingly semantic-preserving at the word level. Recent work designs token-level discriminators to flag and correct perturbations, complements perplexity-based screening (Raina et al., 2020; Han et al., 2020; Minervini and Riedel, 2018), and achieves state-of-the-art with frequency-guided substitution tests (Mozes et al., 2020).

document:
Document-level neural machine translation is an important task and has been abundantly studied with multiple datasets as well as methods.

The mainstream research in this field is the model architecture improvement.
Specifically, several recent attempts extend the Sent2Sent approach to the Doc2Sent-like one.
Wang et al. (2017); Miculicich et al. (2018); Tan et al. (2019, 2020) introduce additional encoders or query layers with attention models and feed the history contexts into decoders.
Maruf and Haffari (2018) and Tu et al. (2018) propose to augment NMT models with a cache-like memory network, which generates the translation depending on the decoder history retrieved from the memory.
Besides, some works intend to resolve this problem in other ways.
Jean and Cho (2019) propose a regularization term encouraging models to focus more on additional context via a multi-level pairwise ranking loss.
Yu et al. (2020) utilize a noisy-channel reranker with Bayes' rule.
Garcia et al. (2019) also share similar ideas with us.
Tiedemann and Scherrer (2017) and Bawden et al. (2017) explore concatenating two consecutive sentences and generating two sentences directly.
Obviously, we leverage much longer information and capture the full context.
Junczys-Dowmunt (2019) cuts documents into long segments and feeds them into training as in BERT Devlin et al., 2019).
There are at least three main differences.
First, they add specific boundary tokens between sentences, whereas we translate original documents without any additional processing.
Second, we propose a multi-resolution training paradigm that shows consistent improvements compared with regular training.
Third, for extremely long documents, they restrict segment length to 1,000 tokens or truncate, while we preserve entire documents and achieve literal document-to-document training and inference.
Finally, our work is also related to long sequence generation models like GPT-1 (Radford et al., 2018), GPT-2, and Transformer-XL (Dai et al., 2019).
These studies suggest that deep neural generators have the potential to process long-range sequences effectively.
Beyond architecture, context selection and discourse modeling have been central to document-level translation.
Voita et al. (2019) design context-aware attention mechanisms that learn when to use extra-sentential cues.
Zhang et al. (2020) propose sentence-level context gates to balance source and history information.
Parallel work explores discourse phenomena such as anaphora resolution and lexical cohesion to reduce inconsistencies across sentences (Lopes et al., 2020).
Evaluation remains challenging because standard sentence-level metrics can under-reward document coherence (Barrault et al., 2019).
Recent test suites attempt to isolate pronoun translation, ellipsis, and tense consistency, enabling more diagnostic assessment (Müller et al., 2020).
In this study, we build on these insights to pursue scalable, end-to-end models that translate whole documents with minimal heuristics.

document: Open-Domain Passage Retrieval has been a hot research topic in recent years. It requires a system to extract evidence passages for a specific question from a large corpus like Wikipedia while delivering both high accuracy and low latency. Traditional approaches such as TF-IDF (Ramos et al., 2003) and BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) rely on lexical overlap between queries and documents. Although these lexical baselines are fast, they fail to capture non-lexical semantic similarity and therefore underperform on retrieval accuracy. With advances in pretrained language models like BERT and RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), cross-encoder architectures have been proposed for retrieval (Vig and Ramea, 2019; Wolf et al., 2019). Despite strong accuracy, cross-encoders are often too slow for production-scale use. Bi-encoder models mitigate this by precomputing dense representations of passages for efficient nearest-neighbor search. Early work pretrains bi-encoders using the Inverse Cloze Task, and DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) popularizes a contrastive objective for learning dense passage embeddings. Building on DPR, subsequent studies improve sampling strategies (Xiong et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021), add pretraining signals (Sachan et al., 2021), or distill knowledge from cross-encoders (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Yang et al., 2021). Our method follows this contrastive learning line but targets modeling granularity directly through a tailored training strategy. Specifically, we optimize at the sentence or phrase level to better align fine-grained evidence with queries. Contrastive learning has rapidly gained traction across modalities, with notable advances in computer vision in (He et al., 2020) inspiring analogous techniques in NLP (Gao et al., 2021; Giorgi et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). For ODPR, two complementary directions have emerged: improving sampling of positives and hard negatives (Manmatha et al., 2017; Xiong et al., 2020), and improving the training framework itself. DensePhrase uses a memory bank akin to MoCo to expand in-batch negatives without increasing GPU usage (Lee et al., 2021; He et al., 2020). Closely related to our goals, Akkalyoncu Yilmaz et al. (2019) explore sentence-level evidence for dense retrieval, focusing on re-ranking rather than first-stage retrieval.

document: One important aspect of our study is the evaluation of crosslingual consistency while performing multilingual NER or EL tasks. We conceptualize consistency as the stability of predictions across equivalent inputs rendered in different languages. This notion aligns with practical demands in multilingual products where error profiles must not drift unpredictably across locales. Prior work has emphasized measuring invariances in entity recognition and linking under constrained settings. In (Bianchi et al., 2021; Conneau et al., 2020 such invariances are associated with transfer protocols that limit domain shift. However, the evaluation of language-invariant properties remains challenging when pretraining data is skewed by region or topic. An ideal scenario assumes that the transformation between languages preserves task-relevant features. In practice, commercially available models often encode domain dependencies that undermine such assumptions. We therefore examine robustness diagnostics tailored to sequence-labeling and entity-linking pipelines. Effective measurement of dataset quality is another aspect of growing significance. Training large language models requires huge amounts of data, and the resulting inferences often reflect dataset biases. Data-centric analyses have shown that quality-aware design decisions can improve overall performance. For example, Swayamdipta et al. (2020) introduced data maps that categorize instances by confidence, variability, and correctness. Their framework highlights out-of-distribution regions, class-probability fluctuations, and annotation-level discrepancies. We extend these ideas to multilingual corpora by quantifying instance difficulty across language pairs. Usually, multilingual datasets are collected from diverse sources with uneven curation standards. It is therefore important to assess whether their utility is representative for native speakers. We find that MasakhaNER (Adelani et al., 2021) exemplifies community-driven collection that aligns with local usage. Such datasets can reduce cultural mismatches and support fairer evaluations. At the same time, coverage gaps persist in low-resource settings despite recent advances. Joshi et al. (2020) characterized this landscape through typological coverage indices and resource trajectories. Their analysis shows that the current state-of-the-art applications are far from universally accessible. We corroborate this finding by auditing error distributions across macro-areas and language families. Methodologically, we adopt a mixed evaluation protocol combining zero-shot transfer and language-specific tuning. This protocol helps disentangle transferability from capacity to absorb limited supervision. We further report per-language calibration metrics to capture confidence alignment. To ensure comparability, we harmonize label sets and entity knowledge bases across languages. We also provide ablations that remove name transliteration to isolate morphological effects. For reproducibility, we release preprocessing scripts and hyperparameters aligned with common baselines. A persistent question is whether consistency metrics correlate with user-perceived quality. We address this by conducting human preference studies on a subset of outputs. The studies reveal that certain inconsistencies are tolerated when semantic intent remains clear. Conversely, subtle entity boundary shifts can disproportionately erode trust in high-stakes domains. We therefore argue for evaluation suites that combine automatic metrics with lightweight human checks. As a pointer to related analysis, we note that similar concerns were raised in (Bianchi, Conneau, and Lample 2021). Overall, our review motivates principled benchmarking that foregrounds data quality, crosslingual equity, and actionable diagnostics.

document: It is well known that reliable uncertainty estimates can be obtained simply by constructing an ensemble of decorrelated neural networks (deep ensemble) (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). However, such a straightforward approach is coupled with substantial computational and memory overhead during training an ensemble, performing inference of all its components, and storing multiple versions of weights, which is a serious obstacle to deploying ensemble-based uncertainty estimation methods in practice. Uncertainty estimation is a built-in capability of Bayesian neural networks (Blundell et al., 2015). However, such models have similar issues as ensembles and also require special training procedures. Recently, it was shown by Gal and Ghahramani (2016) that dropout, a well known regularization technique, is mathematically equivalent to approximate variational inference in the deep Gaussian process. This method, known as Monte Carlo (MC) dropout, uses a Bernoulli approximation to the variational distribution on the network units and introduces no additional parameters for the approximate posterior. MC dropout does not introduce any overhead during training and does not require any additional memory. The main disadvantage of this method is that it often requires many forward-pass samplings, which makes it computationally expensive at inference time. Building on these ideas, researchers have explored lighter alternatives to full Bayesian inference that retain useful uncertainty estimates. Recently, many works have investigated approximate Bayesian inference for neural networks using deterministic approaches (Lee et al., 2018; Van Amersfoort et al., 2020; Mukhoti et al., 2021). These methods do not introduce notable overhead for inference, storing weights, and usually require training time comparable to standard models. However, most of the research in this area has been conducted on computer vision tasks. For text classification, a series of works investigates UE methods for the OOD detection task Podolskiy et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2021; Hu and Khan, 2021). In this work, we focus on the more challenging task of misclassification detection. While OOD detection requires modeling primarily epistemic uncertainty caused by limited training data, misclassification detection also requires modeling aleatoric uncertainty due to noise and ambiguity in data (Mukhoti et al., 2021). We consider recently proposed methods in this area that are evaluated in text processing. Three recent works propose techniques for misclassification detection based on additive regularization of the training loss. Zhang et al. (2019) suggest adding a penalty that reduces the Euclidean distance between training instances of the same class and increases the distance between instances of different classes. He et al. (2020) propose using two components in the loss that reduce the difference between outputs from two versions of a model initialized with different weights. They also use mix-up (Thulasidasan et al., 2019) to generate additional training instance representations that help capture aleatoric uncertainty, along with self-ensembling and a distinctiveness score to measure epistemic uncertainty. Xin et al. (2021) introduce a regularizer that penalizes overconfident instances with high loss. In another recent work, Shelmanov et al. (2021) combine MC dropout with a Determinantal Point Process to improve the diversity of predictions by considering correlations between neurons and sampling diverse units for activation in a dropout layer. In this paper, we conduct a systematic empirical investigation of UE methods on NLP tasks. We evaluate combinations of methods that were not tested before, propose two modifications, and present the first comprehensive study of UE for named entity recognition.
