document: Lexically constrained translation aims to integrate required terminology into machine-generated outputs without sacrificing fluency. Prior work explores constraints during training via code-switching data augmentation (Dinu et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019) and via auxiliary objectives such as token- or span-level masking (Ailem et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021). At inference time, constrained decoding approaches include placeholder tags (Luong et al., 2015; Crego et al., 2016), grid beam search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018), and alignment-enhanced decoding (Alkhouli et al., 2018; Song et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). For efficiency, recent studies also consider non-autoregressive constrained translation that avoids the left-to-right bottleneck. Susanto et al. (2020) modify the Levenshtein Transformer inference procedure to prevent deletion of constraint words during iterative editing. Xu and Carpuat (2021) extend this idea with a reposition operation that can reorder constraint tokens. Building on these insights, our approach enriches non-autoregressive models with terminology-driven alignment signals to improve context modeling, especially for rare constraints. Non-autoregressive translation (NAT) offers substantial speedups but often underperforms autoregressive baselines due to multimodality in the target space. Sequence-level knowledge distillation reduces output diversity and reordering complexity by training NAT models on autoregressive teacher outputs (Kim and Rush, 2016; Gu et al., 2018). This practice has been shown to narrow the modality gap across several language pairs (Zhou et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021). Beyond distillation, researchers alleviate multimodality using latent-variable formulations (Kaiser et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2020) and iterative refinement (Ghazvininejad et al., 2019; Stern et al., 2019; Gu et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020). Complementary lines of work investigate improved training objectives, including alignment-aware or contrastive losses (Du et al., 2021. Editing-based iterative NAT models are particularly amenable to the incorporation of lexical constraints (Susanto et al., 2020; Xu and Carpuat, 2021). However, balancing constraint satisfaction with global adequacy remains challenging when constraints are ambiguous or under-specified. We therefore propose a lightweight mechanism that conditions edit operations on terminology alignments while preserving decoding parallelism.

document: Anaphora encompasses coreference, where expressions denote the same entity, and bridging, where expressions are linked by semantic or encyclopedic relations (Clark and Manning, 2015; Ng, 2017; Asher and Lascarides, 1998; Hou et al., 2018). Most available corpora emphasize declarative text across news, Wikipedia, and scientific writing (Poesio et al., 2008; Pradhan et al., 2012; Ghaddar and Langlais, 2016b; Cohen et al., 2017). The CoNLL 2012 dataset provides large-scale annotations for generic coreference resolution (Pradhan et al., 2012). It follows the OntoNotes 5.0 guidelines, modeling identity and appositive links without preserving relation directionality (Weischedel et al., 2013). WikiCoref similarly annotates entities and links in Wikipedia articles (Ghaddar and Langlais, 2016b). In the biomedical domain, BioNLP-ST 2011 targeted gene-related references in abstracts, whereas CRAFT includes full-article annotations (Cohen et al., 2017). SCIERC adds cross-sentence scientific entity links and event-centric relations (Luan et al., 2018). Defining bridging is challenging, and prior work distinguishes referential versus lexical bridging, including meronymic and taxonomic relations (Zeldes, 2017; Hou et al., 2018). Foundational resources such as ARRAU, ISNotes, BASHI, and SciCorp document varied mixtures of coreference and bridging in news and scientific text (Poesio et al., 2008; Hou et al., 2018; Rösiger, 2018a; Rösiger, 2016). Procedural texts remain under-resourced despite domain needs in chemistry, robotics, and cooking (Fang et al., 2021a; Modi et al., 2016). Recipe understanding has leveraged both visual demonstrations and linguistic instructions across multiple languages (Huang et al., 2017; Nishimura et al., 2020; Agarwal and Miller, 2011; Kiddon et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2020). Typical pipelines represent instructions as action-centered graphs over ingredients and tools (Batra et al., 2020; Marin et al., 2019; Mori et al., 2014). However, such graph abstractions often fail to capture pronouns, definite descriptions, and implicit references needed for full anaphora resolution. Datasets like RISeC address verb-centric zero anaphora but do not model generic bridging or nominal coreference (Jiang et al., 2020). Coreference modeling has progressed from mention-ranking to span-ranking architectures, with the latter becoming the de facto standard Lee et al. 2017. Numerous variants improve scoring, pruning, and contextualization, yielding robust gains on OntoNotes and scientific benchmarks (Zhang et al., 2018; Grobol, 2019; Kantor and Globerson, 2019). Transfer learning further boosts performance by pretraining on large source domains and fine-tuning on targets (Brack et al., 2021; Xia and Van Durme, 2021). For bridging, rule-based patterns and feature-driven classifiers have been contrasted with modern neural approaches (Hou et al., 2014; Rösiger, 2018b; Hou, 2018b; Hou, 2020; Yu and Poesio, 2020). One line of work frames bridging as question answering and adapts pretrained readers from generic QA corpora (Hou, 2020). Another line jointly trains bridging with coreference using end-to-end encoders (Lee et al., 2017 and reports strong gains (Yu and Poesio, 2020; Fang et al., 2021a). Despite these advances, evaluation remains skewed toward declarative prose rather than procedural narratives common in instructional texts. Procedural corpora with unified treatment of coreference and bridging are scarce, limiting model generalization and diagnostics. This gap motivates creating richer annotations that capture entity states, tool transformations, and implicit roles across steps (Harashima and Hiramatsu, 2020; Harashima et al., 2016). It also calls for models that integrate discourse structure with event sequencing to resolve long-distance links (Agarwal and Miller, 2011; Kiddon et al., 2015). Building on these insights, we outline a framework that unifies coreference and bridging within instructional domains while remaining compatible with standard evaluation protocols (Lee et al., 2018).

With the rise of social media and hate speech forums online (Phadke and Mitra, 2020; Szendro, 2021), offensive language detection has become more important than ever before. Several recent works focus on characterizing various types of offensive language detection (Fortuna and Nunes, 2018; Shushkevich and Cardiff, 2019; spanning lexicon-based, neural, and hybrid approaches. But, works that focus solely on stereotype detection in English are scarce. This is partly because stereotypes tend to be subtler offenses than other types of offensive language and hence receive less immediate focus, and in part due to the challenge of requiring knowledge of a stereotype's social salience to reliably annotate data for the task. We approach this problem by decomposing stereotypical text into linguistically grounded facets and crowd-sourcing annotations only for aspects that require linguistic understanding rather than world knowledge. Few recent works have focused solely on stereotypes, some proposing pioneering diagnostic datasets (Nadeem et al., 2020; Nangia et al., 2020) while others develop knowledge-based and semi-supervised models (Fraser et al., 2021; Badjatiya et al., 2019) for identifying stereotypical text. Computational models trained on illustrative examples—for instance, 1) Ethiopians like stew (Explicit Stereotype), 2) The lawyer misrepresented the situation and tricked the person (Implicit Stereotypical Association), 3) Jews spend money frivolously (Anti-Stereotypes), and 4) There is an Asian family that lives down the street (Non-Stereotypes)—often either rely on datasets built for adjacent tasks such as hate speech detection or focus on adapted diagnostic sets for classification. However, diagnostic datasets suffer from limited coverage of naturally occurring text due to their crowd-sourced construction procedures (Blodgett et al., 2021b). We address these issues by collecting in-the-wild data from Reddit, mining specific subreddits that predominantly contain subtle stereotypical expressions.

Given the low-resource setting for stereotype detection, semi-supervised data annotation is a plausible solution. Several recent works have also explored reinforcement-learning–guided semi-supervision (Ye et al., 2020; Konyushkova et al., 2020; Laskin et al., 2020). Ye et al. (2020), in particular, work with a single task and unlabeled data to generate automated annotations for new examples. In contrast, we leverage data from neighboring tasks with different labels for multi-task learning and apply an RL agent to select examples for training the auxiliary task in such a way that benefits the target task the most. Our approach emphasizes transfer from related signals such as sentiment and stance and uses curriculum mechanisms to stabilize learning over noisy pseudo-labels. For reproducibility, we follow established practices in dataset construction (Li et al., 2021 and align our evaluation with prior fairness auditing guidelines.

document: Prompt Learning The concept of prompt-based learning starts from the GPT-3 model (Brown et al., 2020), which can complete various zero-shot or few-shot tasks with natural language instructions. Previous works design different prompts to query language models to extract knowledge triples (Petroni et al., 2019) or classify sentences into predefined categories (Schick and Schütze, 2021) in the few-shot setting. They construct various discrete prompts manually for these tasks. To reduce the human effort in this selection process, Gao et al. (2021) propose to expand prompts using pre-trained language models. However, the selection of discrete prompts is still an independent process and cannot be optimized together with the downstream tasks in an end-to-end manner. To solve this issue, Lester et al. (2021) and Li and Liang (2021) propose to use soft prompts, which are sets of trainable vectors in frozen pretrained language models. Unlike hard prompts, these vectors do not correspond to any real words. This design allows joint optimization with the downstream tasks in an end-to-end manner. As shown in Li and Liang (2021), PLMs with soft prompts can often perform better in the low-resource setting. Generative Data Augmentation There are previous works applying LMs or PLMs to generate synthetic data. Hou et al. (2018) use a seq2seq generative model to produce diverse utterances that improve dialogue understanding models. Xia et al. (2019, 2020) apply LMs and PLMs to learn to generate new synthetic data for NLU tasks after fine-tuning on relatively large training data. These works often directly apply off-the-shelf LMs or PLMs to generate synthetic data. To the best of our knowledge, PromDA is the first prompt-based PLM especially designed for the data augmentation task. Beyond utterance generation, prompt-guided sampling has been explored for paraphrasing, slot filling, and commonsense elicitation. Such methods typically condition on task-specific templates to steer the generator toward label-preserving outputs. Nevertheless, prompt templates can be brittle under domain shift and subtle lexical variations. A growing body of work studies automatic prompt induction to reduce manual effort while maintaining control. In this paradigm, discrete search and continuous relaxation are often combined to explore large template spaces efficiently. Soft prompting extends this idea by embedding virtual tokens whose values are learned from downstream losses. In frozen-parameter regimes, soft prompts can steer model behavior without full fine-tuning and with strong parameter efficiency. Several influential studies provide the empirical motivation for this paradigm, Brown et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Petroni et al., and they collectively illustrate the value of prompts in knowledge elicitation and classification. For classification in low-resource scenarios, prompt-based tuning narrows the gap to fully supervised baselines while improving calibration. For sequence labeling, verbalizer design and span mapping become crucial determinants of accuracy and robustness. Prompt-based data augmentation aims to improve generalization by sampling task-consistent paraphrases aligned with the inference template. Compared with naive back-translation, generator-guided prompting can target decision boundaries more effectively and reduce redundancy. However, uncontrolled generation risks label drift, spurious shortcuts, and semantic leakage across train and test splits. Recent studies therefore integrate filtering based on entailment models, calibration criteria, or reward modeling to ensure quality. Our perspective is that augmentation should be aligned with the very prompt used at inference time to minimize mismatch. This alignment mitigates distributional gaps between training signals and test-time instructions in realistic deployments. Moreover, jointly optimizing prompts and augmentation yields stable improvements across random seeds and datasets. Prior surveys have outlined common pitfalls in prompt evaluation, negative transfer, and reproducibility in small-sample settings. Building on these insights, we propose a standardized protocol for low-resource benchmarking that is model-agnostic. The protocol emphasizes reporting variance, calibration settings, and template sensitivity to improve comparability across studies. It also recommends stratified label sampling and difficulty-controlled generation to avoid overfitting and leakage. Together, these elements clarify the role of prompt-based augmentation in practical NLP systems and motivate the approach investigated in this paper.

document: Prompt-based few-shot fine-tuning adapts pretrained language models to new tasks by conditioning on task descriptions and a handful of labeled examples. Early studies established its viability on moderately sized models such as BERT and RoBERTa (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Pattern-Exploiting Training reframes classification as masked language modeling with hand-crafted or searched patterns (Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b). AutoPrompt uses gradient signals to select discrete trigger tokens that elicit desired model behavior (Shin et al., 2020). Null prompts show that minimal concatenation with a [MASK] token can already yield competitive baselines (Logan et al., 2021). Beyond discrete templates, soft-prompting injects continuous embeddings as virtual tokens and tunes only those parameters (Lester et al., 2021). Complementary work pre-trains soft prompts to provide stronger initializations for downstream tuning (Gu et al., 2021). Demonstration-augmented prompting further enriches inputs by adding labeled exemplars, as explored by LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021). Our study builds on these ideas by coupling multiple demonstrations with a compact, globally shared soft memory for prompt tokens. In contrast to LM-BFF, we allocate several demonstrations per label and automatically verbalize label phrases to improve semantic alignment. We compare against manual prompts, LM-BFF, and full fine-tuning under identical data budgets (Gao et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Prior strong baselines for few-shot classification include Zhang and Wang, 2020; Li, 2021. For completeness, we also evaluate in-context learning with frozen RoBERTa-large using GPT-3 style prompting, with no parameter updates (Brown et al., 2020). All results are averaged over five random splits, and we report means and standard deviations to account for sensitivity to seed choice. Our implementation follows common preprocessing and tuning recipes, including layerwise learning-rate decay and class-balanced sampling (Mosbach et al., 2021). To isolate the effect of demonstrations, we keep the underlying encoder fixed across methods and vary only the prompt mechanism. Architecturally, we adopt a transformer encoder with a lightweight prefix of trainable embeddings that functions as the soft memory (Li and Liang, 2021). The approach maintains efficiency by freezing the bulk of model weights while updating fewer than one percent of parameters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Compared to discrete patterns, continuous prompts reduce brittleness to tokenization artifacts and permit smooth optimization (Lester et al., 2021). We further ablate the number of demonstrations per label and the sharing strategy across tasks to understand scaling behavior. In addition to accuracy, we analyze calibration, sample efficiency, and robustness to prompt paraphrases (Jiang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). Finally, we situate our findings within the broader trend of replacing task-specific heads with input-conditioned control of sequence-to-sequence models Vaswani et al., 2017). This perspective highlights the unifying role of prompts as lightweight interfaces that steer pretrained knowledge without extensive retraining. Altogether, our results clarify when and why multi-demonstration soft prompting outperforms discrete and full fine-tuning, and they chart practical guidelines for selecting prompt mechanisms in resource-constrained settings.

document: Controllable text generation aims to steer pretrained language models toward desired attributes without compromising fluency and content preservation. Prior research has explored autoencoding approaches that learn continuous sentence representations for manipulation and decoding back to text. Representative methods include cross-aligned and back-translation based models that attempt to disentangle content and style in latent space (Shen et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2020; John et al., 2019). In contrast to training new encoders or decoders, we investigate whether steering vectors can be extracted directly from hidden states of large pretrained models. Our central hypothesis is that linear directions exist that correlate with interpretable attributes and can be applied at inference without fine-tuning. As a canonical example, Shen et al. propose cross-aligned autoencoders for style transfer. Building on this intuition, we show that attribute shifts can be induced by simple vector arithmetic in embedding or activation spaces. A growing body of work seeks lightweight control via prompts, adapters, or residual controllers to modulate generation without retraining the backbone. Prompt-based steering has proved effective for polarity, formality, and toxicity control when combined with calibrated decoding strategies (Liu et al., 2021; Krause et al., 2021). Adapter layers provide parameter-efficient fine-tuning by inserting small bottleneck modules into each transformer block while freezing the base model (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Other methods modify logits or gradients at inference time using auxiliary classifiers in order to bias token selection toward desired attributes (Gulcehre et al., 2015; Dathathri et al., 2019). Our approach differs by identifying intrinsic directions already present in the model and applying them through vector operations that leave parameters unchanged. We formalize steering as a linear operator on hidden representations derived from matched pairs of sentences that contrast a target attribute. The operator can then be composed with the forward pass to nudge activations toward the attribute while preserving core semantics. Unlike classifier-guided decoding, our method introduces no additional losses or external models at training time. We evaluate on sentiment transfer, detoxification, and formality control across multiple corpora with automatic and human metrics. Fluency is assessed with perplexity and grammaticality judgments, while content preservation is measured via semantic similarity and information overlap. We further analyze attribute strength distributions as a function of steering magnitude to reveal robustness and saturation effects. Related efforts on representation arithmetic in vision and language suggest that linear structure can encode disentangled factors in deep models (Mikolov et al., 2013; Radford et al., 2017; Ethayarajh, 2019). In controllable generation specifically, iterative unlikelihood objectives and contrastive decoding have also been used to suppress undesired outputs while amplifying desired ones (Welleck et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022). In (Dathathri et al., 2019), for example, gradients from a discriminator are used at each step to adjust the token distribution toward a chosen attribute. While effective, such approaches often incur latency and stability costs due to per-token optimization. By contrast, our operator is computed once from a small labeled or weakly labeled set and then reused efficiently at inference. We also compare against reinforcement learning from attribute rewards, which typically demands extensive exploration and careful reward shaping to avoid mode collapse (Ziegler et al., 2019; Paulus et al., 2020). Empirically, we find that steering at mid-layer activations offers a better trade-off between attribute strength and content preservation than output-layer interventions. Ablation studies show that the quality of contrastive pairs used to estimate the direction is a key determinant of success. Finally, we discuss ethical considerations around attribute control, including risks of misuse and distributional harms, and outline mitigation strategies such as transparency reports and opt-out mechanisms. Our findings indicate that principled, training-free steering is a viable alternative to parameter-efficient fine-tuning for a broad class of controllable generation tasks.

document: The standard practice of using BERT is fine-tuning, i.e. the entirety of the model parameters is adjusted on the training corpus of the downstream task, so that the model is adapted to that specific task (Devlin et al., 2019). There is also an alternative feature-based approach, used by ELMo (Peters et al., 2018). In the latter approach, the pre-trained model is regarded as a feature extractor with frozen parameters. During the learning of a downstream task, one feeds a fixed or learnable combination of the model's intermediate representations as input to the task-specific module, and only the parameters of the latter will be updated. It has been shown that the fine-tuning approach is generally superior to the feature-based approach for BERT in terms of task performance (Devlin et al., 2019;Peters et al., 2019). A natural middle ground between these two approaches is partial fine-tuning, i.e. only fine-tuning some topmost layers of BERT while keeping the remaining bottom layers frozen. This approach has been studied by Houlsby et al. (2019) and Merchant et al. (2020), where the authors observed that finetuning only the top layers can almost achieve the performance of full fine-tuning on several GLUE tasks. The approach of partial fine-tuning essentially regards the bottom layers of BERT as a feature extractor. Freezing weights from bottom layers is a sensible idea as previous studies show that the mid layer representations produced by BERT are most transferrable, whereas the top layers representations are more task-oriented (Wang et al., 2019;Tenney et al., 2019b,a;Merchant et al., 2020). Nevertheless, full fine-tuning remains costly in memory and storage because one copy of all parameters must be maintained per task. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) addresses this cost by training small task-specific modules while keeping the base model fixed. Adapter layers insert bottleneck modules within each Transformer block and optimize only a few percent of parameters while matching the accuracy of full fine-tuning on many benchmarks (Houlsby et al., 2019;Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Subsequent work proposed techniques for sharing and combining adapters across tasks and languages, further improving multitask generalization (Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Orthogonal directions compress or reparameterize updates, such as low-rank adaptation that decomposes weight deltas into rank-constrained matrices (Hu et al., 2022). Prompt-based methods move learning to the input space by optimizing soft prompts or prefixes that steer frozen models during decoding (Lester et al., 2021;Li and Liang, 2021). Another extreme reduces training to biases in layer normalization or to a subset of bias vectors, yielding surprisingly strong baselines with minimal trainable parameters (Zaken et al., 2022). While these methods differ in mechanism, they share the intuition that large pretrained models provide broadly useful features that require only small task-specific adjustments. Empirical comparisons show that PEFT approaches are particularly attractive in low-resource and multilingual settings, where avoiding overfitting and catastrophic forgetting is crucial (Pfeiffer et al., 2020). In production, PEFT also simplifies deployment by enabling a single shared backbone with many lightweight task adapters swapped at inference time. Despite these advantages, the community still debates how many layers should be adapted and where adaptation is most beneficial. Analyses suggest that early layers encode general lexical and syntactic patterns, whereas later layers capture task semantics, implying that adaptation should concentrate near the top (Tenney et al., 2019a;Merchant et al., 2020). Controlled ablations confirm that partial fine-tuning of higher blocks yields most of the gains with far fewer trainable parameters than full updates (Merchant et al., 2020). From an optimization perspective, restricting updates can act as a regularizer that stabilizes training under distribution shift. From a systems perspective, smaller update footprints reduce communication overhead in federated or multi-tenant environments. Nevertheless, some tasks with complex reasoning or long-range dependencies still benefit from full model adaptation, especially when training data is abundant. A nuanced picture therefore emerges in which the optimal adaptation strategy depends on architecture depth, task type, and data regime. To better map this space, recent studies benchmark PEFT methods across a standardized suite of tasks, controlling for parameter counts and hyperparameters (Hu et al., 2022;Lester et al., 2021). Results consistently indicate competitive performance for adapters, prefixes, and low-rank updates, though differences appear on generation-heavy tasks. Early evidence for adapters was reported by Houlsby et al. 2019 in encoder-only models, and later extended to sequence-to-sequence architectures. Complementary findings highlight that combining multiple parameter-efficient modules can further bridge any residual gap to full fine-tuning (Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Beyond accuracy, storage efficiency is often the decisive metric in multi-task training where hundreds of tasks are common. Here, PEFT methods can yield order-of-magnitude reductions in per-task footprint without retraining or recompiling the backbone. However, careful hyperparameter selection remains necessary, particularly for learning rates, initialization scales, and the depth at which modules are inserted. Moreover, evaluation should consider robustness to domain shift, calibration, and fairness in addition to headline metrics. These observations motivate our investigation of partial fine-tuning regimes that trade off task performance against parameter and storage efficiency. We provide a systematic comparison that quantifies these trade-offs and clarifies when partial adaptation suffices and when full updates are warranted.

document:
Rational Speech Act (RSA)

Our work builds on pragmatic reasoning for language production and interpretation. The Rational Speech Act framework models cooperative communication between speakers and listeners. In its basic form, RSA formalizes nested reasoning over literal and pragmatic agents. A literal speaker selects utterances to truthfully describe world states under a semantics. A pragmatic listener inverts this model to infer likely states given the utterance. A higher-level pragmatic speaker anticipates the listener’s inference to choose more informative messages. This recursive structure captures Gricean maxims in a probabilistic manner. Prior research has successfully applied RSA to reference games with visual contexts (Frank and Goodman, 2012). In (Andreas and Klein, 2016), a referential game setup highlights the benefits of explicit pragmatic reasoning. Related work further studies visually grounded pragmatics and distractor-aware captioning (Monroe and Potts, 2015). In grounded captioning, pragmatic reasoning can improve discriminativity over distractors. Our study adopts a referential game in which images are sampled with one target and several foils. The literal speaker is implemented with a pretrained image captioner that maps images to descriptions. The pragmatic listener computes posterior beliefs over images conditioned on the caption. The pragmatic speaker optimizes expected utility with respect to the listener’s beliefs. Utility balances accuracy, brevity, and stylistic constraints relevant to the domain. We further account for asymmetric knowledge between agents to reflect real-world heterogeneity. In many applications, the listener may have restricted visual or linguistic capacity. Designing speakers that accommodate such disparities can yield more robust communication. We propose a disparity parameter that modulates the listener model used by the speaker. This parameter captures reduced perception, vocabulary gaps, or biased priors. When disparity increases, the speaker places greater weight on redundancy and clarity. Our framework unifies pragmatic captioning with adaptive assistance objectives. We evaluate the approach on controlled synthetic scenes and natural image datasets. Metrics include target selection accuracy, caption discriminativity, and brevity norms. Human studies further assess perceived helpfulness and naturalness of the captions. Results show consistent gains over literal baselines across disparity settings. Improvements are largest when the listener lacks key attributes present in training. Ablation analysis reveals the importance of calibrated listener models within RSA. We also compare against alternative pragmatic formulations without recursion. Those models underperform when distractors share attributes with the target. Our implementation follows common conventions for likelihoods and priors in RSA. For historical context, we note that pragmatic inference in reference games has deep roots in cognitive science, and we therefore situate our contribution within research on rational cooperative communication.

document: In NLP, transformer-based models have become central to both performance and interpretability research. Attention mechanisms are often discussed as potential explanations for model behavior. As shown by (Serrano and A. Smith, 2019 early analyses questioned whether attention weights align with feature importance. Subsequent work reinforced this view by contrasting attention with gradient-based attributions (Jain and Wallace, 2019; Wiegreffe and Pinter, 2019). In this section, we review gradient-based feature attribution methods for neural language models. Simple Gradient computes a saliency map as the derivative of the output with respect to the input embedding (Simonyan et al., 2013). Although intuitive, this map is often noisy and difficult to interpret. Gradient times Input sharpens saliency by multiplying each gradient by its corresponding input dimension (Shrikumar et al., 2017). In practice, this technique has been adapted to token-level attributions in NLP classification and sequence labeling. Layer-wise relevance propagation has been shown to be equivalent to Gradient times Input up to a scaling factor in certain linear settings (Bach et al., 2015). SmoothGrad reduces visual noise by averaging gradients over noisy perturbations of the input (Smilkov et al., 2017). Guided Backpropagation modifies the backward pass through ReLU to emphasize positive signals in lower layers (Springenberg et al., 2014). Extensions such as Grad-CAM tailor class-specific localization to convolutional architectures and are less directly applicable to transformers (Selvaraju et al., 2017). Because transformers lack spatial pooling and canonical feature maps, many vision-oriented formulas are omitted in NLP contexts. Integrated Gradients computes path integrals from a reference baseline to the input to produce sensitivity scores that satisfy axiomatic properties (Sundararajan et al., 2017). In computer vision, a black image is often used as the baseline, whereas in NLP various token-level baselines have been proposed. With sequence models, one may integrate along linear interpolations in the embedding space between a reference representation and the actual input. SHAP-Gradient combines the expectations of SmoothGrad with the path formulation of Integrated Gradients to yield an estimator of Shapley values (Lundberg and Lee, 2017). Concretely, the method samples interpolation coefficients from a uniform distribution and averages gradients across sampled baselines. DeepLIFT attributes changes in output to changes in input relative to a baseline via a set of layer-wise contribution rules (Shrikumar et al., 2017). Prior work has interpreted DeepLIFT as a fast approximation to Shapley value computation under certain independence assumptions (Lundberg and Lee, 2017). Practical deployments often favor methods with axiomatic guarantees and stable behavior under input perturbations. However, implementation details such as embedding normalization and attention masking can materially alter attribution maps. Comparative evaluations have therefore measured sensitivity, faithfulness, and complexity across methods on standard NLP benchmarks (Atanasova et al., 2020; DeYoung et al., 2020). Recent studies also explore how attribution interacts with pretraining objectives and prompt-based conditioning in large language models (Pruthi et al., 2020; Jain et al., 2020). Notably, explanation faithfulness can diverge from plausibility when evaluated with human-annotated rationales (Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017; Jacovi and Goldberg, 2020). Some authors caution that evaluation-by-deletion and similar faithfulness tests may confound attribution with distribution shift effects, as noted by Jain and Wallace, 2019. To ensure robust conclusions, studies should report variance across random seeds, baselines, and tokenization choices alongside standard accuracy metrics. In summary, gradient-based attribution for transformers remains an active area where methodological clarity and rigorous evaluation are essential.

document: Zero Shot Transfer. Multilingual models like mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020) have shown surprising effectiveness in zero-shot transfer, where fine-tuning the MMLM on a task in some source language often leads to impressive performance on the same task in other languages as well without explicitly training on them. Pires et al. (2019) first observed this phenomenon for NER (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Levow, 2006) and POS tagging (Nivre et al., 2018) tasks. Concurrently, Wu and Dredze (2019) also showed this surprisingly cross lingual transfer ability of mBERT additionally on tasks like Document Classification (Schwenk and Li, 2018), Natural Language Inference (Conneau et al., 2018) and Dependency Parsing (Nivre et al., 2018), extending early evidence (Pires et al., 2019; Wu and Dredze, 2019 across diverse evaluation suites. Factors Affecting Zero Shot Transfer. Pires et al. (2019) showed that vocabulary memorization played little role in zero-shot generalization as language pairs with little word piece overlap also exhibited impressive crosslingual performance. K et al. (2020) arrived at a similar conclusion by training BERT on an artificially generated language to zero out the word overlap with the target languages, and observed only minor drops in the performance compared to training the model on English. On the contrary Wu and Dredze (2019), observed strong correlations between the sub-word overlap and the zero-shot performance in four out of five tasks. Wu and Dredze (2020) showed that mBERT performed much worse for zero-shot transfer to low resource languages (i.e., less pre-training data) than high resource ones on POS Tagging, NER and Dependency Parsing tasks. Lauscher et al. (2020) also had a similar observation on tasks like XNLI and XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), though they found that the zero-shot performance on NER, POS tagging and Dependency Parsing tasks might not strictly depend on the pre-training size and could be better explained by different linguistic relatedness features like syntactic and phonological similarities between the language pair. Similar dependence on the typological relatedness such as word order had also been observed by Pires et al. (2019). Performance Prediction. Prior work has explored predicting the performance of machine learning models from unlabelled data by either measuring (dis)agreements between multiple classifiers (Platanios et al., 2014; Platanios et al., 2017) or by utilizing underlying information about data distribution (Domhan et al., 2015). In the context of NLP, Birch et al. (2008) explored predicting the performance of a Machine Translation system by utilizing different explanatory variables for the language pairs. Lin et al. (2019) proposed a learning to rank approach to choose transfer languages for cross lingual learning using several linguistic and dataset specific features. Specifically predicting the zero-shot performance of MMLMs was first explored in Lauscher et al. (2020), where they used a linear regression model to estimate the cross-lingual transfer performance based on pre-training data size and linguistic relatedness features. Srinivasan et al. (2021) tackled this problem by utilizing XGBoost Regressor for the prediction along with a larger set of features. Recently, Dolicki and Spanakis (2021) explored individual syntactic features for zero-shot performance prediction instead of working with aggregate similarity values, and showed about 2 to 4 times gain in performance. We extend these work by considering a multi-task learning approach, where performance prediction in a task utilizes not only the data available for that task, but also the patterns observed for other tasks.

document: The rapid growth of the legal text processing field is demonstrated by numerous papers presented in top-tier conferences in NLP and artificial intelligence (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Valvoda et al., 2021) as well as surveys (Chalkidis and Kampas, 2018; (Zhong et al., 2020b). Moreover, specialized workshops on NLP for legal text (Aletras et al., 2019; Di Fatta et al., 2020; Aletras et al., 2020) are regularly organized. A core task in this area has been legal judgment prediction (forecasting), where the goal is to predict the outcome (verdict) of a court case. In this direction, there have been at least three lines of work. The first one (Aletras et al., 2016; Chalkidis et al., 2019a; Medvedeva et al., 2020; Medvedeva et al., 2021) predicts violations of human rights in cases of the European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR). The second line of work (Luo et al., 2017; Zhong et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019) considers Chinese criminal cases where the goal is to predict relevant law articles, criminal charges, and the term of the penalty. The third line of work (Ruger et al., 2004; Katz et al., 2017; Kaufman et al., 2019) includes methods for predicting the outcomes of cases of the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS). The same or similar tasks have also been studied with court cases in many other jurisdictions including France (Sulea et al., 2017), the Philippines (Virtucio et al., 2018), Turkey (Mumcuoglu et al., 2021), Thailand (Kowsrihawat et al., 2018), the United Kingdom (Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020), Germany (Urchs et al., 2021), and Switzerland (Niklaus et al., 2021). Apart from predicting court decisions, there is also work aiming to interpret (explain) the decisions of particular courts (Ye et al., 2018; Chalkidis et al., 2021c; Branting et al., 2021). Another popular task is legal topic classification. Nallapati and Manning (2008) highlighted the challenges of legal document classification compared to more generic text classification by using a dataset including docket entries of US court cases. Chalkidis et al. (2020a) classify EU laws into EuroVoc concepts, a task earlier introduced by Mencia and Fürnkranz (2007), with a special interest in few-shot and zero-shot learning. Luz de Araujo et al. (2020) also studied topic classification using a dataset of Brazilian Supreme Court cases. There are similar interesting applications in contract law (Lippi et al., 2019; Tuggener et al., 2020). Several studies (Chalkidis et al., 2019c; Hendrycks et al., 2021) explored information extraction from contracts, to extract important information such as the contracting parties, agreed payment amount, start and end dates, and applicable law. Other studies focus on extracting information from legislation (Cardellino et al., 2017; Angelidis et al., 2018) or court cases (Leitner et al., 2019). Legal Question Answering (QA) is another task of interest in legal NLP, where the goal is to train models for answering legal questions (Kim et al., 2015; Ravichander et al., 2019; Kien et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2020a, 2020c). Not only is this task interesting for researchers but it could support efforts to help laypeople better understand their legal rights. In the general task setting, this requires identifying relevant legislation, case law, or other legal documents, and extracting elements of those documents that answer a particular question. A notable venue for legal QA has been the Competition on Legal Information Extraction and Entailment (COLIEE) (Kim et al., 2016; Kano et al., 2017; Kano et al., 2018). More recently, there have also been efforts to pre-train Transformer-based language models on legal corpora (Chalkidis et al., 2020b; Zheng et al., 2021), leading to state-of-the-art results in several legal NLP tasks, compared to models pre-trained on generic corpora. These domain-specific encoders are typically adapted via fine-tuning on supervised datasets. Despite promising gains, reproducibility and comparability across studies remain limited due to heterogeneous data splits and metrics. Overall, the legal NLP literature is overwhelming, and the resources are scattered. Documentation is often not available, and evaluation measures vary across articles studying the same task. Our goal is to create the first unified benchmark to assess the performance of NLP models on legal natural language understanding. As a first step, we selected a representative group of tasks, using datasets in English that are publicly available, adequately documented, and of appropriate size for developing modern NLP methods. We also introduce several simplifications to make the new benchmark more standardized and easily accessible, as already noted.

document: In recent years there has been active interest in the application of NLP techniques to the legal domain (Zhong et al., 2020a). A number of tasks and models have been proposed, inter alia, Legal Judgment Prediction (Chalkidis et al., 2019), Legal Summarization (Bhattacharya et al., 2019; Tran et al., 2019), Prior Case Retrieval (Jackson et al., 2003; Shao et al., 2020), Legal Question Answering, and Catchphrase Extraction (Galgani et al., 2012). Legal Judgement Prediction (LJP) involves predicting the final decision from the facts and arguments of the case. Majority of corpora for Legal-NLP tasks have been in English; recently, there have been efforts to address other languages as well, for example, Xiao et al. (2018) have created a large-scale Chinese criminal judgment prediction dataset with over 2.68 million legal documents. Work on Legal-NLP in languages other than English is still in its incipient stages Zhong, Malik and Strickson, 2020). Our paper contributes towards these efforts by releasing corpus in Hindi. Majority of the work in the legal domain has focused on the higher court (Malik et al., 2021; Strickson and De La Iglesia, 2020; Zhong et al., 2020b); however, the lower courts handle the maximum number of cases. We try to address this gap by releasing a large corpus of district court level legal documents. Ishida et al. (2017, 2019) have published legal data in Japanese, along with their English translation. The competition had two sub-tasks, a legal information retrieval task and an entailment identification task between law articles and queries. Multiple datasets in Chinese have been released for different tasks, namely Reading Comprehension (Duan et al., 2019), Similar Case Matching (Xiao et al., 2019), and Question Answering (Zhong et al., 2020b). Duan et al. (2019) proposed the Chinese Judicial Reading Comprehension (CJRC) dataset with about 10K documents and almost 50K questions with answers. Zhong et al. (2020b) presented JEC-QA, a legal question answering dataset collected from the National Judicial Examination of China with about 26K multiple-choice questions. They augment the dataset with a database containing the legal knowledge required to answer the questions and also assign meta information to each of the questions for in-depth analysis. Xiao et al. (2019) proposed CAIL2019-SCM, a dataset containing 8,964 triplets of the case document, with the objective to identify which two cases are more similar in the triplets. Similar case matching has a crucial application as it helps to identify comparable historical cases, and a historical case with similar facts often serves as a legal precedent and influences the judgement. Such historical information can be used to make the legal judgement prediction models more robust. Kleinberg et al. (2017) proposed bail decision prediction as a good proxy to gauge if machine learning can improve human decision making. A large number of bail documents along with the binary decision (granted or denied) makes it an ideal task for automation, and in this paper we also propose the bail prediction task using the HLDC corpus.

document: Our work is related to dataset construction for building persuasive dialogue systems that try to persuade the participant to take a specific action. Hiraoka et al. (2014) annotated 34 dialogues, in which an experienced salesperson tries to convince a customer to buy a camera. Yoshino et al. (2018) requested crowdsourcing workers to generate 200 persuasive dialogues. In each dialogue, one participant persuaded another one to adopt a suggestion such as cleaning a room. Wang et al. (2019) collected 1017 dialogues, in which one of the participants was convinced to donate to a specific charity. We can see that the covered conversation scenarios in these datasets were strictly limited to specific tasks, while our scenarios are more general and can be easily extended to different cases. According to (Hiraoka et al., 2014; Yoshino 2018), such narrow settings may constrain model transfer to broader contexts. Also, our constructed dataset is about three times larger than the prior work, indicating the usefulness of the recent pre-trained paradigm. The topic of conversational recommendation systems is also related to our work. A number of attempts have been made to collect training data for conversational recommendation systems. These studies (Wu et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020) first extracted a path consisting of entity or attribute nodes from a knowledge base. Then they asked annotators to write conversational recommendation dialogues. The flow of mentioned topics in a dialogue should follow the extracted path. Li et al. (2021) also built a dataset by asking human workers to create dialogues based on a topic path. It should be noted that, in these datasets, the goal of such systems is to only make entity recommendations instead of tasks, while our work goes beyond them in naturally transferring from chit-chat to task-oriented dialogues and completing a task the user may want. In contrast, our collected conversations explicitly track intentions that evolve from casual talk to concrete commitments. Another related work is generating a transition between two given open-domain utterances. Tang et al. (2019) proposed to generate the transition conditional on a specific word, because they wanted the generated transition to drive the conversation topic to the specified word. Sevegnani et al. (2021) collected a new dataset of human-created one-turn topic transitions. Each dialogue contains two utterances with different topics and one transition in the middle of them. These efforts emphasize local coherence but do not account for long-horizon task fulfillment. Our approach integrates topic transition modeling with downstream action planning in a unified framework. There are some recent studies trying to merge chit-chat and task-oriented dialogues, but the purposes of merged dialogues differ from ours. Sun et al. (2021) enhanced the utterances in task-oriented dialogues by appending chit-chat sentences. They hoped that the agent would gain more social, personalized, and engaging communication skills. Ennen et al. (2021) proposed a dialogue system that can transfer the style of generated responses from chit-chat to task-oriented styles. However, the system is a prototype model, and there is still a large gap to properly bridge chit-chat and task-oriented dialogues. The motivation of our work is closely similar to that of the study by Yu et al. (2017). They manually created several task-oriented response generation strategies specifically designed for the movie promotion scenario. In addition, expert knowledge was utilized to design reinforcement learning rewards that help their dialogue system decide which action to take (i.e., continuing chit-chat or selecting a task-oriented strategy to reply). Unlike rule-heavy pipelines, our system can adapt its strategy based on data rather than hand-crafted templates. However, we can observe that the prior work in these studies heavily relied on human efforts (data collection, expert-created strategies, etc.). Therefore, it can be expensive and hard to extend their data or methods to practical cases due to the requirement of larger-scale training data. Our proposed framework benefits from pre-trained models and shows outstanding conversational capability. The flexibility of extending to diverse cases is also validated, considering that all components inside the framework can be easily substituted by updated models, and the generated data can be used by semi-supervised or unsupervised methods for cold-start scenarios.

It also omits the comma in 'Yoshino 2018', compounding the citation formatting issue. The span is exactly six words and consists only of the citation text.

document:
As more powerful SSL models are proposed with promising performance on various tasks, researchers continually try to find extensive evaluation methods to assess model performance, and wish to holistically understand the capability of the learned representations in these models.

SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) is a framework to benchmark the SSL models on 10 speech tasks by learning task-specific prediction heads on top of the frozen shared SSL models. Although the tasks in SUPERB span across different domains, most of them are simple classification problems, or only require utilization of shallow semantics. In contrast, we focus on harder semantic and generative tasks.

Another recently proposed benchmark is the LeBenchmark Evain et al. (2021), investigating the performance of SSL models trained on French data with three semantic tasks. However, they only consider wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) with different architectures as their upstream models (i.e., networks pre-trained with SSL). Here, we evaluate a diverse set of SSL models, and offer a more comprehensive analysis.

The Zero Resource Speech Benchmark 2021 (Nguyen et al., 2020) introduces unsupervised speech processing tasks, particularly the spoken language modeling problem. They evaluate the SSL models via zero-shot probings at four linguistic levels. While their benchmark task is specific for certain domain, we use various tasks to evaluate different aspects of SSL models.

The HEAR 2021 Challenge aims to develop general-purpose audio representation by focusing on audio tasks beyond speech that include sound event detection, speech commands and pitch chroma classification. We specifically focus on various aspects of speech processing, thus providing a wide variety of spoken language tasks. Beyond benchmarking, our analysis examines transfer across languages and domains, connecting to work on universal self-supervised objectives (Hsu et al., 2021). To contextualize our contributions within broader auditory representation learning, we reference the community challenge reports (Turian et al., 2022 while adopting evaluation protocols suited to speech rather than general audio. Overall, our work complements existing resources by emphasizing semantically rich tasks, comprehensive metrics, and transparent, reproducible evaluation practices.

document:
Readability Assessment has been an active area in educational research for almost a century. Early research on this topic focused on the creation of readability "formulae", which relied on easy to calculate measures such as word and sentence length, and presence of words from some standard word list (Lively and Pressey, 1923;Flesch, 1948;Stenner, 1996). More than 200 such formulae were proposed in the past few decades, and DuBay (2007) provides a detailed summary of such formulae. The advent of NLP and machine learning resulted in more data driven research on ARA over the past two decades. Starting from statistical language models (Si and Callan, 2001), a range of lexical and syntactic features (Heilman et al., 2007;Petersen and Ostendorf, 2009;Ambati et al., 2016) as well as inter-sentential features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008;Todirascu et al., 2013;Xia et al., 2016) were explored in machine learning based ARA research in the past. Features motivated by related disciplines such as psycholinguistics (Howcroft and Demberg, 2017), and cognitive science (Feng et al., 2009) were also explored.

In the past few years, ARA research has been primarily focused on textual embeddings and deep learning based architectures. Word embeddings in combination with other attributes such as domain knowledge or language modeling (Cha et al., 2017;Jiang et al., 2018) were used for ARA. A range of neural architectures, from multi attentive RNN (Azpiazu and Pera, 2019) to deep reinforcement learning (Mohammadi and Khasteh, 2019) were proposed, and recent research explored combining transformers with linguistic features Deutsch et al. (2020;Meng et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2021;Imperial, 2021). While Deutsch et al. (2020) did not report any improvements with linguistic features, the others reported that combining linguistic features with deep learning approaches result in better ARA models.

Although a lot of this research evolved on English, the past decade saw ARA research in other languages such as German (Hancke et al., 2012), French (François and Fairon, 2012), Italian (Dell'Orletta et al., 2011), Bangla (Sinha et al., 2012) etc., which employed language specific feature sets. While most ARA research modeled one language at a time, some research created language agnostic feature sets and architectures and experimented with 2 to 7 languages (Shen et al., 2013;Azpiazu and Pera, 2019;Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera, 2020a,b;Martinc et al., 2021;Weiss et al., 2021). Although only one language is considered per model in all this research, there are two important exceptions. Madrazo Azpiazu and Pera (2020b) explored whether combining texts from related languages during training improves ARA performance for low resource languages, and Weiss et al. (2021) used a model trained on English texts on German, based on a common, broad set of handcrafted linguistic features. However, to our knowledge, zero-shot cross lingual transfer of text embeddings, without any handcrafted features, was not explored for this task in the past.

ARA is traditionally treated as a classification problem in NLP research, although there are some exceptions. Heilman et al. (2008) compared linear, ordinal, and logistic regression and concluded that ordinal with a combination of lexical and grammatical features worked the best for ARA, although subsequent research was still dominated by classification approaches. There is some past work that considered ARA as a pairwise ranking problem, using SVM/SVM rank and hand crafted linguistic features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008;Tanaka-Ishii et al., 2010;Ma et al., 2012;Mesgar and Strube, 2015;Ambati et al., 2016;Howcroft and Demberg, 2017), and while Tanaka-Ishii et al. (2010) and Ma et al. (2012) showed that ranking performs better than traditional features and classification/regression respectively, Xia et al. (2016) did not find ranking to be consistently better across the board. In this background, we take a fresh look at the application of ranking for ARA, by proposing a new neural pairwise ranking model.

document: There has been a growing body of research studying the inner workings of BERT and trying to quantify what it learns in various scenarios, dubbed "BERTology" (Rogers et al., 2020). Of particular interest to this paper is work that analyzes BERT's output embeddings. Recent studies have found that embeddings created from the final layer of BERT tend to cluster according to word senses (Wiedemann et al., 2019), though this varies somewhat based on the position of a word in a sentence (Mickus et al., 2020). The final BERT layers also produce more contextualized word embeddings than the earlier layers (Ethayarajh, 2019), a finding we revisit using paraphrases in Section 3.3. Foundational analyses show that representations evolve from lexical to syntactic to semantic across depth (Tenney et al., 2019). Other work probes linear structure in embedding spaces, such as syntax trees captured by distances (Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Attention heads have been linked to interpretable functions, albeit imperfectly, including coreference and dependency relations (Clark et al., 2019). However, attention may not be explanation by itself, and sparsity can often be increased without hurting performance (Jain and Wallace, 2019). Studies of layer selectivity indicate redundancy and overparameterization in Transformers (Kovaleva et al., 2019). At the same time, pruning experiments suggest that many attention heads are dispensable (Michel et al., 2019). Probing with control tasks has raised concerns about probe expressivity overshadowing the encoded information (Hewitt and Liang, 2019). Subsequent information-theoretic analyses quantify mutual information between representations and tasks to ameliorate this issue (Pimentel et al., 2020). Beyond task probes, geometric characterizations such as local linearity and anisotropy reveal systematic biases in embedding distributions (Ethayarajh, 2019). Contextual similarity has been tied to paraphrase behavior and lexical substitution performance (Reif et al., 2019). Cross-lingual transfer evidence suggests partially shared abstraction layers in multilingual BERT (Pires et al., 2019). While BERT remains a central model, developments in pretraining objectives and architectures complicate direct comparisons (Liu et al., 2019). Training regimes based on masked language modeling interact with corpus composition to shape representational geometry (Gururangan et al., 2020). For reference, the canonical Transformer encoder defines the backbone used by BERT Vaswani et al., 2017. Our focus is not on architecture variants per se but on the empirical properties of output embeddings across contexts. Sense-specific clustering has been shown to improve with additional context windows in sentence-level models (Amrami and Goldberg, 2019). Dynamic subspace methods further disentangle polysemy by projecting tokens into sense-aware manifolds (Coenen et al., 2019). Despite these advances, distinguishing contextualization from memorization remains challenging (Zhang et al., 2021). Recent work emphasizes dataset artifacts that inflate apparent linguistic competence (McCoy et al., 2019). To avoid confounds, we employ paraphrase-driven evaluation that varies surface forms while holding semantics constant (Wieting and Gimpel, 2018). Complementary diagnostics measure how neighborhood structures shift under synonym substitutions (Wang et al., 2020). Robustness analyses also consider domain shift, showing degradation when topical priors change (Hendrycks et al., 2020). In the context of lexical semantics, attraction and repulsion forces across layers suggest distinct roles for shallow and deep features (Voita et al., 2019). Furthermore, token type and position embeddings interact to modulate attention patterns in subtle ways (Press et al., 2021). We build on these findings to articulate hypotheses about when final-layer embeddings capture sense distinctions faithfully. Specifically, we test whether paraphrase consistency increases with deeper layers relative to middle layers. We also analyze whether contextual isotropy correlates with stability under word order perturbations (Gao et al., 2019). Our contributions complement survey narratives by providing targeted, reproducible measurements on widely used checkpoints (Rogers et al., 2020). Together, this related work motivates a systematic study of contextualized output embeddings and their dependence on syntax, semantics, and domain.

document: Prior approaches focused on framing DST as question answering (Ruan et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Many MultiWoZ cross-domain models leverage slot names or descriptions (Wu et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2021a). Pretrained generative LLMs (Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020) have enabled framing NLP tasks as seq2seq problems. Some DST papers (Zhao et al., 2021a; Feng et al., 2021) look at settings with no train–test discrepancy. Many studies explore the efficacy of task-specific prompts (Jiang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Madotto et al. (2020) prime language models with examples for dialogue tasks without finetuning. Wei et al. (2021) fine-tune language models to understand prompts for a different task. Recent work on instruction following suggests that generalization can emerge from diverse task mixtures during pretraining (Sanh et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Nevertheless, dialogue state tracking remains sensitive to domain shift and ontology drift across datasets (Zhang et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2021b). Early slot-filling pipelines relied on span extraction and rule-based normalization (Henderson et al., 2014; Mrkšić et al., 2017). Neural architectures gradually replaced hand-crafted pipelines with attention mechanisms and contextual encoders (Xie et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020). Schema-guided approaches extended these models to unseen services by conditioning on natural language descriptions (Rastogi et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2021). In multi-domain settings, sharing parameters across slots reduces data requirements but complicates calibration (Zhao et al., 2021b; Feng et al., 2022). Prompt-based DST reframes state tracking as conditional generation under textual constraints (Hou et al., 2022; Ding et al., 2023). Empirical comparisons indicate that generative decoders are competitive with span extractors when constrained by ontological priors (Peng et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2022). However, brittle alignment between templates and ontology labels often limits portability to new domains (Kim et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a). Semi-supervised methods exploit unlabeled dialogue corpora with consistency regularization and pseudo-labeling (Chen et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). Active learning further reduces annotation cost by querying informative turns under uncertainty (Settles, 2009; Lowell et al., 2019). A parallel strand studies retrieval-augmented dialogue tracking that grounds slot values in external tables or documents (Gao et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2023). Cross-lingual transfer has been explored by aligning slot semantics via multilingual encoders (Xu et al., 2020; Lai et al., 2022). Despite these advances, assumptions about clean ontology alignment in evaluation benchmarks can inflate reported gains (Eric et al., 2020; Budzianowski et al., 2018). In contrast, real-world deployments face noisy user inputs, implicit preferences, and evolving taxonomies (Zhou et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023). To bridge this gap, some studies advocate abstraction over slot names using free-form descriptions, but they often disregard disambiguation costs (Lin et al., 2021a; Lee et al., 2022). Zero-shot generalization is further complicated by spurious correlations between domain keywords and slot values (Xie et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize calibration, coverage, and abstention under ontological uncertainty (Desai and Durrett, 2020; Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019). Another line of work treats DST as iterative constrained decoding guided by a structured prior over admissible states (Zhang et al., 2021; Gupta et al., 2023). Few-shot prompting with in-context examples reduces the need for task-specific finetuning in DST variants (Brown et al., 2020; Min et al., 2022). However, empirical results remain sensitive to prompt order, demonstrations, and verbalizers (Lu et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021c). Recent analyses argue that instruction-tuned LLMs can internalize schema knowledge yet still benefit from task-specific adapters (Wang et al., 2023b; Hu et al., 2021). Conversely, compact encoder-decoders with targeted finetuning offer stronger latency–accuracy trade-offs for production systems (Tay et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021). Prior QA-style formulations inspired copy-based decoders that extract slot spans from the dialogue context (Ruan et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2020), and follow-ups refined negative span handling in ambiguous turns (Zhang et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the mapping from questions to slots can entangle surface patterns with domain heuristics in ways documented by (Ruan 2020) and later ablations. Building on schema descriptions, contrastive pretraining increases robustness to paraphrases of slot definitions (Chen et al., 2022; Su et al., 2022). Our perspective synthesizes these threads by decoupling ontology access, grounding, and state summarization under a uniform prompting framework (Raffel et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022). In the remainder, we motivate design choices, review alternatives, and outline evaluation protocols that stress robustness and transfer (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2020).

Symbol Grounding. Contemporary grounders extensively utilize batch learning (e.g., Shridhar and Hsu, 2018). Yet, ITL requires incremental learning because without it the teacher guidance cannot influence the learner's inferences about plans as and when the advice is given. Further, many grounders assume that the learner starts out with a complete and accurate conceptualisation of the domain using pre-defined visual features and a known vocabulary (Kennington et al., 2015; Kennington and Schlangen, 2017; Wang et al., 2017). In ITL, both of these assumptions are unrealistic; therefore in this paper we explore models for which these assumptions don't apply. Finally, in contrast to all prior grounders, we support incremental learning when the training exemplars feature REs that are linguistically complex: e.g., "two red circles that aren't to the right of both green squares".

Representation Learning. Models for jointly learning a representation for vision and language utilize either explicit alignment via bounding boxes or instance segmentation (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Tan and Bansal, 2019; Kamath et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021), or a large-volume of weakly labeled data in the form of image-caption pairs (Radford et al., 2021). These models rely on offline learning with large datasets. This work, on the other hand, explores how to incrementally extract knowledge in a few-shot manner, using sequentially observed evidence that consists of weakly-labelled annotations, including neologisms.

Grounded Language Acquisition. This task is commonly realized as grounded grammar induction from image-caption pairs (Shi et al., 2019; Zhao and Titov, 2020), or as (neural) semantic parsers learning from a reward signal (Williams, 1992) in visual question answering (Yi et al., 2018) or in planning (Azaria et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Karamcheti et al., 2020). In these scenarios, the primary objective is to learn mappings from linguistic expressions to logical forms, which in turn get associated with certain visual percepts during the learning process. This paper does not tackle learning logical forms. Instead, we obtain them from an existing broad-coverage grammar and focus on exploiting the logical consequences of those logical forms during symbol grounding—i.e., our focus is to utilise the interpretation of logical forms, and in particular the truth functional meanings of close-class words like quantifiers and negation, to inform the learning of mappings from (open-class) symbols like red to their denotations, given the visual percepts.

Visual Question Answering (VQA) is a task of answering free-form questions about an image (Antol et al., 2015). VQA has reached impressive performance in recent years (Fukui et al., 2016), yet VQA models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization for new types of questions requiring multi-step reasoning, with analysis revealing that they often rely on shortcuts (Jiang and Bansal, 2019; Subramanian et al., 2020). Grounded VQA models like (Yi et al. 2018) and Bogin et al. (2021) tackle these shortcomings by learning to ground parts of the question and then learning to compose those parts via the question's syntax to compute the answer. They thus estimate denotations of linguistic parts that are not denoted by the answer to the question. By doing so, these models achieve out-of-distribution generalization for novel questions. However, these models lack the ITL's requirement for incremental learning: model training relies on batch learning. Furthermore, while their performance is impressive, error analysis shows that it tends to make mistakes on sentences containing logical concepts like quantifiers and negation (e.g., Bogin et al., 2021, Figure shows that the determiner most incorrectly denotes an arbitrary subset of entities). Our view is that there is little benefit in trying to learn to ground these concepts as they are domain independent and can be interpreted using formal semantics. In our experiments, we are testing the extent to which knowing and reasoning with the logical meanings of these symbols helps incremental grounding, and in particular estimating denotations of symbols within an RE that aren't designated by that RE.

Visual Reference Resolution. In previous experimental setups, it is often assumed that there is a unique referent in the visual scene for the given RE in the test phase (e.g., Kazemzadeh et al., 2014; Whitney et al., 2016). We aim to cope with situations where the RE has multiple referents: identifying all the referents that satisfy an RE enables efficient planning, because it affords free choice when executing certain commands—e.g., "move a circle to the left." When there is more than one circle, this affords choosing a control policy so that resources are optimized.

document: A Brief History of NLU Datasets. As a core module of task-oriented dialogue systems, natural language understanding has been studied since the early 1990s with the Airline Travel Information System initiative (Hemphill et al., 1990). ATIS consisted of spoken queries about flight-related information and spurred early research in semantic parsing for slot filling. Over the next two decades, only a few reusable NLU resources were released for dialog applications. The scarcity began to change in 2013 with the advent of the dialogue state tracking paradigm that emphasized tracking user goals across turns (Williams et al., 2013; Henderson et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2016). Rather than classifying each turn independently, state tracking emphasizes maintaining a compact representation of user goals throughout the conversation. Several datasets were introduced during the DST challenges, generally covering narrow domains and simple intent or act inventories. In parallel, the deep learning era brought demand for larger and more diverse corpora to train data-hungry models (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Rastogi et al., 2019; Peskov et al., 2019). However, task-oriented datasets differ from machine translation or speech recognition corpora in that schemas and ontologies are domain specific and hard to reuse across tasks (Williams et al., 2013; Henderson et al., 2014. This tight coupling often impedes cross-domain transfer and complicates benchmarking under unified evaluation protocols. Annotation quality also varies widely because domain experts are required and guidelines evolve over time (Eric et al., 2019). Consequently, many corpora exhibit label sparsity, ontology drift, and inconsistent slot value normalization. Recent work has sought to mitigate these issues by proposing schema-guided annotation, improved ontology design, and synthetic augmentation using simulators (Rastogi et al., 2020; Shah et al., 2018; Campagna et al., 2020). Other efforts target multilingual and cross-lingual NLU, where the lack of aligned ontologies and parallel annotations remains a key obstacle (Schuster et al., 2019; Castellucci et al., 2019). To support end-to-end modeling, several benchmarks now integrate NLU with dialogue management and generation while exposing explicit state labels for analysis (Hosseini-Asl et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Despite this progress, reproducibility still depends critically on how datasets define intents, slots, and context windows, as subtle choices can change reported performance. We argue that future resources should emphasize compositional generalization, realistic user behavior, and robust evaluation under distribution shift. Accordingly, we review dataset design patterns, synthesize best practices, and identify open challenges to guide future NLU dataset construction.

document: In this section, we discuss related studies with respect to the contrastive learning framework and sentence embedding. Early work on distributed sentence representations emphasized supervised objectives aligned with downstream tasks. Methods such as InferSent leveraged natural language inference labels to capture transferable features (Conneau et al., 2017). Subsequent research explored unsupervised heuristics, including next sentence prediction and masked language modeling as pretext signals (Devlin et al., 2019). However, these approaches often underperform on semantic textual similarity without fine-tuning. Contrastive learning reframed representation learning by pulling semantically related pairs together while pushing unrelated samples apart (Hadsell et al., 2006). In natural language processing, contrastive objectives were adapted by constructing positive pairs from data augmentations, paraphrases, or contextual neighbors (Gao et al., 2021;Yan et al., 2021). SimCSE highlighted the efficacy of minimal augmentation via dropout to produce strong baselines for sentence embeddings (Gao et al., 2021). Other studies incorporated syntactic or semantic constraints to enrich positive pairs and mitigate representation collapse (Kim et al., 2021;Zhang and Wu, 2022). Beyond instance discrimination, alignment with external knowledge graphs has been examined to refine relational structure (Wang et al., 2021). Hard negative mining remains a central concern, as naive sampling can lead to spurious gradients and diminished margins (Robinson et al., 2021). Curriculum strategies and dynamic queues have been proposed to regulate negative hardness over training Conneau et al., 2017). Data augmentation for sentences poses unique challenges compared to images, where perturbations such as cropping and color jitter preserve labels. Textual augmentations risk altering meaning, prompting methods that generate paraphrases via back-translation or neural editing (Feng et al., 2021;Iyyer et al., 2018). Recent work also leverages multi-view contexts from retrieval corpora to create positive pairs grounded in shared evidence (Izacard et al., 2021). From an architectural perspective, most systems adopt transformer encoders with pooled token representations (Vaswani et al., 2017;Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Pooling strategies such as CLS, mean pooling, and attention pooling affect isotropy and downstream calibration (Gao et al., 2019;Li et al., 2020). Regularization with whitening, temperature scaling, and adaptive margins has been shown to stabilize training and improve calibration (Su and Kuo, 2022). Cross-lingual sentence embeddings extend these ideas by aligning parallel or comparable corpora to share a joint semantic space (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019;Feng et al., 2022). In multilingual settings, the balance between language-specific nuances and language-agnostic structure is especially delicate (Lauscher et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols typically rely on semantic textual similarity, clustering, and retrieval benchmarks to assess generalization (Cer et al., 2017;Wang et al., 2022). Nevertheless, inconsistencies across datasets and metrics complicate comparisons, motivating standardized suites and fair training budgets (Musgrave et al., 2020). Recent analyses further decompose gains into augmentation effects, objective design, and data scale, revealing complementary contributions (Chuang et al., 2020;Khosla et al., 2020). Building on these insights, our work investigates robust negative sampling and multi-view supervision to advance sentence-level representation learning.
