document:
Our work is in the research line of recommender systems widely studied because of their practical value in industry. For example, previous work explores users' chatting history to recommend conversations (Zeng et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2020b) and hashtags (Li et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2021), browsing history to recommend news (Wu et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2021), and purchase history to recommend products (Guo et al., 2020). In contrast to most recommendation studies focusing on exploiting target users' personal interest modeling from their historical behaviors, our work largely relies on the wording of a short query to figure out what is needed by a target user because they are anonymous for privacy concerns.

Within several branches of recommendation research, our task is by concept similar to expert recommendation for question answering Nikzad-Khasmakhi et al., 2019). In this field, many previous studies encode expertise knowledge in diverse streams, such as software engineering (Bhat et al., 2018), social activities (Bok et al., 2021), and community forums (Zhang and Wang, 2020). Nevertheless, few of them attempt to model expertise with modern NLP methods beyond keyword matching. On the contrary, language representations play an important role here to tackle our task: we substantially explore how semantic features help characterize doctor expertise, which has not been studied before.

Our work is also related to previous language understanding research over doctor‑patient dialogues on online health forums (Zeng et al., 2020a), where various compelling applications are explored, such as information extraction (Ramponi et al., 2020; Du et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020c), question answering (Pampari et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019), and medical report generation (Enarvi et al., 2020). In comparison with these studies, we focus on doctor expertise and characterize it from both profile metadata and past patient–doctor dialogues, which fills a gap in prior research. Methodologically, we build on pretrained language models to encode short queries and match them to domain expertise signals in a unified representation space (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). This design allows us to transfer supervision from existing dialogue datasets and reduces dependence on user history that may be sparse or unavailable (Kang and McAuley, 2018). At the same time, we contrast our approach with topic or tag recommendation methods that primarily use co‑occurrence statistics (Mikolov et al., 2013; Levy and Goldberg, 2014). For evaluation, we adopt standard ranking metrics and simulate cold‑start scenarios to reflect realistic deployment settings, while acknowledging domain shift between sources and targets (Zhao et al., 2020). We further compare against expert routing systems in community QA, which often rely on handcrafted features and network centrality measures (Zhang et al., 2017; Pal et al., 2012) despite their limited portability across domains. Finally, we situate our contribution within ongoing efforts on retrieval‑augmented recommendation, where semantic retrievers are paired with task‑specific scorers to improve precision (Fisch et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020), and note that prior cross‑domain QA adaptation remains challenging in terms of both annotation and robustness (Fisch et al., 2019; Khashabi et al., 2020b.

document: Entity linking aims to resolve ambiguous mentions in text to entries in a knowledge base. It has been widely studied with approaches ranging from graph-based heuristics to supervised learning (Milne and Witten, 2008; Cucerzan, 2007; Lazic et al., 2015b; Gupta et al., 2017; Raiman and Raiman, 2018). End-to-end neural models that jointly perform mention detection and disambiguation have boosted accuracy in realistic settings (Kolitsas et al., 2018; Cao et al., 2021). Complementary to within-document reasoning, cross-document coreference provides valuable signals for entity disambiguation (Bagga and Baldwin, 1998; Gooi and Allan, 2004; Singh et al., 2011; Barhom et al., 2019). Dutta and Weikum (2015) combine clustering-based decisions with sparse bag-of-words features, which are not well suited to modern embedding-based pipelines. The literature on heuristic candidate generation remains influential (Milne and Witten, 2008; Cucerzan (2007); Lazic et al., 2015b). However, candidate generation alone is insufficient without robust context modeling that accounts for fine-grained semantics and document structure (Cattan et al., 2020; Caciularu et al., 2021; Ravenscroft et al., 2021). Cross-encoders that jointly score mention–entity pairs with full attention deliver strong performance but are computationally heavy at inference time (Raiman and Raiman, 2018). Our work investigates how clustering signals can be exploited to train dual-encoders that preserve efficiency while approaching cross-encoder quality. In particular, we study training curricula that alternate between within-document and cross-document clusters to shape the representation geometry. If prediction efficiency is the only concern, model distillation can compress cross-encoders into lightweight students (Hinton et al., 2015; Izacard and Grave, 2021). We also consider alternatives such as poly-encoders that capture limited interactions at manageable cost (Humeau et al., 2020). Beyond supervised training, semi-automatic mining of hard negatives from large corpora further improves retrieval-oriented linking (Gupta et al., 2017). We evaluate our approach under both standard disambiguation and open-domain entity discovery settings, emphasizing recall at scale. Overall, our results suggest that clustering-based training is a practical route to scalable entity linking without sacrificing much accuracy on strong benchmarks.

document: Entity-based modeling has been the prevailing approach to model coherence in pre-neural models. The entity grid is its most well-known implementa-tion (Barzilay and Lapata, 2008). It represents entities in a two-dimensional array to track their transitions between sentences. Many variations have been proposed to improve this model, e.g., projecting the grid into a graph representation (Guinaudeau and Strube, 2013) or converting the grid to a neural model (Tien Nguyen and Joty, 2017). However, the neural version of the entity grid (Tien Nguyen and Joty, 2017) has two limitations. First, Lai and Tetreault (2018) state that entity grids applied to downstream tasks are often extremely sparse. In their evaluation, it is difficult to find meaningful entity transitions between sentences in the grids. Accordingly, this model performs worse than other neural models. More importantly, this neural model cannot provide any clues of how this model works since Tien Nguyen and Joty (2017) apply a convolutional layer on the entity grid. The feature map of the convolutional layer is not interpretable. They cannot examine which entity is assigned more importantly than others by their model. In contrast, we constrain our model to capture focus on entities using noun phrases. Then our model tracks the changes of focus. Hence, it provides us with an interpretable focus (Section 5). More recently, Moon et al. (2019) propose a neural coherence model to exploit both local and structural aspects. They evaluate their model on an artificial task only, the shuffle test, which determines whether sentences in a document are shuffled or not. However, recent studies (Pishdad et al., 2020) claim that this artificial task is not suitable to evaluate coherence models. Lai and Tetreault (2018) show that the neural coherence models, which achieve the best performance on this task, do not outperform non-neural models on downstream tasks. More recently, Mohiuddin et al. (2021) find a weak correlation between the model performance in artificial tasks and downstream tasks. In our evaluation, we compare Moon et al. (2019) with ours in an artificial task as well as in three downstream tasks. Moon et al. (2019) perform the best in the artificial task, but do not outperform our model in three downstream tasks (Section 4). Despite these concerns, entity-centric models remain competitive due to their transparency and ease of integration with feature-based pipelines. Recent neural architectures attempt to encode discourse structures by modeling sentence ordering and topical continuity with hierarchical encoders (Li and Hovy, 2014; Ji and Smith, 2017). Nevertheless, hierarchical encoders often conflate topic shifts with coherence breaks, leading to unstable behavior across domains (Lai and Tetreault, 2018). A complementary line of work leverages coreference resolution to align mentions across sentences and construct discourse-aware representations (Clark and Manning, 2016; Joshi et al., 2019). While these approaches improve local consistency, they may overlook entity prominence and syntactic roles noted in classic accounts (Barzilay and Lapata, 2008; Guinaudeau and Strube, 2013. Building on these insights, several studies propose attention mechanisms to weight entities according to discourse salience (Liu and Lapata, 2018; Sukhbaatar et al., 2015). Others incorporate syntactic supervision via dependency paths to discern subject continuity from object rementioning (Kobayashi et al., 2020). Beyond modeling, evaluation practice has shifted toward task-oriented benchmarks including essay scoring, summary coherence, and factuality assessment (Novikova et al., 2017; Marie et al., 2021). However, benchmarks differ widely in annotation protocol and difficulty, complicating cross-study comparison (Bhandari et al., 2020). To mitigate this, we adopt a multi-view evaluation that jointly considers discrimination accuracy, ranking robustness, and transfer to content selection. Our method emphasizes interpretable focus tracking by aligning noun phrase chains with learned discourse roles, yielding human-inspectable attributions. We hypothesize that explicit focus transitions can regularize neural encoders and reduce overfitting on artificial shuffling artifacts. Consequently, we formulate training objectives that balance local entity continuity with document-level thematic progression. The remainder of this paper surveys related methods, details our model, and reports comprehensive experiments on multiple corpora.

document:
Learning self-supervised tasks on large-scale multilingual texts has proven to be effective for pretraining cross-lingual language models. Masked language modeling (MLM; Devlin et al. 2019) is typically used to learn cross-lingual encoders such as multilingual BERT (mBERT; Devlin et al. 2019) and XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020). The crosslingual language models can be further improved by introducing external pre-training tasks using parallel corpora. XLM (Conneau and Lample, 2019) introduces the translation language modeling (TLM) task that predicts masked tokens from concatenated translation pairs. ALM utilizes translation pairs to construct codeswitched sequences as input. InfoXLM (Chi et al., 2021b) considers an input translation pair as crosslingual views of the same meaning, and proposes a cross-lingual contrastive learning task. Several pre-training tasks utilize the token-level alignments in parallel data to improve cross-lingual language models (Cao et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2021;Hu et al., 2020a;Chi et al., 2021c).

In addition, parallel data are also employed for cross-lingual sequence-to-sequence pre-training. XNLG (Chi et al., 2020) presents cross-lingual masked language modeling and cross-lingual autoencoding for cross-lingual natural language generation, and achieves the cross-lingual transfer for NLG tasks. These design choices align with earlier encoder-only pretraining paradigms (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020 but adapt them to multilingual transfer. VECO (Luo et al., 2020) utilizes crossattention MLM to pretrain a variable cross-lingual language model for both NLU and NLG. mT6 (Chi et al., 2021a) improves mT5 (Xue et al., 2021) by learning the translation span corruption task on parallel data. ∆LM (Ma et al., 2021) proposes to align pretrained multilingual encoders to improve cross-lingual sequence-to-sequence pre-training. Subsequent work further explores multilingual denoising strategies tailored to sentence- and document-level structures to stabilize training across scripts. Nonetheless, the optimal balance between translation-based supervision and monolingual objectives remains an open question for low-resource languages. In this paper, we focus on summarizing the landscape of cross-lingual pretraining objectives and highlight gaps that motivate more data-efficient learning.

document: Here we first review the related work of DA for NLP, then expound the differences between our method and the major existing ones. According to the methodology of data generation, existing methods can be categorized into three types: rule-based, interpolation-based, and model-based, respectively. Rule-based approaches manipulate tokens or structures using deterministic transformations such as synonyms substitution, template rewriting, or noise injection. These methods are simple and controllable, but they may distort label semantics if applied without constraints. Interpolation-based methods synthesize new samples by mixing features or examples in the embedding or input space. Such strategies can regularize models and smooth decision boundaries, yet they often require careful calibration to avoid unnatural artifacts. Model-based methods rely on learned generators, translating, paraphrasing, or sampling from language models to expand the training distribution. A comprehensive taxonomy of augmentation motivations has been discussed in (Johnson et al., 2020 and it highlights trade-offs among fidelity, diversity, and cost. Compared to prior taxonomies, our work emphasizes task-specific risk controls that preserve causal features while perturbing spurious cues. We frame augmentation as a constrained transformation problem with explicit invariances and equivariances. This perspective clarifies when augmentations should be label-preserving versus label-shifting, a distinction often glossed over in earlier surveys. We also revisit evaluation protocols, arguing that intrinsic similarity metrics are insufficient proxies for downstream generalization. Instead, we propose diagnostic splits that separate robustness gains from mere memorization of synthetic patterns. Finally, we discuss practical considerations such as compute budgets, data governance, and failure analysis to guide deployment in sensitive applications. Together, these points delineate the scope of our approach and situate it within the broader landscape of DA research.

document: Unsupervised POS tagging remains a central challenge in low-resource and cross-lingual NLP. In addition to HMMs and the CRF autoencoder (CRF-AE), a number of approaches have been explored. Clustering-based methods treat tag induction as grouping distributionally similar tokens (Yatbaz et al., 2012; Yuret et al., 201. These studies typically employ k-means over word representations to partition tokens. Yatbaz et al. (2012) learn context-free embeddings by optimizing substitutional similarity derived from n-gram language models. Yuret et al. (2014) extend this idea to produce context-sensitive embeddings that better capture local dependencies. Gupta et al. (2020) introduce a deep clustering objective that projects mBERT features into a lower-dimensional, clustering-friendly space with a reconstruction term. Despite steady improvements on multilingual UD benchmarks, code releases for these systems remain scarce. A complementary line of work maximizes mutual information between word- and context-based predictors of tags. Building on classical distributional objectives, Stratos (2019) formalizes agreement constraints that can be optimized without labeled data. Recent studies investigate whether pretrained language models can serve as sources of inductive bias for unsupervised tagging and parsing. Beyond the Gupta et al. approach, few efforts leverage PLMs directly for POS induction. For unsupervised parsing, Wu et al. (2020) estimate interword correlations via perturbed masking and recover tree structure from those signals. Inspired by structural probes, Kim et al. (2020) infer constituency by measuring proximity between adjacent token representations. Cao et al. (2020) further combine PLMs with classical constituency tests to attain competitive performance without treebank supervision. Parallel to these trends, the CRF-AE has been applied broadly to unsupervised sequence labeling. Ammar et al. (2014) introduce the encoder–decoder formulation, and Cai et al. (2017) adapt it to dependency induction with reconstruction from predicted trees. While originally devised for fully unsupervised learning, subsequent work shows that semi-supervised variants of the CRF-AE can be effective for POS tagging and semantic parsing. For example, Zhang et al. (2017) and Jia et al. (2020) report gains when combining small labeled datasets with unlabeled corpora.

document: Our approach draws insights from multi-label classification (MLC) (Tsoumakas and Katakis, 2007; Zhou, 2006, 2014). One of the earliest approaches for MLC was to transform the problem into multiple binary classification problems while ignoring the correlations between labels (Boutell et al., 2004). Subsequent research emphasized modeling label dependencies through classifier chains and structured prediction (Read et al., 2011; Dembczyński et al., 2012). More recent work has modeled MLC in the sequence-to-sequence framework with a decoder that generates the labels sequentially, thus preserving inter-label correlations (Yang et al., 2018). However, most prior work in MLC focuses on classification and is not directly applicable to machine translation. In contrast, our training strategy is tailored for sequence-to-sequence problems where outputs are ordered and context dependent. Unlike prior work (Yang et al., 2018), SCONES allows us to perform MLC-style training with any underlying NMT architecture by simply changing the loss function. By jointly training all label-specific binary classifiers, our strategy is able to account for label correlations without modifying the decoder topology. Zhang et al. (2020) used an MLC objective to improve machine translation, but their formulation optimized a bag-of-words target and ignored word order. Unlike that approach, we attempt to predict the next token while marginalizing over plausible alternatives at each step. We thus formulate stepwise next-word prediction as an MLC problem to handle intrinsic uncertainty while still producing ordered target sequences. This perspective also connects to minimum risk training and listwise ranking objectives that consider sets of candidates (Shen et al., 2016; Wiseman and Rush, 2016). In practice, our formulation permits efficient batching and reuses standard beam-search infrastructure. The speed-ups from SCONES can be partially attributed to avoiding normalization of the output over the full vocabulary. The same idea motivated earlier work on self-normalized training cf. Mnih and Teh, 2012; Devlin, 2014; Goldberger and Melamud, 2018). As described in Section 2, unlike classic self-normalization, SCONES does not attempt to approximate a globally normalized distribution. Rather, the output layer consists of multiple binary classifiers that do not share probability mass by design, which better represents uncertainty among confusable tokens. This design aligns with recent findings on calibration for NLP models, where overconfident softmax distributions hinder decision making (Guo et al., 2017; Minderer et al., 2021). It also relates to contrastive learning objectives that separate positives from hard negatives without enforcing a simplex constraint (Gutmann and Hyvärinen, 2010; He et al., 2020). From an algorithmic standpoint, our loss decomposes additively across sampled candidate sets and admits unbiased gradient estimates. We further show that the approach can be combined with label smoothing and dropout without degrading the MLC interpretation (Szegedy et al., 2016; Pereyra et al., 2017). Compared to reinforcement learning for sequence prediction, our method requires no task-specific reward design and remains stable across domains (Ranzato et al., 2016; Bahdanau et al., 2017). In ablations, we find that the number of candidate labels per step trades accuracy for speed in a predictable manner. Small candidate sets yield large throughput gains, while larger sets close the gap to fully normalized baselines. Importantly, SCONES acts as a drop-in training criterion and leaves decoding and evaluation protocols unchanged. This property facilitates integration into existing NMT toolkits and multilingual pipelines. Beyond machine translation, the same training strategy applies to grammatical error correction and data-to-text generation with minimal changes (Napoles et al., 2017; Dušek and Jurčíček, 2016). Related work on non-autoregressive and semi-autoregressive models also seeks efficiency, but typically trades off fluency or requires complex rescoring (Gu et al., 2018; Stern et al., 2018). Our approach is orthogonal to these modeling choices and can be layered on top of autoregressive decoders. We additionally discuss connections to energy-based modeling, where unnormalized scores are common and margin-based objectives are effective (LeCun et al., 2006; Song and Kingma, 2021). Empirically, we observe improvements in rare word translation and robustness to exposure bias in long sequences. These gains are consistent with the hypothesis that decoupled binary decisions help the model hedge between semantically similar options. Overall, by reframing token prediction as multi-label classification within a sequence model, we derive a principled training strategy that is both efficient and accurate.

document: Multidomain and multilingual translation systems have received considerable attention in recent years, and an exhaustive survey is beyond the scope of this paper. Domain adaptation for neural machine translation is surveyed in (Chu et al., 2017), while multidomain systems are notably studied by Saunders (2021) and Pham et al. (2021); for multilingual NMT, see Chu and Dabre (2018) and Dabre et al. (2020). We focus below on methods that exploit task relatedness and parameter sharing across languages and domains. Methods leveraging language similarity use proximity signals to inform sharing strategies, as shown by Sen et al. (2019) and Kong et al. (2021). These works propose multi-decoder models with a shared encoder and route language families to separate decoders. Our approach shares the intuition of aligning related tasks but instead learns routing from latent commonalities in data rather than predefining families as in Kong et al. (2021). A complementary line explores language-specific sub-networks, where Frankle and Carbin (2019) study pruning to identify salient parameters for a task. Lin et al. (2021) adapt this idea to multilingual NMT by pruning fine-tuned models to obtain language-dependent parameter subsets. In contrast, we mask layer outputs to map sub-networks to tasks, and we learn masks with variational techniques rather than heuristics. Adaptive sparsity within Transformers has also been explored, with variable depth across tasks proposed by Li et al. (2020). Gong et al. (2021a, 2021b) study selective activation of heads, layers, and feed-forward blocks to tailor capacity per task. Mixture-of-experts architectures extend this trend by replacing single FFN layers with expert pools and learned routing (Lepikhin et al., 2021; Fedus et al., 2021). Adapter-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning has proven effective across tasks (Houlsby et al., 2019; Bapna and Firat, 2019; Pham et al., 2020; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). Unsupervised multilingual adapters further reduce supervision needs (Philip et al., 2020), yet hard domain boundaries can induce catastrophic forgetting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; McCloskey and Cohen, 1989). Recent work examines routing between shared and language-specific pathways during training to address fuzzy domains (Pham et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021), whereas our method explicitly integrates task proximity into the routing mechanism.

document: Many pieces of previous work aim to measure knowledge stored in pretrained language models. Early studies explored whether masked language models encode factual associations that can be surfaced by cloze prompts. Petroni et al. (2019) propose to retrieve factual knowledge in BERT using cloze-style queries. Their experiments show that BERT can recall many relations without any task-specific finetuning. Subsequent work refined prompt design to mitigate sensitivity to surface forms. Jiang et al. (2020b) improve cloze queries using mining and paraphrasing strategies to increase coverage. Concurrently, template-free approaches examined the capacity of sequence-to-sequence models to answer factual questions directly from parameters. Roberts et al. (2020) introduce closed-book question answering as a probe of parametric knowledge. This line of research emphasizes what models know but less about where and how that knowledge is represented. Interpretability studies attempt to localize knowledge to neurons, heads, layers, or subspaces in the network. Elazar et al. (2021) measure and improve the consistency of factual predictions across paraphrases. Other analyses track how training objectives and data mixtures shape the emergence of factual competence. For example, scaling studies suggest that larger models memorize more facts but also generalize better with appropriate regularization. Recent work investigates causal interventions that edit individual facts without broad regressions. Meng et al. (2022) propose rank-one model editing to target specific associations in transformer layers. De Cao et al. (2021) develop Knowledge Editing via Language Models to insert or correct facts post hoc. Despite progress, it remains challenging to disentangle memorized patterns from genuinely compositional reasoning. Probing methods often conflate representational availability with linear extractability. Control experiments with counterfactual prompts and calibrated baselines help reduce misinterpretation. Beyond English, multilingual studies reveal uneven coverage across languages and scripts. Domain adaptation further complicates evaluation when facts shift over time or across corpora. Temporal robustness benchmarks test whether models update beliefs when new evidence appears. Knowledge-intensive tasks such as slot filling, open-domain QA, and entity linking provide complementary signals. Retrieval-augmented models separate storage from reasoning by consulting external sources at inference. Karpukhin et al. (2020) and Lewis et al. (2020) demonstrate strong gains from dense retrieval coupled with generation. Nevertheless, purely parametric probes remain valuable for auditing pretrained checkpoints. In this paper, we focus on opening the black box to identify neurons and pathways most responsible for factual responses. We adopt intervention-based analyses that quantify the causal effect of targeted component perturbations. Our approach complements prompt-based probing by revealing internal structure rather than only output behavior. We evaluate on established factual benchmarks to compare against cloze and closed-book baselines. For cloze probing, we follow prior templates and report calibrated accuracy under prompt paraphrases. For closed-book QA, we reproduce prior settings described in (Roberts et al., 2020 and analyze sensitivity to editing. Overall, our findings connect interpretability insights with practical guidance for responsible model deployment.

document: In this section we mainly discuss and compare related work in the two most relevant directions: the assessment benchmarks to the general narrative comprehension skills; and the tasks specifically designed for character-centered predictions over narratives. Table gives a summary of these narrative comprehension tasks, associated with their required skills of comprehension. We also reviewed studies on character-centered tasks over non-narrative texts like synopses and chit-chat (i.e., not story-related) conversations. Detailed rationales of the required skills for each task are discussed in Appendix A. Early benchmarks emphasized plot-level understanding with simple multiple-choice questions and cloze tests that probe surface coherence rather than deep role reasoning. More recent datasets seek to evaluate temporal and causal reasoning across long contexts, but they often omit explicit modeling of characters and their evolving attributes (Gonzalez et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019. In contrast, character-centered tasks focus on identity resolution, trait inference, intentions, and interactions, requiring persistent memory across scenes. Methods for these tasks range from rule-based pipelines to pretraining with narrative-specific objectives, yet evaluation remains fragmented across domains. Cross-genre comparisons reveal that models trained on short fables or movie synopses tend to overfit stylistic cues and fail to generalize to long-form literary fiction. For instance, approaches targeting persona consistency in conversations often struggle when transferred to literary novels with nested focalization. Building on these observations, we advocate for reporting skill-specific diagnostics and for separating character-level predictions from global story understanding to ensure clearer attribution of model capabilities.

document: Extractive Related Work Generation. Early related work generation systems employed the extractive summarization approach. Hoang and Kan (2010) pioneered the task, developing rules to select sentences following a topic hierarchy tree that was assumed to be given as input. Hu and Wan (2014) grouped sentences into topic-biased clusters with PLSA, modeled sentence importance with SVR and applied a global optimization framework to select sentences. Chen and Zhuge (2019) se-lected sentences from papers that co-cited the same cited papers as the target paper in order to cover a minimum Steiner tree constructed from a paper's keywords. Wang et al. (2019) extracted Cited Text Spans (CTS), the matched text spans in the cited paper that are most related to a given citation. However, these extractive approaches aim to maximally cover the citation texts with the extracted sentences, thus mostly ignoring the reference type citations that are concise and abstractive ( §3.1.3). Abstractive Related Work Generation. Recently, Xing et al. (2020) extend the pointergenerator (See et al., 2017) to take two text inputs, allowing them to recover a masked citation sentence given its neighboring context sentences. Ge et al. (2021) encode the citation context, cited paper's abstract, and citation network and train their model with multiple objectives: sentence salience score regression of the cited paper's abstract, functional role classification of the citation sentence, and citation sentence generation. Chen et al. (2021) propose a relation-aware, multi-document encoder to generate a related work paragraph given a set of cited papers. Luu et al. (2021) fine-tune GPT2 (Radford et al., 2019) on scientific texts and explore several techniques for representing documents, such as using extracted named entities. All of the works described above focus on the generation aspect, while neglecting dataset collection; their datasets are mostly extracted automatically. Moreover, the datasets are not reused, though they are publicly available, because these works all use slightly different problem definitions, and thus the models are not directly comparable (Li and Ouyang, 2022). In this work, we focus on collecting a dataset that is widely applicable to various related work generation settings, rather than proposing another incomparable approach. A key challenge lies in modeling discourse structure so that models avoid redundantly quoting boilerplate phrases from source articles. Graph-based encoders have been explored to link citations and topical threads across documents (Yasunaga et al., 2019). Citation intent classification has likewise been integrated to condition generation on rhetorical roles such as background, method, or comparison (Jurgens et al., 2018;Cohan et al., 2019). Dataset quality remains a bottleneck because noisy alignments between citing and cited spans introduce spurious supervision signals (Fujii and Ishikawa, 2007;Cohan and Goharian, 2017). To mitigate such noise, some works adopt distant supervision with confidence reweighting and curriculum schedules (Zhou and Chen, 2021). However, domain shift across venues and subfields still degrades performance when training on one corpus and evaluating on another (Beltagy et al., 2019). Pre-training on large scientific corpora partly closes this gap by providing field-specific terminology and stylistic priors (Lo et al., 2020;Gururangan et al., 2020). Beyond sentence extraction, controllable generation aims to steer outputs by specifying target venues, novelty levels, or citation density (Keskar et al., 2019;Dathathri et al., 2020). While control signals improve faithfulness to user preferences, they also risk amplifying exposure bias when trained with teacher forcing alone (Holtzman et al., 2020). Recent reinforcement learning approaches optimize evaluation proxies such as coverage, coherence, and factual consistency with respect to a small set of human preferences (Stiennon et al., 2020;Bai et al., 2022). Nevertheless, automatic metrics for related work remain underdeveloped compared with summarization, with limited agreement to expert judgments (Fabbri et al., 2021). Consequently, reproducible benchmarks that standardize input construction, citation grounding, and evaluation protocols are urgently needed, as argued by Li 2022. We therefore release a curated dataset with explicit alignment between citation contexts and cited abstracts, along with strong extractive and abstractive baselines. Our benchmark is designed to encourage modular comparisons across retrieval, planning, and generation components, enabling clearer progress on related work generation.

document: Vision-language few-shot learning. Leveraging aligned image–text corpora, vision–language models trained with contrastive objectives have shown strong zero-shot classification and retrieval performance (Jia et al., 2021; Radford et al., 2021). Beyond purely discriminative alignment, generative strategies that condition language models on visual tokens have emerged as a complementary paradigm (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021; Alayrac et al., 2022). Different from the discriminative manner, Menick et al. (2021) prompt a large language model with a vision prefix in a generative way to enable few-shot transfer. While these systems excel with broad pre-training, their sample efficiency and robustness under distribution shift remain active research challenges Bommasani et al., 2021). This paper focuses on how prompt design, adaptation, and calibration influence few-shot generalization in multimodal settings. Language model prompting. Prompting reframes tasks as text infilling or conditional generation, thereby eliciting knowledge from frozen language models with minimal task-specific supervision (Petroni et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020). Following the taxonomy of prompt-based learning, methods span discrete templates, soft prompt vectors, and instruction tuning (Liu et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Sanh et al., 2022). Early approaches relied on manual cloze prompts with verbalizers, later extended by automatic template mining and calibration techniques (Gao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). Semi-supervised variants such as PET and iPET demonstrate strong few-shot gains by iteratively relabeling unlabeled corpora with ensemble-produced pseudo-labels (Schick and Schütze, 2021a; Schick and Schütze, 2021b). In multimodal scenarios, prompts can target either the language backbone, the visual encoder, or the cross-modal fusion module, trading off flexibility and stability (Li et al., 2022; Jia et al., 2022). Recent work integrates contrastive pre-training with promptable decoders to better exploit weak supervision from captions and alt-text at web scale (Radford et al., 2021; Alayrac et al., 2022). Nevertheless, naively porting unimodal prompt heuristics to multimodal pipelines often underperforms due to modality mismatch, tokenization drift, and label space granularity (Tsimpoukelli et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2022). We therefore analyze prompt transfer through the lens of distributional alignment, calibration, and retrieval-augmented conditioning, grounding claims in controlled few-shot benchmarks. Our study also examines the interaction between prompt length, visual prefix capacity, and in-context example selection strategies (Liu et al., 2021; Rubin et al., 2022). For completeness, we contrast parametric adaptation via soft prompts with lightweight fine-tuning of adapter layers on the same budget to isolate data efficiency effects (Lester et al., 2021; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Finally, we relate our findings to the broader literature on instruction following and emergent abilities in large language models (Wei et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022). Together, these threads situate our contributions within the evolving practice of prompt-driven, vision–language few-shot learning.

document: Continuous prompts. There is a line of work focused on tuning continuous prompts (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Qin and Eisner, 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). A recurring theme in this line of work is the strength of continuous prompt tuning, which results in strong yet compact models compared to conventional architecture fine-tuning approaches. Motivated by the success of continuous prompt tuning, this paper investigates the feasibility of interpreting a learned continuous prompt and its connection to discrete prompts. Discrete prompts. The release of GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) sparked a lot of excitement about the emergent ability of LMs in following discrete natural language prompts. Consequently, countless follow-up studies have used manually designed discrete prompts for probing LMs (Petroni et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020), improving LMs few-shot ability (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Le Scao and Rush, 2021), and their zero-shot ability as well as transferability (Mishra et al., 2021a; Reynolds and McDonell, 2021). While discrete prompts have clear advantages, in addition to being human-readable and thus easily interpretable, we do not have efficient and algorithmic ways of reconstructing them. For example, Shin et al. (2020)'s algorithm discovers discrete prompts, yet the results are not human readable. Prior work also finds that model performance is highly sensitive to small changes in wordings (Mishra et al., 2021a) and that optimization over the discrete prompt space is nontrivial and often highly unstable. Our findings here about the disconnect between continuous prompts and their discrete interpretation provides another perspective on the difficulty of discovering discrete prompts via continuous optimization algorithms that directly or indirectly leverage the continuous space. Despite progress, the mechanisms by which soft prompts encode task information remain poorly understood. Recent studies analyze embedding trajectories and token salience to infer latent structure in learned prompts (Liu et al., 2022; Vu et al., 2022). However, most analyses rely on post hoc probing that may not faithfully capture the causal role of specific prompt dimensions. In contrast, we propose an intervention-based framework that perturbs subspaces of the prompt to test functional hypotheses. Our approach complements concurrent efforts on prompt initialization, regularization, and sparsity (Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2022). It also clarifies when and why continuous prompts can approximate the behavior of handcrafted templates used in few-shot prompting. As demonstrated by Li and Liang, continuous tuning can achieve competitive performance with far fewer trainable parameters. Yet, semantic alignment between learned vectors and human-interpretable tokens is neither guaranteed nor necessary for generalization. Several works investigate transfer across tasks and domains by reusing or composing prompts (Vu et al., 2021; Asai et al., 2022). These studies suggest that modular prompt components can capture reusable inductive biases across related problems. Nevertheless, direct transplantation of a prompt often suffers from distribution shift and spurious correlations. Building on these insights, we evaluate whether interpretation-guided constraints can improve robustness without sacrificing accuracy. We compare against representative baselines in both encoder-only and encoder–decoder architectures, including BERT and T5 (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). To situate our contributions, we next review complementary lines of research on discrete templates and automated prompt discovery. Template mining approaches infer surface patterns from corpora or gradients and then validate candidates with held-out data (Shin et al., 2020; Wallace et al., 2019). While effective in some settings, these methods are sensitive to lexical artifacts and may not scale to complex compositional tasks. Meta-learning formulations treat prompts as fast parameters that adapt rapidly from small support sets (Finn et al., 2017; Bansal et al., 2020). Reinforcement learning has also been applied to optimize discrete prompts with non-differentiable objectives such as calibration or safety (Deng et al., 2022). Our work is orthogonal to these directions and instead examines the interpretability and controllability of already-trained soft prompts. From an evaluation perspective, we adopt diverse metrics beyond accuracy, including faithfulness tests and counterfactual sensitivity. We further analyze failure modes by correlating prompt subspace activations with error typologies on controlled benchmarks. Taken together, the results illuminate how prompt geometry mediates task heuristics learned during fine-tuning. This understanding enables principled design choices for practitioners who must balance efficiency, interpretability, and stability. We conclude by outlining open challenges for bridging continuous and discrete prompting, and by discussing implications for safe deployment in high-stakes applications.

document: Pretrained Language Models. Owing to the great achievements Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) has made, the paradigm of pretrained language models (PLMs) is thriving (Radford et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019b; Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). It is widely recognized that PLMs can learn massive knowledge from large corpora, leading to significant progress in various language tasks. Given such results across extensive NLP tasks, it is timely to systematically evaluate the abilities of PLMs to deepen understanding and facilitate broader application. Language Model Evaluation. Many efforts have studied the evaluation of language model performance and generalization. Liu et al. (2019a) evaluate BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), GPT (Radford et al., 2018), and ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) on a variety of linguistic tasks. Their results suggest that features generated by PLMs are sufficient for high performance on a broad set of tasks but fail on tasks requiring fine-grained linguistic knowledge. Tenney et al. (2019) evaluate similar models on a suite of sub-sentence linguistic analysis tasks, showing that PLMs encode both syntax and semantics in their parameters. Zhou et al. (2020) reach a similar conclusion that PLMs can learn rich knowledge while focusing on evaluating commonsense. However, these studies focus on one dimension of PLM ability evaluation. Benchmark collections such as GLUE (Wang et al., 2019b) and CLUE (Xu et al., 2020) consider mixtures of tasks but lack comprehensive ability-centric coverage. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to systematically evaluate PLMs by defining diverse abilities and performing extensive comparisons. We survey evaluation dimensions spanning linguistic competence, factuality, reasoning, robustness, and efficiency. Prior work on probing examines what linguistic features are linearly recoverable from hidden states (Adi et al., 2017; Hewitt and Manning, 2019; May et al., 2019). Challenge sets target specific phenomena to reduce superficial correlations in training data (McCoy et al., 2019; Warstadt et al., 2020). Adversarial evaluation reveals brittleness under meaning-preserving perturbations (Jia and Liang, 2017; Ribeiro et al., 2020). Calibration metrics assess whether a model’s confidence aligns with empirical accuracy (Guo et al., 2017; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Efficiency-oriented studies analyze trade-offs between parameter count, latency, and accuracy (Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020). Multilingual evaluation highlights cross-lingual transfer and typological challenges (Pires et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). Domain adaptation benchmarks test robustness under distribution shift from news to biomedical or legal text (Gururangan et al., 2020; Chalkidis et al., 2020). In-context learning and instruction tuning redefine evaluation protocols by changing how supervision is presented to the model (Brown et al., 2020; Ouyang et al., 2022). The Transformer architecture remains foundational (see also Vaswani et al., 2017. Yet parameter scaling alone does not guarantee stronger reasoning without careful dataset design (Srivastava et al., 2022; Wei et al., 2022). Recent works argue for ability-specific evaluation suites that control confounders and use targeted diagnostics (Sakaguchi et al., 2021; Khashabi et al., 2020). Our framework unifies dataset selection, prompting strategies, and metric design to enable apples-to-apples comparisons. We adopt standardized pre-processing, consistent evaluation scripts, and public leaderboards to foster reproducibility (Pineau et al., 2021). In addition to accuracy, we track calibration, abstention quality, and cost-aware metrics to reflect real-world deployment needs (Dietterich, 2017; Kamath et al., 2020). For factuality, we incorporate knowledge-intensive tasks and automated verification pipelines (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Petroni et al., 2019). For robustness, we include noise-injection, paraphrase, and counterfactual evaluation to probe reliance on spurious cues (Kaushik et al., 2020; Gardner et al., 2020). For safety and ethics, we use toxicity, bias, and privacy assessments to characterize potential harms (Bender et al., 2021; Solaiman et al., 2019). We report per-ability scorecards and aggregated indices to surface trade-offs among capabilities. Finally, we release code, datasets, and evaluation logs to catalyze standardized, transparent assessment for future PLMs.

document: Older methods for text-based stimulus representation include co-occurrence counts from large corpora and topic distributions learned from text. Early work linked distributional statistics to neural activity using simple semantic features derived from text (Mitchell et al., 2008). Subsequent studies extended these approaches with corpus-driven semantic spaces and models that captured narrative context (Pereira et al., 2013; Huth et al., 2016). Syntactic and discourse features were also leveraged to improve predictions of time-resolved brain responses during reading (Wehbe et al., 2014). Despite these advances, purely text-derived features often failed to account for multimodal aspects of meaning encountered in naturalistic settings. This limitation motivated the exploration of richer semantic and experiential representations that better approximate human conceptual knowledge. Semantic representation models based on distributed word embeddings became prevalent with the availability of large corpora and neural training methods (Pereira et al., 2016; Hollenstein et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). Sentence-level encoders further captured compositional semantics and discourse structure relevant for narrative processing (Sun et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020). Recurrent networks and early Transformer-based language models offered additional gains by modeling sequential dependencies at multiple scales (Jain and Huth, 2018; Gauthier and Levy, 2019; Schwartz et al., 2019). More recently, task-agnostic pretrained Transformers have yielded strong brain encoding performance via contextualized representations that track lexical, syntactic, and semantic cues (Toneva and Wehbe, 2019). Complementing semantic models, experiential attribute approaches represent words using ratings that quantify associations with sensory, motor, affective, and social dimensions (Handjaras et al., 2016; Anderson et al., 2020; Berezutskaya et al., 2020). Such ratings can be continuous or binary, and they enable explicit alignment between conceptual features and neural responses during concept processing (Wang et al., 2017; Jat et al., 2020). Hybrid models that fuse contextual embeddings with experiential features have been proposed to bridge the gap between text-derived signals and grounded cognition (Caucheteux et al., 2021a; Antonello et al., 2021). When used as regressors in voxel-wise encoding models, these enriched representations can disentangle overlapping contributions of lexical, syntactic, and situation-model information. Nevertheless, systematic comparisons across architectures, tasks, and modalities remain limited in scope. A key methodological question is whether generic language models suffice for neural prediction or whether task-specific pretraining can yield measurable benefits. Prior studies often adopted off-the-shelf models without adapting objectives to the stimuli or behavioral paradigms at hand (Pereira et al., 2018). In contrast, task-tuned Transformers might emphasize information that is especially diagnostic for comprehension under reading or listening conditions. Another open issue is the relative contribution of phonological, syntactic, and semantic layers to explaining brain activity at different temporal and spatial scales (Caucheteux et al., 2021a). Addressing these questions requires carefully controlled evaluations that compare representational families under identical encoding pipelines. It also calls for transparent reporting of preprocessing, alignment, and regularization strategies to avoid confounds in cross-study comparisons (Huth et al., 2016; Wehbe et al., 2014). In this work, we synthesize prior findings and design experiments that systematically vary pretraining objectives and supervision signals. We evaluate word, subword, and sentence-level representations across multiple datasets of naturalistic language comprehension in fMRI and MEG. To contextualize our approach, we briefly review evidence on the mapping between hierarchical linguistic structure and cortical organization (Fedorenko et al., 2011; Blank and Fedorenko, 2020). These results suggest distinct but interacting networks sensitive to lexico-semantic content, syntax, and domain-general control, which impose constraints on representational hypotheses. We also consider how model-derived surprisal and entropy correlate with neural markers of prediction and integration during continuous language processing (Hale et al., 2015; Shain et al., 2020). While many reports use proper parenthetical citation style, inconsistencies in the literature complicate meta-analytic synthesis (van Heuven et al., 2014), see (Smith et al. 2019; Lee 2020) for contrasting practices. For completeness, we compare fixed-context embeddings with adapters and lightweight finetuning schemes that tailor internal representations to decoding targets (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020). We then quantify how representational geometry evolves across layers and how such dynamics relate to spatially resolved brain maps (Caucheteux and King, 2022). Building on these insights, our framework integrates stimulus-level controls to separate lexical identity from higher-order semantics. We hypothesize that task-specific pretraining will yield incremental gains over strong baselines when aligned with the cognitive demands of the stimulus. Accordingly, we train reading-optimized and listening-optimized Transformers and assess their predictive fidelity using ridge-regularized encoding models and cross-subject generalization. We further analyze error patterns at the level of regions of interest and individual voxels to identify systematic failures and opportunities for cross-task transfer. Finally, we release code, preprocessing recipes, and detailed ablations to support reproducibility and future benchmarking. Together, these contributions clarify the conditions under which linguistic stimuli representations translate into robust and interpretable neural predictions.

Linguistic studies have long observed the distinct properties of translationese from text originally authored in the same language (Toury, 1980;Gellerstam, 1986;Baker, 1993;Toury, 1995). Recent work in MT identifies the translationese-tooriginal portion of test sets (i.e., sets unaligned with the model direction) as statistically significantly easier (Graham et al., 2020), echoing many previous observations (Toral et al., 2018;Lembersky et al., 2012;Läubli et al., 2018), and thus some suggest excluding this portion from future test sets (Toral et al., 2018;Zhang and Toral, 2019;Graham et al., 2020;Barrault et al., 2019).

Based on this speculated inflation of MT performance due to the translationese in the test set, further work inspects what previous conclusions about the effectiveness of MT models should be recalibrated. Some discover that models with BT mostly improve on the inflated test set but not the other more challenging portion (Toral et al., 2018;Freitag et al., 2019;Edunov et al., 2020; Appendix A, Table 1) and raise concerns that BT is not as effective as expected. Others argue that BT can still improve on both test sets (Edunov et al., 2020).

Our work differs from all previous work in that we bring in two new important factors when considering how translationese affects MT performance, namely the train-test alignment, and data-model alignment. Moreover, beyond the correlation-based analysis in previous papers (Graham et al., 2020;Edunov et al., 2020), we conduct causal inference (Pearl, 2009;Peters et al., 2017) to contribute causal insights on how translationese affects MT.

Translationese has been attributed to interference from source syntax, normalization toward frequent target forms, and cautious lexical choices typical of novice translators (Baker, 1993;Toury, 1995). In MT evaluation, such distributional shifts can inflate scores for certain directions while obscuring generalization in realistic deployment (Graham et al., 2020). Back-translation (BT) interacts with these shifts because synthetic target-like text differs from human-produced originals, potentially aligning more closely with translationese segments (Sennrich et al., 2016;Edunov et al., 2020). Prior datasets often mixed original and translated content without explicit labels, limiting fine-grained analysis (Barrault et al., 2019). Recent corpora annotate text origin, enabling stratified reporting across translationese strata and model directions, building on Zhang and Toral (2019 and related tools (Toral et al., 2018). Nevertheless, practice lags behind recommendations, and leaderboard protocols rarely require origin-aware splits (Graham et al., 2020). We posit that two alignment concepts are essential: train–test alignment, capturing whether training distribution matches the evaluated direction, and data–model alignment, capturing inductive biases that favor particular distributions. The first determines whether improvements stem from genuine robustness or from fortunate overlap with translationese statistics (Peters et al., 2017). The second addresses architecture and objective choices that might privilege fluency over adequacy under distribution shift (Edunov et al., 2020). To empirically study these factors, we construct matched subsets controlling for source–target direction and for origin labels across multiple language pairs (Barrault et al., 2019;Läubli et al., 2018). We then estimate causal effects using adjustment sets identified from a domain graph and validate sensitivity to unobserved confounding (Pearl, 2009). Our findings suggest that improvements attributed to BT are partially mediated by alignment with translationese-heavy test conditions, but that causal gains remain for robust models trained with origin-aware objectives, consistent with Zhang and Toral (2019).

spans: Lopez et al. 2019; Kim and Park 2021
document: In this section, we analyze the literature on the two main evaluation problems tackled in this paper: multi-labeling and class hierarchies, keeping the focus on extreme scenarios (numerous and unbalanced classes). Evaluating multi-label predictions with hundreds or thousands of categories requires metrics that remain informative under severe class imbalance. Early work emphasized micro and macro averaging to reconcile per-instance and per-class perspectives in multilabel classification. However, these aggregates often mask systematic errors against rare labels, which are precisely those of greatest interest in extreme regimes. Hierarchical taxonomies further complicate evaluation because misclassifications may be semantically close or distant depending on tree depth. Consequently, recent research seeks measures that incorporate both label frequency and structural proximity to reflect practical utility. Conventional accuracy is ill-suited when instances may bear multiple labels that interact through exclusivity, subsumption, or mutual reinforcement. Precision and recall defined at the instance level provide partial insight, yet they ignore correlations among labels that arise from ontology constraints. Label ranking approaches reformulate evaluation around ordering quality, using metrics such as average precision and normalized discounted cumulative gain. In extreme multi-label settings, researchers have argued for propensity-scored variants that downweight frequent head labels to counter long-tail bias. These adjustments aim to align model selection with downstream retrieval and recommendation tasks where recall over rare concepts is paramount. Still, standardized reporting remains fragmented, hindering cross-paper comparisons and meta-analyses. Hierarchical evaluation introduces a distinct set of challenges because distance in the taxonomy should modulate penalty severity. Path based losses, lowest common ancestor similarity, and information content weighting are common strategies to operationalize semantic distance. There is also debate regarding whether evaluation should be local to each internal node or global over the entire hierarchy. Local measures facilitate interpretability for error remediation in particular branches, whereas global measures encourage consistent depth-aware behavior. A further complication arises when hierarchies are directed acyclic graphs rather than trees, creating multiple ancestor paths. Robust metrics for such structures must avoid double-counting while preserving the intuition that nearby misclassifications are less harmful. Dataset scale and label sparsity interact to produce high variance estimates unless evaluation is carefully stratified. Bootstrapping over instances and labels, as well as hierarchical block resampling, has been proposed to quantify uncertainty on reported metrics. Cross-dataset benchmarking further stresses the need for protocol-level controls, including fixed candidate label spaces and identical preprocessing, Lopez et al. 2019; Kim and Park 2021. Without such controls, gains may reflect easier distributions, different negative sampling, or annotation guidelines rather than genuine model improvements. Public leaderboards have accelerated progress but occasionally reinforce metric gaming, motivating broader suites of criteria that capture calibration and fairness. Cost-sensitive analysis is especially relevant when false positives on certain branches carry disproportionate operational impact. Representation learning has progressed from bag-of-words and one-vs-rest linear models to architectures that exploit label semantics and hierarchy topology. Joint embedding methods posit a shared space for inputs and labels, enabling similarity based inference that scales sublinearly with the number of classes. Graph neural networks and hyperbolic embeddings explicitly encode tree or DAG geometry to improve both classification and evaluation robustness. Calibration techniques, including temperature scaling and focal losses, have been adapted to multi-label outputs to mitigate overconfidence on tail classes. Weak supervision and semi-supervised learning address annotation scarcity by propagating constraints from the hierarchy and leveraging unlabeled corpora. Recent work also explores counterfactual data augmentation to balance rare labels without distorting the underlying taxonomy. Despite these advances, a consensus on best practices for evaluation across extreme multi-label and hierarchical settings has not fully emerged. Community efforts now advocate theory-backed reporting templates, ablations on imbalance levels, and release of code for metric computation. Emerging application domains such as biomedical ontologies and product catalogs underscore the stakes of principled evaluation at scale. As we synthesize prior findings, we emphasize the complementarity of ranking-based and hierarchy-aware measures under realistic operational constraints. We identify open questions around calibration under taxonomic shifts, uncertainty quantification for rare paths, and reproducible benchmarking protocols. These gaps motivate our experimental design and the recommendations we articulate for future work.

document: Fake news detection has emerged as a pressing research topic in natural language processing and computational social science. Early studies framed the task as document classification using stylistic cues, source metadata, and lexical features. Representative datasets and feature-based baselines appeared in the mid-2010s (Rubin et al., 2016; Wang, 2017; Rashkin et al., 2017; Pérez-Rosas et al., 2018). These works established the problem setting but offered limited generalization across outlets and topics (Sarkar et al., 2018; Atanasova et al., 2019). With the rise of deep learning, researchers shifted to neural encoders that learn representations directly from text. Recurrent neural networks and their gated variants were among the first successful models for this task (Karimi et al., 2018; Nasir et al., 2021). Transformer-based language models have since delivered state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks (Zellers et al., 2019). Nevertheless, single-document encoders often overlook cross-article signals that are crucial for robust detection. One promising direction is to model relations among contemporaneous articles that report on the same events. Prior work explored aligning news content with external knowledge bases to check for inconsistencies (Karimi and Tang, 2019). However, KB coverage gaps and temporal lag can weaken such signals in fast-evolving news cycles. In contrast, cross-document inference can exploit redundancy and contradiction directly among related articles. Beyond textual content, auxiliary signals from user engagements and propagation patterns have proved informative (Shu et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2020). Multimodal approaches further integrate images and metadata to capture stylistic and semantic mismatches (Khattar et al., 2019; Tan, 2020. Yet many systems still treat articles independently and miss discourse-level consistency cues across sources. Building structured representations of entities and events can bridge this gap by enabling aggregation and reasoning. Fine-grained detection targets specific claims, entities, or event mentions within documents rather than entire articles. Such formulations are attractive because they surface the precise loci of misinformation for downstream interventions. However, extracting reliable claim graphs and aligning them across documents remains technically challenging. Existing studies on claim-level detection typically operate on per-article extractions without cross-document consolidation. Closely related is the fact verification task, which assesses the veracity of short statements against retrieved evidence. Large-scale resources have advanced this setting in the general domain (Thorne et al., 2018) and in specialized areas such as climate science and public health (Diggelmann et al., 2020; Wadden et al., 2020; Saakyan et al., 2021). Despite progress, verification systems optimized for short claims struggle to model the internal discourse structure of long-form news. Consequently, methods for document-level misinformation detection and claim-level verification should be seen as complementary. Dataset construction poses a persistent bottleneck because high-quality labels are expensive and time-consuming to obtain. Some corpora derive labels from source reliability, which risks confounding outlet style with truthfulness (Rashkin et al., 2017; Rubin et al., 2016). Others rely on annotations from professional fact-checking sites, yielding higher precision but limited coverage; see Shu et al. 2020 for a survey, and Pérez-Rosas et al. (2018) for a crowd-sourced corpus. Synthetic datasets generated via controllable text generation provide scale while introducing potential distributional artifacts (Zellers et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2020). To harness cross-document signals, we propose constructing within-document graphs that link entities and events extracted by information extraction pipelines. These graphs can then be merged into a cross-document knowledge graph that clusters event mentions and links articles to their constituent elements. Graph neural networks offer a unified framework to encode such structures and to propagate evidence across related articles. In a pipeline architecture, event-level predictions can regularize document-level decisions by reinforcing consistent signals. Our review highlights three open challenges for future work on misinformation detection. First, we need principled evaluation protocols that test generalization across outlets, topics, and time. Second, we must clarify the interplay among content features, propagation signals, and cross-document consistency when designing models (Khattar et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2020). Third, curated benchmarks should report both article-level and claim-level metrics while documenting annotation provenance and potential biases.

Dense retrieval with dual encoders has emerged as a compelling alternative to sparse matching in open-domain search. DPR initializes a bi-encoder with BERT and demonstrates that supervised contrastive training on question–passage pairs outperforms earlier dense methods (Karpukhin et al., 2020). RocketQA extends this idea by iteratively mining hard negatives and retraining with improved supervision (Qu et al., 2021). ANCE further automates negative selection by indexing model snapshots to mine progressively harder distractors during training (Xiong et al., 2021). TAS-B combines balanced topic-aware sampling with knowledge distillation from cross-encoders and late-interaction models such as ColBERT to stabilize learning (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). xMoCo adapts momentum contrast for text by maintaining fast and slow encoders and a large queue of negatives (Yang et al., 2021; He et al., 2020). Despite these advances, robust generalization remains challenging on the diverse BEIR benchmark, where many dense retrievers underperform out of domain (Thakur et al., 2021). This observation has motivated representation pretraining tailored to retrieval beyond generic masked language modeling (Gururangan et al., 2020).

Early work on pretraining for retrieval proposed the Inverse Cloze Task, which forms a query by removing a sentence from its source passage and treats the remainder as its positive context (Lee et al., 2019). Subsequent studies explored combining ICT with masked language modeling and with objectives that explicitly maximize agreement between queries and relevant passages (Chang et al., 2020). Guu et al. pretrain a retriever jointly with a reader to enable end-to-end open-domain question answering, showing strong gains from retrieval-aware training signals (Guu et al., 2020). Condenser modifies Transformer pretraining to encourage information aggregation in the [CLS] representation that is critical for dense retrieval (Gao and Callan, 2021). Building on this idea, coCondenser adds a contrastive objective across passages to structure the embedding space before task tuning (Gao and Callan, 2022). Contriever advances unsupervised pretraining by using large-scale augmentations and in-batch negatives to learn domain-agnostic retrieval representations (Izacard et al., 2021). Other approaches leverage synthetic question generation or mined query–passage pairs to better match the distribution of downstream tasks (Bonifacio et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2021). Taken together, these pretraining strategies aim to produce retrievers that require less or no supervised finetuning while maintaining competitive performance.

Hard negative mining remains central because it shapes the fine structure of the embedding space and affects robustness to spurious lexical overlap. RocketQA demonstrates that self-improving mining cycles can improve recall, but the effect may be brittle when the target domain shifts significantly (Qu et al., 2021). We also note that careless citation formatting occasionally appears in the literature, for example like (Ahmad et al., 2019, which disrupts readability in dense related work sections. ANCE reduces manual curation effort via asynchronous indexing, yet repeated full-corpus encoding can be computationally demanding at scale (Xiong et al., 2021). BEIR highlights that retrievers tuned on a narrow distribution can latch onto dataset-specific artefacts rather than transferable semantics (Thakur et al., 2021). Cross-encoder teachers provide more precise supervision for distillation, but they are costly to train across many domains and tasks (Hofstätter et al., 2021). Multi-stage training that interleaves pretraining, mining, and distillation has been explored to balance efficiency and generalization (Ren et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the community still lacks a comprehensive understanding of which pretraining signals best prepare models for zero-shot retrieval.

Architectural choices also matter, as late-interaction models like ColBERT trade single-vector efficiency for improved recall via token-level matching (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Hybrid dense–sparse systems and learned sparse retrieval have narrowed the gap to traditional term matching while keeping differentiability for end-to-end training (Formal et al., 2021; Lin and Ma, 2021). Meanwhile, regularization via dropout scheduling, query augmentation, and adversarial perturbations has been proposed to reduce overfitting to head queries (Zhan et al., 2021). Recent work also examines multilingual and cross-lingual retrieval, where pretraining on aligned corpora can mitigate vocabulary and morphology mismatches (Asai et al., 2021). Domain-adaptive pretraining on unlabeled in-domain text remains a strong baseline, but it may still underperform when labels are scarce and query styles vary widely (Gururangan et al., 2020). Emerging methods revisit curriculum design to stage negatives from easy to hard in a way that reflects target distributions (Prakash et al., 2022).

In dialogue and conversational search, DPR-PAQ emphasizes the role of domain-matched supervision using synthetic and crawled QA pairs to pretrain before task-specific tuning (Oguz et al., 2021). Beyond retrieval, related pretraining for re-ranking and answer selection further informs how to allocate capacity between query and document encoders (Nogueira et al., 2020). A separate thread studies data selection, showing that sampling based on information gain or disagreement can accelerate training without hurting generalization (Xie et al., 2022). Our perspective synthesizes these strands to argue for objectives that reward cross-domain semantic alignment while maintaining efficient negative sampling. We therefore focus on pretraining signals that do not rely on expensive curated queries yet impart stable inductive biases for retrieval. The next section outlines our methodology and the design choices that operationalize these principles.

document: Adversarial examples in natural language processing have become a focal point of research with the advent of large pre-trained language models. Early work formalized adversarial perturbations for textual inputs by manipulating tokens, gradients, or syntactic structures to expose model brittleness (Ebrahimi et al., 2018; Jia and Liang, 2017; Alzantot et al., 2018). With the introduction of BERT and related architectures, both attack and defense methods increasingly rely on masked or sequence-to-sequence likelihoods to guide edits (Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020). Substitution-based attacks typically sample semantically compatible replacements via masked language models and then filter by semantic similarity or constraint satisfaction (Li et al., 2020; Morris et al., 2020; Garg and Ramakrishnan, 2020). Complementary approaches construct challenge sets through templates or manual interventions to stress-test inference models (McCoy et al., 2019; Glockner et al., 2018; Gardner et al., 2020). Robustness analysis has also expanded to multimodal settings where language guides visual reasoning, as evidenced by diagnostic datasets for caption grounding and visual question answering (Shekhar et al., 2017; Antol et al., 2015; Shah et al., 2019). Several studies argue that vision-language transformers overfit superficial cues rather than compositional semantics (Hendricks and Nematzadeh, 2021; Goyal et al., 2017; Kervadec et al., 2021), echoing findings in text-only models as shown in (Jia et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020. Building on this perspective, automatic perturbation pipelines adapt masked-LM proposals to modify content words while preserving fluency and visual plausibility (Liu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). Recent VQA releases introduce counterfactual splits and robustness benchmarks that target spurious correlations across question types, answers, and scene priors (Hudson and Manning, 2019; Li et al., 2021). In parallel, defenses leverage adversarial training, data augmentation, and calibrated decoding to stabilize predictions under distribution shift (Madry et al., 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Our contribution is to systematize LM-guided perturbations for video-language tasks and to evaluate their effect on both retrieval and question answering under controlled budget and semantics-preserving constraints.

document: The sentence fusion task aims to combine multiple sentences with overlapping content into a single, coherent statement that preserves shared information (Barzilay and McKeown, 2005; Filippova and Strube, 2008). Early approaches explored syntax-driven alignment and compression to identify commonalities across inputs (Marsi and Krahmer, 2005; McKeown et al., 2010). Subsequent work integrated lexical and semantic similarity features to guide content selection and aggregation (Thadani and McKeown, 2013). Variants of the task have been proposed, including sentence union, which aims to merge the union of information rather than just the intersection (Marsi and Krahmer, 2005). A related direction examines "disparate" sentence fusion, where inputs are topically related but share little verbatim overlap and thus require discourse-level reasoning (Elsner and Santhanam, 2011; Geva et al., 2019; Lebanoff et al., 2019; Lebanoff et al., 2020). This scenario typically arises within a single document when authors elaborate different aspects of an event across neighboring sentences. Systems must then infer rhetorical relations, resolve coreference, and manage lexical variation to produce fluent outputs (McKeown et al., 2010; Geva et al., 2019). Data resources for evaluating these abilities remain limited, motivating extended datasets that operationalize a "loose" intersection objective inspired by prior annotation protocols. Our collection follows established guidelines on identifying salient overlaps while permitting the inclusion of complementary facts Barzilay and McKeown, 2005; Filippova and Strube, 2008. In contrast to strict intersection, the loose variant avoids discarding modifiers and attributions that may aid interpretation by downstream summarizers (Thadani and McKeown, 2013). We also consider evaluation protocols that weight factual consistency and discourse coherence more heavily than surface similarity metrics (Lebanoff et al., 2020). Prior studies show that naive concatenation or compression can hallucinate unsupported links, underscoring the need for retrieval-aware and entailment-aware scoring (Geva et al., 2019). To that end, we adopt a pipeline that first retrieves candidate pairs within documents and then applies a neural combiner conditioned on dependency paths and entity chains (Filippova and Strube, 2008; McKeown et al., 2010). Our baselines include structure-aware graph encoders and pretrained sequence-to-sequence models fine-tuned for fusion with coverage constraints (Thadani and McKeown, 2013). We report results across news, encyclopedia, and scientific domains to probe robustness under varied discourse structures (Barzilay and McKeown, 2005). Error analyses highlight frequent failures in temporal ordering and attribution preservation, suggesting benefits from explicit discourse relation modeling (Elsner and Santhanam, 2011). Finally, we position our release as a companion to existing fusion corpora, offering broader genre coverage and more detailed rationales to spur robust progress in disparate and union-style fusion.
