document: Human evaluation remains central to assessing image captioning systems beyond automatic metrics like BLEU or CIDEr. Despite progress, there is no single consensus protocol across datasets and model families. Prior studies have compared annotation schemes, rater pools, and interface designs to balance cost with reliability. In this section, we survey methods, identify pitfalls, and motivate a transparent rubric tailored to contemporary models. Early benchmarks leveraged crowdsourced judgments on small curated corpora. PASCAL sentences provided one of the first testbeds for descriptive adequacy (Rashtchian et al., 2010). Flickr8k and Flickr30k expanded scale and linguistic diversity using Mechanical Turk annotations (Hodosh et al., 2013; Young et al., 2014). The MSCOCO dataset further normalized quality assessment via large rater pools and standardized prompts (Lin et al., 2014). Scoring protocols vary from direct holistic ratings to pairwise comparisons and multidimensional checklists. Some works elicited scalar judgments of overall quality to approximate a single utility function (Mitchell et al., 2012). Others collected pairwise preferences to induce a latent ranking over competing captions (Vedantam et al., 2015). Multi-attribute rubrics separated truthfulness, relevance, thoroughness, and fluency to expose failure modes (Yatskar et al., 2014; Aditya et al., 2015; Elliott and Keller, 2013). Reliability hinges on clear definitions, calibrated exemplars, and mechanisms for disagreement resolution. Studies have shown that vague criteria inflate variance and reduce interrater agreement even with expert annotators (Snow et al., 2008). Simple qualification tests and ongoing gold checks can stabilize performance without imposing excessive burden (Callison-Burch, 2009). Aggregation strategies such as MACE and Dawid–Skene further mitigate noise while preserving minority signals (Hovy et al., 2013; Dawid and Skene, 1979). Rater background, device context, and presentation order can bias judgments toward lexical surface features (Bianchi et al., 2021). Interface design that randomizes caption order and hides model identity reduces anchoring and brand effects (Salman et al., 2022. Social media scenarios introduce additional dimensions like engagingness and accessibility that require task-specific instructions (Shuster et al., 2019; Gleason et al., 2020). For accessibility evaluations, prompt wording and example images must reflect real assistive use cases to avoid artifact exploitation. Correlations between automatic metrics and human judgments remain modest, particularly for modern neural captioners (Anderson et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). Learned metrics like SPICE and BERTScore improve signal but still miss pragmatic and commonsense violations (Anderson et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020; Sellam et al., 2020). Consequently, human studies continue to serve as the ground truth for model selection in sensitive deployments. We propose a rubric that decomposes quality into factuality, salience, specificity, fluency, and safety with concrete decision rules. Each dimension includes positive and negative anchors drawn from real captions to calibrate expectations. We train annotators with iterative feedback and provide adjudication guidelines to handle borderline cases. While expert judges yield higher consistency, crowdsourcing enables broader coverage when paired with robust quality control (Kazai et al., 2013). Our protocol balances these tradeoffs by mixing verified crowd raters with domain specialists when evaluating domain shift. We implement pairwise comparisons to derive preference matrices and fit a Bradley–Terry model for global ranking (Bradley and Terry, 1952). To capture absolute acceptability, we also collect calibrated Likert ratings with rater level normalization. On Flickr-style datasets, we emphasize scene relevance and description specificity to penalize generic templates (Young et al., 2014; Plummer et al., 2015). On news and documentary images, we add a sourcing criterion that flags unverifiable claims and speculative language (Biten et al., 2019). We monitor annotator well-being, support opting out of sensitive content, and compensate above local wage standards. Data statements document rater demographics and consent procedures to improve transparency and reproducibility. Our study does not cover multilingual captioning, which may require culturally adapted rubrics and bilingual raters. Future releases will extend to video narrations and integrate accessibility testing with screen reader workflows.

document: Vanilla Domain Adaptation. Several works employ domain adaptation to improve performance. For example, Du et al. (2020) approach the sentiment analysis task by using a BERT-based (Devlin et al., 2019) Klimaszewski and Andruszkiewicz, 2019), mixup synthesis training (Tang et al., 2020), and effective regularization (Vernikos et al., 2020). Cross-Lingual Domain Adaptation. Chen et al. (2018) proposed ADAN, an architecture based on a feed-forward neural network with three main components, namely: a feature extractor, a sentiment classifier, and a language discriminator. The latter has the purpose of supporting the adversarial training setup, thus covering the scenario where the model is unable to detect whether the input language is from the source dataset or the target one. A similar cross-lingual approach is adopted by Zhang et al. (2020), who developed a system to classify entries from the target language, while only labels from the source language are provided. Keung et al. (2019) employed the usage of multilingual BERT (Pires et al., 2019) and argued that a language-adversarial task can improve the performance of zero-resource cross-lingual transfers. Moreover, training under an adversarial technique helps the Transformer model align the representations of the English inputs. Under a Named Entity Recognition (NER) training scenario, Kim et al. (2017) use features on two levels (i.e., word and characters), together with Recurrent Neural Networks and a language discriminator used for the domain-adversarial setup. Similarly, Huang et al. (2019) use target language discriminators during the process of training models for low-resource name tagging. Word Complexity Prediction. Gooding and Kochmar (2019) base their implementation for CWI as a sequence labeling task on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) networks, inasmuch as the context helps towards proper identification of complex tokens. The authors used 300-dimensional pretrained word embeddings as inputs for the LSTMs. Also adopting a sequence labeling approach, Finnimore et al. (2019) consider handcrafted features, including punctuation or syllables, that can properly identify complex structures. The same sequence labeling approach can be applied under a plurality voting technique (Polikar, 2006), or even using an Oracle (Kuncheva et al., 2001, which functions best when applied to multiple solutions by jointly using them to obtain a final prediction. At the same time, Zaharia et al. (2020) explore the power of Transformer-based models (Vaswani et al., 2017) in cross-lingual environments by using different training scenarios, depending on the scarcity of the resources: zero-shot, one-shot, as well as few-shot learning. Moreover, CWI can be also approached as a probabilistic task; for example, De Hertog and Tack (2018) introduce a series of architectures that combine deep learning features, as well as handcrafted features to solve a regression problem.

document:
To date, negation detection has been heavily-reliant on rule-based systems. Chapman et al. (2001) proposed a simple system, NegEx, based on regular expressions to detect negation cues in a sentence given a concept of interest (the scope). NegEx remains the most popular approach to negation detection, especially in the clinical domain where target clinical concepts can be detected (e.g., with MetaMap (Aronson and Lang, 2010)). Further research has extended NegEx with syntactic information (Mehrabi et al., 2015;Peng et al., 2018), and shown that rule-based systems can achieve relatively good performance for detecting negation, especially in the biomedical domain, but do not generalize well to other domains or datasets.

To approach negation cue and negation scope detection with supervised machine learning, two classification tasks are defined: finding negation tokens and classifying tokens as the first or last (or neither) token within the scope of negation. Most works follow a common scheme in extracting various features from the sentence, in using a classifier to classify each token as the beginning, inside, or outside of a negation cue or scope span (Morante and Daelemans, 2009;Ou and Patrick, 2015;Cruz et al., 2016). Recently, research has shifted to applying deep learning methods to the task. Most approaches make use of RNN-based architectures to encode the input sentences, combined with a softmax layer for classification (Lazib et al., 2019;Chen, 2019). Despite the high performance on common benchmarks, results are biased by the fact that negation scope is often delimited by punctuation and other dataset artefacts (Fancellu et al., 2017). As such, they are potentially only learning domain-specific surface features rather than capturing the true semantics of negation. NegBERT applies a large pre-trained language model to the problem of negation detection, outperforming previous deep learning methods on negation detection, with especially high gains on scope resolution.

Yet, lexicon-driven strategies are sensitive to unseen syntactic patterns. This limitation motivated hybrid methods that augment rules with dependency parsing. Dependency-based cues were found to better delineate scope in long sentences (Councill et al., 2010). Nevertheless, performance drops when parsers are trained outside the target domain (Habernal et al., 2014). Sequence labeling with CRFs and SVMs offered stronger generalization than purely rule-based baselines (Morante and Daelemans, 2009). However, feature engineering for negation is costly and brittle across languages (Councill et al., 2010). The rise of distributed representations reduced reliance on manual features by encoding context automatically (Mikolov et al., 2013). Neural architectures such as BiLSTMs with CRF decoders became standard for scope detection (Katiyar and Cardie, 2017). Attention mechanisms further improved the modeling of long-distance interactions (Bahdanau et al., 2015). Transformer backbones accelerated this trend and enabled large-scale pretraining (Vaswani et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019 which in turn supported more robust cue and scope models. Contextual encoders trained with masked language modeling proved highly transferable to biomedical text (Devlin et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). In clinical narratives, domain-adaptive pretraining yields consistent improvements over general models (Alsentzer et al., 2019). Several works integrate negation supervision into multitask objectives to encourage shared representations (Søgaard and Goldberg, 2016). Others exploit syntactic probes to analyze whether models truly capture negation semantics (Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Despite these advances, systematic generalization to out-of-distribution negation remains challenging (Hossain et al., 2020). Prior evaluations often rely on artifacts such as quotation marks or list punctuation that correlate with scope boundaries (Fancellu et al., 2017). To mitigate artifact overfitting, recent benchmarks include cross-genre splits and adversarial test sets (Gururangan et al., 2020). Transfer learning across domains is further complicated by annotation guidelines that differ in cue inventories and scope granularity (Morante and Blanco, 2012). Cross-lingual negation resources are comparatively scarce, hindering robust multilingual modeling (Jiménez-Zafra et al., 2020). Multitask and multilingual pretraining promise to address data scarcity but require careful calibration of objectives (Conneau and Lample, 2019). Beyond English, morphosyntactic variation challenges cue lexicons and complicates scope heuristics (Moreno et al., 2011). Emerging contrastive objectives try to disentangle factuality, modality, and negation to improve transfer (Gunel et al., 2021). Nevertheless, ablation studies indicate that tokenization choices and subword segmentation can alter cue detection substantially (Sennrich et al., 2016). In this work, we consolidate prior findings and propose evaluation protocols that reduce artifact reliance and emphasize genuine semantic competence.

document: This section reviews back-translation for NMT, broader data augmentation techniques, and semi-supervised machine translation. Early work in phrase-based systems anticipated the idea of translating monolingual data back into the source side. Bojar and Tamchyna (2011) outlined reverse translation as a way to exploit target-side monolingual corpora. Subsequent studies in phrase-based MT, such as Bertoldi and Federico (2009) and Lambert et al. (2011), adapted the procedure for domain adaptation. With neural models, Sennrich et al. (2016a) established back-translation as a core training signal for strong sequence-to-sequence systems. Currey et al. (2017) showed that copying the target side to the source can surprisingly help in low-resource regimes. Caswell et al. (2019) introduced tags to flag synthetic sources, improving the model’s handling of mixed data. To optimize the generation of synthetic sources, researchers compared search strategies for the backward model. Imamura et al., 2018) advocated sampling to create more diverse pseudo-sources than beam search. Edunov et al. (2018) systematically compared sampling, noise injection, and beam search for data generation. Their evidence suggested that stochastic or noisy synthetic data yields a stronger learning signal than deterministic alternatives. Graça et al. (2019) framed back-translation from an optimization perspective and proposed N-best list sampling. More recently, Nguyen et al. (2020) diversified training corpora via multiple forward and backward models. Several works emphasized the importance of controlling quality and diversity in the backward model. Practical pipelines often balance between fluency of pseudo-sources and their lexical variety. Studies have also examined the impact of domain mismatch between monolingual data and parallel test conditions. Beyond back-translation, data augmentation in NMT draws on general machine learning ideas. Norouzi et al. (2016) explored reward-augmented maximum likelihood to shape generation behaviors. Zhang and Zong (2016) proposed structured perturbations tailored to bilingual sequences. Fadaee et al. (2017) synthesized sentences to increase the exposure of rare or unseen tokens. Wang et al. (2018) introduced SwitchOut to perturb inputs while preserving semantics in expectation. Despite their promise, augmentation benefits can vary substantially across datasets and metrics. Recht et al. (2019) documented distribution shifts that complicate reliable generalization assessment. Werpachowski et al. (2019) analyzed variance sources that interact with training set composition. Li et al. (2019) connected augmentation effects to input sensitivity and margin-based robustness. Semi-supervised approaches aim to leverage abundant monolingual data when parallel corpora are scarce. Gulcehre et al. (2015) investigated strategies for incorporating language models and monolingual signals into NMT. He et al. (2016) proposed dual learning to couple translation directions through reconstruction rewards. Lample et al. (2018) demonstrated unsupervised and weakly supervised training with iterative back-translation. Contemporary systems commonly interleave pseudo-labeling, denoising, and consistency training. Gulcehre et al. (2017) integrated target-side language model states within the decoder to guide generation. Wu et al. (2019) compared the utility of source-side and target-side monolingual data for different objectives. Edunov et al. (2020) provided human evaluations indicating that gains hold for natural text and translationese. Open questions remain about detecting harmful synthetic artifacts and calibrating uncertainty in the backward model. Another line of inquiry concerns how augmentation interacts with scaling laws and multilingual transfer. Overall, the literature converges on the view that carefully curated synthetic data is a reliable driver of NMT improvements.

document:
Neural text ranking. A number of so-called cross-attention models concatenate a query and a candidate document into a string and feed it into the model (Han et al., 2020;, which allows the attention mechanism of the model to capture interactions across query and document terms. However, deploying such models to millions or billions of documents is usually intractable due to the exorbitant computational cost. To combat this cost, other studies have explored more efficient models, e.g., dual-encoder models (Karpukhin et al., 2020), BERT with late interaction (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), or using contextual language models to improve term weighting in traditional inverted indexes (Dai and Callan, 2020). While approximate nearest neighbor retrieval has narrowed the gap, practitioners still face a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency in production systems. Caching strategies and index pruning can further reduce latency, but these techniques risk omitting rare yet highly informative signals.

A few studies that are most closely related to this work focus on leveraging the generative nature of pretrained encoder-decoder language models. A natural practice is to directly use the likelihood of generating the query given a document to rank the documents (Zhuang and Zuccon, 2021; Zhuang et al., 2021b; Lesota et al., 2021). However, these methods mostly perform substantially worse than cross-attention ranking models. Another work (dos Santos et al., 2020) transforms the likelihood of generating the query into a discriminative loss, where an "unlikelihood" loss is introduced for negative query-document pairs. Despite relatively better performance than using vanilla maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), we found that their method still underperforms cross-attention ranking models. Our proposed method uses a combination of query generation loss and a cross-entropy loss on a specific token, which is capable of achieving comparable performance to cross-attention models. Building on encoder-decoder priors, we also consider reranking with shallow lexical constraints to stabilize optimization across domains. As a formative reference, Vatswani et al. provides early evidence that generation signals can regularize ranking.

Other work (Ju et al., 2021) uses query generation as an auxiliary task during training and shows improved performance. However, the proposed model still takes both a query and a document as input in the main ranking task and hence would be as costly as cross-attention ranking models during inference. In contrast, we decouple representation building from interaction computation to enable partial precomputation without sacrificing fidelity.

Efficient neural IR. Due to the excessive computational cost of inference in pretrained language models, there is a series of studies aiming to improve the efficiency.

A major trend is to distill expensive models into cheaper ones (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019). Some distillation approaches have specifically focused on text ranking applications (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhuang et al., 2021a; Chen et al., 2021a; Hofstätter et al., 2021).

Another trend is to improve model efficiency by modifying the model architecture. A typical approach used by ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) and PreTTR (MacAvaney et al., 2020) defers query-document interactions to upper layers so that part of the model can be pre-computed. Our model can be categorized into this class of models, except that the late interaction is naturally aligned with the decomposition of encoder-decoder models. This alignment allows us to better leverage knowledge learned by the model during pretraining, and can be the reason behind our stronger performance compared to ColBERT and PreTTR.

There are a couple of other efficient model structures, such as early exiting (Soldaini and Moschitti, 2020; Xin et al., 2020), Transformer-Kernel (TK) model (Hofstätter et al., 2020), and contextualized offline relevance weighting (Chen et al., 2021b). In terms of storage cost, Cohen et al. (2021) proposed the succinct document representation which reduces the dimension of token representation to compress document representations. These techniques are orthogonal to our study and can be combined with our work to further improve the time and storage efficiency.

document: Modern applications increasingly require document embeddings that preserve utility while protecting sensitive information. Prior work has largely focused on modeling semantics without explicit privacy guarantees. In this paper, we revisit the design of document embeddings through the lens of controlled sentence indistinguishability. We consider a document as a finite sequence of sentences and aim to learn a mapping to a low-dimensional space. Our perspective aligns with recent efforts in privacy-preserving representation learning, yet departs in how it treats local perturbations. Instead of masking tokens or obfuscating entities, we treat any single sentence replacement as a plausible perturbation of the document. This perspective motivates a randomized mechanism that balances stability with informativeness for downstream tasks. Related strands of research on adversarial robustness and text anonymization offer partial inspiration Nguyen et al., 2019). However, these approaches rarely formalize indistinguishability at the sentence level within a document. We therefore articulate a setting in which each document is a point in a product space of sentences. A randomized embedding is then defined as a measurable mapping from documents to vectors in a Euclidean space. The key requirement is that swapping one sentence for another has limited impact on the distribution of embeddings. Such a requirement supports privacy by limiting sentence-level memorization and supports robustness by smoothing predictions. To operationalize this idea, we propose a mechanism that injects calibrated randomness during representation construction. Mechanistically, the embedding aggregates sentence-level summaries through a stochastic selector and a noise-aware projector. The mechanism is designed to maintain predictive signal while diluting the influence of any individual sentence. In parallel, we introduce a family of utility metrics that capture downstream classification and retrieval performance. Our theoretical analysis connects these metrics to the parameters of the mechanism via concentration bounds. The analysis yields guidance for choosing noise scales and sampling rates as a function of document length. We complement theory with algorithms that are practical for large corpora and modern accelerators. The training objective interleaves contrastive goals with stability-promoting regularizers that penalize sentence sensitivity. We also discuss how our framework interfaces with pretraining objectives commonly used in language models. While many prior papers evaluate privacy heuristically, we adopt a measurable indistinguishability criterion rooted in randomized mappings. Our approach situates document embeddings within a broader design space that includes clustering and metric learning. We further position our work against recent evaluations of privacy leakage in text encoders (Khan et al., 2017; Li 2018). Unlike defenses that rely on heavy redaction, our mechanism preserves fluency and topical coherence. We report experiments across review datasets, email threads, and multi-turn messages to test generality. The results show stable performance under sentence replacement and strong accuracy on standard benchmarks. Ablations indicate that both the stochastic selector and the projector are necessary to meet the indistinguishability target. We additionally find that representation smoothing improves calibration of uncertainty estimates. A sensitivity study confirms that the mechanism scales gracefully with the number of sentences per document. We analyze failure cases where highly idiosyncratic sentences still exert outsized influence on embeddings. Finally, we outline ethical considerations and discuss deployment contexts that benefit from controlled indistinguishability. Together, these contributions offer a principled path toward privacy-aware document embeddings without sacrificing utility. We release code, proofs, and preprocessed data to facilitate transparent evaluation and reproducibility.

document: Event representation learning is a central problem in natural language understanding. Prior work has examined causal, temporal, and entailment relations among events (Ning et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2020). Such representations benefit tasks including event schema induction (Li et al., 2020), narrative modeling (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008; Li et al., 2018; Lee and Goldwasser, 2019), and knowledge graph construction (Sap et al., 2019). Many efforts have focused on learning distributed event representations from heterogeneous supervision. Despite different motivations, a common idea is to exploit explicit relations as training signals. These signals can be grouped into discourse relations obtained automatically (Zheng et al., 2020), manually annotated external knowledge such as sentiments and intents (Lee and Goldwasser, 2018; Ding et al., 2019), and co-occurrence information (Weber et al., 2018). Prior work emphasizes the first two categories, leaving the effective use of co-occurrence relatively underexplored. Although discourse cues and external knowledge are fine-grained and accurate, they cover only a narrow subset of event relations defined a priori. By contrast, document-level co-occurrence is abundant yet underutilized in current models. We propose to exploit document-level co-occurrence to learn event representations without any additional annotations. Recent advances in instance-wise contrastive learning have improved unsupervised visual and textual representations (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020a; Grill et al., 2020; Zbontar et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2021). This line of work learns an embedding where positives are pulled together and negatives are pushed apart, typically using the InfoNCE objective (van den Oord et al., 2019). Unlike margin losses with a single positive and negative, InfoNCE naturally handles many negatives per batch. In our approach, we extend contrastive learning to a weakly supervised setting by leveraging co-occurring events as implicit positives. Following the design choices summarized in (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020a; Hu, 2021), we adopt momentum encoders and sizable negative sets. We further tailor the positive pair definition to treat events that co-occur within a document as soft positives with temperature-calibrated weights. Complementary to contrastive learning, deep clustering has emerged as a powerful tool for representation learning (Caron et al., 2018; Caron et al., 2020; Zhan et al., 2020; Asano et al., 2020). Inspired by optimal transport formulations, we map instance embeddings to a set of learnable prototypes while balancing assignment entropy (Caron et al., 2020; Asano et al., 2020). Distinct from prior work, our method jointly performs weakly supervised contrastive learning and prototype-based clustering to capture both local and global event regularities.

(2020a); Hu (2021)' or simply a parenthetical citation without 'in'. The span is only the citation text and contains exactly 11 words.

document: Deep Transformer models have recently achieved substantial gains in neural machine translation and other sequence modeling tasks. However, increasing depth exacerbates optimization instability and computational cost. Vanishing or exploding gradients, slow convergence, and degraded validation performance commonly emerge as the stack grows. Early work proposed stronger residual pathways and careful normalization to stabilize very deep encoders and decoders Bapna et al., 2018). Subsequent studies assessed pre-normalization versus post-normalization and showed that pre-norm architectures enable deeper training with modest quality trade-offs (Xiong et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Dropout, stochastic depth, and scheduled layer dropping further regularize deep models to avoid overfitting while preserving capacity at convergence (Huang et al., 2016; Fan et al., 2020). LayerDrop and its variants reduce training compute by randomly skipping layers, yielding robust ensembles at test time (Fan et al., 2020). Another practical axis of improvement is progressive stacking, in which a shallow model is trained first and then depth is increased during continued training (Gong et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). This curriculum on depth mitigates optimization barriers and reuses learned representations efficiently (Li et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation and intermediate supervision can also guide deeper students using signals from strong but shallower teachers (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019). On the optimizer side, adaptive methods with warmup and cosine decay schedules remain standard for stable deep training (Vaswani et al., 2017; Loshchilov and Hutter, 2017). Weight initialization strategies tailored to residual networks help control signal propagation across dozens of layers (Zhang et al., 2019; Touvron et al., 2021). Normalization choices such as LayerNorm, RMSNorm, and ScaleNorm further influence training dynamics at large depth (Ba et al., 2016; Zhang and Sennrich, 2019; Child, 2020). From a systems perspective, pipeline and tensor parallelism allow deeper Transformers to train within practical memory and time budgets (Narayanan et al., 2021; Shoeybi et al., 2019). Reversible residual networks reduce activation memory by reconstructing layer inputs on the fly, enabling deeper stacks without expensive checkpointing (Gomez et al., 2017; Kitaev et al., 2020). In parallel, continuous-depth formulations connect Transformers to ordinary differential equations and neural ODEs for adaptive computation (Chen et al., 2018; Rubanova et al., 2019). ODE-inspired Transformers aim to trade discrete layer counts for continuous trajectories that can be integrated with error control at training and inference (Zhu et al., 2022). Compared with strictly stacked layers, such models may balance stability, expressivity, and computational efficiency in a unified framework (Zhu et al., 2022). Nevertheless, empirical results still indicate that conventional deep stacks remain highly competitive when carefully tuned on strong baselines (Ott et al., 2018; Nguyen and Salazar, 2019). A key open question is how to allocate parameters across depth and width given a fixed compute budget and data scale (Kaplan et al., 2020; Levine et al., 2020). Recent scaling studies suggest diminishing returns from depth alone unless accompanied by sufficient regularization and large-batch optimization (Kaplan et al., 2020; Shallue et al., 2019). At inference time, early-exit mechanisms and test-time layer dropping can reduce latency while retaining translation quality for many inputs (Elbayad et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020). Domain adaptation techniques such as continued pretraining and parameter-efficient finetuning interact with depth, sometimes favoring deeper encoders for low-resource transfer (Gururangan et al., 2020; Houlsby et al., 2019). Architectural refinements including lightweight convolutional feed-forward blocks and gated activations also support deeper networks without prohibitive overhead (Wu et al., 2019; Shazeer, 2020). In addition, improved positional representations can stabilize information flow across many layers, which is crucial for long sequences typical in translation (Shaw et al., 2018; Press et al., 2021). In this work we view ODE Transformer as orthogonal to the aforementioned strategies and therefore complementary in practice (Lu et al., 2018; An et al., 2021). We hypothesize that combining continuous-depth computation with progressive stacking and robust normalization will yield deeper yet better-behaved models for large-scale translation. The remainder of this section contextualizes these directions and motivates our experimental choices for efficient and stable deep Transformer training.

document: Suicide detection and prevention using NLP methods has caught the attention of many researchers. Due to the privacy restrictions associated with clinical databases, researchers have used publiclyavailable data from social media with manual annotations for recognizable signals of mental health issues (Coppersmith et al., 2015;Shing et al., 2018), as first popularized by Coppersmith et al. 2015. The majority of methods, however, focus on detecting suicidal attempts or accessing suicide propensity of users based on social media posts (Coppersmith et al., 2015;Bhat and Goldman-Mellor, 2017;Shing et al., 2018;Zirikly et al., 2019). As such, these prior work has only relied the setting of overall text classification that fails to explore fine-grained analysis/classification at word level to reveal suicide-related events as we do. Prior research effort for ED has introduced various methods for this problem, including feature engineering (Ahn, 2006;Ji and Grishman, 2008;Li et al., 2013) and deep learning (Chen et al., 2015;Wang et al., 2019;Cui et al., 2020) models. However, such prior work mainly utilizes the ED datasets with general event types and formal texts, i.e., ACE-05 (Walker et al., 2006), that might not be helpful for specific domains. Recently, there have been some effort on creating new datasets for ED in more specific domains, including biomedical texts (Kim et al., 2009), literary texts (Sims et al., 2019), and cybersecurity texts (Satyapanich et al., 2020). However, none of existing ED datasets explore suicide-related events in social media texts. In contrast, our study frames suicide-related event detection as a sequence labeling problem to capture triggers and arguments at the token level. We review prior work on mental health detection in social media that incorporates linguistic signals, temporal cues, and user-level context (De Choudhury et al., 2013;Sinyor et al., 2017;Guntuku et al., 2017). While these approaches show strong performance on risk screening, they rarely model the compositional structure of events, a gap emphasized by recent ED surveys (Nguyen et al., 2016;Wadden et al., 2019). To situate our contribution, we compare with general-domain neural ED models that leverage contextual embeddings and span-based decoding (Nguyen and Grishman, 2015;Chen et al., 2015;Yang and Mitchell, 2016). For social media, data weakly labeled by keywords or subreddit membership has been used to bootstrap classifiers, a strategy later consolidated in shared tasks and community resources. Event-centric modeling has instead prioritized domain adaptation techniques and schema generalization across corpora (Liu et al., 2018;Huang et al., 2018;Du and Cardie, 2020). In mental health NLP, there is a growing interest in transparent pipelines that include lexicon induction, trigger normalization, and human-in-the-loop validation (Chancellor et al., 2019;Gibson et al., 2022). We also draw on research about ethical considerations, including data minimization, consent models, and harm mitigation strategies (Benton et al., 2017;Hovy and Spruit, 2016). As a practical matter, domain shift from clinical narratives to noisy user-generated text necessitates robust pretraining and normalization of colloquialisms (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019), with empirical evidence on demographic covariates and temporal dynamics (Bhat and Goldman-Mellor, and 2017). Finally, we position our dataset in relation to prior resources for suicide risk and affect, noting differences in annotation unit, label granularity, and release policies (Shing et al., 2018;Gaur et al., 2019), and we summarize open challenges for reliable ED in this sensitive domain.

document: Diacritization aims to restore missing vowel and gemination marks in abjad scripts and is critical for readability, text-to-speech, and downstream NLP. In Hebrew, most graphemes are consonantal and optional niqqud marks are rarely printed in modern prose. By contrast, Arabic uses a comparable set of diacritics but the orthographic conventions and error profiles differ substantially between the two languages. Research specifically targeting Hebrew diacritization remains sparse relative to Arabic, and earlier systems mostly relied on word-level features and lexica. Early Arabic work framed diacritization as sequence labeling with character-level recurrent networks (Belinkov and Glass, 2015). Subsequent studies experimented with deeper bidirectional LSTMs and richer input features for ambiguous cases (Abandah et al., 2015; Zalmout and Habash, 2017). Other efforts cast the task as a character-to-character transduction problem within encoder–decoder architectures (Mubarak et al., 2019). Morphology-aware pipelines have also been advocated to reduce hallucinations and enforce agreement constraints (Habash, 2010; Eskander et al., 2013). However, fully supervised analyzers are often brittle outside curated domains and expensive to maintain at scale. To balance robustness and cost, recent work investigates lightweight neural models augmented with constrained decoding or lattice inputs (Tīrãn, 2018; Alqahtani et al., 2020). For Hebrew, available corpora are smaller and noisier, complicating cross-domain generalization and calibration. Prior attempts to adapt Arabic resources to Hebrew report mixed transfer gains due to divergent phonotactics and morphological templaticity (Sagi and Tsarfaty, 2012). A frequently cited baseline is a 3-layer BiLSTM trained with cross-entropy over diacritic tags Belinkov and Glass, 2015). Extensions separate the prediction of core vowel marks from secondary marks to reduce class explosion (Abandah et al., 2015). More recent encoder–decoder methods predict both the character stream and diacritic sequence jointly to capture deletions and insertions (Mubarak et al., 2019; Abandah et al., 2022). Hybrid architectures integrate morphological analyzers for lattice constraints or candidate pruning at inference time (Zalmout and Habash, 2017; Pasha et al., 2014). Beyond architecture, data augmentation via synthetic vocalization and noisy channel perturbations has been shown to improve recall on rare templates (Shahrour et al., 2019). Evaluation typically uses WER-like metrics at the mark, character, and word levels, though correlation with perceived readability remains debated (Biadsy et al., 2009). Domain shifts across news, social media, and religious texts further stress models, exposing brittle heuristics in tokenization and normalization (Darwish et al., 2018). In production scenarios, latency, memory footprint, and on-device personalization are as important as raw accuracy. Our work situates Hebrew diacritization within this landscape and emphasizes modularity so that morphological priors can be injected without bespoke analyzers. We also report ablations that disentangle capacity from data scaling and regularization to clarify where gains originate. For context, we reimplement competitive baselines using BILSTM-CRF taggers and transformer encoders, following common setups in Arabic diacritization (Belinkov and Glass, 2015; Mubarak et al., 2019). We further compare against a simple rule-based fallback for out-of-vocabulary tokens as proposed in classical Hebrew grammars (Kahn, 2013). Related strands include semi-supervised pretraining on unvocalized text and multitask learning with morphological tagging (Seddah et al., 2020; Klein and Tsarfaty, 2021). We additionally reference resources that curate parallel vocalized corpora and alignments to aid error analysis (Suleman and Al-Obeidat, 2019). Finally, we note claims that purely neural systems underperform analyzer-driven pipelines on long-tail morphology, a position echoed by Habash 2017 in morphological evaluation. These observations motivate our approach and frame the contributions presented in the remainder of this paper.

document:
Our focus in this paper is the evaluation of social biases in English and not debiasing methods. We defer the analysis for languages other than English and developing debiasing methods for sense embeddings to future work. Hence, we limit the discussion here only to bias evaluation methods.

Biases in Static Embeddings: The Word Embedding Association Test (WEAT; Caliskan et al., 2017) evaluates the association between two sets of target concepts (e.g. male vs. female) and two sets of attributes (e.g. Pleasant (love, cheer, etc.) vs. Unpleasant (ugly, evil, etc.)). Here, the association is measured using the cosine similarity between word embeddings. Ethayarajh et al. (2019 showed that WEAT systematically overestimates the social biases and proposed relational inner-product association (RIPA), a subspace projection method, to overcome this problem. Word Association Test (WAT; Du et al., 2019) calculates a gender information vector for each word in an association graph (Deyne et al., 2019) by propagating information related to masculine and feminine words. Additionally, word analogies are used to evaluate gender bias in static embeddings (Bolukbasi et al., 2016;Manzini et al., 2019;Zhao et al., 2018). Loureiro and Jorge (2019) showed specific examples of gender bias in static sense embeddings. However, these datasets do not consider word senses, hence unfit for evaluating social biases in sense embeddings.

Biases in Contextualised Embeddings: May et al. (2019) extended WEAT to sentence encoders by creating artificial sentences using templates and used cosine similarity between the sentence embeddings as the association metric. Follow-up work compared alternative association metrics and found that sentence-level bias estimates can depend strongly on the choice of templates. More recent analyses examine how subword tokenization and layer pooling affect measured bias in contextual encoders. Despite methodological progress, benchmarking practices remain fragmented across datasets, domains, and linguistic phenomena. To address this fragmentation, we synthesize common evaluation desiderata and propose a taxonomy of bias probes applicable to both static and contextual embeddings. Our taxonomy emphasizes transparent reporting and reproducible protocols, building on prior recommendations by Smith et al., 2020; Jones et al., 2021, and we release code to encourage standardized comparisons.

(2020) and Jones et al. (2021)'.

document: There is limited literature on adversarial attacks for structured prediction compared to classification-oriented studies. This gap is notable because outputs like parse trees and label sequences impose interdependent constraints that adversaries can exploit. Foundational adversarial training for NLP focused on tasks such as text classification, machine translation, and dialogue systems, demonstrating both robustness gains and optimization instabilities (Liang et al., 2018; Alzantot et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Ebrahimi et al., 2018; Cheng et al., 2019a). For structured prediction, researchers have begun to adapt these ideas to parsers, taggers, and sequence-to-sequence decoders with task-specific loss shaping (Zheng et al., 2020; Smith, 2019; Lee, 2018. Notably, Zheng et al. (2020) propose replacing tokens with part-of-speech matched alternatives to target dependency parsers. Complementary work studies character-level and subword perturbations that respect morphological constraints while preserving semantic plausibility. Training-time defenses typically combine adversarially perturbed inputs with clean data and adjust decoding constraints to maintain well-formed outputs. Recent surveys synthesize progress across these areas and emphasize the need for standardized evaluation and open-source tooling. In parallel, several studies explore adversarial example generation for translation and tagging Cheng et al., 2020a; Ebrahimi et al., 2018) to evaluate model transfer and calibration. Our work extends this line by coupling lexically constrained attacks with structure-aware training objectives that preserve global consistency. We also systematize threat models, datasets, and decoding settings to enable reproducible and comparable results across structured prediction benchmarks.

document: Early attempts to use QG for educational applications involved generating gap-fill or "cloze" questions 1 (Taylor, 1953) from textbooks (Agarwal and Mannem, 2011). One may optionally choose to generate distractors to make these questions multiple choice (Narendra et al., 2013; Correia et al. 2012). This procedure has been shown to be effective in classroom settings (Zavala and Mendoza, 2018) and students' scores on this style of generated question correlate positively with their scores on human-written questions (Guo et al., 2016). However, there are many situations where gap-fill questions are not effective, as they are only able to ask about specific unambiguous key terms. In recent years, with the advent of large crowdsourced datasets for extractive question answering (QA) such as SQuAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2018), neural models have become the primary methods of choice for generating traditional interrogative style questions (Kurdi et al., 2019). A common task formulation for neural QG is to phrase the task as answer-aware, that is, given a context passage C = {c 0 , ..., c n } and an answer span within this context A = {c k , ..., c k+l } such that k ≥ 0 and k + l ≤ n, train a model to maximize P (Q|A, C) where Q = {q 0 , ..., q m } are the tokens in the question. These models are typically evaluated using n-gram overlap metrics such as BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR (Papineni et al., 2002;Lin, 2004;Banerjee and Lavie, 2005) with the reference being the original human-authored question as provided by the extractive QA dataset. The feasibility of using answer-aware neural QG in an educational setting was investigated by Wang et al., 2018, who used a BiLSTM encoder (Zhang et al., 2015) to encode C and A and a unidirectional LSTM decoder to generate Q. They trained on the SQuAD dataset (Rajpurkar et al., 2018) and evaluated on textbooks from various domains (history, sociology, biology). They showed that generated questions were largely grammatical, relevant, and had high n-gram overlap with human-authored questions. However, given that we may not always have a convenient list of key terms to use as answer spans for an input passage, we desire to move past answer-aware QG models by first identifying salient spans likely to serve as answers and then generating questions conditioned on them. To this end, we first identify salient spans in the input that are most likely to be used as answer targets for questions. We can then take these extracted answer spans and give them as input to an answer-aware QG model P (Q|A, C). This modeling choice allows for more controllable QG and more direct modeling of term salience. Previous work done by Subramanian et al. (2018) trained a BiLSTM Pointer Network (Vinyals et al., 2015) for answer extraction and showed that it outperformed an entity-based baseline when predicting answer spans from SQuAD passages, yet their human evaluation centered on question correctness and fluency rather than the relevance of answer selection, and similar studies did not explicitly ask annotators about topical relevance (Willis et al., 2019;Cui et al., 2021;Wang et al., 2019;Du and Cardie, 2018;Alberti et al., 2019;Back et al., 2021).

(2018)'. Both are four-word citation spans and represent realistic formatting mistakes.

Sparse seq2seq models. Our proposed α-ReLU transformation is based on the α-entmax transformation of Peters et al. (2019), which in turn is a generalization of the sparsemax transformation (Martins and Astudillo, 2016). In our work, we study sparseness at the output of a neural network. Nevertheless, there are a number of works aimed at sparsification within a neural network. For example, Malaviya et al. (2018); Peters et al. (2019);Correia et al. (2019) show that sparsemax and αentmax can replace softmax in the attention mechanism with some success. A recent work of Zhang et al. (2021) attempted to replace softmax with a component-wise ReLU in the attention mechanism. Unfortunately, in its pure form, this replacement leads to the inability of the model to learn at all, since its loss function does not decrease during optimization. The authors solve this problem by adding a normalizing layer on top of the attention layer.

These and other works (Zhang et al., 2019) state that sparsity in the weights of attention produces more interpretable patterns. However, Meister et al. (2021) questioned this claim and were unable to find clear evidence to support it. Therefore, in this work, we focused on the application of α-ReLU to the output of the transformer model, and not to the mechanism of attention, but at the same time we do not deny the possibility of studying the latter.

Self-normalization. Self-normalizing training aims to bypass the need of normalization during inference time. This is done by tweaking the learning mechanism so that the sum of all predictions sums (approximately) to a constant value. Theoretical work on why this works is poorly understood (Andreas et al., 2015 but early work in neural machine translation has shown its empirical value. Vaswani et al. (2013) achieves that by using noisecontrastive estimation (the neural model is used to re-rank the output of a hierarchical phrase-based machine translation system). Noise-contrastive estimation is also the standard training mechanism for word2vec (more popular than the alternative hierarchical softmax), which also eschews any expensive normalization. Differently, Devlin et al. (2014) changes the training loss to include a factor that encourages the normalizing factor to be 1. At inference time, this is just assumed and decoding time is reported to achieve a 15x speed-up. We also discuss links between self-normalization and calibrated confidence in probabilistic decoding. Prior work on temperature scaling in language models has similar goals but different inductive biases (Guo et al., 2017). In machine translation, constrained decoding often interacts poorly with heavy normalization, motivating lightweight substitutes. Recent benchmarks on sparse sequence modeling report mixed results depending on the entropy of the target distribution. Finally, we outline open problems in connecting sparsity-promoting losses with identifiability in attention layers.

Pretrained Language Models. Pretraining large-scale language models significantly improves the performance of downstream tasks. There are three types of pretrained models. First, autoencoding models learn a bidirectional contextualized encoder for natural language understanding via denoising objectives (Devlin et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2019; Lan et al., 2020; Clark et al., 2020). Second, autoregressive models are trained with a left-to-right language modeling objective (Radford et al., 2018a,b; Brown et al., 2020). Third, encoder–decoder models are pretrained for sequence-to-sequence tasks (Song et al., 2019; Bi et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020).

Among encoder–decoder models, BART conducts NLU tasks by feeding the same input into the encoder and decoder, and taking the final hidden states of the decoder. Instead, T5 Raffel et al., 2020) formulates most language tasks in the text-to-text framework. However, both models require more parameters to outperform autoencoding models such as RoBERTa. UniLM (Dong et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2020) unifies three pretraining paradigms under the masked language modeling objective with different attention masks.

NLU as Generation. Previously, pretrained language models completed classification tasks for NLU with linear classifiers on the learned representations. GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018b) and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) show that generative language models can complete NLU tasks such as question answering by directly predicting the correct answers without finetuning, given task instructions or a few labeled examples. However, generative models require many more parameters to work due to the limit of unidirectional attention. Recently, PET (Schick and Schütze, 2020a,b) reformulates input examples as cloze questions with patterns similar to the pretraining corpus in the few-shot setting. It has been shown that, combined with gradient-based finetuning, PET can achieve better performance in the few-shot setting than GPT-3 while requiring only 0.1% of its parameters. Similarly, Athiwaratkun et al., 2020 and Paolini et al. (2020) convert structured prediction tasks, such as sequence tagging and relation extraction, to sequence generation tasks.

Blank Language Modeling explores a complementary direction by pretraining with blank infilling and evaluating in both NLU and generation tasks (Donahue et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2020).

document: Sentence Representation Learning examines how to encode sentences into fixed-size vectors for broad downstream utility. Early work explored learning objectives tied to context prediction and autoencoding to obtain general-purpose features (Kiros et al., 2015; Hill et al., 2016). Subsequent studies distinguish between supervised and unsupervised paradigms for training encoders (Conneau et al., 2017; Cer et al., 2018). In supervised settings, annotated entailment and similarity datasets provide signals that shape semantic geometry (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018). Popular sentence-level models such as InferSent and SBERT leverage these resources to deliver strong transfer (Cer et al., 2018; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). In contrast, unsupervised approaches attempt to derive meaningful representations without task labels, often via distributional assumptions (Hill et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020). As a simple baseline, pooling pre-trained word embeddings remains competitive for many tasks (Mikolov et al., 2013). The advent of pre-trained language models has shifted attention toward adapting contextual encoders for sentence-level use (Devlin et al., 2019). A recurring challenge is the anisotropy of contextual embeddings, which compresses information along dominant directions (Ethayarajh, 2019; Li et al., 2020). Remedies include flow-based transformations and whitening to enhance isotropy and separability (Li et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). Beyond geometric post-processing, contrastive objectives have emerged as a powerful means of calibrating sentence embeddings. Besides, recent works Gao et al., 2021) adopt contrastive learning to refine the representations of PLMs. These methods generally form positive pairs from perturbed views of the same sentence and negative pairs from unrelated sentences. The quality of augmentations and negatives, however, critically affects both alignment and uniformity in the learned space. Contrastive Learning has a long trajectory in computer vision, where instance discrimination pretext tasks established strong baselines (Hadsell et al., 2006). Modern formulations employ temperature-scaled InfoNCE losses and large queues or memory banks to stabilize training (He et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020). Data augmentation plays a central role by generating semantically consistent positives through cropping, color jitter, and other transformations (Chen et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2021). When ported to language, analogous augmentations include back-translation, token shuffling, and dropout masking (Fang and Xie, 2020; Yan et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2021). For sentence representation learning, these augmentations approximate invariances that preserve semantics while encouraging robustness. At the same time, naive negative sampling can introduce false negatives that hinder optimization, especially in semantically dense corpora. Several works therefore explore hard-negative mining using retrieval heuristics or pre-computed similarity graphs (Robinson et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017). Other approaches modulate loss terms to downweight uncertain negatives or to reweight positives based on difficulty (Sohn, 2016; Wang and Liu, 2021). Supervised contrastive learning extends the objective to use label-aware positives (Khosla et al., 2020. In the sentence domain, labels may correspond to entailment relations, paraphrase clusters, or discourse roles depending on the dataset. Another design choice concerns the temperature parameter, which controls the concentration of the softmax over similarities (Wang and Liu, 2021). Careful tuning of temperature interacts with batch size and the number of negatives, creating trade-offs between efficiency and accuracy. Recent research also examines multi-view augmentations that jointly perturb lexical content and syntactic structure to better capture meaning (Yan et al., 2021). Beyond pairwise losses, listwise and neighborhood-preserving objectives have been proposed to align local manifolds more faithfully (Zhang and Ma, 2021). For evaluation, standardized sentence transfer suites assess performance on classification, clustering, and retrieval tasks (Conneau and Kiela, 2018). On semantic textual similarity benchmarks, contrastively tuned encoders often close the gap with supervised baselines while retaining efficiency (Gao et al., 2021). When combined with whitening or centering, these objectives mitigate anisotropy and yield more uniformly distributed representations (Huang et al., 2021). Nonetheless, domain mismatch between augmentation heuristics and target tasks can degrade robustness in practical deployments. Mitigation strategies include task-aware augmentation selection and curriculum schedules that vary perturbations across training. Additionally, incorporating pseudo-labels from teacher models can supply soft positives for low-resource settings (Izacard and Grave, 2020). Open challenges persist in understanding how negatives should be selected in semantically overlapping corpora and how to prevent representation collapse without large batches. Overall, the literature suggests that combining principled augmentations, calibrated losses, and modest post-processing yields strong and transferable sentence embeddings.

document: Several benchmarks have been developed to evaluate general language understanding over the past few years. GLUE (Wang et al., 2019b) is one of the first frameworks developed as a formal challenge affording straightforward comparison between taskagnostic transfer learning techniques. SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a), styled after GLUE, introduces a new set of more difficult language understanding datasets. Other similarly motivated benchmarks include DecaNLP (McCann et al., 2018), which recasts a set of target tasks into a general questionanswering format and prohibit task-specific parameters, and SentEval (Conneau and Kiela, 2018), which evaluates explicitly fixed-size sentence embeddings. Non-English benchmarks include Rus-sianSuperGLUE (Shavrina et al., 2020) and CLUE (Xu et al., 2020), which is a community-driven benchmark with nine Chinese natural language understanding tasks. These benchmarks in the general domain provide a north star goal for researchers and are part of the reason we can confidently say we have made great strides in our field. For BioNLP, many datasets and benchmarks have been proposed (Wang et al., 2020;Li et al., 2016;Wu et al., 2019) which promote biomedical language understanding (Beltagy et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2020). Tsatsaronis et al. (2015) propose biomedical language understanding datasets as well as a competition on largescale biomedical semantic indexing and question answering. Jin et al. (2019) propose PubMedQA, a novel biomedical question answering dataset collected from PubMed abstracts. Pappas et al. (2018) propose BioRead, which is a publicly available cloze-style biomedical machine reading compre-hension (MRC) dataset. Gu et al. (2020) create a leaderboard featuring the Biomedical Language Understanding & Reasoning Benchmark (BLURB). Unlike a general domain corpus, the annotation of a biomedical corpus needs expert intervention and is labor-intensive and time-consuming. Moreover, most of the benchmarks are based on English; ignoring other languages means that potentially valuable information may be lost, which can be helpful for generalization. In this study, we focus on Chinese to fill the gap and aim to develop the first Chinese biomedical language understanding benchmark. Note that Chinese biomedical text is linguistically different from English and has its domain characteristics, necessitating an evaluation BioNLP benchmark designed explicitly for Chinese. Prior Chinese biomedical NLP research has often focused on specific tasks such as named entity recognition, relation extraction, and document classification. While task-specific datasets exist, they differ in annotation guidelines, label spaces, and evaluation metrics, which complicates fair comparison across methods. A unified benchmark can standardize task definitions and reporting, enable rigorous ablations, and better reveal strengths and weaknesses of pretrained models. Recent domain-adaptive pretraining efforts on Chinese clinical notes and biomedical literature have shown promising gains for downstream tasks (Peng et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020). However, the field still lacks a comprehensive, multi-task suite with consistent splits, baselines, and public leaderboards for Chinese BioNLP. We therefore survey available corpora and identify representative tasks spanning concept extraction, normalization, relation extraction, sentence similarity, natural language inference, and question answering. For each task, we align or refine label schemas, provide standardized train–dev–test partitions, and supply strong baselines using modern encoder architectures (Devlin et al., 2019;Lan et al., 2020). We also include cross-domain evaluations that contrast performance on clinical notes versus scientific abstracts to assess domain transfer. To promote transparency, we release scripts for preprocessing, evaluation, and reproducible experimental settings, and we document dataset licensing and privacy considerations. Our design emphasizes robust evaluation by reporting macro and micro metrics where appropriate and by including calibration and efficiency indicators. We further analyze error patterns by linguistic category, domain source, and entity type, relating them to known challenges in Chinese processing such as segmentation ambiguity and terminology variation (Sun et al., 2020. Finally, we position our benchmark as a foundation for future multilingual biomedical evaluation by proposing mapping protocols to align Chinese labels with English-centric resources (Wei et al., 2021). We anticipate that a shared, open benchmark will accelerate method development and foster more reproducible, clinically relevant Chinese BioNLP research.

document: Biomedical NEL is challenging because the underlying ontology (most often UMLS) is extremely large and annotated training data requires rare and expensive expertise. Loureiro and Jorge (2020) presented MedLinker, which predicts the semantic type of a medical mention and filters candidates of the wrong type. MedLinker was tested on the MedMentions task of concept linking Mohan and Li, 2019), improving above TaggerOne, the baseline model for MedMentions which did not use deep learning. MedLinker splits entity linking into candidate recognition and linking, combining a BiLSTM-CRF for contextual matching with approximate dictionary matching to increase recall. In cross-lingual settings, dictionary matching is not always applicable, so we report results on the same MedMentions dataset in 5.2. Prior work shows that in-domain text can yield gains over general-domain language models (Gu et al., 2020). Accordingly, BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020) and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019) pretrain on scientific text for biomedical NEL. SapBERT (Liu et al., 2021) exploits UMLS graph structure to learn mention encodings aligned with synonym relations, and combining it with PubMedBERT pretraining (Gu et al., 2020) further boosts NEL. Experiments show SapBERT outperforms domain-specific BERT variants on BC5CDR, and we compare against these approaches on BC5CDR and MedMentions. Indexing the biomedical literature requires precise detection of medical concepts, motivating models that generalize to unseen entities. Mohan et al. (2021) proposed LRR, a low-resource recognition and linking model that integrates linking predictions into mention segmentation. We adopt LRR’s bottom-up candidate generation (see 4.2) and address its main drawback with a UMLS dictionary fine-tuning technique that expands candidate pairs on part of the training data. We explain the motivation in 4.5 and demonstrate contributions via ablations in 5.4. Cross-lingual NEL (xNEL) grounds source-language mentions in a target-language KB, often requiring both translation and transliteration when alphabets and phonologies differ. Bitton et al. (2020) curated Camoni, an annotated corpus of Hebrew health-community posts where noisy user text exacerbates transliteration challenges. For instance, the query “How do I know I have fibromyalgia?” may fail retrieval when written as the noisy transliteration ‘fibromialgia’. Bitton et al. (2020) introduced MDTEL for Hebrew–English NEL on noisy OHC text and evaluated it on Camoni. We retain MDTEL’s unsupervised UMLS dictionary mapping from English to Hebrew via an attention-based encoder–decoder, but replace later stages with multilingual BERT based candidate generation, high-recall matching, and contextual filtering. These components yield substantial improvements over MDTEL on Camoni while maintaining applicability to English documents.

document: Previously, most length-controllable approaches in abstractive summarization focused on stopping decoding at a particular time. An early ad-hoc approach (Rush et al., 2015) generated the EOS token by assigning a score of −∞ to tokens in the vocabulary and produced a fixed number of words. LenEmb and LenInit introduced length embeddings into the decoder to guide generation (Kikuchi et al., 2016). LC incorporated the desired length into the first layer of a CNN encoder (Liu et al., 2018). Fan et al. (2018) predefined special markers to denote different length ranges and prepended inputs with such markers during training and testing. Takase and Okazaki (2019) extended sinusoidal positional encoding to account for stepwise remaining length. GOLC optimized LenEmb and LC by formalizing a loss with an overlength penalty (Makino et al., 2019). Bian et al. (2019) treated LenInit and LenEmb as an agent and adjusted the reward to incorporate the desired length. LenAtten added a length attention unit to exploit length information based on the stepwise remaining length (Yu et al., 2021). Other length-controllable approaches determined content via length-aware intermediate summaries. LPAS extracted a word sequence with the desired length from the source document and generated a summary using a non–length-controllable model conditioned on the document and the extracted sequence (Saito et al., 2020). MLS first produced a general summary and then fed it to a length-controllable model (Sarkhel et al., 2020). Compared with previous methods, our approach controls the length of generated summaries by pretraining a length-aware information selection model on a length-balanced dataset. It can also generate summaries with length close to the target in zero-shot length control. This design avoids brittle length tokens while maintaining content fidelity across varying budgets. In addition, the model exposes an interpretable knob that can be adjusted without retraining. We further analyze how content selection interacts with compression under strict constraints. Empirically, we show consistent improvements across news and scientific domains. Our ablations confirm that learning to select yields better controllability than learning to stop. Recently, many studies fine-tune pretrained transformer seq2seq models on summarization datasets (Lewis et al., 2020; Zhang et al. 2020. These baselines achieve strong ROUGE improvements with minimal task-specific engineering. Our approach is implemented atop a transformer seq2seq model and can be combined with pretrained checkpoints without modification. We emphasize that our mechanism is orthogonal to architectural changes such as pointer networks or coverage penalties. By decoupling selection from realization, the method generalizes across compression ratios. Prior work on budgeted summarization explores token-level knapsack objectives and reinforcement learning. However, such methods often require delicate reward shaping and can be unstable. Curriculum strategies that gradually tighten length constraints have also been proposed. In contrast, we enforce the target budget directly via an auxiliary selection head. Length-conditioned planning is further supported by content salience predictors derived from extractive signals. We hypothesize that explicit selection encourages the decoder to avoid verbosity. To probe this claim, we measure off-target overflow and underflow relative to the requested budget. We also analyze sensitivity to mis-specified length prompts at inference time. The results indicate robust adherence up to moderate distribution shifts in desired lengths. Moreover, human raters prefer our outputs when budgets are extreme, suggesting better prioritization. Our contributions are threefold: a simple selection module for precise length control, a training recipe for balanced supervision, and a comprehensive evaluation under strict budgets. We release code and data splits to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

We discuss several lines of research in controllable text generation.

Prompts For Prompt-based control, a context is prepended to a language model input. The prepended item is related to the type of desired output. This method has been used to manipulate the syntax (Dušek and Jurčíček, 2016;Goyal and Durrett, 2020) and semantics (Wen et al., 2015;Chen et al., 2019) of the output. Alternatively, the prepended item can provide semantic control in order to cover a given topic (Wang et al., 2019a), mention specified entities (Fan et al., 2019), or display a certain attribute (Hu et al., 2017;Balakrishnan et al., 2019) or even exhibit a style of text (Keskar et al., 2019. These methods suffer from the inability to exert fine-control, that is, a change in any one of the input prompts will change the whole generated text. Furthermore, all these works utilise non-expansive features for their prompts, which prevents them from making iterative improvements to existing generated text.

Content Planning Despite the impressive progress made in many generation tasks, earlier neural models are known to produce low-quality content (Wiseman et al., 2017), often with low relevance, and poor discourse structure. Content-based planning approaches (Moryossef et al., 2019;Yao et al., 2019;Hua and Wang, 2019) were added into neural systems to enhance content relevance. Hua et al. (2021) extended previous content planning approaches by dividing the content plan into different types of information; 'Entities', 'Claim' and 'Concepts'. An alternative planning approach Hua and Wang (2020) focuses on where the key phrases should be placed in the generated text in order to improve coherence. The text generator is then conditioned on the provided key phrases and their respective positions in text. Similar to our approach, these methods allow users varying levels of custom control by manual augmentation of the planning module output.

Syntactic or Discourse Control Various works (Li et al., 2020;Nguyen et al., 2020) utilised syntactic parse trees with the transformer structure to gain syntactic control or improve interpretability on short-form text generation tasks. With a focus on improving long-form text generation, Ji and Huang (2021) used a VAE structure to model RST discourse relations between successive elementary discourse units (spans of text). We build upon their approach by using a more expressive binary tree formalisation of RST. This formalisation extends the modelling of sequential elementary discourse units by also modelling nuclearity and relationship between non sequential discourse units.

Extending on previous works, Wang et al. (2019b) attempted to couple tree structures and transformers. We instead embed the tree structure of RST into transformers through constrained attention in a separate RST embedding dimension. Recent advances in controllable decoding complement these planning and prompting approaches by constraining the search procedure. Techniques such as nucleus sampling and constrained beam search allow models to trade off diversity and fidelity in a principled manner (Holtzman et al., 2020;Anderson et al., 2017). However, decoding-time control is often brittle when objectives conflict, motivating joint training strategies that align latent representations with target attributes (Krause et al., 2020). Parallel to these trends, human-in-the-loop editing frameworks seek to combine automatic generation with iterative author guidance to reduce cognitive load (Yuan et al., 2021;Stiennon et al., 2020). Our work situates itself at this intersection by imposing discourse-aware constraints while preserving flexibility for user-driven adjustments.

document: We study the problem of identifying toxic spans in user-generated text. Prior research typically treats toxicity as a document-level or comment-level decision, but span-level identification provides actionable guidance to moderators and users. Accordingly, the literature divides into toxicity detection and opinion word extraction, each offering complementary insights for locating offensive content. Classical toxicity detection benchmarks include Wikipedia talk pages and community forums (Wulczyn et al., 2017; Borkan et al., 2019; Zampieri et al., 2019), while Schmidt and Wiegand 2017 introduced influential feature analyses. Despite strong performance, such classifiers do not specify which substrings trigger the decision and thus limit interpretability. Span-level detection reframes the task as highlighting minimal toxic substrings that justify the overall label. Approaches encompass sequence tagging with conditional random fields, pointer networks, and span classifiers built on contextual encoders (Ma and Hovy, 2016; Peters et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019). Pretrained transformers have become central backbones for both classification and tagging in this domain (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). However, models trained solely for classification often overfit spurious cues such as usernames, quoting conventions, or platform-specific markup. Opinion word extraction research targets sentiment-bearing tokens and offers modeling tools for span localization (Liu et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2016). In that line, lexicon-augmented CRFs and neural taggers have been widely deployed to mark subjective expressions (Wang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017; Li and Lam, 2017). Nonetheless, direct transfer from sentiment spans to toxic spans is difficult because harassment can be sarcastic, implicit, or reclaimed by targets. Context modeling remains essential to disambiguate offensive uses from benign mentions, particularly in code-mixed and conversational data (Waseem and Hovy, 2016; ElSherief et al., 2018). Compositionality further complicates detection because multiword insults and metaphors distribute toxicity across several tokens. Influential representation learning advances are often attributed to the transformer architecture Vaswani et al. 2017, which enables long-range dependency modeling and transfer. For span detection, token-level rationale supervision encourages faithful explanations aligned with human highlights (Jain et al., 2020; DeYoung et al., 2020). However, collecting fine-grained rationales is costly and can suffer from annotator subjectivity and low agreement. Weak supervision strategies combine lexicons, heuristics, and pattern-based votes to supervise models at scale (Ratner et al., 2017; Hancock et al., 2018). Recently introduced shared tasks and datasets have standardized evaluation for toxic span detection and catalyzed comparative analyses (Pavlopoulos et al., 2021). Nevertheless, cross-domain transfer remains challenging because euphemisms, community norms, and platform affordances shift over time. Domain adaptation via adversarial learning and instance reweighting mitigates but does not eliminate these distribution shifts (Ganin et al., 2016; Wang and Deng, 2018). Multilingual settings introduce additional issues in subword segmentation, morphological variation, and culturally specific slurs. To address such variability, recent work augments encoders with character-level channels and morphology-aware features (Akbik et al., 2018; Aguilar et al., 2020). Fairness research further examines whether span predictions disproportionately implicate identity terms and proposes debiasing interventions (Dixon et al., 2018; Park et al., 2018). From an application perspective, span-level rationales enable targeted redaction, user education, and more transparent appeals processes. Building on these insights, our method unifies sequence tagging with span pruning and mask-based saliency regularization to improve precision and coverage. We position our contribution relative to prior classifiers and extractors through controlled comparisons and ablations across datasets and domains. The following sections detail our model, datasets, and evaluation protocols and report findings that contextualize prior trends.
