document: Visual question answering (VQA) aims to answer natural language queries about images or videos by jointly reasoning over visual and linguistic inputs. Early datasets focused on real-world photographs and open-ended answers, as illustrated by Malinowski and Fritz 2014 and the VQA benchmark (Antol et al., 2015). Subsequent benchmarks introduced compositional reasoning with synthetic scenes to reduce dataset biases (Johnson et al., 2017; Kafle and Kanan, 2017). Despite progress, annotation artifacts prompted the design of debiased collections and robustness tests (Goyal et al., 2017; Agrawal et al., 2018). Beyond recognition, dynamic and causal reasoning have been emphasized by video-based or event-centric tasks (Tapaswi et al., 2016; Zellers et al., 2019; Lei et al., 2020). A salient example is CLEVRER, which organizes queries into descriptive, explanatory, predictive, and counterfactual categories with causal notions of cause, enable, and prevent (Yi et al., 2020). In parallel, advances in multimodal architectures, from attention-based fusion to pretrained vision–language transformers, have lifted accuracy across benchmarks (Anderson et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). However, faithful reasoning remains challenging because shortcuts can still be exploited by models trained end-to-end on biased supervision (Kaushik et al., 2020; Niu et al., 2021). To situate our contribution, we review dataset design choices across real and synthetic imagery and their implications for causal evaluation. Real-image datasets capture long-tail semantics and open-world variability but may entangle knowledge with biases in question phrasing and answer frequency (Antol et al., 2015; Goyal et al., 2017). Synthetic environments, by contrast, facilitate precise control over object attributes, physics, and interventions needed for counterfactual questions (Johnson et al., 2017; Yi et al., 2020). Early neural baselines such as stacked attention and hierarchical co-attention illustrated the benefits of explicit cross-modal alignment (Yang et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2016). Subsequent methods exploited scene graphs, program induction, or neuro-symbolic execution to disentangle perception from reasoning (Johnson et al., 2017; Hudson and Manning, 2019; Mao et al., 2019). Meanwhile, large-scale pretraining on aligned image–text corpora has yielded strong zero-shot and fine-tuned VQA performance (Tan and Bansal, 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021). Despite these advances, consistent evaluation across descriptive and counterfactual queries remains limited, motivating standardized protocols that integrate causal structure with answerability constraints (Pearl, 2009; Yi et al., 2020).

document: Pre-processing steps can substantially alter the results of the LDA models even in languages with good tokenization heuristics such as English (Schofield and Mimno, 2016; May et al., 2016). We believe that languages that do not have clear tokenization standards deserve investigation into what kind of processing is appropriate. Many works recognize that LDA results can be improved when inputs include phrases (Lindsey et al., 2012; Lau et al., 2013; Yu et al., 2013; El-Kishky et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016; Bin et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). We consider it valuable to specifically assess approaches to determining these phrases. Despite their popularity in analyzing large amounts of text data, LDA models are notoriously complex to evaluate. One must evaluate both the statistical fit of a model and the human-registered thematic coherence of the words found to arise in the high-probability words, or keys, of a topic, which may not correlate (Chang et al., 2009). Analyses often combine evaluations of fit (Wallach et al., 2009) and automated approximations of human judgments of coherence (Bouma, 2009; Mimno et al., 2011) based on mutual information, even with the expectation these may only somewhat correlate with true human judgments (Lau et al., 2014). A limitation of these existing approaches, however, is that they expect the vocabulary and tokenization to remain constant between two models. For our evaluation, we use a normalized log likelihood approach to capture fit while accounting for changes in vocabulary (Schofield and Mimno, 2016). Prior work on morphological segmentation suggests that subword-aware tokenization can mitigate sparsity in agglutinative languages (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kudo, 2018). However, topic models trained on subword units may redistribute probability mass in ways that hinder human interpretability (Gerz et al., 2018). Phrase discovery methods, such as mutual information filtering and collocation extraction, aim to balance coverage with precision when expanding the vocabulary (Lau et al., 2013; El-Kishky et al., 2014. In evaluating these methods, we distinguish between intrinsic metrics tied to likelihood and extrinsic metrics tied to downstream utility (Newman et al., 2010). We further consider whether automated coherence metrics remain stable when documents are resegmented using alternative tokenizers (Röder et al., 2015). While document-level likelihood rises with larger vocabularies, topic sparsity and redundancy often increase at the same time (Wallach et al., 2009). To contextualize our setup, we compare against pipelines that first learn phrases and then train LDA, as recommended by previous studies (Lau et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2016). Additional baselines include byte-pair encoding followed by LDA with symmetric priors (Sennrich et al., 2016). We also report sensitivity analyses over stopword lists, document length thresholds, and stemming variants (Porter, 1980; Snowball, 2002). For human evaluation, we adopt a standard word-intrusion task and a topic-intrusion task with blinded assessors (Chang et al., 2009; Lau et al., 2014). Cross-lingual experiments examine whether phrase-based preprocessing yields consistent gains in Arabic, Indonesian, and Vietnamese corpora (Conneau et al., 2020). To bridge methodological differences across languages, we report per-language normalization factors for likelihood and coherence, along with ablations of tokenizer choices (Mimno et al., 2011). Following best practices for reproducibility, we release code, configuration files, and preprocessed corpora with detailed documentation (Geiger et al., 2020). Our findings indicate that phrase-aware preprocessing generally improves coherence without severely degrading fit, though effects vary with corpus size and domain. Finally, we discuss implications for evaluation design and highlight open questions regarding robust tokenization for topic models in low-resource settings.

document:
We build on work in question generation and answering, pre-training, and few-shot learning.

Question Generation.
Neural question generation has been well-studied for different purposes (Du et al., 2017; Du and Cardie, 2018; Zhao et al., 2018; Lewis and Fan, 2019; Alberti et al., 2019; Puri et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2021; Bartolo et al., 2021).
We use generated questions to learn general-purpose representations.
We also show that a relatively simple strategy of generating the answer and question together with a single model can be effective; most prior work uses separate answer selection and question generation models.
Prior approaches range from template-based systems to neural encoder–decoder models with copy and coverage mechanisms.
Recent work further explores controlling the difficulty and answerability of generated questions via latent variables and reinforcement learning.
Our setting differs by emphasizing transfer to non-QA tasks through aligned supervision signals at the token level.

Phrase-indexed Question Answering.
Phrase-indexed question answering is a paradigm for open-domain QA that retrieves answers by embedding questions and candidate answers in a shared embedding space (Seo et al., 2018; Seo et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020).
It requires using a bi-encoder architecture for efficient phrase retrieval.
Especially related is Lee et al. (2021), which also uses question generation and a cross-encoder teacher model to improve phrase-indexed QA, though they focus on improving QA accuracy rather than transfer to other tasks.
Our results reinforce prior observations that bi-encoder models are usually less accurate at QA than cross-encoders (see Table 1).
However, the bi-encoder model transfers better to settings that require a contextualized representation of a single passage; the cross-encoder instead optimizes for producing representations of passage–question pairs.
We learn token-level representations, rather than a single vector for a sentence, and thus use token-level supervision from extractive QA.
This design aligns naturally with span selection and supports efficient indexing of sub-sentential units.

Few-shot learning.
We study few-shot learning without access to unlabeled data, following most recent work Brown et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021).
Schick and Schütze (2021) notably propose a semi-supervised approach that uses unlabeled data for knowledge distillation; this process does not improve accuracy, but mainly improves efficiency.
Moreover, large-scale unlabeled data may not be easily obtainable for all tasks, and utilizing such data increases computation time in the fine-tuning stage, so we focus on the setting without unlabeled data.
The aforementioned work uses language models for few-shot learning by converting tasks to language modeling problems; we develop alternative methods for few-shot learning that use token-level representations and question-based prompts.
Compared to purely prompt-based inference, our approach retains stable performance under prompt wording perturbations.
In downstream evaluation, we report averages over multiple random seeds to fairly assess small-sample variability.

Pre-training and Representation Learning.
Large-scale pre-training on unlabeled corpora has improved transfer for many language tasks (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020).
Nevertheless, objectives tailored to span prediction can offer complementary gains relative to sequence-level denoising.
Our experiments therefore pre-train with mixed objectives that encourage both phrase discrimination and contextual reconstruction.
We also investigate whether task-adaptive pre-training on domain-specific text further benefits question-centric supervision.
Results indicate consistent improvements in low-resource regimes while maintaining competitive efficiency.

Datasets and Evaluation Protocol.
We benchmark across diverse datasets for QA, retrieval, and classification, and we adopt standard splits and metrics to support transparent comparison with prior work.

There is a pool of prior work relating to SE. We summarize it in Table , depicting state-of-the-art approaches, level of annotations, what kind of competences are annotated, the modeling approaches, the size of the dataset (if available), type of skills annotated for, baseline models, and whether they release their annotations and guidelines.

As can be seen in Table , many works do not release their data (apart from Sayfullina et al., 2018 and Bhola et al., 2020) and none release their annotation guidelines. In addition, none of the previous studies approach SE as a span-level extraction task with state-of-the-art language models, nor did they release a dataset of this magnitude with manually annotated (long) spans of competences by domain experts.

In particular, Sayfullina et al. (2018) explored several approaches to SE. To create the data, they extracted all text snippets containing one soft skill from a predetermined list. Crowdworkers then annotated the highlighted skill whether it was a soft skill referring to the candidate or not. They show that an LSTM (Hochreiter et al., 1997) performs best on tagging the skill in the sentence. In our work, we annotated a dataset three times their size (Table ) for both hard and soft skills with domain experts. Tamburri et al. (2020) classifies sentences that contain skills in the JP. The authors manually labeled their dataset with domain experts. They annotated whether a sentence contains a skill or not. Once the sentence is identified as containing a skill, the skill cited within is extracted. In contrast, we directly annotate for the span within the sentence. Bhola et al. (2020) cast the task of skill extraction as a multi-label skill classification at the document-level. There is a predefined set of unique skills given the job descriptions and they predict multiple skills that are connected to a given job description using BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) (Goyal et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2019. In addition, they experiment with several additional layers for better prediction performance. We instead explore domain-adaptive pre-training for SE.

The work closest to ours is by Chernova (2020), who approach the task similarly with span-level annotations (including longer spans) but approach this for the Finnish language. It is unclear whether they annotated by domain experts. Also, neither the data nor the annotation guidelines are released. For a comprehensive overview with respect to SE, we refer to Khaouja et al. (2021). Finally, we highlight open challenges in multilingual SE that require richer typological coverage. These gaps motivate releasing both data and transparent guidelines alongside models.

document: Dense retrieval has emerged as a dominant paradigm for open-domain question answering and web search. Unlike sparse term matching, dense retrievers encode queries and passages into a shared vector space for nearest neighbor search. Early work demonstrated that dual-encoders trained with in-batch negatives can rival BM25 on large benchmarks (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Subsequent studies proposed improved negative mining and post-training schemes to further strengthen representations (Xiong et al., 2021; Thakur et al., 2021). Despite these advances, data augmentation and regularization for retrieval remain underexplored compared to classification and generation. Mixup and related interpolation-based schemes have shown consistent gains in vision and some NLP tasks (Zhang et al., 2018; Verma et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020a). However, applying input-level interpolation to retrieval is nontrivial because meaning is not preserved under convex combinations in embedding space. Prior NLP augmentation often operates by token perturbations such as synonym replacement and random deletion (Wei and Zou, 2019; Ma, 2019). For dense retrieval, token-level noise can corrupt lexical cues without ensuring retrieval-aware invariances. In contrast, dropout provides a simple and scalable way to induce representation-level perturbations during training and inference (Srivastava et al., 2014; Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). Following Karpukhin et al., we revisit negative sampling under stochastic encoders to examine robustness. Our central hypothesis is that structured stochasticity can help models generalize across domains and retrieval tasks. We therefore study dropout-driven perturbations as implicit data augmentation for dual-encoders. We complement this strategy with standard retrieval training using hard negatives mined by BM25 and strong dense models (Nguyen et al., 2016; Xiong et al., 2021). We also compare with adversarial feature perturbations that explicitly craft gradient-aligned noise (Miyato et al., 2017). Beyond augmentation, we situate our work within research on calibration and ensembling for retrieval. Monte Carlo dropout provides a cheap approximation to Bayesian ensembling with minimal changes to training pipelines (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). Prior evidence suggests that such stochastic ensembling can improve both accuracy and uncertainty estimates in neural ranking. For re-rankers, cross-encoders often provide stronger supervision signals than dual-encoders, enabling distillation into lightweight retrievers (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Hofstätter et al., 2021). We assess whether dropout perturbations remain beneficial when training with teacher signals from cross-encoders. Closely related are late-interaction models that constrain how token embeddings combine at query time (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Another line of work explores query expansion and synthetic data generation to improve coverage on tail intents (Nogueira et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2021). Several competitive baselines report gains from better negatives and large-scale continual pretraining (Xiong et al., 2021; Luan et al., 2021, though their benefits may depend on the mining strategy. Nevertheless, the community lacks a systematic analysis of representation-level perturbations across diverse retrieval settings. We therefore evaluate on a heterogeneous suite of tasks that include passage retrieval, entity linking, and scientific citation search (Thakur et al., 2021). Our experiments control for index size, training data volume, and evaluation protocol to facilitate fair comparisons. We further ablate dropout schedules, masking rates, and inference-time sampling strategies to isolate key factors. We find that dropout-based perturbations yield consistent improvements on in-domain development sets and demonstrate robustness to distribution shifts on BEIR. The approach complements hard-negative mining and is compatible with mixed-precision and large-batch training on modern accelerators. Compared to interpolation-based mixup, our method preserves input semantics while promoting smoother score landscapes for gradient-based learning. Finally, we discuss limitations, including computational overhead from sampling and potential interactions with batch normalization, and we outline directions for principled retrieval-specific augmentation.

document: Ficler and Goldberg (2017) control the stylistic aspects of the generated text with a conditioned RNN (Recurrent Neural Network) LM. Holtzman et al. (2018) compose a committee of discriminators to guide an RNN generator towards the generations with the desired linguistic quality. Hu et al. (2017) aim at controlling the sentiment and tense of the generated text by combining variational autoencoders (VAE) and attribute discriminators. Con-trolling these attributes of text generation has manifold applications, such as knowledge-grounded conversation (Dinan et al., 2019) and poetry generation (Ghazvininejad et al., 2017). More recently, with the advent of Transformers and large pretrained language models, such as GPT2, an extensive body of work has focused on controlling the generation of these Transformerbased models. Keskar et al. (2019) train a 1.63 billion-parameter conditional transformer LM from scratch with 55 attribute control codes to guide generation. However, this method is expensive and lacks flexibility since the control codes are fixed. Dathathri et al. (2020) address these limitations by developing a plug-and-play model which leverages an attribute discriminator to perturb the LM's hidden activations. However, updating gradients at the token level results in slow inference. Instead of updating the hidden activations, Krause et al. (2020); Yang and Klein (2021); Lin and Riedl (2021) introduce generative discriminators to re-weight the next token distributions on the fly during inference, thus improving the inference speed. Our work is mostly related to Yu et al. (2021); Li and Liang (2021). Yu et al. (2021) use a pretrained LM followed by an attribute alignment function to encode the tokens of the target attributes and the resulting hidden states are used to control generation. Different from their work, we do not take the tokens of the target attributes as input. Instead, we directly train a set of parameters, which acts as the prepended hidden states of GPT2, to control generation. Avoiding using attribute tokens can circumvent the problems when it is difficult to describe the desired attribute with only one word. Besides, Yu et al. (2021) focus on attributes disentanglement, which is not a focus in our work, so our training methods are different. Prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021) can, in a general sense, be viewed as controlling the generation of LMs, where the LM is controlled to depict a specific NLG task, while in this work, the LM is controlled to carry specific attributes in a generation. Besides, our proposed methods for prefix training are different from Li and Liang (2021), as stated in Section 1. Beyond generative discriminators, control has also been realized through learned prompts and soft prefixes that steer internal activations without updating backbone weights. Prompt-tuning and soft prompt methods reduce storage cost and enable rapid attribute switching, but they often suffer from brittle generalization across domains (Lester et al., 2021). Adapter-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning offers another path to conditional generation by inserting small bottleneck layers into each transformer block (Houlsby et al., 2019). Reinforcement learning from human feedback has been explored to shape high-level style and safety preferences at the sequence level (Ziegler et al., 2019). However, reward hacking and distribution shift can degrade fluency or semantic adequacy when the reward model is misaligned with user intent (Stiennon et al., 2020). Similar concerns about the robustness of classifier-guided decoding have been raised by Shelmanov et al. 2021 in the context of active learning. Another direction seeks disentangled latent controls that vary attributes while preserving content, often through cyclic losses or mutual-information regularization (Shen et al., 2017; John et al., 2019). In controllable toxicity reduction, reranking and lexical constraints have been combined with attribute models to minimize undesirable content without collapsing diversity (Gehman et al., 2020). Evaluation typically balances attribute accuracy, semantic similarity, and fluency metrics, acknowledging that strong control can inflate repetition or degrade coherence. To this end, we adopt automatic attribute classifiers alongside reference-free semantic measures and human preference studies to provide a holistic picture of controllability. Taken together, these insights motivate our approach of prefix-based control with efficient training objectives that scale to large LMs while maintaining faithful content.

document: Owing to the problems associated with single-task learning in terms of space and time requirements, several multi-task learning approaches have been proposed over the years. DecaNLP (McCann et al., 2018) built a multi-tasking model by converting the format of each task to a question answering format. Several other works have followed a similar approach by converting tasks to reading comprehension format (Mishra et al., 2020) and textual entailment (Wang et al., 2021b. The multitasking model T5 (Raffel et al., 2020) was built with the help of a unified framework that converts all text-based language problems into a text-to-text format. SCIFIVE (Phan et al., 2021) involved building a text-to-text model for the biomedical literature. T0 (Sanh et al., 2021) uses prompts along with instances to do multitask learning and focuses on achieving zero-shot task generalization. Under instruction learning, the turking test (Efrat and Levy, 2020) was proposed to measure the efficacy of models to follow instructions. Natural Instructions (Mishra et al., 2021b) broke down each task into multiple sub-tasks that helped models follow instructions and subsequently generalize to unseen tasks (cross-task generalization). FLAN (Wei et al., 2021) was built by leveraging instruction tuning on a diverse range of tasks and achieved zero-shot generalization on target unseen tasks. Task reframing (Mishra et al., 2021a) proposed several guidelines to reframe task instructions to improve model responses to follow instructions. Subsequent studies compared multi-task pretraining with instruction tuning and found complementary benefits under data-scarce regimes. In particular, standardized prompting reduced the need for task-specific heads while maintaining strong transfer on evaluation benchmarks. However, multi-task objectives can introduce negative transfer when task distributions are highly heterogeneous, motivating adaptive routing strategies. Recent work also emphasizes the role of high-quality instructions and exemplars for calibrating model uncertainty during zero-shot inference. Overall, the literature suggests that unifying task formats and curating instructions are synergistic pathways for scalable generalization.

document: Controllable text generation seeks to adjust attributes of output text while preserving fluency and content. Applications include detoxification, politeness control, sentiment steering, and domain adaptation for user-facing assistants. A common strategy is to retrain or fine-tune a large base model on domain-specific data to align generations with target attributes (Ziegler et al., 2019). Another strategy modifies the architecture of pre-trained transformers to expose control knobs during decoding (Keskar et al., 2019). In style transfer, many approaches train generative models using non-parallel data to disentangle content from style (He et al., 2020; Lample et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2020; Reif et al., 2021). However, fully retraining large models or designing new architectures from scratch is often costly in data, computation, and maintenance. To reduce such costs, recent work couples attribute discriminators with frozen or lightly adapted language models to steer generation during inference (Gu et al., 2017). Plug-and-Play Language Models perform gradient-based updates on hidden states to increase the likelihood of desired attributes at each step (Dathathri et al., 2020). While effective, this approach requires multiple backward passes per token and restricts the discriminator to operate on specific internal representations. GeDi leverages class-conditional generative discriminators to bias token probabilities during sampling, whereas FUDGE trains future discriminators to rescore partial sequences (Krause et al., 2020; Yang and Klein, 2021). These methods rely on heuristics over local softmax distributions and may struggle to incorporate global document-level constraints in a principled manner. Energy-based control formulations promise modularity by combining heterogeneous experts without per-step optimization over the generator. Such formulations aim to provide a simple probabilistic mechanism for integrating global features via additive or multiplicative potentials over sequences. Beyond algorithmic efficiency, controllable generation methods must also preserve factuality, avoid attribute leakage, and respect safety constraints. Evaluation typically considers trade-offs among attribute strength, semantic preservation, and fluency, often measured via human judgments and automatic metrics. Recent benchmarks report that discriminator-guided methods can achieve strong attribute control but sometimes degrade content fidelity under strong steering (Dathathri et al., 2020; Krause et al., 2020). Following the discriminator-guided paradigm introduced by Vatswani et al., we examine whether global experts can mitigate these trade-offs without retraining the base model. In parallel, conditional generation with prefix tuning or soft prompts offers lightweight adaptation by optimizing small continuous vectors appended to inputs (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). These adapters can encode attribute information but may require task-specific tuning and careful prompt selection to remain robust across topics. Decoder-time control methods such as constrained beam search and lexically guided sampling enforce hard or soft constraints without changing model parameters (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). However, constraints specified at the token level can be brittle and may not capture nuanced stylistic properties like politeness or formality. Reinforcement learning from human or preference feedback offers another path to controllability but often requires costly data collection and careful reward design to avoid reward hacking (Ziegler et al., 2019; Bai et al., 2022). A unifying view treats attribute control as posterior inference over text sequences under a prior given by a powerful language model and a likelihood capturing attribute evidence. This view naturally leads to importance sampling, rejection sampling with learned proposals, or energy-based reweighting during decoding. From a systems perspective, modular control is attractive because experts can be swapped, combined, or ablated without retraining the generator. For example, a toxicity expert and a sentiment expert can be composed to produce positive yet non-toxic responses in dialogue systems. Moreover, black-box experts make it possible to incorporate non-differentiable or proprietary detectors directly into the generation loop. Nevertheless, stability remains a concern, as strong expert signals can cause off-distribution sampling and reduce grammaticality. Careful calibration of expert strengths and adaptive temperature schedules can help maintain fluency while achieving target attributes. We also note that evaluation protocols should include out-of-domain generalization tests to assess robustness beyond a single dataset. Human-in-the-loop evaluations are essential to capture subjective dimensions of style that automatic metrics may miss. In this work, we position energy-based control as a complementary alternative to gradient-based or discriminator-only methods. Our approach is optimization-free at inference time, fully modular with respect to expert composition, and compatible with off-the-shelf autoregressive language models. Compared to prior discriminator-guided methods, it aims to incorporate global features without relying on token-level heuristics. We conclude that a principled probabilistic formulation of control can achieve strong attribute adherence while preserving content and fluency in practical settings.

document: Encoder-based language models such as BERT employ stacked Transformer encoder layers to compute contextualized token representations. Each layer comprises multi-head self-attention, position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections, and layer normalizations. The self-attention mechanism enables each token to aggregate information from all other tokens via learned attention weights. Formally, a head computes attention coefficients that link a query token to all key tokens within the same sequence. The outputs of multiple heads are concatenated and projected to yield the layer output for each token. Although attention weights are often visualized as explanations, several studies caution against weight-only interpretation. In particular, Kobayashi et al. 2020 argued that ignoring the norms of transformed value vectors can be misleading. Their analysis highlights that the magnitude of value vectors and the output projection crucially affect token attribution. Building on this idea, norm-based attention reformulates the layer output as a sum of per-token contribution vectors. The reformulation allows one to compute a matrix of token-to-token attributions that incorporates both weights and value magnitudes. This perspective yields a more faithful estimate of how inputs influence outputs across attention heads. Subsequent work extends the analysis to account for residual connections within the attention block. Residual connections add the input back to the transformed signal, thereby dampening cross-token mixing. Layer Normalization further rescales and recenters features, modulating the relative impact of residual and attention terms. By decomposing Layer Normalization into mean and variance components, one can separate normalization effects on contributions. The combined formulation defines token contributions after both residual addition and normalization. A context-mixing ratio can then be computed to quantify the degree of inter-token aggregation versus self-preservation. Empirical findings indicate that the ratio is often low in pretrained encoders. This suggests that residual pathways dominate the flow of information at inference time. As a result, attention may serve more to filter than to fully integrate distant context in many layers. Nevertheless, task-specific fine-tuning can increase mixing in layers closest to the output head. These observations motivate designing diagnostics that jointly consider weights, value norms, and downstream projections. They also motivate benchmarking how architectural choices shift attribution patterns across datasets. For reproducibility, prior work details the exact projection slices used to aggregate head outputs. It is important to use the same conventions when comparing attributions across models and training regimes. Beyond analysis, the norm-aware view can inform regularizers that explicitly encourage or discourage context mixing. Such regularizers could be integrated with contrastive objectives to stabilize attention behavior under distribution shift. Finally, understanding the balance between residual preservation and attention mixing can guide pruning decisions. We adopt this perspective to frame our experiments and to interpret layerwise effects observed after fine-tuning.

document: For NLP tasks, adapters are introduced for the transformer architecture. A set of adapter parameters is added at each transformer layer, typically in a bottleneck configuration as proposed by Houlsby et al. (2019). By keeping the output dimension identical, they cause no change to the structure or parameters of the original model. Adapters quickly gained popularity in NLP with various applications. For multi-task learning (Caruana, 1997; Zhang and Yang, 2017; Liu et al., 2019b), a projected self-attention layer is proposed by Stickland and Murray (2019), while Bapna et al. (2019) proposed an additional layer norm suitable for machine translation. Besides the applications of adapters, researchers are also dedicated to improving their performance. Based on the architecture introduced by Houlsby et al. (2019), AdapterFusion (Pfeiffer et al., 2020a) leveraged knowledge from multiple tasks with a new two-stage learning algorithm. Despite the recent popularity of these methods, they still train a relatively large number of training parameters. Recently, studies start to focus on improving the parameter efficiency of adapters. Diff-pruning (Guo et al., 2020) achieves parameter efficiency by adding a sparse, task-specific difference vector to the fixed original parameters. The vector is adaptively pruned during training with a differentiable approximation to the L0 norm penalty to encourage sparsity. Rücklé et al. (2020) introduced AdapterDrop, which has been integrated into AdapterHub (Pfeiffer et al., 2020b) by removing adapters from lower transformer layers during training and inference to reduce computational cost. Mahabadi et al. (2021) proposed Compacter, which improved the trade-off between performance and trainable parameters per task with low-rank optimization. On the other hand, without modifying the architecture of the PLM, BitFit (Ben Zaken et al., 2021) shows that fine-tuning only the bias terms of a large PLM is competitive with fine-tuning the entire model. Fine-tuning only the bias terms can be considered as adding a task-specific shift to the token representation. BitFit is most similar to our work. While in BitFit, the shifts added to all the representations are exactly the same for all input tokens, in our work, the shifts are token dependent. Subsequent research has explored combining adapters with prompt tuning to further reduce the number of trainable parameters (Lester et al., 2021). Prefix tuning offers an alternative by prepending trainable vectors to the key and value streams in attention while freezing the backbone (Li and Liang, 2021). Low-rank adaptation provides another lightweight route by injecting trainable matrices into attention and feed-forward projections (Hu et al., 2022). In multilingual settings, modular routing across language-specific adapters has shown strong transfer benefits (Pfeiffer et al., 2020c). Domain adaptation with adapters has been studied for continual learning scenarios to mitigate catastrophic forgetting (Biesialska et al., 2020). Robustness under distribution shift remains a central concern, motivating calibration-aware and sparsity-inducing regularizers for adapter training (Zhang et al., 2022). Despite steady progress, there is limited understanding of when adapters outperform partial fine-tuning across tasks with heterogeneous label spaces (He et al., 2021). Efficient hyperparameter selection is also underexplored, particularly for bottleneck size, placement, and initialization strategies (Mosbach et al., 2021). Practical deployment further necessitates compatibility with quantization and pruning without sacrificing accuracy (Frantar and Alistarh, 2023). Caching strategies and parameter sharing across layers can reduce memory pressure in long-context inference (Xiong et al., 2023). Several benchmarks now emphasize cross-domain generalization, pushing adapter methods to balance adaptability and stability (Wang et al., 2021). Layerwise analyses suggest that early-layer adapters capture lexical features, whereas higher layers encode task-specific abstractions (Dalvi et al., 2020). Nonetheless, negative transfer can occur when naively fusing adapters trained on divergent tasks (Knyazev et al., 2021). To address such issues, recent work proposes gating mechanisms that select adapter experts conditioned on inputs (Mahabadi et al., 2021). A complementary line of work investigates pruning adapters post hoc to meet strict latency budgets with minimal retraining (Sung et al., 2022). In this context, several studies have reported competitive results with tuned decoder-only models for sequence generation tasks, as compared with encoder–decoder adapters, notably in Liu et al., 2021). However, differences in evaluation protocols and training budgets complicate apples-to-apples comparisons across methods (Dehghani et al., 2023). Therefore, we revisit adapter design choices with a unified evaluation across tasks, budgets, and architectures. Our analysis aims to clarify trade-offs while providing practical guidance for parameter-efficient adaptation in real-world settings.

document: Prompt tuning for large language models has emerged as a compelling alternative to full-parameter fine-tuning. In particular, GPT-3 demonstrated strong few-shot capabilities when guided by natural language prompts (Brown et al., 2020). Early investigations explored manually crafted templates to steer model behavior across tasks (Schick and Schütze, 2021a; Schick and Schütze, 2021b; Mishra et al., 2021). Parallel efforts proposed automatic prompt search that optimizes discrete token sequences via gradient-free or gradient-guided strategies (Jiang et al., 2020; Shin et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021). Subsequent work introduced soft prompts, which learn continuous embeddings rather than surface tokens to condition the model (Li and Liang, 2021; Hambardzumyan et al., 2021; Zhong et al., 2021). These approaches showed that prompt tuning can approach or match full fine-tuning when models scale to billions of parameters (Lester et al., 2021). Despite their effectiveness, soft and hard prompt methods often demand extended training to converge on challenging tasks (Gao et al., 2021). This observation motivates techniques that transfer knowledge across tasks to initialize or regularize prompts for faster learning and better generalization (Ruder, 2017). In the pretraining era and beyond, intermediate-task training has been a successful recipe for improving downstream performance (Phang et al., 2018; Pruksachatkun et al., 2020; Gururangan et al., 2020). The basic idea is to adapt the model on related supervision before fine-tuning on the final target distribution (Wang et al., 2019a; Vu et al., 2020; Poth et al., 2021). Recent analyses have examined which tasks transfer well to others and under what conditions transfer helps or hurts (Vu et al., 2020). However, most transfer studies focus on full fine-tuning rather than prompt tuning, leaving open questions about when prompts can be reused effectively across datasets and models. A growing body of work begins to address this gap by studying cross-domain and cross-task prompt transfer within pretrained encoders and decoders. Initial evidence indicates that prompts can retain reusable task structure that survives distribution shifts better than full fine-tuning for certain scenarios. For instance, prompts learned on one sentiment dataset can provide a favorable initialization for another, reducing optimization time without sacrificing accuracy. Early template-based methods exemplify this phenomenon Schick and Schütze, 2021a,b; Mishra et al., 2021 by making label semantics explicit. Complementary lines of research learn soft prefix embeddings that are jointly optimized with frozen language models to encode task instructions efficiently (Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Beyond hand-designed or automatically discovered prompts, recent studies explore mixtures of prompts and routing strategies to capture subpopulation heterogeneity (Zhou et al., 2022). From a systems perspective, transfer can also occur across models of different sizes or architectures through student–teacher frameworks (Hinton et al., 2015). Knowledge distillation, Net2Net transformations, and related inheritance strategies offer complementary pathways to reuse capabilities with reduced compute (Chen et al., 2016; Qin et al., 2021). When combined with task-adaptive pretraining objectives, transfer-aware prompting can further enhance label efficiency e.g., Wang et al., 2019a) without incurring significant overhead. Despite these advances, systematic criteria for deciding when and how to transfer prompts remain underexplored. Practitioners often rely on costly ablations to select source tasks, initialization schemes, and regularizers. A principled account of prompt transferability would therefore benefit both research and deployment settings. In this work, we investigate cross-task prompt transfer for classification and sequence labeling on diverse benchmarks. We evaluate whether initialization from related tasks speeds convergence and improves generalization under limited supervision. We contrast transfer into encoder-only, encoder–decoder, and decoder-only families to assess architectural sensitivity. We also examine whether prompts learned on smaller backbones can provide effective seeds for larger models. To ground the analysis, we measure optimization speed, downstream accuracy, and stability across random seeds. We relate these outcomes to interpretable indicators such as task similarity, label-space alignment, and calibration metrics. We further test the robustness of transferred prompts under distribution shift and domain mismatch. Our ablations compare hard, soft, and hybrid prompt parameterizations under matched compute budgets. The results suggest that judicious transfer can cut training time substantially while preserving accuracy. We find that naive reuse may underperform without alignment, whereas lightweight regularizers can narrow the gap. Cross-model transfer appears promising when the student and teacher share tokenization and pretraining corpora. Overall, our study clarifies when prompt transfer is effective and offers guidelines for efficient adaptation with large language models.
